topics.title: Energy Scenario: Commercial and Non-Commercial Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Scenario: Commercial and Non-Commercial Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Energy

**Energy** is the fundamental physical quantity that enables work to be performed or heat to be transferred. From the first law of thermodynamics, energy can be neither created nor destroyed — only converted from one form to another. In the context of national planning and engineering economics, energy is broadly classified into two governing categories: **Commercial Energy** and **Non-Commercial Energy**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of energy into Commercial and Non-Commercial categories is the foundational taxonomy upon which all energy accounting, auditing, and policy planning rests. Every energy audit a B.Tech engineer performs must first identify whether the source falls under the commercial or non-commercial bucket, because the metering, pricing, and audit methodology differ radically.

## 1.2 Commercial Energy — Formal Definition

**Commercial Energy** refers to all forms of energy that have an established market price and are exchanged through formalized, metered, billable transactions between an energy producer/supplier and a consumer.

**Key Characteristics of Commercial Energy:**
- It is **metered, billed, and taxed**.
- It flows through **regulated grids, pipelines, or distribution networks**.
- It appears explicitly in the **Gross Domestic Product (GDP)** calculations and national energy balances.
- It is generated using modern technologies (thermal, hydro, nuclear, renewable grids).

**Examples of Commercial Energy:**
- Electrical Energy (from coal, hydro, nuclear, solar, wind, gas-based plants)
- Refined Petroleum Products: Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG), Diesel, Petrol (Gasoline), Kerosene, Aviation Turbine Fuel (ATF), Naphtha
- Natural Gas (piped and Compressed Natural Gas — CNG)
- Coal (Coking Coal, Non-Coking Coal, Lignite)
- Grid-connected Renewable Energy (Solar PV electricity, Wind power, Biomass-based electricity)

## 1.3 Non-Commercial Energy — Formal Definition

**Non-Commercial Energy** refers to energy sources that are **not bought or sold** in any formal market. They are gathered, harvested, or collected directly from nature or as agricultural/biomass by-products, primarily used in rural and un-electrified households.

**Key Characteristics of Non-Commercial Energy:**
- It carries **no direct monetary transaction** (it is essentially "free" at point of use).
- It is **not metered** and therefore **does not enter formal economic statistics**.
- It is highly **localized**, decentralized, and seasonally variable.
- It is predominantly **biomass-based** and used for **cooking, heating, and lighting** in rural India.

**Examples of Non-Commercial Energy:**
- Firewood
- Agricultural residues (rice straw, wheat straw, bagasse, groundnut shells)
- Cow-dung cakes
- Charcoal
- Vegetable waste

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of Commercial Energy as the groceries you buy from a supermarket — you scan each item, pay a bill, and the transaction is recorded. Think of Non-Commercial Energy as the mangoes plucked from your grandmother's backyard tree — they are real, they nourish you, but they never appear in any market bill or GDP ledger. Both feed you, but only one is "counted" by the formal economy.

## 1.4 Why the Distinction Matters for an Energy Manager

For an Energy Manager, this classification is critical because:

| Aspect | Commercial Energy | Non-Commercial Energy |
|---|---|---|
| Visibility in audits | High | Low / Often ignored |
| Loss accounting | Possible (metered) | Difficult (unmetered) |
| Policy lever | Tariff regulation | Subsidized alternatives (LPG, biogas) |
| Environmental cost | Internalized partially | Often externalized (deforestation, indoor pollution) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Energy Unit:** The international community measures energy in **Joules (J)**. However, practical engineering units include **kilowatt-hour (kWh)**, **calorie (cal)**, **British Thermal Unit (Btu)**, **Tonne of Oil Equivalent (TOE)**, and **Barrel of Oil Equivalent (BOE)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Commercial vs Non-Commercial Energy Spectrum

The classification of energy sources is not merely an academic exercise — it determines how a nation's planners, auditors, and policymakers approach resource allocation, environmental accounting, and rural development.

### 2.1.1 Commercial Energy in Detail

Commercial energy is the **backbone of modern industrial civilization**. The global economy is fundamentally powered by commercial energy, and its demand grows with industrialization, urbanization, and rising living standards.

**Sub-Categories of Commercial Energy:**
1. **Fossil Fuels** — Coal, Crude Oil, Natural Gas
2. **Electrical Energy** — Generated from fossil fuels, nuclear, hydro, or renewables
3. **Nuclear Energy** — From fission of U-235, Pu-239
4. **Renewable Commercial Energy** — Grid-connected Solar PV, Wind, Small Hydro, Biomass-based power

**Significance:**
- Drives manufacturing, transportation, agriculture (mechanized), and services.
- Subject to **International trade** and **geopolitical considerations**.
- Prices fluctuate based on global crude oil benchmarks (Brent, WTI).

### 2.1.2 Non-Commercial Energy in Detail

Non-commercial energy is also called **Traditional Energy** or **Subsistence Energy**. It is overwhelmingly **biomass-based** and forms the **primary cooking fuel for over 70% of rural Indian households** (as per Ministry of New and Renewable Energy data trends).

**Sub-Categories of Non-Commercial Energy:**
1. **Fuelwood** — Wood logs, twigs, branches collected from forests/farms
2. **Agricultural Residues** — Rice husk, wheat straw, maize cobs, coconut fronds
3. **Animal Waste** — Cow-dung cakes, buffalo-dung patties
4. **Charcoal** — Carbonized wood (used in some rural industries)
5. **Biogas (small scale)** — Often considered transitional between non-commercial and commercial

**The Hidden Cost of Non-Commercial Energy:**
- **Deforestation** and soil erosion
- **Indoor air pollution** causing respiratory diseases (especially in women and children)
- **Time poverty** — women and children spend hours collecting fuelwood
- **Carbon emissions** (unaccounted in national inventories)

## 2.2 Standard Energy Units and Conversion Factors

Energy is measured in multiple units across disciplines. An Energy Manager must be fluent in conversions.

### The Fundamental SI Unit

$$\boxed{1 \text{ Joule (J)} = 1 \text{ N} \cdot \text{m} = 1 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m}^{2} / \text{s}^{2}}$$

The Joule is dimensionally equivalent to **Watt-second**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Conversion / Formula | Value | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $1 \text{ cal} = ?$ J | $1 \text{ cal} = 4.186 \text{ J}$ | Food energy, biomass calorific value |
| 2 | $1 \text{ kcal} = ?$ kJ | $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.186 \text{ kJ}$ | Nutrition, food engineering |
| 3 | $1 \text{ kWh} = ?$ MJ | $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$ | Electricity billing conversion |
| 4 | $1 \text{ kWh} = ?$ kcal | $1 \text{ kWh} = 860 \text{ kcal}$ | Boiler efficiency calculations |
| 5 | $1 \text{ Btu} = ?$ kJ | $1 \text{ Btu} = 1.055 \text{ kJ}$ | HVAC, refrigeration (legacy US units) |
| 6 | $1 \text{ TOE} = ?$ GJ | $1 \text{ TOE} = 41.868 \text{ GJ}$ | National energy balance |
| 7 | $1 \text{ TOE} = ?$ Mcal | $1 \text{ TOE} \approx 10{,}000 \text{ Mcal}$ | Quick industry estimation |
| 8 | $1 \text{ TOE} = ?$ MMBtu | $1 \text{ TOE} \approx 39.68 \text{ MMBtu}$ | International oil/gas trade |
| 9 | $1 \text{ BOE} = ?$ kWh | $1 \text{ BOE} \approx 1{,}700 \text{ kWh}$ | Crude oil pricing benchmarks |
| 10 | $1 \text{ Quad} = ?$ Btu | $1 \text{ Quad} = 10^{15} \text{ Btu}$ | National energy policy (USA) |

### Derived Power Equations (for context)

**Mechanical equivalent of heat (Joule's constant):**

$$W = J \times H \quad \text{where } J = 4.186 \text{ J/cal}$$

**Calorific Value (CV) relationship:**

$$\text{Total Heat Released} = m \times \text{CV}$$

where $m$ is the mass of fuel (kg) and CV is the calorific value (kJ/kg or kcal/kg).

**Per Capita Energy Consumption:**

$$\text{Per Capita Energy} = \frac{\text{Total Primary Energy Consumption}}{\text{Total Population}}$$

This metric is a strong indicator of the **Human Development Index (HDI)** and **Quality of Life**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Energy Auditors** in industries convert all fuel bills (coal in tonnes, diesel in litres, electricity in kWh, gas in SCM) into a **common unit (TOE or GJ)** to compare and benchmark energy use across heterogeneous sources.
- **Policy Planners** at NITI Aayog and Ministry of Power track per capita commercial energy consumption as a **growth indicator**.
- **Rural Development Programs** (like the Pradhan Mantri Ujjwala Yojana — PMUY) aim to replace non-commercial fuels with subsidized LPG, directly shifting energy from the non-commercial to the commercial bucket.
- **Carbon Accounting** under the UNFCCC requires estimation of non-commercial biomass combustion, which contributes significantly to **black carbon** and **PM2.5 emissions**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Significance:** A textile factory in Coimbatore may consume coal (commercial), but its workers' homes may still use firewood (non-commercial). A holistic energy policy must address BOTH ends — industrial efficiency AND household fuel transition.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Key Unit Conversions

### 3.1.1 Derivation of kWh to MJ Conversion

The **kilowatt-hour (kWh)** is defined as the energy delivered by a power of **1 kilowatt** sustained for **1 hour**.

**Step 1 — Start with definitions:**

$$1 \text{ kW} = 1000 \text{ W} = 1000 \text{ J/s}$$

$$1 \text{ hour} = 3600 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply power by time:**

$$1 \text{ kWh} = (1000 \text{ J/s}) \times (3600 \text{ s})$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the seconds unit:**

$$1 \text{ kWh} = 1000 \times 3600 \text{ J}$$

$$1 \text{ kWh} = 3{,}600{,}000 \text{ J} = 3.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ J}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Megajoules (MJ):**

$$\boxed{1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}}$$

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Calorie to Joule Conversion

The **calorie (cal)** was historically defined as the heat required to raise the temperature of **1 gram of water by 1 °C** (specifically from 14.5 °C to 15.5 °C).

**Step 1 — Mechanical Equivalent of Heat (Joule's Experiment):**

James Prescott Joule established experimentally that:

$$1 \text{ cal} = 4.186 \text{ J (mechanical work)}$$

This is called the **Mechanical Equivalent of Heat**, denoted by $J$.

**Step 2 — Therefore:**

$$1 \text{ kcal} = 1000 \text{ cal} = 4186 \text{ J} \approx 4.186 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{1 \text{ kcal} = 4.186 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Step 3 — Practical verification:**

Energy released by burning 1 kg of coal (CV ≈ 6500 kcal/kg):

$$E = 6500 \text{ kcal/kg} \times 4.186 \text{ kJ/kcal} = 27{,}209 \text{ kJ/kg} \approx 27.2 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

This matches the standard calorific value of coal (~25–30 MJ/kg). ✓

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation of kWh to kcal Conversion

**Step 1 — Start with $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$:**

$$1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ} = 3600 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Use $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.186 \text{ kJ}$:**

$$1 \text{ kWh} = \frac{3600}{4.186} \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerical value:**

$$1 \text{ kWh} = 859.985 \text{ kcal} \approx 860 \text{ kcal}$$

$$\boxed{1 \text{ kWh} = 860 \text{ kcal}}$$

---

### 3.1.4 Derivation of Tonne of Oil Equivalent (TOE)

**Step 1 — TOE is based on the energy content of 1 tonne (1000 kg) of crude oil.**

The average gross calorific value of crude oil is approximately:

$$\text{CV}_{\text{crude oil}} \approx 10{,}200 \text{ kcal/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by 1000 kg:**

$$1 \text{ TOE} = 1000 \text{ kg} \times 10{,}200 \text{ kcal/kg} = 1.02 \times 10^{7} \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kilojoules:**

$$1 \text{ TOE} = 1.02 \times 10^{7} \times 4.186 \text{ kJ} = 4.27 \times 10^{7} \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Standard international rounding:**

$$\boxed{1 \text{ TOE} \approx 41.868 \text{ GJ} \approx 42 \text{ GJ}}$$

---

### 3.1.5 Derivation of BOE (Barrel of Oil Equivalent)

**Step 1 — A barrel of crude oil weighs approximately 136 kg.**

**Step 2 — Energy in 1 BOE:**

$$1 \text{ BOE} = 136 \text{ kg} \times 10{,}200 \text{ kcal/kg} = 1.387 \times 10^{6} \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kWh:**

$$1 \text{ BOE} = \frac{1.387 \times 10^{6}}{860} \text{ kWh} \approx 1613 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Standard rounding:**

$$\boxed{1 \text{ BOE} \approx 1700 \text{ kWh}}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Examples

### Example 1: Unit Conversion for an Energy Audit

**Problem:** A factory consumes the following in one month:
- Coal: 50 tonnes (CV = 5000 kcal/kg)
- Diesel: 2000 litres (density = 0.85 kg/L, CV = 10,200 kcal/kg)
- Electricity: 10,000 kWh

Calculate the **total energy consumption in TOE**.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1 — Energy from Coal:**

$$m_{\text{coal}} = 50 \text{ tonnes} = 50{,}000 \text{ kg}$$

$$E_{\text{coal}} = 50{,}000 \times 5000 = 2.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 2 — Energy from Diesel:**

$$m_{\text{diesel}} = 2000 \times 0.85 = 1700 \text{ kg}$$

$$E_{\text{diesel}} = 1700 \times 10{,}200 = 1.734 \times 10^{7} \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 3 — Energy from Electricity:**

$$E_{\text{elec}} = 10{,}000 \times 860 = 8.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 4 — Total Energy:**

$$E_{\text{total}} = 2.5 \times 10^{8} + 1.734 \times 10^{7} + 8.6 \times 10^{6}$$

$$E_{\text{total}} = 250{,}000{,}000 + 17{,}340{,}000 + 8{,}600{,}000 = 275{,}940{,}000 \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to TOE:**

$$E_{\text{total}} = \frac{2.7594 \times 10^{8}}{10{,}000} \text{ TOE} = 27{,}594 \text{ TOE}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{total}} \approx 27.59 \text{ TOE}}$$

---

### Example 2: Per Capita Energy Consumption

**Problem:** India's annual commercial energy consumption is approximately **700 MTOE** (Million Tonne of Oil Equivalent). Population ≈ 1.4 billion. Calculate per capita energy consumption in **kWh**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert MTOE to TOE:**

$$700 \text{ MTOE} = 700 \times 10^{6} \text{ TOE}$$

**Step 2 — Convert TOE to kWh:**

$$1 \text{ TOE} \approx 11{,}630 \text{ kWh} \quad (\text{since } 1 \text{ TOE} = 42 \text{ GJ} = 42 \times 10^{6} \text{ kJ} = \frac{42 \times 10^{6}}{3600} \text{ kWh})$$

$$E_{\text{total}} = 700 \times 10^{6} \times 11{,}630 = 8.141 \times 10^{12} \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Per Capita:**

$$\text{Per Capita} = \frac{8.141 \times 10^{12}}{1.4 \times 10^{9}} = 5815 \text{ kWh/person/year}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Per Capita Consumption} \approx 5815 \text{ kWh/year}}$$

This is far lower than the USA (~70,000 kWh/person/year) or the world average (~20,000 kWh/person/year), reflecting India's developmental energy gap.

---

### Example 3: Non-Commercial Energy Estimation

**Problem:** A rural village of 500 households uses 2 kg of firewood per household per day for cooking. CV of firewood = 3500 kcal/kg. Estimate the annual energy use in **GJ**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Daily firewood use:**

$$m_{\text{daily}} = 500 \times 2 = 1000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — Annual firewood use:**

$$m_{\text{annual}} = 1000 \times 365 = 3.65 \times 10^{5} \text{ kg/year}$$

**Step 3 — Annual energy in kcal:**

$$E = 3.65 \times 10^{5} \times 3500 = 1.2775 \times 10^{9} \text{ kcal/year}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to GJ:**

$$E = 1.2775 \times 10^{9} \times 4.186 \times 10^{-6} \text{ GJ}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 5348 \text{ GJ/year}}$$

This represents 127 TOE of **non-commercial, unaccounted energy** consumed by one village annually — energy that does not appear in any formal economic statistic.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Commercial Energy | Non-Commercial Energy |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Coal, oil, gas, grid electricity, nuclear | Firewood, dung, crop residue |
| Trade | Bought and sold | No market transaction |
| Units | kWh, TOE, BOE | kg, tonnes, m³ |
| Measurement | Metered | Estimated / Surveyed |
| Cost to user | Monetary price | Time + health cost |
| Environmental impact | Industrial emissions | Deforestation, indoor pollution |
| Policy focus | Tariff, efficiency, renewables | Substitution (LPG, biogas, solar cooker) |
| Share in India's rural household cooking | ~30% (rising) | ~70% (declining) |
| Energy density | High (coal ≈ 25 MJ/kg) | Low (firewood ≈ 14 MJ/kg, wet biomass lower) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Energy Classification Hierarchy

The following Mermaid block diagrams the complete taxonomy of energy sources as classified under KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Primary Energy Sources]:::root

    rootA --> commercialA[Commercial Energy]:::commercial
    rootA --> noncommercialA[Non Commercial Energy]:::noncommercial

    commercialA --> fossilA[Fossil Fuels]:::commercial
    commercialA --> electricalA[Electrical Energy]:::commercial
    commercialA --> nuclearA[Nuclear Energy]:::commercial
    commercialA --> renA[Grid Renewable Energy]:::commercial

    fossilA --> coalA[Coal and Lignite]
    fossilA --> oilA[Crude Oil and Petroleum Products]
    fossilA --> gasA[Natural Gas and CNG]

    electricalA --> thermalA[Thermal Power]
    electricalA --> hydroA[Hydro Power]
    electricalA --> solarA[Solar PV Grid]
    electricalA --> windA[Wind Power]

    renA --> bagasseA[Bagasse Cogeneration]
    renA --> wasteA[Urban Waste to Energy]

    noncommercialA --> woodA[Firewood and Charcoal]
    noncommercialA --> agriA[Agricultural Residues]
    noncommercialA --> dungA[Animal Dung Cakes]
    noncommercialA --> vegA[Vegetable and Organic Waste]

    commercialA -.Metered and Billed.-> usageA[Industrial, Commercial, Urban Household Use]
    noncommercialA -.Unmetered and Free.-> usageB[Rural Household Cooking and Heating]

    classDef root fill:#1f2937,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef commercial fill:#fef3c7,color:#78350f,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px
    classDef noncommercial fill:#dcfce7,color:#14532d,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — India's Energy Flow

This diagram illustrates how energy flows through India's economy from primary source to end-use, distinguishing commercial (metered) and non-commercial (unmetered) pathways.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sourceP[Primary Energy Source]:::src
    sourceP --> branchC[Commercial Pathway]:::comm
    sourceP --> branchN[Non Commercial Pathway]:::nonc

    branchC --> refineP[Refining and Processing]
    refineP --> gridP[Grid Distribution]
    gridP --> meterP[Metered Delivery]
    meterP --> billP[Energy Billing in kWh TOE]
    billP --> auditP[Energy Audit and Accounting]
    auditP --> policyP[Energy Policy and Tariff]

    branchN --> collectP[Household Collection]
    collectP --> burnP[Uncontrolled Combustion]
    burnP --> wasteP[Hidden Emissions Indoor Pollution Deforestation]
    wasteP --> surveyP[Estimated via Sample Surveys Only]

    policyP --> transitionP[Policy Push Ujjwala Biogas Solar Cooker]
    surveyP --> transitionP
    transitionP --> branchN

    classDef src fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0c1f5c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef comm fill:#fde68a,color:#78350f,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:2px
    classDef nonc fill:#bbf7d0,color:#14532d,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Energy Unit Conversion Reference Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    centralX[Central Energy Unit Joule SI Base]:::core

    centralX --> mechX[Mechanical Energy]
    centralX --> thermX[Thermal Energy]
    centralX --> elecX[Electrical Energy]

    mechX --> ftlbX[foot pound force]
    thermX --> calX[Calorie 4.186 J]
    thermX --> btuX[Btu 1055 J]
    elecX --> kwhX[kWh 3.6 MJ]
    elecX --> whX[Watt hour 3600 J]

    calX --> kcalX[kcal 4.186 kJ]
    btuX --> mmbtuX[MMBtu 10 to power 6 Btu]
    kwhX --> toeX[TOE 11630 kWh]
    kwhX --> boeX[BOE 1700 kWh]

    classDef core fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff,stroke:#3b0764,stroke-width:2px
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Model Question Paper Pattern (Reference)

As per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech regulations for an Elective/Professional Core course like **PESFT741**:
- **Part A:** Short-answer questions carrying 3 marks each (typically 5–6 questions per module, answer any 3).
- **Part B:** Long-answer questions carrying 14 marks each (typically with internal choice between two full questions; each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each).

---

## 5.2 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Differentiate between Commercial Energy and Non-Commercial Energy with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Commercial Energy** refers to energy forms that are bought and sold in the market at an established price, are metered, and form part of formal economic accounting.

**Non-Commercial Energy** refers to energy forms that are not traded in the market, are typically gathered by households (mostly rural), and do not appear in formal GDP or energy statistics.

| Aspect | Commercial Energy | Non-Commercial Energy |
|---|---|---|
| Transaction | Paid, metered | Free, unmetered |
| Examples | Electricity, LPG, Petrol, Coal | Firewood, cow-dung cakes, crop residue |
| Examples (alt) | Natural Gas, Diesel | Bagasse, agricultural waste |

Examples:
- Commercial: Grid electricity, LPG cylinder
- Non-Commercial: Firewood, cow-dung cake

*(Marks: 1 for definition each + 0.5 each for examples = 3 marks)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**State the SI unit of energy and the conversion factor between 1 kWh and MJ.**

**Model Answer:**

The **SI unit of energy** is the **Joule (J)**, defined as:

$$1 \text{ J} = 1 \text{ N} \cdot \text{m} = 1 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$$

Conversion:

$$1 \text{ kWh} = 1000 \text{ W} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 3.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ J} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$$

*(Marks: 1 for SI unit + 2 for derivation/conversion = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B1 — Choice A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**A. (a)** Explain in detail the classification of energy into Commercial and Non-Commercial categories. Discuss the role of each in the Indian energy scenario with relevant statistics. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Energy sources are broadly classified into two categories:

**1. Commercial Energy:**
Commercial energy is the energy that is available to consumers on payment of a price. It is metered, billed, and forms part of the formal economy. Major sources include:
- **Fossil Fuels:** Coal (used in 70% of India's power generation), Crude Oil (India imports ~85% of its crude requirement), Natural Gas.
- **Electrical Energy:** Generated from thermal, hydro, nuclear, and renewable sources and distributed through the grid.
- **Renewables (grid-tied):** Solar PV, wind, biomass power.

In India, commercial energy accounts for approximately **65–70% of total primary energy consumption**, and this share is rising due to urbanization, industrialization, and government programs like **Saubhagya** (electricity to all households) and **Ujjwala** (LPG to rural poor).

**2. Non-Commercial Energy:**
Non-commercial energy is the energy that is collected free of cost by households, primarily in rural areas. Major sources include:
- **Firewood** (largest component, ~60% of non-commercial energy)
- **Agricultural residues** (rice straw, wheat straw, bagasse)
- **Animal dung** (cow-dung cakes)
- **Charcoal** (in select rural industries)

In India, **~70% of rural households** still depend on non-commercial fuels for cooking, despite the rapid expansion of LPG distribution. This dependence causes deforestation (~5 million hectares of forest loss annually attributed to fuelwood collection) and indoor air pollution (causing ~4 million premature deaths globally per year).

**Conclusion:** A balanced energy strategy must promote commercial energy efficiency (industrial audits, renewables) **and** transition non-commercial users to cleaner commercial fuels (LPG, biogas, electric induction cooking).

*(Marks: 2 for classification, 2 for sources, 2 for Indian statistics, 1 for conclusion = 7 marks)*

---

**A. (b)** A household consumes 5 kg of firewood per day (CV = 3500 kcal/kg) and 50 kWh of electricity per month. Calculate the total monthly energy consumption in **MJ** and determine the **share of non-commercial energy** as a percentage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Daily non-commercial energy (firewood):**

$$E_{\text{firewood, daily}} = 5 \text{ kg} \times 3500 \text{ kcal/kg} = 17{,}500 \text{ kcal/day}$$

**Step 2 — Monthly non-commercial energy (30 days):**

$$E_{\text{firewood, monthly}} = 17{,}500 \times 30 = 5{,}25{,}000 \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to MJ using $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.186 \text{ kJ} = 4.186 \times 10^{-3} \text{ MJ}$:**

$$E_{\text{firewood, monthly}} = 5{,}25{,}000 \times 4.186 \times 10^{-3} = 2197.65 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 4 — Commercial energy (electricity):**

$$E_{\text{electricity}} = 50 \text{ kWh} \times 3.6 \text{ MJ/kWh} = 180 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 5 — Total energy:**

$$E_{\text{total}} = 2197.65 + 180 = 2377.65 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 6 — Non-commercial share:**

$$\text{Share} = \frac{2197.65}{2377.65} \times 100 = 92.43\%$$

**Result:**
$$\boxed{E_{\text{total}} \approx 2377.65 \text{ MJ}, \quad \text{Non-Commercial Share} \approx 92.43\%}$$

*(Marks: 1 for firewood daily, 1 for firewood monthly, 1 for conversion, 1 for electricity, 1 for total, 2 for share percentage = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B2 — Choice B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**B. (a)** With neat sketches/flowcharts, explain the various sources of Commercial and Non-Commercial Energy in India. Discuss the environmental implications of each category. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Commercial Energy Sources:**

1. **Coal:** India's largest commercial energy source (~45% of primary energy mix). Used in thermal power plants and industries. Environmental impact: CO₂ emissions (~820 g/kWh from coal-based power), fly ash, SO₂, NOₓ.

2. **Petroleum and Natural Gas:** Crude oil is refined into petrol, diesel, kerosene, LPG. Natural gas is used in fertilizers, power, and CNG vehicles. Environmental impact: vehicular emissions, oil spills, methane leakage.

3. **Electricity (grid):** Distributed mix of thermal, hydro, nuclear, and renewable. Environmental impact varies by generation source.

4. **Nuclear Energy:** Provides baseload power with low carbon footprint; challenges include radioactive waste management.

5. **Grid Renewables:** Solar, wind, small hydro — minimal operational emissions but require land and rare materials.

**Non-Commercial Energy Sources:**

1. **Firewood:** Collected from forests; causes deforestation, soil erosion, and habitat loss.
2. **Cow-Dung Cakes:** Burning emits particulate matter (PM2.5), CO, and methane; contributes to indoor air pollution.
3. **Agricultural Residues:** Open-field burning (especially in Punjab/Haryana) causes severe winter air pollution in Delhi-NCR.
4. **Vegetable Waste:** Decays anaerobically producing methane, a potent greenhouse gas.

**Environmental Comparison Summary:**

| Category | Carbon Emissions | Local Pollution | Land Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Commercial (fossil) | High (CO₂) | SO₂, NOₓ, PM | Mining footprint |
| Commercial (renewable) | Low (life-cycle) | Minimal | Solar/Wind farms |
| Non-Commercial | Moderate (black carbon) | High (indoor PM) | Deforestation |

*(Marks: 2 for commercial sources, 2 for non-commercial sources, 2 for environmental implications, 1 for conclusion = 7 marks)*

---

**B. (b)** Define the following terms with their conversion values:
- (i) Tonne of Oil Equivalent (TOE)
- (ii) Barrel of Oil Equivalent (BOE)
- (iii) British Thermal Unit (Btu)

A refinery processes 10,000 barrels of crude oil per day. Calculate the equivalent daily energy output in **GJ** and **kWh**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions:**

**(i) TOE:** The energy released by burning one metric tonne (1000 kg) of crude oil. Standard value:

$$1 \text{ TOE} = 41.868 \text{ GJ} \approx 42 \text{ GJ} = 10{,}000 \text{ Mcal}$$

**(ii) BOE:** The energy released by burning one barrel (~136 kg or 159 litres) of crude oil. Standard value:

$$1 \text{ BOE} \approx 5.8 \text{ MMBtu} \approx 1700 \text{ kWh} \approx 6.12 \text{ GJ}$$

**(iii) Btu:** The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of one pound of water by 1 °F.

$$1 \text{ Btu} = 1055 \text{ J} = 1.055 \text{ kJ}$$

**Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Daily energy in BOE:**

$$E_{\text{daily}} = 10{,}000 \text{ BOE}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to GJ (using $1 \text{ BOE} \approx 6.12 \text{ GJ}$):**

$$E_{\text{daily}} = 10{,}000 \times 6.12 = 61{,}200 \text{ GJ}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kWh (using $1 \text{ GJ} = \frac{10^{6}}{3600} \text{ kWh} = 277.78 \text{ kWh}$):**

$$E_{\text{daily}} = 61{,}200 \times 277.78 = 1.7 \times 10^{7} \text{ kWh}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{daily}} = 61{,}200 \text{ GJ} = 1.7 \times 10^{7} \text{ kWh}}$$

*(Marks: 1 each for three definitions = 3 marks; 2 for BOE-to-GJ conversion; 2 for GJ-to-kWh conversion = 7 marks total)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Unit consistency trap:** Students frequently mix kWh with MJ without using the **3.6 conversion factor**. Always write the conversion equation before substituting numbers.
> - **TOE rounding error:** Different textbooks use 42 GJ or 41.868 GJ. KTU accepts both, but **always show the working** to avoid losing method marks.
> - **"Calorific value" confusion:** Remember CV of **firewood (dry) ≈ 3500–4000 kcal/kg**, but **wet firewood ≈ 1500–2000 kcal/kg**. Read the problem statement carefully.
> - **Distinguish BOE vs TOE:** BOE is per **barrel**, TOE is per **tonne**. They are NOT the same number — never interchange.
> - **Indian statistics:** If you cite a number (e.g., "70% rural households use firewood"), it adds marks. Generic answers without data lose 1–2 marks in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Energy Scenario**

**Core Definitions:**
- ☐ Energy = capacity to do work (SI unit: Joule)
- ☐ Commercial Energy = metered, priced, billed energy
- ☐ Non-Commercial Energy = free, unmetered, biomass-based energy

**Key Examples:**
- ☐ Commercial: Coal, electricity, LPG, diesel, natural gas, nuclear, grid renewables
- ☐ Non-Commercial: Firewood, cow-dung cake, agricultural residue, charcoal

**Critical Conversions (Memorize!):**
- ☐ $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ} = 860 \text{ kcal}$
- ☐ $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.186 \text{ kJ}$
- ☐ $1 \text{ TOE} = 41.868 \text{ GJ} \approx 11{,}630 \text{ kWh}$
- ☐ $1 \text{ BOE} \approx 1700 \text{ kWh} \approx 6.12 \text{ GJ}$
- ☐ $1 \text{ Btu} = 1055 \text{ J} \approx 1.055 \text{ kJ}$

**Indian Context:**
- ☐ India imports ~85% of its crude oil and ~50% of natural gas
- ☐ Coal accounts for ~70% of power generation
- ☐ ~70% of rural households still use non-commercial cooking fuels
- ☐ Per capita commercial energy consumption ≈ 6000 kWh/year (vs world avg. ~20,000)

**Engineering Significance:**
- ☐ Non-commercial energy is **environmentally costly** (deforestation, indoor pollution)
- ☐ Commercial energy transitions require policy push (PMUY, Saubhagya)
- ☐ Energy auditors must convert all forms to a **common unit (TOE or GJ)** for benchmarking

**Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- ☐ Never confuse **calorie (food)** with **calorie (physics)** — context matters
- ☐ Always **show unit cancellation** step in derivations
- ☐ Use **significant figures** appropriate to the precision given in the problem
- ☐ **Cite Indian statistics** wherever possible to earn full marks
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Primary Energy Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Primary Energy Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Primary Energy Resource?

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):**  
> A **Primary Energy Resource** is a naturally occurring form of energy that has not been subjected to any human-engineered conversion or transformation process. It is the **raw, indigenous, or naturally available energy form** found in nature, which can be directly harvested, extracted, or tapped to produce useful secondary forms of energy (such as electricity, refined fuels, or heat).

In simple engineering terms, it is the **"first form"** of energy as it exists in the Earth's crust, atmosphere, oceans, or core — **before** we burn it, refine it, rotate it through a turbine, or split its atoms in a reactor.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Raw Apple vs. Apple Juice" Model

> [!NOTE]  
> **Analogy — Raw Apple vs. Apple Juice:**  
> Think of energy like an **apple**.  
> - A **primary energy resource** is the **whole, raw apple plucked directly from the tree** (coal, crude oil, sunlight, wind, uranium ore, falling water).  
> - A **secondary energy resource** is the **apple juice** we squeeze, pasteurize, and bottle (electricity from a coal plant, petrol from crude oil, hydrogen from electrolysis).  
> - We can only **drink the juice (use the energy)** because converting it is what makes it useful in our modern devices. The juice is more usable, but the apple is the **source**.  
>  
> Just as an apple must first exist before juice, primary energy must exist before any useful work or electricity can be produced.

---

## 1.3 Why Primary Energy Resources Matter to an Energy Manager

For a B.Tech Energy Management professional, understanding primary energy is **foundational** for three reasons:

1. **Resource Accounting** — Every nation tallies its *primary energy supply (PES)* in standardized units (Joules, Toe, TCE) to plan imports, security, and policy.
2. **Conversion Loss Awareness** — Recognizing that all primary-to-secondary conversions have inherent **second-law (exergy) losses** is the first step in energy auditing.
3. **Sustainability Mapping** — A clear primary energy inventory reveals the **renewable vs. non-renewable split**, which is central to the **Energy Conservation Act 2001** and subsequent KTU-evaluated case studies.

---

## 1.4 Standardized Energy Units Used in Primary Resource Accounting

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Bold Constants & Standard Units Used Internationally (and in KTU Numerical Problems):**
> - **1 kiloWatt-hour (kWh)** = **3.6 × 10⁶ J** = **3.6 MJ** ≈ **860.42 kcal**
> - **1 Tonne of Oil Equivalent (Toe)** = **41.868 GJ** = **41.868 × 10⁹ J**
> - **1 Tonne of Coal Equivalent (TCE)** = **29.288 GJ** (based on GCV of standard coal)
> - **1 BTU (British Thermal Unit)** = **1055.06 J**
> - **1 Quad** = **10¹⁵ BTU** = **1.055 × 10¹⁸ J**
> - **1 Exajoule (EJ)** = **10¹⁸ J**

These constants are **non-negotiable** in KTU numerical answer scripts — examiners award full marks only for correct unit conversion chains.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formal Classification of Primary Energy Resources

Primary energy resources are classified along **two major axes** as per KTU Module 1 syllabus:

### A. Based on Regeneration / Renewability

| # | Class | Definition | Engineering Examples |
|---|-------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Non-Renewable (Conventional / Stock Resources)** | Finite in quantity; formed over **geological timescales** (millions of years); depleted by human use faster than nature can replenish. | Coal, Lignite, Crude Oil (Petroleum), Natural Gas, Uranium-235, Thorium-232 |
| 2 | **Renewable (Non-Conventional / Flow Resources)** | Naturally replenished on a **human timescale**; flux-limited but not stock-limited. | Solar (direct + indirect), Wind, Hydel (small & large), Biomass, Geothermal, Tidal, Wave, Ocean Thermal, Hydrogen (as energy carrier) |

### B. Based on Continuous vs. Batch Availability

| # | Class | Definition |
|---|-------|------------|
| 1 | **Continuous** | Available 24×7 (e.g., geothermal, tidal — predictable; biomass — storable) |
| 2 | **Intermittent / Variable** | Available only at certain times (e.g., solar — diurnal & weather-dependent; wind — fluctuating) |

---

## 2.2 The Six Major Sub-Families of Primary Energy (Detailed Walkthrough)

### 2.2.1 Fossil Fuels (The Dominant Non-Renewable Family)

Formed from the anaerobic decomposition of buried organic matter (plants, algae, plankton) over **100–400 million years** under high pressure and temperature.

- **Coal** — Solid; classified by rank: **Peat → Lignite → Bituminous → Anthracite** (increasing carbon % and calorific value).
  - GCV range: **Lignite (~10 MJ/kg)** to **Anthracite (~33 MJ/kg)**.
- **Crude Oil (Petroleum)** — Liquid; a complex mixture of hydrocarbons (C₅–C₄₀). Average GCV ≈ **42–45 MJ/kg**.
- **Natural Gas** — Gaseous; primarily **methane (CH₄ ≈ 85–95%)**. GCV ≈ **38–55 MJ/m³** (or 50 MJ/kg).

### 2.2.2 Nuclear Energy (Uranium / Thorium Based)

Energy released from **fission** of heavy nuclei (²³⁵U, ²³⁹Pu) or **fusion** of light nuclei (D, T — not yet commercial).
- 1 kg of ²³⁵U ≈ energy equivalent of **~20,000 tonnes of coal** (≈ 2.5 × 10⁶ kWh thermal).
- India holds ~**25% of global thorium reserves** (Kerala's monazite sands in Chavara).

### 2.2.3 Solar Energy

The **primordial driver** of almost all energy on Earth (fossil fuels are stored ancient solar energy). Direct applications: PV, solar thermal, solar architecture.

### 2.2.4 Wind Energy

Kinetic energy of moving air masses driven by **solar-driven atmospheric pressure differentials**. A modern 3 MW turbine extracts ~**6 GWh/year**.

### 2.2.5 Hydel (Hydroelectric) Energy

**Gravitational potential energy** of water stored at elevation; released through turbines.

### 2.2.6 Bioenergy / Biomass

Energy stored in **recent biological matter** (wood, crop residue, biogas, biodiesel, municipal solid waste). Considered renewable as long as the regrowth rate ≥ consumption rate.

### 2.2.7 Geothermal, Tidal, Wave, OTEC

Marginal but high-baseload renewables; covered in detail in later KTU modules.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Primary Energy Conversions (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]  
> All formulas assume **GCV (Gross / Higher Calorific Value)** unless explicitly stated. KTU questions typically default to GCV for primary energy accounting.

| S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|-------|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Energy from fuel mass | $E = m \times \text{GCV}$ | MJ, if m in kg and GCV in MJ/kg | Most-used base formula |
| 2 | Energy from fuel volume (gas) | $E = V \times \text{GCV}_{\text{vol}}$ | MJ, if V in m³ and GCV in MJ/m³ | For natural gas accounting |
| 3 | kWh ↔ Joules | $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$ | J, kWh | Always written explicitly in answer scripts |
| 4 | Toe ↔ Joules | $1 \text{ Toe} = 41.868 \text{ GJ}$ | J, Toe | Used for national-level statistics |
| 5 | TCE ↔ Joules | $1 \text{ TCE} = 29.288 \text{ GJ}$ | J, TCE | Used in India's MoSPI energy statistics |
| 6 | NCV from GCV | $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 0.09 \times H \times 2442$ | kJ/kg | Where H = hydrogen mass fraction, 2442 = latent heat of vaporization (kJ/kg) at 25 °C |
| 7 | Energy content of Uranium | $E_{U} \approx 8 \times 10^{13} \text{ J/kg}$ | J/kg | Thermal energy from complete fission |
| 8 | Wind power available | $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ | W | ρ = air density, A = swept area, v = wind speed |
| 9 | Hydro power available | $P = \eta \rho g Q h$ | W | η = efficiency, ρ = 1000 kg/m³, Q = flow, h = head |
| 10 | Solar constant (AM 1.5) | $I_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$ | W/m² | Extraterrestrial irradiance |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Critical note for KTU scripts:** The **NCV–GCV** formula (Row 6) uses the empirical **0.09 factor (mass fraction of hydrogen lost as water vapor)** and the **2442 kJ/kg** latent heat of steam at 25 °C. This is a KTU-favourite derivation question.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **National Energy Planning (Govt. of India, NITI Aayog):** All primary energy is converted to **MToe (Million Tonnes of Oil Equivalent)** for unified reporting.
- **Emission Accounting:** Knowing the **GCV** of each fuel × its **carbon content %** lets a manager calculate the **CO₂ emission factor** (kg CO₂ per kg fuel), required for **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme compliance under the BEE.
- **Energy Auditing:** The auditor's first task is to draw the **primary-to-secondary energy Sankey diagram** of the plant.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Conversion Between Primary Energy Units (kWh → Toe → TCE)

This is the most frequently tested KTU numerical in Module 1. We must show **every step explicitly**.

**Given:**  
Energy $E = 5000 \text{ kWh}$ (electrical energy produced at a plant).  
**Find:** Equivalent energy in (a) MJ, (b) Toe, (c) TCE.

### Step 1 — Convert kWh to MJ (Joules)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E \text{ (in J)} &= E \text{ (in kWh)} \times 3.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/kWh} \\
E &= 5000 \times 3.6 \times 10^{6} \\
E &= 18{,}000 \times 10^{6} \text{ J} \\
E &= 1.8 \times 10^{10} \text{ J} \\
E &= 1.8 \times 10^{4} \text{ GJ} \\
E &= 18 \text{ GJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** We are simply scaling the unit. 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J is a **defined constant** (since 1 W = 1 J/s and 1 hour = 3600 s, then 1 kWh = 1000 × 3600 = 3,600,000 J). The student must show this chain to claim full marks.

### Step 2 — Convert GJ to Toe

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ Toe} &= 41.868 \text{ GJ} \\
\Rightarrow E \text{ (in Toe)} &= \frac{E \text{ (in GJ)}}{41.868} \\
E &= \frac{18}{41.868} \\
E &\approx 0.4299 \text{ Toe}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Toe is defined as the energy released by burning 1 tonne of crude oil (GCV ≈ 41.868 GJ/tonne). We perform a simple **dimensional division**.

### Step 3 — Convert GJ to TCE

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ TCE} &= 29.288 \text{ GJ} \\
\Rightarrow E \text{ (in TCE)} &= \frac{E \text{ (in GJ)}}{29.288} \\
E &= \frac{18}{29.288} \\
E &\approx 0.6147 \text{ TCE}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** TCE uses the GCV of **standard reference coal** (29.288 GJ/tonne), which is why 1 Toe > 1 TCE in calorific terms (oil is energy-denser than coal).

### Step 4 — Cross-Verification Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\text{TCE}}{\text{Toe}} &= \frac{41.868}{29.288} \\
&\approx 1.4296 \text{ TCE per Toe}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> This ratio (~1.43) is a **standard KTU short-answer data point**. Memorize it for rapid cross-checking in exams.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: NCV (Net Calorific Value) from GCV (Gross Calorific Value)

**Given:** A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis:
- Carbon (C) = 70%, Hydrogen (H) = 5%, Sulphur (S) = 1%, Nitrogen (N) = 1.5%, Oxygen (O) = 8%, Ash (A) = 10%, Moisture (M) = 4.5% (by mass).  
- GCV (from bomb calorimeter) = **30 MJ/kg**.

**Find:** NCV at constant pressure.

### Step 1 — State the Theoretical Basis

When hydrogen in fuel burns, it produces water:
$$
2 H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2 H_2O
$$
In a **bomb calorimeter**, this water condenses → releases **full latent heat** → hence **GCV**.
In an **industrial furnace** (flue gas exits hot), this water **stays as vapor** → **latent heat is lost** → hence **NCV**.

### Step 2 — Apply the Empirical KTU Formula

$$
\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 0.09 \times H \times (2442 \text{ kJ/kg})
$$

where **2442 kJ/kg** is the **latent heat of vaporization of water at 25 °C** (the standard reference temperature for calorific values in India, per IS:1350).

### Step 3 — Substitute Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NCV} &= 30{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg} - 0.09 \times 5 \times 2442 \\
&= 30{,}000 - 0.45 \times 2442 \\
&= 30{,}000 - 1098.9 \\
&= 28{,}901.1 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
&\approx 28.9 \text{ MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Physical Interpretation

The **1098.9 kJ/kg** lost is the **latent heat carried away by water vapor** in the flue gas. This represents **~3.66% of GCV** — a non-trivial efficiency loss in any boiler without **economizers / flue gas condensing heat recovery**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Primary Energy Factor (PEF) for Grid Electricity

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **The PEF is a KTU-favourite 14-mark question.** It is the ratio of primary energy consumed at the power plant to the electrical energy delivered at the consumer end.

**Given:** A coal-fired power plant has:
- Net plant thermal efficiency, $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = 38\%$
- Transmission & distribution (T&D) loss = 20%

**Find:** Primary Energy Factor (PEF) per kWh delivered.

### Step 1 — Energy Input to Plant per kWh Output

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Primary fuel input} &= \frac{1 \text{ kWh (electrical)}}{\eta_{\text{thermal}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.38} \\
&\approx 2.6316 \text{ kWh (thermal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Account for T&D Losses

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{To deliver 1 kWh to consumer, plant must generate} &= \frac{1}{1 - 0.20} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.80} \\
&= 1.25 \text{ kWh at plant busbar}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Combine for PEF

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PEF} &= \frac{1.25}{0.38} \\
&\approx 3.289 \text{ kWh (primary) per kWh (delivered)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Significance

> [!NOTE]  
> **India's grid PEF ≈ 2.5 to 3.5 kWh(primary)/kWh(electrical)** depending on the year and source mix. This factor is the **official multiplier** used in BEE star-labeling and ECBC building code calculations. A student who **does not write "primary energy input per delivered kWh"** loses 2 marks in a typical 14-mark KTU answer.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Primary Resources

| Parameter | Coal | Crude Oil | Natural Gas | Uranium | Solar (PV) | Wind | Hydro | Biomass |
|-----------|------|-----------|-------------|---------|------------|------|-------|---------|
| **State** | Solid | Liquid | Gas | Solid (ore) | Flux (radiation) | Flux (kinetic) | Flux (potential) | Solid/gas/liquid |
| **GCV (typical)** | 20–33 MJ/kg | 42–45 MJ/kg | 38–55 MJ/m³ | 8 × 10⁵ MJ/kg-equiv | 1.367 kW/m² (AM1.5) | 0.5–1.0 kW/m² (swept) | 1–5 kW/m² (reservoir) | 10–20 MJ/kg |
| **Renewable?** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ (fission) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (cyclic) |
| **CO₂ emission (kg/GJ)** | 95–105 | 70–80 | 50–60 | ~0 (direct) | 0 (operational) | 0 (operational) | 0 (operational) | 0 (net, if sustainable) |
| **Reserve base (India)** | ~360 Bt | ~770 Mt (refining) | ~1.4 Tcm | ~96,000 t U | Insolation: 4–7 kWh/m²/day | ~302 GW @ 100 m | ~145 GW (economic) | ~500 Mt/yr agri residue |
| **Conversion device** | Boiler | Engine/Refinery | Gas turbine | Reactor | PV cell | Turbine | Turbine | Boiler/digester |
| **Capacity factor** | 70–85% | — | — | 85–95% | 15–25% | 25–40% | 30–60% | 60–85% |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write "renewable = green = zero carbon" without distinguishing **operational** vs. **lifecycle** emissions. PV has ~**40–60 g CO₂-eq/kWh** lifecycle (manufacturing-driven). Wind is **10–20 g CO₂-eq/kWh**. The KTU 2024 scheme expects **scope-aware** answers.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Primary Energy Resources

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete classification tree** of primary energy resources, with subdivisions and representative examples. This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** designed to capture the engineering taxonomy demanded by the KTU Module 1 syllabus.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[PRIMARY ENERGY RESOURCES]
    rootA --> nodeB[CONVENTIONAL NON-RENEWABLE]
    rootA --> nodeC[NON-CONVENTIONAL RENEWABLE]
    rootA --> nodeD[NUCLEAR FISSION]

    nodeB --> nodeE[FOSSIL FUELS]
    nodeB --> nodeF[URANIUM ORE]

    nodeE --> nodeG[COAL]
    nodeE --> nodeH[CRUDE OIL]
    nodeE --> nodeI[NATURAL GAS]
    nodeE --> nodeJ[PEAT AND LIGNITE]

    nodeG --> nodeG1[ANTHRACITE HIGH GRADE]
    nodeG --> nodeG2[BITUMINOUS]
    nodeG --> nodeG3[SUB BITUMINOUS]
    nodeG --> nodeG4[LIGNITE LOW GRADE]

    nodeC --> nodeK[SOLAR DIRECT]
    nodeC --> nodeL[WIND KINETIC]
    nodeC --> nodeM[HYDRO POTENTIAL]
    nodeC --> nodeN[BIO MASS]
    nodeC --> nodeO[GEOTHERMAL]
    nodeC --> nodeP[TIDAL AND WAVE]
    nodeC --> nodeQ[OCEAN THERMAL OTEC]
    nodeC --> nodeR[HYDROGEN FUEL PRIMARY]

    nodeD --> nodeS[U 235 FISSION]
    nodeD --> nodeT[THORIUM 232 BREEDER]
    nodeD --> nodeU[PLUTONIUM 239 FAST]

    nodeN --> nodeN1[WOOD AND CROP RESIDUE]
    nodeN --> nodeN2[BIOGAS]
    nodeN --> nodeN3[ETHANOL BIODIESEL]
    nodeN --> nodeN4[MUNICIPAL SOLID WASTE]

    nodeK --> nodeK1[PHOTOVOLTAIC]
    nodeK --> nodeK2[CONCENTRATED SOLAR]
    nodeK --> nodeK3[SOLAR THERMAL]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reading the diagram:** The root splits into **three** primary families (Conventional, Renewable, Nuclear). Each branch then expands into sub-families with their standard KTU-recognized examples. This is the exact hierarchy expected in a Module 1 viva or 7-mark descriptive answer.

---

## 4.2 Primary-to-Secondary Energy Conversion Flow

The following block diagram illustrates the **functional flow** from a primary resource (raw form) to a secondary resource (useful form) to end-use service, which is the conceptual backbone of any energy audit or energy management plan.

```mermaid
graph LR
    pa1[PRIMARY RESOURCE COAL OIL GAS URANIUM SUN WIND WATER BIOMASS] --> pa2[CONVERSION DEVICE BOILER REFINERY REACTOR TURBINE PV DIGESTER]
    pa2 --> pa3[SECONDARY ENERGY ELECTRICITY REFINED FUEL HEAT HYDROGEN]
    pa3 --> pa4[END USE SERVICE LIGHTING MECHANICAL WORK SPACE HEAT TRANSPORT COOLING]
    pa4 --> pa5[USEFUL OUTPUT WITH LOSSES]
    pa5 -.-> pa6[WASTE HEAT EXHAUST RADIATION]
    pa2 -.-> pa6
    pa3 -.-> pa6
```

**Engineering Insight:**  
Every **arrow** represents a **conversion stage** governed by the **First & Second Law of Thermodynamics**. The **dashed arrows** represent **losses (irreversibilities)**. The KTU 2024 syllabus demands that an energy manager **identify, quantify, and minimize** these loss flows — which is the entire purpose of the course.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
> **Define the term "Primary Energy Resource". Distinguish it from a Secondary Energy Resource with two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Primary Energy Resource** is a naturally occurring form of energy found in nature, which has not been converted or processed by humans (e.g., coal, crude oil, natural gas, sunlight, wind, uranium, biomass).

A **Secondary Energy Resource** is the form of energy obtained after the conversion of a primary resource through a human-engineered process; it is the more refined, transportable, and directly usable form (e.g., electricity, refined petroleum products like petrol/diesel, hydrogen gas produced via electrolysis).

| Aspect | Primary | Secondary |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| Source | Natural | Derived/Processed |
| Examples | Coal, Sunlight, Uranium | Electricity, Petrol, Hydrogen |
| Direct usability | Often low | High |

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
> **List any four renewable primary energy resources and state one merit and one limitation of each.**

**Model Answer (4 × 0.75 ≈ 3 marks):**

| Resource | Merit | Limitation |
|----------|-------|------------|
| **Solar** | Abundant, zero operational emissions | Intermittent (day/night), low energy density (~1 kW/m²) |
| **Wind** | No fuel cost, scalable | Intermittent, noise & bird-bat mortality |
| **Hydro** | High efficiency (~90%), long life | Displaces communities, large land submergence |
| **Biomass** | Storable, dispatchable | Deforestation risk, lifecycle emissions if not sustainable |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**  
> In 14-mark primary energy questions, students commonly **lose 2–3 marks** by:  
> (a) Writing the **NCV formula** without specifying the **2442 kJ/kg latent heat at 25 °C** reference.  
> (b) Using **GCV** in industrial-furnace problems where **NCV** is required.  
> (c) **Forgetting the unit chain** in numerical conversions (kWh → MJ → Toe).  
> (d) Confusing **renewable** with **non-polluting** — they are not synonyms.  
> Always **box the final numerical answer with units** and **state assumptions explicitly**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Classify primary energy resources in detail, with suitable examples. Discuss the difference between **renewable and non-renewable** resources, citing their relative shares in India's primary energy mix (as per the latest Ministry of Power / MoSPI data). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The ultimate analysis of a coal sample is as follows (by mass %):  
C = 68%, H = 5%, S = 1%, N = 1.5%, O = 9%, Ash = 11%, Moisture = 4.5%.  
The **GCV** of the coal (determined in a bomb calorimeter) is **28.5 MJ/kg**.  
Calculate the **NCV** (Net Calorific Value) of the coal in kJ/kg. State the engineering significance of the difference. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks — full structured response]**

**Classification of Primary Energy Resources:**

Primary energy resources are classified into the following major categories:

1. **Conventional / Non-Renewable (Stock) Resources** — finite, exhaustible  
   a. **Fossil Fuels** — Coal, Crude Oil, Natural Gas, Peat, Lignite  
   b. **Nuclear Fuels** — Uranium-235, Thorium-232, Plutonium-239  

2. **Non-Conventional / Renewable (Flow) Resources** — naturally replenished  
   a. **Solar** — direct radiation, PV, concentrated solar power  
   b. **Wind** — kinetic energy of air  
   c. **Hydel** — potential energy of water  
   d. **Biomass** — wood, agricultural residue, biogas, biodiesel  
   e. **Geothermal** — heat from Earth's interior  
   f. **Tidal, Wave, OTEC** — ocean energy forms  

**India's Primary Energy Mix (Approximate, per MoSPI / IEA):**

| Source | Approximate Share |
|--------|-------------------|
| Coal | ~44% |
| Oil | ~24% |
| Natural Gas | ~6% |
| Biomass & Waste | ~10% |
| Renewables (Hydro, Solar, Wind, etc.) | ~13% |
| Nuclear | ~3% |

**Renewable vs. Non-Renewable — Core Distinction:**

| Aspect | Non-Renewable | Renewable |
|--------|---------------|-----------|
| Formation time | Millions of years | Continuous / short cycle |
| Stock vs. Flow | Stock (depletable) | Flow (replenishable) |
| Examples | Coal, Oil, Gas, Uranium | Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass |
| Carbon footprint (operational) | High | Low / zero |
| Long-term sustainability | Unsustainable | Sustainable |

**(b) [7 marks — Step-by-Step Numerical]**

**Given:** H (hydrogen mass fraction) = 5% = 0.05; GCV = 28.5 MJ/kg = 28,500 kJ/kg.

**Step 1 — Write the empirical NCV formula (must be stated):**

$$
\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 0.09 \times H \times 2442
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NCV} &= 28{,}500 - 0.09 \times 0.05 \times 2442 \\
&= 28{,}500 - 0.0045 \times 2442 \\
&= 28{,}500 - 10.989 \\
&= 28{,}489.01 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert and present the final answer:**

$$
\boxed{\text{NCV} \approx 28{,}489 \text{ kJ/kg} \approx 28.49 \text{ MJ/kg}}
$$

> **Note (correction for the solution above):** In standard KTU problems where H is given in **mass % directly (not as fraction)**, the formula is applied as:  
> $\text{NCV (kJ/kg)} = \text{GCV (kJ/kg)} - 9 \times H(\%)\times 2.442$ OR equivalently with the **0.09 × H × 2442** form where H is the **mass fraction**.  
>  
> **Re-computing correctly with H = 5% (mass fraction 0.05):**  

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NCV} &= 28{,}500 - 0.09 \times 5 \times 2442 \\
&= 28{,}500 - 0.45 \times 2442 \\
&= 28{,}500 - 1098.9 \\
&= 27{,}401.1 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
\boxed{\text{NCV} \approx 27{,}401 \text{ kJ/kg} \approx 27.4 \text{ MJ/kg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Significance of the GCV–NCV Difference:**

The GCV–NCV gap of **~1098.9 kJ/kg** represents the **latent heat of vaporization of water** produced from fuel-bound hydrogen — heat that is **lost with the hot flue gas** in an industrial furnace. In a **condensing economizer**, this latent heat can be partially recovered, improving boiler efficiency by **2–4%**. This is why the BEE's **PAT scheme** and **ECBC** code reference NCV (not GCV) for plant-level efficiency accounting.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** What are the **standard units** used for primary energy accounting? Convert **5000 kWh** of electrical energy into (i) MJ, (ii) Toe, and (iii) TCE. Show all conversion steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thermal power plant operates with a **net thermal efficiency of 35%** and the **grid T&D loss is 22%**. Calculate the **Primary Energy Factor (PEF)** per kWh delivered to the consumer. If the consumer is billed for **1000 kWh** in a month, how many **kWh of primary thermal fuel** has the plant actually consumed? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Standard Units (must list):** kWh, Joule (MJ/GJ), Toe, TCE, BTU, Quad.

**Given:** E = 5000 kWh

**(i) Conversion to MJ:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 5000 \times 3.6 \text{ MJ} \\
\boxed{E &= 18{,}000 \text{ MJ} = 18 \text{ GJ}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Conversion to Toe** (using 1 Toe = 41.868 GJ):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{18 \text{ GJ}}{41.868 \text{ GJ/Toe}} \\
\boxed{E &\approx 0.4299 \text{ Toe}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Conversion to TCE** (using 1 TCE = 29.288 GJ):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{18 \text{ GJ}}{29.288 \text{ GJ/TCE}} \\
\boxed{E &\approx 0.6147 \text{ TCE}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = 0.35$; T&D loss = 22% = 0.22.

**Step 1 — Plant generation per delivered kWh:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Plant generation} &= \frac{1 \text{ kWh (delivered)}}{1 - 0.22} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.78} \\
&\approx 1.2821 \text{ kWh (at plant busbar)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Primary fuel input per plant-generated kWh:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Primary fuel input} &= \frac{1.2821}{0.35} \\
&\approx 3.6631 \text{ kWh (thermal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PEF} \approx 3.66 \text{ kWh(primary)} / \text{kWh(delivered)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Primary fuel consumed for monthly 1000 kWh consumption:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total primary fuel} &= 1000 \times 3.6631 \\
\boxed{\approx 3663 \text{ kWh (thermal)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation:**  
The consumer's "1000 kWh" **actually represents ~3663 kWh of coal-equivalent thermal energy** burned at the plant. This factor (PEF) is the **BEE's official multiplier** for green-building and PAT-scheme carbon accounting.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Use this as your last-night revision sheet for KTU Module 1 — Primary Energy Resources:**

- ✅ **Primary energy** = naturally occurring, **unprocessed** form; **Secondary energy** = derived/converted form (e.g., electricity, petrol, hydrogen).
- ✅ **Six major families** of primary energy: **Fossil (coal/oil/gas), Nuclear (U/Th), Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass** (+ Geothermal/Tidal/Wave as minor).
- ✅ **Classification axes**: (i) **Renewable vs. Non-Renewable**; (ii) **Conventional vs. Non-Conventional**; (iii) **Continuous vs. Intermittent**.
- ✅ **Coal ranks (increasing GCV)**: Peat < Lignite < Sub-bituminous < Bituminous < Anthracite.
- ✅ **Natural gas** is predominantly **methane (CH₄ ~85–95%)**; GCV ≈ 50 MJ/kg.
- ✅ **Uranium-235** releases energy equivalent to **~20,000 tonnes of coal per kg** (fission).
- ✅ **NCV–GCV formula** (most-tested): $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 0.09 \times H \times 2442$, where 2442 kJ/kg = latent heat at 25 °C.
- ✅ **Unit constants to memorize verbatim**: 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ; 1 Toe = 41.868 GJ; 1 TCE = 29.288 GJ; 1 BTU = 1055.06 J.
- ✅ **Solar constant** (AM 1.5 extraterrestrial) = **1367 W/m²**.
- ✅ **Wind power equation**: $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ — note the **cubic dependence on v** (often tested as a 3-mark conceptual).
- ✅ **Hydro power equation**: $P = \eta \rho g Q h$.
- ✅ **Primary Energy Factor (PEF)** = (kWh primary fuel)/(kWh delivered to consumer) = $1/[\eta_{\text{thermal}} \times (1 - \text{T\&D loss})]$.
- ✅ **India's primary energy mix (memorize approximate %s)**: Coal ~44%, Oil ~24%, Gas ~6%, Renewables ~13%, Biomass ~10%, Nuclear ~3%.
- ✅ **PEF of Indian grid** ≈ 2.5 to 3.5 (used in BEE/ECBC calculations).
- ✅ **Renewable ≠ Zero-carbon (lifecycle)** — PV has ~50 g CO₂/kWh lifecycle; wind has ~15 g CO₂/kWh; nuclear ~12 g CO₂/kWh.
- ✅ **Always state the 2442 kJ/kg reference temperature (25 °C)** when using the NCV–GCV formula — examiners check this.
- ✅ **For numericals, always box the final answer with units** and **show the unit chain** (kWh → J → MJ → GJ → Toe/TCE).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commercial Energy Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Commercial Energy Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Commercial Energy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Commercial Energy** is defined as the form of energy that is **bought and sold** in the open market for a definite price (or tariff) and is available to the consumers on payment of a price. It is also referred to as **Secondary Energy** or **Market Energy**, and is distinct from *non-commercial energy* (such as firewood, animal dung, agricultural waste, etc.) which is collected freely and is not traded commercially.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PESFT741, **Commercial Energy Production** encompasses the engineered extraction, conversion, and distribution of energy from primary sources (fossil fuels, uranium, hydro reservoirs, solar irradiance, wind currents, biomass, etc.) into usable secondary carriers such as **electricity, refined petroleum fuels (diesel, petrol, kerosene, LPG), and thermal energy (steam, hot water)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (IEA Definition):** An energy source is classified as *commercial* if it has been **marketed, regulated, or monetized** through formal utility infrastructure, transmission grids, or licensed distribution networks.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of **commercial energy** as the difference between:

| Analogy Scenario | Non-Commercial | Commercial |
|---|---|---|
| **Drinking water** | Water drawn from a village well or river | Bottled water purchased from a store |
| **Transport** | Walking or using a bullock-cart for free | Riding a paid Ola/Uber taxi |
| **Lighting** | Burning a kerosene lamp from home-stored oil | Using LED bulbs powered by paid electricity |

In **all cases**, the *energy itself* is the same — but the *commercial version* has been **processed, packaged, metered, and delivered through a paying channel**.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip (2-Mark Question Pattern):** The single most-asked definition in KTU boards is: *"Differentiate between commercial and non-commercial energy."* Memorize the fact that **commercial energy = paid + grid/fuel-station available**, while **non-commercial = free + household-collected**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Energy Units & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following conversion factors and units are **permanently listed in KTU data handbooks** and are essential for Module 1 numerical problems.

- **1 kWh** $=$ **3.6 MJ** $=$ **3412 BTU** $=$ **859.85 kcal**
- **1 Toe** (Tonne of Oil Equivalent) $=$ **42 GJ** $=$ **11.63 MWh** $=$ **1.428 Toe per tonne of coal**
- **1 BTU** $=$ **1055.06 J**
- **1 Quad (10¹⁵ BTU)** $=$ **1.055 × 10¹⁸ J** $=$ **293.07 TWh**
- **Standard Coal Equivalent (SCE):** A coal with **GCV = 29.3 MJ/kg (7000 kcal/kg)** is the reference metric. All Indian energy statistics are normalized to SCE.
- **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** measured at constant volume with all water products in liquid form.
- **Net Calorific Value (NCV)** $=$ **GCV − 0.09 × H × 584** (where H = % hydrogen by mass in fuel, 584 = latent heat of vaporization of water in cal/g).

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic is a High-Yield KTU Module

> [!WARNING]
> **Module 1 — Commercial Energy Production — is the foundation of the entire PESFT741 course.** Every later module (Energy Audit, Energy Economics, Conservation Techniques) builds upon the *sources* and *conversion efficiencies* introduced here. Failing to master this module will cascade into poor performance in Modules 2, 3, and 4.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Commercial Energy Production

Commercial energy production is classified into two broad streams — **Conventional** and **Non-Conventional** (Renewable). The KTU syllabus demands mastery of both classifications, with a clear technical understanding of *how* each is produced.

### 2.1.1 Conventional (Non-Renewable) Commercial Energy Sources

Conventional commercial energy production relies on **finite, exhaustible** primary sources that have been geologically stored over millions of years.

**1. Coal-based Thermal Power Production**
- The process chain is: **Coal Mining → Pulverization → Combustion in Boiler → Steam Generation → Turbine Expansion → Generator → Step-up Transformer → Grid.**
- The dominant commercial pathway globally; in India, coal contributes ~50% of installed power capacity.
- The Rankine cycle is the thermodynamic backbone.

**2. Oil & Natural Gas Production**
- Crude oil is extracted via **onshore/offshore drilling**, transported via pipelines or tankers, and **refined** at distillation columns into petrol, diesel, kerosene, LPG, naphtha, etc.
- Natural gas is transported as **CNG** (compressed) or **LNG** (liquefied at −162 °C) for use in power plants, fertilizers, and city gas distribution.

**3. Nuclear Power Production**
- Uses **Uranium-235** or **Plutonium-239** as fuel in a controlled fission chain reaction.
- Heat is transferred via primary and secondary coolant loops (Pressurized Heavy Water Reactor — PHWR — is the Indian standard).
- 1 kg of U-235 yields energy equivalent to **~20,000 tonnes of coal**.

### 2.1.2 Non-Conventional (Renewable) Commercial Energy Sources

**1. Hydroelectric Power**
- Potential energy of stored water → kinetic → mechanical (turbine) → electrical (generator).
- Capacity factor: 30%–60% depending on run-of-river vs. reservoir type.

**2. Solar Photovoltaic (PV)**
- Direct conversion of sunlight into electricity via semiconductor p-n junction.
- Module efficiency: 18%–22% for crystalline silicon.

**3. Solar Thermal**
- Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) using parabolic troughs or heliostats.

**4. Wind Energy**
- Kinetic energy of moving air → mechanical torque → electricity.
- Power extracted: $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p$ (Betz limit: $C_p \leq 0.593$).

**5. Biomass / Biogas**
- Combustion, gasification, or anaerobic digestion of organic matter.

**6. Geothermal, Tidal, Wave, OTEC**
- Site-specific but commercially deployed in select geographies.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula a student must memorize** for Module 1 numericals. KTU valuation scripts assume direct substitution of these expressions.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Energy Output} = \eta \times \text{Energy Input}$ | First-law efficiency of any conversion | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p$ | Wind power extracted by a turbine | W (watts) |
| 3 | $C_p^{\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Betz coefficient (max theoretical) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $E_{\text{solar}} = A \times I \times \eta_{\text{PV}} \times t$ | Solar PV energy yield | kWh |
| 5 | $Q = m \times \text{GCV}$ | Heat released by fuel mass $m$ | kJ or kcal |
| 6 | $\text{Plant Load Factor (PLF)} = \frac{\text{Actual Energy Generated (kWh)}}{\text{Installed Capacity (kW)} \times \text{Hours in Period}}$ | Utilization index | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Capacity Factor} = \frac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Rated Capacity}}$ | Long-term utilization | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 0.09 \times H \times 584$ | Net vs. gross calorific value | kcal/kg |
| 9 | $\text{Dulong Formula: } \text{GCV} = 8080 C + 34500 \left( H - \frac{O}{8} \right) + 2240 S$ | GCV from ultimate analysis (kcal/kg) | kcal/kg |
| 10 | $\text{Heat Rate} = \frac{\text{Fuel Input} \times \text{GCV}}{\text{Power Output}}$ | Plant thermal efficiency inverse | kJ/kWh or kcal/kWh |
| 11 | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{3600}{\text{Heat Rate (kJ/kWh)}} \times 100$ | Boiler/plant efficiency | % |
| 12 | $\text{Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)} = \frac{\text{Fuel Input}}{\text{Power Output}}$ | kg/kWh | kg/kWh |
| 13 | $E_{\text{hydel}} = \rho g Q H \eta$ | Hydropower from head $H$, flow $Q$ | W |

> [!TIP]
> **Constants to memorize:** $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.225$ kg/m³ (at sea level), $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000$ kg/m³, $1\ \text{hp} = 746\ \text{W}$, $1\ \text{cal} = 4.186\ \text{J}$.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of These Formulas

- **PLF and Capacity Factor** are reported annually by CEA (Central Electricity Authority, India) for every power plant. They form the **basis of tariff petitions, PPA (Power Purchase Agreement) negotiations, and energy audit benchmarks.**
- **Heat Rate and SFC** are key **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** in thermal power stations; reducing them directly translates to fuel cost savings.
- **Dulong's formula** is used in coal-fired boiler audits to estimate GCV from ultimate analysis (%C, %H, %O, %S) when direct bomb-calorimeter measurement is unavailable.
- **Betz limit** explains why no wind turbine can extract more than **59.3%** of the kinetic energy in wind — a foundational concept in renewable energy design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Step Mandate:** Every algebraic transition in this section is shown explicitly. KTU valuation does **not** award partial marks for "the final answer alone" — each logical step (1 mark in 7-mark sub-questions) must be visible in the answer script.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship between GCV and NCV

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement
Show that $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 0.09 \times H \times 584$ for a hydrocarbon fuel where $H$ is the mass percentage of hydrogen and the latent heat of vaporization of water at 25 °C is **584 cal/g**.

### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1 — Define GCV and NCV conceptually.**
- **GCV (Gross / Higher Calorific Value):** Total heat released when 1 kg of fuel is completely combusted and the products are cooled to 25 °C with **all water vapor condensed to liquid**.
- **NCV (Net / Lower Calorific Value):** Total heat released when 1 kg of fuel is completely combusted and the products are cooled to 25 °C, but the **water vapor escapes as vapor** (i.e., latent heat is NOT recovered).

**Step 2 — Identify the source of the difference.**
The difference between GCV and NCV is the **latent heat carried away by the water vapor** formed during combustion.

**Step 3 — Compute the mass of water formed per kg of fuel.**
The combustion of hydrogen proceeds as:
$$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$$

This means 4 kg of hydrogen produces 36 kg of water.
Therefore, **1 kg of hydrogen produces 9 kg of water**.

If $H$ is the mass percentage of hydrogen in the fuel, then the mass of water produced per kg of fuel is:
$$m_{\text{water}} = 9 \times \frac{H}{100}\ \text{kg} = 0.09 H\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the latent heat lost.**
Latent heat of vaporization of water at 25 °C $= 584$ cal/g $= 584{,}000$ cal/kg $= 584$ kcal/kg.
$$\text{Latent heat lost} = 0.09 H \times 584\ \text{kcal/kg of fuel}$$

**Step 5 — Write the final relation.**
$$\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 0.09 H \times 584\ \text{kcal/kg}$$

$\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the factor 0.09?** Because $9 \times \frac{H}{100} = 0.09 H$. KTU examiners **specifically test** whether students remember this conversion.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Plant Thermal Efficiency from Heat Rate

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement
A thermal power plant consumes **800 tonnes of coal per day** with a GCV of **5000 kcal/kg** and generates **200 MWh** of electrical energy per day. Calculate (a) Heat Rate, (b) Plant Thermal Efficiency.

### 3.2.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**
- Coal consumption = 800 tonnes/day $= 800{,}000$ kg/day
- GCV of coal = 5000 kcal/kg
- Electrical output = 200 MWh/day $= 200{,}000$ kWh/day

**Step 1 — Compute total heat input per day.**
$$Q_{\text{in}} = m \times \text{GCV} = 800{,}000 \times 5000 = 4 \times 10^9\ \text{kcal/day}$$

**Step 2 — Convert heat input to kJ.**
Since $1\ \text{kcal} = 4.186\ \text{kJ}$:
$$Q_{\text{in}} = 4 \times 10^9 \times 4.186 = 1.6744 \times 10^{10}\ \text{kJ/day}$$

**Step 3 — Convert electrical output to kJ.**
$$E_{\text{out}} = 200{,}000\ \text{kWh} \times 3600\ \text{kJ/kWh} = 7.2 \times 10^8\ \text{kJ/day}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Heat Rate.**
$$\text{Heat Rate} = \frac{Q_{\text{in}}}{E_{\text{out}}\ \text{(kWh)}} = \frac{1.6744 \times 10^{10}}{200{,}000} = 83{,}720\ \text{kJ/kWh}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Plant Thermal Efficiency.**
$$\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{3600}{\text{Heat Rate (kJ/kWh)}} \times 100 = \frac{3600}{83{,}720} \times 100 \approx 4.30\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Forgetting to convert kcal → kJ before dividing. If students compute heat rate as $\frac{4 \times 10^9}{200{,}000} = 20{,}000\ \text{kcal/kWh}$ and then mistakenly apply $\eta = \frac{860}{\text{HR}}$, they get a wrong value. **Always unify units first.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Wind Power — Applying the Betz Limit

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement
A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of **80 m** and operates at a wind speed of **12 m/s**. The air density is **1.225 kg/m³**. Calculate (a) The maximum extractable power at Betz limit, (b) The mechanical power output if the turbine operates at $C_p = 0.40$.

### 3.3.2 Exhaustive Solution

**Given:**
- $D = 80$ m $\Rightarrow R = 40$ m
- $v = 12$ m/s
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Compute the swept area of the rotor.**
$$A = \pi R^2 = \pi (40)^2 = 1600\pi \approx 5026.55\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Write the wind power equation.**
$$P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p$$

**Step 3 — Compute $v^3$.**
$$v^3 = 12^3 = 1728\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}^3$$

**Step 4 — Compute maximum power at Betz limit ($C_p = 0.593$).**
$$P_{\max} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1728 \times 0.593$$
$$P_{\max} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1728 \times 0.593$$
$$\Rightarrow P_{\max} = 3{,}212{,}536\ \text{W} \approx 3.21\ \text{MW}$$

**Step 5 — Compute power at $C_p = 0.40$.**
$$P_{0.40} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1728 \times 0.40$$
$$\Rightarrow P_{0.40} = 2{,}167{,}000\ \text{W} \approx 2.17\ \text{MW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Betz limit insight:** Even at $C_p = 0.40$, the turbine is operating at 67.5% of its theoretical maximum. Real commercial turbines are designed around $C_p = 0.35$–$0.45$ for the best lift-to-drag trade-off.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Conventional vs. Non-Conventional

| Parameter | Conventional (Fossil) | Non-Conventional (Renewable) |
|---|---|---|
| **Renewability** | Finite, exhaustible | Inexhaustible, naturally replenished |
| **CO₂ Emissions** | High (coal $\approx 820$ g CO₂/kWh) | Negligible (wind/solar) to low (biomass) |
| **Plant Load Factor** | 60%–85% (thermal), 85% (nuclear) | 15%–25% (solar), 25%–40% (wind) |
| **Capital Cost (per kW)** | ₹40–60 million/MW (thermal) | ₹40–70 million/MW (solar PV); ₹60–100 million/MW (wind) |
| **Operating Cost** | High (fuel dominant) | Very low (no fuel cost) |
| **Land Footprint** | Moderate (ash ponds, cooling towers) | High (solar farms); low (wind per MW) |
| **Energy Payback Period** | Continuous extraction | Solar PV: 1–2 yrs; Wind: 6–12 months |
| **Storage Feasibility** | Inherent (coal piles, gas caverns) | Requires batteries / pumped hydro |
| **Start-up Time** | Hours (thermal); days (nuclear) | Seconds (inverter-based) |
| **Grid Stability Impact** | Synchronous inertia (stabilizing) | Inverter-based (needs synthetic inertia) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Coal-Based Thermal Power Plant (Rankine Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Coal Handling Plant] --> B[Pulverizer / Coal Mill]
    B --> C[Boiler Furnace - Combustion]
    C --> D[Steam Generator - High Pressure Steam]
    D --> E[Superheater - 535 C 16 MPa]
    E --> F[High Pressure Turbine HPT]
    F --> G[Reheater - Recycle to 535 C]
    G --> H[Intermediate Pressure Turbine IPT]
    H --> I[Low Pressure Turbine LPT]
    I --> J[Condenser - Cold Water Cooling]
    J --> K[Condensate Pump]
    K --> L[Deaerator - Removes Dissolved O2]
    L --> M[Feedwater Pump - High Pressure]
    M --> N[Economizer - Preheats by Flue Gas]
    N --> C
    F --> O[Electrical Generator]
    H --> O
    I --> O
    O --> P[Step-up Transformer]
    P --> Q[HV Transmission Grid]
    C --> R[ESP - Electrostatic Precipitator]
    R --> S[Stack - Flue Gas Exhaust]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **closed Rankine loop** is A → C → D → E → F → G → H → I → J → K → L → M → N → C. The **electrical extraction path** is F → O, H → O, I → O. The **flue-gas path** is C → R → S (pollution-control branch).

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Solar PV Power Generation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Solar Irradiance G - W per m2] --> B[PV Array - Multiple Modules]
    B --> C[DC Junction Box - Combiner]
    C --> D[MPPT Charge Controller]
    D --> E[DC to AC Inverter]
    E --> F[Step-up Transformer]
    F --> G[Grid Synchronization Unit]
    G --> H[LV or MV Distribution Network]
    B -.-> I[Battery Bank - Optional Storage]
    I -.-> D
    J[Net Meter] -.-> H
```

> [!TIP]
> **Optional storage branch** (dotted line): B → I → D. Without batteries, the system is **grid-tied and curtailable**; with batteries, the system becomes a **hybrid PV-plus-storage installation**.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wind Resource - Kinetic Energy 1 by 2 rho v cubed] --> B[Rotor Blades - Aerodynamic Lift]
    B --> C[Gearbox - Steps up RPM]
    C --> D[Generator - Doubly Fed Induction or PMSM]
    D --> E[Power Converter - AC to AC with VFD]
```
