```mermaid
    E --> F[Step-up Transformer]
    F --> G[Grid Interface - Protection Relays]
    H[Yaw Control System] -.-> B
    I[Pitch Control System] -.-> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Auxiliary control loops** (dotted): H (yaw control orients the nacelle into the wind) and I (pitch control regulates blade angle to prevent over-speed at high wind).

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Commercial Energy Sources

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Commercial Energy Sources] --> B[Conventional Non-Renewable]
    A --> C[Non-Conventional Renewable]
    B --> B1[Coal Thermal Power]
    B --> B2[Oil and Gas Fired Plants]
    B --> B3[Nuclear Fission U-235 Pu-239]
    C --> C1[Solar PV and CSP]
    C --> C2[Wind Onshore and Offshore]
    C --> C3[Hydroelectric Large and Small]
    C --> C4[Biomass and Biogas]
    C --> C5[Geothermal Tidal Wave OTEC]
    B1 --> D[Secondary Carriers: Electricity, Steam, Diesel, Petrol, LPG, Hydrogen]
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All seven pathways ultimately converge into the same secondary energy carriers** — electricity, refined fuels, or thermal steam. This is why KTU's Module 1 final question is often: *"Compare the secondary energy carrier efficiencies of any two commercial production chains."*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Part A = 3 marks each (short answer, ~50 words), Part B = 14 marks each (with sub-parts, ~5–7 lines per sub-answer). Internal choice in Part B is **mandatory** for every ESE module question.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define commercial energy. List four examples of commercial energy sources.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Commercial energy is the form of energy that is bought and sold in the market for a definite price and supplied to consumers through a regulated distribution network.
>
> **Examples (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
> 1. Electricity (from coal-based thermal, hydro, nuclear, solar, wind).
> 2. Refined petroleum fuels (petrol, diesel, kerosene, aviation turbine fuel).
> 3. Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) and Compressed Natural Gas (CNG).
> 4. Piped Natural Gas (PNG) and producer gas for industrial thermal use.

---

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State and explain Dulong's formula for the gross calorific value of coal.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Statement (1 mark):** Dulong's empirical formula for GCV (in kcal/kg) from ultimate analysis of coal is:
> $$\text{GCV} = 8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S$$
> where $C, H, O, S$ are mass percentages of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and sulphur, respectively.
>
> **Explanation (2 marks):**
> - The term $8080\,C$ represents heat from carbon combustion ($\approx 8080$ kcal per kg of C).
> - The term $34500\,(H - O/8)$ accounts for the heat released by net available hydrogen (subtracting oxygen-bound hydrogen, since 1 kg of O consumes $1/8$ kg of H).
> - The term $2240\,S$ represents heat from sulphur combustion.
> - The formula assumes complete combustion and gives results within $\pm 3\%$ of bomb-calorimeter measurements for most bituminous coals.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a coal-based thermal power plant. State the function of any four major components.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A thermal power plant uses **500 tonnes of coal per day** with GCV = **4500 kcal/kg**. The plant generates **150 MWh of electricity per day** with an auxiliary consumption of **8%**. Calculate (i) Heat supplied per day in kJ, (ii) Heat Rate in kJ/kWh, (iii) Net Plant Thermal Efficiency, and (iv) Specific Fuel Consumption.

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

> **Block diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram; students should sketch the Rankine cycle with labelled components.
>
> **Working (3 marks):** Coal from the **coal handling plant** is fed to the **pulverizer** where it is ground to a fine powder. The pulverized coal is blown into the **boiler furnace** where it burns in a stream of preheated air, releasing flue gases that heat the water tubes surrounding the furnace. The water converts to high-pressure, high-temperature steam (typically **16 MPa, 535 °C**). This steam expands through the **high-pressure, intermediate-pressure, and low-pressure turbines** in sequence, each stage extracting work. The exhaust steam enters the **condenser** where it is cooled by circulating water from a cooling tower, condensing back to liquid water. The condensate is pumped through a **deaerator** (to remove dissolved oxygen that would otherwise corrode the boiler tubes) and then pressurized by a **feedwater pump**. An **economizer** (in the flue gas path) preheats the feedwater using waste heat from the exhaust gases, improving overall efficiency. A **superheater** raises the steam temperature above the saturation point before it enters the turbine. The turbine shaft is coupled to a **synchronous generator** that produces electrical energy, which is stepped up via a transformer and fed into the grid.
>
> **Function of four components (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Pulverizer:** Reduces coal to $< 75$ micron size for rapid and complete combustion.
> 2. **Economizer:** Recovers waste heat from flue gas to preheat feedwater, raising boiler efficiency by 5%–8%.
> 3. **Condenser:** Condenses exhaust steam at low pressure to create a vacuum that draws more steam through the turbine, increasing work output.
> 4. **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP):** Captures $> 99\%$ of fly-ash particulates from flue gas to comply with emission norms.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Coal = 500 tonnes/day $= 5 \times 10^5$ kg/day
- GCV = 4500 kcal/kg
- Gross generation = 150 MWh/day $= 1.5 \times 10^5$ kWh/day
- Auxiliary consumption = 8%

**Step 1 — Compute Heat Input (i).**
$$Q_{\text{in}} = 5 \times 10^5 \times 4500 = 2.25 \times 10^9\ \text{kcal/day}$$
Converting to kJ (1 kcal = 4.186 kJ):
$$Q_{\text{in}} = 2.25 \times 10^9 \times 4.186 = 9.4185 \times 10^9\ \text{kJ/day}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Net Output after auxiliary deduction.**
$$E_{\text{net}} = 1.5 \times 10^5 \times (1 - 0.08) = 1.38 \times 10^5\ \text{kWh/day}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Heat Rate (ii).**
$$\text{Heat Rate} = \frac{Q_{\text{in}}}{E_{\text{net}}} = \frac{9.4185 \times 10^9}{1.38 \times 10^5} = 68{,}250\ \text{kJ/kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Net Plant Thermal Efficiency (iii).**
$$\eta_{\text{net}} = \frac{3600}{68{,}250} \times 100 = 5.27\%$$

**Step 5 — Compute Specific Fuel Consumption (iv).**
$$\text{SFC} = \frac{5 \times 10^5\ \text{kg/day}}{1.38 \times 10^5\ \text{kWh/day}} = 3.62\ \text{kg/kWh}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Always subtract auxiliary consumption** before computing net efficiency — failing to do so loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Always convert kcal to kJ** before dividing by kWh — inconsistent units is the #1 reason for zero marks in numericals.
> 3. **SFC must be expressed in kg/kWh**, not kg/MWh (common unit-confusion error).
> 4. Show all 5 steps — each step is worth ~1.4 marks.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1] — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of a wind energy conversion system (WECS) with a block diagram. Derive the expression for power extracted by a wind turbine and state the Betz limit.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wind farm has **20 turbines**, each with a rotor diameter of **60 m**, operating at an average wind speed of **10 m/s** and an average power coefficient of **0.35**. Air density is **1.20 kg/m³**. Calculate (i) Total swept area of the farm, (ii) Total power generated in MW, (iii) Annual energy generation in MWh assuming a **30% capacity factor**, and (iv) The number of households (each consuming **200 kWh/month**) that can be served by this farm.

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

> **Block diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid diagram.
>
> **Working (2 marks):** Kinetic energy of moving air is intercepted by the rotor blades, which use aerodynamic lift (similar to aircraft wings) to convert it into rotational mechanical energy. The low-speed shaft (typically 15–20 RPM) is connected via a **gearbox** that steps up the speed to ~1500 RPM (suitable for the generator). The **generator** (DFIG or PMSM type) converts mechanical energy to electrical energy. A **power electronic converter** conditions the variable-frequency output to grid-frequency AC. The **yaw system** keeps the nacelle pointed into the wind, and the **pitch system** regulates blade angle to prevent overspeed.
>
> **Derivation of wind power (2.5 marks):** The kinetic energy flux through a swept area $A$ at wind speed $v$ is:
> $$\dot{E} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} v^2 = \frac{1}{2} (\rho A v) v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$$
> Only a fraction $C_p$ (power coefficient) can be extracted due to the **continuity condition** (air must keep flowing). Therefore:
> $$P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p$$
>
> **Betz limit (0.5 marks):** The maximum theoretical value of $C_p$ is $C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$, derived by Albert Betz in 1919. No turbine can exceed 59.3% of the available wind power.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Number of turbines $N = 20$
- $D = 60$ m $\Rightarrow R = 30$ m
- $v = 10$ m/s
- $C_p = 0.35$
- $\rho = 1.20$ kg/m³
- Capacity factor = 30%
- Household consumption = 200 kWh/month

**Step 1 — Compute swept area per turbine.**
$$A_1 = \pi R^2 = \pi (30)^2 = 900\pi \approx 2827.43\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute total swept area of farm (i).**
$$A_{\text{total}} = 20 \times 2827.43 = 56{,}548.67\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute power per turbine.**
$$P_1 = 0.5 \times 1.20 \times 2827.43 \times (10)^3 \times 0.35$$
$$P_1 = 0.5 \times 1.20 \times 2827.43 \times 1000 \times 0.35 = 593{,}760\ \text{W} \approx 0.594\ \text{MW}$$

**Step 4 — Compute total power of farm (ii).**
$$P_{\text{farm}} = 20 \times 0.594 = 11.88\ \text{MW}$$

**Step 5 — Compute annual energy at 30% capacity factor (iii).**
$$E_{\text{annual}} = 11.88 \times 8760 \times 0.30 = 31{,}217\ \text{MWh/yr}$$

**Step 6 — Compute households served (iv).**
Per household annual consumption $= 200 \times 12 = 2400$ kWh/yr $= 2.4$ MWh/yr.
$$\text{Households} = \frac{31{,}217}{2.4} \approx 13{,}007\ \text{households}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Pitfalls for Wind Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by $N$** (number of turbines) — losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using $C_p = 0.593$ (Betz) instead of the given $C_p = 0.35$** — read the problem carefully.
> 3. **8760 hours/year**, not 365 × 24 (same value, but write the multiplication explicitly).
> 4. **Capacity factor is dimensionless** — students often confuse it with efficiency and apply it incorrectly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist — must be memorized before any Module 1 examination.**

- **Commercial Energy** = energy that is **bought, sold, metered, and distributed** through formal channels (electricity, refined fuels, LPG, CNG, piped gas).
- **Non-commercial energy** = freely collected (firewood, cow-dung, agricultural residue, solar drying, wind pumping).
- **Coal is the dominant commercial primary fuel in India** (~50% of installed capacity), followed by renewables (~31%), gas, nuclear, and hydro.
- **Three pillars of commercial energy policy:** (1) Energy security, (2) Energy equity (universal access), (3) Environmental sustainability.
- **The four major coal-based thermal plant components** every student must draw and label: Boiler, Turbine, Condenser, Generator — plus auxiliaries (Economizer, Superheater, ESP, Cooling tower).
- **Rankine cycle** is the thermodynamic foundation of all steam-based power plants.
- **GCV vs. NCV:** $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 0.09 H \times 584$ kcal/kg. NCV is always **lower** than GCV.
- **Dulong's formula:** $\text{GCV} = 8080 C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240 S$ kcal/kg.
- **Betz limit** $= 16/27 = 0.593$ — no wind turbine can extract more than 59.3% of incident wind kinetic energy.
- **Wind power** $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p$ — power scales with the **cube** of wind speed, doubling wind speed increases power eightfold.
- **Heat rate** is the inverse of thermal efficiency × 3600 — a modern supercritical plant achieves 7000–8000 kJ/kWh; subcritical plants are 9000–11000 kJ/kWh.
- **Capacity factor** in India: Solar PV ~ 15%–25%, Wind ~ 25%–40%, Coal thermal ~ 60%–80%, Nuclear ~ 85%–90%.
- **Standard units to memorize:** $1$ Toe $= 42$ GJ; $1$ kWh $= 3.6$ MJ; $1$ BTU $= 1055$ J; **SCE reference** = coal with GCV 29.3 MJ/kg.
- **India's installed power capacity (as of 2024):** ~430 GW total, with renewables crossing 180 GW — the fastest-growing commercial energy sector.
- **Environmental impact of fossil fuels:** Coal $\approx 820$ g CO₂/kWh; Diesel $\approx 720$ g CO₂/kWh; Natural Gas $\approx 490$ g CO₂/kWh; Solar/Wind $\approx 40$–50 g CO₂/kWh lifecycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Wisdom:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1 is typically worth **15 marks** in the End Semester Exam (one 14-mark question with internal choice, plus 1 mark from a Part A short-answer). Mastering this module sets the stage for the rest of PESFT741.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Final Energy Consumption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Final Energy Consumption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Final Energy Consumption (FEC)** is the total quantity of energy, measured in **tonnes of oil equivalent (toe)**, **kilojoules (kJ)**, **kilowatt-hours (kWh)**, or **British Thermal Units (BTU)**, that is delivered to the end-user for all energy purposes, encompassing **industrial**, **transportation**, **residential**, **commercial / institutional**, **agricultural**, and **non-energy** uses. It represents the energy content of fuels and electricity actually consumed at the point of final use, *after* deducting the energy losses incurred during the transformation of **primary energy** (such as coal, crude oil, natural gas, uranium, biomass, hydro, wind, and solar) into usable energy carriers (such as gasoline, diesel, LPG, electricity, and refined fuels).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Final Energy Consumption is the **intermediate stage** in the energy flow chain:
> **Primary Energy → Final Energy → Useful Energy → Energy Services.**
> It is the variable most often reported in national energy balances published by agencies such as the **International Energy Agency (IEA)**, **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) – India**, and the **Ministry of Power, Government of India**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine a large dam (the **primary energy reservoir**) holding millions of litres of raw water.

- The water travels through long canals, tunnels, and pipelines, losing a significant volume through **seepage and evaporation** (these are the *conversion and transmission losses*).
- The water that finally reaches your kitchen tap is the **Final Energy** — clean, measurable, billed, and ready for use.
- When you fill a bucket to wash clothes, only a portion of that water actually does the cleaning; the rest splashes away. This is the gap between *Final Energy* and *Useful Energy*.
- The actual washing of clothes is the **Energy Service**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy matters for engineers:** Final Energy Consumption is the **billing meter** equivalent in the energy economy. It is what industries, transport operators, and households *actually pay for*, making it the most actionable variable for any **Energy Manager** or **Energy Auditor** working under the framework of the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001** of India.

## 1.3 Distinction Between Primary, Final, and Useful Energy

| Stage | Definition | Typical Examples | Loss Type Before This Stage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Energy** | Energy obtained directly from nature | Coal, crude oil, natural gas, uranium, hydro, solar, wind, biomass | None (source) |
| **Final Energy** | Energy delivered to the end-user after conversion & distribution | Electricity, gasoline, diesel, LPG, kerosene, natural gas (piped), charcoal | Conversion + T&D losses |
| **Useful Energy** | Energy that actually performs the desired work | Heat in a furnace, mechanical work in a motor shaft, light from a bulb, cooling from an AC | End-use conversion losses |
| **Energy Services** | The actual benefit desired | Cooking food, transporting goods, lighting a room, cooling a building | Application losses |

## 1.4 Standard Conversion Constants (Memorise for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are the **standard calorific conversion factors** universally accepted in energy statistics:
>
> - **1 toe = 41.868 GJ = 11.630 MWh = 41.868 × 10⁹ J**
> - **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 860 kcal**
> - **1 BTU = 1055.06 J = 0.252 kcal**
> - **1 kcal = 4.1868 kJ = 3.968 BTU**
> - **1 Joule = 1 N·m = 1 W·s**

## 1.5 Physical Constants

- **Speed of light (c) = 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **Avogadro's number (Nₐ) = 6.022 × 10²³ /mol**
- **Universal gas constant (R) = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**

These constants are relevant when converting chemical energy of fuels (e.g., calorific value per mole) into standard energy units used in FEC accounting.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Energy Cascade — Step-by-Step Logic

The journey from a coal seam or a solar panel to the energy bill you pay is governed by a **cascading efficiency chain**. Each stage multiplies with the next, producing a low overall efficiency.

**Step 1 — Primary Energy Extraction (η₁)**
Raw energy is extracted. For fossil fuels, this involves mining or drilling, with on-site losses. For renewables, this is the gross resource captured.

**Step 2 — Energy Conversion (η_conv)**
Primary energy is transformed into a usable energy carrier.
- *Coal-fired power plant:* Chemical energy → Thermal energy → Mechanical energy → Electrical energy.
- *Petroleum refinery:* Crude oil → Gasoline / diesel / kerosene (fractional distillation, cracking, reforming).
- Typical **η_conv for thermal power plant = 33 % – 42 %**.

**Step 3 — Transmission and Distribution (η_T&D)**
The converted energy is transported to the end-user.
- For electricity, **η_T&D ≈ 0.80 – 0.90** in India (revised to higher with the Saubhagya and UDAY schemes).
- For fuels, transportation losses (evaporation, pipeline leaks) typically account for **1 – 3 %**.

**Step 4 — End-Use Conversion (η_end)**
The final carrier is converted into the actual desired form.
- Electric motor: **η ≈ 0.85 – 0.95** (premium IE3/IE4 motors up to **96 %**).
- Domestic LPG stove: **η ≈ 0.55 – 0.65**.
- Fluorescent lamp: **η ≈ 0.15 – 0.20**; LED: **η ≈ 0.40 – 0.50**.

**Step 5 — Useful Energy Output**
This is what finally performs the desired task.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these seven equations — they cover > 90 % of numerical questions in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Real-world Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Final Energy Balance | $E_{FE} = E_{PE} - L_{conv} - L_{T\&D}$ | $E_{PE}$ = Primary energy, $L_{conv}$ = conversion loss, $L_{T\&D}$ = T&D loss (all in **toe** or **MJ**) | National energy accounting |
| 2 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{conv} \times \eta_{T\&D} \times \eta_{end}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Comparing technologies |
| 3 | Useful Energy | $E_{useful} = E_{FE} \times \eta_{end}$ | Same units as $E_{FE}$ | End-use efficiency studies |
| 4 | Energy Intensity (Sector) | $EI = \dfrac{FEC_{sector}}{GDP_{sector}}$ | **toe / crore ₹** or **kWh / $** | Cross-sector benchmarking |
| 5 | Energy Intensity (Per Capita) | $EI_{pc} = \dfrac{FEC_{national}}{Population}$ | **toe / person** | International comparison |
| 6 | Sector-wise FEC Total | $FEC_{total} = FEC_{ind} + FEC_{trans} + FEC_{res} + FEC_{com} + FEC_{agr} + FEC_{non\text{-}energy}$ | **toe / year** | National energy planning |
| 7 | Kaya Identity Component | $CO_2 = Pop \times \dfrac{GDP}{Pop} \times \dfrac{FEC}{GDP} \times \dfrac{CO_2}{FEC}$ | tonnes CO₂ | Decarbonisation analysis |
| 8 | Conversion to toe | $E_{toe} = \dfrac{E_{MJ}}{41.868}$ | **toe** | Standardising mixed-fuel data |
| 9 | Conversion to kWh | $E_{kWh} = \dfrac{E_{MJ}}{3.6}$ | **kWh** | Power-sector reporting |
| 10 | Percentage Loss | $\%L = \left(\dfrac{L_{conv} + L_{T\&D}}{E_{PE}}\right)\times 100$ | **%** | Quick audit calculations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol convention used:** In all KTU official solutions, $\eta$ is rendered as "eta", $\Sigma$ as "Sigma", and subscripts are written without italics. Always show the **unit** in your final answer for full marks.

## 2.3 Sector-Wise Composition of Final Energy Consumption (India — Indicative Pattern)

| Sector | Approx. Share of FEC (India) | Dominant Carriers | Key Energy Services |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Industrial** | **40 – 45 %** | Coal, electricity, natural gas, diesel | Process heat, motive power, boilers |
| **Transport** | **18 – 22 %** | Diesel, petrol, aviation turbine fuel, electricity (EVs) | Freight movement, passenger mobility |
| **Residential** | **12 – 16 %** | LPG, electricity, firewood/dung (traditional), kerosene | Cooking, lighting, space cooling |
| **Commercial / Institutional** | **8 – 10 %** | Electricity, diesel (DG sets), natural gas | Lighting, HVAC, IT loads |
| **Agriculture** | **5 – 8 %** | Diesel, electricity, biomass | Pumping, tractors, post-harvest processing |
| **Non-energy use** | **6 – 8 %** | Naphtha, lubricants, bitumen | Feedstock for petrochemicals, lubricants |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **BEE-certified Energy Audit** (under the **PAT – Perform Achieve and Trade** scheme of the Government of India), the auditor begins with the **FEC data** of the plant. This figure is then disaggregated into:

1. **Fuel-based FEC** (coal, furnace oil, LSHS, natural gas)
2. **Electricity-based FEC** (kWh consumption)
3. **Non-conventional FEC** (biomass, solar, waste heat)

Only after this disaggregation can **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** — for example, **kWh per tonne of cement** or **GJ per tonne of steel** — be calculated and benchmarked against best-practice standards.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Energy Service" hierarchy in any audit report is:**
> **FEC → SEC → M&V (Measurement & Verification) → ECM (Energy Conservation Measure) → ECM payback.**
> This is the KTU-mandated audit methodology.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Overall Efficiency From Primary to Useful Energy

We begin with the **definition of conversion efficiency** at each stage.

**Stage 1 — Conversion of Primary to Final Energy**

The efficiency of energy conversion is the ratio of the energy content of the final carrier to the energy content of the primary input:

$$\eta_{conv} = \frac{E_{FE}}{E_{PE}}$$

Rearranging for Final Energy:

$$E_{FE} = \eta_{conv} \times E_{PE}$$

**Stage 2 — Transmission and Distribution to the End-User**

A fraction of $E_{FE}$ is lost as heat in transmission lines, pipeline leakage, and evaporation. The efficiency is:

$$\eta_{T\&D} = \frac{E_{delivered}}{E_{FE}}$$

Therefore, the energy actually reaching the consumer is:

$$E_{delivered} = \eta_{T\&D} \times E_{FE} = \eta_{T\&D} \times \eta_{conv} \times E_{PE}$$

**Stage 3 — End-Use Conversion at the Consumer**

At the consumer's premises, the final carrier is converted into useful work (heat, light, motion). The end-use efficiency is:

$$\eta_{end} = \frac{E_{useful}}{E_{delivered}}$$

Hence, the useful energy is:

$$E_{useful} = \eta_{end} \times E_{delivered}$$

**Final — Substituting All Stages**

$$E_{useful} = \eta_{end} \times \eta_{T\&D} \times \eta_{conv} \times E_{PE}$$

Defining the **overall efficiency** $\eta_{overall}$:

$$\boxed{\eta_{overall} = \eta_{conv} \times \eta_{T\&D} \times \eta_{end}}$$

This multiplicative form is why even small drops in each stage cause a disproportionately large fall in $\eta_{overall}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical implication for a coal-based electricity chain in India:**
> $\eta_{overall} = 0.38 \times 0.85 \times 0.70 = 0.226$.
> This means only **~ 22.6 %** of the original chemical energy in coal becomes useful work at the consumer's end. The remaining **77.4 %** is lost across the chain.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Final Energy Consumption from Calorific Value and Mass

When energy data is given as **mass of fuel** and its **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)**, the FEC is computed as:

$$FEC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \times GCV_i$$

where:
- $m_i$ = mass (in **kg** or **tonnes**) of the $i^{th}$ fuel
- $GCV_i$ = gross calorific value (in **kJ/kg** or **MJ/kg**)
- $n$ = number of distinct fuel types

**Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style 14-mark question pattern):**

*Question:* A manufacturing plant consumes the following in one month: **120 tonnes of coal** (GCV = 25 MJ/kg), **15,000 litres of furnace oil** (density 0.95 kg/L, GCV = 42 MJ/kg), and **80,000 kWh of electricity**. Calculate (a) the total FEC in GJ, and (b) the FEC in **toe**.

**Step 1 — Coal energy**

$$E_{coal} = 120{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 25 \text{ MJ/kg} = 3{,}000{,}000 \text{ MJ} = 3000 \text{ GJ}$$

The 120 tonnes is converted to kg by multiplying by 1000.

**Step 2 — Furnace oil energy**

First, compute the mass: $m = 15{,}000 \text{ L} \times 0.95 \text{ kg/L} = 14{,}250 \text{ kg}$

Then, $E_{FO} = 14{,}250 \text{ kg} \times 42 \text{ MJ/kg} = 598{,}500 \text{ MJ} = 598.5 \text{ GJ}$

**Step 3 — Electricity energy**

Electricity is already in kWh; convert to MJ: $E_{elec} = 80{,}000 \text{ kWh} \times 3.6 \text{ MJ/kWh} = 288{,}000 \text{ MJ} = 288 \text{ GJ}$

**Step 4 — Total FEC in GJ**

$$FEC_{total} = 3000 + 598.5 + 288 = 3886.5 \text{ GJ}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to toe**

$$FEC_{toe} = \frac{3886.5 \text{ GJ}}{41.868 \text{ GJ/toe}} = 92.84 \text{ toe}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on kWh ↔ MJ conversion:**
> $1 \text{ kWh} = 1000 \text{ W} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 3.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ J} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$
> This is a **derived unit**, not an experimental constant.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Intensity and Decoupling

**Per-capita FEC** is a standard development indicator:

$$EI_{pc} = \frac{FEC_{national}}{P}$$

where $P$ = population.

**Energy-GDP elasticity (e)** is defined as the ratio of percentage change in FEC to percentage change in GDP:

$$e = \frac{\%\Delta FEC}{\%\Delta GDP}$$

If $e < 1$ → **decoupling achieved** (economy grows faster than energy use).
If $e > 1$ → **rebound effect** or **inefficient growth**.
If $e = 1$ → **proportional growth**.

**Derivation from the Kaya Identity:**

$$CO_2 = P \times \frac{GDP}{P} \times \frac{FEC}{GDP} \times \frac{CO_2}{FEC}$$

Taking logarithms and differentiating with respect to time $t$:

$$\frac{1}{CO_2}\frac{dCO_2}{dt} = \frac{1}{P}\frac{dP}{dt} + \frac{1}{(GDP/P)}\frac{d(GDP/P)}{dt} + \frac{1}{(FEC/GDP)}\frac{d(FEC/GDP)}{dt} + \frac{1}{(CO_2/FEC)}\frac{d(CO_2/FEC)}{dt}$$

This decomposes emissions growth into **population**, **affluence (per-capita GDP)**, **energy intensity (FEC/GDP)**, and **carbon intensity (CO₂/FEC)**. For any energy policy, the **FEC/GDP** term is the most actionable — directly linked to energy efficiency.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Common Sources of Error in KTU Solutions

| Step | Common Mistake | Correct Approach | Mark Penalty (typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unit conversion | Using $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ kJ}$ | $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$ | **2 marks** |
| Density of fuel | Treating litres as kg directly | Multiply by density (kg/L) | **2 marks** |
| GCV vs NCV | Mixing Higher and Lower Heating Values | Use GCV unless explicitly told NCV | **1 mark** |
| Primary vs Final | Counting refinery losses in FEC | Refinery loss belongs to **conversion** stage | **3 marks** |
| Electricity losses | Ignoring T&D loss in chain | Apply $\eta_{T\&D}$ multiplicatively | **3 marks** |
| Sign convention | Writing efficiency as loss | $\eta + L = 1$ only for a single stage, not chain | **2 marks** |
| Final unit | Reporting FEC in mixed units | Standardise to either **GJ** or **toe** | **1 mark** |

## 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Example — Sector-Wise FEC Analysis

*Problem:* A district has the following sectoral annual energy data: **Industry 8500 toe**, **Transport 3200 toe**, **Residential 2400 toe**, **Commercial 1100 toe**, **Agriculture 600 toe**, **Non-energy 400 toe**. The district's GDP is **₹ 12,000 crore** and population is **2.4 million**. Calculate (a) total FEC, (b) per-capita FEC, (c) energy intensity per GDP, and (d) the percentage share of each sector.

**Solution — Step-by-step:**

**(a) Total FEC**

$$FEC_{total} = 8500 + 3200 + 2400 + 1100 + 600 + 400 = 16{,}200 \text{ toe}$$

**(b) Per-capita FEC**

$$EI_{pc} = \frac{16{,}200 \text{ toe}}{2.4 \times 10^{6} \text{ persons}} = 6.75 \times 10^{-3} \text{ toe/person} = 6.75 \text{ kg toe/person}$$

Note: $1 \text{ toe} = 1000 \text{ kg toe}$, so we multiply by $10^3$.

**(c) Energy intensity (per GDP)**

$$EI_{GDP} = \frac{16{,}200 \text{ toe}}{12{,}000 \text{ crore ₹}} = 1.35 \text{ toe per crore ₹}$$

**(d) Percentage shares**

$$\%_{ind} = \frac{8500}{16200} \times 100 = 52.47\%$$

$$\%_{trans} = \frac{3200}{16200} \times 100 = 19.75\%$$

$$\%_{res} = \frac{2400}{16200} \times 100 = 14.81\%$$

$$\%_{com} = \frac{1100}{16200} \times 100 = 6.79\%$$

$$\%_{agr} = \frac{600}{16200} \times 100 = 3.70\%$$

$$\%_{non\text{-}energy} = \frac{400}{16200} \times 100 = 2.47\%$$

**Sanity check:** $52.47 + 19.75 + 14.81 + 6.79 + 3.70 + 2.47 = 99.99 \approx 100\%$ ✓ (rounding error)

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Final Energy Consumption. How is it different from Primary Energy Consumption? **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, Set A]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Final Energy Consumption (FEC) is the total energy delivered to end-users for energy and non-energy purposes, measured in **toe** or **GJ**, after deducting the energy lost in the conversion of primary energy (such as coal, crude oil, natural gas) into energy carriers (such as electricity, gasoline, diesel, LPG).

**Differences (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Primary Energy | Final Energy |
|---|---|---|
| Definition stage | Source extraction | End-user delivery |
| Includes conversion loss? | Yes (losses occur after this stage) | No (losses already removed) |
| Typical forms | Coal, crude oil, uranium, biomass, hydro, wind, solar | Electricity, gasoline, diesel, LPG, piped gas, kerosene |
| Measured at | Mine mouth, well head, dam head | Consumer meter, fuel dispenser |
| Used in | Transformation plants | Industry, transport, households |

---

### Q2. State and briefly explain the four terms of the Kaya Identity. **[KTU University Exam — December 2023, Module 1, Set B]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Kaya Identity expresses **CO₂ emissions** as the product of four factors:

$$CO_2 = P \times \frac{GDP}{P} \times \frac{FEC}{GDP} \times \frac{CO_2}{FEC}$$

**(1) $P$ — Population (0.5 mark):** Total number of people. Drives the scale of all energy demand.

**(2) $GDP/P$ — Per-capita GDP or affluence (0.5 mark):** Average income per person. Higher affluence increases per-capita FEC.

**(3) $FEC/GDP$ — Energy intensity of GDP (1 mark):** The most engineering-actionable term. Lowering it = decoupling economic growth from energy use. This is the primary target of energy efficiency programmes such as **PAT (Perform Achieve and Trade)** in India.

**(4) $CO_2/FEC$ — Carbon intensity of energy (1 mark):** Depends on the fuel mix. Replacing coal with renewables reduces this factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Connect to Module 1:** The third term ($FEC/GDP$) directly uses the **Final Energy Consumption** variable that is the focus of this module.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, Module 1 carries a long-answer choice. Solve EITHER Q-A OR Q-B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive FEC Calculation and Chain-Efficiency Analysis

**A factory uses 250 tonnes of coal per month with GCV = 24 MJ/kg, 18,000 litres of furnace oil with density 0.96 kg/L and GCV = 43 MJ/kg, and 90,000 kWh of grid electricity. The coal is burnt in a captive boiler with η_boiler = 70 %, the furnace oil is burnt in a thermic fluid heater with η_heater = 80 %, and the grid has an average η_T&D = 84 % and η_end-use = 92 % for electric motors. Calculate: (a) the total monthly Final Energy Consumption in GJ and in toe, and (b) the useful thermal energy output from the boiler and the useful mechanical work from the motors, assuming the boiler uses 60 % of the coal mass and the motors use 75 % of the electricity. Discuss the engineering implications.** **[KTU University Exam — December 2024, Module 1, Set A]**

#### Part (a) — Total Monthly FEC (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Coal energy content** (1 mark)

$$E_{coal} = 250{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 24 \text{ MJ/kg} = 6{,}000{,}000 \text{ MJ} = 6000 \text{ GJ}$$

**Step 2 — Furnace oil mass and energy** (1 mark)

$$m_{FO} = 18{,}000 \text{ L} \times 0.96 \text{ kg/L} = 17{,}280 \text{ kg}$$

$$E_{FO} = 17{,}280 \text{ kg} \times 43 \text{ MJ/kg} = 743{,}040 \text{ MJ} \approx 743.04 \text{ GJ}$$

**Step 3 — Electricity energy** (1 mark)

$$E_{elec} = 90{,}000 \text{ kWh} \times 3.6 \text{ MJ/kWh} = 324{,}000 \text{ MJ} = 324 \text{ GJ}$$

**Step 4 — Total FEC in GJ** (1 mark)

$$FEC_{total} = 6000 + 743.04 + 324 = 7067.04 \text{ GJ}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to toe** (1 mark)

$$FEC_{toe} = \frac{7067.04 \text{ GJ}}{41.868 \text{ GJ/toe}} = 168.81 \text{ toe}$$

**Step 6 — Verification and standardisation** (2 marks)

| Component | Mass / Volume | Energy (GJ) | Share (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coal | 250,000 kg | 6000.00 | 84.90 |
| Furnace oil | 17,280 kg | 743.04 | 10.51 |
| Electricity | 90,000 kWh | 324.00 | 4.59 |
| **Total** | — | **7067.04** | **100.00** |

The unit standardisation and tabular summary complete the sub-part. Full marks require the explicit statement **"FEC in toe = 168.81 toe."**

#### Part (b) — Useful Energy Outputs (7 marks)

**Useful thermal energy from boiler (3 marks):**

The boiler uses **60 % of the coal mass**: $m_{boiler} = 0.60 \times 250{,}000 = 150{,}000 \text{ kg}$

Coal energy to boiler: $E_{coal,boiler} = 150{,}000 \times 24 = 3{,}600{,}000 \text{ MJ} = 3600 \text{ GJ}$

Useful thermal output: $E_{th,useful} = 3600 \text{ GJ} \times 0.70 = 2520 \text{ GJ}$

**Useful mechanical work from motors (3 marks):**

The motors use **75 % of the electricity**: $E_{motor} = 0.75 \times 90{,}000 = 67{,}500 \text{ kWh}$

This electricity already accounts for the grid (84 %) but not the motor end-use efficiency (92 %).

Apply end-use efficiency:
$$E_{mech} = 67{,}500 \text{ kWh} \times 0.92 = 62{,}100 \text{ kWh} = 223.56 \text{ GJ}$$

**Engineering Implications (1 mark):**

1. The **furnace oil and coal combustion stages** lose 20 – 30 % of FEC immediately, so the largest energy-saving opportunity is in **combustion air pre-heaters** and **flue gas heat recovery (economisers)**.
2. The **electric motor fleet** with **92 % efficiency** is good, but switching to **IE4 / IE5 premium motors** could yield an additional 3 – 5 % saving.
3. The **fossil fuel share (95.41 %)** dominates; **co-firing biomass** or **solar thermal integration** can reduce carbon intensity of FEC significantly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Sectoral Analysis and Energy Intensity

**The following data is provided for a state in India: Industry FEC = 1,80,000 TJ, Transport = 75,000 TJ, Residential = 55,000 TJ, Commercial = 28,000 TJ, Agriculture = 14,000 TJ, Non-energy = 8,000 TJ. The state's GDP is ₹ 18 lakh crore and population is 35 million. (a) Calculate the total FEC in toe, the per-capita FEC in kg-toe/person, and the energy intensity in toe per crore ₹. (b) Compare the sectoral pattern with all-India averages and discuss three engineering strategies to reduce FEC in the dominant sector.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, Set B]**

#### Part (a) — Total FEC, Per-Capita, and Energy Intensity (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Total FEC in TJ** (1 mark)

$$FEC_{total} = 180{,}000 + 75{,}000 + 55{,}000 + 28{,}000 + 14{,}000 + 8{,}000 = 3{,}60{,}000 \text{ TJ}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to toe** (1 mark)

$$1 \text{ TJ} = 10^{12} \text{ J} = \frac{10^{12}}{41.868 \times 10^{9}} \text{ toe} = 23.8846 \text{ toe}$$

$$FEC_{toe} = 3{,}60{,}000 \times 23.8846 = 85{,}98{,}456 \text{ toe} \approx 85.98 \text{ Mtoe}$$

**Step 3 — Per-capita FEC in kg-toe/person** (2 marks)

$$EI_{pc} = \frac{85{,}98{,}456 \text{ toe}}{35 \times 10^{6} \text{ persons}} = 0.2457 \text{ toe/person} = 245.7 \text{ kg-toe/person}$$

**Step 4 — Energy intensity per GDP** (2 marks)

Note: ₹ 18 lakh crore = ₹ 18 × 10⁵ crore = ₹ 1,80,00,000 crore.

$$EI_{GDP} = \frac{85{,}98{,}456 \text{ toe}}{18 \times 10^{5} \text{ crore}} = \frac{85.98 \text{ Mtoe}}{1.8 \text{ M crore}} = 0.0478 \text{ toe per crore ₹}$$

Equivalent: $47.8 \text{ kg-toe per crore ₹}$.

**Step 5 — Sectoral shares** (1 mark)

| Sector | FEC (TJ) | Share (%) |
|---|---|---|
| Industry | 180,000 | 50.00 |
| Transport | 75,000 | 20.83 |
| Residential | 55,000 | 15.28 |
| Commercial | 28,000 | 7.78 |
| Agriculture | 14,000 | 3.89 |
| Non-energy | 8,000 | 2.22 |
| **Total** | **360,000** | **100.00** |

#### Part (b) — Comparison and Strategies (7 marks)

**Comparison with all-India average (2 marks):**

| Indicator | State (this problem) | India typical |
|---|---|---|
| Industry share of FEC | **50.00 %** | 40 – 45 % |
| Transport share | **20.83 %** | 18 – 22 % |
| Per-capita FEC | **245.7 kg-toe** | ~ 600 kg-toe (India average) |
| Energy intensity | **47.8 kg-toe/₹cr** | ~ 70 – 80 kg-toe/₹cr |

**Observation:** The state has a **more industrialised energy mix** than the national average, with **industry dominating at 50 %** — higher than the national 40 – 45 % band. Per-capita FEC is lower, indicating either **higher efficiency** or **lower per-capita affluence**.

**Three engineering strategies to reduce Industrial FEC (5 marks):**

1. **Waste-heat recovery (2 marks):** Industrial boilers and furnaces typically reject **30 – 50 %** of input energy as flue gas and surface heat. Installing **economisers**, **air pre-heaters**, and **waste-heat boilers** can recover 10 – 20 % of FEC. For a 50 % industry share, this is the single highest-impact intervention.

2. **Efficient motor and VFD retrofits (2 marks):** Electric motor systems consume ~ 60 % of industrial electricity. Replacing standard induction motors with **IE3/IE4** units and installing **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** on pumps, fans, and compressors can save 15 – 30 % of motor-related FEC.

3. **Process integration and pinch analysis (1 mark):** Using **pinch analysis** to optimise heat exchanger networks and recover process heat internally can yield **10 – 25 %** steam and fuel savings without new capital equipment for energy generation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in FEC Problems:**
> 1. **Mixing kWh and MJ:** Many students write $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ kJ}$ instead of $3.6 \text{ MJ}$. This single error **costs 2 marks** in the conversion step.
> 2. **Ignoring density of liquid fuels:** Treating $1 \text{ L} = 1 \text{ kg}$ for furnace oil and diesel costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting T&D loss:** Electricity is delivered energy, not primary; the chain efficiency must be applied at the **end-use** step, not on the primary stage. Misapplying this **costs 3 marks**.
> 4. **Percentage rounding:** Always show the sum equals 100 % (or 99.9 % with rounding acknowledged) for full mark on the share table.
> 5. **Reporting unit only in final answer:** Marks are explicitly allocated for "mentioning the unit" — students frequently miss this and lose **1 mark** per sub-part.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Final Energy Consumption (Module 1, PESFT741):**
>
> - **Energy chain:** Primary → Final → Useful → Service. Memorise the four stages in order.
> - **Key formula:** $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{conv} \times \eta_{T\&D} \times \eta_{end}$ — **multiplicative, not additive**.
> - **Standard conversions:** $1 \text{ toe} = 41.868 \text{ GJ} = 11.630 \text{ MWh}$; $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$.
> - **FEC ≠ Primary Energy:** FEC is the energy **delivered** to the consumer, after subtracting conversion and T&D losses.
> - **Sectoral pattern (India):** Industry > Transport > Residential > Commercial > Non-energy > Agriculture.
> - **Kaya Identity:** $CO_2 = P \times (GDP/P) \times (FEC/GDP) \times (CO_2/FEC)$ — the **FEC/GDP** term is the energy manager's primary lever.
> - **Energy intensity:** Two forms — per GDP (toe/₹cr) and per capita (kg-toe/person). Both are used in international comparisons.
> - **End-use efficiency range:** LEDs ~ 0.40, LPG stoves ~ 0.60, electric motors ~ 0.90, IES-grade boilers > 0.90.
> - **Audit workflow:** FEC → disaggregation → SEC → M&V → ECM → Payback period.
> - **Policy anchors for India:** Energy Conservation Act 2001, PAT scheme, BEE star labelling, ECBC for commercial buildings, UJALA for LED distribution.
> - **Highest-leverage sector:** Industry consumes the largest share, so the largest absolute savings come from **industrial energy efficiency**.
> - **Units trap:** Always specify **toe, GJ, MJ, or kWh** clearly. Mixed-unit answers lose marks.
> - **Sanity check:** Sectoral percentages must add to **~ 100 %** (within rounding tolerance of ±0.05 %).
> - **Engineering mindset:** Treat every problem as a **multi-stage efficiency chain**, not a single calculation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Indian Energy Scenario
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Indian Energy Scenario

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Indian Energy Scenario** refers to a comprehensive analytical study of the structure, supply, demand, generation mix, consumption patterns, resource availability, and policy framework that governs the production, distribution, and utilization of energy across the Republic of India. It encompasses the assessment of both **conventional (fossil & nuclear)** and **non-conventional (renewable)** energy sources, sectoral energy end-use, and India's position in the global energy landscape.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** The Indian Energy Scenario is the macro-level study of *energy resources* (reserves, potential, geographic distribution), *energy infrastructure* (generation, transmission, distribution capacities), *energy demand* (industrial, domestic, agricultural, transport), and *energy policy* (national missions, regulatory bodies like CEA, MNRE, BEE) of India as a single integrated system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Energy Wallet"

Imagine India's energy sector as a **household monthly budget (wallet)**:
- **Income (Energy Supply)** = Coal, Oil, Gas, Hydro, Solar, Wind, Nuclear, Biomass
- **Expenses (Energy Demand)** = Industries (biggest spender), Agriculture, Transport, Domestic, Commercial
- **Debt (Energy Imports)** = Crude oil & gas purchased from foreign nations (India imports ~85% crude and ~50% natural gas)
- **Savings (Renewables)** = Indigenous solar, wind, hydro that reduce future "import bills"

A family whose expenses exceed income must either **borrow (import energy)**, **cut spending (energy efficiency/conservation)**, or **increase income (develop renewables, exploit domestic reserves)**. The Indian Energy Scenario is exactly this **"energy wallet balance sheet"** of the nation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology — Know These Cold for KTU Exams:**
> - **Primary Energy:** Energy in its raw form (coal, crude oil, sunlight, wind).
> - **Final Energy:** Energy delivered to end-users (electricity, gasoline, LPG).
> - **Useful Energy:** Energy actually performing the work (light, motion, heat).
> - **Energy Intensity:** Energy consumed per unit of GDP (TOE per million USD).
> - **Per Capita Consumption:** Energy use per person per year (kgoe or TOE).
> - **Energy Security:** Uninterrupted availability of energy at affordable prices.

### 1.3 Fundamental Energy Metrics (Bold Standardized Constants)

| Metric | Value/Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **1 TOE (Tonne of Oil Equivalent)** | 41.868 GJ or 11.63 MWh | Universal energy unit for cross-source comparison |
| **1 kgoe** | 41.868 MJ | Used for per-capita measurements |
| **1 MTOE** | 10⁶ TOE | Megatonne — National-level energy accounting |
| **GW → TWh conversion** | 1 GW × 8760 h × PLF = TWh | 1 GW plant at 100% PLF = 8.76 TWh/year |
| **Plant Load Factor (PLF)** | (Actual Energy / Max Possible) × 100% | Efficiency indicator of power plant |
| **Energy Intensity of India** | ~0.20 kgoe/USD (2023) | Lower = more energy-efficient economy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of the Indian Energy Scenario** (Frequently asked in KTU 2-mark questions):
> 1. **Supply Side** — Reserves, resources, generation capacity.
> 2. **Demand Side** — Sectoral consumption (industry > transport > agriculture > domestic > commercial).
> 3. **Policy Side** — National Electricity Plan, NEP, Integrated Energy Policy (IEP 2006), National Mission on Enhanced Energy Efficiency (NMEEE), UJALA, PAT scheme.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of Indian Energy Resources

#### A. Conventional Energy Resources (~75% of primary mix)

1. **Coal (The Backbone — ~45% of primary energy)**
   - India possesses the **2nd largest coal reserves globally** (after USA/Russia depending on classification).
   - Proved reserves: **~360 billion tonnes** (Geological Survey of India estimate).
   - States: Jharkhand, Odisha, Chhattisgarh, West Bengal, Madhya Pradesh.
   - Major type: **Bituminous & sub-bituminous** (high ash content, low calorific value ~3500–4500 kcal/kg vs. global average ~6000 kcal/kg).
   - **Why it matters:** Despite pollution, coal sustains ~70% of India's electricity generation due to its indigenous availability.

2. **Crude Oil & Natural Gas (Hydrocarbons — ~30% of primary mix)**
   - India is **import-dependent** for crude oil (~85% imported) and natural gas (~50% imported).
   - Domestic crude production: ~30 MMTPA; consumption: ~230 MMTPA.
   - Major basins: Mumbai High, Assam-Arakan, Cambay, Krishna-Godavari, Cauvery, Rajasthan.
   - Strategic reserves: Underground storage at Visakhapatnam, Mangalore, Padur (Phase-I: 5.33 MMT).

3. **Nuclear Power (~2% of installed capacity)**
   - Installed capacity: ~8 GW (target 22.5 GW by 2031).
   - Uranium reserves: Limited (Jharkhand, Andhra Pradesh, Meghalaya).
   - Imported fuel: 3-stage nuclear program (Pressurized Heavy Water Reactors → Fast Breeder Reactors → Thorium-based).

4. **Large Hydro (>25 MW) — ~10% of installed capacity**
   - Potential: ~145 GW (economically exploitable ~96 GW).
   - States: Himachal Pradesh, Uttarakhand, J&K, North-East, Sikkim.

#### B. Non-Conventional / Renewable Energy Sources (~25% and growing)

1. **Solar Energy** — Estimated potential: **~750 GWp** (assuming 3% wasteland).
2. **Wind Energy** — Estimated potential: **~302 GW** at 120m hub height.
3. **Biomass & Bagasse Cogeneration** — ~25 GW.
4. **Small Hydro (<25 MW)** — ~21 GW potential.
5. **Tidal & Wave** — Experimental stage (Gulf of Kutch, Gulf of Cambay).
6. **Geothermal** — Pilot stage (Manikaran, Puga Valley, Surajkund).
7. **Hydrogen (Green Hydrogen)** — Mission launched 2023, target 5 MMT by 2030.

### 2.2 Sectoral Energy Consumption Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> **The Energy Pie Chart of India (Approximate Distribution):**
> - Industry: ~35–40%
> - Transport: ~14–18%
> - Agriculture: ~8–10%
> - Domestic/Residential: ~20–25%
> - Commercial: ~8–10%
> - Others: ~3–5%

**Why industry dominates:** Cement, steel, aluminum, fertilizers, chemicals, paper, textiles are highly energy-intensive. The transport sector is rapidly growing due to motorization.

### 2.3 India's Position in the Global Energy Map

| Parameter | India | World Average | China | USA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Total Primary Energy Consumption** | ~800 MTOE | — | ~4500 MTOE | ~2200 MTOE |
| **Per Capita Consumption (kgoe)** | ~580 | ~1900 | ~2700 | ~6800 |
| **Energy Intensity (kgoe/USD)** | 0.20 | 0.18 | 0.18 | 0.14 |
| **Renewables Share** | ~25% (in electricity) | ~30% | ~30% | ~22% |
| **CO₂ Emissions Rank** | 3rd | — | 1st | 2nd |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** India has **~17% of world population** but consumes only **~6% of world primary energy**. This low per-capita figure signals huge latent demand — driving India's push for renewables, nuclear, and efficiency.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Use `\vert` — no `|` characters)

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy Conversion | $1 \text{ TOE} = 41.868 \text{ GJ} = 11.63 \text{ MWh}$ | Universal energy unit |
| 2 | Calorific Value of Coal | $CV = \dfrac{\text{Heat released (kcal)}}{\text{Mass of fuel (kg)}}$ | kcal/kg; Indian coal: 3500–4500 |
| 3 | Annual Energy from Plant | $E = P \times PLF \times 8760$ | TWh = GW × PLF × 8760/1000 |
| 4 | Per Capita Consumption | $PC = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy Consumed}}{\text{Population}}$ | kgoe or GJ/person |
| 5 | Energy Intensity | $EI = \dfrac{\text{Total Primary Energy}}{\text{GDP (constant USD)}}$ | TOE per million USD |
| 6 | Energy Independence Ratio | $EIR = \dfrac{\text{Domestic Production}}{\text{Total Consumption}} \times 100$ | %; India: ~65% overall |
| 7 | Capacity Addition Required | $\Delta C = \dfrac{\Delta D}{8760 \times PLF}$ | GW per TWh demand |
| 8 | Cumulative Installed Capacity | $C_{cum} = C_{existing} + C_{new}$ | GW |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the Indian Energy Scenario is the foundation for:
- **Power system planning engineers** — sizing future generation.
- **Energy auditors & managers** — knowing baseline to find savings.
- **Policy analysts** — evaluating RPO (Renewable Purchase Obligation), REC (Renewable Energy Certificates), carbon credits.
- **Industrial managers** — switching from imported fuels to indigenous renewables.
- **Building designers** — net-zero, ECBC compliance, ECBC+.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Converting Coal Quantity to TOE

**Problem:** A thermal power plant consumes **1.5 million tonnes** of coal per year. Given the average calorific value is **4000 kcal/kg**, calculate the primary energy in **MTOE**.

**Given:**
- Mass of coal: $m = 1.5 \times 10^6$ tonnes $= 1.5 \times 10^9$ kg
- Calorific Value: $CV = 4000$ kcal/kg
- 1 kcal = 4.1868 kJ
- 1 TOE = $41.868 \times 10^9$ kJ = $41.868$ GJ

**Step 1:** Total heat released by coal (in kcal)

$$Q_{kcal} = m \times CV$$

$$Q_{kcal} = 1.5 \times 10^9 \text{ kg} \times 4000 \text{ kcal/kg}$$

$$Q_{kcal} = 6.0 \times 10^{12} \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 2:** Convert kcal to kJ using $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.1868 \text{ kJ}$

$$Q_{kJ} = 6.0 \times 10^{12} \times 4.1868 \text{ kJ}$$

$$Q_{kJ} = 25.1208 \times 10^{12} \text{ kJ} = 2.51208 \times 10^{13} \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3:** Convert kJ to TOE using $1 \text{ TOE} = 41.868 \times 10^9 \text{ kJ}$

$$E_{TOE} = \dfrac{2.51208 \times 10^{13}}{41.868 \times 10^9}$$

$$E_{TOE} = \dfrac{2.51208}{41.868} \times 10^{4}$$

$$E_{TOE} = 0.0600 \times 10^{4}$$

$$E_{TOE} = 600 \text{ TOE} = 0.0006 \text{ MTOE}$$

**Step 4 (Short-cut method):** Directly use 1 TOE ≈ 10⁷ kcal of oil equivalent

$$\text{Oil equivalent} \approx 1 \text{ TOE per } 10^7 \text{ kcal}$$

$$E_{TOE} = \dfrac{6.0 \times 10^{12}}{1.0 \times 10^{7}} = 6.0 \times 10^{5} \text{ TOE} = 0.6 \text{ MTOE}$$

**Note:** The minor difference is due to the exact conversion factor vs. approximation. The KTU-accepted answer: **≈ 0.6 MTOE**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Generation from a Coal-Based Power Plant

**Problem:** A 1000 MW thermal power plant operates at a **Plant Load Factor (PLF) of 75%**. Calculate:
(a) Annual energy generated in **TWh**.
(b) Coal required per year if heat rate is **2500 kcal/kWh**.

**Given:**
- Installed Capacity: $P = 1000 \text{ MW} = 1 \text{ GW}$
- PLF = 75% = 0.75
- Hours in a year = 8760 h
- Heat Rate: $HR = 2500$ kcal/kWh
- Calorific Value of coal: $CV = 4000$ kcal/kg

**Part (a): Annual Energy Generated**

$$E = P \times PLF \times t$$

$$E = 1000 \text{ MW} \times 0.75 \times 8760 \text{ h}$$

$$E = 6.57 \times 10^6 \text{ MWh} = 6.57 \text{ TWh}$$

**Part (b): Coal Required**

**Step 1:** Total energy = $6.57 \times 10^6$ MWh $= 6.57 \times 10^9$ kWh

**Step 2:** Total heat required

$$Q = E \times HR = 6.57 \times 10^9 \times 2500 \text{ kcal}$$

$$Q = 1.6425 \times 10^{13} \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 3:** Mass of coal

$$m = \dfrac{Q}{CV} = \dfrac{1.6425 \times 10^{13}}{4000}$$

$$m = 4.106 \times 10^9 \text{ kg} = 4.106 \times 10^6 \text{ tonnes} = 4.106 \text{ MMT}$$

**Answer:** Annual generation = **6.57 TWh**; Coal required ≈ **4.1 million tonnes/year**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Forecasting Future Capacity Requirement

**Problem:** India's current electricity demand is **1500 TWh** and is projected to grow at **5% CAGR** for 10 years. What additional capacity (GW) is required, assuming an average PLF of 55%?

**Step 1:** Future demand using compound growth

$$D_{future} = D_{present} \times (1 + r)^n$$

$$D_{future} = 1500 \times (1.05)^{10}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $(1.05)^{10}$

$$(1.05)^{10} \approx 1.6289$$

$$D_{future} = 1500 \times 1.6289 = 2443.4 \text{ TWh}$$

**Step 3:** Additional demand

$$\Delta D = 2443.4 - 1500 = 943.4 \text{ TWh}$$

**Step 4:** Required additional capacity (at PLF = 0.55)

$$\Delta C = \dfrac{\Delta D}{PLF \times 8760 \text{ h}}$$

$$\Delta C = \dfrac{943.4 \times 10^9 \text{ kWh}}{0.55 \times 8760 \text{ h}}$$

$$\Delta C = \dfrac{943.4 \times 10^9}{4818} \text{ kW}$$

$$\Delta C = 1.958 \times 10^8 \text{ kW} = 195.8 \text{ GW}$$

**Answer:** India needs to add **~196 GW** of new generation capacity in the next 10 years.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Energy Sources

| Source | Indigeneous Availability | Carbon Intensity (kgCO₂/kWh) | Cost (₹/kWh) | Reliability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Coal** | High (abundant) | ~0.95 | 3.5–5.0 | High (24×7) |
| **Natural Gas** | Low (50% imported) | ~0.45 | 5.0–8.0 | High |
| **Nuclear** | Medium (uranium limited) | ~0.012 | 4.0–6.0 | Very High |
| **Large Hydro** | High (~145 GW potential) | ~0.024 | 3.0–5.0 | High (seasonal) |
| **Solar PV** | Very High (~750 GW) | ~0.05 | 2.5–4.0 | Intermittent |
| **Onshore Wind** | Very High (~302 GW) | ~0.011 | 3.0–5.0 | Intermittent |
| **Biomass** | High | ~0.39 | 5.0–7.0 | Dispatchable |
| **Diesel (backup)** | Imported | ~0.85 | 14–20 | High |

---

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`**  
**"Briefly explain the salient features of the Indian Energy Scenario with reference to per capita energy consumption and import dependence."** **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The Indian energy sector is characterized by the following salient features:
1. **Low Per Capita Consumption:** India's per capita primary energy consumption is only ~580 kgoe, far below the world average of ~1900 kgoe, indicating significant unmet demand and growth potential.
2. **High Import Dependence:** India imports ~85% of its crude oil, ~50% of its natural gas, and is dependent on foreign suppliers for coking coal (~25% imported). This creates **energy security risks** and exposes the economy to global price volatility.
3. **Coal-Dominant Mix:** Coal accounts for ~45% of primary energy and ~70% of electricity generation, making India the world's second-largest coal consumer.
4. **Rising Renewables Share:** India has committed to 500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030 (Panchamrit at COP-26).
5. **Sectoral Diversity:** Industry (~38%) is the largest consumer, followed by domestic, transport, and agriculture sectors.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often quote outdated figures. Use **latest 2023–24** data. Also remember: **per capita consumption ≠ electricity per capita**. They are different (electricity per capita is ~1300 kWh/year vs energy per capita ~580 kgoe).

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, May 2023]`**  
**"List the major conventional and non-conventional energy resources available in India. Mention the estimated potential of any two renewables."** **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Conventional Energy Resources:**
- Coal, Crude Oil, Natural Gas, Nuclear (Uranium/Thorium), Large Hydro.

**Non-Conventional (Renewable) Energy Resources:**
- Solar, Wind, Biomass, Bagasse, Small Hydro, Tidal, Wave, Geothermal, Hydrogen.

**Estimated Potential of Two Renewables:**
- **Solar Energy:** ~750 GWp (assuming 3% of India's wasteland area, MNRE estimate).
- **Wind Energy:** ~302 GW at 120 m hub height (NIWE assessment at 120m, earlier estimate was 49 GW at 50m).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students confuse "technical potential" with "economic potential" or "installed capacity." Always specify: potential = resource availability, not what is installed.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

#### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"With the help of a pie chart and data, describe in detail the Indian Energy Scenario covering: (a) the structure of energy supply, generation capacity, and reserves; and (b) the sectoral energy demand and policy initiatives taken by the Government of India."**

##### **Part (a) — Energy Supply, Generation Capacity, and Reserves [7 Marks]**

**1. Energy Supply Mix (Primary Energy, 2023 data):**
- Coal: ~45%
- Oil: ~25%
- Natural Gas: ~6%
- Renewables (incl. large hydro): ~13%
- Biofuels & Waste: ~9%
- Nuclear: ~2%

**2. Installed Electricity Capacity (≈ 430 GW, mid-2024):**
- Coal: ~205 GW (~50%)
- Renewables: ~190 GW (~44% including large hydro)
- Gas: ~25 GW (~6%)
- Nuclear: ~8 GW (~2%)
- Diesel: ~0.5 GW

**3. Reserves of Major Fuels:**
- **Coal:** Proved reserves ≈ 360 billion tonnes (Bt); economically mineable for ~150+ years.
- **Crude Oil:** Proved reserves ≈ 600 MMT; reserve life < 10 years at current production rate.
- **Natural Gas:** Proved reserves ≈ 1400 BCM (billion cubic meters); reserve life ~12–15 years.
- **Uranium:** Adequate for PHWR stage; thorium reserves huge (~9,63,000 tonnes in monazite sands of Kerala, Odisha, Tamil Nadu).

**4. Renewable Energy Status (as of mid-2024):**
- Solar installed: ~85 GW (target 500 GW non-fossil by 2030).
- Wind installed: ~47 GW.
- Total non-fossil (incl. nuclear + large hydro): ~200 GW (~46% of total).

**Conclusion of part (a):** India's energy supply is heavily reliant on coal, with rising renewable contribution. Reserves of hydrocarbons are limited, justifying aggressive renewables and efficiency push.

---

##### **Part (b) — Sectoral Energy Demand and Policy Initiatives [7 Marks]**

**1. Sectoral Energy Demand (Approximate % of Total):**

| Sector | Share |
|---|---|
| Industry | 35–40% |
| Transport | 14–18% |
| Domestic | 20–25% |
| Agriculture | 8–10% |
| Commercial | 8–10% |

**2. Key Policy Initiatives:**

1. **National Solar Mission (JNNSM, 2010)** — 100 GW solar target; later revised to 300 GW by 2030.
2. **Ujwal DISCOM Assurance Yojana (UDAY, 2015)** — Financial turnaround of power distribution companies.
3. **Saubhagya Scheme (2017)** — Free electricity connections to all un-electrified households.
4. **Perform Achieve Trade (PAT) Scheme** — Cap-and-trade for energy-intensive industries (under NMEEE).
5. **UJALA / SLNP** — LED bulb distribution (>36 crore LEDs issued, ~9500 MW peak demand avoided).
6. **ECBC & Eco Niwas Samhita** — Energy codes for commercial and residential buildings.
7. **Green Hydrogen Mission (2023)** — 5 MMT production target by 2030.
8. **PM-KUSUM** — Solar pumps for agriculture; reduces diesel subsidy burden.
9. **Panchamrit Declaration at COP-26 (Glasgow, 2021)** — 500 GW non-fossil capacity, 50% renewable share, 1 billion-tonne CO₂ reduction by 2030; net-zero by 2070.

**Conclusion of part (b):** Industrial sector dominates demand, but domestic and transport are growing rapidly. The government has launched a multi-pronged policy framework emphasizing renewables, efficiency, and equity.

---

#### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**"Discuss the role of renewable energy sources in the Indian Energy Scenario. In your answer, cover: (a) the potential, current installed capacity, and challenges of solar and wind energy in India; and (b) the strategic importance of energy conservation, demand-side management, and future energy security for India."**

##### **Part (a) — Solar and Wind Energy: Potential, Capacity, Challenges [7 Marks]**

**1. Solar Energy:**
- **Potential:** ~750 GWp (3% of India's land area). Rajasthan, Gujarat, Ladakh have highest insolation (5.5–7.0 kWh/m²/day).
- **Installed Capacity:** ~85 GW (2024), making India the **4th largest solar PV market globally**.
- **Major Initiatives:** Bhadla Solar Park (Rajasthan, 2.25 GW — one of world's largest), Pavagada (Karnataka, 2 GW), Rewa (Madhya Pradesh, 750 MW — record low tariff ₹2.97/kWh).
- **Challenges:** Intermittency (no generation at night), land acquisition, grid integration, storage cost, dust soiling, financing.

**2. Wind Energy:**
- **Potential:** ~302 GW at 120 m hub height (revised from 49 GW at 50 m).
- **Installed Capacity:** ~47 GW (2024). India is the **4th largest wind market globally**.
- **Major Sites:** Muppandal (Tamil Nadu), Jaisalmer (Rajasthan), Kutch (Gujarat), Kayathar (Tamil Nadu).
- **Challenges:** Wind variability, low wind in non-monsoon months, evacuation infrastructure, repowering old turbines (no incentive), and offshore wind still nascent (target 30 GW offshore by 2030).

**3. Other Renewables:**
- Biomass: ~10 GW
- Small Hydro: ~5 GW
- Waste-to-Energy: ~0.5 GW

---

##### **Part (b) — Energy Conservation, Demand-Side Management, and Future Security [7 Marks]**

**1. Energy Conservation Importance:**
- India's per capita electricity is ~1300 kWh vs. world average ~3500 kWh. Bridging this gap through *supply alone* requires massive capital and emissions. **Conservation is the cheapest "source" of energy.**
- ECBC norms for buildings, BEE star ratings for appliances, mandatory audits for Designated Consumers (industries consuming > 1.5 MTOE/year).

**2. Demand-Side Management (DSM):**
- Shifting loads to off-peak (e.g., EV charging at night, agricultural pumps during solar peak).
- Time-of-Day (ToD) tariffs to incentivize off-peak usage.
- DSM regulations by State Electricity Regulatory Commissions (SERCs).
- Smart meters, AMI, and real-time consumption feedback.

**3. Energy Security for India:**
- Reducing import dependence via renewables and biofuels.
- Strategic crude oil reserves (5.33 MMT, Phase-I).
- Diversifying import sources (Middle East, Russia, Latin America, Africa).
- Promoting EVs and public transport to reduce oil demand.
- Building indigenous manufacturing: National Programme on Advanced Solar Cell R&D, PLI scheme for solar manufacturing.

**4. Future Energy Mix (Vision 2047):**
- Coal share reducing to ~30% (vs 50% today).
- Renewables share rising to ~50% in installed capacity.
- Nuclear expanded to ~40 GW.
- Green hydrogen in industry and transport.
- Battery storage: ~42 GWh by 2030.

**Conclusion of part (b):** Renewables are not just an environmental choice but a strategic necessity for India's energy security. Conservation complements supply-side efforts and reduces the need for expensive new capacity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Numerical mismatch:** Writing "India uses 70% of renewable energy" (incorrect — it's 25–30% of primary energy, not 70%). Be precise.
> 2. **Forgetting policy year and authority:** A 14-mark answer must mention *what, when, and by whom* for each policy (e.g., "NMEEE launched in 2008, implemented by BEE").
> 3. **Mixing up data sources:** Confusing CEA (electricity) data with MNRE (renewables) data.
> 4. **Skipping a flow diagram:** Always include at least one **block diagram / pie chart** in a 14-mark answer; it earns you 1–2 easy marks.
> 5. **No conclusion:** A 14-mark answer without a closing conclusion loses ~1 mark. Always summarize.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Indian Energy Scenario = Supply + Demand + Reserves + Policy framework of India.
- **Per Capita Primary Energy:** ~580 kgoe (low vs. world average ~1900 kgoe).
- **Per Capita Electricity:** ~1300 kWh/year.
- **Coal Share:** ~45% primary, ~70% electricity generation, ~50% installed capacity.
- **Crude Oil Import Dependence:** ~85%.
- **Natural Gas Import Dependence:** ~50%.
- **Total Installed Capacity (2024):** ~430 GW (Coal 50%, Renewables 44%, Gas 6%, Nuclear 2%).
- **Largest Solar Park:** Bhadla (Rajasthan, 2.25 GW).
- **Lowest Solar Tariff:** ₹2.00–2.50/kWh in recent auctions.
- **Solar Potential:** ~750 GWp.
- **Wind Potential:** ~302 GW (at 120m).
- **Panchamrit Targets (COP-26):** 500 GW non-fossil, 50% renewable, 1 BT CO₂ cut by 2030; **Net-Zero by 2070**.
- **Key Acts/Authorities:** Electricity Act 2003, CEA, BEE, MNRE, CERC, SERCs.
- **Key Missions:** JNNSM (Solar), UJALA (LEDs), UDAY (DISCOMs), PAT (Industrial efficiency), Green Hydrogen Mission (2023).
- **Energy Security Tools:** Strategic petroleum reserves, renewables push, nuclear 3-stage program, EV adoption.
- **3-Stage Nuclear Program:** PHWR → FBR (breeds Pu-239) → Thorium-based (uses India's vast thorium).
- **Energy Conversion:** $1 \text{ TOE} = 41.868 \text{ GJ} = 11.63 \text{ MWh}$.
- **PLF Formula:** $E_{annual} = P \times PLF \times 8760$.
- **Largest Coal Reserves State:** Jharkhand > Odisha > Chhattisgarh.
- **Major Hydro Potential States:** Himachal, Uttarakhand, NE, Sikkim.
- **Largest Hydro Plant Operational:** Tehri Dam (2.4 GW, Uttarakhand).
- **Highest Solar Insolation:** Rajasthan / Ladakh.
- **Wind Belt States:** Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, Karnataka, Rajasthan.
- **To remember for KTU:** Industry = biggest energy consumer, Coal = biggest electricity source, Renewables = fastest growing, Import = biggest vulnerability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Mantra (3-2-1 Formula):**
> - **3 biggest facts:** Coal dominant, Oil-imported, Renewables booming.
> - **2 biggest missions:** Solar Mission + Green Hydrogen Mission.
> - **1 biggest target:** Net-Zero by 2070.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sectoral Energy Consumption (Domestic, Industrial and Other Sectors)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sectoral Energy Consumption (Domestic, Industrial and Other Sectors)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sectoral Energy Consumption** is the systematic classification and quantitative assessment of total energy used across distinct economic and social segments of a nation or region. Under the standard accounting framework adopted by the **International Energy Agency (IEA)** and the **Ministry of Power, Government of India**, energy consumption is broadly partitioned into the following five primary sectors:

1. **Domestic (Residential) Sector** — Energy consumed in private households for lighting, space conditioning, water heating, cooking, refrigeration, and operation of household appliances.
2. **Industrial Sector** — Energy consumed by manufacturing units, process industries, heavy engineering plants, and construction activities.
3. **Commercial Sector** — Energy consumed in offices, retail outlets, hotels, hospitals, educational institutions, and government buildings.
4. **Transportation Sector** — Energy consumed for movement of goods and people via road, rail, air, and waterways.
5. **Agriculture Sector** — Energy consumed for irrigation pumps, farm machinery, fertilizer production, and post-harvest processing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 explicitly mandates that students must be able to *identify, classify, and quantify* energy consumption in the **Domestic, Industrial, and remaining (Commercial + Agricultural + Transport)** sectors, and recognize the dominant end-uses driving each.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a country's total annual energy bill as a giant pizza. That pizza is sliced into five major pieces, one for each sector. The **Industrial slice** is usually the biggest piece (largest consumer), followed by the **Transportation slice**, then **Domestic**, then **Commercial**, and finally **Agriculture** as the smallest slice in developed economies.

Now, within each slice, there are sub-toppings — for example, in the **Domestic slice**, the "toppings" are lighting, fans, air conditioning, and geysers. Just as a chef would want to know which topping uses the most cheese (energy) to optimize the recipe, an energy manager must identify which **end-use** consumes the most energy to plan effective conservation strategies.

This is the essence of **sectoral energy analysis**: slice the total energy pie, identify the largest slices, drill down to the biggest toppings (end-uses), and apply targeted conservation measures.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

The two foundational metrics used across all sectors are:

- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** Energy consumed per unit of output (e.g., kWh per tonne of cement, MJ per kg of steel, kWh per capita per year for households).
- **Energy Intensity (EI):** Total energy consumed per unit of Gross Domestic Product (e.g., MJ per USD of GDP).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** The internationally accepted unit for primary energy accounting is **tonne of oil equivalent (toe)**, where **1 toe = 41.868 GJ = 11.63 MWh**.

### 1.4 Sectoral Share — Indian Context (Approximate Reference Values)

> [!NOTE]
> The figures below reflect typical secondary-source estimates of India's installed electrical energy consumption distribution. These are provided for illustrative purposes and should not be quoted as KTU-mandated fixed values.

| Sector | Approx. Share of Electrical Energy |
|---|---|
| Industrial | 41 % – 50 % |
| Domestic | 24 % – 30 % |
| Commercial | 8 % – 10 % |
| Agriculture | 17 % – 20 % |
| Transportation (electric) | 2 % – 3 % |

*(Note: Transport is dominated by petroleum products and hence appears small in electrical terms only.)*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Domestic (Residential) Sector — End-Use Breakdown

Energy consumption in Indian households is dominated by **space cooling (fan + air conditioner)**, **lighting**, and **water heating**, with regional variations. The Energy Efficiency Services Limited (EESL) and BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency) national surveys typically report the following indicative end-use split:

- **Lighting** — ~20 % to 25 %
- **Space Cooling (Fans + ACs)** — ~35 % to 45 % (largest growing share)
- **Refrigeration** — ~15 %
- **Water Heating (Geysers)** — ~10 % to 15 %
- **Cooking (LPG + Electric induction)** — ~10 %
- **Entertainment & Electronics** — ~5 % to 10 %

**Why this matters:** A student of energy management must recognize that **any blanket energy-saving policy in the residential sector without targeting ACs and fans will be largely ineffective**, since cooling loads dominate.

### 2.2 Industrial Sector — End-Use Classification

The industrial sector is the most heterogeneous. Industries are classified as **Energy-Intensive** (e.g., Aluminium, Cement, Steel, Fertilizer, Pulp & Paper, Chlor-Alkali) or **Non-Energy-Intensive** (e.g., Assembly, Textiles, Food Processing).

The major industrial end-uses are:

- **Process Heat** (boilers, furnaces, kilns) — typically the largest share, 50 % to 70 %.
- **Motor-Driven Systems** (pumps, fans, compressors) — 20 % to 30 %.
- **Lighting** — 3 % to 5 %.
- **Space Conditioning & HVAC** — 2 % to 5 %.
- **Electrochemical / Electrolytic Processes** — sector-specific (e.g., aluminium smelting).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The industrial sector is the **primary target** for Energy Management because it offers the **largest absolute savings potential** and the **highest return on investment (ROI)** through motor rewinding/VFD retrofits, waste-heat recovery, and process integration.

### 2.3 Commercial Sector

Includes shopping malls, IT parks, hospitals, hotels, and government offices. Dominant end-uses are **HVAC (40 %–60 %)**, **Lighting (20 %–30 %)**, and **Plug loads / IT equipment (15 %–25 %)**. This sector is growing rapidly in India due to urbanization.

### 2.4 Agriculture Sector

Energy is consumed mainly in:
- **Irrigation Pumps** (electric and diesel) — dominant.
- **Farm Machinery** (tractors, harvesters).
- **Fertilizer Production** (upstream, counted under industrial).
- **Post-Harvest** (cold storage, milling).

### 2.5 Transportation Sector

Dominated by **petroleum products (diesel + petrol)**. Electrification of railways and growth of electric vehicles (EVs) is gradually shifting this share toward electricity. For KTU 2024, focus remains on petroleum dependency and EV-driven transition.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = P \times t$ | Basic electrical energy consumption | kWh |
| 2 | $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{Q_{\text{output}}}$ | Specific Energy Consumption | kWh/unit |
| 3 | $\text{EI} = \dfrac{E_{\text{primary}}}{\text{GDP}}$ | Energy Intensity | MJ/\$ or toe/\$ |
| 4 | $\text{Energy Cost} = E \times R$ | Annual energy expenditure | ₹/year |
| 5 | $\text{SPP} = \dfrac{I}{\text{Annual Savings}}$ | Simple Payback Period | years |
| 6 | $\text{Savings} \% = \dfrac{E_{\text{before}} - E_{\text{after}}}{E_{\text{before}}} \times 100$ | Percentage energy savings | % |
| 7 | $Q = U \times A \times \Delta T \times t$ | Heat transfer through building envelope (HVAC) | kWh or MJ |
| 8 | $\text{Boiler Efficiency} \eta = \dfrac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{Q_{\text{fuel}}} \times 100$ | Thermal efficiency of boiler/furnace | % |
| 9 | $\text{Power} = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \cos\phi$ | Three-phase electrical power | kW |
| 10 | $\text{Motor Loading} \% = \dfrac{P_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\text{rated}}} \times 100$ | Motor load fraction | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Use $\vert x \vert$ (latex vertical bar) for absolute value — never the pipe character `$\vert x \vert$` written as raw `\|x\|` — inside table cells, as raw pipes break markdown table syntax.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding sectoral energy consumption enables:

- **National Energy Planning** — designing generation capacity mix.
- **Policy Targeting** — e.g., India's UJALA LED program targets the **Domestic lighting** end-use.
- **Demand-Side Management (DSM)** — load shifting away from peak hours.
- **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme** by BEE — applied to **Energy-Intensive Industries**.
- **Building Energy Codes (ECBC)** — applied to the **Commercial sector**.
- **Star Labeling Program** — applied to **appliances in Domestic sector**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Annual Energy Consumption of a Domestic Household

**Problem Context:** A typical urban Indian household uses the following appliances:

- 4 LED bulbs × 9 W × 6 hours/day
- 3 Ceiling fans × 75 W × 12 hours/day
- 1 Refrigerator × 150 W × 24 hours/day (compressor runs ~40 % of the time)
- 1 Air Conditioner (1.5 tonne) × 1500 W × 6 hours/day
- 1 Water Geyser × 2000 W × 1 hour/day
- 1 Television × 100 W × 5 hours/day

**Step 1 — Calculate daily energy consumption per appliance using** $E = P \times t$:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{LED}} &= 4 \times 9\,\text{W} \times 6\,\text{h} = 216\,\text{Wh/day} \\
E_{\text{Fan}} &= 3 \times 75\,\text{W} \times 12\,\text{h} = 2700\,\text{Wh/day} \\
E_{\text{Fridge}} &= 1 \times 150\,\text{W} \times 24\,\text{h} \times 0.40 = 1440\,\text{Wh/day} \\
E_{\text{AC}} &= 1 \times 1500\,\text{W} \times 6\,\text{h} = 9000\,\text{Wh/day} \\
E_{\text{Geyser}} &= 1 \times 2000\,\text{W} \times 1\,\text{h} = 2000\,\text{Wh/day} \\
E_{\text{TV}} &= 1 \times 100\,\text{W} \times 5\,\text{h} = 500\,\text{Wh/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Sum daily consumption:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{daily}} &= 216 + 2700 + 1440 + 9000 + 2000 + 500 \\
&= 15856\,\text{Wh/day} = 15.856\,\text{kWh/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to annual consumption:**

$$E_{\text{annual}} = 15.856 \times 365 = 5787.44\,\text{kWh/year}$$

**Step 4 — End-use percentage breakdown:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\%_{\text{AC}} &= \frac{9000}{15856} \times 100 = 56.76\,\% \\
\%_{\text{Fan}} &= \frac{2700}{15856} \times 100 = 17.03\,\% \\
\%_{\text{Geyser}} &= \frac{2000}{15856} \times 100 = 12.61\,\% \\
\%_{\text{Fridge}} &= \frac{1440}{15856} \times 100 = 9.08\,\% \\
\%_{\text{TV}} &= \frac{500}{15856} \times 100 = 3.15\,\% \\
\%_{\text{LED}} &= \frac{216}{15856} \times 100 = 1.36\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Analytical Insight:** The air conditioner alone consumes **more than half (≈ 57 %)** of household electricity — confirming the KTU observation that **space cooling is the dominant domestic end-use**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) in an Industrial Plant

**Problem Context:** A cement plant produces **1,20,000 tonnes** of clinker per year and consumes **9,00,000 kWh** of electrical energy and **2,50,000 m³** of natural gas (calorific value **35 MJ/m³**).

**Step 1 — Compute electrical energy in MJ:**

$$E_{\text{elec}} = 9,00,000 \times 3.6 = 32,40,000\,\text{MJ}$$

**Step 2 — Compute thermal energy from gas:**

$$E_{\text{gas}} = 2,50,000 \times 35 = 87,50,000\,\text{MJ}$$

**Step 3 — Total primary energy:**

$$E_{\text{total}} = 32,40,000 + 87,50,000 = 1,19,90,000\,\text{MJ}$$

**Step 4 — Specific Energy Consumption:**

$$\text{SEC} = \frac{1,19,90,000}{1,20,000} = 99.92\,\text{MJ/tonne of clinker}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to GCal/tonne for benchmark reporting:**

$$\text{SEC} = \frac{99.92}{4184} \approx 0.0239\,\text{GCal/tonne}$$

> [!NOTE]
