> **Benchmark Reference:** Best-practice SEC for modern Indian cement plants is in the range of **0.70 to 0.80 GCal/tonne of clinker** for thermal energy alone; the electrical SEC is typically around **90–110 kWh/tonne**. The figure computed above is indicative of an aggregated view.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Simple Payback Period for an Industrial Retrofit

**Problem Context:** A factory replaces 10 induction motors (each 15 kW) with energy-efficient IE3 motors. Cost per motor = ₹25,000. Old motor efficiency = 88 %, new motor efficiency = 93 %. Annual operating hours = 5000. Electricity tariff = ₹7/kWh.

**Step 1 — Power loss in old motors:**

$$P_{\text{loss,old}} = 10 \times 15 \times \left(\frac{1}{0.88} - 1\right) = 150 \times 0.1364 = 20.45\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 2 — Power loss in new motors:**

$$P_{\text{loss,new}} = 10 \times 15 \times \left(\frac{1}{0.93} - 1\right) = 150 \times 0.0753 = 11.29\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Annual energy savings:**

$$\Delta E = (20.45 - 11.29) \times 5000 = 45,800\,\text{kWh/year}$$

**Step 4 — Annual monetary savings:**

$$S = 45,800 \times 7 = \text{₹ }3,20,600/\text{year}$$

**Step 5 — Total investment:**

$$I = 10 \times 25,000 = \text{₹ }2,50,000$$

**Step 6 — Simple Payback Period:**

$$\text{SPP} = \frac{2,50,000}{3,20,600} \approx 0.78\,\text{years} \approx 9.4\,\text{months}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Conclusion:** Industrial motor retrofits are among the **fastest-paying** energy conservation measures, justifying BEE's strong push under the PAT scheme.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sectoral Energy Flow — National Consumption Architecture

The diagram below maps the hierarchical structure of national energy consumption as studied under the KTU 2024 Module 1 framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[National Total Energy Consumption] --> nodeB[Domestic Sector]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Industrial Sector]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Commercial Sector]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Agriculture Sector]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Transportation Sector]

    nodeB --> nodeB1[Lighting]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Space Cooling Fans AC]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Refrigeration]
    nodeB --> nodeB4[Water Heating]
    nodeB --> nodeB5[Cooking Appliances]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[Process Heat Boilers Furnaces]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Motor Driven Systems Pumps Fans]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Compressed Air]
    nodeC --> nodeC4[Lighting and HVAC]
    nodeC --> nodeC5[Electrochemical Processes]

    nodeD --> nodeD1[HVAC Systems]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Commercial Lighting]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Plug Loads IT Equipment]
    nodeD --> nodeD4[Refrigeration Cold Chain]

    nodeE --> nodeE1[Irrigation Pumps]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Farm Machinery Diesel]
    nodeE --> nodeE3[Cold Storage and Post Harvest]

    nodeF --> nodeF1[Road Transport Diesel Petrol]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[Railways Diesel and Electric]
    nodeF --> nodeF3[Aviation and Shipping]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Domestic Sector End-Use Monitoring

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputMains[Grid Supply and Tariff Meter] --> distBox[Distribution Board]
    distBox --> ledLoad[LED and CFL Loads]
    distBox --> fanLoad[Ceiling Fan and AC Load]
    distBox --> heatLoad[Geyser and Induction Load]
    distBox --> fridgeLoad[Refrigerator and Electronics]
    ledLoad --> meter1[Sub Energy Meter]
    fanLoad --> meter2[Sub Energy Meter]
    heatLoad --> meter3[Sub Energy Meter]
    fridgeLoad --> meter4[Sub Energy Meter]
    meter1 --> audit[Energy Audit Dashboard]
    meter2 --> audit
    meter3 --> audit
    meter4 --> audit
    audit --> report[Sectoral End Use Report]
```

### 4.3 Industrial Sector Decision Logic for Energy Conservation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Industrial Energy Audit Trigger] --> collectGather[Collect Monthly Electricity and Fuel Bills]
    collectGather --> breakDown[End Use Breakdown Motors Boilers HVAC]
    breakDown --> rankLoad[Rank Loads by Annual kWh and Cost]
    rankLoad --> identifyMajor[Identify Top Three Energy Intensive Loads]
    identifyMajor --> evalTech[Evaluate Retrofit Options VFD Heat Recovery Star Motors]
    evalTech --> calcPayback[Compute Simple Payback and ROI]
    calcPayback --> decision{Payback less than 2 years}
    decision --> yesImpl[Implement Retrofit Immediately]
    decision --> noDefer[Defer or Seek Subsidies]
    yesImpl --> verifyMonitor[Install Sub Metering and Verify Savings]
    noDefer --> reviewCycle[Re-evaluate at Next Audit Cycle]
    verifyMonitor --> reviewCycle
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a functional stage in the audit and conservation workflow. In KTU examinations, such block diagrams are commonly asked as **3-mark short questions** in Part A, with marks awarded for correctly identifying the inputs, processes, and outputs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*List the major end-uses of energy in the Indian domestic sector and identify which end-use has the highest growth rate.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The major end-uses in the domestic sector are:
1. Lighting
2. Space cooling (fans and air conditioners)
3. Refrigeration
4. Water heating (geysers)
5. Cooking appliances
6. Entertainment and electronics

Among these, **space cooling (air conditioning)** has the **highest growth rate** due to rising disposable incomes, falling AC prices, increasing urban heat island effects, and rising ambient temperatures across India. According to BEE and IEA reports, AC stock in Indian households is projected to grow at a compound annual growth rate exceeding **10 %**, making it the **dominant and fastest-growing residential load**.

*Mark distribution:* 1 mark for listing end-uses, 1 mark for identifying the fastest-growing load, 1 mark for justifying with a valid reason.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
*Define Specific Energy Consumption (SEC). Why is it a more meaningful metric than total energy consumption for industrial benchmarking?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** is defined as the energy consumed per unit of physical output produced. For example, **kWh per tonne of cement** or **MJ per kg of steel**.

**Why SEC is more meaningful:**
1. **Normalization:** SEC removes the effect of production volume, allowing fair comparison between plants of different sizes.
2. **Benchmarking:** Industries can compare their SEC against national/international best-practice values.
3. **Trend Tracking:** A plant can monitor its own SEC over years to track efficiency improvements, even when output varies.
4. **Policy Targeting:** Government schemes like PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) by BEE set SEC reduction targets rather than absolute reduction targets.

*Mark distribution:* 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for justification points.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Module 1 Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*(a)* Classify the major sectors of energy consumption in a national economy. Explain in detail the end-use breakdown of the **industrial sector**, highlighting the top three energy-intensive sub-systems and the typical percentage share of each. (7 marks)

*(b)* A manufacturing unit consumes **5,00,000 kWh/year** of electrical energy and **2,00,000 kg/year** of furnace oil (calorific value = 42 MJ/kg). The annual production is **1,000 tonnes** of finished product. Calculate:
- (i) The total primary energy in GJ.
- (ii) The Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) in MJ/tonne.
- (iii) The percentage share of electrical vs thermal energy.
- (iv) If the electrical SEC is to be reduced by 10 % through VFD installation on motors (costing ₹3,00,000 with annual savings of ₹90,000), determine the **Simple Payback Period**. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The major sectors are: Domestic, Industrial, Commercial, Agriculture, and Transportation.

In the **industrial sector**, the end-use breakdown is:

| End-Use Sub-System | Typical Share |
|---|---|
| Process Heat (boilers, furnaces, kilns, ovens) | 50 % – 70 % |
| Motor-Driven Systems (pumps, fans, compressors) | 20 % – 30 % |
| HVAC and Space Conditioning | 5 % – 10 % |
| Lighting | 2 % – 5 % |
| Electrochemical / Electrolytic | Sector-specific |

The **top three energy-intensive sub-systems** are:
1. **Process Heat Generation** — The largest share, since most process industries rely on raising steam or generating hot gases. **Boiler efficiency improvement** offers 5 %–15 % fuel savings.
2. **Motor-Driven Systems** — Motors consume a major share across all industries. Replacing standard motors with **IE3/IE4 premium efficiency motors** and installing **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** on variable-torque loads (fans, pumps) yields 20 %–50 % savings.
3. **Compressed Air Systems** — Often called the "fourth utility," compressed air is notoriously inefficient, with typical system efficiencies of only 10 %–15 %. Leak reduction alone can save 20 %–30 % of compressor energy.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Total primary energy in GJ:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{elec}} &= 5,00,000 \times 3.6 = 18,00,000\,\text{MJ} \\
E_{\text{thermal}} &= 2,00,000 \times 42 = 84,00,000\,\text{MJ} \\
E_{\text{total}} &= 18,00,000 + 84,00,000 = 1,02,00,000\,\text{MJ} = 10,200\,\text{GJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Specific Energy Consumption:**

$$\text{SEC} = \frac{1,02,00,000}{1000} = 10,200\,\text{MJ/tonne}$$

**(iii) Percentage share of electrical vs thermal:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\%_{\text{elec}} &= \frac{18,00,000}{1,02,00,000} \times 100 \approx 17.65\,\% \\
\%_{\text{thermal}} &= \frac{84,00,000}{1,02,00,000} \times 100 \approx 82.35\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Simple Payback Period:**

$$\text{SPP} = \frac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}} = \frac{3,00,000}{90,000} = 3.33\,\text{years}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Module 1 Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*(a)* Explain in detail the end-use energy consumption pattern in the **domestic (residential) sector** in India. Discuss the major energy conservation opportunities with at least two specific examples per end-use. (7 marks)

*(b)* A city has **50,000 households**. Each household consumes an average of **120 kWh/month** at a tariff of **₹6.50/kWh**. The municipal authority plans to launch an LED replacement program: replacing 4 conventional 60 W bulbs with 4 LED 9 W bulbs per household. Assume 6 hours of daily use.
- (i) Calculate the city-wide annual energy savings in kWh.
- (ii) Calculate the city-wide annual monetary savings in ₹.
- (iii) If the total program cost is ₹2 crore, determine the payback period in months.
- (iv) Comment on the environmental impact in terms of avoided CO₂ emissions, taking the grid emission factor as **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh**. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The Indian domestic sector exhibits a distinctive end-use pattern shaped by climate, income level, and appliance ownership:

**1. Lighting (≈ 20 % – 25 %):**
- Conservation opportunities:
  - Replacement of incandescent bulbs with **LED bulbs** (saves 80 % energy per lumen).
  - Use of **daylight sensors** and **occupancy sensors** in rooms.

**2. Space Cooling (Fans + ACs) (≈ 35 % – 45 %, and growing):**
- Conservation opportunities:
  - Setting AC thermostat to **24 °C** (BEE recommendation) saves 6 % electricity per degree raised.
  - Use of **BEE 5-star rated ACs** with inverter technology.
  - Replacement of conventional fans with **BLDC fans** (saves 50 % energy).

**3. Refrigeration (≈ 15 %):**
- Conservation opportunities:
  - Defrosting coils regularly and maintaining door seals.
  - Setting temperature to **4 °C** in fridge and **–18 °C** in freezer.

**4. Water Heating (≈ 10 % – 15 %):**
- Conservation opportunities:
  - Installing **solar water heaters** (offsets 100 % electrical heating).
  - Insulating geyser storage tanks and pipes.

**5. Cooking and Other (≈ 10 %):**
- Conservation opportunities:
  - Using **pressure cookers** instead of open pans.
  - Using **induction cooktops** in place of electric coil heaters (≈ 50 % more efficient).

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) City-wide annual energy savings:**

Power reduction per household per bulb:
$$\Delta P = 60 - 9 = 51\,\text{W per bulb}$$
Power reduction per household (4 bulbs):
$$\Delta P_{\text{HH}} = 4 \times 51 = 204\,\text{W}$$

Daily energy savings per household:
$$\Delta E_{\text{HH}} = 204 \times 6 = 1224\,\text{Wh/day} = 1.224\,\text{kWh/day}$$

Annual savings per household:
$$\Delta E_{\text{HH,annual}} = 1.224 \times 365 = 446.76\,\text{kWh/year}$$

City-wide annual savings:
$$\Delta E_{\text{city}} = 50,000 \times 446.76 = 2,23,38,000\,\text{kWh/year}$$

**(ii) City-wide annual monetary savings:**

$$S_{\text{city}} = 2,23,38,000 \times 6.50 = \text{₹ }14,51,97,000 \approx \text{₹ }14.52\,\text{crore/year}$$

**(iii) Payback period:**

$$\text{SPP} = \frac{2,00,00,000}{14,51,97,000} = 0.1377\,\text{years} \approx 1.65\,\text{months}$$

**(iv) Environmental impact — avoided CO₂ emissions:**

$$\text{CO}_2\,\text{avoided} = 2,23,38,000 \times 0.82 = 1,83,17,160\,\text{kg/year} \approx 18,317\,\text{tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** The LED retrofit program offers an extraordinarily short payback (~1.65 months) and avoids approximately **18,317 tonnes of CO₂ emissions annually** — equivalent to the carbon sequestration of roughly **3 lakh mature trees** per year. This explains why the Government of India's UJALA program has distributed over **36 crore LED bulbs** as of recent years.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often forget to convert **kWh to MJ** when calculating total primary energy. **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ** — memorize this conversion.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** In payback period problems, students confuse **annual monetary savings** with **annual energy savings**. Always multiply by tariff to get the monetary figure.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** In end-use percentage problems, students forget to include the duty cycle (e.g., refrigerator compressor runs only ~40 % of the time). Applying 24 hours × full wattage inflates the answer by 2.5×.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Students often quote **"specific energy consumption"** without specifying the **denominator unit**. Always write **kWh/tonne**, **MJ/kg**, etc.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** In motor efficiency problems, the formula **P_input = P_output / η** must be used carefully. Power loss = (P_output / η) − P_output = P_output × (1 − η)/η.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Five sectors:** Domestic, Industrial, Commercial, Agriculture, Transportation — memorize the relative shares.
- **Domestic sector dominant end-use:** Space cooling (AC + fan) — **~40 % to 55 %** in urban households.
- **Industrial sector dominant end-use:** Process heat (50 %–70 %) and motor-driven systems (20 %–30 %).
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** Always normalize energy by output (tonnes, kg, units) — this is the **single most important metric** in industrial energy management.
- **Energy Intensity (EI):** Total energy per unit of GDP — used for **national-level** comparison.
- **Conversion constants to memorize:**
  - **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ**
  - **1 toe = 41.868 GJ**
  - **1 cal = 4.184 J**
- **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** is the apex Indian body for energy efficiency policy and star labeling.
- **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme applies to **energy-intensive industries** (8 sectors initially: Aluminium, Cement, Chlor-Alkali, Fertilizer, Iron & Steel, Paper & Pulp, Thermal Power, Textiles).
- **Star labeling** applies to appliances — higher stars = lower electricity consumption.
- **UJALA program** targets domestic lighting through LED distribution — a flagship DSM initiative.
- **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code)** applies to commercial buildings with connected load ≥ 100 kW or contract demand ≥ 120 kVA.
- **Three-phase power formula:** $P = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \cos\phi$ — applicable wherever industrial motors are discussed.
- **Motor retrofit savings** are typically the fastest-paying energy conservation measure — payback often less than 2 years.
- **Refrigerator duty cycle:** assume **40 %** unless otherwise stated in the problem.
- **Lighting retrofit (incandescent → LED):** saves **~80 %** wattage per equivalent lumen output.
- **AC thermostat setting of 24 °C** is the BEE-recommended optimal — every 1 °C lower setting adds ~6 % to electricity consumption.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Needs of Growing Economy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Needs of Growing Economy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Needs of a Growing Economy** refers to the systematic study of the relationship between a nation's **economic growth (measured by GDP)** and its corresponding **energy demand** across all sectors — industrial, agricultural, domestic, commercial, and transport — over a defined planning horizon.

The energy sector is widely recognized as the **lifeline of any modern economy**. A growing economy requires more energy to:
- Power industries and manufacturing units
- Run transportation networks
- Support urbanization and modern lifestyles
- Drive agricultural mechanization
- Sustain digital and service-based infrastructure

The energy demand of a nation is a function of:
$$\text{Energy Demand} = f(\text{GDP}, \text{Population}, \text{Industrialization}, \text{Urbanization}, \text{Technology}, \text{Lifestyle})$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Energy as Food for the Economy"**

Think of an economy as a **human body**. Just as the body needs calories (food) to perform work, grow, and stay alive, an economy needs **energy (in all forms)** to produce goods, deliver services, and expand. 

- **Calorie Intake** ↔ **Total Energy Consumption** (in Joules / TOE)
- **Body Weight / Activity** ↔ **GDP** (economic output)
- **Metabolism Efficiency** ↔ **Energy Intensity** (energy used per unit GDP)

A growing economy is like a growing child — it eats more food, demands better quality nutrition, and the diet becomes more diverse. Similarly, a growing economy demands **more energy**, of **higher quality** (cleaner, more reliable), and from a **diverse mix** of sources (renewable, nuclear, fossil).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Energy Units (Bold for KTU Emphasis)**
> - **1 TOE (Tonne of Oil Equivalent) = 41.868 GJ = 11.63 MWh**
> - **1 Mtoe = 4.1868 × 10¹⁵ J**
> - **1 Quad = 1.055 × 10¹⁸ J = 25.2 Mtoe**
> - **GDP per capita** is measured in USD (constant prices)
> - **Energy Intensity** is measured in **TOE per million USD of GDP**

### 1.4 India at a Glance (KTU Board Data Point)

> [!NOTE]
> As per India's energy outlook, India is the **world's 3rd largest energy consumer** after China and the USA. India's primary energy demand is projected to grow at **~4–5% CAGR**, making it a critical case study in the KTU syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Economy–Energy Coupling Model

The relationship between economic growth and energy consumption can be analyzed using **four hypotheses** widely tested in KTU-relevant literature:

1. **Neutrality Hypothesis:** No causality between energy and GDP.
2. **Conservation Hypothesis:** GDP growth causes energy consumption (GDP → Energy).
3. **Growth Hypothesis:** Energy consumption causes GDP growth (Energy → GDP).
4. **Feedback Hypothesis:** Bidirectional causality (Energy ↔ GDP).

### 2.2 Why Growing Economies Need More Energy

A growing economy needs more energy because of these **structural drivers**:

- **Industrialization:** Manufacturing heavy industries (steel, cement, chemicals) are energy-intensive.
- **Urbanization:** Cities consume more electricity per capita than rural areas.
- **Motorization:** Rise in private vehicles increases petroleum demand.
- **Electrification:** Expansion of electricity access to rural households (e.g., India's Saubhagya scheme).
- **Rising Middle-Class Aspirations:** Air conditioners, refrigerators, and electronics drive peak demand.
- **Digital Economy:** Data centers, cloud computing, and AI workloads are power-hungry.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Units** | **Engineering Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Intensity (EI) | $EI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{GDP}$ | TOE / million USD | Measures economy's energy efficiency |
| Per Capita Energy | $E_{pc} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{Population}$ | TOE / person | Compares standard of living |
| GDP from Energy | $GDP = \dfrac{E_{total}}{EI}$ | Million USD | Projects GDP if energy is known |
| Energy Elasticity | $\epsilon = \dfrac{\%\Delta E}{\%\Delta GDP}$ | Dimensionless | Predicts future demand |
| Compound Growth | $E_{n} = E_{0} \cdot (1+r)^{n}$ | TOE | Long-term demand projection |
| Sectoral Share | $S_{i} = \dfrac{E_{i}}{E_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Energy mix planning |
| Carbon Intensity | $CI = \dfrac{CO_2}{GDP}$ | kg CO₂ / USD | Environmental policy metric |
| Population Projection | $P_{n} = P_{0} \cdot e^{rt}$ | Persons | Demand forecasting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition for Elasticity (KTU Favourite):**
> - If $\epsilon > 1$ → Economy is **energy-intensive** (heavy industry-driven)
> - If $\epsilon = 1$ → Energy grows **proportionally** with GDP
> - If $\epsilon < 1$ → Economy is becoming **energy-efficient** (service-driven)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Long-Term Energy Planning:** Government bodies like CEA (Central Electricity Authority) and NITI Aayog use these models to forecast India's energy demand up to 2070.
- **Investment Decisions:** Power plants, refineries, and grid infrastructure are sized using demand projections.
- **Policy Making:** Subsidies, taxes, and renewable targets are derived from elasticity analysis.
- **Sustainability Reporting:** Carbon intensity metrics feed into NDCs (Nationally Determined Contributions) under the Paris Agreement.

### 2.5 The IEA's Energy Ladder Concept

As percapita income rises, the energy mix shifts up the **energy ladder**:

$$\text{Biomass} \rightarrow \text{Coal} \rightarrow \text{Oil} \rightarrow \text{Natural Gas} \rightarrow \text{Electricity} \rightarrow \text{Renewables/Nuclear}$$

This is why developing nations like India show high coal dependence, while developed economies are transitioning to renewables.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Long-Term Energy Demand Projection Using Compound Growth

**Problem Setup:** A country consumed **500 Mtoe** in the year 2024. If the economy is expected to grow at **6% per annum** and energy elasticity is **0.8**, what is the projected energy demand in **2034** (10 years)?

**Step 1 — Identify the energy growth rate from elasticity:**
$$\epsilon = \frac{\%\Delta E}{\%\Delta GDP}$$

$$\text{Energy Growth Rate (}r\text{)} = \epsilon \times \text{GDP Growth Rate}$$

$$r = 0.8 \times 6\% = 4.8\% \text{ per annum}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the compound growth formula:**
$$E_{n} = E_{0} \cdot (1 + r)^{n}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**
$$E_{2034} = 500 \cdot (1 + 0.048)^{10}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the growth factor:**
$$\begin{aligned}
(1.048)^{10} &= e^{10 \cdot \ln(1.048)} \\
&= e^{10 \cdot 0.04688} \\
&= e^{0.4688} \\
&= 1.5980
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Final Demand:**
$$E_{2034} = 500 \times 1.5980 = 799.0 \text{ Mtoe}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The country's energy demand will rise by **59.8% in a decade**, requiring massive capacity additions — a typical KTU ESE 14-mark scenario question.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: GDP Estimation Using Energy Intensity

**Problem Setup:** A state's total energy consumption is **40 Mtoe**. The state's energy intensity is **0.25 TOE per million USD of GDP**. Calculate the state's GDP.

**Step 1 — Recall the Energy Intensity formula:**
$$EI = \frac{E_{total}}{GDP}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for GDP:**
$$GDP = \frac{E_{total}}{EI}$$

**Step 3 — Unit conversion:**
$$E_{total} = 40 \text{ Mtoe} = 40 \times 10^6 \text{ TOE}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values:**
$$\begin{aligned}
GDP &= \frac{40 \times 10^6 \text{ TOE}}{0.25 \text{ TOE per million USD}} \\
GDP &= 160 \times 10^6 \text{ million USD} \\
GDP &= 160{,}000 \text{ million USD} \\
GDP &= 160 \text{ Billion USD}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Per Capita Energy and Standard of Living

**Problem Setup:** India in 2025 has a population of **1.44 billion** and total energy consumption of **900 Mtoe**. Compare with USA: population **335 million**, energy consumption **2,200 Mtoe**.

**Step 1 — India's per capita energy:**
$$\begin{aligned}
E_{pc,India} &= \frac{900 \times 10^6 \text{ TOE}}{1.44 \times 10^9 \text{ persons}} \\
&= 0.625 \text{ TOE/person}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — USA's per capita energy:**
$$\begin{aligned}
E_{pc,USA} &= \frac{2{,}200 \times 10^6 \text{ TOE}}{335 \times 10^6 \text{ persons}} \\
&= 6.567 \text{ TOE/person}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Ratio:**
$$\frac{E_{pc,USA}}{E_{pc,India}} = \frac{6.567}{0.625} \approx 10.5$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** An average American consumes **~10.5× more energy** than an average Indian. This gap represents the **"Energy Convergence Potential"** — the latent energy demand that India must meet to reach developed-nation living standards.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Sectoral Energy Mix Analysis

**Problem Setup:** A country has the following sectoral energy consumption (Mtoe): Industry = 250, Transport = 150, Domestic = 80, Agriculture = 50, Commercial = 70. Calculate sectoral shares.

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= 250 + 150 + 80 + 50 + 70 = 600 \text{ Mtoe} \\
S_{Industry} &= \frac{250}{600} \times 100 = 41.67\% \\
S_{Transport} &= \frac{150}{600} \times 100 = 25.00\% \\
S_{Domestic} &= \frac{80}{600} \times 100 = 13.33\% \\
S_{Agriculture} &= \frac{50}{600} \times 100 = 8.33\% \\
S_{Commercial} &= \frac{70}{600} \times 100 = 11.67\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Check:** $\sum S_{i} = 100\%$ ✅
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Energy–Economy Coupling Flow

This block diagram illustrates how GDP growth drives sectoral energy demand, which feeds back into economic expansion.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Population Growth] --> B[Economic Expansion GDP]
    C[Urbanization] --> B
    D[Industrialization] --> B
    E[Technology Advancement] --> B
    B --> F[Industrial Energy Demand]
    B --> G[Transport Energy Demand]
    B --> H[Domestic Energy Demand]
    B --> I[Agricultural Energy Demand]
    B --> J[Commercial Energy Demand]
    F --> K[Total Primary Energy Supply]
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Energy Security Concerns]
    K --> M[Environmental Impact CO2 Emissions]
    L --> N[Energy Policy and Planning]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Energy Efficiency Programs]
    N --> P[Renewable Energy Push]
    O --> B
    P --> B
```

### 4.2 Sectoral Energy Demand Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Energy Demand of Growing Economy] --> B[Industrial Sector]
    A --> C[Transport Sector]
    A --> D[Domestic Sector]
    A --> E[Agricultural Sector]
    A --> F[Commercial Sector]
    B --> B1[Steel and Cement]
    B --> B2[Chemicals and Petrochemicals]
    B --> B3[Manufacturing]
    C --> C1[Road Transport]
    C --> C2[Railways]
    C --> C3[Aviation and Shipping]
    D --> D1[Lighting and Appliances]
    D --> D2[Cooking and Heating]
    D --> D3[Space Cooling]
    E --> E1[Pumping Sets]
    E --> E2[Tractors and Harvesters]
    F --> F1[Office Buildings]
    F --> F2[Hospitality and Retail]
```

### 4.3 The Energy Ladder Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Level 1 Biomass and Wood] -->|Rising Income| B[Level 2 Coal]
    B -->|Industrialization| C[Level 3 Oil and Petroleum]
    C -->|Urbanization| D[Level 4 Natural Gas]
    D -->|Electrification| E[Level 5 Grid Electricity]
    E -->|Sustainability Goals| F[Level 6 Renewables and Nuclear]
```

### 4.4 Energy Forecasting Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Historical Data Collection] --> B[Identify Economic Indicators]
    B --> C[Compute Energy Intensity]
    C --> D[Estimate Elasticity Coefficient]
    D --> E[Apply Demand Projection Models]
    E --> F[Linear Regression Method]
    E --> G[End Use Method]
    E --> H[Econometric Method]
    F --> I[Validate Against Past Trends]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Generate Long Term Energy Forecast]
    J --> K[Capacity Planning and Policy]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term **Energy Intensity** and explain its significance in assessing the energy performance of a growing economy.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Energy Intensity (EI) is defined as the amount of energy consumed per unit of Gross Domestic Product (GDP).

$$EI = \frac{\text{Total Energy Consumption (TOE)}}{\text{GDP (Million USD)}}$$

**Significance (2 Marks):**
- A **low EI** indicates an energy-efficient economy (more output per unit of energy input), typically associated with service-driven economies like Japan or the UK.
- A **high EI** signals an energy-inefficient, industrial-heavy economy (more energy used to produce one unit of GDP), as seen in China or India.
- EI is a key indicator used by the **World Bank** and **IEA** to rank economies.
- A **declining EI over time** is the global benchmark of sustainable growth — decoupling economic output from energy consumption.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Explain the **Energy Ladder** concept with reference to developing economies.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Energy Ladder Hypothesis** (proposed by Leach, 1992) describes how household and national energy choices evolve with rising income:

1. **Lowest Rung — Traditional Biomass:** Rural poor use firewood, dung, and agricultural residues for cooking.
2. **Middle Rungs — Coal, Kerosene, LPG:** As incomes rise, transition to commercial fuels.
3. **Higher Rungs — Electricity and Piped Gas:** Urbanization drives shift to cleaner, more convenient energy.
4. **Top Rung — Renewables and Clean Energy:** Sustainability concerns push adoption of solar, wind, and hydrogen.

For India, the **Pradhan Mantri Ujjwala Yojana (PMUY)** is a real-world application of accelerating movement up the energy ladder by providing LPG to rural households, displacing biomass.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

## ✦ QUESTION A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]

**(a)** With the help of suitable formulas, derive the relationship between **GDP, Energy Consumption, and Energy Intensity**. Discuss how energy elasticity of demand is used in long-term energy planning. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A country consumed 600 Mtoe of energy in 2020. If its GDP grows at 7% per annum and energy elasticity is 0.75, project the energy demand for 2030. Also calculate the per capita consumption if the population grows from 1.2 billion to 1.35 billion by 2030. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Fundamental Relationship (2 Marks):**
The relationship between GDP, energy consumption, and energy intensity is mathematically expressed as:

$$EI = \frac{E}{GDP} \quad \Rightarrow \quad E = EI \times GDP \quad \Rightarrow \quad GDP = \frac{E}{EI}$$

where,
- $E$ = Total primary energy consumption (in TOE)
- $GDP$ = Gross Domestic Product (in Million USD)
- $EI$ = Energy Intensity (TOE / Million USD)

**Step 2 — Concept of Energy Elasticity (2 Marks):**
Energy elasticity of demand $\epsilon$ measures the responsiveness of energy consumption to changes in GDP:

$$\epsilon = \frac{\%\Delta E}{\%\Delta GDP} = \frac{\Delta E / E}{\Delta GDP / GDP}$$

If GDP grows at $g\%$ per year and elasticity is $\epsilon$, then energy growth rate is:

$$r = \epsilon \times g\%$$

**Step 3 — Use in Long-Term Planning (3 Marks):**

| **Elasticity Range** | **Interpretation** | **Planning Strategy** |
|---|---|---|
| $\epsilon > 1$ | Energy-intensive economy | Build large baseload capacity (coal, nuclear) |
| $\epsilon = 1$ | Proportional growth | Match capacity addition with GDP growth |
| $\epsilon < 1$ | Energy-efficient economy | Focus on renewables, efficiency, and DSM |

Energy elasticity is used by planning bodies like **CEA, NITI Aayog, and IEA** to:
- Forecast demand 20–50 years ahead
- Decide **fuel mix** (coal vs gas vs renewables)
- Justify **energy infrastructure investments**
- Set **efficiency targets** under Perform, Achieve & Trade (PAT) scheme

---

### Part (b) — Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute Energy Growth Rate (1 Mark):**
$$r = \epsilon \times g = 0.75 \times 7\% = 5.25\% \text{ per annum}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Compound Growth Formula (3 Marks):**
$$E_{n} = E_{0} \cdot (1+r)^{n}$$

$$E_{2030} = 600 \times (1.0525)^{10}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Growth Factor (2 Marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
(1.0525)^{10} &= e^{10 \times \ln(1.0525)} \\
&= e^{10 \times 0.05115} \\
&= e^{0.5115} \\
&\approx 1.6677
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Final Energy Demand (1 Mark):**
$$E_{2030} = 600 \times 1.6677 \approx 1000.6 \text{ Mtoe}$$

**Step 5 — Per Capita Consumption in 2030:**
$$\begin{aligned}
E_{pc,2030} &= \frac{1000.6 \times 10^6 \text{ TOE}}{1.35 \times 10^9 \text{ persons}} \\
&\approx 0.741 \text{ TOE/person}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## ✦ QUESTION B — (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **causality hypotheses** between energy consumption and economic growth. Discuss their implications for energy policy in a developing country like India. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A state's industrial sector consumes 300 Mtoe and has GDP contribution of 1,000 Billion USD. The domestic sector consumes 100 Mtoe for 50 million people. Compare the **energy intensity of industry** and **per capita domestic energy use**, and comment on which sector offers more conservation potential. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — The Four Causality Hypotheses (4 Marks):**

| **Hypothesis** | **Direction** | **Meaning** | **Policy Implication** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Neutrality | No link | Energy and GDP are independent | No need for energy planning linked to growth |
| Conservation | GDP → Energy | Economic growth causes energy use | Focus on supply-side expansion |
| Growth | Energy → GDP | Energy drives growth | Prioritize energy availability |
| Feedback | Energy ↔ GDP | Bidirectional causality | Balance supply and efficiency policies |

**Step 2 — Implications for India (3 Marks):**

For India, empirical studies (e.g., Kumar & Managi, 2010; Toman & Jempa, 2001) mostly support the **Feedback Hypothesis**:
- Without adequate energy, GDP growth stalls → so supply expansion (e.g., 500 GW renewable target by 2030) is essential.
- But unbridled growth in energy hurts the environment → so demand-side management, BEE star labelling, and ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) are equally critical.
- India follows a **dual strategy**: aggressive capacity addition + efficiency standards.

---

### Part (b) — Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Industrial Energy Intensity (2 Marks):**
$$GDP_{industry} = 1000 \text{ Billion USD} = 10^6 \text{ Million USD}$$

$$EI_{industry} = \frac{300 \times 10^6 \text{ TOE}}{10^6 \text{ Million USD}} = 0.30 \text{ TOE/Million USD}$$

**Step 2 — Domestic Per Capita Energy (2 Marks):**
$$E_{pc,domestic} = \frac{100 \times 10^6 \text{ TOE}}{50 \times 10^6 \text{ persons}} = 2.0 \text{ TOE/person}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison and Conservation Analysis (3 Marks):**

| **Sector** | **Metric** | **Value** | **Conservation Strategy** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Industrial | Energy Intensity | 0.30 TOE/M USD | Waste heat recovery, efficient motors, cogeneration |
| Domestic | Per Capita Energy | 2.0 TOE/person | LED lighting, BEE appliances, building codes |

> **Conclusion:** Both sectors offer massive conservation potential. However, **industry has larger absolute savings potential** (process heat accounts for ~50% of industrial energy), while the **domestic sector has higher marginal units saved per rupee invested** due to scalable interventions like LED bulbs and star-rated ACs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Always convert Mtoe → TOE (multiply by $10^6$) when computing per capita or intensity. Many students lose 2 marks by mixing units.
> 2. **Elasticity Sign Error:** A **declining** EI does NOT mean energy demand is falling — it means **energy grows slower than GDP**. Don't confuse the two.
> 3. **Compound Growth Misuse:** Use $(1+r)^n$ for **multiplicative** projection; use $e^{rn}$ only when converting via natural logs. Board examiners check this.
> 4. **Causality Diagram Confusion:** In feedback hypothesis, draw **arrows in BOTH directions** between Energy and GDP. One-direction arrows cost 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting Population Growth:** Energy demand projections without population growth **underestimate** demand. KTU loves to test this.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Energy Intensity** = Energy / GDP → Declining EI = improving efficiency.
- ✅ **Energy Elasticity** $\epsilon = \%\Delta E / \%\Delta GDP$ → determines how fast energy demand grows.
- ✅ If $\epsilon < 1$ → Economy is **decoupling** energy from growth (good sign).
- ✅ **Per capita energy** reflects standard of living (USA ~6.5 TOE vs India ~0.6 TOE).
- ✅ **Energy Ladder:** Biomass → Coal → Oil → Gas → Electricity → Renewables.
- ✅ **Causality hypotheses:** Neutrality, Conservation, Growth, Feedback.
- ✅ **Sectoral demand drivers:** Industrialization, Urbanization, Motorization, Electrification.
- ✅ **Key units:** 1 TOE = 41.868 GJ = 11.63 MWh; 1 Mtoe = $10^6$ TOE.
- ✅ **India's rank:** 3rd largest energy consumer globally; primary energy growth ~4–5% CAGR.
- ✅ **Long-term forecast** uses compound growth: $E_n = E_0(1+r)^n$ with $r = \epsilon \times g$.
- ✅ **Energy policy tools:** PAT scheme, BEE labelling, ECBC, DSM, renewable targets.
- ✅ Always **verify units** before final answers in KTU ESE — most marks lost here.
- ✅ Remember the **Feedback Hypothesis** is most relevant for India — two-way arrow.
- ✅ **Conservation potential:** Industry (process heat, motors) + Domestic (appliances, lighting).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Intensity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Intensity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Energy Intensity (EI)** is a pivotal macro-level and micro-level sustainability metric defined as the ratio of the total quantity of energy consumed to a specific normalizing denominator — typically the Gross Domestic Product (GDP), industrial output, physical production unit, floor area, or per-capita consumption. Mathematically, it quantifies how much energy is required to produce one unit of economic value or physical product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> Energy Intensity = $\dfrac{\text{Total Energy Consumption (in Energy Units)}}{\text{GDP / Output / Floor Area / Unit Production (in Monetary or Physical Units)}}$

The two most universally recognized KTU-canonical forms are:

1. **National / Macro-Level Form:** 
$$EI_{\text{national}} = \frac{\text{TPEC (Total Primary Energy Consumption)}}{\text{GDP}} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{toe}}{\text{Million USD}} \right]$$

2. **Sectoral / Industrial Form:**
$$EI_{\text{industrial}} = \frac{\text{Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)}}{\text{Unit of Output}} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{kWh}}{\text{tonne}} \right] \text{ or } \left[ \frac{\text{GJ}}{\text{unit}} \right]$$

Where **TPEC = Total Primary Energy Consumption**, **GDP = Gross Domestic Product**, **toe = Tonnes of Oil Equivalent**, and **SEC = Specific Energy Consumption**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your **monthly household electricity bill** is ₹6,000 and your family earned ₹1,20,000 that month.

- Energy Intensity of your household = $\dfrac{₹6{,}000}{₹1{,}20{,}000}$ = **₹0.05 of energy per ₹1 of income**.

> A lower ratio = your household is **energy-efficient** (you spend less on power for every rupee you earn).
> A higher ratio = your household is **energy-wasteful** (you burn through power relative to what you produce).

Now scale this analogy up to a **nation**, **factory**, or **building** — that is exactly what Energy Intensity captures in energy management engineering. It is the "**fuel-economy equivalent**" of a country or industry.

### 1.3 Why Energy Intensity Matters in KTU / BEE / PAT Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this metric is central to Module 1 of PESFT741:**
> - It is the **baseline KPI (Key Performance Indicator)** used by India's **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** under the **Perform, Achieve & Trade (PAT)** scheme.
> - Industries are mandated to reduce their **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** — a direct derivative of Energy Intensity.
> - UN Sustainable Development Goal **SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy)** and **SDG 13 (Climate Action)** are tracked using Energy Intensity as a primary indicator.
> - **India's NDC (Nationally Determined Contribution)** target: reduce energy intensity of GDP by **45%** by 2030 (vs. 2005 levels).

### 1.4 Units & Standard Conversions (Frequently Tested)

| Normalizing Denominator | Energy Intensity Unit | Typical Use |
|-------------------------|-----------------------|-------------|
| GDP (PPP-adjusted) | $\text{toe} / \text{Million USD}_{\text{PPP}}$ | Cross-country benchmarking |
| GDP (Nominal) | $\text{kgoe} / \text{USD}$ | IEA / World Bank reports |
| Physical Output (mass) | $\text{kWh} / \text{tonne}$ | Cement, Steel, Aluminium |
| Physical Output (volume) | $\text{MJ} / \text{litre}$ | Petroleum refining |
| Floor Area | $\text{kWh} / \text{m}^2 / \text{year}$ | Buildings (ECBC) |
| Per Capita | $\text{toe} / \text{person}$ | Residential energy use |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant to Memorize:** $1 \text{ toe} = 41.868 \text{ GJ} = 11.63 \text{ MWh}$ (approximately).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Decomposition of Energy Intensity

Energy Intensity is not a static number; it is governed by **structural, technical, and behavioral** drivers. The **Kaya Identity**, originally proposed by Japanese energy economist **Yoichi Kaya**, is the KTU-canonical decomposition framework.

**Kaya Identity (Anchor Formula for Module 1):**

$$\text{CO}_2 = P \times \frac{G}{P} \times \frac{E}{G} \times \frac{\text{CO}_2}{E}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Population
- $\dfrac{G}{P}$ = GDP per capita (economic prosperity)
- $\dfrac{E}{G}$ = **Energy Intensity of GDP** (energy efficiency of the economy)
- $\dfrac{\text{CO}_2}{E}$ = Carbon intensity of energy (fossil fuel mix)

> The middle factor $\dfrac{E}{G}$ is the **Energy Intensity** term — the engineering lever we control to reduce emissions independently of population or economic growth.

### 2.2 Categories of Energy Intensity

- **Primary Energy Intensity (PEI):** Uses primary energy (before conversion losses). Useful for cross-country comparisons.
- **Final Energy Intensity (FEI):** Uses final/usable energy (after conversion losses). Useful for end-use sector analysis.
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** Micro-level variant, normalized to a single unit of physical output. Most relevant for industrial energy audits.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the high-yield formula bank for KTU 2024 ESE questions on Energy Intensity.**

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Typical Units |
|---|---------|--------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $EI = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{G_{\text{GDP}}}$ | Macro energy intensity | $\text{toe} / \text{Million USD}$ |
| 2 | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{\text{consumed}}}{M_{\text{output}}}$ | Specific energy consumption | $\text{GJ} / \text{tonne}$ |
| 3 | $EI_{\text{per capita}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{population}}}$ | Per capita energy use | $\text{toe} / \text{person}$ |
| 4 | $\% \Delta EI = \dfrac{EI_{\text{new}} - EI_{\text{base}}}{EI_{\text{base}}} \times 100$ | Percentage change in EI | dimensionless (\%) |
| 5 | $E_{\text{saved}} = EI_{\text{base}} \times G_{\text{new}} - E_{\text{actual}}$ | Energy saved due to EI reduction | toe or GJ |
| 6 | $EI_{\text{weighted avg}} = \dfrac{\sum (EI_i \times G_i)}{\sum G_i}$ | Sectoral-weighted aggregate EI | unit-consistent |
| 7 | $TEE = EI \times G_{\text{projected}}$ | Total Energy Estimation | energy units |
| 8 | $CF = \dfrac{E_{\text{fossil}}}{E_{\text{total}}}$ | Carbonization Factor (related) | dimensionless |

### 2.4 Energy Intensity vs. Energy Efficiency — The Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do NOT confuse in exam answers** (this is a frequently tested KTU conceptual question):

| Parameter | Energy Intensity | Energy Efficiency |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------|
| Definition | Energy used per unit of economic/physical output | Ratio of useful output energy to input energy |
| Nature | **Indicator / Metric** (descriptive) | **Performance Ratio** (causal) |
| Typical Form | $\text{MJ} / \text{₹}$ or $\text{kWh} / \text{tonne}$ | dimensionless (\%) |
| Scope | Macro to micro | Component / process level |
| Improves by | Technology, fuel switching, structural change | Better equipment, controls, processes |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **PAT Scheme (India):** SEC targets are set for 13 designated energy-intensive sectors (aluminium, cement, chlor-alkali, fertilizer, iron & steel, paper, petrochemicals, etc.).
2. **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code):** ECBC+ compliance benchmarks commercial buildings at **$\leq 90 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{year}$** — a direct Energy Intensity cap.
3. **International Benchmarking:** IEA ranks countries; **India's energy intensity** dropped from 0.39 kgoe/USD (2000) to ~0.18 kgoe/USD (2022).
4. **Energy Audit Baseline:** Year 0 baseline EI = $SEC_{\text{baseline}}$; Year 1 post-ECM = $SEC_{\text{achieved}}$. Savings = $SEC_{\text{baseline}} - SEC_{\text{achieved}}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Linking the Kaya Identity to Energy Intensity

**Goal:** Demonstrate how energy intensity emerges as a multiplicative factor in the Kaya decomposition.

**Step 1 — Write Kaya Identity directly:**
$$\text{CO}_2 = P \times \frac{G}{P} \times \frac{E}{G} \times \frac{\text{CO}_2}{E}$$

**Step 2 — Algebraically rearrange to isolate the Energy Intensity term $\dfrac{E}{G}$:**
$$\frac{E}{G} = \frac{\text{CO}_2}{P \times \frac{G}{P} \times \frac{\text{CO}_2}{E}}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the middle term:**
The factor $\dfrac{E}{G}$ is **Energy Intensity of GDP**. Its reciprocal $\dfrac{G}{E}$ is **Energy Productivity**.

**Step 4 — Express Energy Productivity:**
$$\text{Energy Productivity} = \frac{G}{E} \quad [\text{₹/toe or USD/toe}]$$

**Step 5 — Final interpretation:**
Energy Intensity and Energy Productivity are reciprocals. Reducing EI by 1\% through efficiency gains is mathematically equivalent to increasing Energy Productivity by ~1.01\%.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Percentage Reduction in Energy Intensity — India's NDC Target

**Given:**
- Baseline year: 2005
- Baseline Energy Intensity: $EI_{2005} = 0.3964 \text{ kgoe/USD}$
- Target year: 2030
- Target reduction: **45\%** below 2005 levels

**Find:** Energy Intensity in 2030.

**Step 1 — Apply the percentage reduction formula:**
$$EI_{2030} = EI_{2005} \times \left(1 - \frac{\% \text{ reduction}}{100}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**
$$EI_{2030} = 0.3964 \times (1 - 0.45)$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**
$$EI_{2030} = 0.3964 \times 0.55$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**
$$EI_{2030} = 0.2180 \text{ kgoe/USD}$$

> **Interpretation:** India aims to halve the energy required to produce one USD of GDP by 2030 — a *decoupling* of economic growth from energy consumption.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Savings via SEC Reduction (Industrial Case Study)

**Scenario:** A cement plant produces 2.5 million tonnes/year with $SEC = 3.2 \text{ GJ/tonne}$. After installing waste heat recovery (WHR), SEC drops to 2.85 GJ/tonne.

**Step 1 — Compute baseline energy consumption:**
$$E_{\text{base}} = SEC_{\text{base}} \times M_{\text{output}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$E_{\text{base}} = 3.2 \times 2.5 \times 10^6$$

$$E_{\text{base}} = 8.0 \times 10^6 \text{ GJ/year}$$

**Step 3 — Compute post-ECM consumption:**
$$E_{\text{new}} = 2.85 \times 2.5 \times 10^6 = 7.125 \times 10^6 \text{ GJ/year}$$

**Step 4 — Compute annual savings:**
$$\Delta E = E_{\text{base}} - E_{\text{new}} = 8.0 \times 10^6 - 7.125 \times 10^6$$

$$\Delta E = 0.875 \times 10^6 \text{ GJ/year}$$

**Step 5 — Express in toe (multiply by 0.02388):**
$$\Delta E = 0.875 \times 10^6 \times 0.02388 \approx 20{,}895 \text{ toe/year}$$

**Step 6 — Percentage reduction in EI:**
$$\% \Delta SEC = \frac{3.2 - 2.85}{3.2} \times 100 = 10.9375\%$$

> **Conclusion:** The cement plant reduces its Energy Intensity (SEC) by **10.94\%**, saving **~20,895 toe/year**.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Weighted-Aggregate Energy Intensity

A district has three industrial sectors:

| Sector | GDP Share $G_i$ (M₹) | Energy Intensity $EI_i$ (toe/M₹) |
|--------|----------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Steel | 200 | 0.85 |
| Cement | 150 | 0.62 |
| Textile | 100 | 0.30 |

**Find:** Aggregate Energy Intensity.

**Step 1 — Apply weighted-average formula:**
$$EI_{\text{agg}} = \frac{\sum (EI_i \times G_i)}{\sum G_i}$$

**Step 2 — Numerator:**
$$\sum (EI_i \times G_i) = (0.85 \times 200) + (0.62 \times 150) + (0.30 \times 100)$$

$$= 170 + 93 + 30 = 293 \text{ toe}$$

**Step 3 — Denominator:**
$$\sum G_i = 200 + 150 + 100 = 450 \text{ M₹}$$

**Step 4 — Aggregate:**
$$EI_{\text{agg}} = \frac{293}{450} \approx 0.6511 \text{ toe/M₹}$$

> **Interpretation:** The district as a whole consumes **0.6511 toe of energy per million rupees of GDP** — a single composite indicator enabling cross-district benchmarking.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Determinants of Energy Intensity

| Determinant | Mechanism | Example |
|-------------|-----------|---------|
| **Structural change** | Shift from heavy industry to services | Service-led economies have lower EI |
| **Technological upgrade** | Adoption of best-available technology (BAT) | Replacing IE2 motors with IE4 |
| **Fuel switching** | Coal → Natural gas → Renewables | Carbonization factor drops |
| **Behavioral change** | Energy literacy, demand-side management | Turning off idle loads |
| **Climate** | Heating/cooling demand varies | Cold countries have higher EI |
| **Subsidies / Pricing** | Misaligned tariffs inflate consumption | Industrial cross-subsidies |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — How Energy Intensity is Computed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Energy Source Coal Oil Gas RE] --> B[Primary Energy Mix TPEC]
    B --> C[Conversion Sector Losses]
    C --> D[Final Energy Delivered]
    D --> E1[Industry]
    D --> E2[Transport]
    D --> E3[Residential]
    D --> E4[Agriculture]
    E1 --> F1[GDP Component]
    E2 --> F2[GDP Component]
    E3 --> F3[GDP Component]
    E4 --> F4[GDP Component]
    F1 --> G[Total GDP]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    B --> H[Energy Intensity Macro]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Comparison vs Baseline]
    I --> J[Policy Target e.g. PAT NDC]
    J --> K[Energy Conservation Measures ECMs]
```

### 4.2 Kaya Identity Decomposition Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total CO2 Emissions] --> B[Population P]
    A --> C[GDP per Capita G over P]
    A --> D[Energy Intensity E over G]
    A --> F[Carbon Intensity CO2 over E]
    D --> D1[Structural Component]
    D --> D2[Technical Component]
    D --> D3[Behavioral Component]
    D1 --> D1a[Sectoral Mix Shift]
    D2 --> D2a[Efficient Equipment]
    D3 --> D3a[Demand Side Management]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Industrial SEC Reduction Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step1 Establish Baseline SEC] --> S2[Step2 Energy Audit Level 2 or 3]
    S2 --> S3[Step3 Identify Energy Conservation Measures]
    S3 --> S4[Step4 Technical Economic Feasibility]
    S4 --> S5[Step5 Implement ECMs]
    S5 --> S6[Step6 Measure Post Implementation SEC]
    S6 --> S7[Step7 Compute SEC reduction percent]
    S7 --> S8{Is target achieved?}
    S8 -- No --> S3
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Step8 Report to BEE PAT scheme]
    S9 --> S10[Step9 Trade Energy Saving Certificates]
```

### 4.4 Energy Intensity Reduction Strategy Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Reduce Energy Intensity] --> R1[Low Cost Housekeeping]
    R --> R2[Medium Cost Process Optimization]
    R --> R3[High Cost Technology Upgrade]
    R --> R4[Long Term Fuel Switching]
    R1 --> R1a[Shut off idle loads]
    R1 --> R1b[Compressed air leak repair]
    R2 --> R2a[VFD on motors]
    R2 --> R2b[Heat recovery]
    R3 --> R3a[Replace IE2 with IE4]
    R3 --> R3b[High efficiency boilers]
    R4 --> R4a[Biomass cofiring]
    R4 --> R4b[Solar thermal]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
Define Energy Intensity. List any two normalizing denominators used in its calculation.

**Model Answer:**

> **Energy Intensity (EI)** is defined as the ratio of total energy consumption to a normalizing denominator such as GDP, industrial output, floor area, or population. It measures how much energy is required to produce one unit of economic value or physical product.
>
> Two normalizing denominators:
> 1. **Gross Domestic Product (GDP)** — used at national level, expressed as toe per Million USD.
> 2. **Physical Output (mass/volume)** — used at industrial level, expressed as kWh per tonne or GJ per litre.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
Differentiate between Energy Intensity and Specific Energy Consumption (SEC). Give one industrial example for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Energy Intensity | Specific Energy Consumption |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------------------|
| Scope | Macro to sectoral level | Process / equipment level |
| Example | India's EI = 0.18 kgoe/USD (2022) | Cement SEC = 3.2 GJ/tonne |

> Energy Intensity is used for **benchmarking economies**, while SEC is used for **benchmarking individual processes** within an industry. Both are reciprocal to *energy productivity* and *process efficiency*, respectively.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **Kaya Identity**. Show how Energy Intensity appears as a multiplicative factor in it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel plant produces 1.2 million tonnes/year of crude steel. The baseline Specific Energy Consumption is **6.5 GJ/tonne**. After implementing energy-efficient continuous casting and recovering waste flue gas heat, the SEC reduces to **5.85 GJ/tonne**.

Compute:
- (i) Annual energy savings in GJ and in **toe**. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) Percentage reduction in Energy Intensity. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) Annual CO₂ avoided if grid emission factor = 0.72 tCO₂/MWh. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

> The **Kaya Identity**, proposed by Yoichi Kaya (1990), decomposes total CO₂ emissions into four multiplicative factors:

$$\text{CO}_2 = P \times \frac{G}{P} \times \frac{E}{G} \times \frac{\text{CO}_2}{E}$$

> The factor $\dfrac{E}{G}$ is the **Energy Intensity** term. It is the energy used per unit of GDP. Policy levers that lower this term — technology upgrade, fuel switching, structural change — directly reduce emissions *without* requiring population reduction or GDP contraction. This is the central insight: **Energy Intensity is the engineering lever in the Kaya framework**.

**Model Solution — Part (b)(i):**

$$E_{\text{base}} = 6.5 \times 1.2 \times 10^6 = 7.8 \times 10^6 \text{ GJ/year}$$

$$E_{\text{new}} = 5.85 \times 1.2 \times 10^6 = 7.02 \times 10^6 \text{ GJ/year}$$

$$\Delta E = 7.8 \times 10^6 - 7.02 \times 10^6 = 0.78 \times 10^6 \text{ GJ/year}$$

Converting to toe (1 GJ = 0.02388 toe):

$$\Delta E = 0.78 \times 10^6 \times 0.02388 \approx 18{,}626 \text{ toe/year}$$

**Model Solution — Part (b)(ii):**

$$\% \Delta EI = \frac{6.5 - 5.85}{6.5} \times 100 = 10\%$$

**Model Solution — Part (b)(iii):**

Convert savings to MWh: $0.78 \times 10^6 \text{ GJ} = 0.78 \times 10^6 / 3.6 = 2.1667 \times 10^5 \text{ MWh}$

$$\text{CO}_2 \text{ avoided} = 2.1667 \times 10^5 \times 0.72 \approx 1.56 \times 10^5 \text{ tCO}_2 / \text{year}$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of Energy Intensity as a Key Performance Indicator (KPI) in India's **PAT (Perform, Achieve & Trade)** scheme. Mention any three sectors covered under PAT. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A district has three industrial sectors with the following data:

| Sector | GDP $G_i$ (₹ Crore) | Energy Intensity $EI_i$ (toe/₹ Crore) |
|--------|----------------------|---------------------------------------|
| Fertilizer | 300 | 1.10 |
| Pulp & Paper | 250 | 0.75 |
| Chemical | 200 | 0.45 |

Compute:
- (i) The aggregate Energy Intensity of the district. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) If the district targets a **15% reduction** in aggregate EI over 5 years, what should be the target EI? **(2 marks)**
- (iii) Comment on which sector must prioritize ECMs. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

> Under the **PAT scheme** (notified under the Energy Conservation Act 2001), energy-intensive industries are assigned SEC targets. Industries that **outperform** their target earn **Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts)** that can be traded on power exchanges.
>
> **Energy Intensity (SEC)** is the primary KPI because:
> 1. It normalizes energy use to physical output, enabling fair comparison across plants of different capacities.
> 2. It provides a **single number** that regulators and auditors can track year-on-year.
> 3. It forms the basis of the tradable permit mechanism (ESCerts).
>
> **Three sectors covered under PAT (current cycle):**
> 1. Aluminium
> 2. Cement
> 3. Iron & Steel
> *(Other valid answers: Chlor-Alkali, Fertilizer, Pulp & Paper, Petrochemicals, Textiles, Railways, Refineries, DISCOMs, Commercial Buildings.)*

**Model Solution — Part (b)(i):**

$$EI_{\text{agg}} = \frac{\sum (EI_i \times G_i)}{\sum G_i}$$

Numerator:
$$(1.10 \times 300) + (0.75 \times 250) + (0.45 \times 200) = 330 + 187.5 + 90 = 607.5$$

Denominator:
$$300 + 250 + 200 = 750$$

$$EI_{\text{agg}} = \frac{607.5}{750} = 0.81 \text{ toe/₹ Crore}$$

**Model Solution — Part (b)(ii):**

$$EI_{\text{target}} = 0.81 \times (1 - 0.15) = 0.81 \times 0.85 = 0.6885 \text{ toe/₹ Crore}$$

**Model Solution — Part (b)(iii):**

> **Fertilizer** sector must prioritize Energy Conservation Measures because:
> 1. Its individual EI (**1.10 toe/₹ Crore**) is the highest among the three sectors.
> 2. Fertilizer production (especially ammonia/urea) is energy-intensive with significant process heat and hydrogen feedstock demands.
> 3. Even a partial reduction in its EI would substantially reduce the weighted aggregate due to its high EI × large GDP weight.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Students frequently forget to convert GJ → toe or GJ → MWh when answering Part B numericals. Always convert to a consistent unit before applying the grid emission factor.
> 2. **Confusing EI with SEC:** EI at the national level uses GDP; SEC at the industrial level uses physical output. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Kaya Identity misrepresentation:** Do NOT write Kaya as additive — it is strictly multiplicative. Each factor is independent and dimensionless when divided.
> 4. **Rounding errors:** Always retain 4 significant digits until the final step.
> 5. **PAT scheme confusion:** PAT is *not* PERT/CPM or pure cap-and-trade; it is a **target-based energy efficiency** mechanism unique to India.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Energy Intensity (Module 1, PESFT741):**

- ✅ **Definition:** $EI = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{\text{GDP or Output}}$ — energy per unit of value produced.
- ✅ **Macro vs. Micro:** Macro EI uses GDP; micro EI (SEC) uses physical output.
- ✅ **Unit Conversions:** $1 \text{ toe} = 41.868 \text{ GJ} = 11.63 \text{ MWh}$.
- ✅ **Kaya Identity:** $\text{CO}_2 = P \times \dfrac{G}{P} \times \dfrac{E}{G} \times \dfrac{\text{CO}_2}{E}$ — Energy Intensity is the third factor.
- ✅ **EI vs. Energy Productivity:** They are reciprocals; lower EI = higher productivity.
- ✅ **India's NDC target:** 45% reduction in EI of GDP by 2030 (vs 2005 baseline).
- ✅ **PAT scheme:** Uses SEC (a form of EI) as the trading KPI for industries.
- ✅ **ECBC benchmark:** Commercial buildings target $\leq 90 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{year}$.
- ✅ **Reduction Formula:** $\% \Delta EI = \dfrac{EI_{\text{base}} - EI_{\text{new}}}{EI_{\text{base}}} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Aggregate EI (weighted):** $EI_{\text{agg}} = \dfrac{\sum (EI_i \times G_i)}{\sum G_i}$.
- ✅ **Levers to reduce EI:** Technology upgrade, fuel switching, structural change, DSM.
- ✅ **Distinguish clearly:** Energy Intensity (metric) ≠ Energy Efficiency (ratio).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Long Term Energy Scenario
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Long Term Energy Scenario

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Long Term Energy Scenario (LTES)** is a quantitatively rigorous, internally consistent, and time-bound projection (typically spanning **20 to 50 years**) of the future state of an energy system, encompassing demand, supply, conversion, delivery, and end-use technologies, evaluated under explicitly stated assumptions regarding economic growth, demographic shifts, technology costs, fuel prices, and policy interventions.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a long-term energy scenario is not a single "forecast" but a **family of alternative pathways** (Reference/Business-as-Usual, High Growth, Low Carbon, Net-Zero, etc.) that together bracket the plausible range of the future energy landscape, enabling strategic decision-making in investment, policy, and technology deployment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"A Long Term Energy Scenario is a structured, model-based projection of future energy demand and supply under a defined set of socio-economic, technological, and policy assumptions, used to evaluate alternative pathways for sustainable energy planning over a horizon of 20–50 years."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are planning a 25-year road trip across a country whose road network, fuel stations, and population will all change. You cannot drive "blind" — you need **multiple maps** (scenarios) showing:

| Trip Element | Energy System Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Population along the route | Future population & urbanization |
| Fuel station availability | Energy supply infrastructure (refineries, RE plants) |
| Vehicle fuel efficiency | Energy intensity of the economy |
| Toll / tax policy | Carbon taxes, subsidies, regulations |
| New expressways being built | Emerging technologies (hydrogen, storage) |

A **Long Term Energy Scenario** is exactly that: a "**25-year roadmap**" of how the energy system of a city, state, country, or the world might evolve, drawn under different assumptions, so that policymakers can decide *which road to build first*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Long Term"?** Short-term planning (< 5 years) is operational (which turbine to install next quarter). Long-term scenarios (20–50 years) are **strategic** — they decide lock-in decisions like building a nuclear plant, a hydrogen pipeline, or a smart grid, which have **30–60 year asset lives**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Boundary Parameters

- **Time Horizon (T)**: 20–50 years (typical KTU reference: **2030, 2040, 2047, 2070**)
- **Spatial Boundary**: National / State / Sectoral / Global
- **Sectoral Coverage**: Industry, Transport, Residential, Commercial, Agriculture
- **Demand Unit**: **Mtoe** (Million Tonnes of Oil Equivalent) or **EJ** (Exajoule) or **TWh**
- **1 Mtoe = 0.041868 EJ = 11.63 TWh** (1 TWh = 0.086 Mtoe)
- **Carbon Unit**: **MtCO₂** (Million tonnes of CO₂) or **GtCO₂**

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion Constants (Memorize):**
> - $1 \text{ Mtoe} = 41.868 \text{ TJ}$
> - $1 \text{ TWh} = 3.6 \text{ PJ}$
> - $1 \text{ GtCE} = 0.667 \text{ GtCO}_2 \text{ equivalent}$
> - Standard calorific value of coal: **5500 kcal/kg = 23.0 GJ/t**
> - Standard calorific value of petroleum: **10,200 kcal/kg = 42.7 GJ/t**
> - Standard calorific value of natural gas: **9,000 kcal/kg = 38.3 GJ/m³**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Long-Term Energy Scenario — Six Logical Pillars

Every credible LTES (whether IEA's *World Energy Outlook*, EIA's *International Energy Outlook*, CEA's *National Electricity Plan*, or NITI Aayog's *India Energy Scenarios 2047*) is built on the same six pillars:

### Pillar 1 — Baseline Year and Historical Calibration
- The "**anchor**" of any scenario is the most recent year for which reliable, audited energy balance data exists.
- India: **2021–22** is the latest base year used by CEA/MoSPI.
- All projections are *deltas* above this calibrated base.
- **Why this matters**: A 1% error in base year propagates to 15–20% error in 2047 projections.

### Pillar 2 — Demand-Side Drivers (the "Why we consume more/less")
1. **Population growth** and household formation
2. **GDP growth** and structural economic shift (Services vs Industry)
3. **Urbanization rate** (urban dwellers consume 2–4× more energy per capita)
4. **Energy efficiency** improvements (LED, star-rated appliances, electric vehicles)
5. **Lifestyle / behavioural changes** (telepresence, sharing economy)
6. **Price elasticities** of demand for each fuel

### Pillar 3 — Supply-Side Drivers (the "How we meet demand")
1. Domestic resource availability (coal, gas, hydro, RE potential)
2. **Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)** trajectories for each technology
3. Import dependence and geopolitics
4. Storage technology maturity
5. Grid infrastructure expansion
6. Policy mandates (Renewable Purchase Obligation, Ethanol Blending Programme)

### Pillar 4 — Modelling Framework
- **Bottom-up** (technology-rich): MARKAL, MESSAGE, TIMES, LEAP, OSeMOSYS
- **Top-down** (macroeconomic): CGE (Computable General Equilibrium) models
- **Hybrid**: Combining both
- **Backcasting**: Working backwards from a desired end-state (e.g., Net-Zero 2070)
- **Forecast**: Extrapolating from current trends

### Pillar 5 — Scenario Archetypes
| Archetype | Description | KTU Term |
|---|---|---|
| **Reference / BAU** | Continuation of current policies | Business-as-Usual |
| **Stated Policies (STEPS)** | All announced policies fully implemented | Policy Scenario |
| **Announced Pledges (APS)** | NDCs, net-zero targets honoured | Pledged Scenario |
| **Net Zero Emissions (NZE)** | 1.5 °C aligned pathway | Decarbonization Scenario |
| **Delayed Action** | Postponed transition → higher cumulative cost | Stranded-Asset Scenario |

### Pillar 6 — Output Variables
- Primary energy supply by fuel
- Final energy consumption by sector
- Installed capacity by technology
- CO₂ emissions trajectory
- Investment requirements
- Energy import dependence (%)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table. It is your Board-Exam bible for Module 1 numericals.**

| # | Formula / Identity | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{E}{GDP} \times GDP$ | E = Energy, GDP = Output | Mtoe, ₹ Cr | Decompose demand growth |
| 2 | $I_e = \dfrac{E}{GDP}$ | Energy intensity | toe / ₹ Lakh | Compare economies |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_{E,GDP} = \dfrac{\%\Delta E}{\%\Delta GDP}$ | Elasticity of energy demand | dimensionless | Forecast demand |
| 4 | $E_t = E_0 \cdot (1+g)^t$ | Simple geometric growth | Mtoe | Quick BAU projection |
| 5 | $CAGR = \left(\dfrac{E_n}{E_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1$ | Compound Annual Growth Rate | % | Back-calculate growth |
| 6 | $Kaya\ Identity: CO_2 = P \times \dfrac{GDP}{P} \times \dfrac{E}{GDP} \times \dfrac{CO_2}{E}$ | Kaya decomposition | GtCO₂ | Emission decomposition |
| 7 | $E_{final} = E_{primary} \times \eta$ | Conversion efficiency | % | Primary to final |
| 8 | $LCOE = \dfrac{\sum \dfrac{C_t + F_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum \dfrac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$ | Levelized cost | ₹/kWh | Tech comparison |
| 9 | $E_{demand} = f(P, GDP, I_e, T, P_{fuel})$ | Multi-driver demand model | Mtoe | Long-term forecast |
| 10 | $D = 1 - \dfrac{I_{e,t}}{I_{e,0}}$ | Decoupling index (relative) | % | Policy assessment |
| 11 | $CO_2 = E_{primary} \times EF$ | Emission = Energy × Emission Factor | MtCO₂ | Inventory |
| 12 | $Gap = E_{demand} - E_{supply}$ | Demand–Supply gap | Mtoe | Planning metric |
| 13 | $DDP = \dfrac{Net\ Imports}{Total\ Energy\ Available}$ | Import Dependence | % | Energy security |
| 14 | $R(t) = R_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$ | Resource depletion curve | EJ | Long-term supply ceiling |

> [!WARNING]
> In the markdown tables above, the pipes `|` in mathematical expressions are written using `\vert` to keep the table structure intact. Do NOT use raw `|` inside formula cells.

## 2.3 The Kaya Identity — KTU's Favourite Decarbonization Tool

Proposed by Japanese economist **Yoichi Kaya (1990)**, the identity breaks down total CO₂ emissions into four multiplicative factors:

$$
CO_2 = P \times \left(\frac{GDP}{P}\right) \times \left(\frac{E}{GDP}\right) \times \left(\frac{CO_2}{E}\right)
$$

Where:
- $P$ = Population
- $GDP/P$ = Per-capita income (affluence)
- $E/GDP$ = Energy intensity of the economy
- $CO_2/E$ = Carbon intensity of the energy mix (often called the **emission factor**)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight (asked in 2022 & 2023):** The Kaya identity shows that to decarbonize, a country can act on **any one or combination** of: (a) reduce population growth, (b) decouple GDP from energy, (c) decarbonize the energy mix. (a) and (b) are socio-economic, (c) is technological. Hence **all LTES in IEA NZE 2050 rely on aggressive reduction in $CO_2/E$**.

## 2.4 Bottom-Up vs Top-Down Models — Engineering Trade-off

| Feature | Bottom-Up (MARKAL/TIMES) | Top-Down (CGE) |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Plant/Technology | Sectoral/Macroeconomic |
| Data Need | Very high | High |
| Policy Test | Excellent (technology-specific) | Limited |
| Behavioural Feedback | Weak | Strong (price feedback) |
| Best For | Technology-mix scenarios | Tax/subsidy impact |

The hybrid approach (used by IEA, NITI Aayog) is now the **gold standard** for national LTES.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **National Planning**: India's **NEP (National Electricity Plan)**, **National Energy Policy draft**, **Net-Zero 2070 roadmap**.
- **Corporate**: Energy-intensive industries (steel, cement, refining) use 30-year scenarios for capex decisions.
- **Investor Risk**: ESG mandates, carbon-stranding risk for coal plants evaluated via LTES.
- **Climate Negotiations**: India's **NDC (Nationally Determined Contribution)** under Paris Agreement is built directly on LTES.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Long-Term Energy Demand via Elasticity Method

**Statement:** Given a known base-year energy consumption $E_0$, a projected annual GDP growth rate $g_{GDP}$, a projected population growth rate $g_P$, and a known energy-GDP elasticity $\varepsilon$, derive the long-term energy demand at year $t$.

### Step 1 — Define the elasticity
Elasticity of energy demand with respect to GDP is defined as the ratio of relative change in energy consumption to relative change in GDP:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\%\Delta E}{\%\Delta GDP} = \frac{\Delta E / E}{\Delta GDP / GDP}
$$

### Step 2 — Express the discrete growth version
For a small time step of one year, $\%\Delta E \approx g_E$ (annual growth rate of energy) and $\%\Delta GDP \approx g_{GDP}$:

$$
g_E = \varepsilon \cdot g_{GDP}
$$

### Step 3 — Generalize with population term (per-capita energy)
Per-capita energy $e = E / P$ has elasticity $\varepsilon'$:

$$
g_e = \varepsilon' \cdot g_{GDP} \quad \text{(per-capita income elasticity)}
$$

### Step 4 — Compound over $t$ years
Total energy at year $t$ is:

$$
E_t = E_0 \cdot (1 + g_E)^t
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the elasticity
$$
E_t = E_0 \cdot \left[1 + \varepsilon \cdot g_{GDP}\right]^t
$$

### Step 6 — Final compact form
$$
\boxed{E_t = E_0 \cdot (1 + \varepsilon \cdot g_{GDP})^t}
$$

**Interpretation:** If $\varepsilon = 0.7$ and $g_{GDP} = 6\%$, then $g_E = 4.2\%$. Over 25 years, $E_{25} = E_0 \times (1.042)^{25} \approx 2.80 \cdot E_0$ — i.e., energy demand nearly **triples in 25 years** under BAU.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate) Back-Calculation

**Statement:** Given energy consumption in two years ($E_0$ and $E_n$), derive the average annual growth rate.

### Step 1 — Write the compound growth equation
$$
E_n = E_0 \cdot (1 + g)^n
$$

### Step 2 — Divide by $E_0$
$$
\frac{E_n}{E_0} = (1 + g)^n
$$

### Step 3 — Take the $n$-th root on both sides
$$
\left(\frac{E_n}{E_0}\right)^{1/n} = 1 + g
$$

### Step 4 — Isolate $g$
$$
\boxed{g = \left(\frac{E_n}{E_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1}
$$

**Worked KTU Numerical Example:**
India's primary energy consumption: $E_0 = 595$ Mtoe (2010) and $E_{12} = 820$ Mtoe (2022). Find CAGR.

Step 1: $E_n / E_0 = 820 / 595 = 1.3782$

Step 2: $(1.3782)^{1/12} = ?$

Step 3: Take natural log: $\ln(1.3782) = 0.3203$; divide by 12: $0.02669$

Step 4: Exponentiate: $e^{0.02669} = 1.02705$

Step 5: $g = 1.02705 - 1 = 0.02705 = 2.705\%$

$$
\boxed{g \approx 2.7\% \text{ per annum (India primary energy CAGR, 2010–2022)}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Decoupling Index from Energy Intensity Time Series

**Statement:** Quantify how successfully an economy is decoupling energy consumption from GDP growth.

### Step 1 — Define energy intensity at time $t$
$$
I_{e,t} = \frac{E_t}{GDP_t}
$$

### Step 2 — Define the (relative) decoupling index
$$
D_{rel} = 1 - \frac{I_{e,t}}{I_{e,0}}
$$

If $D_{rel} > 0$: **decoupling** is occurring. If $D_{rel} < 0$: economy is re-coupling (worsening).

### Step 3 — Worked Example
A country has $E_0 = 100$ Mtoe, $GDP_0 = 100$ trillion ₹ in 2010. In 2020, $E_t = 110$ Mtoe, $GDP_t = 160$ trillion ₹.

Step A: $I_{e,0} = 100 / 100 = 1.0$ Mtoe / ₹ trillion

Step B: $I_{e,t} = 110 / 160 = 0.6875$ Mtoe / ₹ trillion

Step C: $D_{rel} = 1 - (0.6875 / 1.0) = 1 - 0.6875 = 0.3125$

$$
\boxed{D_{rel} = 31.25\% \text{ — economy has decoupled 31.25% over the decade}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** "Relative decoupling" (above) is more common. "Absolute decoupling" means $E_t < E_0$ while $GDP_t > GDP_0$ — a much harder target achieved by only a handful of EU countries post-2010.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Primary-to-Final Energy Conversion Chain

**Statement:** Convert installed capacity (GW) into annual energy generation (TWh), then into final energy, accounting for plant load factor, transmission losses, and end-use efficiency.

### Step 1 — Annual generation from capacity
$$
E_{gen} = P_{inst} \times 8760 \times PLF
$$
where $P_{inst}$ in GW, $8760$ = hours/year, $PLF$ = Plant Load Factor (fraction).

### Step 2 — Energy delivered to consumer (after T&D losses)
$$
E_{del} = E_{gen} \times (1 - L_{T\&D})
$$
where $L_{T\&D}$ is the fraction of transmission and distribution loss (India average ~18–20%).

### Step 3 — Useful energy at end-user
$$
E_{useful} = E_{del} \times \eta_{enduse}
$$
where $\eta_{enduse}$ is the end-use efficiency (e.g., LED bulb $\eta = 0.4$, electric resistance heater $\eta = 0.95$, electric motor $\eta = 0.90$).

### Step 4 — Worked Example (KTU 14-mark standard)
A city plans 5 GW solar PV addition. $PLF = 0.25$, $L_{T\&D} = 0.18$, $\eta_{enduse} = 0.70$ (average of all appliances). Find useful energy.

Step A: $E_{gen} = 5 \times 8760 \times 0.25 = 10{,}950$ GWh $= 10.95$ TWh

Step B: $E_{del} = 10.95 \times (1 - 0.18) = 8.979$ TWh

Step C: $E_{useful} = 8.979 \times 0.70 = 6.285$ TWh

Step D: In Mtoe: $6.285 \text{ TWh} \times 0.086 = 0.540$ Mtoe

$$
\boxed{E_{useful} \approx 6.29 \text{ TWh} \approx 0.54 \text{ Mtoe per year}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Demand–Supply Gap Analysis (Annual Outlook Framework)

**Statement:** Project future demand and supply, then compute the gap that must be closed by new capacity or imports.

### Step 1 — Project demand
$$
E^{D}_t = E^{D}_{t-1} \cdot (1 + g_D)
$$

### Step 2 — Project supply from existing fleet
$$
E^{S,existing}_t = \sum_{i} P_i \times 8760 \times PLF_i \times (1 - d_i)^t
$$
where $d_i$ is the annual derating/retirement rate.

### Step 3 — Compute gap
$$
Gap_t = E^{D}_t - E^{S,existing}_t
$$

### Step 4 — Required new capacity (residual)
$$
P_{new} = \frac{Gap_t / 8760}{PLF_{new}}
$$

**Worked KTU Example:**
Base year demand $E^D_0 = 1500$ TWh; growth $g_D = 5\%$. Existing fleet delivers 1400 TWh with $PLF = 0.60$ and retirement $d = 2\%$. Find new capacity required in year 5.

Step A: $E^D_5 = 1500 \times (1.05)^5 = 1500 \times 1.2763 = 1914.4$ TWh

Step B: $E^{S,existing}_5 = 1400 \times (0.98)^5 = 1400 \times 0.9039 = 1265.5$ TWh

Step C: $Gap_5 = 1914.4 - 1265.5 = 648.9$ TWh

Step D: $P_{new} = 648.9 / (8760 \times 0.60) = 0.1234$ TW = 123.4 GW

$$
\boxed{\text{India needs roughly 123 GW of new capacity in the 5th year to close the gap.}}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Long-Term Energy Scenario Modelling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Base Year Data Audit] --> B[Historical Calibration]
    B --> C[Identify Demand Drivers]
    B --> D[Identify Supply Drivers]
    C --> E[Develop Demand Model]
    D --> F[Develop Supply Model]
    E --> G[Scenario Archetype Selection]
    F --> G
```
