    G --> G1[Reference BAU]
    G --> G2[Stated Policies]
    G --> G3[Net Zero Pathway]
    G1 --> H[Run Optimization Engine TIMES or MARKAL]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Compute Output Variables]
    I --> I1[Primary Energy by Fuel]
    I --> I2[Final Energy by Sector]
    I --> I3[Installed Capacity Mix]
    I --> I4[CO2 Emissions]
    I --> I5[Investment Requirement]
    I1 --> J[Stakeholder Validation]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    I5 --> J
    J --> K[Final LTES Report]
    K --> L[Policy Recommendation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** Boxes A–B = Data foundation; C–D = Driver identification; E–F = Model construction; G = Branching into scenarios; H = Optimization engine; I = Output variables (this is the part most often asked in KTU exams); J–L = Translation to policy.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Kaya Identity Decomposition Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total CO2 Emissions] --> B[Population P]
    A --> C[Affluence GDP per capita]
    A --> D[Energy Intensity E per GDP]
    A --> E[Carbon Intensity CO2 per E]
    B --> B1[Declining growth in most OECD]
    B --> B2[Rising in Africa]
    C --> C1[Convergence among nations]
    C --> C2[Saturation effect]
    D --> D1[Industrial structural shift]
    D --> D2[Efficiency improvement]
    D --> D3[Service economy rise]
    E --> E1[Fuel switching coal to gas to RE]
    E --> E2[CCS deployment]
    E --> E3[Renewable scaling]
    D1 --> F[Decarbonization Pathway]
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Application:** When asked "How will India achieve its Net-Zero 2070 target?", the answer is mapped directly onto the Kaya tree. India must attack the bottom-left levers: **Energy intensity reduction + Carbon intensity reduction** of the energy mix.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Sectoral Energy Demand Mapping (India Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Primary Energy 820 Mtoe] --> B[Coal 280]
    A --> C[Oil 240]
    A --> D[Natural Gas 65]
    A --> E[Hydro 35]
    A --> F[Nuclear 12]
    A --> G[RE Solar Wind Bio 80]
    A --> H[Other 108]
    B --> I[Power Sector 600]
    C --> J[Transport 250]
    D --> K[Industry 100]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    I --> L[Final Energy 600]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Industry 250]
    L --> N[Transport 220]
    L --> O[Residential 90]
    L --> P[Commercial 25]
    L --> Q[Agriculture 15]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the numerical fallback:** Because precise physical drawings are not possible in Mermaid, the diagram above is rendered as a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** showing how primary energy flows into final energy via sectoral intermediates. KTU examiners accept this in lieu of hand-drawn pie charts.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Scenario Uncertainty Funnel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Range of Plausible Futures] --> B[BAU 60 percent probability]
    A --> C[Policy 25 percent probability]
    A --> D[Net Zero 10 percent probability]
    A --> E[Disruption Black Swan 5 percent]
    B --> B1[High Carbon Lock-in]
    B --> B2[Continued Import Dependence]
    C --> C1[Moderate Decarbonization]
    C --> C2[Stranded Asset Risk Mid]
    D --> D1[Deep Decarbonization]
    D --> D2[Major Structural Shift]
    E --> E1[Geopolitical Shock]
    E --> E2[Tech Breakthrough]
    E --> E3[Pandemic Scale Event]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define Long Term Energy Scenario (LTES). List any four output variables produced by an LTES study.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Long Term Energy Scenario is a structured, model-based projection of future energy demand and supply over a 20–50 year horizon, evaluated under explicit assumptions about economic, demographic, technological, and policy variables. *(2 marks)*

**Four output variables:** *(1 mark — ¼ mark each)*
1. Primary energy supply by fuel (coal, oil, gas, RE, nuclear)
2. Final energy consumption by sector (industry, transport, residential, commercial, agriculture)
3. Installed power capacity mix (GW by technology)
4. CO₂ emissions trajectory (MtCO₂/year)

> [!TIP]
> KTU expects the **definition first, then 4 bullet points**. Do not write paragraphs in Part A.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Explain the Kaya Identity and state its significance in long-term energy planning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Kaya Identity is a multiplicative decomposition of total CO₂ emissions into four factors:

$$
CO_2 = P \times (GDP/P) \times (E/GDP) \times (CO_2/E)
$$

*(2 marks for statement + formula)*

**Significance:** It allows policymakers to identify the dominant lever for emission reduction. For example, India's decarbonization strategy focuses on reducing **$E/GDP$ (energy intensity)** through efficiency and **$CO_2/E$ (carbon intensity)** through renewable scaling, since population and affluence levers are socio-economically constrained. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B Module 1 questions offer an **internal choice** between two 14-mark alternatives. The model solution below covers both.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **OPTION A**

**Q.A (a)** Define Long Term Energy Scenario. With a neat block diagram, explain the modelling workflow of an LTES study. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A Long Term Energy Scenario (LTES) is a quantitatively rigorous projection of the future state of an energy system (demand, supply, conversion, emissions) over a 20–50 year horizon, built upon explicit assumptions regarding socio-economic, technological, and policy drivers, and used as a decision-support tool for energy planning and policy formulation.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to **Mermaid Diagram 1 in Section 4.1** of these notes. The student should reproduce it from memory, showing:
- Base year data → Calibration → Demand/Supply drivers → Scenario selection → Optimization → Outputs → Policy

**Workflow Explanation (3 marks):**
The workflow consists of six stages:
1. **Base Year Audit**: Establish the most recent year for which reliable energy balance data exists.
2. **Driver Identification**: Quantify exogenous drivers — population, GDP, fuel prices, technology costs.
3. **Scenario Design**: Construct 3–5 internally consistent storylines (BAU, Stated Policy, Net-Zero).
4. **Optimization Run**: Solve using MARKAL/TIMES/LEAP with cost-minimization subject to constraints.
5. **Output Generation**: Tabulate capacity additions, fuel consumption, emissions, investments.
6. **Validation & Policy**: Compare against historical trends, perform sensitivity analysis, recommend policy.

---

**Q.A (b)** A country had an energy consumption of **500 Mtoe in 2015**, projected to reach **1100 Mtoe in 2045**. *(7 marks)*
- (i) Calculate the CAGR of energy demand.
- (ii) If GDP grew at 6% per annum in the same period and the energy-GDP elasticity is **0.85**, find the projected energy in 2045 using the elasticity method and compare with the CAGR-based value.
- (iii) Comment on the difference.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) CAGR (2 marks):**
$$
g = \left(\frac{E_n}{E_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1 = \left(\frac{1100}{500}\right)^{1/30} - 1
$$
$$
g = (2.20)^{0.0333} - 1
$$
Step 1: $\ln(2.20) = 0.7885$
Step 2: $0.7885 / 30 = 0.02628$
Step 3: $e^{0.02628} - 1 = 0.02663$
$$
\boxed{g \approx 2.66\% \text{ per annum}}
$$

**(ii) Elasticity Method (3 marks):**
Energy growth rate: $g_E = \varepsilon \cdot g_{GDP} = 0.85 \times 0.06 = 0.051 = 5.1\%$
$$
E_{2045} = 500 \times (1.051)^{30}
$$
Step 1: $\ln(1.051) = 0.04974$
Step 2: $0.04974 \times 30 = 1.4922$
Step 3: $e^{1.4922} = 4.446$
$$
E_{2045} = 500 \times 4.446 = 2223 \text{ Mtoe}
$$

**(iii) Comparison (2 marks):**
The elasticity method projects **2223 Mtoe**, while the CAGR-extrapolated value is **1100 Mtoe**. The elasticity method assumes energy grows proportionally with GDP scaled by elasticity, which reflects a scenario where no aggressive efficiency improvement is in place. The CAGR of 2.66% implies either structural decoupling, major efficiency gains, or under-reporting in the base year. The wide divergence highlights the **sensitivity of LTES to elasticity assumption** — a 0.1 change in $\varepsilon$ changes 30-year demand by 18–25%.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — **OPTION B**

**Q.B (a)** Explain the **Kaya Identity** with its mathematical formulation. Discuss how India can use each of the four Kaya levers to achieve its **Net-Zero 2070 target**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Mathematical Statement (2 marks):**
$$
CO_2 = P \times \frac{GDP}{P} \times \frac{E}{GDP} \times \frac{CO_2}{E}
$$
- $P$ = Population
- $GDP/P$ = Per-capita GDP
- $E/GDP$ = Energy intensity
- $CO_2/E$ = Carbon intensity of energy mix

**Lever-Wise Application to India's Net-Zero 2070 (5 marks — 1.25 each):**

1. **Population ($P$):** India is projected to peak at ~1.65 billion around 2060. Population stabilization helps marginally. Policy lever: family planning, demographic transition to lower fertility.

2. **Affluence ($GDP/P$):** India targets $5 trillion economy by ~2030 and upper-middle-income status. Cannot be suppressed — it must rise. **Lever is not relevant for reduction**; instead, the goal is to make the affluence rise **on a low-carbon pathway**.

3. **Energy Intensity ($E/GDP$):** India must reduce energy intensity by ~5% per annum. Levers:
   - PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme for industries
   - ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) for buildings
   - Star labelling programme for appliances
   - Fuel efficiency standards for vehicles (CAFE norms)
   - Modal shift to rail in freight

4. **Carbon Intensity ($CO_2/E$):** The **biggest lever** for India. Levers:
   - 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030
   - Green Hydrogen Mission
   - Coal plant retirement (only supercritical/ultra-supercritical new)
   - Ethanol blending (E20 by 2025–26)
   - 30% EV sales share by 2030
   - CCUS for hard-to-abate sectors (steel, cement)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> Students often **define all four levers but skip the specific Indian policy/action under each**. You will lose **2–3 marks** if you only write generic statements like "reduce energy intensity" without naming the *PAT scheme*, *ECBC*, *CAFE norms*, *Green Hydrogen Mission*, etc. The KTU 2024 scheme is **policy-aware** — examiner expects concrete Indian policy names.

---

**Q.B (b)** A state plans to add **2 GW of solar PV** and **1 GW of wind** in the next decade. Solar PLF = 22%, Wind PLF = 35%, T&D loss = 16%, end-use efficiency for the served load = 0.75. Calculate the **annual useful energy delivered** in TWh and Mtoe. Comment on the role of such capacity addition in long-term energy scenarios. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Annual generation (2 marks):**
Solar: $E_{gen,S} = 2 \text{ GW} \times 8760 \text{ h} \times 0.22 = 3854.4$ GWh $= 3.854$ TWh
Wind: $E_{gen,W} = 1 \text{ GW} \times 8760 \text{ h} \times 0.35 = 3066.0$ GWh $= 3.066$ TWh
Total generation: $3.854 + 3.066 = 6.920$ TWh

**Step 2 — Energy delivered after T&D loss (2 marks):**
$E_{del} = 6.920 \times (1 - 0.16) = 6.920 \times 0.84 = 5.813$ TWh

**Step 3 — Useful energy at end-use (2 marks):**
$E_{useful} = 5.813 \times 0.75 = 4.360$ TWh

**Step 4 — Convert to Mtoe (1 mark):**
$E_{useful} = 4.360 \times 0.086 = 0.375$ Mtoe

$$
\boxed{E_{useful} \approx 4.36 \text{ TWh/year} \approx 0.375 \text{ Mtoe/year}}
$$

**Comment on LTES role (2 marks):**
In a long-term energy scenario, such capacity additions are **building blocks** of the supply-side story. They:
- Reduce the demand–supply gap without carbon emissions.
- Lower the $CO_2/E$ factor in the Kaya identity, directly contributing to Net-Zero pathways.
- Displace coal-based generation in the merit-order dispatch.
- However, they introduce **intermittency**, requiring storage or flexible backup, which must be explicitly modelled in LTES (e.g., as a 4-hour battery equivalent in the IEA NZE scenario).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Re-read this section the night before the exam.**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- **LTES** = 20–50 year structured projection of energy system, NOT a single forecast
- **BAU** = Business-as-Usual / Reference Scenario
- **STES** = Stated Policies Scenario
- **NZE** = Net-Zero Emissions Scenario (1.5 °C aligned)
- **CAGR** = Compound Annual Growth Rate
- **Decoupling** = $\Delta E < \Delta GDP$ (relative) or $\Delta E < 0$ while $\Delta GDP > 0$ (absolute)

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Formulas
1. $E_t = E_0 \cdot (1 + g)^t$ — Geometric growth
2. $g = (E_n/E_0)^{1/n} - 1$ — CAGR back-calculation
3. $g_E = \varepsilon \cdot g_{GDP}$ — Elasticity-based growth
4. $CO_2 = P \times (GDP/P) \times (E/GDP) \times (CO_2/E)$ — **Kaya Identity**
5. $E_{gen} = P_{inst} \times 8760 \times PLF$ — Capacity to generation
6. $I_e = E/GDP$ — Energy intensity
7. $D_{rel} = 1 - (I_{e,t}/I_{e,0})$ — Decoupling index
8. $DDP = \dfrac{Net\ Imports}{Total\ Energy\ Available}$ — Import dependence
9. $LCOE$ — Levelized Cost of Energy (memorize structure, not derivation)

### 🔑 Critical Units & Conversions
- $1 \text{ Mtoe} = 41.868 \text{ TJ} = 11.63 \text{ TWh}$
- $1 \text{ TWh} = 0.086 \text{ Mtoe}$
- $1 \text{ GtCO}_2 = 1/0.667 = 1.499 \text{ GtCE}$ (approx)
- $8760$ = hours per year (always use this in capacity-to-energy conversion)
- India's average PLF: Coal ~55%, Solar ~22%, Wind ~35%, Hydro ~40%, Nuclear ~80%

### 🔑 India-Specific Data Points (Frequently Asked)
- India primary energy: ~820 Mtoe (2022)
- India's energy import dependence: ~40–45%
- India's Net-Zero target year: **2070**
- India's non-fossil capacity target: 500 GW by 2030
- 50% of cumulative electric power from non-fossil sources by 2030
- India's INDC commitment to UNFCCC: Reduce emissions intensity of GDP by 45% from 2005 levels by 2030

### 🔑 Modelling Tools to Know
- **Bottom-up**: MARKAL, MESSAGE, TIMES, LEAP, OSeMOSYS
- **Top-down**: Computable General Equilibrium (CGE)
- **Hybrid**: NITI Aayog's India Energy Model, IEA's WEM (World Energy Model)
- **Forecasting techniques**: Time-series (ARIMA), Econometric (regression), End-use, Technological diffusion (S-curve)

### 🔑 KTU-Examiner Favourite Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing "**final**" and "**primary**" energy (Primary > Final due to conversion losses)
- ❌ Forgetting the $8760$ hours/year factor in capacity-energy problems
- ❌ Writing elasticity as a *percentage* — it is **dimensionless ratio of percentages**
- ❌ Mixing Mtoe and TWh without conversion (loses 1 mark instantly)
- ❌ Writing Kaya identity without naming **all four factors**
- ❌ Using raw `|` inside markdown formula tables (breaks table structure)
- ❌ Citing fake textbook references — KTU 2024 strictly prohibits fabricated citations
- ❌ Writing generic "renewable energy" answers in Net-Zero questions — name **specific Indian policy/mission**

### 🔑 Suggested 14-Mark Answer Structure (Time Management)
- Spend **2 minutes** reading the question
- Spend **1 minute** planning the structure (definition + 3 sub-parts)
- Spend **10 minutes** writing the answer
- Spend **1 minute** on diagrams and final review
- Always end with a **box around the final numerical answer**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Pricing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Pricing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Energy Pricing** is the systematic economic mechanism by which the cost of electrical (and thermal) energy supplied to consumers is determined, structured, and recovered. It is a tariff-based financial framework that recovers the *aggregate cost of generation, transmission, distribution, and a regulated return on capital investment* from the end-user, while simultaneously acting as a **demand-side management (DSM)** signal to encourage energy-efficient behaviour.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, energy pricing covers **three interdependent pillars**:
1. **Cost Recovery Pricing** (engineering economics perspective)
2. **Load Management Pricing** (DSM perspective)
3. **Social / Environmental Cost Pricing** (sustainability perspective)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examiner Insight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** When a question asks to "discuss energy pricing," you must cover ALL three pillars. Many students write only about tariff slabs and lose marks. Always mention **economic efficiency + equity + environmental externalities**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Bill" Model

Imagine you walk into a buffet restaurant:

| Restaurant Component | Energy Equivalent | What it Recovers |
|---------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| Entry cover charge (₹200 flat, irrespective of food) | **Fixed / Demand Charge** | Capital cost, meter cost, infrastructure |
| Cost per plate of food (₹400/plate) | **Energy Charge (per kWh)** | Fuel cost, variable O&M |
| Penalty if you waste food → extra charge | **Power Factor Penalty** | Reactive power burden |
| Weekend buffet pricing (costlier on Saturday) | **Time-of-Use (TOU) Tariff** | Peak-load generation cost |

Just as the restaurant uses **multiple pricing levers** to recover costs AND influence customer behaviour, the electricity utility uses a **multi-part tariff** to recover its costs AND nudge consumers toward efficient consumption.

## 1.3 Why Energy Pricing Matters

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Functions of an Energy Tariff (Bhatia, S.C. framework):**
> 1. **Revenue Function** — Recover the utility's total cost of service (TCOS).
> 2. **Allocative Function** — Signal the *true marginal cost* of energy at each hour.
> 3. **Distributional Function** — Ensure equity across consumer classes (domestic, commercial, industrial).

The total cost of electricity supply has two broad physical components which translate directly into billing:

- **Fixed Cost (Capacity Cost):** Independent of how much energy is consumed. Recovers interest on capital, depreciation, salaries, insurance. Unit: **₹/kW/month** or **₹/kVA/month**.
- **Variable Cost (Energy / Running Cost):** Directly proportional to energy (kWh) consumed. Recovers fuel cost, water charges. Unit: **₹/kWh**.

---

## 1.4 Standard Pricing Benchmarks You Must Memorise

The following internationally and nationally standardised metrics recur in KTU exam questions:

- **₹/kWh** — Energy charge (running cost)
- **₹/kW or ₹/kVA** — Demand charge (capacity cost)
- **₹/kVARh or PF penalty in %** — Reactive power penalty
- **TOD (Time-of-Day) multipliers:** Peak ≈ 1.5 × normal, Off-peak ≈ 0.75 × normal (Kerala State Electricity Board Ltd – KSEB reference range)
- **Discount rate (i):** Typically **10% – 12%** for KTU numerical problems unless otherwise stated.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Energy Pricing Methods

Energy pricing methods are broadly classified as follows:

### A. From the Utility's Revenue Perspective (Cost-Based Pricing)

#### 2.1.1 Flat (Single-Part) Tariff
A uniform rate (₹/kWh) charged regardless of consumption level or load factor. Mathematically:

$$
\text{Total Bill} = R \times E
$$

where $R$ is the flat rate (₹/kWh) and $E$ is energy consumed (kWh).

**Drawback:** No incentive for load factor improvement; penalises high-LF consumers unfairly.

#### 2.1.2 Two-Part Tariff
A combination of a fixed (demand) charge and an energy (running) charge:

$$
\text{Total Bill} = A \times D + B \times E
$$

where:
- $A$ = fixed/demand charge (₹/kW or ₹/kVA)
- $D$ = maximum demand (kW or kVA)
- $B$ = energy charge (₹/kWh)
- $E$ = energy consumed (kWh)

This is the **most common industrial tariff in India**.

#### 2.1.3 Three-Part Tariff / Block-Rate Tariff
Energy is charged at progressively increasing (or decreasing) rates in blocks (slabs):

$$
\text{Bill} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i \times E_i
$$

where $R_i$ is the rate for the $i^{th}$ block and $E_i$ is the energy consumed in that block.

#### 2.1.4 Maximum Demand Tariff
Same as two-part, but the demand charge uses the **billing demand** (highest of actual demand or 80% of contract demand).

#### 2.1.5 Power Factor Tariff
Penalises or rewards based on PF deviation from a reference (usually 0.85 lag or unity). Two variants:

- **kVA tariff:** Bill = $R_{kVA} \times kVAh$
- **PF penalty clause:** Bill = $B \times E + \text{Penalty} \times \left(\dfrac{0.85}{\cos\phi_{actual}} - 1\right)$

### B. From the Consumer Behaviour Perspective (Demand-Based Pricing)

#### 2.1.6 Time-of-Use (TOU) Tariff
Different rates for different time windows:

$$
\text{Rate} = \begin{cases} R_p & \text{for peak hours} \\ R_s & \text{for shoulder hours} \\ R_o & \text{for off-peak hours} \end{cases}
$$

#### 2.1.7 Real-Time Pricing (RTP)
Rate changes hourly (or in 15-min blocks) based on the **system marginal price (SMP)**. Theoretically the most economically efficient but hardest to implement.

#### 2.1.8 Critical Peak Pricing (CPP)
A hybrid: flat rate most of the year, with very high rates on a few "critical peak" days called by the utility in advance.

#### 2.1.9 Interruptible Tariff
Lower base rate, but the utility can remotely curtail load during emergencies in exchange for a discounted tariff.

### C. From the Social / Regulatory Perspective

#### 2.1.10 Marginal Cost Pricing (Short-Run / Long-Run)
Tariff set equal to the **marginal cost** of supplying an additional kWh. Promotes economic efficiency but may not recover total fixed cost.

#### 2.1.11 Average Cost Pricing
Tariff = Total Cost / Total Energy. Guarantees cost recovery but ignores marginal signals.

#### 2.1.12 Social Cost-Benefit Pricing
Internalises environmental externalities (carbon cost, pollution cost). Adds a "carbon adder" to the tariff.

---

## 2.2 Economic Evaluation Metrics — Used to Justify Any Tariff-Driven Energy Project

When a manager shifts loads to avail TOU tariff benefits, or installs capacitors to avoid PF penalties, the **financial viability** is established using the following metrics:

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Metric | Formula | Unit | Used For |
|---|--------|---------|------|----------|
| 1 | Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \dfrac{I}{\text{Annual Savings}}$ | years | Quick screening |
| 2 | Discounted Payback | $DPP$ — first $t$ s.t. $\sum CF_t/(1+i)^t \geq I$ | years | Time-value aware |
| 3 | Net Present Value | $NPV = -I + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t} + \dfrac{S}{(1+i)^n}$ | ₹ | Profitability |
| 4 | Internal Rate of Return | $i^*$ s.t. $NPV(i^*) = 0$ | % | Yield comparison |
| 5 | Life Cycle Cost (LCC) | $LCC = I + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{A_t}{(1+i)^t} - \dfrac{S}{(1+i)^n}$ | ₹ | Tariff comparison |
| 6 | Levelized Cost of Energy | $LCOE = \dfrac{\sum_t C_t/(1+i)^t}{\sum_t E_t/(1+i)^t}$ | ₹/kWh | Self-generation |
| 7 | Uniform Annual Equivalent | $UAE = NPV \times \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | ₹/year | Comparing unequal-life options |
| 8 | Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) | $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | dimensionless | Converts $P \to A$ |
| 9 | Sinking Fund Factor (SFF) | $SFF = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | dimensionless | Converts $F \to A$ |
| 10 | Single Payment Present Worth | $P = F/(1+i)^n$ | ₹ | Future → Present |
| 11 | Average Cost of Electricity | $AC = \dfrac{\text{Total Bill}}{\text{Units consumed (kWh)}}$ | ₹/kWh | Tariff comparison |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Naming Note (Avoiding LaTeX Pitfalls):**
> In tables, the absolute value or conditional operator is rendered using `\vert` or `$\vert$` (e.g., $\vert CF_t \vert$) to prevent markdown table column-breakage. Never write `|CF|` inside a table row.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Economics of TOU Tariff — Worked Intuition

Suppose a factory operates 3 shifts uniformly vs. concentrates production in off-peak hours only.

- Off-peak rate $R_o = 4$ ₹/kWh, peak rate $R_p = 8$ ₹/kWh.
- Annual energy $E = 5 \times 10^6$ kWh.

Uniform-shift bill:
$$
\text{Bill}_{\text{uniform}} = \dfrac{E}{3} \times R_p + \dfrac{E}{3} \times R_s + \dfrac{E}{3} \times R_o
$$

Load-shifted bill (move 60% of load to off-peak):
$$
\text{Bill}_{\text{shifted}} = 0.20E \cdot R_p + 0.20E \cdot R_s + 0.60E \cdot R_o
$$

The **savings** form the *annual cash inflow* in the NPV/payback equation justifying the load-management investment.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

| Field | Application of Energy Pricing Knowledge |
|-------|------------------------------------------|
| Industrial energy audit | Calculate bill savings from capacitor installation to improve PF and avoid penalty |
| DSM program design | Compute ROI of shifting captive loads to off-peak hours |
| Captive power feasibility | LCOE of diesel/gas/solar genset vs grid tariff |
| Building energy management | Block-tariff aware HVAC scheduling in commercial buildings |
| Smart-grid analytics | RTP forecasting and dynamic load scheduling |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Two-Part Tariff Rate (Average Cost)

Given a two-part tariff: Bill = $A \cdot D + B \cdot E$, the **average cost per unit** paid by the consumer is:

$$
\text{Average Cost (AC)} = \dfrac{\text{Total Bill}}{E} = \dfrac{A \cdot D + B \cdot E}{E}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
AC = B + \dfrac{A \cdot D}{E}
$$

Substituting **Load Factor** ($LF = \dfrac{\text{Avg Demand}}{\text{Max Demand}} = \dfrac{E/(8760 \cdot h)}{D}$) and recognising that $E = D \cdot LF \cdot T$ where $T$ is hours of use:

$$
AC = B + \dfrac{A}{LF \cdot T}
$$

> **Economic Insight:** Lower Load Factor ⇒ Higher effective average cost. This is the *theoretical justification* for the demand charge — it forces consumers to *raise their Load Factor*, which in turn flattens the utility's generation curve and reduces average generation cost.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Simple Payback Period

A capital investment $I$ yields annual cash savings (or profit) of $S$ each year. By definition, payback occurs when cumulative savings recover the investment.

$$
\text{Cumulative Savings}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{t} S_k
$$

For uniform annual savings $S_k = S$:

$$
\text{Cumulative Savings}(t) = S \cdot t
$$

Setting Cumulative Savings = Investment:

$$
S \cdot t^* = I
$$

Solving for $t^*$:

$$
\boxed{SPP = \dfrac{I}{S}}
$$

> **Step-by-step logic explanation:**
> Step 1 — Define payback as the *first year* in which savings equal or exceed investment.
> Step 2 — Assume uniform annual savings (this is the KTU default unless stated otherwise).
> Step 3 — Solve the linear equation.
> Step 4 — The result is in **years**. If fractional, e.g., 3.4 years, interpret as 3 years and 5 months (0.4 × 12).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Net Present Value (NPV)

Start with a future cash flow $CF_t$ at year $t$. Its **present value** at discount rate $i$ is:

$$
PV_t = \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}
$$

The NPV sums the present values of all future cash flows and subtracts the initial investment:

$$
NPV = -I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t} + \dfrac{S_n}{(1+i)^n}
$$

where $S_n$ is the salvage value at end of year $n$.

If cash flows are uniform ($CF_t = A$) for $n$ years, we can collapse the geometric series using the capital recovery factor:

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^t} = \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{NPV = -I_0 + A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n} + \dfrac{S_n}{(1+i)^n}}
$$

> **Step-by-step logic explanation:**
> Step 1 — Convert each year's cash flow to *today's money* via the discount factor $(1+i)^{-t}$.
> Step 2 — Sum all PVs.
> Step 3 — The summation of a uniform series gives the geometric series result above.
> Step 4 — Subtract the initial outflow.
> Step 5 — If NPV ≥ 0 ⇒ project acceptable; if NPV < 0 ⇒ reject.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)

We want the uniform annual amount $A$ equivalent to a present value $P$ over $n$ years at rate $i$.

By definition of NPV = 0 for an amortisation:

$$
P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}
$$

Solving for $A$:

$$
A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

The bracketed multiplier is the **Capital Recovery Factor**:

$$
\boxed{CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}}
$$

> **Economic Insight:** CRF is the bridge between *lump-sum* capital cost and *annualised* tariff impact. When a utility justifies a demand charge, it is essentially recovering the CRF × capital cost per kW per year.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem — TOU Tariff Savings + Payback

**Problem (KTU Typical 14-mark style):**

A small-scale industry operates from 09:00 to 18:00 (9 hours/day, 300 days/year). Its installed load is 500 kW, with an average operating load of 320 kW. The utility offers a TOU tariff:

- Peak (06:00 – 10:00 & 18:00 – 22:00): ₹ 8.50 / kWh
- Off-peak (22:00 – 06:00): ₹ 4.20 / kWh
- Normal (10:00 – 18:00): ₹ 6.30 / kWh

The industry is considering adding a 200 kW solar PV + battery storage system costing **₹ 1.2 crore**, with annual O&M of ₹ 4 lakh. The system will shift 30% of the normal-hours consumption to off-peak equivalent by storing solar energy. Useful life = 15 years, salvage = ₹ 8 lakh, discount rate = 10%.

**Tasks:**
1. Compute annual energy bill before and after the solar installation.
2. Compute annual savings.
3. Compute Simple Payback Period.
4. Compute NPV and comment on viability.

### Step 1 — Annual Energy Before Solar

Total operating hours per year = $9 \times 300 = 2700$ hours.

$$
E_{\text{annual}} = 320 \text{ kW} \times 2700 = 864{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

All 9 hours of operation (09:00 to 18:00) fall in the **normal slot (10:00–18:00)** but actually 09:00–10:00 falls in the *peak* slot. Splitting:

- Peak hours per day = 1 hour (09:00–10:00) → 300 hours/year
- Normal hours per day = 8 hours (10:00–18:00) → 2400 hours/year

$$
E_{\text{peak, before}} = 320 \times 300 = 96{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
E_{\text{normal, before}} = 320 \times 2400 = 768{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

Bill before:

$$
\text{Bill}_{\text{before}} = 96{,}000 \times 8.50 + 768{,}000 \times 6.30
$$

$$
\text{Bill}_{\text{before}} = 816{,}000 + 4{,}838{,}400 = 5{,}654{,}400 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

### Step 2 — Annual Energy After Solar

Solar system shifts 30% of normal-hours consumption:

$$
E_{\text{shifted}} = 0.30 \times 768{,}000 = 230{,}400 \text{ kWh}
$$

This energy is now treated as off-peak (battery-discharged) at ₹ 4.20/kWh. Note: the *generation* still occurs during daylight (normal slot), but for billing we count energy consumed during off-peak equivalent. The simplification is that the solar generation directly displaces 30% of the normal-rate energy.

Revised billed consumption:

- Peak: $96{,}000$ kWh @ ₹ 8.50
- Normal: $768{,}000 \times 0.70 = 537{,}600$ kWh @ ₹ 6.30
- Off-peak equivalent (from solar): $230{,}400$ kWh @ ₹ 4.20

$$
\text{Bill}_{\text{after}} = 96{,}000 \times 8.50 + 537{,}600 \times 6.30 + 230{,}400 \times 4.20
$$

$$
\text{Bill}_{\text{after}} = 816{,}000 + 3{,}386{,}880 + 967{,}680 = 5{,}170{,}560 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

### Step 3 — Annual Savings

$$
\text{Savings}_{\text{gross}} = 5{,}654{,}400 - 5{,}170{,}560 = 483{,}840 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

Net savings (after O&M):

$$
\text{Savings}_{\text{net}} = 483{,}840 - 400{,}000 = 83{,}840 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

### Step 4 — Simple Payback

$$
SPP = \dfrac{I}{\text{Net Savings}} = \dfrac{1{,}20{,}00{,}000}{83{,}840} \approx 14.31 \text{ years}
$$

### Step 5 — NPV

$$
NPV = -1{,}20{,}00{,}000 + 83{,}840 \times \dfrac{(1.10)^{15} - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^{15}} + \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^{15}}
$$

Compute the annuity factor:

$$
(1.10)^{15} = 4.1772
$$

$$
\text{Annuity PV factor} = \dfrac{4.1772 - 1}{0.10 \times 4.1772} = \dfrac{3.1772}{0.41772} \approx 7.6061
$$

Salvage PV:

$$
\dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{4.1772} \approx 1{,}91{,}517 \text{ ₹}
$$

Therefore:

$$
NPV = -1{,}20{,}00{,}000 + 83{,}840 \times 7.6061 + 1{,}91{,}517
$$

$$
NPV = -1{,}20{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}37{,}696 + 1{,}91{,}517 \approx -71{,}70{,}787 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Comment:** NPV is **negative** ⇒ project **NOT financially viable** purely on TOU arbitrage. The industry would need either a subsidy, carbon credit revenue, or a higher TOU differential to make the project viable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why negative NPV is pedagogically valuable:** It teaches the student that *not every green energy project passes the financial test on energy-pricing arbitrage alone*. KTU examiners love this nuance — you earn bonus marks by explaining it.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Components of Electricity Tariff

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Electricity Tariff] --> B[Fixed Charge]
    A --> C[Variable Charge]
    A --> D[Regulatory Levies]
    A --> E[PF Penalty or Rebate]
    B --> B1[Demand Charge per kW or kVA per month]
    B --> B2[Meter Rent]
    B --> B3[Fixed Customer Charge]
    C --> C1[Energy Charge per kWh]
    C --> C2[Fuel Cost Adjustment]
    C --> C3[Time of Use Differentials]
    D --> D1[Electricity Duty]
    D --> D2[Taxes and Cess]
    E --> E1[Below 0.85 PF triggers Penalty]
    E --> E2[Above 0.95 PF may get Rebate]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Pricing Strategy Classification

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Energy Pricing Strategies] --> P1[Cost Based]
    P --> P2[Demand Based]
    P --> P3[Social and Environmental Based]
    P1 --> P1A[Flat Tariff]
    P1 --> P1B[Two Part Tariff]
    P1 --> P1C[Three Part Tariff]
    P1 --> P1D[Marginal Cost Pricing]
    P2 --> P2A[Time of Use TOU]
    P2 --> P2B[Real Time Pricing RTP]
    P2 --> P2C[Critical Peak Pricing CPP]
    P2 --> P2D[Interruptible Tariff]
    P3 --> P3A[Social Cost Benefit]
    P3 --> P3B[Carbon Adjusted Tariff]
    P3 --> P3C[Subsidy Cross Tariff]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — TOU Tariff Slot Architecture (Typical Indian Utility)

```mermaid
graph TD
    H[24 Hour Day] --> S1[00:00 to 06:00 Night Off Peak]
    H --> S2[06:00 to 10:00 Morning Peak]
    H --> S3[10:00 to 18:00 Normal Slot]
    H --> S4[18:00 to 22:00 Evening Peak]
    H --> S5[22:00 to 24:00 Late Evening Off Peak]
    S1 --> R1[Rate Lowest]
    S2 --> R2[Rate Highest]
    S3 --> R3[Rate Moderate]
    S4 --> R4[Rate Highest]
    S5 --> R5[Rate Lowest]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Block Tariff Bill Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Units Consumed in kWh]) --> Q1{Which Block}
    Q1 -->|Block 1 upto 100 units| B1[Rate R1]
    Q1 -->|Block 2 101 to 300| B2[Rate R2]
    Q1 -->|Block 3 above 300| B3[Rate R3]
    B1 --> Sum[Bill equal to 100 times R1]
    B2 --> Sum
    B3 --> Sum
    Sum --> Add[Add Fixed Charge and Duty]
    Add --> End([Final Bill Amount])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for Block Tariff:**
> In a **rising block** tariff (Indian domestic), each block is charged at its own rate, NOT the highest. A common student mistake is to charge the *entire* consumption at the marginal slab rate.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**"Define Time-of-Use (TOU) tariff and state any two of its advantages."**

**Model Answer:**
TOU tariff is a multi-rate energy pricing structure in which the per-unit energy charge varies across different time windows of the day, typically distinguishing peak, shoulder, and off-peak hours. The rate is **pre-announced** by the utility and held constant for each window for a billing cycle (as opposed to RTP, which is hourly).

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Reflects true marginal cost** — generation cost during peak hours (using costly peaker plants) is genuinely higher; TOU passes this signal to consumers.
2. **Incentivises load shifting** — consumers move discretionary loads to off-peak, flattening the system load curve and **reducing overall system cost and emissions**.
3. (Optional 3rd point) **Defers capacity addition** — reduced peak demand postpones investment in new generation.

*(3 marks — 1.5 marks definition + 1.5 marks advantages)*

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**"Distinguish between flat rate tariff and two-part tariff."**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Flat Rate Tariff | Two-Part Tariff |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------|
| Structure | Single rate ₹ per kWh | Fixed charge + per-kWh charge |
| Recovers fixed cost | Indirectly averaged | Explicitly via fixed/demand component |
| Impact on Load Factor | No incentive to improve | Consumer rewarded for higher LF |
| Fairness | Penalises good-LF consumers | More equitable |
| Use case | Domestic, small loads | Industrial, commercial |

*(3 marks — table + one-line commentary)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **three main types of cost-based electricity tariffs** used in India. (7 marks)

**(b)** A factory has the following load data:

- Contract demand: 800 kVA
- Maximum demand recorded: 720 kVA
- Energy consumed: 4.5 lakh kWh per month
- PF during billing: 0.78 lag

Tariff applicable:
- Demand charge: ₹ 450 per kVA per month
- Energy charge: ₹ 6.80 per kWh
- PF penalty: $1.5\%$ on energy bill for every 0.01 fall below 0.85 PF

Compute:
1. Total monthly bill without PF correction.
2. Total monthly bill after installing capacitors to improve PF to 0.95.
3. Annual savings and the simple payback if capacitor cost is ₹ 9.5 lakh. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Three Cost-Based Tariffs (7 marks)

**1. Flat Rate Tariff**
A single uniform rate $R$ (₹/kWh) is charged regardless of consumption or load factor.

$$
\text{Bill} = R \times E
$$

*Limitation:* No incentive for load factor improvement; cross-subsidises inefficient consumers.

**2. Two-Part Tariff**
Bill comprises a fixed component (on maximum demand $D$) and a variable component (on energy $E$):

$$
\text{Bill} = A \cdot D + B \cdot E
$$

where $A$ is in ₹/kVA/month and $B$ is in ₹/kWh. Standard industrial tariff in India.

**3. Block-Rate Tariff (Three-Part / Inclining Block)**
Energy is divided into blocks; each block has a different rate:

$$
\text{Bill} = R_1 E_1 + R_2 E_2 + \dots + R_n E_n
$$

Encourages conservation by making higher consumption costlier per unit. Used for domestic and small commercial consumers.

*(Sketches may be a bar chart of slab rates — typical representation in KTU answer sheets.)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given data:**
- Contract demand = 800 kVA, Recorded MD = 720 kVA
- Billing demand = max(MD, 80% of contract demand) = max(720, 640) = **720 kVA**
- Energy = 4,50,000 kWh/month
- PF = 0.78 lag (below reference 0.85 ⇒ penalty applies)
- Tariff: ₹450/kVA, ₹6.80/kWh, 1.5% penalty per 0.01 PF fall below 0.85

**Step 1 — Bill without PF correction**

Demand charge:

$$
720 \times 450 = 3{,}24{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

Energy charge:

$$
4{,}50{,}000 \times 6.80 = 30{,}60{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

PF deviation from 0.85:

$$
\Delta PF = 0.85 - 0.78 = 0.07
$$

Number of 0.01 units of deviation:

$$
n = 7
$$

Penalty rate:

$$
7 \times 1.5\% = 10.5\%
$$

Penalty amount:

$$
0.105 \times 30{,}60{,}000 = 3{,}21{,}300 \text{ ₹}
$$

Total bill:

$$
\boxed{3{,}24{,}000 + 30{,}60{,}000 + 3{,}21{,}300 = 37{,}05{,}300 \text{ ₹/month}}
$$

**Step 2 — Bill after PF correction to 0.95**

New billing demand at 0.95 PF (since kW remains same; kVA reduces):

Original kW = $720 \times 0.78 = 561.6$ kW.

New kVA at 0.95 PF:

$$
D_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{561.6}{0.95} = 591.16 \text{ kVA}
$$

Demand charge (new):

$$
591.16 \times 450 \approx 2{,}66{,}022 \text{ ₹}
$$

Energy charge: same energy, same kWh rate = ₹ 30,60,000
PF penalty: 0.95 > 0.85 ⇒ **no penalty**.

Total new bill:

$$
\boxed{2{,}66{,}022 + 30{,}60{,}000 = 33{,}26{,}022 \text{ ₹/month}}
$$

**Step 3 — Monthly and Annual Savings**

Monthly savings:

$$
37{,}05{,}300 - 33{,}26{,}022 = 3{,}79{,}278 \text{ ₹}
$$

Annual savings:

$$
3{,}79{,}278 \times 12 = 45{,}51{,}336 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Simple Payback**

$$
SPP = \dfrac{9{,}50{,}000}{45{,}51{,}336} \approx 0.209 \text{ years} \approx 2.5 \text{ months}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The capacitor installation is **highly attractive** with a payback of under 3 months, primarily driven by PF penalty elimination.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]`

**(a)** Explain the **concept of marginal cost pricing** and contrast it with **average cost pricing**. Under what conditions does each method recover the utility's total cost? (7 marks)

**(b)** An industry consumes 30 lakh kWh per year under a flat rate of ₹ 7.20/kWh. The utility proposes a new three-part tariff:
- Fixed charge: ₹ 8,000/month
- Demand charge: ₹ 350/kVA/month
- Energy charge: ₹ 5.90/kWh

The industry's maximum demand is 250 kVA at 0.82 PF.

Compute:
1. Annual bill under existing flat tariff.
2. Annual bill under proposed tariff.
3. Whether switching to the new tariff is beneficial. If yes, compute the simple payback on a ₹ 4.5 lakh investment required for tariff-migration (metering changes, panel upgrades). (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Marginal vs Average Cost Pricing (7 marks)

**Marginal Cost Pricing (MCP):**
Tariff is set equal to the *incremental cost of producing one additional kWh* (i.e., the short-run marginal cost, or in long-run, the long-run marginal cost LRMC).

$$
P_{\text{MCP}} = \dfrac{dTC}{dE} = MC
$$

**Properties:**
- Promotes **allocative efficiency** (P = MC is the welfare-maximising condition).
- May **not recover total fixed cost** because MC < AC when there are economies of scale or when the utility is a natural monopoly.
- Requires **supplementary fixed/demand charges** to recover sunk capital.

**Average Cost Pricing (ACP):**

$$
P_{\text{ACP}} = \dfrac{TC}{E_{\text{total}}} = AC
$$

**Properties:**
- **Guarantees full cost recovery** by construction.
- However, it **overcharges** inframarginal consumers and **underprices** peak-period consumption — distorting demand signals.
- Static and insensitive to time-of-use.

**Reconciliation:** Modern hybrid tariffs (e.g., India's two-part or three-part tariff) effectively combine ACP (via fixed/demand charges to recover capital) with MCP (via energy charge differentiated by TOU).

#### Part (b) — Tariff Migration Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Annual bill under existing flat tariff**

$$
30{,}00{,}000 \times 7.20 = 2{,}16{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual bill under proposed tariff**

Fixed charge annual:

$$
8{,}000 \times 12 = 96{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

Demand charge annual (assume billing demand = 250 kVA):

$$
250 \times 350 \times 12 = 10{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

Energy charge annual:

$$
30{,}00{,}000 \times 5.90 = 1{,}77{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

Total new bill:

$$
96{,}000 + 10{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}77{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}88{,}46{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual savings**

$$
2{,}16{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}88{,}46{,}000 = 27{,}54{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

Simple Payback:

$$
SPP = \dfrac{4{,}50{,}000}{27{,}54{,}000} \approx 0.163 \text{ years} \approx 2 \text{ months}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Switching to the three-part tariff is **highly beneficial** with a payback of approximately **2 months** — assuming the industry does not drastically alter its operating profile.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in Energy Pricing Questions:**
>
> 1. **Block tariff misconception (–2 marks):** Charging the entire consumption at the highest slab rate instead of using the *incremental block* method. The 201st unit is charged at the 2nd-slab rate, not the 3rd.
>
> 2. **Billing demand confusion (–2 marks):** Forgetting the 80% of contract demand clause. If recorded MD = 100 kW but contract = 200 kVA, billing demand = 160 kVA, not 100.
>
> 3. **PF penalty formula (–2 marks):** Penalty is usually applied on the **energy bill component only**, not on the total bill including demand charges. Read the question carefully.
>
> 4. **Discount rate omission (–1 mark):** When asked for NPV, students forget to include salvage value's PV or use the wrong discount rate.
>
> 5. **Mixing cost concepts (–1 mark):** Confusing marginal cost with average cost, or fixed cost with capital cost in a one-line definition.
>
> 6. **Not drawing diagrams (–1 to 2 marks):** For 14-mark theory questions, KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly values schematic / block diagrams. Always include at least one hand-drawn (or Mermaid-style) figure.
>
> 7. **Skipping units in numericals (–1 mark):** Always carry the unit (₹, years, kWh, kVA) through every step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Energy Pricing (Module 1)**

- [ ] Energy pricing has **three functions**: revenue, allocative, distributional.
- [ ] Cost components are **fixed (capacity)** and **variable (energy)**.
- [ ] **Flat tariff** = single rate ₹/kWh; mathematically $R \times E$.
- [ ] **Two-part tariff** = $A \cdot D + B \cdot E$. Standard industrial.
- [ ] **Three-part / Block tariff** = sum over slabs at increasing/decreasing rates.
- [ ] **TOU tariff** = differentiated rates by time-of-day (peak / shoulder / off-peak).
- [ ] **RTP** = hourly SMP-based; **CPP** = flat with rare critical spikes; **Interruptible** = cheaper but utility can curtail.
- [ ] **Marginal Cost Pricing** = $P = MC$; efficiency maximising but may not recover fixed cost.
- [ ] **Average Cost Pricing** = $P = AC$; cost recovery guaranteed but inefficient.
- [ ] **PF penalty** is calculated on the **energy charge only**, per 0.01 fall below reference PF (typically 0.85 lag).
- [ ] **Billing demand** = max(actual MD, 80% of contract demand) unless otherwise stated.
- [ ] **Simple Payback** = $I / S$ (years). **NPV** requires the discount factor $(1+i)^{-t}$.
- [ ] **CRF** = $\dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ — converts $P$ to uniform annual $A$.
- [ ] **SFF** = $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ — converts $F$ to uniform annual $A$.
- [ ] For TOU tariff savings calculations, savings = $\sum (\text{rate differential}) \times (\text{shifted kWh})$.
- [ ] Indian utilities typically charge **demand charge in ₹/kVA** (not kW) — convert via PF.
- [ ] **LCOE** and **LCC** are the standard metrics for comparing self-generation vs grid.
- [ ] Always include **environmental externality** (carbon cost) when discussing modern tariffs.
- [ ] KTU 2024 expects **diagrams + numericals + theory** in a balanced 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Energy Security** is defined as the uninterrupted availability of energy sources at an affordable price in a sustainable manner, ensuring that a nation, region, or organization has reliable access to sufficient energy resources to meet current and future demand without exposing itself to unacceptable economic, geopolitical, or environmental risks.

According to the **International Energy Agency (IEA)**, energy security is broadly defined along two temporal dimensions:
- **Short-term energy security**: The ability of the energy system to react promptly to sudden imbalances between supply and demand (e.g., fuel shortages, price shocks).
- **Long-term energy security**: The ability of the energy system to support economic development and deliver energy services sustainably over the long horizon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus Definition:** Energy Security = **Availability** + **Accessibility** + **Affordability** + **Acceptability** (the "4 A's Framework"). Every KTU board question on this topic tests at least one of these four pillars.

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of Energy Security (4 A's Framework)

| Pillar | Meaning | Practical Example |
|--------|---------|-------------------|
| **Availability** | Physical existence of energy resources | Domestic coal reserves, solar insolation |
| **Accessibility** | Ability to extract, refine, and deliver energy | Pipeline networks, transmission grid |
| **Affordability** | Price within reach of consumers and industry | Subsidized LPG, regulated electricity tariffs |
| **Acceptability** | Social/environmental alignment | Low-emission renewables, clean coal tech |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Family Kitchen Pantry"**
>
> Imagine your home kitchen pantry. *Energy security* for a country is like ensuring your pantry is always stocked.
> - **Availability** = The food items are physically present in the pantry (you bought rice and dal).
> - **Accessibility** = The pantry door is unlocked and the shelves are reachable (no broken ladder).
> - **Affordability** = The groceries fit within your monthly budget.
> - **Acceptability** = The food is hygienic and culturally preferred (no spoiled items).
>
> If any one of these four fails — say, the rice is there but the door is jammed, or prices skyrocket — your household faces an "energy insecurity" event, just like a country facing a fuel crisis or a power blackout.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)

- **Energy Independence** does **NOT** equal Energy Security. A country can import **80%** of its energy yet remain secure (e.g., Japan, South Korea), provided imports are diversified and contracts are stable.
- The **Energy Security Index (ESI)** of a nation is quantified using parameters such as:
  * Import dependency ratio
  * Diversity of energy mix (Herfindahl-Hirschman Index for energy)
  * Strategic petroleum reserves (in days of net imports)
  * Per capita energy consumption
- **India's Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR)** capacity ≈ **5.5 million metric tonnes (MMT)** — equivalent to approximately **9.5 days** of national consumption (as of 2024).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Energy Security Equation (Conceptual Decomposition)

Energy Security is a **multi-dimensional function** that can be expressed operationally as:

$$ES = f(\text{Availability}, \text{Accessibility}, \text{Affordability}, \text{Acceptability}, \text{Diversity}, \text{Resilience})$$

### 2.1.1 Logical Steps Behind the Framework
1. **Step 1 — Resource Audit**: Identify domestic fossil reserves, renewable potential, and nuclear capacity.
2. **Step 2 — Demand-Supply Gap Analysis**: Forecast energy demand under Business-As-Usual (BAU) and aggressive-efficiency scenarios.
3. **Step 3 — Import Dependency Mapping**: Quantify the percentage of primary energy imported.
4. **Step 4 — Diversification Scoring**: Use the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) for the energy mix.
5. **Step 5 — Risk Mitigation Layer**: Add strategic reserves, demand response, and storage.
6. **Step 6 — Policy & Acceptability Filter**: Align with Paris Agreement NDCs, SDGs, and social acceptance.

## 2.2 Mathematical Quantification — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles every formula a KTU student **must memorize** for the Energy Security module. Table cells use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid markdown-breaking pipes.

| # | Formula / Metric | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Import Dependency Ratio (IDR)** | $IDR = \dfrac{\text{Energy Imports}}{\text{Total Primary Energy Supply}} \times 100$ | **%**; Lower = More Secure |
| 2 | **Energy Diversity (HHI)** | $HHI = \sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i^{\,2}$ where $s_i$ = share of $i$-th source | Dimensionless; HHI $\in [\tfrac{1}{N}, 1]$ |
| 3 | **Diversification Threshold (Ideal)** | $HHI_{ideal} = \dfrac{1}{N}$ (equal share) | Used as benchmark |
| 4 | **Reserve Coverage Ratio (RCR)** | $RCR = \dfrac{\text{Strategic Reserves}}{\text{Daily Net Imports}}$ | **Days** |
| 5 | **Energy Intensity (EI)** | $EI = \dfrac{\text{Total Primary Energy}}{\text{GDP}}$ | **TOE / Million \$** |
| 6 | **Self-Sufficiency Index (SSI)** | $SSI = 1 - IDR$ | Fraction (0 to 1) |
| 7 | **Supply Disruption Cost** | $SDC = P_{spike} \times Q_{unmet}$ | Currency units |
| 8 | **Energy Per Capita** | $EPC = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy Consumed}}{\text{Population}}$ | **kWh/person** or **TOE/person** |

### 2.2.1 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **HHI** is used by energy planners to evaluate how concentrated (risky) or diversified (resilient) a national fuel mix is. India targets an HHI drop via the **500 GW non-fossil capacity** target by 2030.
- **RCR** drives the size of strategic reserves. IEA mandates member nations to maintain **≥ 90 days** of net imports; India is rapidly expanding to this benchmark.
- **EI** is used by the World Bank and IEA to benchmark industrial efficiency — a key lever to enhance energy security *without* importing more fuel.

## 2.3 Strategic Approaches to Energy Security

1. **Diversification of Supply Sources** — Multiple countries, multiple fuels.
2. **Domestic Resource Optimization** — Coal-bed methane, tight gas, offshore blocks.
3. **Renewable Energy Expansion** — Solar, wind, hydro, biomass.
4. **Energy Efficiency & Conservation** — PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme, ECBC norms.
5. **Strategic Petroleum Reserves** — Underground salt cavern storage.
6. **Demand-Side Management (DSM)** — Time-of-Day tariffs, smart grids.
7. **Indigenous Technology Development** — Hydrogen mission, small modular reactors (SMRs).
8. **Diplomatic & Trade Agreements** — Long-term LNG contracts (e.g., Qatar, USA).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Herfindahl-Hirschman Energy Index (HHI)

The HHI is borrowed from industrial economics and adapted to energy-mix concentration. We derive its form below.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the share vector.**
Let a country have $N$ primary energy sources. The share of the $i$-th source is:

$$s_i = \dfrac{E_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} E_j}, \quad \text{where} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i = 1$$

**Step 2 — Define concentration as a quadratic form.**
The energy planner wishes to capture the "concentration" of the mix. The simplest symmetric, non-negative function that does so is the sum of squared shares:

$$C = \sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i^{\,2}$$

**Step 3 — Boundary conditions.**
- *Maximum concentration* (single source dominates): $s_1 = 1$, others $\to 0$. Then $C_{max} = 1^2 = 1$.
- *Minimum concentration* (perfectly equal share): $s_i = \tfrac{1}{N}$ for all $i$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{min} &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(\dfrac{1}{N}\right)^{2} = N \cdot \dfrac{1}{N^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Normalize to [0,1] (optional).**
A normalized concentration index is:

$$C_{norm} = \dfrac{C - \tfrac{1}{N}}{1 - \tfrac{1}{N}}$$

This is sometimes called the **Energy Diversification Index (EDI)** in reverse (security = $1 - C_{norm}$).

**Step 5 — Worked Example.**
Consider a national mix with shares: Coal 50%, Oil 25%, Gas 10%, Renewables 10%, Nuclear 5%.

$$
\begin{aligned}
HHI &= (0.50)^{2} + (0.25)^{2} + (0.10)^{2} + (0.10)^{2} + (0.05)^{2} \\
&= 0.2500 + 0.0625 + 0.0100 + 0.0100 + 0.0025 \\
&= 0.3350
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpretation: An HHI of 0.335 indicates a **moderately concentrated** mix (coal-dominated). The ideal benchmark for 5 sources is $1/5 = 0.20$. The gap $0.335 - 0.20 = 0.135$ reflects concentration risk.

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Intensity Reduction Trajectory

Energy intensity is the key metric that ties economic growth to energy demand.

**Step 1 — Define.**

$$EI(t) = \dfrac{E_{total}(t)}{GDP(t)}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d(EI)}{dt} &= \dfrac{1}{GDP}\dfrac{dE}{dt} - \dfrac{E}{GDP^{2}}\dfrac{d(GDP)}{dt} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{E}{GDP}\left[\dfrac{1}{E}\dfrac{dE}{dt} - \dfrac{1}{GDP}\dfrac{d(GDP)}{dt}\right] \\[6pt]
&= EI \cdot \left[\epsilon_{E} - g\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\epsilon_{E}$ = growth rate of energy demand and $g$ = real GDP growth rate.

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**
If $g > \epsilon_{E}$ (GDP grows faster than energy demand), then $\tfrac{d(EI)}{dt} < 0$, i.e., energy intensity falls — this is the **"decoupling"** that underlies energy security gains.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — India's Energy Security Snapshot

| Parameter | India (Approx., 2024) | Developed Benchmark | Security Implication |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------|-----------------------|
| Import Dependency (oil) | **~85%** | USA ~10%, Japan ~90% (but stable contracts) | High vulnerability to price shocks |
| Import Dependency (natural gas) | **~50%** | EU ~80% | Moderate risk |
| Coal Self-Sufficiency | **~95%** | — | Strategic advantage |
| Renewable Share (installed capacity) | **~43%** | Target 50% by 2030 | Improving rapidly |
| Strategic Petroleum Reserve | **9.5 days** | IEA mandate: 90 days | **Critical gap** |
| Energy Intensity (TOE/\$1000 GDP) | **~0.20** | Global avg ~0.19 | Approaching global parity |
| Per Capita Electricity | **~1300 kWh** | Global avg ~3500 kWh | Demand growth = future stress |

## 3.4 KTU High-Yield Numerical Template

A typical 7-mark Part B question asks: *"A nation's energy mix is Coal 60%, Oil 20%, Gas 10%, Nuclear 5%, Renewables 5%. Compute the HHI and comment on energy security."*

**Model Answer Skeleton:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given shares: } & s_1 = 0.60,\; s_2 = 0.20,\; s_3 = 0.10,\; s_4 = 0.05,\; s_5 = 0.05 \\
HHI &= (0.60)^2 + (0.20)^2 + (0.10)^2 + (0.05)^2 + (0.05)^2 \\
&= 0.36 + 0.04 + 0.01 + 0.0025 + 0.0025 \\
&= 0.4150 \\
\text{Ideal HHI (N=5)} &= \tfrac{1}{5} = 0.2000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment**: The HHI of 0.4150 is more than double the ideal benchmark, indicating a highly coal-concentrated energy mix with significant supply-side vulnerability. To enhance energy security, the planner should target an aggressive renewable expansion and natural gas pipeline network to flatten the share distribution.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Energy Security System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Domestic Energy Sources] --> C[Energy System Core]
    B[Imported Energy Sources] --> C
    C --> D{4 As Security Filter}
    D --> E[Availability Check]
    D --> F[Accessibility Check]
    D --> G[Affordability Check]
    D --> H[Acceptability Check]
    E --> I[Strategic Reserve Layer]
    F --> J[Infrastructure Network Layer]
    G --> K[Subsidy and Tariff Layer]
    H --> L[Policy and NDC Compliance Layer]
    I --> M[Energy Security Outcome]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Resilient and Secure Energy Supply]
    P[External Shocks] -.-> C
    Q[Climate Targets] -.-> C
```

## 4.2 Energy Security Risk-Response Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    r1[Supply Shock Detected] --> r2{Reserves Available}
    r2 -- Yes --> r3[Draw from Strategic Reserve]
    r2 -- No --> r4[Activate Demand Response]
    r3 --> r5[Stabilize Price]
    r4 --> r5
    r5 --> r6[Restore Energy Security]
    s1[Diversification Plan] -.-> r2
    s2[Renewable Capacity] -.-> r2
    s3[Long-term Contracts] -.-> r2
```

## 4.3 Mapping the Four Pillars to Indian Policy Levers

```mermaid
graph TB
    P1[Availability] --> L1[Domestic Coal and Renewables]
    P1 --> L2[Strategic Petroleum Reserves]
    P2[Accessibility] --> L3[Pipeline Network]
    P2 --> L4[Smart Grid Infrastructure]
    P3[Affordability] --> L5[Subsidies and DBT]
    P3 --> L6[Regulated Tariff Policy]
    P4[Acceptability] --> L7[National Hydrogen Mission]
    P4 --> L8[500 GW Renewable Target]
    L1 --> OUT[Energy Security for India]
    L2 --> OUT
    L3 --> OUT
    L4 --> OUT
    L5 --> OUT
    L6 --> OUT
    L7 --> OUT
    L8 --> OUT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define energy security and list its four pillars.**

**Model Answer:**
Energy security is the uninterrupted availability of energy sources at an affordable price in a sustainable manner, with minimal exposure to geopolitical, economic, and environmental risks. The four pillars are:
1. **Availability** — physical presence of energy resources.
2. **Accessibility** — ability to deliver energy to end-users.
3. **Affordability** — pricing within reach of consumers.
4. **Acceptability** — alignment with environmental and social norms.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index in the context of energy security?**

**Model Answer:**
The Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) is a quantitative measure of concentration in a nation's energy mix. It is the sum of the squared shares of each energy source:

$$HHI = \sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i^{\,2}$$

A higher HHI indicates greater concentration (less secure), while a lower HHI closer to $1/N$ indicates better diversification and hence higher energy security.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1 Choice A]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the four pillars of energy security. Discuss how each pillar is addressed in India's energy policy.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The energy mix of a country is as follows: Coal 55%, Oil 25%, Natural Gas 10%, Renewables 7%, Nuclear 3%. Calculate the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index and comment on the country's energy security. Suggest two policy interventions.

**Model Answer — (a):**

The **four pillars of energy security** form the analytical foundation of any national energy policy:

1. **Availability** ensures that energy resources physically exist — domestic reserves of coal, oil, gas, uranium, plus the renewable potential of solar, wind, hydro, and biomass. In India, availability is being strengthened through the **Coal India expansion**, **Hydrocarbon Exploration and Licensing Policy (HELP)**, and the **National Solar Mission**.

2. **Accessibility** refers to the infrastructure that moves energy from source to consumer — pipelines, transmission lines, refineries, and storage. India addresses this through the **PMUY (Pradhan Mantri Ujjwala Yojana)** for clean cooking fuel access, the **city gas distribution (CGD) network expansion**, and the **One Nation One Grid** initiative.

3. **Affordability** ensures that prices remain within the paying capacity of households and industry. India uses **Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT)** for LPG subsidies, **time-of-day tariffs**, and the **UDAY scheme** for DISCOM financial turnaround.

4. **Acceptability** demands alignment with climate, environment, and social goals. India pursues this through its **Panchamrit** pledge at COP-26 (500 GW non-fossil by 2030), the **Green Hydrogen Mission**, and adherence to **Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)**.

**Model Answer — (b):**

Given shares: $s_1 = 0.55,\; s_2 = 0.25,\; s_3 = 0.10,\; s_4 = 0.07,\; s_5 = 0.03$

$$
\begin{aligned}
HHI &= (0.55)^2 + (0.25)^2 + (0.10)^2 + (0.07)^2 + (0.03)^2 \\
&= 0.3025 + 0.0625 + 0.0100 + 0.0049 + 0.0009 \\
&= 0.3808
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ideal benchmark** for $N = 5$ sources: $HHI_{ideal} = 1/5 = 0.2000$

**Comment**: The computed HHI of **0.3808** is nearly **1.9 times** the ideal benchmark, signalling a highly coal-concentrated mix with elevated supply-side vulnerability. Two recommended interventions:
1. **Accelerate Renewable Capacity Addition** to push the renewables share above 25% by 2030, thereby flattening the share distribution.
2. **Expand Strategic Petroleum Reserves** and sign long-term LNG contracts to cushion oil-price shocks and reduce import-concentration risk.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice B]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between energy security and energy independence. Why is energy independence not a necessary condition for energy security?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A nation consumes 500 MTOE of primary energy, of which 300 MTOE is imported. Its GDP is 2.5 trillion USD. The population is 1.4 billion. Calculate: (i) Import Dependency Ratio, (ii) Self-Sufficiency Index, (iii) Energy Intensity, (iv) Per Capita Energy. Comment on the energy security implications.

**Model Answer — (a):**

| Parameter | Energy Security | Energy Independence |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| **Definition** | Reliable, affordable, sustainable access to energy | Self-reliance with zero imports |
| **Scope** | Includes imports, contracts, diversification | Excludes all imports |
| **Goal** | Resilience + affordability | Autarky |
| **Examples** | Japan, South Korea | None fully; Norway is close |

Energy independence is **not necessary** for energy security because:
- Global trade and long-term contracts (e.g., Japan-Qatar LNG) can provide **stable, diversified, affordable** supply.
- For resource-poor but capital-rich nations, importing energy is economically rational.
- Energy security is fundamentally about **risk management**, not autarky.

**Model Answer — (b):**

Given: $E_{total} = 500$ MTOE, $E_{import} = 300$ MTOE, $GDP = 2.5$ trillion USD, $P = 1.4$ billion.

**(i) Import Dependency Ratio (IDR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IDR &= \dfrac{300}{500} \times 100 = 60\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Self-Sufficiency Index (SSI):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SSI &= 1 - 0.60 = 0.40
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Energy Intensity (EI):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI &= \dfrac{500 \text{ MTOE}}{2500 \text{ billion USD}} = 0.20 \text{ TOE per 1000 USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Per Capita Energy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EPC &= \dfrac{500 \text{ MTOE}}{1.4 \text{ billion}} \approx 0.357 \text{ TOE/person}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Security Implications**: A 60% IDR is **moderately high** and exposes the country to global price volatility. The low per capita consumption (0.357 TOE) signals under-development — energy demand will **rise** as the economy grows, deepening import dependence unless renewable capacity and efficiency policies scale up rapidly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> - **Common Mistake #1**: Students often confuse **energy security** with **energy independence** and write one-word answers; the board deducts **2 marks** for not addressing the four pillars.
> - **Common Mistake #2**: In HHI numericals, students forget to verify $\sum s_i = 1$. Always re-check shares sum to unity before squaring.
> - **Common Mistake #3**: When asked for "strategic interventions," students list generic answers ("use renewables") without quantifying the target share — the board expects **policy-specific** answers (e.g., *500 GW non-fossil by 2030*).
> - **Common Mistake #4**: Mixing up units — *TOE*, *MTOE*, *kWh* — costs **0.5 to 1 mark** in numericals. Always write the unit in the final answer.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ☐ **Energy Security ≠ Energy Independence**; security is multidimensional.
- ☐ Four pillars: **Availability, Accessibility, Affordability, Acceptability**.
- ☐ **HHI** formula: $HHI = \sum s_i^{\,2}$; ideal benchmark $= 1/N$.
- ☐ **IDR** formula and **SSI = 1 − IDR**.
- ☐ **Energy Intensity** = $E_{total} / GDP$; lower is more secure and efficient.
- ☐ India targets **500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030** and **Net-Zero by 2070**.
- ☐ IEA's **90-day reserve benchmark**; India is currently at **~9.5 days** — a strategic gap.
- ☐ **Decoupling**: GDP grows faster than energy demand → energy security improves.
- ☐ **Diversification** (multi-fuel, multi-source) is the single most-cited KTU answer.
- ☐ Strategic reserves, demand-side management, and renewables are the **three pillars of response** to supply shocks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Conservation and Its Importance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Energy Conservation and Its Importance ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Energy Conservation** is defined as the practice, principle, and strategic policy of reducing the quantity of energy required to perform a given task, deliver a service, or manufacture a product, through the intelligent application of technology, behavioural modification, and improved operational methodologies. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for the course *PESFT741 - Energy Management and Conservation*, energy conservation is positioned as the **foundational pillar** of any sustainable industrial and domestic energy strategy. It is distinct from *energy generation* (creating new energy) and *energy substitution* (replacing one fuel with another); conservation fundamentally aims to **avoid wasteful energy use** at the source itself. Mathematically expressed at its simplest, conservation of energy follows the **First Law of Thermodynamics**: > The total energy of an isolated system is **constant**; energy can be neither created nor destroyed, only **transformed** from one form to another. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition Snapshot:** Energy Conservation = (Energy Input − Energy Wasted) / Energy Input, optimized for maximum service output per unit of energy consumed. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaky Water Tank" Model Imagine your home has a **water storage tank (Energy Source)** on the roof. Every day, the municipal corporation (Energy Supplier) pumps water (Energy) up to the tank. From this tank, water flows through pipes to your kitchen, bathroom, and garden (Useful Work). Now, observe two scenarios: - **Scenario A — No Conservation (The Leaky Tank):** The tank has cracks, the pipes leak, the garden sprinkler runs all night, and the tap drips continuously. To meet your *actual needs* (a bath and cooking), you must waste massive amounts of water, and the supplier must pump much more. **Cost = High; Resource depletion = High.** - **Scenario B — Energy Conservation Applied:** The cracks are sealed (Maintenance), pipes are insulated (Efficiency), the sprinkler has a timer (Control), and the dripping tap is fixed (Behavioural change). Now, the *same tank* with *less pumped water* serves *all your needs perfectly*. **Cost = Low; Resource depletion = Low.** **The Leak in the Tank is "Wasted Energy."** Energy conservation simply means **plugging the leaks** — getting the maximum useful work out of every joule of energy we already have. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Summary:** Energy conservation is about **doing more with less**, not about doing less overall. It is the smart, ethical, and economical use of energy to avoid unnecessary losses. ### 1.3 Why "Conservation" ≠ "Sacrifice" (Clarifying a Common Misconception) A frequent student misconception is confusing "conservation" with "curtailment" or "rationing." In KTU parlance: | Term | Meaning | Example | |---|---|---| | **Conservation** | Using energy more *efficiently* | Switching to LED bulbs (same light, 80% less electricity) | | **Curtailment** | Using energy *less* | Sitting in the dark instead of switching on lights | | **Substitution** | Using a *different* energy form | Solar water heater instead of electric geyser | > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** If an exam question asks for the *difference between energy conservation and energy efficiency*, the most accurate KTU-standard answer is: *Energy efficiency is a subset/method of achieving energy conservation. Conservation is the broader goal; efficiency is one of the tools to reach it.* ### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Highlighted for Memorization) The following are the **standard metrics** every KTU student must memorize verbatim: - **Energy Efficiency (η):** Ratio of useful energy output to total energy input, expressed as a percentage. Unit: **dimensionless (%)**. - **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** Energy consumed per unit of production (e.g., kWh per ton of steel). Unit: **kWh/ton**. - **Energy Intensity (EI):** Energy consumed per unit of GDP or per capita. Unit: **toe/GDP or MJ/₹**. - **Payback Period:** Time required for energy savings to equal the initial investment cost. Unit: **years**. - **Return on Investment (ROI):** Financial gain from energy savings. Unit: **% per annum**. These metrics act as the **universal language** of energy management. Mastery of these terms is mandatory for scoring in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Energy Conservation (Structured Logic) Energy conservation, as a discipline, rests on **three operational pillars**. Every practical KTU question can be mapped to one or more of these: **Pillar 1 — Reduction of Energy Waste (Elimination of Losses)** - Identify and eliminate every form of waste heat, friction, leakage, radiation, and inefficient combustion. - *Why it matters:* In Indian industries, **30–50% of input energy** is typically lost as waste heat, flue gases, or inefficient processes. Even a 10% reduction yields massive national-level savings. - *How it is done:* Regular energy audits, waste heat recovery systems, insulation upgrades, leak detection programs. **Pillar 2 — Improvement of Energy Efficiency (Doing More with Less)** - Upgrade to high-efficiency equipment: IE3/IE4 motors, variable frequency drives (VFDs), LED lighting, inverter air conditioners, high-efficiency boilers. - *Why it matters:* Efficiency improvements reduce the **specific energy consumption** of every unit of output, lowering both cost and emissions simultaneously. - *How it is done:* Equipment retrofitting, process optimization, adoption of Best Available Techniques (BAT). **Pillar 3 — Behavioural and Operational Modification (Human Factor)** - Switch off idle equipment, optimize process schedules, train operators, and cultivate an energy-conscious culture. - *Why it matters:* Studies by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), Government of India** show that behavioural changes alone can deliver **5–15% energy savings** at zero capital cost. - *How it is done:* Awareness campaigns, ISO 50001 energy management systems, operator training, posted reminders. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Favourite Question Pattern:** "Discuss the three pillars of energy conservation with industrial examples." — Prepare this with confidence. ### 2.2 Importance of Energy Conservation — The "Five National Imperatives" Energy conservation is not merely an engineering choice; it is a **national strategic necessity**. For a B.Tech student, understanding the importance requires a multi-dimensional view: 1. **Economic Imperative:** India imports over **85% of its crude oil** and significant quantities of natural gas and coal. Every unit of energy saved is a unit of foreign exchange preserved. For a country importing fuels worth billions of dollars annually, conservation directly strengthens the **Balance of Payments**. 2. **Environmental Imperative:** Every kWh of electricity saved in a coal-fired power plant avoids approximately **0.9 kg of CO₂ emissions** (based on India's average grid emission factor of ~0.7–0.9 tCO₂/MWh). Conservation directly addresses **climate change, air pollution, and acid rain**. 3. **Energy Security Imperative:** Reduced demand reduces dependence on volatile international fuel markets, insulating the national economy from price shocks and supply disruptions. 4. **Social Imperative:** Access to affordable energy is a prerequisite for poverty alleviation, healthcare, education, and quality of life. Conservation allows scarce energy resources to serve more people. 5. **Industrial Competitiveness Imperative:** High energy costs erode the profit margins of industries. Energy-efficient firms are globally competitive. Energy conservation is a **profit-enhancement strategy**, not just a cost-cutting one. ### 2.3 The Energy Hierarchy (5R Model — High-Yield Concept) The **5R Model** is a globally accepted framework for energy management priority, and KTU expects students to know it cold: | Priority Level | Action | Description | Cost Implication | |---|---|---|---| | **R1** | **Reduce** | Use less energy by avoiding waste | Lowest cost | | **R2** | **Reuse** | Use waste energy for another purpose (e.g., waste heat recovery) | Moderate cost | | **R3** | **Recycle** | Convert waste energy back to useful form (e.g., cogeneration) | Moderate cost | | **R4** | **Recover** | Capture and store lost energy | Higher cost | | **R5** | **Replace** | Substitute with renewable or cleaner energy | Highest cost | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Golden Rule (Exam-Worthy):** Always apply the lowest-priority R first. The cheapest, most effective energy is the energy **never used** (Reduce > Replace). Renewable energy adoption comes *last* in this hierarchy because it is the most capital-intensive. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table) This consolidated table is your **go-to reference** for solving any numerical problem on this topic in the KTU 2024 University Examination. | # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units | Application / Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | First Law of Thermodynamics | $\Delta U = Q - W$ | kJ, kJ, kJ | Energy cannot be created or destroyed | | 2 | Energy Efficiency (η) | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{E_{input}} \times 100\%$ | **%** (dimensionless) | Always between 0 and 100% | | 3 | Energy Loss (L) | $L = E_{input} - E_{useful}$ | kWh, MJ, toe | Difference between input and useful output | | 4 | Loss Factor | $LF = 1 - \eta$ | dimensionless | Fraction of input energy wasted | | 5 | Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{consumed}}{\text{Production Output}}$ | kWh/ton, MJ/kg | Lower SEC = higher efficiency | | 6 | Annual Energy Cost Saving (AECS) | $AECS = \Delta E \times C_u$ | ₹/year | $\Delta E$ = energy saved; $C_u$ = unit cost | | 7 | Simple Payback Period (SPP) | $SPP = \dfrac{I}{AECS}$ | years | $I$ = investment; faster payback = better | | 8 | Return on Investment (ROI) | $ROI = \dfrac{AECS}{I} \times 100\%$ | **% per year** | Higher ROI = more attractive project | | 9 | Net Present Value (NPV) | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | ₹ | $CF_t$ = cash flow; $r$ = discount rate | | 10 | CO₂ Emission Reduction | $CO_2 = \Delta E \times EF$ | kg, tonnes | $EF$ = emission factor (kg CO₂/kWh) | | 11 | Energy Intensity of Economy | $EI = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy}}{\text{GDP}}$ | toe/₹ Crore | National-level metric | | 12 | Heat Value (Calorific Value) | $CV = \dfrac{\text{Heat released}}{\text{Mass of fuel}}$ | kJ/kg, kcal/m³ | Higher CV = more energy per unit fuel | | 13 | Equivalent Electrical Energy | $E_{eq} = m \times CV \times \eta_{conv}$ | kWh | $m$ = mass; $\eta_{conv}$ = conversion efficiency | > [!WARNING] > **Unit Conversion Trap:** KTU examiners frequently test unit conversions.
