## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*What is a "Designated Consumer" under the Energy Conservation Act 2001?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Designated Consumer is any industrial, commercial, or institutional establishment that has been formally notified by the Central Government in the Official Gazette under **Section 14** of the EC Act 2001 as a consumer of energy above prescribed thresholds. Such establishments are legally bound to appoint certified Energy Managers and Energy Auditors, conduct periodic energy audits, and comply with energy efficiency norms prescribed by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE). Currently, 15 industrial and commercial sectors (Aluminium, Cement, Fertilizer, Steel, Refineries, Railways, Airports, Hotels, Hospitals, Shopping Malls, etc.) are covered under this notification framework.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*State the role of the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
BEE is the statutory nodal agency established under **Section 3** of the EC Act 2001, with its headquarters in **New Delhi**. Its primary roles are:
1. Coordinating and implementing policies and programmes for efficient use of energy.
2. Notifying standards and labelling of energy-consuming appliances.
3. Promoting energy audits and certifying Energy Managers/Auditors.
4. Advising the Central Government on energy conservation matters.
5. Administering the Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder:** Part B questions in Module 1 carry **14 marks** split as **7 marks × 2 sub-parts**. Students must write approximately **5–6 pages** of answer script per Part B question.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Modeled Question]*

**(a)** Explain the salient features of the Energy Conservation Act 2001 in detail. [7 Marks]

**(b)** Discuss the role and functions of the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) in the context of the EC Act. [7 Marks]

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

The salient features of the Energy Conservation Act 2001 are as follows:

**(1) Statutory Framework for Energy Conservation:** The Act provides the first comprehensive legal framework in India for promoting efficient use of energy and its conservation, applicable to all sectors of the economy. [1 mark]

**(2) Establishment of Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE):** Section 3 of the Act establishes BEE as the central nodal agency headquartered in New Delhi, headed by a Director General appointed by the Central Government. BEE coordinates policy formulation, programme design, and stakeholder engagement. [1 mark]

**(3) Designation of Energy-Intensive Consumers:** Section 14 empowers the Central Government to notify energy-intensive industries and establishments as "Designated Consumers." Currently, 15 sectors are notified, covering aluminium, cement, fertilizers, iron & steel, pulp & paper, refineries, textiles, railways, airports, hotels, hospitals, and shopping malls. [1 mark]

**(4) Mandatory Energy Managers and Auditors:** Section 13 requires every Designated Consumer to appoint certified Energy Managers and accredited Energy Auditors. Certification is granted through examinations and accreditation processes administered by BEE and partner certifying bodies. Energy audits are mandated every 3 years. [1 mark]

**(5) Standards & Labelling Programme:** BEE administers the star rating system (1 to 5 stars) for notified appliances and equipment. Currently covering over 35 appliance categories, the programme enables consumer-driven efficiency improvements and has reduced national electricity demand by an estimated 60,000 MW equivalent cumulatively. [1 mark]

**(6) Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC):** Section 14(p) empowers the government to prescribe ECBC for commercial buildings. First notified in 2007 (now ECBC+ 2017), it applies to buildings with connected load ≥ 100 kW or conditioned area ≥ 500 m² and mandates 25–50% energy savings. [1 mark]

**(7) Central and State Energy Conservation Funds:** Sections 12 and 16 establish dedicated funds (CECF and SECF) financed through grants, fees, and penalties, ensuring financial sustainability of EC programmes. [0.5 mark]

**(8) Penalty Provisions:** Section 26 prescribes penalties (up to INR 1 crore post-2022 amendment) for non-compliance, with adjudication by designated officers and appeals to the Appellate Tribunal under Section 27. [0.5 mark]

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

The Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) plays the following roles and functions under the EC Act 2001:

**(1) Statutory Authority:** BEE is the **principal statutory body** created under Section 3 of the Act to spearhead India's energy efficiency mission. It functions as an attached office of the Ministry of Power with operational autonomy. [1 mark]

**(2) Policy Coordination and Advisory Role:** BEE advises the Central and State Governments on policies, programmes, and measures for energy conservation. It formulates national energy efficiency strategies and coordinates their implementation. [1 mark]

**(3) Standards & Labelling (S&L) Administration:** BEE designs, notifies, and updates the **mandatory star labelling programme** for over 35 categories of appliances. This enables consumers to make informed choices and drives manufacturers toward higher efficiency. [1 mark]

**(4) Energy Audit Ecosystem:** BEE accredits **Energy Auditing Firms and Individual Energy Auditors** through Quality Council of India / NABCB-accredited agencies. It administers the certification examinations for Energy Managers and Energy Auditors. [1 mark]

**(5) Designated Consumer Compliance:** BEE monitors compliance of notified Designated Consumers by reviewing their periodic energy returns, audit reports, and energy consumption baselines. [1 mark]

**(6) Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC):** BEE developed ECBC for commercial buildings (2007) and its upgraded version ECBC+ (2017). It trains municipal authorities and architects on ECBC compliance. [1 mark]

**(7) Schemes and Programmes:** BEE implements flagship schemes such as **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme**, **UJALA LED distribution**, **Standards & Labelling**, **ECBC compliance**, and now the **Indian Carbon Market (2022 amendment)**. [1 mark]

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative Choice for Internal Selection]*

**(a)** Describe the provisions of the Energy Conservation Act 2001 related to the appointment of Energy Managers and Energy Auditors in Designated Consumers. [7 Marks]

**(b)** Explain the major amendments brought in by the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022 and their significance. [7 Marks]

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

The EC Act 2001 contains detailed provisions for the certification and deployment of energy professionals in Designated Consumers:

**(1) Statutory Mandate (Section 13):** Every Designated Consumer is mandated to employ **one or more certified Energy Managers** who shall be responsible for the energy management activities of the establishment. [1 mark]

**(2) Certification Authority (Section 13(1)):** The Central Government notifies a **Certifying Authority** to conduct examinations for Energy Managers and Energy Auditors. Initially, this function was carried out by BEE; later, it was restructured to allow multiple certifying bodies accredited by the National Accreditation Board for Certification Bodies (NABCB). [1 mark]

**(3) Qualifications and Examination:** Candidates must possess a **bachelor's degree in engineering or equivalent** with prescribed minimum experience, and pass the **National Certification Examination** conducted by the Certifying Authority. [1 mark]

**(4) Mandatory Energy Audit (Section 14(e)):** Every Designated Consumer must conduct an **energy audit** at intervals prescribed by the government (currently every 3 years) by an accredited Energy Auditor. [1 mark]

**(5) Accreditation of Energy Auditors:** BEE, in coordination with the Quality Council of India, accredits Energy Auditors and Energy Auditing Firms under a **graded system (Grade 1, 2, 3)** based on qualifications, experience, and team capability. [1 mark]

**(6) Duties of Energy Manager:** The certified Energy Manager is responsible for:
- Implementing energy conservation measures.
- Maintaining energy consumption records.
- Filing periodic energy returns to BEE.
- Coordinating with the Energy Auditor for audit activities.
- Recommending energy-efficient technologies. [1 mark]

**(7) Penalty for Non-Appointment:** Failure to appoint an Energy Manager or conduct an energy audit attracts penalty under Section 26, with adjudication by the Designated Officer. [1 mark]

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

The Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act, 2022 brought in transformative changes to address India's climate commitments and emerging carbon economy:

**(1) Indian Carbon Market (ICM):** The amendment introduced a **legal framework for trading Carbon Credit Certificates**. Energy-intensive industries will receive tradable certificates for reducing their carbon intensity below baselines, creating a market-based incentive for decarbonization. This is operationalized through the **Carbon Credit Trading Scheme, 2023**. [1.5 marks]

**(2) Mandate for Non-Fossil Energy Sources:** Designated Consumers in industrial and commercial sectors are now mandated to source a **minimum percentage of their energy from non-fossil sources** (renewables, green hydrogen, nuclear). The exact percentage is notified by the Central Government based on sectoral feasibility. [1.5 marks]

**(3) Extension of ECBC to Residential Buildings:** Prior to 2022, ECBC applied only to commercial buildings. The amendment empowers the government to prescribe energy conservation standards for **residential buildings** as well, operationalized through the **Eco Niwas Samhita** and state-level by-laws. [1.5 marks]

**(4) Enhanced Penalty Provisions:** The penalty ceiling has been raised significantly — from the earlier INR 10 lakh to up to **INR 1 crore per violation** for repeated non-compliance, with imprisonment extending up to 3 years for the most severe cases. [1 mark]

**(5) Energy Service Companies (ESCOs):** The amendment formally recognizes ESCOs as **accredited participants** in delivering energy efficiency projects, thereby strengthening the third-party energy services market. [1 mark]

**(6) Significance:** Collectively, the 2022 amendment transforms the EC Act from a purely energy-efficiency-focused statute into a **climate-aligned, market-driven decarbonization instrument**, aligned with India's **net-zero 2070** commitment and **Panchamrit** targets announced at COP-26. [0.5 mark]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "BEE established by EC Act" with "BEE established by notification":** BEE is established **statutorily under Section 3 of the Act**, not by executive order. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this error.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Section References:** Every answer on EC Act features must cite the relevant **Section number** (Sec. 3 for BEE, Sec. 14 for Designated Consumers, Sec. 13 for Energy Managers, Sec. 26 for Penalties). A 14-mark answer without section citations loses at least 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Treating EC Act and ECBC as the Same:** ECBC is **a code notified under the EC Act**, not the Act itself. Students often write "ECBC is the Act" — this is a factual error.
>
> 4. **Missing the 2022 Amendment:** KTU 2024 examiners expect the **2022 Amendment's three headline additions** (Carbon Market, Non-fossil mandate, Residential ECBC scope) in any question on "recent developments" or "features of the EC Act."
>
> 5. **Confusing Designated Consumers with all industries:** Not every industry is a Designated Consumer. Only those notified by the Central Government fall under the obligation framework.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Pin These to Your Memory:**

- ⚡ EC Act 2001 = **Act No. 52 of 2001**, assented **29 Sept 2001**, enforced **1 March 2002**, under **Ministry of Power**.
- ⚡ **BEE** is the statutory nodal agency under **Section 3**, HQ **New Delhi**, headed by a **Director General**.
- ⚡ **Designated Consumers** are notified under **Section 14** — currently **15 sectors** including Aluminium, Cement, Fertilizer, Steel, Refineries, Railways, Airports, Hotels, Hospitals.
- ⚡ Every Designated Consumer must appoint a **certified Energy Manager** (Section 13) and conduct **mandatory energy audit every 3 years**.
- ⚡ **Standards & Labelling** = 1–5 star rating for **35+ appliance categories**.
- ⚡ **ECBC** applies to commercial buildings ≥ 100 kW or 500 m² → **25–50% energy savings**.
- ⚡ **Penalty** under Section 26 — up to **INR 1 crore** post-2022 amendment.
- ⚡ **2010 Amendment** strengthened penalties and expanded Designated Consumer scope.
- ⚡ **2022 Amendment** added **(1) Indian Carbon Market (ICM)**, **(2) Non-fossil energy mandate**, **(3) ECBC for residential buildings**, **(4) Enhanced penalties**, **(5) ESCO recognition**.
- ⚡ Appellate mechanism under **Section 27** → **Appellate Tribunal for Electricity**.
- ⚡ **Central EC Fund (Sec. 12)** and **State EC Funds (Sec. 16)** finance EC programmes.
- ⚡ Cumulative avoided generation through BEE programmes ≈ **60,000 MW equivalent** (FY 2022–23).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Energy: Its Various Forms and Conservation: Electricity Basics - Direct Current and Alternative Currents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Energy: Its Various Forms and Conservation: Electricity Basics - Direct Current and Alternative Currents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Energy — The Fundamental Definition

**Energy** is defined as the capacity of a physical system to perform **work** or to transfer **heat**. In the International System of Units (SI), energy is measured in **Joules (J)**. From the perspective of thermodynamics and energy management, energy is the universal currency that links all physical, chemical, biological, and electrical processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Energy is the ability to do work or produce change. It is a scalar quantity (has magnitude only, no direction) and is conserved in all physical transformations — this is the **First Law of Thermodynamics**.

The mathematical expression for mechanical energy is given by:

$$E = F \cdot d$$

where $F$ is the applied force in Newtons (N) and $d$ is the displacement in metres (m). One Joule equals one Newton-metre: **1 J = 1 N·m**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Account of Nature"

Imagine energy as the **balance in a bank account**. You can have money in different "sub-accounts" (savings, current, fixed deposits), but the **total money is always the same** — it just changes form. Similarly, energy exists in many forms (kinetic, potential, electrical, thermal), but the **total energy of an isolated system never changes**. You can withdraw from one form and deposit into another, but the total balance remains constant. This is the **Law of Conservation of Energy**.

## 1.2 Various Forms of Energy

Energy manifests in numerous forms in nature and engineering systems. The major classifications are:

| Category | Form | Symbol/Unit | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical | Kinetic Energy | $\frac{1}{2}mv^2$ | Energy of motion |
| Mechanical | Potential Energy | $mgh$ | Stored energy of position |
| Thermal | Heat Energy | $Q = mc\Delta T$ | Random molecular motion |
| Electrical | Electrical Energy | $E = VIt$ | Flow of charge |
| Chemical | Chemical Energy | Stored in bonds | Released in reactions |
| Radiant | Light / EM Energy | $E = h\nu$ | Electromagnetic waves |
| Nuclear | Nuclear Energy | $E = \Delta mc^2$ | Mass–energy equivalence |
| Sound | Acoustic Energy | Pressure waves | Mechanical vibrations |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "list the forms of energy," examiners expect at least **six** distinct forms. Always classify them under **Kinetic, Potential, Thermal, Electrical, Chemical, Radiant, and Nuclear** categories for full credit.

## 1.3 Law of Conservation of Energy

The **Law of Conservation of Energy** states that energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another. The total energy of an isolated system remains constant.

$$E_{\text{total, initial}} = E_{\text{total, final}}$$

In differential form:

$$dE_{\text{system}} + dE_{\text{surroundings}} = 0$$

## 1.4 Electricity — The Backbone of Modern Energy Systems

**Electricity** is the flow of electric charge, typically through conductors, driven by a potential difference (voltage). It is one of the most versatile forms of energy because:
- It can be **generated** from many sources (thermal, hydro, solar, wind, nuclear).
- It can be **transmitted** over long distances with minimal losses.
- It can be **converted** efficiently into light, heat, motion, and sound.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** In energy management, electricity is the most monitored and optimized form of energy because it is the **final energy carrier** reaching end-users, and it is the easiest to measure, meter, and control.

## 1.5 Direct Current (DC) — Definition and Intuition

**Direct Current (DC)** is the unidirectional flow of electric charge, in which the current flows in a **single direction** with a **constant magnitude** (in ideal DC). The voltage polarity remains fixed over time.

**Analogy:** Imagine water flowing through a pipe in **one direction continuously** — like a mountain stream feeding a lake. The flow never reverses. That is DC.

Common sources of DC:
- Batteries (cells)
- Solar photovoltaic panels
- DC generators
- Rectified AC supplies

## 1.6 Alternating Current (AC) — Definition and Intuition

**Alternating Current (AC)** is the flow of electric charge that **periodically reverses direction**, with the magnitude varying sinusoidally with time. The voltage polarity alternates between positive and negative half-cycles.

**Analogy:** Imagine a piston pump pushing water **forward, then backward, then forward** in a rhythmic pattern — that is AC. The water still does useful work (it oscillates), but the net direction reverses.

Common sources of AC:
- AC generators (alternators)
- Power grid supply (50 Hz in India, 60 Hz in USA)
- Inverters (convert DC to AC)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Specific Note:** In India, the standard AC supply frequency is **50 Hz**, supplied as **230 V single-phase** for domestic and **415 V three-phase** for industrial consumers. The waveform is nominally **sinusoidal**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Energy Forms — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 Kinetic Energy (KE)
Kinetic energy is the energy possessed by a body due to its motion. The derivation stems from the **work–energy theorem**, where the work done by a net force on a body equals its change in kinetic energy.

$$KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

where $m$ is mass in **kg** and $v$ is velocity in **m/s**.

**Why this matters:** Wind turbines, flywheels, and rotating machine parts all store energy in kinetic form. In energy audits, the KE of moving fluids (air, water, steam) is critical for system analysis.

### 2.1.2 Potential Energy (PE)
Potential energy is the stored energy of position or configuration. Gravitational PE is the most common form.

$$PE = mgh$$

where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ is the acceleration due to gravity and $h$ is the height in metres.

**Engineering utility:** Hydroelectric power stations convert gravitational PE of stored water into electrical energy via turbines.

### 2.1.3 Thermal Energy
Thermal energy is the internal kinetic energy of molecules. It is transferred as heat, measured in Joules.

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

where $c$ is the **specific heat capacity** in J/(kg·K) and $\Delta T$ is the temperature change in Kelvin.

**Energy management link:** Waste heat recovery systems, insulation, and combined heat and power (CHP) plants all aim to capture and reuse thermal energy.

### 2.1.4 Electrical Energy
Electrical energy is the energy delivered by the flow of electric charge through a potential difference.

$$E = V \cdot I \cdot t = P \cdot t$$

The instantaneous power in any electrical system is:

$$P = V \cdot I$$

For resistive loads: $P = I^2 R = \dfrac{V^2}{R}$

### 2.1.5 Chemical Energy
Chemical energy is stored in the bonds between atoms. It is released during **exothermic reactions** such as combustion, respiration, and electrochemical discharge.

$$E_{\text{chemical}} = \text{Bond energy of products} - \text{Bond energy of reactants}$$

### 2.1.6 Radiant (Electromagnetic) Energy
Radiant energy travels as electromagnetic waves at the speed of light.

$$E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$$

where $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s is **Planck's constant** and $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s is the speed of light.

## 2.2 Conservation of Energy — Logical Steps

The principle of conservation of energy follows directly from the **First Law of Thermodynamics**:

1. **Step 1:** Energy entering a system must equal the energy leaving the system plus the change in stored energy.
2. **Step 2:** For a closed system: $\Delta U = Q - W$ (where $Q$ is heat added, $W$ is work done).
3. **Step 3:** For an isolated system: $Q = 0$ and $W = 0$, so $\Delta U = 0$.
4. **Step 4:** Therefore, total energy is constant — energy is **conserved**.
5. **Step 5:** Energy may degrade in quality (exergy destruction), but the **quantity** is preserved.

## 2.3 Direct Current (DC) — Operational Theory

In DC, the current and voltage are **time-invariant**. The waveform on a graph is a **horizontal line** (constant value).

Key characteristics:
- **Magnitude:** Constant ($I(t) = I_{\text{DC}}$)
- **Direction:** Unidirectional
- **Polarity:** Fixed (positive and negative terminals do not swap)
- **Frequency:** $f = 0$ Hz (no oscillation)

**Average and RMS values for DC:** For a constant DC signal $I$:
$$I_{\text{avg}} = I_{\text{DC}}, \quad I_{\text{rms}} = I_{\text{DC}}$$

## 2.4 Alternating Current (AC) — Operational Theory

In AC, the voltage and current vary **sinusoidally** with time. The general form of a sinusoidal AC voltage is:

$$v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

where:
- $V_m$ is the **peak voltage** (maximum value) in Volts
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the **angular frequency** in rad/s
- $f$ is the **frequency** in Hz
- $\phi$ is the **phase angle** in radians
- $t$ is time in seconds

The corresponding current is:

$$i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t + \phi - \theta)$$

where $\theta$ is the **phase difference** between voltage and current, determined by the load.

### RMS Value of Sinusoidal AC — Critical Concept

The **Root Mean Square (RMS)** value is the effective DC equivalent that delivers the same heating effect (power) in a resistive load. For a pure sine wave:

$$V_{\text{rms}} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \cdot V_m$$

$$I_{\text{rms}} = \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \cdot I_m$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Critical Point:** When the KTU exam says "230 V AC supply," this is the **RMS value**, not the peak. The peak voltage is $V_m = V_{\text{rms}} \times \sqrt{2} = 230 \times 1.414 \approx 325$ V. Failing to clarify this distinction costs marks.

## 2.5 AC Power — The Three Components

For a single-phase AC system, the instantaneous power varies. The **average real power** depends on the phase angle $\theta$:

$$P = V_{\text{rms}} \cdot I_{\text{rms}} \cdot \cos\theta \quad \text{(Watts, W)}$$

The **reactive power** is:

$$Q = V_{\text{rms}} \cdot I_{\text{rms}} \cdot \sin\theta \quad \text{(Volt-Ampere Reactive, VAR)}$$

The **apparent power** is:

$$S = V_{\text{rms}} \cdot I_{\text{rms}} \quad \text{(Volt-Ampere, VA)}$$

The **power factor** is:

$$\text{pf} = \cos\theta = \frac{P}{S}$$

The relationship between $P$, $Q$, and $S$ forms the **Power Triangle**:

$$S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Description | Unit |

| Mechanical Work | $W = F \cdot d$ | Work done by a force | J (Joule) |
| Kinetic Energy | $KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ | Energy of motion | J |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Kinetic Energy from the Work–Energy Theorem

**Given:** A body of mass $m$ moving with initial velocity $u$ is accelerated to final velocity $v$ by a constant net force $F$ over a displacement $s$.

**To Prove:** $KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$

### Step 1: Write the Work Done by the Net Force

$$W = F \cdot s$$

### Step 2: Express Force Using Newton's Second Law

By Newton's second law, the net force is:

$$F = ma$$

### Step 3: Substitute into the Work Equation

$$W = ma \cdot s$$

### Step 4: Use the Kinematic Equation Relating Displacement, Velocity, and Acceleration

For motion with uniform acceleration, the kinematic relation is:

$$v^2 = u^2 + 2as$$

Rearranging for $as$:

$$as = \frac{v^2 - u^2}{2}$$

### Step 5: Substitute Back into the Work Equation

$$W = m \cdot \frac{v^2 - u^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{W = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 - \frac{1}{2}mu^2}$$

### Step 6: Interpret the Result Using the Work–Energy Theorem

The work done equals the change in kinetic energy:

$$W = \Delta KE = KE_{\text{final}} - KE_{\text{initial}}$$

Therefore, the kinetic energy of a body moving with velocity $v$ (starting from rest, $u = 0$) is:

$$\boxed{KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of RMS Value of a Sinusoidal AC Waveform

**Given:** A sinusoidal current $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$ flowing through a resistor $R$.

**To Prove:** $I_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}}$

### Step 1: Write the Instantaneous Power Dissipated in the Resistor

$$p(t) = i^2(t) \cdot R = I_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \cdot R$$

### Step 2: Find the Average Power Over One Complete Cycle

The average power over one period $T$ is:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T p(t) \, dt$$

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{I_m^2 R}{T} \int_0^T \sin^2(\omega t) \, dt$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the Integral Using the Trigonometric Identity

Recall the identity:

$$\sin^2(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$$

Applying this:

$$\int_0^T \sin^2(\omega t) \, dt = \int_0^T \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, dt$$

$$= \frac{1}{2} \left[ t - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2\omega} \right]_0^T$$

### Step 4: Apply the Period Boundary Condition

Since $\omega T = 2\pi$, the sine term vanishes at both limits:

$$= \frac{1}{2} \left[ T - 0 - 0 + 0 \right] = \frac{T}{2}$$

### Step 5: Substitute Back into the Average Power Expression

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{I_m^2 R}{T} \cdot \frac{T}{2} = \frac{I_m^2 R}{2}$$

### Step 6: Equate to the DC Equivalent

For a DC current $I_{\text{rms}}$ to produce the same heating:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = I_{\text{rms}}^2 \cdot R$$

Equating:

$$I_{\text{rms}}^2 R = \frac{I_m^2 R}{2}$$

$$I_{\text{rms}}^2 = \frac{I_m^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{rms}} = \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \, I_m}$$

The same derivation applies to voltage:

$$V_{\text{rms}} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Average Power in a Single-Phase AC Circuit

**Given:** $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ and $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t - \theta)$ for a load with phase angle $\theta$.

**To Prove:** $P_{\text{avg}} = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \cos\theta$

### Step 1: Write Instantaneous Power

$$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot I_m \sin(\omega t - \theta)$$

### Step 2: Apply the Product-to-Sum Identity

Using the identity:

$$\sin A \sin B = \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos(A - B) - \cos(A + B)\right]$$

Let $A = \omega t$ and $B = \omega t - \theta$:

$$p(t) = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} \left[ \cos(\theta) - \cos(2\omega t - \theta) \right]$$

### Step 3: Average Over One Cycle

The average of $\cos(2\omega t - \theta)$ over a complete period is **zero** (it is a pure sinusoid):

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} \cos\theta$$

### Step 4: Substitute RMS Values

Using $V_m = \sqrt{2} \, V_{\text{rms}}$ and $I_m = \sqrt{2} \, I_{\text{rms}}$:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\sqrt{2} V_{\text{rms}} \cdot \sqrt{2} I_{\text{rms}}}{2} \cos\theta = \frac{2}{2} V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \cos\theta$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{avg}} = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \cos\theta \text{ Watts}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Power Factor

**Given:** $P = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \cos\theta$ and $S = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}}$.

**To Prove:** $\text{pf} = \cos\theta = P / S$

### Step 1: Take the Ratio

$$\frac{P}{S} = \frac{V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \cos\theta}{V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}}}$$

### Step 2: Cancel Common Terms

$$\frac{P}{S} = \cos\theta$$

$$\boxed{\text{Power Factor (pf)} = \cos\theta = \frac{\text{Real Power (W)}}{\text{Apparent Power (VA)}}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Energy Forms with Examples

| Form | Equation | Variable Meaning | Real Engineering Example | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Kinetic | $\frac{1}{2}mv^2$ | $m$ = mass, $v$ = velocity | Wind turbine blade (2000 kg, 30 m/s) | J |
| Potential | $mgh$ | $g$ = 9.81, $h$ = height | Hydro reservoir at 100 m head | J |
| Thermal | $mc\Delta T$ | $c$ = sp. heat, $\Delta T$ = temp change | Boiler water heating | J |
| Electrical | $VIt$ | $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current, $t$ = time | 1 kW heater running 1 hour = 1 kWh | J or Wh |
| Chemical | $\Delta H_{\text{reaction}}$ | Enthalpy change | Combustion of methane (–55.5 MJ/kg) | J |
| Radiant | $h\nu$ | $h$ = Planck const, $\nu$ = frequency | Solar irradiance (1000 W/m²) | J |
| Nuclear | $\Delta mc^2$ | $c$ = $3 \times 10^8$ m/s | Uranium-235 fission | J |
| Sound | Pressure wave energy | Acoustic intensity | Industrial noise (85 dB) | J |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — AC vs DC Power

**Problem:** A domestic AC supply of 230 V (RMS), 50 Hz drives a resistive load drawing 5 A (RMS). Calculate the peak voltage, peak current, average power, and energy consumed in 24 hours.

### Solution

**Step 1: Peak Voltage**

$$V_m = V_{\text{rms}} \times \sqrt{2} = 230 \times 1.414 = 325.22 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Peak Current**

$$I_m = I_{\text{rms}} \times \sqrt{2} = 5 \times 1.414 = 7.07 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Average Power (Resistive Load, pf = 1)**

$$P = V_{\text{rms}} \times I_{\text{rms}} \times \cos\theta = 230 \times 5 \times 1 = 1150 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Energy Consumed in 24 Hours**

$$E = P \times t = 1150 \times 24 = 27{,}600 \text{ Wh} = 27.6 \text{ kWh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **billing unit** (kWh) used by KSEB (Kerala State Electricity Board) for domestic tariff calculation. Students should be familiar with kWh as a unit of electrical energy for energy management contexts.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Q: Define energy. State the law of conservation of energy with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Energy is defined as the capacity of a system to do work or produce change. It is a scalar physical quantity measured in Joules (J) in the SI system.

**Law of Conservation of Energy:** Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another. The total energy of an isolated system remains constant.

**Mathematical Statement:**
$$E_{\text{initial}} = E_{\text{final}}$$

**Example (3 marks allocation):**
- **1 mark** — correct definition
- **1 mark** — law statement
- **1 mark** — example with form transformation

**Example:** In a hydroelectric power plant, the **potential energy** of water stored at a height is converted into **kinetic energy** as it falls, which rotates the turbine producing **mechanical energy**, and the generator converts it into **electrical energy**. The total energy remains conserved at every stage (neglecting losses).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]

**Q: Differentiate between Direct Current (DC) and Alternating Current (AC) with waveforms.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Direct Current (DC) | Alternating Current (AC) |
|---|---|---|
| Direction of flow | Unidirectional | Bidirectional (reverses periodically) |
| Magnitude | Constant with time | Varies sinusoidally with time |
| Frequency | 0 Hz (no oscillation) | 50 Hz (India) / 60 Hz (USA) |
| Waveform | Straight horizontal line | Sinusoidal wave |
| Sources | Battery, Solar PV, DC generator | Alternator, Power grid, Inverter |
| Transmission | Not efficient for long distance | Highly efficient via transformers |
| RMS value | Equals peak value | Peak divided by $\sqrt{2}$ |
| Applications | Electronics, EVs, Battery charging | Motors, Lighting, Industrial loads |

**Waveform Sketch (describe):**
- **DC:** A horizontal line at $V = V_{\text{DC}}$
- **AC:** A sine wave oscillating between $+V_m$ and $-V_m$

**Mark split (3 marks):**
- **1 mark** — direction/frequency difference
- **1 mark** — magnitude/waveform difference
- **1 mark** — applications or sources

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the various forms of energy with suitable examples. State the law of conservation of energy and explain its significance in energy management. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the RMS value of a sinusoidal AC waveform. A 230 V, 50 Hz AC supply is connected to a resistive heater drawing 10 A. Calculate the peak voltage, peak current, average power consumed, and the total energy used in 8 hours. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Energy exists in multiple forms that are interconvertible. The major forms include mechanical, thermal, electrical, chemical, radiant, nuclear, and sound energy.

**Forms of Energy (4 marks — 0.5 mark each for naming + 0.25 mark for example):**

1. **Kinetic Energy** ($KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$): Energy of motion. Example: Wind turning turbine blades.
2. **Potential Energy** ($PE = mgh$): Energy of position. Example: Water stored in a reservoir.
3. **Thermal Energy** ($Q = mc\Delta T$): Molecular motion energy. Example: Steam in a boiler.
4. **Electrical Energy** ($E = VIt$): Flow of charge. Example: Current in a conductor.
5. **Chemical Energy** (bond energy): Stored in molecular bonds. Example: Combustion of coal, gasoline.
6. **Radiant Energy** ($E = h\nu$): Electromagnetic waves. Example: Solar radiation reaching Earth.
7. **Nuclear Energy** ($E = \Delta mc^2$): From mass–energy equivalence. Example: Uranium fission in reactors.
8. **Sound Energy**: Mechanical vibration. Example: Acoustic waves from industrial machinery.

**Law of Conservation of Energy (1 mark):**
Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another. The total energy of an isolated system remains constant.

**Significance in Energy Management (1 mark):**
In energy management, the conservation law is the foundational principle. It means that energy savings must come from:
- **Improving conversion efficiency** (e.g., efficient motors, LED lighting)
- **Reducing losses** (e.g., insulation, power factor correction)
- **Recovering waste energy** (e.g., waste heat recovery, cogeneration)
The goal is to **deliver more useful work per unit of energy input**, not to create energy.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation of RMS Value (4 marks):**

For a sinusoidal current $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$ flowing through resistance $R$:

**Step 1:** Instantaneous power:
$$p(t) = i^2(t) \cdot R = I_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \cdot R$$

**Step 2:** Average power over one period $T$:
$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T I_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \cdot R \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Use $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:
$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{I_m^2 R}{2T} \int_0^T [1 - \cos(2\omega t)] \, dt = \frac{I_m^2 R}{2T} \cdot T = \frac{I_m^2 R}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Equating to DC equivalent: $I_{\text{rms}}^2 R = \dfrac{I_m^2 R}{2}$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{rms}} = \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}}}$$

Similarly, $V_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$.

**Numerical Problem (3 marks — 0.75 mark each):**

Given: $V_{\text{rms}} = 230$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $I_{\text{rms}} = 10$ A, $t = 8$ h, resistive load (pf = 1).

**Peak Voltage:**
$$V_m = V_{\text{rms}} \times \sqrt{2} = 230 \times 1.414 = 325.22 \text{ V}$$

**Peak Current:**
$$I_m = I_{\text{rms}} \times \sqrt{2} = 10 \times 1.414 = 14.14 \text{ A}$$

**Average Power:**
$$P = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \cos\theta = 230 \times 10 \times 1 = 2300 \text{ W} = 2.3 \text{ kW}$$

**Energy in 8 Hours:**
$$E = P \times t = 2.3 \text{ kW} \times 8 \text{ h} = 18.4 \text{ kWh}$$

Converting to Joules:
$$E = 18.4 \times 3.6 \times 10^6 = 6.624 \times 10^7 \text{ J}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Alternative]

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of DC and AC electricity. Compare their characteristics, generation methods, and applications. Discuss why AC is preferred for power transmission. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industrial load draws 50 A from a 415 V, 50 Hz three-phase AC supply at a power factor of 0.85 lagging. Calculate the real power, reactive power, apparent power, and the capacitance required to improve the power factor to 0.95 lagging. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**DC Definition (1 mark):** Direct Current is the unidirectional flow of electric charge with constant magnitude. The voltage and current do not change polarity with time. Example sources: batteries, solar PV, DC generators. Applications: electronics, electric vehicle batteries, LED lighting, electroplating.

**AC Definition (1 mark):** Alternating Current is the bidirectional flow of electric charge that varies sinusoidally with time. The voltage polarity reverses periodically at a frequency of 50 Hz in India. Sources: alternators, inverters, power grid. Applications: industrial motors, household supply, HVAC systems.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | DC | AC |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Unidirectional | Alternating (bidirectional) |
| Magnitude | Constant | Varies sinusoidally |
| Frequency | 0 Hz | 50/60 Hz |
| Polarity | Fixed (+ and −) | Alternates |
| Transformation | Difficult (DC-DC converter) | Easy (transformers) |
| Transmission loss | High ($I^2R$) | Low (high voltage step-up) |
| Generation | DC generator/rectifier | AC alternator |
| Storage | Easy (batteries) | Difficult |
| Motor types | DC motor, BLDC | Induction motor, synchronous |

**Why AC is Preferred for Power Transmission (2 marks):**
1. **Voltage Transformation:** AC can be easily stepped up or stepped down using transformers. High voltage reduces current for the same power, minimizing $I^2R$ transmission losses.
2. **Economy:** AC generators and motors are simpler, cheaper, and more robust than DC machines (no commutator needed).
3. **Long-Distance Transmission:** High-voltage AC (HVAC) and HVDC systems enable efficient bulk power transfer across hundreds of kilometres.
4. **Grid Synchronization:** Multiple AC generators can be synchronized in phase and frequency to feed a common grid.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $V_L = 415$ V (line voltage, 3-phase), $I_L = 50$ A, pf$_1$ = $\cos\theta_1 = 0.85$ lagging, target pf$_2$ = $\cos\theta_2 = 0.95$ lagging, $f = 50$ Hz.

**Step 1: Real Power (1.5 marks)**
$$P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\theta_1 = \sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 50 \times 0.85$$

$$P = 1.732 \times 415 \times 50 \times 0.85 = 30{,}540.7 \text{ W} \approx 30.54 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Apparent Power (1 mark)**
$$S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L = 1.732 \times 415 \times 50 = 35{,}929 \text{ VA} \approx 35.93 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 3: Reactive Power (Initial) (1 mark)**
$$Q_1 = S \sin\theta_1 = S \sqrt{1 - \cos^2\theta_1}$$

$\sin\theta_1 = \sqrt{1 - 0.85^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.7225} = \sqrt{0.2775} = 0.5268$

$$Q_1 = 35{,}929 \times 0.5268 = 18{,}927 \text{ VAR} \approx 18.93 \text{ kVAR}$$

**Step 4: New Reactive Power after Correction (1 mark)**
$\sin\theta_2 = \sqrt{1 - 0.95^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.9025} = \sqrt{0.0975} = 0.3122$

$$Q_2 = P \tan\theta_2 = 30{,}540.7 \times \frac{\sin\theta_2}{\cos\theta_2} = 30{,}540.7 \times \frac{0.3122}{0.95} = 10{,}035 \text{ VAR} \approx 10.04 \text{ kVAR}$$

**Step 5: Required Capacitive Reactive Power (1 mark)**
$$Q_C = Q_1 - Q_2 = 18{,}927 - 10{,}035 = 8{,}892 \text{ VAR} \approx 8.89 \text{ kVAR}$$

**Step 6: Capacitance Calculation (1.5 marks)**
$$Q_C = \frac{V_L^2}{X_C} = V_L^2 \cdot \omega C = V_L^2 \cdot 2\pi f C$$

$$C = \frac{Q_C}{2\pi f V_L^2} = \frac{8{,}892}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 415^2}$$

$$C = \frac{8{,}892}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 50 \times 172{,}225} = \frac{8{,}892}{54{,}107{,}000}$$

$$C \approx 1.643 \times 10^{-4} \text{ F} = 164.3 \text{ μF per phase}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing line and phase quantities in 3-phase problems:** In a star connection, $V_{\text{phase}} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$; in a delta connection, $I_{\text{phase}} = I_L / \sqrt{3}$. Always state the connection type.
> 2. **Using peak instead of RMS:** KSEB and all domestic supplies quote **RMS values**. Mixing up peak and RMS leads to a factor of $\sqrt{2}$ error (~41% wrong answer).
> 3. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ factor in 3-phase power:** Three-phase power formulas are $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\theta$, NOT $P = V I \cos\theta$. Forgetting $\sqrt{3}$ loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not mentioning units in energy management context:** Always state the answer in **kWh** (billing unit) when the question is in a domestic/industrial context.
> 5. **Skipping derivation steps:** The KTU board requires showing all integral steps. Just writing $I_{\text{rms}} = I_m / \sqrt{2}$ without derivation loses 3–4 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1**

- **Energy** is the capacity to do work; SI unit is the **Joule (J)**; scalar quantity.
- Major forms: **Kinetic, Potential, Thermal, Electrical, Chemical, Radiant, Nuclear, Sound**.
- **Kinetic Energy:** $KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ (J)
- **Potential Energy:** $PE = mgh$ (J), with $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- **Thermal Energy:** $Q = mc\Delta T$ (J)
- **Law of Conservation of Energy:** Total energy of an isolated system is constant; energy cannot be created or destroyed — only transformed.
- **Direct Current (DC):** Unidirectional, constant magnitude, frequency = 0 Hz, sources include batteries and solar PV.
- **Alternating Current (AC):** Bidirectional, sinusoidal, frequency = **50 Hz** in India, supply = **230 V RMS (single-phase)** or **415 V RMS (three-phase)**.
- **AC Waveform:** $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ where $\omega = 2\pi f$
- **RMS Value:** $V_{\text{rms}} = V_m / \sqrt{2} \approx 0.707 V_m$ (effective DC equivalent for power calculation)
- **Real Power:** $P = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \cos\theta$ (W)
- **Reactive Power:** $Q = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \sin\theta$ (VAR)
- **Apparent Power:** $S = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}}$ (VA)
- **Power Factor:** $\text{pf} = P / S = \cos\theta$ (dimensionless, 0 to 1)
- **Power Triangle:** $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$
- **3-Phase Power:** $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\theta$ (always include the $\sqrt{3}$ factor)
- **Energy consumed:** $E = P \times t$ measured in **kWh** (1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J) — this is the **billing unit** for electricity.
- **AC transmission is preferred** over DC for long distances because voltage can be stepped up/down using transformers, reducing $I^2R$ losses.
- **Power factor correction** is achieved by adding capacitors in parallel with inductive loads (motors), reducing the reactive component of current.
- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s (for radiant energy)
- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (for nuclear and radiant energy)
- **Significance in Energy Management:** Conservation of energy mandates that all energy savings come from improving **conversion efficiency**, **reducing losses**, and **recovering waste energy** — not from generating new energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For the 14-mark questions in Module 1, always structure your answer as: (1) **Definition** (2 marks), (2) **Explanation/derivation** (4–5 marks), (3) **Numerical/diagram/application** (5–6 marks), and (4) **Conclusion or significance** (1–2 marks). This structure aligns with the KTU 2024 marking scheme and the Course Outcome (CO1: Understanding energy fundamentals) assessment pattern.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electricity Tariff
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electricity Tariff

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Electricity Tariff

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electricity Tariff** is the schedule of rates, charges, and pricing structures levied by an electrical utility (supply authority) upon various categories of consumers for the consumption of electrical energy, structured to recover the cost of generation, transmission, distribution, plus a reasonable return on investment while promoting equitable energy use.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **Energy Management and Conservation (PESFT741)**, an electricity tariff is the **monetary instrument** that links a consumer's electrical usage pattern (kW demand, kWh energy, power factor, and time-of-use) to the amount billed. The tariff is the central economic lever used by utilities to **shape consumer load behavior** and **internalize externalities** like poor power factor or peak-time overconsumption.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):** The topic of Electricity Tariff forms the foundation of energy cost analysis. Students must master the types of tariffs, their mathematical formulation, the role of load factor, power factor penalty/reward clauses, and the strategic selection of an optimal tariff to minimize the per-unit energy cost (₹/kWh).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of an **electricity tariff** like the pricing menu of a **gym membership combined with a buffet restaurant**:

- The **gym membership** part is a *fixed monthly fee* (like the fixed/demand charge in a two-part tariff) — you pay it whether you use the gym or not, because the gym has to keep the building open, equipment running, and staff paid.
- The **buffet part** is the *per-plate charge* (like the running charge per kWh) — the more you eat (consume), the more you pay.
- Now, the clever gym-restaurant hybrid may also charge you extra if you bring 50 friends at peak dinner hours (this is like a **maximum demand charge** or **time-of-use peak penalty**).
- And if your buffet plate has too many salads and very little rice, the restaurant charges a "balance fee" (this is like a **power factor penalty** — your load is imbalanced, you pay extra).

In the same way, an electricity tariff is structured to recover the utility's *fixed capacity costs* (the gym) and *variable energy costs* (the buffet), while incentivizing consumers to behave in a way that's good for the grid (eat during off-peak hours, maintain a balanced meal, and don't overcrowd the gym).

## 1.3 Core Physical & Economic Constants in Tariff Design

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum Demand | MD or kW_max | kW | Highest average load over a billing period (typically 15-min or 30-min interval) |
| Energy Consumed | E | kWh | Total active energy over billing period |
| Load Factor | LF | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Ratio of average load to maximum demand |
| Power Factor | cos φ or PF | dimensionless (lagging) | Ratio of real power to apparent power |
| Apparent Power | S | kVA | Vector sum of real and reactive power |
| Billing Demand | BD | kW or kVA | Demand on which the bill is computed (may be ≥ 90% of contract demand) |
| Average Rate | AR | ₹/kWh | Total bill divided by total kWh consumed |
| Effective Rate | ER | ₹/kWh | Net cost per kWh after all rebates/penalties |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Constants:**
> - **Demand Integration Period (India):** **15 minutes** as per CEA (Central Electricity Authority) regulations.
> - **Billing Month:** **30 days = 720 hours** (unless otherwise specified).
> - **Contract Demand:** Sanctioned load (in kVA) by the distribution licensee.
> - **Minimum Billing Demand:** Usually **80% to 90%** of contract demand, even if actual MD is lower.

## 1.4 Why Electricity Tariffs Matter in Energy Management

The tariff structure is the **single most important external motivator** for an industrial or commercial consumer to invest in energy conservation measures (ECMs). A well-designed tariff encourages:
- **Load factor improvement** (spreading demand to flatten the load curve)
- **Power factor correction** (reducing kVA demand and avoiding penalties)
- **Peak shaving** (shifting loads to off-peak hours under ToU tariffs)
- **Demand limiting** (avoiding exceeding sanctioned load)
- **Efficient equipment selection** (favoring high-efficiency motors, drives, lighting)

> [!TIP]
> In KTU board exams, a numerical problem on electricity tariff almost always tests your ability to:
> 1. Compute the **total monthly bill** under a given tariff structure.
> 2. Compare **two tariff options** and recommend the cheaper one.
> 3. Determine the **break-even load factor** between two tariffs.
> 4. Calculate the **savings from power factor improvement** or **load factor improvement**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives of a Well-Designed Electricity Tariff

A tariff must simultaneously satisfy multiple — often conflicting — objectives:

1. **Cost Recovery:** The total revenue collected must cover the utility's fixed costs (capacity, infrastructure), variable costs (fuel, O&M), and a reasonable profit margin.
2. **Fairness & Equity:** Different consumer classes (domestic, commercial, industrial) must be charged proportional to their cost-to-serve.
3. **Simplicity & Transparency:** The bill should be easy to compute and understand.
4. **Load Management Incentive:** Encourage consumers to improve load factor, correct power factor, and shift loads to off-peak.
5. **Revenue Stability:** Provide predictable income to the utility, even when energy sales fluctuate.
6. **Energy Conservation:** Penalize wasteful consumption and reward efficient usage.

## 2.2 Types of Electricity Tariffs — Exhaustive Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following classification is the **canonical taxonomy** prescribed in KTU Module 1. Every type appears in exam questions at least once every 2–3 years.

### 2.2.1 Simple Tariffs

**(a) Flat Demand Rate Tariff**
- Charge: A fixed amount **per kW of maximum demand**, irrespective of energy consumed.
- Bill = $\text{Rate} \times \text{MD (kW)}$
- Rarely used in practice because it ignores energy consumption.

**(b) Flat Energy Rate (Uniform Rate) Tariff**
- Charge: A fixed amount **per kWh of energy consumed**, irrespective of maximum demand.
- Bill = $\text{Rate} \times E \text{ (kWh)}$
- Used for domestic and small commercial consumers.

**(c) Block Rate Tariff**
- Energy consumption is divided into **blocks** (slabs); each block has a different rate.
- First block: subsidized rate (lifeline consumption).
- Subsequent blocks: progressively higher rates.
- **Declining block** (industry-favorable): Rate decreases with higher consumption.
- **Inclining block** (conservation-favorable): Rate increases with higher consumption (used in India for domestic consumers).

### 2.2.2 Multi-Part Tariffs

**(d) Two-Part Tariff (Most Common for Industrial/Commercial)**
- **Part 1 (Fixed):** Rate per kW of **maximum demand** (₹/kW) — recovers capacity cost.
- **Part 2 (Running):** Rate per kWh of **energy consumed** (₹/kWh) — recovers energy cost.

$$
\text{Total Bill} = R_a \times \text{MD (kW)} + R_b \times E \text{ (kWh)}
$$

where $R_a$ = demand rate (₹/kW), $R_b$ = energy rate (₹/kWh).

**(e) Maximum Demand Tariff with KWh Charge (MD + kWh Tariff)**
- Same as two-part, but the demand charge is applied on the **actual maximum demand** (or the billing demand, whichever is higher).
- Sometimes the demand charge is applied on a **sliding scale** that increases with MD.

**(f) Three-Part Tariff (KVA Maximum Demand Tariff)**
- **Part 1:** Rate per kVA of **apparent power demand** (₹/kVA).
- **Part 2:** Rate per kWh of energy consumed.
- **Part 3:** Power factor penalty or bonus clause.

$$
\text{Total Bill} = R_a \times \text{MD (kVA)} + R_b \times E \text{ (kWh)} + \text{PF Penalty}
$$

**(g) Power Factor Tariff**
- The energy charge (₹/kWh) is **adjusted (up or down)** based on the consumer's average power factor.
- A **reference power factor** (usually 0.85 or 0.90 lag) is set.
- If actual PF < reference → **penalty** (higher rate per kWh).
- If actual PF > reference → **rebate** (lower rate per kWh).
- Encourages installation of capacitor banks for PF correction.

**(h) Time-of-Use (ToU) Tariff**
- Different rates for **peak hours**, **shoulder hours**, and **off-peak hours**.
- Peak: Highest rate (e.g., 6 PM – 10 PM).
- Off-peak: Lowest rate (e.g., 10 PM – 6 AM).
- Encourages load shifting — directly tied to energy conservation.

**(i) Seasonal Tariff**
- Different rates in **summer vs. winter** (or monsoon vs. dry season).
- Used in regions with significant seasonal load variation.

**(j) Demand-Interval Tariff (Real-Time Pricing)**
- Rate changes at very short intervals (hourly or 15-min) based on wholesale market price.
- Used in deregulated power markets.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THESE EQUATIONS — they appear in 95% of numerical tariff problems.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{MD} = \dfrac{E}{T \times \text{LF}}$ | Maximum demand from energy and load factor | kW |
| 2 | $E = \text{MD} \times T \times \text{LF}$ | Energy consumed | kWh |
| 3 | $\text{LF} = \dfrac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{MD}} = \dfrac{E / T}{\text{MD}}$ | Load factor | unitless |
| 4 | $\text{kVA} = \dfrac{\text{kW}}{\cos \phi}$ | Apparent demand from real demand and PF | kVA |
| 5 | $\text{Bill}_{2\text{-part}} = R_a \cdot \text{MD} + R_b \cdot E$ | Two-part tariff total bill | ₹ |
| 6 | $\text{Bill}_{\text{kVA}} = R_a \cdot \text{kVA} + R_b \cdot E$ | Three-part (kVA) tariff total bill | ₹ |
| 7 | $\text{AR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Bill}}{E}$ | Average rate per kWh | ₹/kWh |
| 8 | $\text{PF Penalty} = E \times R_b \times \left( \dfrac{\cos \phi_{\text{ref}}}{\cos \phi_{\text{actual}}} - 1 \right)$ | Power factor penalty formula | ₹ |
| 9 | $\text{PF Rebate} = E \times R_b \times \left( 1 - \dfrac{\cos \phi_{\text{ref}}}{\cos \phi_{\text{actual}}} \right)$ | Power factor rebate (when PF > ref) | ₹ |
| 10 | $\text{ER} = \dfrac{\text{Total Bill (with PF adjustment)}}{E}$ | Effective rate per kWh | ₹/kWh |
| 11 | $\text{Load Factor for min cost} = \sqrt{\dfrac{R_a}{R_b \cdot T}}$ | LF at which average rate is minimum | unitless |
| 12 | $\text{Bill}_{\text{ToU}} = R_{\text{peak}} \cdot E_{\text{peak}} + R_{\text{off}} \cdot E_{\text{off}}$ | Time-of-use tariff bill | ₹ |

**Where:**
- $E$ = Energy consumed in kWh
- $T$ = Billing period in hours (typically 720 h/month)
- $\text{MD}$ = Maximum demand in kW
- $\text{LF}$ = Load factor (0 to 1)
- $R_a$ = Rate per kW (₹/kW) or per kVA (₹/kVA)
- $R_b$ = Rate per kWh (₹/kWh)
- $\cos \phi$ = Power factor
- Subscript "ref" = reference/standard PF (e.g., 0.85)
- Subscript "actual" = consumer's actual PF

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Tariff Analysis

In real-world energy management consulting, tariff analysis is the **first step** of an energy audit because:
- It tells the auditor what the consumer is **actually paying per kWh** (not just the energy charge, but the demand charge and PF penalty).
- It identifies **conservation opportunities** with the fastest payback: e.g., a consumer paying ₹8/unit with a 0.7 PF can save up to 15–20% of the bill by installing a capacitor bank (one-time investment with 6–12 month payback).
- It enables **optimal tariff selection** when multiple options are offered (e.g., HT vs. LT, ToU vs. flat).
- It supports **negotiation of contract demand** to avoid paying for unutilized capacity.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When a question asks to "recommend the most economical tariff," always:
> 1. Calculate the **average rate (AR)** under each option.
> 2. The option with the **lower AR** is preferred (assuming same energy and demand).
> 3. State the **annual monetary saving** to strengthen the answer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Rate Minimization in a Two-Part Tariff

**Problem Setup:** A consumer has a two-part tariff with demand rate $R_a$ (₹/kW) and energy rate $R_b$ (₹/kWh). The consumer's maximum demand is fixed at MD kW, and the total operating hours in the billing period is $T$ hours. Find the load factor at which the **average rate (₹/kWh) is minimum**.

### Step 1: Express the Total Bill

The total monthly bill under a two-part tariff is:

$$
\text{Bill} = R_a \times \text{MD} + R_b \times E
$$

where MD is the maximum demand in kW.

### Step 2: Express Energy in Terms of MD, T, and Load Factor

The energy consumed is the integral of power over time. For a simplified calculation, we use the definition of load factor:

$$
E = \text{Average Load} \times T = (\text{MD} \times \text{LF}) \times T
$$

Substituting this into the bill equation:

$$
\text{Bill} = R_a \times \text{MD} + R_b \times \text{MD} \times \text{LF} \times T
$$

### Step 3: Express the Average Rate (AR) per kWh

$$
\text{AR} = \dfrac{\text{Bill}}{E} = \dfrac{R_a \times \text{MD} + R_b \times \text{MD} \times \text{LF} \times T}{\text{MD} \times \text{LF} \times T}
$$

### Step 4: Simplify the Expression

Factor out MD from the numerator:

$$
\text{AR} = \dfrac{\text{MD} \left( R_a + R_b \cdot \text{LF} \cdot T \right)}{\text{MD} \cdot \text{LF} \cdot T}
$$

Cancel MD from numerator and denominator:

$$
\text{AR} = \dfrac{R_a + R_b \cdot \text{LF} \cdot T}{\text{LF} \cdot T}
$$

### Step 5: Split the Fraction into Two Terms

$$
\text{AR} = \dfrac{R_a}{\text{LF} \cdot T} + R_b
$$

### Step 6: Interpret the Result

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Average Rate (AR) is a decreasing function of Load Factor (LF).**
>
> Since $R_a$, $R_b$, and $T$ are all positive constants, as LF increases, the term $\dfrac{R_a}{\text{LF} \cdot T}$ decreases, and AR approaches $R_b$ asymptotically.
>
> **Conclusion:** To minimize the average cost per kWh, the consumer must **maximize the load factor** — i.e., run the plant as close to its maximum demand as possible for as many hours as feasible.

### Step 7: Special Case — AR = 2 × R_b

Set $\text{AR} = 2 R_b$ to find the LF at which the demand charge equals the energy charge:

$$
2 R_b = \dfrac{R_a}{\text{LF} \cdot T} + R_b
$$

$$
R_b = \dfrac{R_a}{\text{LF} \cdot T}
$$

$$
\text{LF} = \dfrac{R_a}{R_b \cdot T}
$$

This is a useful benchmark: below this LF, the demand charge dominates the bill; above it, the energy charge dominates.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern):**

> A small-scale industry has a sanctioned load of **500 kVA** and operates for **24 hours/day, 30 days/month** (i.e., $T = 720$ hours). Its maximum demand is recorded as **400 kW** and the total monthly energy consumption is **2,16,000 kWh**. The tariff applicable is a **three-part tariff** with the following structure:
> - Demand charge: **₹250 per kVA per month**
> - Energy charge: **₹4.00 per kWh**
> - Reference power factor: **0.85 lagging**
> - Power factor penalty: For every 0.01 fall in PF below 0.85, an additional **1%** of the energy bill is charged. Conversely, a rebate of **0.5%** per 0.01 rise above 0.85.

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The average power factor, monthly bill, and average rate per kWh.
**(b)** The savings (₹/month) if the power factor is improved to **0.95 lagging** by installing a capacitor bank.

### Part (a) Solution — Full Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Compute the actual power factor.**

The maximum demand is given in kW. The contract is in kVA. The power factor during peak demand is:

$$
\cos \phi_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{\text{kW}}{\text{kVA}} = \dfrac{400 \text{ kW}}{500 \text{ kVA}} = 0.80
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Load Factor.**

$$
\text{Average Load} = \dfrac{E}{T} = \dfrac{2,16,000 \text{ kWh}}{720 \text{ h}} = 300 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\text{LF} = \dfrac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{MD}} = \dfrac{300}{400} = 0.75
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Billing Demand (in kVA).**

Per the KSEB (Kerala State Electricity Board) standard rule, the billing demand is the **higher of** the actual maximum demand or **80% of the contract demand** (assuming 80% is the minimum billing factor for this problem).

$$
\text{Minimum billing kVA} = 0.80 \times 500 = 400 \text{ kVA}
$$

$$
\text{Actual MD in kVA} = \dfrac{400 \text{ kW}}{0.80} = 500 \text{ kVA}
$$

The actual kVA demand (500 kVA) is higher than the minimum billing kVA (400 kVA). Therefore:

$$
\text{Billing kVA} = 500 \text{ kVA}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Demand Charge.**

$$
\text{Demand Charge} = R_a \times \text{Billing kVA} = 250 \times 500 = ₹1,25,000
$$

**Step 5: Compute the Energy Charge (before PF adjustment).**

$$
\text{Energy Charge} = R_b \times E = 4.00 \times 2,16,000 = ₹8,64,000
$$

**Step 6: Compute the Power Factor Penalty.**

The actual PF (0.80) is **below** the reference PF (0.85).

$$
\Delta \text{PF} = 0.85 - 0.80 = 0.05
$$

The penalty is 1% per 0.01 drop, so:

$$
\text{Penalty \%} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.01} \times 1\% = 5\%
$$

$$
\text{PF Penalty} = 5\% \times \text{Energy Charge} = 0.05 \times 8,64,000 = ₹43,200
$$

**Step 7: Compute the Total Monthly Bill.**

$$
\text{Total Bill} = \text{Demand Charge} + \text{Energy Charge} + \text{PF Penalty}
$$

$$
\text{Total Bill} = 1,25,000 + 8,64,000 + 43,200 = ₹10,32,200
$$

**Step 8: Compute the Average Rate per kWh.**

$$
\text{AR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Bill}}{E} = \dfrac{10,32,200}{2,16,000} = ₹4.778 / \text{kWh} \approx ₹4.78 / \text{kWh}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of "Effective Rate" formula:**
> Using the KTU formula: $\text{PF Penalty per kWh} = R_b \times \left( \dfrac{\cos \phi_{\text{ref}}}{\cos \phi_{\text{actual}}} - 1 \right)$
> $= 4.00 \times \left( \dfrac{0.85}{0.80} - 1 \right) = 4.00 \times 0.0625 = ₹0.25 \text{ per kWh}$
> Total effective rate = $R_b + 0.25 + \text{Demand charge per kWh}$
> Demand charge per kWh = $\dfrac{1,25,000}{2,16,000} = ₹0.579$
> Effective rate = $4.00 + 0.25 + 0.579 = ₹4.829$ (slight rounding difference — both methods are accepted in KTU evaluation.)

---

### Part (b) Solution — Savings from PF Improvement

**Step 1: New PF = 0.95 lagging.**

Since the new PF (0.95) is **above** the reference PF (0.85), the consumer is eligible for a **rebate**.

**Step 2: Compute the change in PF.**

$$
\Delta \text{PF} = 0.95 - 0.85 = 0.10
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Rebate Percentage.**

Rebate is 0.5% per 0.01 rise above 0.85:

$$
\text{Rebate \%} = \dfrac{0.10}{0.01} \times 0.5\% = 5\%
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Rebate Amount.**

$$
\text{PF Rebate} = 5\% \times \text{Energy Charge} = 0.05 \times 8,64,000 = ₹43,200
$$

**Step 5: Compute the New Total Bill.**

$$
\text{New Total Bill} = 1,25,000 + 8,64,000 - 43,200 = ₹9,45,800
$$

**Step 6: Compute the Monthly Savings.**

$$
\text{Savings} = 10,32,200 - 9,45,800 = ₹86,400 \text{ per month}
$$

**Step 7: Compute the Annual Savings.**

$$
\text{Annual Savings} = 86,400 \times 12 = ₹10,36,800
$$

**Step 8: Bonus — Reduction in kVA Demand.**

The kVA demand at the new PF is:

$$
\text{New kVA} = \dfrac{400 \text{ kW}}{0.95} = 421.05 \text{ kVA}
$$

This is a reduction from 500 kVA to 421.05 kVA — a saving of **78.95 kVA**, which also reduces the demand charge. However, since the problem does not explicitly state that the kVA charge is recomputed, we have conservatively kept the demand charge constant.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** In Part (b), always state the **assumption** you are making — e.g., "Assuming the demand charge remains the same since the billing kVA is now below the contract demand." Examiners reward clear assumptions.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Tariff Selection — Analytical Matrix

**Problem:** A consumer has a maximum demand of **200 kW** and monthly energy consumption of **80,000 kWh**. Two tariffs are offered:

| Component | Tariff X | Tariff Y |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed charge | ₹150 per kW | ₹100 per kW |
| Energy charge | ₹1.20 per kWh | ₹1.50 per kWh |

**Find the cheaper tariff and the break-even energy consumption.**

### Step 1: Compute the Bill under Tariff X

$$
\text{Bill}_X = 150 \times 200 + 1.20 \times 80,000 = 30,000 + 96,000 = ₹1,26,000
$$

$$
\text{AR}_X = \dfrac{1,26,000}{80,000} = ₹1.575 \text{ per kWh}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Bill under Tariff Y

$$
\text{Bill}_Y = 100 \times 200 + 1.50 \times 80,000 = 20,000 + 1,20,000 = ₹1,40,000
$$

$$
\text{AR}_Y = \dfrac{1,40,000}{80,000} = ₹1.75 \text{ per kWh}
$$

### Step 3: Compare the Two Tariffs

$$
\Delta \text{Bill} = 1,40,000 - 1,26,000 = ₹14,000
$$

**Conclusion:** Tariff X is cheaper by **₹14,000/month** (or ₹1,68,000/year).

### Step 4: Compute the Break-Even Energy Consumption

At the break-even point, $\text{Bill}_X = \text{Bill}_Y$:

$$
150 \times 200 + 1.20 \cdot E = 100 \times 200 + 1.50 \cdot E
$$

$$
30,000 + 1.20 E = 20,000 + 1.50 E
$$

$$
30,000 - 20,000 = 1.50 E - 1.20 E
$$

$$
10,000 = 0.30 E
$$

$$
E_{\text{break-even}} = \dfrac{10,000}{0.30} = 33,333.33 \text{ kWh}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If the consumer's monthly energy consumption is **above 33,333 kWh**, Tariff X is cheaper. If **below 33,333 kWh**, Tariff Y is cheaper. Since the actual consumption (80,000 kWh) is well above the break-even, **Tariff X** is the correct recommendation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# ⚡ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Electricity Tariffs

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ELECTRICITY TARIFFS] --> B[SIMPLE TARIFFS]
    A --> C[MULTI-PART TARIFFS]
    A --> D[MODERN TARIFFS]

    B --> B1[Flat Demand Rate<br/>Rs per kW of MD]
    B --> B2[Flat Energy Rate<br/>Rs per kWh]
    B --> B3[Block Rate<br/>Slab wise Rs per kWh]

    C --> C1[Two-Part Tariff<br/>Rs per kW plus Rs per kWh]
    C --> C2[Three-Part Tariff<br/>Rs per kVA plus Rs per kWh plus PF clause]
    C --> C3[Maximum Demand Tariff<br/>MD based with kWh charge]
    C --> C4[Power Factor Tariff<br/>PF adjusted Rs per kWh]

    D --> D1[Time of Use Tariff<br/>Peak shoulder off peak]
    D --> D2[Seasonal Tariff<br/>Summer winter rates]
    D --> D3[Real Time Pricing<br/>Hourly wholesale linked]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B1 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B2 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B3 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C1 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C3 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C4 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D1 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D2 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D3 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow: How a Three-Part Tariff Bill is Computed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[START: Consumer Data Input] --> S2[Record Maximum Demand in kW]
    S2 --> S3[Record Monthly Energy in kWh]
    S3 --> S4[Record Operating Hours T]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Load Factor<br/>LF equals E divided by MD times T]
    S5 --> S6[Compute Apparent Demand<br/>kVA equals kW divided by cos phi]
    S6 --> S7[Determine Billing Demand<br/>Higher of actual MD or 80 percent contract]
    S7 --> S8[Compute Demand Charge<br/>Rs a times Billing kVA]
    S8 --> S9[Compute Base Energy Charge<br/>Rs b times E in kWh]
    S9 --> S10[Compare Actual PF with Reference PF]
    S10 --> S11{Actual PF<br/>Decision}
    S11 -->|PF below ref| S12[Apply PF Penalty<br/>Plus percent on energy charge]
    S11 -->|PF above ref| S13[Apply PF Rebate<br/>Minus percent on energy charge]
    S11 -->|PF equals ref| S14[No adjustment]
    S12 --> S15[Sum All Components]
    S13 --> S15[Sum All Components]
    S14 --> S15[Sum All Components]
    S15 --> S16[Compute Average Rate<br/>AR equals Total Bill divided by E]
    S16 --> S17[OUTPUT: Final Bill and AR]

    style S1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S11 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S17 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Tariff Impact on Consumer Behavior

```mermaid
graph LR
    E[TARIFF SIGNAL] --> F[CONSUMER RESPONSE]
    F --> G[LOAD SHIFTING<br/>Shift to off peak]
    F --> H[PF CORRECTION<br/>Install capacitor bank]
    F --> I[LOAD FACTOR IMPROVEMENT<br/>Stagger operations]
    F --> J[DEMAND LIMITING<br/>Auto shutdown schemes]
    G --> K[ENERGY COST REDUCTION]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[REVENUE FOR UTILITY]
    L --> M[GRID STABILITY]

    style E fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style K fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style M fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Power Triangle — Foundation of the kVA Tariff

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[POWER TRIANGLE] --> B0[Real Power P in kW<br/>Horizontal leg]
    A0 --> C0[Reactive Power Q in kVAr<br/>Vertical leg]
    A0 --> D0[Apparent Power S in kVA<br/>Hypotenuse]
    B0 --> E0[cos phi equals P divided by S]
    C0 --> F0[sin phi equals Q divided by S]
    D0 --> G0[Improving PF reduces S<br/>for same P, lowers kVA charge]

    style A0 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G0 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for the Power Triangle Diagram:** The relationship is governed by the Pythagorean identity $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$. For a fixed real power $P$, a lower reactive power $Q$ (achieved by adding capacitors) produces a smaller hypotenuse $S$, which directly reduces the kVA demand charge under a three-part tariff.
