# ⚡ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define Electricity Tariff. State any four desirable characteristics of a good tariff.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Electricity tariff is the schedule of rates and charges framed by an electricity supply undertaking (utility) for the supply of electrical energy to various categories of consumers. It is the price mechanism through which the cost of generation, transmission, and distribution is recovered from the consumers.

**Four Desirable Characteristics of a Good Tariff:**

1. **Fair and Equitable:** The tariff should be just and reasonable, distributing the cost of service fairly among different consumer classes in proportion to their cost-to-serve.
2. **Simple and Understandable:** The structure of the tariff should be simple so that an ordinary consumer can understand it and compute the bill.
3. **Revenue Adequate:** The tariff should generate sufficient revenue for the utility to cover all legitimate costs (fixed and variable) and yield a reasonable return on investment.
4. **Encourages Efficient Use:** The tariff should incentivize consumers to maintain a high load factor, improve power factor, and conserve energy — typically by penalizing inefficiency (e.g., low PF, high peak demand).

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Definition (1 mark) + 4 characteristics (½ mark each = 2 marks). Total = 3 marks.

---

### Question A2: Distinguish between a Two-Part Tariff and a Three-Part Tariff.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Two-Part Tariff | Three-Part Tariff |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of components** | Two: demand charge + energy charge | Three: kVA demand + kWh + power factor clause |
| **Demand basis** | kW of maximum demand | kVA of apparent demand |
| **Power factor handling** | Not explicitly addressed | Explicit PF penalty or rebate clause |
| **Incentive for PF correction** | Weak (no direct reward) | Strong (rebate for high PF) |
| **Common usage** | Small commercial, agricultural | Large industrial, HT consumers |
| **Mathematical form** | $R_a \times \text{kW} + R_b \times \text{kWh}$ | $R_a \times \text{kVA} + R_b \times \text{kWh} \pm \text{PF adj.}$ |
| **Recovery of capacity cost** | Partially (kW-based) | More accurately (kVA-based) |

**Conclusion:** A three-part tariff is more sophisticated and is preferred for large industrial consumers because it explicitly links the demand charge to apparent power, thereby penalizing low power factor.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "Two-part tariff has only two rates" — the **correct statement** is "Two-part tariff has **two components** (a demand component and an energy component)".

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 1]**

A manufacturing unit operates **10 hours/day for 26 days per month** (i.e., $T = 260$ hours). Its **maximum demand** is **800 kW** at a power factor of **0.75 lagging**. The **monthly energy consumption** is **1,50,000 kWh**. The applicable tariff is:

- **Fixed charge:** ₹300 per kVA per month
- **Energy charge:** ₹5.00 per kWh
- **Reference power factor:** 0.85 lagging
- **Power factor penalty clause:** For every 0.01 drop in PF below 0.85, a 2% surcharge on the energy bill. For every 0.01 rise above 0.85, a 1% rebate on the energy bill.

**(a)** Calculate the **total monthly bill** and the **average rate** per kWh. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the consumer installs a capacitor bank and improves the power factor to **0.95 lagging**, calculate the **monthly savings** in the bill. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute the Apparent Demand in kVA.**

$$
\text{kVA}_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{\text{kW}}{\cos \phi} = \dfrac{800}{0.75} = 1066.67 \text{ kVA}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Demand Charge.**

$$
\text{Demand Charge} = ₹300 \times 1066.67 = ₹3,20,000
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Base Energy Charge.**

$$
\text{Energy Charge} = ₹5.00 \times 1,50,000 = ₹7,50,000
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Power Factor Penalty.**

$$
\Delta \text{PF} = 0.85 - 0.75 = 0.10
$$

$$
\text{Penalty \%} = \dfrac{0.10}{0.01} \times 2\% = 20\%
$$

$$
\text{PF Penalty} = 0.20 \times 7,50,000 = ₹1,50,000
$$

**Step 5: Total Bill.**

$$
\text{Total Bill} = 3,20,000 + 7,50,000 + 1,50,000 = ₹12,20,000
$$

**Step 6: Average Rate.**

$$
\text{AR} = \dfrac{12,20,000}{1,50,000} = ₹8.133 \text{ per kWh}
$$

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — 7 Marks

**Step 1: New kVA at PF = 0.95.**

$$
\text{New kVA} = \dfrac{800}{0.95} = 842.11 \text{ kVA}
$$

**Step 2: New Demand Charge.**

$$
\text{New Demand Charge} = 300 \times 842.11 = ₹2,52,632
$$

**Step 3: Energy Charge stays the same (energy consumption unchanged).**

$$
\text{Energy Charge} = ₹7,50,000
$$

**Step 4: Compute the PF Rebate.**

$$
\Delta \text{PF}_{\text{new}} = 0.95 - 0.85 = 0.10
$$

$$
\text{Rebate \%} = \dfrac{0.10}{0.01} \times 1\% = 10\%
$$

$$
\text{PF Rebate} = 0.10 \times 7,50,000 = ₹75,000
$$

**Step 5: New Total Bill.**

$$
\text{New Total Bill} = 2,52,632 + 7,50,000 - 75,000 = ₹9,27,632
$$

**Step 6: Monthly Savings.**

$$
\text{Savings} = 12,20,000 - 9,27,632 = ₹2,92,368 \text{ per month}
$$

**Step 7: Annual Savings.**

$$
\text{Annual Savings} = 2,92,368 \times 12 = ₹35,08,416
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting to recompute the demand charge** when PF changes (since kVA = kW/PF). The kVA demand reduces from 1066.67 to 842.11, giving a substantial demand charge saving. Students who keep the demand charge at ₹3,20,000 will lose **3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing penalty vs. rebate**: A drop in PF attracts a penalty (charge added), while a rise attracts a rebate (charge subtracted). Marks are lost for sign errors.
> 3. **Ignoring the operating hours** in the problem: 10 hours × 26 days = 260 hours. The hours are only relevant for load factor calculation; if not asked, this is a distractor. Students often waste time computing load factor when not required.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Module 1]**

A consumer's plant has a **maximum demand of 600 kW** and consumes **3,00,000 kWh** per month. Two tariff options are available:

| Parameter | Tariff P | Tariff Q |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed charge | ₹200 per kW | ₹50 per kVA |
| Energy charge | ₹3.00 per kWh | ₹3.50 per kWh |
| Power factor | — | 0.80 lagging (average) |

**(a)** Compute the **total bill** under each tariff and find the **cheaper option**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the **monthly energy consumption at the break-even point** between the two tariffs. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Tariff P Bill.**

$$
\text{Bill}_P = (200 \times 600) + (3.00 \times 3,00,000) = 1,20,000 + 9,00,000 = ₹10,20,000
$$

**Step 2: Tariff Q Bill.**

First, find the kVA demand assuming average PF = 0.80:

$$
\text{kVA} = \dfrac{600}{0.80} = 750 \text{ kVA}
$$

$$
\text{Bill}_Q = (50 \times 750) + (3.50 \times 3,00,000) = 37,500 + 10,50,000 = ₹10,87,500
$$

**Step 3: Comparison.**

$$
\Delta \text{Bill} = 10,87,500 - 10,20,000 = ₹67,500
$$

**Conclusion:** **Tariff P is cheaper** by ₹67,500 per month.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — 7 Marks

**Step 1: At break-even, $\text{Bill}_P = \text{Bill}_Q$.**

Note: For Tariff Q, the kVA depends on the **maximum demand in kW** (which is fixed at 600 kW for a given month) and the power factor (assumed constant at 0.80). So the kVA demand component is constant at ₹37,500. Only the energy component varies with $E$.

**Step 2: Set up the equation.**

$$
200 \times 600 + 3.00 \times E = 50 \times 750 + 3.50 \times E
$$

$$
1,20,000 + 3.00 E = 37,500 + 3.50 E
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $E$.**

$$
1,20,000 - 37,500 = 3.50 E - 3.00 E
$$

$$
82,500 = 0.50 E
$$

$$
E_{\text{break-even}} = \dfrac{82,500}{0.50} = 1,65,000 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

If the monthly energy consumption is **above 1,65,000 kWh**, Tariff P is cheaper. If it is **below 1,65,000 kWh**, Tariff Q is cheaper. Since the actual consumption (3,00,000 kWh) is well above the break-even, **Tariff P** is the optimal choice.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Computing kVA incorrectly:** Students often write $\text{kVA} = \text{kW} \times \text{PF}$ instead of $\text{kVA} = \text{kW} / \text{PF}$. This is a **fundamental error** and results in 0 marks for the relevant sub-part.
> 2. **Confusing break-even with average rate:** The break-even point refers to the energy at which two tariffs yield the **same total bill**, not the same average rate.
> 3. **Ignoring PF in Tariff Q:** If you forget to convert kW to kVA, the demand charge for Tariff Q will be ₹30,000 (50 × 600) instead of ₹37,500 (50 × 750), and the entire break-even calculation will be wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Electricity Tariff**

- **Definition:** Tariff = price schedule for electrical energy supplied by a utility to different consumer categories.

- **Key Tariff Types (must remember all 6):**
  1. Flat demand rate (₹/kW of MD)
  2. Flat energy rate (₹/kWh)
  3. Block rate (slab-wise ₹/kWh)
  4. Two-part (₹/kW + ₹/kWh)
  5. Three-part (₹/kVA + ₹/kWh + PF clause)
  6. Time-of-use (peak + off-peak rates)

- **Critical Conversions:**
  - $\text{kVA} = \text{kW} / \cos \phi$
  - $E = \text{MD} \times T \times \text{LF}$
  - $\text{LF} = E / (\text{MD} \times T)$

- **Core Equations:**
  - Two-part: $\text{Bill} = R_a \cdot \text{MD} + R_b \cdot E$
  - Three-part: $\text{Bill} = R_a \cdot \text{kVA} + R_b \cdot E \pm \text{PF adj.}$
  - Average Rate: $\text{AR} = \text{Total Bill} / E$

- **PF Penalty Formula (most-tested):**
  $$\text{PF Penalty} = E \cdot R_b \cdot \left( \dfrac{\cos \phi_{\text{ref}}}{\cos \phi_{\text{actual}}} - 1 \right)$$

- **Minimum Billing Demand:** Usually 80% of contract demand — always check the problem statement.

- **Standard Operating Hours in India:** Use $T = 720$ hours (24 × 30) only if the problem does not specify otherwise.

- **Strategic Insight:** Improving load factor → reduces average rate. Improving PF → reduces kVA demand → reduces demand charge + earns rebate.

- **Exam Strategy:**
  1. Always **list given data** at the top of your solution.
  2. State the **formula** before substituting values.
  3. Show **units** at every step.
  4. Round off to **2 decimal places** in the final answer.
  5. For "recommend" questions, end with a **bold conclusion sentence**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Basics - Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermal Basics - Fuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Fuels (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **fuel** is a combustible substance containing **carbon (C)** and **hydrogen (H)** as the principal combustible elements, along with varying proportions of **sulfur (S)**, **oxygen (O)**, **nitrogen (N)**, **ash**, and **moisture**, which upon combustion with oxygen (or air) liberates a significant amount of **heat energy** that can be economically utilised for domestic, industrial, or propulsion purposes.

The usable heat released per unit mass (or volume) of the fuel is termed its **calorific value (CV)** or **heat of combustion**, expressed in **kJ/kg** (solid/liquid) or **kJ/m³** (gaseous).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Energy Sandwich"

Imagine a fuel as a **chemical sandwich**: the buns are **carbon (C)** atoms and the filling is **hydrogen (H)** atoms, all held together by chemical bonds. When you place this sandwich into a "furnace oven" (supply oxygen), the bonds break, and the atoms rush to bond with oxygen instead — releasing the bond-energy difference as **heat** (and light). 

- A **dry wood log** = a loose sandwich (low energy density, lots of filler/moisture).
- A **liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) cylinder** = a tightly packed sandwich (high energy density, almost pure hydrocarbons).

The "tightness of packing" is what engineers call **calorific value**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Fuels

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Primary Classification Axis:**
> Fuels are classified primarily by their **physical state at standard ambient conditions** (25 °C, 1 atm) and secondarily by their **occurrence** (natural vs. derived).

| Primary Class | Examples | Energy Density (approx.) |
|---|---|---|
| **Solid Fuels** | Wood, coal, peat, lignite, bituminous, anthracite, coke, charcoal | 10,000 – 33,000 kJ/kg |
| **Liquid Fuels** | Petrol, diesel, kerosene, LPG (liquefied), ethanol, methanol, furnace oil | 35,000 – 45,000 kJ/kg |
| **Gaseous Fuels** | Natural gas (NG), LPG (gaseous), biogas, producer gas, water gas, coal gas | 8,000 – 55,000 kJ/m³ |

---

## 1.4 Secondary Classification — Natural vs. Derived

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary (Natural) Fuels:** Found directly in nature — coal, crude oil, natural gas, wood.
> **Secondary (Derived) Fuels:** Manufactured/artificially — coke, gasoline, diesel, kerosene, LPG, producer gas, water gas, biogas.

---

## 1.5 Why "Thermal Basics – Fuels" Matters in Energy Management

In the **KTU 2024 Energy Management course**, this topic forms the **thermodynamic foundation** for:
- Conducting an **energy audit** (Module 1–2 transition),
- Computing **boiler efficiency** and **combustion efficiency** (Module 2),
- Estimating **flue gas losses** and **CO₂ footprint** (Module 3),
- Selecting fuels for **cogeneration / tri-generation plants** (Module 4).

Without mastering fuel calorimetry, no energy balance on a furnace, boiler, IC engine, or gas turbine can be performed.

---

|---|---|---|
| Universal Gas Constant (R) | **8.314** | kJ/(kmol·K) |
| Molecular weight of dry air | **28.97** | kg/kmol |
| Molecular weight of O₂ | **32.00** | kg/kmol |
| Molecular weight of N₂ | **28.016** | kg/kmol |
| Molecular weight of CO₂ | **44.01** | kg/kmol |

## 1.7 The Three Pillars of Fuel Quality (KTU Mnemonic — **CVCC**)

> [!NOTE]
> Every KTU question on fuel selection ultimately tests one of these three:
> 1. **C**alorific Value (energy released per unit mass/volume),
> 2. **C**ombustion Characteristics (ignition temp, flame speed, flammability limits),
> 3. **C**onstituent Analysis (proximate & ultimate analysis — fixed carbon, VM, ash, moisture, C/H/S/N/O).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Calorific Value — The Heart of Fuel Science

### 2.1.1 Higher Calorific Value (HCV) / Gross Calorific Value (GCV)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The total amount of heat liberated when **1 kg of fuel is completely burnt** and the products of combustion are **cooled back to the original (ambient) temperature**, with the **water vapour produced during combustion being condensed** to liquid.

Because latent heat of condensation of water (≈ **2,260 kJ/kg** at 100 °C) is recovered, HCV > LCV.

### 2.1.2 Lower Calorific Value (LCV) / Net Calorific Value (NCV)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The total amount of heat liberated when **1 kg of fuel is completely burnt** and the products of combustion are **cooled back to the original temperature**, but the **water vapour leaves as vapour** (i.e., latent heat of vaporisation is NOT recovered).

Most industrial boilers operate with flue gas exit temperatures > 100 °C, so **LCV is the practically relevant value** for boiler efficiency calculations.

### 2.1.3 Mathematical Relationship between HCV and LCV

The difference arises solely from the **mass of hydrogen** present in the fuel and the **moisture** originally present in the fuel.

For a fuel containing:
- **H** = mass fraction of hydrogen (per kg of fuel),
- **M** = mass fraction of moisture (per kg of fuel),

the water formed = 9 H kg per kg of fuel (since 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O ⇒ 4 kg H produces 36 kg H₂O ⇒ factor 9).

So the latent heat lost per kg of fuel = **9 × H × 2,260 + M × 2,260 kJ**.

$$\boxed{\text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - 9\,H \times 2260 - M \times 2260 \;\; \text{kJ/kg}}$$

### 2.1.4 Dulong's Formula (Empirical — for Coal)

For ultimate analysis giving C, H, O, S in **mass %**:

$$\boxed{\text{HCV} = 33800\,C + 144200\,\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9270\,S \;\; \text{kJ/kg}}$$

This is the **most-tested KTU formula** for solid-fuel calorific problems.

---

## 2.2 Stoichiometric Combustion Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **Stoichiometric combustion** = chemically exact (theoretical) combustion — just enough oxygen to fully oxidise C → CO₂, H → H₂O, S → SO₂, with **no free O₂** left over.

### 2.2.1 Combustion of Carbon

$$C + O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 393.5 \text{ MJ per kmol of C}$$

Mass ratio: **1 kg C requires 2.667 kg O₂**, gives 3.667 kg CO₂.

### 2.2.2 Combustion of Hydrogen

$$2H_2 + O_2 \longrightarrow 2H_2O + 483.7 \text{ MJ per kmol of } H_2$$

Mass ratio: **1 kg H₂ requires 8 kg O₂**, gives 9 kg H₂O.

### 2.2.3 Combustion of Sulphur

$$S + O_2 \longrightarrow SO_2 + 297.0 \text{ MJ per kmol of S}$$

Mass ratio: **1 kg S requires 1 kg O₂**, gives 2 kg SO₂.

---

## 2.3 Theoretical (Stoichiometric) Air Requirement

For a fuel containing C, H, O, S (all in kg per kg of fuel):

$$\boxed{O_2 \text{ required} = \dfrac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O \;\; \text{kg O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}}$$

Air contains 23.2 % O₂ by mass, so:

$$\boxed{\text{Theoretical Air} = \dfrac{O_2 \text{ required}}{0.232} \;\; \text{kg air per kg fuel}}$$

For **gaseous fuels** (per m³ at NTP):

$$\boxed{O_2 \text{ required} = \left[2C_{CO_2} + 0.5C_{H_2} + \sum m\,C_{C_mH_n} - C_{O_2}\right] \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^3 \text{ fuel}}$$

---

## 2.4 Excess Air & Actual Air Supply

In real furnaces, **more than stoichiometric** air is supplied to ensure complete combustion (otherwise CO forms, fuel is wasted, pollution increases).

$$\boxed{\% \text{ Excess Air} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Air} - \text{Theoretical Air}}{\text{Theoretical Air}} \times 100}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Air supplied}}{\text{Fuel mass (or volume)}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Industrial Rule-of-Thumb (Memorise):**
> - Hand-fired coal furnace: 30–50 % excess air
> - Pulverised-coal furnace: 15–25 % excess air
> - Oil-fired furnace: 10–20 % excess air
> - Gas-fired furnace: 5–10 % excess air

---

## 2.5 Combustion Efficiency (η_combustion)

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{combustion}} = \dfrac{\text{Heat actually released by combustion}}{\text{HCV of fuel supplied}} \times 100\,\%}$$

The "heat actually released" excludes:
- **Dry flue gas losses** (sensible heat of hot dry flue gas),
- **Moisture loss** (latent + sensible heat of water vapour),
- **Incomplete combustion losses** (CO, soot, unburnt C),
- **Radiation & convection losses** from furnace walls.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Units | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LCV = HCV − 9H·2260 − M·2260 | kJ/kg | Net CV from gross CV |
| 2 | HCV = 33800C + 144200(H − O/8) + 9270S | kJ/kg | Dulong's formula |
| 3 | O₂ (theoretical) = (8/3)C + 8H + S − O | kg O₂/kg fuel | Stoichiometric O₂ for solid/liquid fuels |
| 4 | Theoretical Air = O₂ / 0.232 | kg air/kg fuel | Stoichiometric air for solid/liquid fuels |
| 5 | Excess Air % = (A_act − A_th)/A_th × 100 | % | Real-furnace air excess |
| 6 | AFR = A_actual / Fuel | kg/kg or m³/m³ | Air-fuel ratio |
| 7 | C + O₂ → CO₂ | — | Combustion of C |
| 8 | 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O | — | Combustion of H |
| 9 | S + O₂ → SO₂ | — | Combustion of S |
| 10 | Theoretical O₂ (gas) = 2[CO₂] + 0.5[H₂] + Σm[CₘHₙ] − [O₂] | m³/m³ | Stoichiometric O₂ for gaseous fuels |
| 11 | Dry flue gas loss = m_g × c_pg × (T_g − T_a) | kJ/kg fuel | Sensible heat lost in dry flue gas |
| 12 | η_combustion = 100 − Σ(losses) | % | Combustion efficiency from losses |

> [!NOTE]
> In all formula tables above, the symbol `\vert` or `\mid` is used for "absolute value" or "divided by" to preserve the markdown pipe structure. The student should remember that **C, H, O, S, M** are mass fractions (kg per kg fuel), and **calorific values are always positive**.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of These Formulas

- **Power-plant boiler design** — sizing the FD/ID fans and economiser based on theoretical air + excess air.
- **Energy audit of an industrial furnace** — quantifying the dry flue gas loss using Orsat analysis (O₂, CO₂, CO in flue gas).
- **Selection of alternative fuels** — comparing HCV/LCV per rupee (₹/MJ) for switching decisions.
- **Emission control** — controlling excess air minimises NOₓ (thermal NOₓ rises sharply with excess O₂ at high T).
- **IC engine tuning** — stoichiometric AFR is critical; petrol ≈ 14.7, diesel ≈ 14.5.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of HCV → LCV Conversion Formula

We begin with a rigorous step-by-step algebraic derivation, which is **guaranteed to appear** as a 7-mark derivation question in KTU ESE.

### Given:

A fuel of mass 1 kg contains:
- H kg of hydrogen,
- M kg of moisture,
- The rest (carbon, sulphur, etc.) does NOT produce water on combustion.

### Step 1: Combustion of Hydrogen

The combustion reaction of hydrogen is:

$$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$$

Molecular masses: H₂ = 2 kg/kmol, H₂O = 18 kg/kmol.

Per kg of H₂ burnt, water produced = (18 / 2) = **9 kg of H₂O**.

So, **per kg of fuel, water formed from hydrogen = 9H kg**.

### Step 2: Total Water Present in Combustion Products

Total water per kg of fuel = water from hydrogen combustion + original moisture in fuel

$$W_{total} = 9H + M \;\; \text{kg water per kg fuel}$$

### Step 3: Latent Heat Released if Water Condenses

The latent heat of vaporisation of water at standard conditions ≈ **2,260 kJ/kg** (more precisely, 2,257 at 100 °C; KTU uses 2,260).

Latent heat recovered per kg of fuel (in HCV test):

$$Q_{latent} = (9H + M) \times 2260 \;\; \text{kJ per kg fuel}$$

### Step 4: The Energy Balance

By definition:
- **HCV** includes the latent heat (water is condensed).
- **LCV** excludes the latent heat (water leaves as vapour).

Therefore:

$$\text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - Q_{latent}$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{\text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - 9H\,(2260) - M\,(2260) \;\; \text{kJ/kg}}$$

### Step 5: Sanity Check (Numerical)

Suppose a coal has H = 0.05 (5 %), M = 0.02 (2 %), HCV = 28,000 kJ/kg.

$$\text{LCV} = 28000 - (9 \times 0.05 \times 2260) - (0.02 \times 2260)$$

$$\text{LCV} = 28000 - 1017 - 45.2 = 26,937.8 \;\; \text{kJ/kg}$$

✔ Reasonable — LCV is lower than HCV by about 3.8 % as expected.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Dulong's Formula

### Step 1: Heat Released per kg of Each Combustible Element

Per kg of element oxidised (using standard heats of combustion at 25 °C, 1 atm):

| Element | Heat released (kJ/kg of element) |
|---|---|
| C → CO₂ | 33,800 |
| H₂ → H₂O (liq) | 144,200 |
| S → SO₂ | 9,270 |

### Step 2: Correction for "Internal Oxygen"

Some fuels contain chemically bound oxygen (O). This oxygen is assumed to first combine with H₂ already present in the fuel (to form H₂O), so the **"free" hydrogen available for combustion is reduced**.

Mass of H₂ that combines with 1 kg of O₂ in the fuel:

$$H_{\text{combined}} = \dfrac{O}{8} \;\; \text{kg}$$

(The ratio is from 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O ⇒ 4 kg H₂ : 32 kg O₂ = 1 : 8.)

So the **net hydrogen available for combustion** = (H − O/8) kg.

### Step 3: Add Up the Contributions

$$\text{HCV} = (33{,}800 \times C) + \left[144{,}200 \times \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right)\right] + (9{,}270 \times S)$$

$$\boxed{\text{HCV} = 33{,}800\,C + 144{,}200\,\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9{,}270\,S \;\; \text{kJ/kg}}$$

### Step 4: Typical Numerical Validation

A coal with C = 0.65, H = 0.05, O = 0.08, S = 0.02 (mass fractions):

$$\text{HCV} = 33800 \times 0.65 + 144200 \times (0.05 - 0.08/8) + 9270 \times 0.02$$

$$\text{HCV} = 21970 + 144200 \times 0.04 + 185.4$$

$$\text{HCV} = 21970 + 5768 + 185.4 = 27{,}923.4 \;\; \text{kJ/kg}$$

✔ Matches typical bituminous coal HCV (24,000–30,000 kJ/kg).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Stoichiometric Air for Solid/Liquid Fuel

### Step 1: Oxygen Required for Each Element (per kg)

| Element | Stoichiometric O₂ (kg/kg element) |
|---|---|
| C → CO₂ | 32/12 = **2.667** |
| H₂ → H₂O | 32/4 = **8.000** |
| S → SO₂ | 32/32 = **1.000** |

### Step 2: Subtract Internal Oxygen

If the fuel itself contains O kg of oxygen per kg, this is **already inside** the fuel and must be subtracted:

$$O_{2,\text{net required}} = 2.667\,C + 8\,H + 1.0\,S - O$$

$$\boxed{O_{2,\text{net required}} = \dfrac{8}{3}\,C + 8\,H + S - O \;\; \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}}$$

### Step 3: Convert O₂ Mass to Air Mass

Dry air composition by mass: **23.2 % O₂, 76.8 % N₂**.

$$\text{Theoretical Air} = \dfrac{O_{2,\text{net required}}}{0.232}$$

$$\boxed{A_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{8}{3}\,C + 8\,H + S - O}{0.232} \;\; \text{kg air/kg fuel}}$$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Complete Combustion Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> This **integrated worked example** is the type that appears as a 14-mark Part B question in KTU ESE.

### Problem Statement
A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass:
- C = 70 %, H = 5 %, O = 8 %, S = 2 %, N = 1.5 %, Ash = 13.5 %.

The fuel is burnt with **30 % excess air**. Calculate:
1. HCV by Dulong's formula,
2. LCV,
3. Theoretical O₂ required (kg/kg fuel),
4. Theoretical air required (kg/kg fuel),
5. Actual air supplied (kg/kg fuel),
6. Air-fuel ratio.

### Solution

**Part (1): HCV by Dulong's Formula**

$$\text{HCV} = 33800(0.70) + 144200\left(0.05 - \dfrac{0.08}{8}\right) + 9270(0.02)$$

$$\text{HCV} = 23660 + 144200(0.04) + 185.4$$

$$\text{HCV} = 23660 + 5768 + 185.4 = 29{,}613.4 \;\; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Part (2): LCV**

$$\text{LCV} = 29613.4 - 9(0.05)(2260) - 0(2260) = 29613.4 - 1017 = 28{,}596.4 \;\; \text{kJ/kg}$$

(Assume M = 0 since moisture not given.)

**Part (3): Theoretical O₂**

$$O_{2,\text{th}} = \dfrac{8}{3}(0.70) + 8(0.05) + 0.02 - 0.08$$

$$O_{2,\text{th}} = 1.8667 + 0.40 + 0.02 - 0.08 = 2.2067 \;\; \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Part (4): Theoretical Air**

$$A_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{2.2067}{0.232} = 9.512 \;\; \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Part (5): Actual Air with 30 % Excess**

$$A_{\text{actual}} = A_{\text{th}} \times (1 + 0.30) = 9.512 \times 1.30 = 12.366 \;\; \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Part (6): Air-Fuel Ratio**

$$\boxed{\text{AFR} = 12.366 : 1 \;\;\text{(mass basis)}}$$

---

## 3.5 Orsat Analysis — Interpreting Flue Gas Composition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Orsat apparatus** absorbs flue gas sequentially in:
> 1. **KOH solution** → absorbs CO₂ (gives % CO₂),
> 2. **Pyrogallol solution** → absorbs O₂ (gives % O₂),
> 3. **Ammoniacal cuprous chloride** → absorbs CO (gives % CO),
> 4. The remaining gas is **N₂** (by difference).

For a fuel burnt with **dry flue gas** containing %CO₂ = a, %O₂ = b, %CO = c, %N₂ = d (where a + b + c + d = 100):

**Excess air (in dry flue gas basis):**

$$\boxed{\% \text{ Excess Air} = \dfrac{O_2}{0.264\,N_2 - (O_2 - CO/2)} \times 100}$$

A simpler **approximate formula** commonly used in KTU:

$$\boxed{\% \text{ Excess Air} \approx \dfrac{O_2 \times 100}{0.268\,N_2 - \dfrac{CO}{2} - O_2} \;\; \text{(dry basis)}}$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Q:** Define **Higher Calorific Value (HCV)** and **Lower Calorific Value (LCV)** of a fuel. Why is LCV preferred over HCV for boiler efficiency calculations?

**Model Answer (3 marks — key points):**

- **HCV (Gross CV):** Total heat liberated by complete combustion of 1 kg of fuel when the products are cooled to the original temperature and the **water vapour formed is condensed** (latent heat recovered).
- **LCV (Net CV):** Total heat liberated when products are cooled to the original temperature but **water vapour leaves as vapour** (latent heat not recovered).
- **Reason LCV preferred in boilers:** In actual boiler operation, flue gas exits at temperatures > 100 °C, so water vapour does NOT condense. Hence the latent heat is **NOT recovered**, and the *practically available* heat is the LCV. Using HCV would **overestimate** boiler efficiency.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **Q:** Write the stoichiometric combustion equations for carbon, hydrogen, and sulphur. Hence state the theoretical O₂ requirement per kg of each element.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad (\text{O}_2 \text{ required} = 32/12 = 2.667 \text{ kg/kg C})$$

$$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O \quad (\text{O}_2 \text{ required} = 32/4 = 8.000 \text{ kg/kg H}_2)$$

$$S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2 \quad (\text{O}_2 \text{ required} = 32/32 = 1.000 \text{ kg/kg S})$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question Choice A [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified]

> **(a) [7 marks]** A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 65 %, H = 4 %, O = 8 %, S = 1.5 %, N = 1.0 %, Ash = 20.5 %. Moisture content = 2 %. Calculate:
> 1. HCV using Dulong's formula,
> 2. LCV of the fuel.

> **(b) [7 marks]** The same coal is burnt in a furnace with 35 % excess air. Calculate:
> 1. Theoretical O₂ required (kg/kg fuel),
> 2. Theoretical air required (kg/kg fuel),
> 3. Actual air supplied (kg/kg fuel),
> 4. Air-fuel ratio (mass basis).

---

#### Model Solution to Question Choice A

**Part (a)(1) — HCV by Dulong's Formula**

$$\text{HCV} = 33800(0.65) + 144200\left(0.04 - \dfrac{0.08}{8}\right) + 9270(0.015)$$

$$= 21970 + 144200(0.03) + 139.05$$

$$= 21970 + 4326 + 139.05$$

$$\boxed{\text{HCV} = 26{,}435.05 \;\; \text{kJ/kg}}$$

**Part (a)(2) — LCV**

$$\text{LCV} = 26435.05 - 9(0.04)(2260) - (0.02)(2260)$$

$$= 26435.05 - 813.6 - 45.2$$

$$\boxed{\text{LCV} = 25{,}576.25 \;\; \text{kJ/kg}}$$

**Part (b)(1) — Theoretical O₂**

$$O_{2,\text{th}} = \dfrac{8}{3}(0.65) + 8(0.04) + 0.015 - 0.08$$

$$= 1.7333 + 0.32 + 0.015 - 0.08$$

$$\boxed{O_{2,\text{th}} = 1.988 \;\; \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}}$$

**Part (b)(2) — Theoretical Air**

$$A_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{1.988}{0.232} = \boxed{8.569 \;\; \text{kg air/kg fuel}}$$

**Part (b)(3) — Actual Air**

$$A_{\text{act}} = 8.569 \times (1 + 0.35) = \boxed{11.568 \;\; \text{kg air/kg fuel}}$$

**Part (b)(4) — Air-Fuel Ratio**

$$\boxed{\text{AFR} = 11.568 : 1 \;\; \text{(mass basis)}}$$

---

### ⭐ Question Choice B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]

> **(a) [7 marks]** With neat sketches, explain the working of the **Orsat apparatus** for flue gas analysis. State the chemical reagents used in each absorption bulb and the order of absorption.

> **(b) [7 marks]** The dry flue gas analysis from a furnace shows: CO₂ = 12 %, O₂ = 6 %, CO = 1 %, N₂ = 81 %. Calculate:
> 1. The percentage of excess air supplied,
> 2. The Air-Fuel Ratio if the fuel is pure carbon.

---

#### Model Solution to Question Choice B

**Part (a) — Orsat Apparatus Working**

(Refer to Section 4.4 Block Diagram of Orsat Apparatus.)

The Orsat apparatus is a portable device used to analyse **dry flue gas** (after removing moisture).

**Step-by-step procedure:**
1. A measured volume (100 mL) of cooled, dry flue gas is drawn into the graduated burette.
2. The gas is passed into **Bulb 1 containing KOH solution** (specific gravity 1.24–1.28), which absorbs **CO₂**. Reduction in volume = % CO₂.
3. The remaining gas is passed into **Bulb 2 containing pyrogallol solution** (alkaline), which absorbs **O₂**. Reduction in volume = % O₂.
4. The remaining gas is passed into **Bulb 3 containing ammoniacal cuprous chloride**, which absorbs **CO**. Reduction in volume = % CO.
5. The **residual gas is N₂** (recorded by difference).

All measurements are at **atmospheric pressure and ambient temperature**, with the water-jacket ensuring constant temperature.

**Order of absorption:** **CO₂ → O₂ → CO → N₂ (by difference).**

**Part (b)(1) — Percentage Excess Air**

Given (dry basis):
- CO₂ = 12 %, O₂ = 6 %, CO = 1 %, N₂ = 81 %.

For a carbonaceous fuel, the carbon balance (per kg-mole of dry flue gas):

Carbon in fuel = CO₂ + CO = 12 + 1 = **13 parts** (mole %).

Oxygen supplied in air (theoretical basis, since N₂/O₂ ratio in air = 79/21):
$$O_{2,\text{supplied}} = N_2 \times \dfrac{21}{79} = 81 \times 0.2658 = 21.53 \text{ parts}$$

Oxygen used in combustion:
- O₂ used for CO₂ = 12 (since C + O₂ → CO₂)
- O₂ used for CO = 0.5 (since C + ½O₂ → CO)
- Total O₂ consumed = 12 + 0.5 = 12.5 parts.

Excess O₂ = O₂ supplied − O₂ consumed = 21.53 − 12.5 = **9.03 parts**.

$$\% \text{ Excess Air} = \dfrac{\text{Excess O}_2}{O_2 \text{ used}} \times 100 = \dfrac{9.03}{12.5} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{ Excess Air} = 72.24 \,\%}$$

**Part (b)(2) — AFR for Pure Carbon Fuel**

For pure C: 1 kmol C requires 1 kmol O₂.
- Theoretical O₂ per kg C = 2.667 kg (from Section 2.2.1).
- Theoretical air per kg C = 2.667 / 0.232 = **11.495 kg air/kg C**.
- Actual air supplied (with 72.24 % excess) = 11.495 × 1.7224 = **19.798 kg air/kg C**.

$$\boxed{\text{AFR} = 19.80 : 1 \;\; \text{(mass basis)}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Read Carefully Before Exam):**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract internal oxygen** in Dulong's formula — student writes `144200 × H` instead of `144200 × (H − O/8)`. **Cost: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Using 2300 instead of 2260** for latent heat of vaporisation of water. Both are accepted, but be **consistent** — examiners deduct 0.5 marks for inconsistency within the same answer.
> 3. **Confusing mass ratio (23.2 %) with mole ratio (21 %)** for O₂ in air when computing theoretical air. **Cost: 2 marks.**
> 4. **Writing HCV = LCV + 9H(2260)** without mentioning the moisture term. **Cost: 1 mark.**
> 5. **In Orsat analysis, writing "N₂ = 100 − (CO₂ + O₂ + CO)"** but forgetting to explicitly state "by difference". Examiners expect the **method statement**.
> 6. **Not converting percentages to mass fractions** before substituting in Dulong's formula (writing 65 instead of 0.65). **Cost: 3 marks.**
> 7. **In Excess Air calculation, using dry flue gas formula without specifying "dry basis"** in the answer. **Cost: 0.5 marks.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin to your wall before ESE):**

- ✅ A **fuel** is a combustible substance whose energy comes from the oxidation of C, H, and S.
- ✅ **HCV > LCV**; LCV is the practical value for boilers.
- ✅ **HCV − LCV** difference is purely from latent heat of water formed = **(9H + M) × 2260 kJ/kg fuel.**
- ✅ **Dulong's formula** is the empirical formula for HCV from ultimate analysis:
  $$\text{HCV} = 33800\,C + 144200\,\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9270\,S \;\; \text{kJ/kg}$$
- ✅ **Theoretical O₂** for a solid/liquid fuel = `(8/3)C + 8H + S − O` (all mass fractions).
- ✅ **Theoretical air** = O₂ ÷ 0.232 (mass basis).
- ✅ **Excess Air %** = `(A_actual − A_theoretical) / A_theoretical × 100`.
- ✅ **Orsat order:** CO₂ (KOH) → O₂ (Pyrogallol) → CO (Cu₂Cl₂) → N₂ (residue).
- ✅ **Typical excess air**: hand-fired coal 30–50 %, oil furnace 10–20 %, gas furnace 5–10 %.
- ✅ **Combustion efficiency** = 100 − Σ(all losses); main losses = dry flue gas + moisture + incomplete combustion + radiation.
- ✅ **Heating values to remember**:
  - Methane ≈ 55,500 kJ/m³
  - LPG ≈ 50,000 kJ/kg
  - Diesel ≈ 45,000 kJ/kg
  - Coal (bituminous) ≈ 25,000–30,000 kJ/kg
  - Wood (dry) ≈ 14,000–17,000 kJ/kg
- ✅ **Constant** to remember: Latent heat of water at 100 °C ≈ **2,260 kJ/kg**.
- ✅ **Universal gas constant** R = **8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Energy Contents of Fuel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Thermal Energy Contents of Fuel ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of **Energy Management and Conservation** under the KTU 2024 B.Tech syllabus, the **Thermal Energy Content of a Fuel** is defined as the total quantity of heat released when a unit mass (or unit volume, for gaseous fuels) of the fuel undergoes **complete combustion** with oxygen (or air) under **standard isobaric** or **isochoric** conditions, with the products of combustion cooled back to the initial reference temperature (**25 °C / 298 K**). This thermal energy content is conventionally classified into two board-defined metrics: - **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** — also known as the **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** or **Higher Calorific Value**. - **Net Calorific Value (NCV)** — also known as the **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** or **Lower Calorific Value**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim):** > *"The calorific value of a fuel is the amount of heat liberated in calories (or joules) per unit mass or volume of the fuel when it is completely burnt and the products of combustion are cooled to the initial temperature of the fuel."* Mathematically, the calorific value $CV$ is expressed in **kJ/kg** (solid/liquid fuels), **kJ/Nm³** (gaseous fuels), or sometimes **kcal/kg** (legacy CGS units). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a fuel as a **"chemical battery"** that stores solar energy captured millions of years ago through photosynthesis. When we ignite the fuel, we are simply **unlocking** this stored energy, much like withdrawing cash from a bank account. Now, imagine two different people counting the cash: - **Person A (GCV)** — counts every single rupee, including the small change that was lost as water vapour escaping into the atmosphere. - **Person B (NCV)** — counts only the practical, usable cash, acknowledging that some money (heat) was carried away by the water vapour's **latent heat of vaporization**. In real-world boiler engineering, the **water vapour produced during combustion** leaves the stack at temperatures well above 100 °C, carrying with it significant latent heat. Hence, **NCV < GCV**, and NCV is the **practically usable heat** for steam generation, gas turbines, and IC engines. > [!NOTE] > **Memory Anchor for KTU Exam:** *"GCV is the TOTAL energy released (laboratory-measured). NCV is the USABLE energy after deducting latent heat losses of water vapour."* ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard Reference Temperature:** $T_{ref} = 25\ ^\circ C = 298.15\ K$ - **Standard Reference Pressure:** $p_{ref} = 101.325\ \text{kPa} = 1\ \text{atm}$ - **Latent Heat of Vaporization of Water at 25 °C:** $h_{fg} = 2442\ \text{kJ/kg}$ (≈ **2442 kJ/kg**) - **Latent Heat at 100 °C (condenser of bomb calorimeter):** $h_{fg} = 2260\ \text{kJ/kg}$ (≈ **2260 kJ/kg**) - **Molar mass of water:** $M_{H_2O} = 18.015\ \text{g/mol}$ - **Molar mass of hydrogen:** $M_{H_2} = 2.016\ \text{g/mol}$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Energy Conversion Constant for KTU Numerical Problems:** > $1\ \text{kcal} = 4.186\ \text{kJ}$, so $1\ \text{kcal/kg} = 4.186\ \text{kJ/kg}$. > KTU numerical answers **must** be reported in SI units (**kJ/kg** or **kJ/Nm³**). --- ### 1.4 Why Two Calorific Values? — The Underlying Chemistry When a hydrocarbon fuel $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z\text{S}_w$ combusts, the hydrogen atoms in the fuel oxidize to form **water vapour ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$)**. The combustion reaction is: $$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z\text{S}_w + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + w\,\text{SO}_2$$ In a **Bomb Calorimeter**, the products cool back to room temperature, so the water vapour **condenses** to liquid water — releasing its **latent heat of condensation** into the calorimeter. This includes the total energy in the measurement. In a **real boiler or furnace**, however, the flue gases escape at high temperatures (typically **150 °C to 250 °C**). The water vapour remains gaseous, and its latent heat of vaporization is **lost up the stack**. Therefore, the practical usable heat is the **Net Calorific Value (NCV)**. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown #### A. Gross Calorific Value (GCV) — Laboratory Reality The GCV is the **true, total** thermal energy released per unit fuel when combustion products are returned to **25 °C** and water exists in **condensed (liquid)** form. It is measured **directly** in a constant-volume **Bomb Calorimeter** (Mahler–Atwater type) or a constant-pressure **Boy's Gas Calorimeter**. **Key Logic Steps:** 1. Fuel is burned inside a sealed stainless-steel bomb surrounded by a known mass of water. 2. The temperature rise of water is measured with a sensitive Beckmann thermometer. 3. Cooling corrections (Newton's law of cooling, Regnault–Pfaundler correction) are applied. 4. The total heat released = (mass of water + water equivalent of calorimeter) × rise in temperature. 5. Dividing by the mass of fuel yields **GCV**. #### B. Net Calorific Value (NCV) — Engineering Reality The NCV is the heat available **after** subtracting the latent heat carried away by water vapour formed during combustion. It is the value used in all **thermal efficiency** and **boiler heat balance** calculations. **Key Logic Steps:** 1. Hydrogen in fuel oxidizes to water: $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$. 2. Mass of water formed per kg of fuel = $9 \times H$ (where $H$ is the % hydrogen by mass), because 2 kg H₂ produces 18 kg H₂O, so 1 kg H₂ produces 9 kg H₂O. 3. This 9H kg of water vaporizes and escapes, losing **latent heat**. 4. Therefore: $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9H \times h_{fg}$. #### C. The Dulong Formula (Empirical Predictor) When elemental analysis (%C, %H, %O, %S, %N, %Ash) is known, GCV can be predicted **without** burning the fuel. This is the **Dulong's Formula**, extremely high-yield for KTU derivations and numerical problems. The empirical relation accounts for the heat released by each combustible element and a small correction for oxygen already bonded in the fuel. ### 2.2 Why This Matters in Energy Management > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Utility for KTU 2024 (Energy Auditor Perspective):** > - NCV is the input to **Plant Thermal Efficiency** = $\frac{\text{Useful heat output}}{\text{Fuel mass} \times \text{NCV}}$. > - GCV vs NCV reconciliation is a mandatory item in **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** audit reports in India. > - Difference between GCV and NCV can be **8% to 12%** for natural gas (due to high H₂ content), which significantly affects fuel cost calculations. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | **Applicable When** | |-------|-------------|-----------------|-----------|---------------------| | 1 | $GCV = \dfrac{(W + w)\Delta T}{m_f}$ | Bomb calorimeter relation (constant volume) | kJ/kg | Direct lab measurement | | 2 | $NCV = GCV - 9H \times h_{fg}$ | NCV from GCV & %H | kJ/kg | Hydrogen-bearing fuels | | 3 | $NCV = GCV - 9H \times 2442$ | Standard form ($h_{fg} = 2442$ kJ/kg) | kJ/kg | KTU numerical default | | 4 | $GCV = \dfrac{1}{100}\left[\tfrac{8080\,C}{3} + 34{,}500\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\right]$ | **Dulong's Formula (CGS, kcal/kg)** | kcal/kg | Solid fuels from elemental analysis | | 5 | $GCV = \dfrac{1}{100}\left[33{,}823\,C + 1{,}44{,}320\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9395\,S\right]$ | **Dulong's Formula (SI, kJ/kg)** | kJ/kg | KTU 2024 preferred SI form | | 6 | $H_{2,O} = 9 \times \dfrac{H}{100}$ | Mass of water formed per kg fuel | kg water/kg fuel | Universal | | 7 | $\text{Latent heat loss} = M_{H_2O} \times h_{fg}$ | Heat lost as flue gas moisture | kJ/kg | NCV derivation | | 8 | $\text{Thermal Eff.} = \dfrac{\text{Useful Output}}{\text{Fuel} \times NCV} \times 100$ | Plant efficiency (BEE convention) | % | Energy audit | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Notation Convention:** In Dulong's formula, $C$, $H$, $O$, $S$ represent the **percentage by mass** of Carbon, Hydrogen, Oxygen, and Sulphur respectively in the fuel on a dry basis. --- ### 2.4 The Hydrogen-to-Water Mass Factor — Deep Explanation For every **2 kg** of hydrogen burned, **18 kg** of water is produced (from stoichiometry: $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$). $$\text{Ratio} = \frac{18\ \text{kg H}_2\text{O}}{2\ \text{kg H}_2} = 9\ \frac{\text{kg H}_2\text{O}}{\text{kg H}_2}$$ Therefore, if the fuel contains $H\%$ hydrogen by mass, the water produced per kg of fuel is $\frac{9H}{100}$ kg. The total latent heat lost per kg of fuel is therefore $\dfrac{9H}{100} \times h_{fg}$, which equals $9H$ kcal/kg when $h_{fg}$ is taken as 100 kcal/kg (legacy CGS), or $9H \times 2442 / 1000$ kJ/kg in SI form. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I — Relationship Between GCV and NCV **Starting Premise:** Consider 1 kg of fuel containing $H$ kg of hydrogen (i.e., $H/100$ kg if $H$ is in %). **Step 1: Combustion reaction for hydrogen** $$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$$ From the stoichiometry: - 2 kg of H₂ produces 18 kg of H₂O - 1 kg of H₂ produces 9 kg of H₂O **Step 2: Mass of water vaporized per kg of fuel** $$m_{H_2O} = 9 \times \frac{H}{100}\ \text{kg}$$ where $H$ is the percentage of hydrogen by mass in the fuel. **Step 3: Total latent heat carried away by water vapour** $$Q_{latent} = m_{H_2O} \times h_{fg} = \frac{9H}{100} \times h_{fg}$$ **Step 4: GCV–NCV relationship** By definition, NCV excludes this latent heat loss, so: $$\begin{aligned} NCV &= GCV - Q_{latent} \\[6pt] NCV &= GCV - \frac{9H}{100} \times h_{fg} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5: Substituting $h_{fg} = 2442\ \text{kJ/kg}$ (at 25 °C)** $$\begin{aligned} NCV &= GCV - \frac{9H}{100} \times 2442 \\[6pt] NCV &= GCV - 219.78\,H \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > For quick KTU calculations, this is often simplified to $NCV = GCV - 0.09\,H \times 2442$, where $H$ is the mass percentage of hydrogen. --- ### 3.2 Derivation II — Dulong's Empirical Formula for GCV **Starting Premise:** A fuel contains carbon (C%), hydrogen (H%), oxygen (O%), sulphur (S%) by mass, with the rest being nitrogen, ash, and moisture. Heat released per kg of each element when burned (approx.): - Carbon $\rightarrow$ CO₂: ~8080 kcal/kg of C - Hydrogen $\rightarrow$ H₂O (gross, liquid): ~34,500 kcal/kg of H₂ - Sulphur $\rightarrow$ SO₂: ~2240 kcal/kg of S **Step 1: Heat from carbon combustion** $$Q_C = \frac{C}{100} \times 8080\ \text{kcal/kg}$$ **Step 2: Gross heat from hydrogen (including latent heat of condensation of water)** $$Q_{H,\text{gross}} = \frac{H}{100} \times 34{,}500\ \text{kcal/kg}$$ **Step 3: Oxygen correction (oxygen already bonded with hydrogen reduces net H₂ available)** Oxygen bonded with hydrogen forms $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ as $\text{H}_2\text{O} \equiv \tfrac{1}{8}\text{O}$ by mass. So the equivalent hydrogen "lost" to oxidation is $O/8$ per 100 kg of fuel. Therefore: $$\text{Net available hydrogen} = H - \frac{O}{8}$$ **Step 4: Heat from sulphur** $$Q_S = \frac{S}{100} \times 2240\ \text{kcal/kg}$$ **Step 5: Summing all contributions** $$\begin{aligned} GCV &= Q_C + Q_{H,\text{gross}} + Q_S \\[6pt] GCV &= \frac{8080\,C}{100} + \frac{34{,}500\left(H - \tfrac{O}{8}\right)}{100} + \frac{2240\,S}{100} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6: Simplifying (CGS / kcal/kg form)** $$\boxed{GCV = \frac{1}{100}\left[8080\,C + 34{,}500\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\right]\ \text{kcal/kg}}$$ **Step 7: Converting to SI units (kJ/kg) by multiplying each constant by 4.186** $$\begin{aligned} 8080 \times 4.186 &\approx 33{,}823 \\[4pt] 34{,}500 \times 4.186 &\approx 1{,}44{,}320 \\[4pt] 2240 \times 4.186 &\approx 9395 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 8: Final Dulong's Formula in SI form** $$\boxed{GCV = \frac{1}{100}\left[33{,}823\,C + 1{,}44{,}320\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9395\,S\right]\ \text{kJ/kg}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Convention:** Always express your final answer in **kJ/kg**. If given a problem in kcal/kg, explicitly convert and mention the conversion factor $1\ \text{kcal} = 4.186\ \text{kJ}$. --- ### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A coal sample has the following elemental analysis by mass: C = 70%, H = 6%, O = 8%, S = 2%, N = 1.5%, Ash = 12.5%. Calculate (a) GCV using Dulong's formula, (b) NCV using $h_{fg} = 2442\ \text{kJ/kg}$. **Solution:** **Part (a): GCV using Dulong's formula (SI)** $$\begin{aligned} GCV &= \frac{1}{100}\left[33{,}823 \times 70 + 1{,}44{,}320 \times \left(6 - \tfrac{8}{8}\right) + 9395 \times 2\right] \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{100}\left[33{,}823 \times 70 + 1{,}44{,}320 \times (6 - 1) + 9395 \times 2\right] \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{100}\left[23{,}67{,}610 + 1{,}44{,}320 \times 5 + 18{,}790\right] \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{100}\left[23{,}67{,}610 + 7{,}21{,}600 + 18{,}790\right] \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{100}\left[31{,}08{,}000\right] \\[6pt] GCV &= 31{,}080\ \text{kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Part (b): NCV from GCV** $$\begin{aligned} NCV &= GCV - 9H \times h_{fg} / 100 \\[6pt] &= 31{,}080 - \frac{9 \times 6 \times 2442}{100} \\[6pt] &= 31{,}080 - \frac{131{,}868}{100} \\[6pt] &= 31{,}080 - 1318.68 \\[6pt] NCV &\approx 29{,}761\ \text{kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Result:** $GCV = 31{,}080\ \text{kJ/kg}$, $NCV = 29{,}761\ \text{kJ/kg}$. Difference ≈ **4.24%** (typical for bituminous coal). ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Energy Flow During Fuel Combustion The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete thermodynamic and chemical pathway showing how GCV transitions into NCV, and where latent heat losses occur. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fuel Input
Mass m kg] --> B[Combustion Chamber
with Stoichiometric Air] B --> C[Hot Combustion Products
at T flame approx 1500 to 2000 C] C --> D{Heat Transfer
Pathway} D --> E[Useful Heat Output
to Boiler Tubes or Engine] D --> F[Flue Gas Exhaust
containing H2O vapour] F --> G[Latent Heat of Vaporization
9H x h_fg Lost] G --> H[NCV Effective Energy
GCV minus Losses] E --> H H --> I[Plant Thermal Efficiency
NCV / Fuel Mass Input] A --> J[GCV Total Energy
Bomb Calorimeter Reading] J --> H J --> K[Latent Heat Subtract
2442 kJ per kg water] K --> H ``` ### 4.2 Bomb Calorimeter — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Stainless Steel Bomb] --> P2[Fuel Pellet
plus Ignition Wire] P1 --> P3[Oxygen Supply
at 25 to 30 atm] P1 --> P4[Water Jacket
Mass W kg] P4 --> P5[Stirrer Motor
for Uniform Mixing] P4 --> P6[Beckmann Thermometer
Delta T Measurement] P6 --> P7[Cooling Correction
Regnault Pfaundler] P7 --> P8[GCV Calculation
W plus w times Delta T divided by m] ``` ### 4.3 Energy Transformation Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD T1[Chemical Energy in Fuel Bonds
GCV kJ per kg] --> T2[Combustion Reaction] T2 --> T3[Sensible Heat of Products] T2 --> T4[Latent Heat of Water Vapour
2260 to 2442 kJ per kg] T3 --> T5[Useful Process Heat
NCV kJ per kg] T4 --> T6[Stack Loss
Unrecoverable] T5 --> T7[Plant Work Output or Steam Generation] ``` ### 4.4 GCV vs NCV Comparison Matrix (Block View) ```mermaid flowchart LR GCV[A Gross Calorific Value
Higher Heating Value HHV] -->|Subtract 9H times h_fg| NCV[B Net Calorific Value
Lower Heating Value LHV] GCV --> C1[Measured in Bomb Calorimeter
Constant Volume Process] GCV --> C2[Water in Liquid Form
at 25 C Reference] NCV --> D1[Used in Boiler Efficiency
and Gas Turbine Calculations] NCV --> D2[Water in Vapour Form
Exits at Stack Temperature] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Takeaway:** Always use **NCV** for industrial efficiency computations and **GCV** only for fuel purchasing contracts and regulatory reporting (e.g., CIL coal grade specifications in India). ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Define Gross Calorific Value (GCV) and Net Calorific Value (NCV) of a fuel. Why is NCV always less than GCV? (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** **GCV (Gross Calorific Value):** It is the total amount of heat released when **1 kg of solid (or 1 Nm³ of gaseous) fuel** is completely burnt and the products of combustion are cooled back to **25 °C**, with the water vapour formed **condensing to liquid**. **NCV (Net Calorific Value):** It is the usable heat released when 1 kg of fuel is completely burnt and the combustion products leave the system at the **stack temperature**, with the water vapour **remaining as vapour**. **Why NCV < GCV:** During real combustion, the water formed from hydrogen oxidation leaves the system as **vapour**, carrying away its **latent heat of vaporization** ($h_{fg} = 2442$ kJ/kg at 25 °C). This latent heat is recovered in the bomb calorimeter (where vapour condenses) but is lost in practical systems. Hence, $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9H \cdot h_{fg}$, so NCV is always less than GCV. --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** A fuel has 6% hydrogen by mass. If its GCV is 30,000 kJ/kg, calculate the NCV. Take $h_{fg} = 2442$ kJ/kg. (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** Given: $H = 6\%$, $GCV = 30{,}000$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 2442$ kJ/kg. Mass of water formed per kg fuel: $$m_{H_2O} = \frac{9 \times H}{100} = \frac{9 \times 6}{100} = 0.54\ \text{kg}$$ Latent heat loss: $$Q_{latent} = 0.54 \times 2442 = 1318.68\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ Therefore: $$\text{NCV} = 30{,}000 - 1318.68 = 28{,}681.32\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Answer:** $NCV \approx 28{,}681$ kJ/kg. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **`(a)`** Define calorific value. With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **Bomb Calorimeter** used to determine the GCV of a solid fuel. List the various cooling and radiation corrections applied. **(7 Marks)** **`(b)`** A coal sample contains C = 65%, H = 5%, O = 12%, S = 3%, N = 2.5%, Ash = 12.5%. Determine: (i) GCV using Dulong's formula (in both kcal/kg and kJ/kg) (ii) NCV of the fuel Take $h_{fg} = 2442$ kJ/kg. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution to Question A:** **Part (a): Bomb Calorimeter — Working Principle** A bomb calorimeter is a constant-volume combustion device used to determine the GCV of solid and liquid fuels. **Constructional Block Diagram:** | Component | Function | |-----------|----------| | Stainless-steel bomb (sealed) | Houses the fuel and oxygen under 25–30 atm pressure | | Fuel pellet & ignition wire | Provides ignition energy (~1 cal) via electrical fuse | | Copper calorimeter vessel | Holds a known mass W of water (typically 2–3 kg) | | Outer double-walled jacket | Provides thermal insulation | | Stirrer (motor-driven) | Maintains uniform water temperature | | Beckmann thermometer | Measures temperature rise ΔT to ±0.001 °C | **Working Steps:** 1. A known mass $m_f$ of fuel pellet is placed in the crucible inside the bomb. 2. The bomb is filled with pure oxygen to ~25 atm. 3. The bomb is immersed in a known mass $W$ of water inside the calorimeter. 4. The fuel is ignited electrically; combustion occurs at constant volume. 5. Heat released raises the water temperature by ΔT (corrected for radiation). 6. The GCV is calculated as: $$GCV = \frac{(W + w)\,\Delta T}{m_f}$$ where $w$ is the water equivalent of the calorimeter (calibrated separately). **Cooling / Radiation Corrections Applied:** - **Regnault–Pfaundler Correction** — accounts for heat lost to surroundings during the test. - **Newton's Law of Cooling Correction** — for finite combustion time. - **Ignition Wire Correction** — subtracts heat from the cotton thread / fuse wire (~1 cal). - **Acid Formation Correction** — heat of formation of HNO₃ and H₂SO₄ from N and S (~0.1–0.5% of GCV). **Part (b): Numerical Calculation** Given: C = 65%, H = 5%, O = 12%, S = 3%, N = 2.5%, Ash = 12.5%. Check: $65 + 5 + 12 + 3 + 2.5 + 12.5 = 100\%$ ✓ **(i) GCV using Dulong's Formula (CGS — kcal/kg):** $$\begin{aligned} GCV_{\text{kcal/kg}} &= \frac{1}{100}\left[8080\,C + 34{,}500\left(H - \tfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\right] \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{100}\left[8080 \times 65 + 34{,}500 \times \left(5 - \tfrac{12}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 3\right] \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{100}\left[5{,}25{,}200 + 34{,}500 \times (5 - 1.5) + 6720\right] \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{100}\left[5{,}25{,}200 + 34{,}500 \times 3.5 + 6720\right] \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{100}\left[5{,}25{,}200 + 1{,}20{,}750 + 6720\right] \\[6pt] &= \frac{6{,}52{,}670}{100} \\[6pt] &= 6526.7\ \text{kcal/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Converting to SI (kJ/kg):** $$GCV_{kJ/kg} = 6526.7 \times 4.186 = 27{,}320.8\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ **(ii) NCV:** $$\begin{aligned} NCV &= GCV - \frac{9H}{100} \times h_{fg} \\[6pt] &= 27{,}320.8 - \frac{9 \times 5}{100} \times 2442 \\[6pt] &= 27{,}320.8 - 0.45 \times 2442 \\[6pt] &= 27{,}320.8 - 1098.9 \\[6pt] NCV &= 26{,}221.9\ \text{kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Final Answer:** - $GCV = 6526.7\ \text{kcal/kg} = 27{,}320.8\ \text{kJ/kg}$ - $NCV = 26{,}221.9\ \text{kJ/kg}$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **`(a)`** Explain the **Dulong's Formula** for estimating the calorific value of a solid fuel from its ultimate analysis. State clearly the assumptions and limitations. **(7 Marks)** **`(b)`** A fuel oil has the following composition by mass: C = 84%, H = 12%, O = 2%, S = 2%. The remaining being inert matter. Calculate: (i) Minimum mass of air required for complete combustion of 1 kg of fuel. (ii) GCV and NCV of the fuel using Dulong's formula. Take $h_{fg} = 2442$ kJ/kg. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution to Question B:** **Part (a): Dulong's Formula — Theory, Assumptions & Limitations** **Statement:** Dulong's formula is an empirical equation that predicts the GCV of a solid/liquid fuel from its elemental (ultimate) analysis. The standard SI form is: $$GCV = \frac{1}{100}\left[33{,}823\,C + 1{,}44{,}320\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9395\,S\right]\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Underlying Theory:** The total heat released is the sum of heats contributed by: 1. Carbon burning to CO₂: ~33,823 kJ/kg of C 2. Hydrogen burning to H₂O (gross): ~1,44,320 kJ/kg of H₂, with correction for oxygen already bonded in the fuel (oxygen oxidizes hydrogen internally, so available H₂ reduces by O/8) 3. Sulphur burning to SO₂: ~9395 kJ/kg of S **Key Assumptions:** 1. Combustion is **complete and stoichiometric**. 2. All carbon forms CO₂ (no CO formation). 3. All hydrogen forms H₂O (liquid state for GCV). 4. Sulphur fully converts to SO₂. 5. The empirical constants (8080, 34,500, 2240 kcal/kg) are fuel-independent. 6. Heat of formation of nitrogen compounds, ash, and moisture is negligible. **Limitations:** 1. **Not accurate for very young fuels** (peat, lignite) where the constants deviate. 2. **Ignores moisture content** of the fuel — gives dry-basis GCV. 3. **Ignores ash composition effects** on combustion enthalpy. 4. **Inaccurate for fuels with high nitrogen** or high volatile content. 5. **Constants were derived for coal** — using them for biomass or waste-derived fuels introduces 5–15% error. 6. Does not account for **CO formation** in incomplete combustion. **Part (b): Numerical Calculation** Given: C = 84%, H = 12%, O = 2%, S = 2% (inerts = 0%). **(i) Minimum mass of air for complete combustion of 1 kg fuel:** Oxygen required stoichiometrically: - For carbon: $O_2^{(C)} = \dfrac{32}{12} \times 0.84 = 2.24\ \text{kg}$ - For hydrogen: $O_2^{(H)} = \dfrac{32}{4} \times 0.12 = 0.96\ \text{kg}$ - For sulphur: $O_2^{(S)} = \dfrac{32}{32} \times 0.02 = 0.02\ \text{kg}$ - Total O₂ required: $2.24 + 0.96 + 0.02 = 3.22$ kg - Less O₂ already in fuel: $0.02$ kg - **Net O₂ required:** $3.22 - 0.02 = 3.20$ kg Mass of air (air contains 23.2% O₂ by mass): $$m_{air} = \frac{3.20}{0.232} = 13.79\ \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$ **(ii) GCV using Dulong's formula (SI):** $$\begin{aligned} GCV &= \frac{1}{100}\left[33{,}823 \times 84 + 1{,}44{,}320 \times \left(12 - \tfrac{2}{8}\right) + 9395 \times 2\right] \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{100}\left[28{,}41{,}132 + 1{,}44{,}320 \times (12 - 0.25) + 18{,}790\right] \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{100}\left[28{,}41{,}132 + 1{,}44{,}320 \times 11.75 + 18{,}790\right] \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{100}\left[28{,}41{,}132 + 16{,}95{,}760 + 18{,}790\right] \\[6pt] &= \frac{45{,}55{,}682}{100} \\[6pt] GCV &= 45{,}556.82\ \text{kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **NCV Calculation:** $$\begin{aligned} NCV &= GCV - \frac{9H}{100} \times h_{fg} \\[6pt] &= 45{,}556.82 - \frac{9 \times 12}{100} \times 2442 \\[6pt] &= 45{,}556.82 - 1.08 \times 2442 \\[6pt] &= 45{,}556.82 - 2637.36 \\[6pt] NCV &= 42{,}919.46\ \text{kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Final Answer:** - Minimum air required = **13.79 kg/kg fuel** - $GCV = 45{,}556.82\ \text{kJ/kg}$ - $NCV = 42{,}919.46\ \text{kJ/kg}$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Points — Read Carefully:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert % to mass fraction:** Students often plug $H = 12$ instead of $H = 12/100 = 0.12$ in the Dulong's formula — leading to answers **100× larger**. Always use $H/100$. > 2. **Using $h_{fg} = 2260$ vs 2442:** $h_{fg} = 2442$ kJ/kg is at 25 °C (KTU standard). $h_{fg} = 2260$ kJ/kg is at 100 °C (legacy). Verify which value the question gives. > 3. **Mixing kcal/kg and kJ/kg:** Don't forget the 4.186 conversion factor. Report **both** if Dulong's formula is asked in both units. > 4. **Oxygen correction in Dulong's formula:** The term $(H - O/8)$ is the **net hydrogen**. Skipping the $O/8$ gives inflated GCV. > 5. **NCV vs GCV in efficiency formulas:** Use **NCV** for plant thermal efficiency (BEE India standard), not GCV. Many students lose 2–3 marks here. > 6. **NCV = 0 implies zero energy?** No — NCV is the **gross minus latent heat of water**. Even pure carbon has NCV = GCV since there's no H₂ to form water. > 7. **Step mark allocation in KTU:** Show **all intermediate substitutions** explicitly. KTU examiners allot partial marks only when intermediate steps are visible. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Calorific Value =** heat released per unit mass (solid/liquid) or per Nm³ (gas) on complete combustion. - **GCV (HHV):** water condenses to liquid; latent heat recovered; measured in **bomb calorimeter**. - **NCV (LHV):** water remains as vapour; latent heat **lost up the stack**; used in **practical efficiency** calculations. - **Relation:** $NCV = GCV - 9H \times h_{fg}/100$, where $h_{fg} = 2442$ kJ/kg (at 25 °C). - **Hydrogen-to-water factor 9** comes from stoichiometry: 1 kg H₂ → 9 kg H₂O. - **Dulong's Formula (SI, kJ/kg):** $$GCV = \frac{1}{100}\left[33{,}823\,C + 1{,}44{,}320\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9395\,S\right]$$ - **Dulong's Formula (CGS, kcal/kg):** $$GCV = \frac{1}{100}\left[8080\,C + 34{,}500\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\right]$$ - **Conversion:** $1\ \text{kcal} = 4.186\ \text{kJ}$ (always quote SI answer in KTU). - **Bomb Calorimeter:** constant volume, ~25 atm oxygen, water-cooled jacket, Beckmann thermometer. - **Boy's Gas Calorimeter:** constant pressure, used for gaseous fuels. - **Cooling corrections** in bomb calorimetry: Regnault–Pfaundler, fuse wire, acid formation. - **Oxygen correction** in Dulong's formula accounts for H₂ already oxidized in the fuel. - **NCV is the engineering quantity** for boiler efficiency, gas turbine work, IC engine calculations. - **GCV is the contractual quantity** for coal pricing, BEE audits, regulatory reporting. - **Typical GCV range:** Coal 20,000–35,000 kJ/kg; Fuel oil 42,000–45,000 kJ/kg; Natural gas 38,000–55,000 kJ/Nm³. - **NCV ≈ 92–96% of GCV** for typical coals; **NCV ≈ 89–92% of GCV** for fuel oils; **NCV ≈ 89–91% of GCV** for natural gas. - **Energy Management Application:** Difference between GCV and NCV is one of the **largest sources of fuel cost calculation error** in Indian thermal power plants — addressed in BEE PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme audits.

topics.title: Temperature and Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Temperature and Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Temperature

**Temperature** is a fundamental physical quantity that quantitatively expresses the degree of hotness or coldness of a body or system. From a thermodynamic perspective, temperature is defined as the property that determines the direction of spontaneous heat flow between two bodies in thermal contact. Two bodies in thermal equilibrium are said to be at the same temperature — this is the **Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics**.

In statistical mechanics, temperature is a measure of the **average kinetic energy of the molecules** of a substance. Higher temperature implies greater molecular motion; lower temperature implies reduced molecular activity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Equivalent):**
> Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles in a system and determines the direction of heat transfer. It is an intensive property (independent of the mass of the system) and a state function in thermodynamics.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Crowd in a Hall"

Imagine a packed concert hall:
- When the crowd is calm and orderly, the average movement of people is low → this represents **low temperature**.
- When the crowd is jumping, dancing, and pushing, the average energy per person is high → this represents **high temperature**.

The **total crowd energy** corresponds to heat content (enthalpy/internal energy), while the **average energy per person** is the temperature. Even a small group dancing wildly can have a higher "temperature" than a large calm crowd.

### Standard Units of Temperature

| Scale | Symbol | Reference Points |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Kelvin (SI) | K | 0 K = Absolute Zero, 273.15 K = 0 °C |
| Celsius | °C | 0 °C = Ice point, 100 °C = Steam point |
| Fahrenheit | °F | 32 °F = Ice point, 212 °F = Steam point |
| Rankine | °R | 0 °R = Absolute Zero, 491.67 °R = 0 °C |

> [!NOTE]
> The SI base unit is the **Kelvin (K)**, defined as 1/273.16 of the thermodynamic temperature of the triple point of pure water. The 2019 SI redefinition fixes the Boltzmann constant at **1.380649 × 10⁻²³ J/K** as exact.

---

## 1.2 Pressure

**Pressure** is defined as the **normal force exerted per unit area** by a fluid (liquid or gas) on a real or imaginary surface. Mathematically:

$$P = \frac{F}{A}$$

where **F** is the force perpendicular to the surface and **A** is the area over which the force is distributed. The SI unit of pressure is the **Pascal (Pa)**, where 1 Pa = 1 N/m².

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** Pressure is the force per unit area applied in a direction perpendicular to the surface of an object. It is an intensive thermodynamic property that, along with temperature and volume, defines the state of a pure substance.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Elephant vs. The Lady in Heels"

Consider two identical instances of force — say 500 N:
- Distributed over the heel of a stiletto (area ≈ 1 cm²): the **pressure** is enormous — it pierces the floor.
- Distributed over the foot of an elephant (area ≈ 1000 cm²): the **pressure** is tiny — the elephant walks harmlessly.

**Same force, vastly different pressure — because pressure depends on the area over which the force acts.**

### Types of Pressure in Engineering Practice

| Type | Definition | Notation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Atmospheric | Pressure exerted by the weight of the atmosphere | $P_{atm}$ |
| Gauge | Pressure measured relative to atmospheric | $P_{gauge}$ |
| Absolute | True pressure measured from perfect vacuum (zero reference) | $P_{abs}$ |
| Vacuum | Pressure below atmospheric | $P_{vac}$ |
| Differential | Difference between two pressure points | $\Delta P$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key relation:** $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$. Standard atmospheric pressure is **101.325 kPa (1.01325 bar)** at sea level.

### Why Temperature and Pressure Matter in Energy Management

In **Energy Management and Conservation (PESFT741)**, temperature and pressure are the two most measured variables because:

1. They directly govern the **state and energy content** of working fluids (steam, refrigerants, air, water).
2. **Heat transfer rates** are driven by temperature differences (Newton's Law of Cooling, Fourier's Law).
3. **Equipment efficiency** (boilers, turbines, compressors, heat exchangers) is highly sensitive to operating T and P.
4. **Energy savings calculations** (e.g., insulation effectiveness, leak detection) require accurate T and P measurement.
5. The **combustion process** in furnaces and boilers depends critically on pressure for fuel atomization and air delivery.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic Connection — T, P, V, n

For an **ideal gas**, the state of a system is fully described by the relationship between four variables: temperature (T), pressure (P), volume (V), and the amount of substance (n, in moles). The governing equation is the **Ideal Gas Law**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Temperature in gas-law equations **must always be expressed on an absolute scale** (Kelvin or Rankine). Using Celsius or Fahrenheit will yield mathematically nonsensical (negative) answers.

## 2.2 Temperature Scale Conversion Logic

All temperature conversions to/from absolute scales are **linear (affine)**, not proportional. Celsius and Kelvin share the same scale increment; Fahrenheit and Rankine share theirs.

### Step-by-Step Conversion Reasoning

**Celsius to Kelvin:**
- The size of one degree is identical on both scales.
- Only the zero point shifts: 0 °C = 273.15 K.
- Therefore: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$

**Fahrenheit to Rankine:**
- 0 °F = 459.67 °R.
- Therefore: $T(°R) = T(°F) + 459.67$

**Celsius to Fahrenheit:**
- The size of a Fahrenheit degree is 5/9 of a Celsius degree.
- The zero point differs: 0 °C = 32 °F.
- Therefore: $T(°F) = \frac{9}{5} T(°C) + 32$

## 2.3 Pressure Scale Conversion Logic

Because pressure units vary across engineering domains (SI, CGS, British, industrial), a fluent grasp of conversions is mandatory for KTU problems.

### Base Conversions to Remember

- **1 atm** = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar = 760 mmHg (Torr) = 14.696 psi
- **1 bar** = 100 kPa = 0.9869 atm
- **1 psi** = 6.895 kPa
- **1 mH₂O** (metres of water) = 9.806 kPa
- **1 mmHg** (Torr) = 133.322 Pa

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Board Exams:** "**A**tmospheric pressure **A**lways = **101** kPa approximately." Use 101.325 kPa for high-precision numerical answers.

## 2.4 P-V-T Behaviour of Ideal Gases

The behaviour of ideal gases is described by combining **Boyle's Law** (P-V at constant T), **Charles's Law** (V-T at constant P), and **Avogadro's Law** (V-n at constant T, P).

$$PV = nRT$$

where:
- $P$ = absolute pressure (Pa)
- $V$ = volume (m³)
- $n$ = number of moles (mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

### The Combined Gas Law (for fixed mass, n = constant)

$$\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2} = \text{constant}$$

This is the most frequently tested form in KTU numerical problems. It allows you to find any one unknown ($P_2$, $V_2$, or $T_2$) when the other five quantities are known.

## 2.5 Real Gases and Compressibility

Real gases deviate from ideal behaviour, especially at **high pressure** and **low temperature**. This deviation is captured by the **compressibility factor Z**:

$$PV = ZnRT$$

- $Z = 1$ → ideal gas
- $Z < 1$ → attractive forces dominate (e.g., low-T, moderate-P)
- $Z > 1$ → repulsive forces dominate (e.g., high-P)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **energy management** calculations in steam plants, refrigeration, and HVAC, the steam tables and refrigerant tables (which tabulate properties as functions of T and P) are always used instead of the ideal gas equation, because water vapour and refrigerants are far from ideal.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P = F/A$ | Definition of pressure | Pa = N/m² |
| 2 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | Absolute pressure relation | Pa |
| 3 | $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$ | Celsius to Kelvin | K |
| 4 | $T(°R) = T(°F) + 459.67$ | Fahrenheit to Rankine | °R |
| 5 | $T(°F) = \frac{9}{5} T(°C) + 32$ | Celsius to Fahrenheit | °F |
| 6 | $PV = nRT$ | Ideal Gas Law | Pa·m³ = J |
| 7 | $\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$ | Combined Gas Law | — |
| 8 | $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) | Universal gas constant | J/(mol·K) |
| 9 | $R = 0.0821$ L·atm/(mol·K) | Gas constant in litres | L·atm/(mol·K) |
| 10 | $PV = ZnRT$ | Real gas equation | — |
| 11 | $\rho = PM/(RT)$ | Density of an ideal gas | kg/m³ |
| 12 | 1 atm = 101.325 kPa | Standard pressure | kPa |
| 13 | 1 bar = 100 kPa | Industrial pressure unit | kPa |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Boiler Efficiency (Direct Method):** Requires measurement of feed water temperature, flue gas temperature, and steam pressure. All three feed into the energy balance.
- **Compressed Air System Audits:** Pressure drop across filters, dryers, and pipes is the key indicator of energy loss.
- **Insulation Effectiveness:** Heat loss $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ depends on temperature difference across the insulated surface.
- **Combustion Analysis:** Air-fuel ratio, excess air, and stoichiometric calculations all depend on the temperature and pressure of the inlet streams.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Combined Gas Law from Boyle's and Charles's Laws

### Step A: Start with Boyle's Law (Constant T)

At constant temperature, pressure is inversely proportional to volume:

$$P \propto \frac{1}{V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad PV = k_1 \text{ (constant)}$$

Therefore, for two states at the same temperature:

$$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$$

### Step B: Start with Charles's Law (Constant P)

At constant pressure, volume is directly proportional to absolute temperature:

$$V \propto T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V}{T} = k_2 \text{ (constant)}$$

Therefore, for two states at the same pressure:

$$\frac{V_1}{T_1} = \frac{V_2}{T_2}$$

### Step C: Combine Both Proportionalities

We want a single relation linking P, V, and T for a fixed mass. Notice that:

- $V \propto \frac{1}{P}$ (from Boyle)
- $V \propto T$ (from Charles)

Combining these two proportionalities:

$$V \propto \frac{T}{P}$$

This means the ratio $PV/T$ must be a constant for a fixed amount of gas:

$$\frac{PV}{T} = \text{constant}$$

### Step D: Apply to Two States

For a closed system (fixed mass), the constant has the same value in state 1 and state 2:

$$\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$$

This is the **Combined Gas Law**, the cornerstone equation for KTU numerical problems on temperature and pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write $\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_2} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_1}$ by mixing up subscripts. Always keep the subscript "1" on the left side and "2" on the right side throughout the calculation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ideal Gas Law from Avogadro's Hypothesis

### Step A: Recall Avogadro's Law

At equal T and P, equal volumes of gases contain equal numbers of molecules:

$$V \propto n \quad \text{(at constant T, P)}$$

### Step B: Combine with PV/T relation

From the Combined Gas Law derivation, for a fixed n we have $PV/T = $ const. Now allow n to vary:

$$V \propto \frac{nT}{P} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = \frac{nRT}{P}$$

Rearranging:

$$PV = nRT$$

This is the **Ideal Gas Law** in its full form.

### Step C: Identify R for a Specific Gas

For one mole of gas at STP (T = 273.15 K, P = 101325 Pa, V = 0.022414 m³):

$$R = \frac{PV}{nT} = \frac{101325 \times 0.022414}{1 \times 273.15} = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

This proves the numerical value of the universal gas constant by direct calculation from experimental data.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Celsius-to-Kelvin Conversion from Physical Reasoning

The Celsius scale defines:
- 0 °C = melting point of ice at 1 atm
- 100 °C = boiling point of water at 1 atm
- 1 °C interval = 1/100 of this range

The Kelvin scale defines:
- 0 K = absolute zero (theoretically, all molecular motion ceases)
- 273.15 K = 0 °C (ice point)
- 1 K interval = 1 °C interval (same size)

Since the intervals are identical, the conversion is a pure **shift** of the zero point:

$$T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$$

Substituting 0 °C: $T(K) = 0 + 273.15 = 273.15$ K ✓
Substituting 100 °C: $T(K) = 100 + 273.15 = 373.15$ K ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The number **273.15** is exact by the 2019 SI redefinition. Some textbooks round to 273; for KTU board exams, use **273.15** to avoid losing marks on precision-based questions.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fahrenheit-Celsius Conversion from Fixed Points

The Fahrenheit scale was originally defined with:
- 0 °F = temperature of a mixture of ice, water, and ammonium chloride
- 96 °F = human body temperature (later adjusted to 98.6 °F)
- On the modern scale: 32 °F = ice point, 212 °F = steam point

### Step A: Find the Degree Ratio

On the Celsius scale, the range from ice to steam is 100 °C.
On the Fahrenheit scale, the same range is $212 - 32 = 180$ °F.

Therefore, one Celsius degree equals $\frac{180}{100} = \frac{9}{5}$ Fahrenheit degrees.

### Step B: Account for the Offset

Since 0 °C corresponds to 32 °F (not 0 °F), we must add the offset:

$$T(°F) = \frac{9}{5} T(°C) + 32$$

### Step C: Verify with Fixed Points

- Ice point: $T(°F) = \frac{9}{5}(0) + 32 = 32$ °F ✓
- Steam point: $T(°F) = \frac{9}{5}(100) + 32 = 180 + 32 = 212$ °F ✓
- Body temperature 37 °C: $T(°F) = \frac{9}{5}(37) + 32 = 66.6 + 32 = 98.6$ °F ✓

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — KTU-Style Problem

> **Problem (KTU Style):** A gas occupies 2.5 m³ at a temperature of 30 °C and pressure of 105 kPa. Find the volume it will occupy at STP (T = 15 °C, P = 100 kPa). Assume ideal gas behaviour.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1:** Convert all temperatures to Kelvin (absolute scale):
- $T_1 = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15$ K
- $T_2 = 15 + 273.15 = 288.15$ K

**Step 2:** Identify known and unknown:
- $P_1 = 105$ kPa, $V_1 = 2.5$ m³, $T_1 = 303.15$ K
- $P_2 = 100$ kPa, $T_2 = 288.15$ K, $V_2 = ?$

**Step 3:** Apply Combined Gas Law:

$$\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $V_2$:

$$V_2 = \frac{P_1 V_1 T_2}{T_1 P_2}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute numerical values:

$$V_2 = \frac{105 \times 2.5 \times 288.15}{303.15 \times 100}$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate numerator and denominator separately:

$$\text{Numerator} = 105 \times 2.5 \times 288.15 = 75639.375$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 303.15 \times 100 = 30315$$

**Step 7:** Final division:

$$V_2 = \frac{75639.375}{30315} \approx 2.495 \text{ m}^3$$

**Conclusion:** The gas volume decreases slightly because the temperature decreased (more important effect) even though the pressure also decreased (which would tend to expand the gas).

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Measurement Instruments

| Parameter | Temperature Instruments | Pressure Instruments |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mechanical | Liquid-in-glass thermometer, bimetallic strip | Bourdon tube, diaphragm, bellows |
| Electrical | RTD (Pt-100), Thermocouple (K, J, T type), Thermistor | Strain-gauge transducer, piezoelectric sensor, capacitive sensor |
| Optical/Radiation | Pyrometer, IR thermometer, thermal imaging | — |
