| Reference Standard | Triple point of water (273.16 K) | Dead-weight tester, primary standard manometer |
| Typical Range | -200 °C to 3000 °C | 1 Pa to 1000 MPa |
| KTU Exam Tip | Know Seebeck effect for thermocouples | Know gauge vs. absolute reference |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Temperature Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Fluid] --> B[Sensor Element RTD Thermocouple Pyrometer]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Amplification Linearization Cold Junction Compensation]
    C --> D[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    D --> E[Microprocessor Signal Processing]
    E --> F[Display HMI DCS SCADA]
    F --> G[Control Action Valve Damper Heater]
    G --> H[Energy Management Decision]
    H -.Feedback.-> A
```

**Interpretation:** This diagram shows the complete signal flow chain in a modern industrial temperature measurement loop. The sensor converts thermal energy into an electrical signal, which is then conditioned, digitized, processed, displayed, and finally used to make energy management decisions that loop back to influence the process. Every energy audit and management decision is built upon this chain.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pressure Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Process Pressure Source] --> P2[Primary Sensor Bourdon Diaphragm Strain Gauge]
    P2 --> P3[Mechanical to Electrical Conversion]
    P3 --> P4[Signal Amplifier and Filter]
    P4 --> P5[ADC Conversion]
    P5 --> P6[Microcontroller with Linearization and Temperature Compensation]
    P6 --> P7[Output Display 4-20 mA HART Protocol]
    P7 --> P8[Energy Management System Leak Detection Compressor Optimization]
    P8 --> P9[Actuator Valve Control]
    P9 --> P1
```

**Interpretation:** The pressure measurement chain mirrors the temperature chain but is tuned for force/area detection. The output is typically a 4–20 mA current signal, which is the industrial standard for noise-immune long-distance transmission. Energy management applications (e.g., compressed air leak detection) depend entirely on the accuracy of this chain.

## 4.3 Thermodynamic State Map — T-P-V Surfaces

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph State Variables
        SV1[Temperature T in K]
        SV2[Pressure P in Pa]
        SV3[Volume V in m3]
        SV4[Amount n in mol]
    end
    subgraph Governing Law
        GL[Ideal Gas Equation PV equals nRT]
    end
    SV1 --> GL
    SV2 --> GL
    SV3 --> GL
    SV4 --> GL
    GL --> O1[State Point on P-V-T Surface]
    GL --> O2[Process Path on P-V-T Surface]
    GL --> O3[Energy Calculation Internal Energy Enthalpy]
```

**Interpretation:** The P-V-T state surface of a substance is the geometric representation of all possible equilibrium states. Any point on this surface is a valid state; any line is a process. Energy management calculations (work, heat, internal energy changes) reduce to geometric operations (areas under curves) on projections of this surface.

## 4.4 Pressure Reference Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z[Absolute Zero Vacuum 0 Pa] --> A1[Atmospheric Pressure 101.325 kPa]
    A1 --> G1[Gauge Pressure Region Above Atmospheric]
    A1 --> V1[Vacuum Region Below Atmospheric]
    G1 --> H1[High Pressure Industrial 10 MPa and above]
    G1 --> M1[Medium Pressure Compressed Air 0.7 to 1.5 MPa]
    G1 --> L1[Low Pressure HVAC 1 to 10 kPa]
    V1 --> RV[Rough Vacuum 100 kPa to 1 Pa]
    RV --> HV[High Vacuum Below 1 Pa]
    HV --> UHV[Ultra High Vacuum Below 10 to the power of negative 7 Pa]
```

**Interpretation:** This hierarchy clarifies the four commonly used pressure references in engineering: absolute (from perfect vacuum), gauge (from atmospheric), vacuum (negative gauge), and differential (between two points). The KTU board exam frequently tests whether students can correctly convert between these regions, since an incorrect reference choice can flip the sign of an answer and lead to "impossible" physical results.

## 4.5 Conversion Master Flowchart — Temperature and Pressure Units

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TC1[Celsius] -->|Add 273.15| TK[Kelvin]
    TC1 -->|Multiply 9 by 5 and add 32| TF[Fahrenheit]
    TF -->|Add 459.67| TR[Rankine]
    TK -->|Subtract 273.15| TC1
    TF -->|Subtract 32 then multiply 5 by 9| TC1
    TR -->|Subtract 459.67| TF
    PC1[Pascal Pa] -->|Divide by 1000| PK[kPa]
    PK -->|Divide by 100| PB[bar]
    PK -->|Multiply by 0.009869| PA[atm]
    PB -->|Multiply by 14.696| PPI[psi]
    PA -->|Multiply by 760| PT[mmHg or Torr]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define temperature. Explain the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics and its significance in temperature measurement.**

**Model Answer:**

Temperature is a thermodynamic property that indicates the degree of hotness or coldness of a body. It is an intensive property and determines the direction of spontaneous heat transfer between two systems in thermal contact.

The **Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics** states: *If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other.*

**Significance in temperature measurement:**
- The Zeroth Law justifies the use of a **thermometer** as a third reference system.
- A thermometer placed in thermal contact with a body reaches thermal equilibrium.
- The reading on the thermometer (e.g., length of mercury column, resistance of RTD) is then a valid indicator of the body's temperature.
- Without the Zeroth Law, temperature measurement would have no theoretical foundation.

**[3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for Zeroth Law statement, 1 for measurement significance]**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Distinguish between absolute pressure, gauge pressure, and vacuum pressure. A pressure gauge reads 85 kPa at a location where atmospheric pressure is 98 kPa. Find the absolute pressure.**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Reference | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Absolute | Perfect vacuum (zero) | Thermodynamics, gas law calculations |
| Gauge | Local atmospheric | Industrial gauges, most process measurements |
| Vacuum | Below atmospheric | Condensers, vacuum systems |

**Numerical Solution:**

Given: $P_{gauge} = 85$ kPa, $P_{atm} = 98$ kPa.

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge} = 98 + 85 = 183 \text{ kPa}$$

**[3 marks: 1 for distinction, 1 for relation, 1 for numerical calculation]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Derive the Combined Gas Law from Boyle's Law and Charles's Law. A gas occupies 0.5 m³ at 1.2 atm and 27 °C. What will be its volume at STP (1 atm, 0 °C)?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** State Boyle's Law: At constant T, $P \propto \frac{1}{V}$, hence $PV = $ const, so $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$.

**Step 2:** State Charles's Law: At constant P, $V \propto T$ (T in Kelvin), hence $\frac{V_1}{T_1} = \frac{V_2}{T_2}$.

**Step 3:** Combine both relations. Since $V \propto \frac{1}{P}$ and $V \propto T$, we have $V \propto \frac{T}{P}$, hence $\frac{PV}{T} = $ constant.

**Step 4:** Apply to two states: $\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$.

**Step 5:** Numerical problem — convert all to absolute and consistent units.

$T_1 = 27 + 273.15 = 300.15$ K, $P_1 = 1.2$ atm, $V_1 = 0.5$ m³
$T_2 = 0 + 273.15 = 273.15$ K, $P_2 = 1$ atm, $V_2 = ?$

**Step 6:** Substitute:

$$V_2 = \frac{P_1 V_1 T_2}{T_1 P_2} = \frac{1.2 \times 0.5 \times 273.15}{300.15 \times 1}$$

**Step 7:** Calculate: $V_2 = \frac{163.89}{300.15} = 0.546$ m³.

**Final Answer:** $V_2 \approx 0.546$ m³ at STP.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Explain the principle, construction, and working of a Bourdon tube pressure gauge. State two advantages and two limitations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** A Bourdon tube is a curved metallic tube of elliptical cross-section. When internal pressure is applied, the tube tends to straighten (the cross-section becomes more circular), producing a displacement at the free end proportional to the applied pressure.

**Construction:**
- A flattened, curved tube (usually C-shaped, helical, or spiral) made of phosphor bronze, beryllium copper, or stainless steel.
- One end is fixed and connected to the process pressure.
- The free end is sealed and connected via a mechanical linkage to a gear-and-pinion assembly that drives a pointer over a graduated scale.

**Working:**
1. Process pressure enters the fixed end of the Bourdon tube.
2. The internal pressure tries to make the elliptical cross-section circular.
3. The curved tube slightly straightens, moving the free end.
4. This small linear motion is converted to rotary motion of the pointer via the link-and-lever mechanism.
5. The pointer indicates the gauge pressure on the dial.

**Advantages:**
1. Simple, robust, and inexpensive.
2. Good accuracy (typically ±0.5% to ±2% of full scale) for a wide range.

**Limitations:**
1. Not suitable for highly corrosive or very viscous fluids without special materials.
2. Hysteresis and mechanical wear can reduce long-term accuracy.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD). State the relationship between resistance and temperature. Mention the materials commonly used.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Process Temperature] → [RTD Sensing Element (Pt-100)] → [Wheatstone Bridge]
→ [Amplifier] → [ADC] → [Microcontroller] → [Display / 4-20 mA Output]
```

**Working Principle:** The RTD works on the principle that the **electrical resistance of a metal increases with temperature** in a nearly linear manner.

**Mathematical Relationship:**

$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha (T - T_0) \right]$$

where:
- $R_T$ = resistance at temperature T
- $R_0$ = resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (usually 0 °C)
- $\alpha$ = temperature coefficient of resistance (for platinum, $\alpha = 0.00385$ /°C)

**For Pt-100:** $R_0 = 100$ $\Omega$ at 0 °C, so at 100 °C, $R_T \approx 138.5$ $\Omega$.

**Materials Used:**
1. **Platinum** (most common, Pt-100, Pt-1000) — high accuracy, stability, wide range (-200 °C to 850 °C).
2. **Copper** — lower cost, limited range, used where moderate accuracy is acceptable.
3. **Nickel** — higher $\alpha$, used in some industrial applications.

**Advantages:** High accuracy, good stability, linear response, suitable for precision measurements.
**Limitations:** Requires excitation current (self-heating), lower sensitivity than thermocouples at high temperatures.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**A thermocouple is used to measure the temperature of a furnace. The hot junction is at 800 °C and the cold junction is at 30 °C. The thermoelectric EMF generated is 33.27 mV. The reference table value of EMF at 800 °C (with cold junction at 0 °C) is 33.29 mV. Calculate the true furnace temperature using the law of intermediate temperatures. Given: $dE/dT = 0.041$ mV/°C at 800 °C.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Hot junction temperature: $T = 800$ °C
- Cold junction temperature: $T_c = 30$ °C
- Measured EMF: $E_{measured} = 33.27$ mV
- Tabulated EMF at 800 °C (cold junction at 0 °C): $E_{800} = 33.29$ mV
- Seebeck coefficient: $S = dE/dT = 0.041$ mV/°C at 800 °C

**Step 1 — Apply the Law of Intermediate Temperatures:**

The thermoelectric EMF generated by a thermocouple with junctions at $T$ and $T_c$ equals the algebraic difference of the EMFs with junctions at $T$ and 0 °C, and at $T_c$ and 0 °C:

$$E(T, T_c) = E(T, 0) - E(T_c, 0)$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the EMF that would be generated if cold junction were at 0 °C:**

The measured EMF corresponds to a hot junction temperature of 800 °C with cold junction at 30 °C. The true furnace EMF (cold junction at 0 °C) is higher because the cold junction correction is missing.

**Step 3 — Cold Junction Compensation:**

The missing EMF due to the cold junction being at 30 °C instead of 0 °C is:

$$E_{correction} = S \times T_c = 0.041 \times 30 = 1.23 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 4 — True EMF at furnace temperature with cold junction at 0 °C:**

$$E_{true} = E_{measured} + E_{correction} = 33.27 + 1.23 = 34.50 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 5 — Now find the true temperature corresponding to this EMF using the Seebeck coefficient:**

$$\Delta T = \frac{E_{true} - E_{800}}{S} = \frac{34.50 - 33.29}{0.041} = \frac{1.21}{0.041} = 29.51 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 6 — True furnace temperature:**

$$T_{true} = 800 + 29.51 = 829.51 \text{ °C} \approx 830 \text{ °C}$$

**Conclusion:** The true furnace temperature is approximately **830 °C**, which is higher than the apparent 800 °C reading because of the cold-junction temperature effect.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Temperature & Pressure Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K in gas law problems (loses 2–3 marks instantly).** If you use 27 °C instead of 300.15 K, your final answer is off by a factor of ~10, and the examiner will mark it wrong even if the algebra is correct.
> 2. **Confusing gauge and absolute pressure.** When a problem says "the gauge reads 5 bar," that is NOT the absolute pressure. Add 1.013 bar (or 1 bar in approximate problems) to get absolute. Many students forget this and produce impossible answers.
> 3. **Mixing units within a single equation.** If P is in kPa and V is in m³, R for ideal gas must be 8.314 J/(mol·K). If P is in atm and V is in litres, R must be 0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K). Mixed units = wrong answer = zero marks.
> 4. **Omitting the cold-junction correction in thermocouple problems.** The given tabulated EMF is for a cold junction at 0 °C. If the actual cold junction is warmer, the measured EMF is lower than the true value, and the true temperature is higher than the apparent reading.
> 5. **Writing PV = nRT without specifying that T is absolute.** Examiners actively look for this clarification; omitting it may cost a mark.
> 6. **In conversion problems, students often confuse the constant 273 vs 273.15.** For KTU 2024 Scheme (post-2019 SI), use **273.15** unless the problem explicitly says "approximate."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Temperature** is an intensive property; heat is extensive. Do not confuse the two.
- **Always use absolute temperature (K or °R)** in any gas-law calculation.
- **The Zeroth Law** is the foundation of all temperature measurement — state it correctly when asked.
- **The three primary temperature scales** are Kelvin (SI), Celsius (practical), and Fahrenheit (US). Know exact fixed-point values: 273.15, 32, 212.
- **Ideal Gas Law** $PV = nRT$ with $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) applies to low-pressure, high-temperature gases.
- **Real gases** require the **compressibility factor Z** or look-up tables; steam and refrigerants are always real gases.
- **Combined Gas Law** $\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$ is the workhorse equation for fixed-mass process calculations.
- **Absolute pressure = gauge pressure + atmospheric pressure** (when gauge is above atmospheric).
- **Standard atmospheric pressure** is **101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar = 760 mmHg = 14.696 psi**.
- **1 bar = 100 kPa** (exact), 1 atm = 1.01325 bar (approximated as 1 bar in many industrial contexts).
- **Pressure measurement devices:** Bourdon tube (most common mechanical), strain gauge (most common electrical), piezoelectric (dynamic), manometer (primary standard).
- **Temperature measurement devices:** Liquid-in-glass, bimetallic strip, RTD (Pt-100), Thermocouple (K-type most common), Pyrometer (for high temperatures).
- **Cold-junction compensation** is mandatory in thermocouple circuits; the **Law of Intermediate Temperatures** is the governing principle.
- **Bourdon tube** works on the principle of cross-section deformation under pressure.
- **RTD relationship:** $R_T = R_0[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]$ with $\alpha_{Pt} = 0.00385$ /°C.
- **Energy management link:** T and P are the two most-measured variables in any energy audit; they directly govern state, efficiency, and losses of every thermal system.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Heat Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Heat Capacity ($C$)** is defined as the amount of thermal energy (heat) that must be transferred to a body to raise its temperature by exactly **one degree** (1 K or 1 °C). It is an extensive thermodynamic property of a system.

$$C = \lim_{\Delta T \to 0} \frac{\delta Q}{\Delta T}$$

where:
- $\delta Q$ = infinitesimal amount of heat transferred (J)
- $\Delta T$ = change in temperature (K or °C)
- **SI Unit:** J/K (Joule per Kelvin)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Capacity vs. Specific Heat Capacity — A Critical Distinction**
> - **Heat Capacity ($C$):** Total heat required for the *entire body*; depends on mass. Units: **J/K**
> - **Specific Heat Capacity ($c$):** Heat required per *unit mass*; an **intensive property** independent of sample size. Units: **J/(kg·K)**
>
> The relationship is: $C = m \cdot c$

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bucket" Intuition

Imagine two buckets on a sunny terrace — one a small steel mug and the other a large clay pot filled with water. If you pour the *same quantity of sunlight* (heat energy $Q$) on both:

- The **small steel mug** heats up **quickly** to a high temperature → it has a **low heat capacity**.
- The **large clay pot** barely changes temperature even after a long time → it has a **high heat capacity**.

> The clay pot can *store more thermal energy per degree of temperature rise* because water inherently resists temperature change. This "resistance to temperature change" is exactly what **heat capacity** measures.

In engineering terms:
- Materials with **high $c$** (e.g., water: $c = 4186$ J/(kg·K)) are excellent **thermal reservoirs** and coolants.
- Materials with **low $c$** (e.g., copper: $c = 385$ J/(kg·K)) heat and cool rapidly, making them ideal for **heat exchangers and fins**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this critical in Energy Management?**
> Heat capacity dictates the *thermal inertia* of a system. Buildings with high thermal mass (concrete, water tanks) require **less air-conditioning energy** because they naturally buffer temperature swings — a cornerstone of passive energy conservation design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Heat Capacity

Heat capacity is not a single fixed value — it depends on **how** the heat is added to the system:

1. **Heat Capacity at Constant Volume ($C_v$)**
   - Heat added while volume is held fixed.
   - All heat goes into raising internal energy ($U$).
   - $C_v = \left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V$

2. **Heat Capacity at Constant Pressure ($C_p$)**
   - Heat added while pressure is held fixed.
   - Heat goes into both internal energy rise **and** work done by expansion ($P\,\Delta V$).
   - $C_p = \left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P$ where $H$ is enthalpy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For any substance, **$C_p > C_v$** because at constant pressure, part of the supplied heat performs expansion work ($\delta W = P\,dV$), leaving less energy available to raise the temperature.

---

### 2.2 Why Two Paths Exist — Thermodynamic Reasoning

For an infinitesimal reversible process, the **First Law of Thermodynamics** gives:

$$\delta Q = dU + P\,dV$$

- At **constant $V$**: $dV = 0 \Rightarrow \delta Q = dU \Rightarrow C_v = \left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V$
- At **constant $P$**: Define enthalpy $H = U + PV \Rightarrow dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP$
   With $dP = 0$: $dH = \delta Q \Rightarrow C_p = \left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P$

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Condition |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $C = m \cdot c$ | Total heat capacity from specific heat | $C$ in J/K |
| 2 | $C_p - C_v = R$ | Mayer's relation (ideal gas, molar form) | J/(mol·K) |
| 3 | $C_p - C_v = \dfrac{R}{M}$ | Mayer's relation (specific, per unit mass) | J/(kg·K) |
| 4 | $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v}$ | Specific heat ratio (adiabatic index) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\delta Q = m \cdot c \cdot dT$ | Sensible heat transfer (no phase change) | J |
| 6 | $Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ | Total sensible heat for finite temperature change | J |
| 7 | $C_p = C_v + \dfrac{V^2 T \alpha^2}{\kappa_T}$ | General (real substance) — not on exam, reference only | J/(mol·K) |
| 8 | $Q = m \cdot L$ | Latent heat (phase change, $\Delta T = 0$) | J |
| 9 | $C_v = \dfrac{f}{2}R$ | Ideal gas (monatomic $f=3$, diatomic $f=5$) | J/(mol·K) |
| 10 | $c_{\text{water}} = 4186$ | Specific heat of liquid water (reference constant) | J/(kg·K) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of Heat Capacity in Energy Management**
> 1. **HVAC Load Estimation:** $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ directly gives the heating/cooling load of buildings.
> 2. **Thermal Energy Storage (TES):** Selecting storage media with high $c$ (molten salts, water) maximizes stored energy per kg.
> 3. **Waste Heat Recovery:** Lower $C_p$ of exhaust gases means less energy is lost in flue gas per degree of cooling — useful for economizer sizing.
> 4. **Process Industry:** Reactor cooling jackets are sized using $C_p$ of process fluids.

---

### 2.4 Key Physical Insights (The "Why" Behind the Math)

- **For solids and liquids:** $C_p \approx C_v$ because thermal expansion work $P\,dV$ is negligible. A single value ($c$) is usually sufficient.
- **For ideal gases:** The difference $C_p - C_v$ is exactly the universal gas constant $R$ (per mole), a direct consequence of the ideal gas law.
- **Temperature dependence:** Real materials show $c = c(T)$ — e.g., $c$ of copper varies measurably between 0 °C and 500 °C. For precise engineering work, average $c$ over the temperature range is used:

$$\bar{c} = \frac{1}{T_2 - T_1}\int_{T_1}^{T_2} c(T)\,dT$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Mayer's Relation ($C_p - C_v = R$ for an Ideal Gas)

We start from the First Law applied to a closed system undergoing a reversible process:

$$\delta Q = dU + P\,dV$$

By definition:

$$C_v = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V \quad \text{and} \quad C_p = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 1 — Enthalpy Definition**

Enthalpy $H$ is defined as:

$$H = U + PV$$

Differentiating both sides gives:

$$dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into First Law at Constant Pressure**

At constant pressure ($dP = 0$):

$$dH = dU + P\,dV = \delta Q$$

Therefore:

$$C_p = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 3 — Express $dH$ for an Ideal Gas**

For an ideal gas, $PV = nRT$ (where $n$ = moles, $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K)). Differentiating $PV$ with respect to $T$ at constant $P$:

$$P\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = nR$$

Now expand $dH$ keeping both variables:

$$dH = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left[P\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P + V\right]dP$$

**Step 4 — Apply Constant Pressure Condition ($dP = 0$)**

$$\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V + P\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

Recognizing the two definitions:

$$C_p = C_v + P\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Ideal Gas Law**

For one mole ($n = 1$) of ideal gas: $PV = RT$, so at constant $P$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{R}{P}$$

**Step 6 — Final Result (Mayer's Relation)**

$$C_p = C_v + P \cdot \frac{R}{P} = C_v + R$$

$$\boxed{C_p - C_v = R}$$

where $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) is the universal gas constant. Per unit mass (specific form), $c_p - c_v = R/M$ with $M$ = molar mass in kg/kmol.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Sensible Heat Equation

Sensible heat is the heat exchanged that produces a measurable temperature change (as opposed to latent heat, which causes phase change).

For a body of mass $m$ with specific heat $c$ undergoing temperature change $\Delta T = T_2 - T_1$:

$$Q = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} m \cdot c(T)\,dT$$

If $c$ is approximately constant over the range:

$$Q = m \cdot c \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$$

For multiple substances in thermal contact (no phase change), heat balance:

$$\sum_i m_i c_i \Delta T_i = 0$$

This is the governing equation for **calorimetry problems** and **HVAC load estimation**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Specific Heat Ratio ($\gamma$) for Common Ideal Gases

Using $C_v = \frac{f}{2}R$ where $f$ = degrees of freedom:

| Gas Type | $f$ | $C_v$ | $C_p = C_v + R$ | $\gamma = C_p/C_v$ |
|----------|-----|-------|------------------|--------------------|
| Monatomic (He, Ar) | 3 | $\frac{3}{2}R$ | $\frac{5}{2}R$ | $5/3 = 1.667$ |
| Diatomic (N₂, O₂, air) | 5 | $\frac{5}{2}R$ | $\frac{7}{2}R$ | $7/5 = 1.400$ |
| Polyatomic (CO₂, CH₄) | 6 | $\frac{6}{2}R$ | $\frac{7}{2}R$ | $7/6 = 1.286$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air (diatomic dominant)** is treated as $\gamma = 1.4$ in almost all energy management and combustion calculations — this is the standard KTU assumption.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Problem:** 50 kg of water at 30 °C is to be heated to 80 °C in an electric geyser. Find:
(a) Heat energy required (take $c_w = 4186$ J/(kg·K))
(b) Cost at ₹8 per kWh
(c) Time taken if heater rating is 2 kW (assume 100% efficiency)

**Solution:**

(a) Heat required:

$$Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T = 50 \times 4186 \times (80 - 30)$$

$$Q = 50 \times 4186 \times 50 = 1.0465 \times 10^{7} \text{ J}$$

(b) Convert to kWh:

$$Q_{\text{kWh}} = \frac{1.0465 \times 10^{7}}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} = 2.907 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\text{Cost} = 2.907 \times 8 = \text{₹}\,23.26$$

(c) Time:

$$t = \frac{Q_{\text{kWh}}}{P_{\text{kW}}} = \frac{2.907}{2} = 1.453 \text{ hours} \approx 87.2 \text{ min}$$

This single example shows how **specific heat capacity** flows from a thermodynamic property to an **energy bill** — directly bridging thermodynamics with energy economics, the heart of this course.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Heat Capacity (Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heat Capacity C]
    A --> B[At Constant Volume Cv]
    A --> C[At Constant Pressure Cp]
    A --> D[Specific vs Molar]
    D --> D1[Specific c J per kg K]
    D --> D2[Molar C J per mol K]
    B --> B1[dQ at dV equals 0]
    B --> B2[All heat raises internal energy]
    C --> C1[dQ at dP equals 0]
    C --> C2[Heat raises enthalpy H equals U plus PV]
    C --> E[Mayer Relation]
    B --> E
    E --> E1[Cp minus Cv equals R for ideal gas]
    E --> E2[gamma equals Cp divided by Cv]
    E2 --> F1[Monatomic gamma equals 1.667]
    E2 --> F2[Diatomic gamma equals 1.400]
    E2 --> F3[Polyatomic gamma equals 1.286]
```

### 4.2 Energy Flow in Sensible Heating (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Heat Source] -->|Q in| m1[Mass m kg]
    m1 --> c[Specific Heat c]
    c --> dt[Temperature Rise Delta T]
    dt --> out[Stored Sensible Heat Q equals m c Delta T]
    out --> app1[HVAC Load Estimation]
    out --> app2[Thermal Energy Storage]
    out --> app3[Process Heating Load]
    out --> app4[Calorimetry Problems]
```

### 4.3 Cp vs Cv — Physical Process Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Same Heat Q supplied]
    A --> B[Process 1 Constant Volume]
    A --> C[Process 2 Constant Pressure]
    B --> B1[All Q raises internal energy dU]
    B --> B2[Temperature rise delta Tv]
    B --> B3[Cv equals Q divided by delta Tv]
    C --> C1[Part Q does expansion work P dV]
    C --> C2[Remaining raises internal energy]
    C --> C3[Temperature rise delta Tp]
    C --> C4[Cp equals Q divided by delta Tp]
    B3 --> D[Since Cp greater than Cv]
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[delta Tp less than delta Tv for same Q]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for Exam Writing:**
> In KTU 14-mark answers, a labelled schematic like the one above fetches **1–2 easy marks** for visual presentation, even before the math begins. Always include a simple flow diagram when the question asks for a comparison (e.g., "$C_p$ vs $C_v$").
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define specific heat capacity. State its SI unit.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Specific heat capacity ($c$) is defined as the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of **one kilogram** of a substance by **one Kelvin** (or 1 °C). It is an intensive property.

$$c = \frac{Q}{m \cdot \Delta T}$$

**SI Unit:** J/(kg·K). For water at standard conditions, $c_w = 4186$ J/(kg·K).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse *specific heat capacity* (per kg) with *heat capacity* (per body). They are **NOT the same**. Mark deduction of ½ mark if units are mismatched.

---

**Q2. State and explain Mayer's relation for an ideal gas.**
`[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Mayer's relation states that for an ideal gas, the difference between molar heat capacities at constant pressure and constant volume equals the universal gas constant.

$$C_p - C_v = R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

**Physical reason:** At constant pressure, a portion of supplied heat performs expansion work ($P\,\Delta V$), so a larger quantity of heat must be supplied to achieve the same temperature rise — making $C_p > C_v$ by exactly $R$ for one mole of ideal gas.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Derive Mayer's relation $C_p - C_v = R$ for an ideal gas. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **First Law for a closed system:**

$$\delta Q = dU + \delta W = dU + P\,dV$$

2. **Definition of $C_v$** (at constant $V$, $dV = 0$):

$$dU = C_v\,dT \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_v = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

3. **Enthalpy definition:**

$$H = U + PV \quad \Rightarrow \quad dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP$$

4. **At constant $P$** ($dP = 0$), the First Law becomes:

$$\delta Q_{P} = dH = C_p\,dT$$

5. **For ideal gas:** $PV = RT$ (per mole). Differentiating at constant $P$:

$$P\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = R$$

6. **Substituting into the $C_p$ expression:**

$$C_p = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V + P\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

$$C_p = C_v + R$$

$$\boxed{C_p - C_v = R}$$

---

**(b) A building's concrete mass is estimated at 200,000 kg with an average specific heat of 880 J/(kg·K). During a summer day, the temperature rises from 25 °C to 38 °C. Calculate (i) the heat absorbed by the structure and (ii) the equivalent air-conditioning load in TR-tonnes if this heat is to be removed in 4 hours. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Heat absorbed:**

$$Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q = 200{,}000 \times 880 \times (38 - 25)$$

$$Q = 200{,}000 \times 880 \times 13 = 2.288 \times 10^{9} \text{ J}$$

**(ii) Convert to TR-tonnes:**

1 TR (ton of refrigeration) = 3.517 kW = $3.517 \times 3600 = 12{,}661.2$ kJ/h

Energy to be removed in 4 hours:

$$Q = 2.288 \times 10^{9} \text{ J} = 2.288 \times 10^{6} \text{ kJ}$$

Heat removal rate:

$$\dot{Q} = \frac{2.288 \times 10^{6}}{4} = 5.72 \times 10^{5} \text{ kJ/h}$$

In TR:

$$\text{Load} = \frac{5.72 \times 10^{5}}{12{,}661.2} \approx 45.18 \text{ TR}$$

**Engineering Insight:** This is precisely why **thermal mass** (high $m \cdot c$) is a passive cooling strategy — the structure absorbs heat during the day, reducing peak AC demand. Designing buildings with exposed concrete (Trombe walls) exploits this principle.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the difference between heat capacity at constant pressure and constant volume for an ideal gas. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram (schematic):** Draw two cylinders — one rigid (constant $V$) and one with a frictionless piston (constant $P$) — each receiving identical heat $\delta Q$.

**Explanation:**

- **At Constant Volume ($C_v$):** The boundary is rigid; no boundary work is done. All supplied heat raises the internal energy of the gas:

$$\delta Q_V = dU = C_v\,dT$$

- **At Constant Pressure ($C_p$):** The piston moves out; the gas does expansion work $P\,dV$. Only part of the supplied heat raises internal energy; the rest performs work:

$$\delta Q_P = dU + P\,dV = dH = C_p\,dT$$

- **Comparison:** Since $C_p$ must supply *both* internal energy rise *and* expansion work, $C_p > C_v$ by exactly $R$ for an ideal gas.

- **Ratio:** $\gamma = C_p / C_v = 1.4$ for air (diatomic), used in adiabatic process equations $PV^\gamma = \text{const}$.

---

**(b) 0.5 m³ of air at 30 °C is heated at constant pressure to 90 °C. Calculate the heat supplied and the work done. Assume air behaves as an ideal gas with $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K). (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Find mass of air.**

Initial conditions: $T_1 = 303$ K, $V_1 = 0.5$ m³, $P_1 = 101.325$ kPa (assumed atmospheric).

Using ideal gas law: $PV = mRT$

$$m = \frac{P_1 V_1}{R T_1} = \frac{101.325 \times 0.5}{0.287 \times 303} = \frac{50.6625}{86.961} \approx 0.5826 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Heat supplied at constant pressure.**

$$Q_P = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1) = 0.5826 \times 1.005 \times (363 - 303)$$

$$Q_P = 0.5826 \times 1.005 \times 60 \approx 35.13 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Work done at constant pressure.**

$$W = P \cdot \Delta V = mR(T_2 - T_1) = 0.5826 \times 0.287 \times 60$$

$$W \approx 10.03 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Verify via First Law.**

$$\Delta U = Q - W = 35.13 - 10.03 = 25.10 \text{ kJ}$$

Cross-check with $C_v$: $c_v = c_p - R = 1.005 - 0.287 = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K)

$$\Delta U = m \cdot c_v \cdot \Delta T = 0.5826 \times 0.718 \times 60 \approx 25.10 \text{ kJ} \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting atmospheric pressure assumption:** If $P$ is not given, assume $P = 101.325$ kPa. Losing 1 mark here is the #1 mistake.
> 2. **Unit confusion:** Mixing $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) with $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K) — these are *different* quantities. Use $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K) for mass-based calculations.
> 3. **Temperature in Kelvin:** Always convert °C → K before substituting.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Heat Capacity ($C$):** Heat to raise temperature of *the whole body* by 1 K → Units: **J/K**
- **Specific Heat ($c$):** Heat per unit mass per 1 K → Units: **J/(kg·K)**
- **Molar Heat ($C_m$):** Heat per mole per 1 K → Units: **J/(mol·K)**
- **Mayer's Relation:** $C_p - C_v = R$ (ideal gas) — *most-tested formula*
- **Air Values:** $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K), $\gamma = 1.4$
- **Water:** $c_w = 4186$ J/(kg·K) — *highest among common liquids*
- **Sensible Heat Equation:** $Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ — *foundation of HVAC load calculations*
- **Latent Heat:** $Q = m \cdot L$ (no $\Delta T$ during phase change)
- **$\gamma$ values:** Monatomic = 1.667, Diatomic = 1.4, Polyatomic ≈ 1.286
- **Engineering link:** Specific heat of building materials directly determines *passive cooling/heating potential*
- **Energy bill link:** Every kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J = ₹8–10 (India domestic average) — quantify everything!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensible and Latent Heat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sensible and Latent Heat

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sensible Heat** is the quantity of heat energy absorbed or released by a substance that results in a measurable change in its temperature, without any change in its physical state (phase). The term *sensible* originates from the Latin *sensibilis*, meaning *that which can be sensed* — because the resulting temperature change can be detected by a thermometer or by the human skin.

**Latent Heat** (from the Latin *latere*, meaning *to lie hidden*) is the quantity of heat energy absorbed or released by a substance during a phase change (solid ↔ liquid ↔ gas) at constant temperature and pressure. This energy is "hidden" because it does not manifest as a temperature change; it instead breaks or forms intermolecular bonds.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A clear distinction between sensible and latent heat is foundational to all energy audits, HVAC load calculations, and psychrometric analyses. Every Module-1 problem on heat addition or removal begins here.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The Boiling Kettle:**
Imagine you place a **1 kg** steel kettle containing **1 litre** of water on a stove at sea-level pressure (**101.325 kPa**).

- **Stage 1 — Sensible Heating:** From room temperature (say **25 °C**) up to **100 °C**, the water gets progressively hotter. Touch it, and you feel the temperature rising. The thermometer needle climbs. This is **sensible heat** in action.
- **Stage 2 — Latent Heating:** Once the water reaches **100 °C**, it begins to boil. Even though the stove keeps pouring in energy, the thermometer stays glued at **100 °C**. All that incoming energy is being used to tear water molecules apart into steam — invisible to a thermometer. This is **latent heat**.
- **Stage 3 — Superheating (Sensible Again):** The steam leaving the spout can be heated further above **100 °C** (superheated steam), and again, the thermometer will detect this rise — sensible heat resumes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** Whenever energy crosses a phase boundary (ice → water → steam), it does so at a constant temperature, and that energy is **latent**. Anywhere else, it is **sensible**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Specific heat capacity of water (c_p, liquid):** **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**
- **Latent heat of fusion of water (L_f):** **333.55 kJ/kg** (at 0 °C)
- **Latent heat of vaporization of water (L_v):** **2260 kJ/kg** (at 100 °C, 1 atm)
- **Specific heat of ice:** **2.093 kJ/(kg·K)**
- **Specific heat of steam (at constant pressure):** **2.010 kJ/(kg·K)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Sensible Heat

The sensible heat exchange of a substance depends on three parameters:

1. **Mass (m)** — More mass → more molecules → more heat required.
2. **Specific Heat Capacity (c)** — A material-specific property quantifying how much energy is needed to raise 1 kg by 1 K.
3. **Temperature Change (ΔT)** — The magnitude of the desired heating or cooling.

**Mechanism (Why & How):**
- Energy supplied increases the **kinetic energy** of molecules → faster molecular vibration/translation → higher measurable temperature.
- The relationship is linear because, for most engineering substances (over small temperature ranges), c is approximately constant.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Latent Heat

Latent heat is required to overcome the **intermolecular binding forces** holding molecules in a particular phase:

- **Fusion (melting):** Energy breaks the rigid lattice of a solid → molecules gain mobility → liquid forms.
- **Vaporization (boiling/evaporation):** Energy overcomes surface tension and cohesive forces → molecules escape into the gaseous phase.
- **Condensation:** Reverse of vaporization — energy is **released** when gas condenses to liquid.
- **Solidification (freezing):** Reverse of fusion — energy is **released** when liquid becomes solid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Latent heat of vaporization is always greater than latent heat of fusion** for the same substance, because vaporization requires complete separation of molecules, while fusion only requires loosening of the lattice.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensible Heat | $Q_s = m \, c \, \Delta T$ | m = mass; c = specific heat; ΔT = temp change | kJ or J |
| Latent Heat (Fusion) | $Q_f = m \, L_f$ | L_f = latent heat of fusion | kJ |
| Latent Heat (Vaporization) | $Q_v = m \, L_v$ | L_v = latent heat of vaporization | kJ |
| Total Heat (Heating from solid to gas) | $Q_{total} = m[c_{ice}\Delta T_1 + L_f + c_{water}\Delta T_2 + L_v + c_{steam}\Delta T_3]$ | Sum of all stages | kJ |
| Heat Capacity | $C = m \, c$ | Total capacity of a body | kJ/K |
| Specific Enthalpy Change | $\Delta h = c \, \Delta T$ | Per unit mass sensible enthalpy | kJ/kg |
| Phase Change Enthalpy | $\Delta h_{phase} = L$ | Per unit mass latent enthalpy | kJ/kg |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HVAC Design:** Cooling coil sizing requires computing both the sensible cooling load (temperature drop) and latent cooling load (moisture removal).
- **Refrigeration Cycles:** The refrigerant absorbs latent heat at the evaporator and rejects it at the condenser.
- **Energy Audits in Boilers:** Calculating feed-water heating (sensible) vs. steam generation (latent) is essential for estimating fuel savings.
- **Thermal Energy Storage (TES):** Phase-Change Materials (PCMs) store energy as latent heat — far denser than sensible storage.
- **District Cooling / Heating:** Sensible heat exchangers preheat or precool streams using recovered energy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Sensible Heat Equation

**Starting Principle:** The First Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system at constant pressure (no work other than boundary work):

$$
Q = \Delta U + W
$$

For a constant-pressure process with negligible shaft work:

$$
Q = \Delta H
$$

Where $\Delta H$ is the change in enthalpy:

$$
\Delta H = m \, c_p \, \Delta T
$$

Therefore, equating the two:

$$
Q_s = m \, c_p \, \Delta T
$$

**Step-by-Step Justification:**

- Step 1: Heat supplied at constant pressure changes the internal energy *and* does boundary work as the substance expands.
- Step 2: The combined effect is conveniently captured by enthalpy $H = U + pV$.
- Step 3: The differential change in enthalpy per unit mass for an incompressible substance is $dh = c_p \, dT$.
- Step 4: Integrating from initial temperature $T_1$ to final temperature $T_2$:

$$
\Delta h = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} c_p \, dT
$$

- Step 5: For small ranges where $c_p \approx \text{constant}$, this integrates to:

$$
\Delta h = c_p \, (T_2 - T_1) = c_p \, \Delta T
$$

- Step 6: Multiplying by mass gives the total sensible heat:

$$
Q_s = m \, c_p \, \Delta T
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Latent Heat Equation

**Starting Principle:** During a phase transition at constant pressure and temperature, all supplied energy goes into changing the molecular configuration, not the kinetic energy.

For a unit mass undergoing phase change:

$$
q_{latent} = h_2 - h_1
$$

Where $h_2$ and $h_1$ are the specific enthalpies of the two phases in equilibrium at the transition temperature.

Defining the latent heat $L$ as:

$$
L = h_2 - h_1
$$

Therefore, for total mass $m$:

$$
Q_{latent} = m \, L
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Heating Ice to Steam

**Problem:** Calculate the total heat required to convert **2 kg** of ice at **−10 °C** into steam at **120 °C** at standard atmospheric pressure.

**Given:**
- Mass, $m = 2 \text{ kg}$
- Initial temperature, $T_i = -10 \, °\text{C} = 263 \text{ K}$
- Final temperature, $T_f = 120 \, °\text{C} = 393 \text{ K}$
- $c_{ice} = 2.093 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- $L_f = 333.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $c_{water} = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- $L_v = 2260 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $c_{steam} = 2.010 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$

**Solution — Five Distinct Stages:**

**Stage A — Sensible Heating of Ice (from −10 °C to 0 °C):**

$$
Q_A = m \, c_{ice} \, \Delta T = 2 \times 2.093 \times (0 - (-10))
$$

$$
Q_A = 2 \times 2.093 \times 10 = 41.86 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Stage B — Melting (Phase Change at 0 °C):**

$$
Q_B = m \, L_f = 2 \times 333.55 = 667.10 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Stage C — Sensible Heating of Water (from 0 °C to 100 °C):**

$$
Q_C = m \, c_{water} \, \Delta T = 2 \times 4.186 \times (100 - 0)
$$

$$
Q_C = 2 \times 4.186 \times 100 = 837.20 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Stage D — Vaporization (Phase Change at 100 °C):**

$$
Q_D = m \, L_v = 2 \times 2260 = 4520.00 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Stage E — Sensible Heating of Steam (from 100 °C to 120 °C):**

$$
Q_E = m \, c_{steam} \, \Delta T = 2 \times 2.010 \times (120 - 100)
$$

$$
Q_E = 2 \times 2.010 \times 20 = 80.40 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Total Heat Required:**

$$
Q_{total} = Q_A + Q_B + Q_C + Q_D + Q_E
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 41.86 + 667.10 + 837.20 + 4520.00 + 80.40 = 6146.56 \text{ kJ}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observations:** Notice that the **latent heat of vaporization (4520 kJ)** alone accounts for **~73.5%** of the total energy demand. This is precisely why condensation-based heat recovery in steam systems is the single largest energy-saving opportunity in process industries.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Sensible Heat | Latent Heat |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature Change | Yes (ΔT ≠ 0) | No (ΔT = 0 during phase change) |
| Phase Change | No | Yes |
| Measurability | Detectable by thermometer | Hidden from thermometer |
| Magnitude (water example) | ~4.186 kJ per kg per K | ~2260 kJ per kg for vaporization |
| Molecular Effect | Increases kinetic energy | Changes potential energy (bonds) |
| Engineering Application | Preheating feedwater, cooling air | Steam generation, refrigeration, PCM storage |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Heat Addition Pathway — From Ice to Superheated Steam

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["ICE at -10 C"] -->|Q_A Sensible: m x c_ice x dT| nodeB["ICE at 0 C"]
    nodeB -->|Q_B Latent Fusion: m x L_f| nodeC["WATER at 0 C"]
    nodeC -->|Q_C Sensible: m x c_water x dT| nodeD["WATER at 100 C"]
    nodeD -->|Q_D Latent Vaporization: m x L_v| nodeE["SATURATED STEAM at 100 C"]
    nodeE -->|Q_E Sensible: m x c_steam x dT| nodeF["SUPERHEATED STEAM at 120 C"]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Sensible vs. Latent Heat in an HVAC System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph sensZone["SENSIBLE HEAT DOMAIN"]
        sensIn["Return Air at 28 C"] --> coilS["Cooling Coil Surface"]
        coilS --> sensOut["Supply Air at 14 C"]
    end
    subgraph latZone["LATENT HEAT DOMAIN"]
        latIn["Moisture in Return Air"] --> coilL["Dehumidification on Coil"]
        coilL --> latOut["Condensate Drain at 14 C"]
    end
    coilS -. shared coil .- coilL
    sensIn --> latIn
    sensOut --> latOut
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Energy Audit Application

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn["Fuel Input Q_in"] --> nodeBoil["Boiler"]
    nodeBoil -->|Sensible: Feedwater Heating| nodeHW["Hot Water at T1"]
    nodeBoil -->|Latent: Vaporization| nodeST["Steam at 100 C"]
    nodeBoil -->|Flue Gas Losses| nodeLoss["Stack Loss"]
    nodeHW --> nodeUse["Process Heating"]
    nodeST --> nodeUse
    nodeUse -->|Latent Recovery| nodeRec["Condensate Return"]
    nodeUse -->|Sensible Recovery| nodeRec
    nodeRec --> nodeBoil
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Arrows with the `-.->` (dotted) notation represent shared physical boundaries (e.g., the same coil handling both sensible and latent duties). Solid arrows represent sequential energy flow.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Distinguish between sensible heat and latent heat with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Sensible Heat | Latent Heat |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Heat causing temperature change | Heat causing phase change |
| Temperature Effect | Measurable change | No temperature change |
| Example | Heating water from 20 °C to 80 °C | Melting of ice at 0 °C |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition of each + 1 for example)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Why is the latent heat of vaporization of water greater than its latent heat of fusion?*

**Model Answer:**

The latent heat of vaporization is greater than the latent heat of fusion because:
1. During **fusion**, only the rigid crystalline lattice of ice is partially broken — molecules still remain close together in liquid water.
2. During **vaporization**, molecules must be **completely separated** from one another against cohesive forces, which requires significantly more energy.
3. For water specifically: $L_v = 2260 \text{ kJ/kg}$ vs. $L_f = 333.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

*(3 marks: 2 for explanation + 1 for numerical comparison)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern 2024 Scheme]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the expression for total heat required to convert a given mass of ice at a temperature below 0 °C into steam at a temperature above 100 °C. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Calculate the total heat required to convert **5 kg** of ice at **−15 °C** to steam at **150 °C**. Given: $c_{ice} = 2.1 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $L_f = 334 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $c_{water} = 4.18 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $L_v = 2260 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $c_{steam} = 2.01 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to Section 3.3 derivation. Total heat is the sum of three sensible stages and two latent stages:

$$
Q_{total} = m \left[ c_{ice} \, \Delta T_1 + L_f + c_{water} \, \Delta T_2 + L_v + c_{steam} \, \Delta T_3 \right]
$$

The derivation follows from integrating the specific enthalpy change $dh = c \, dT$ across sensible zones and applying $L = h_2 - h_1$ across phase boundaries. **(7 marks: 2 marks for stating each of the five stages + 2 marks for summation logic)**

**(b)** Step-by-step calculation:

**Stage A** — Ice from −15 °C to 0 °C:

$$
Q_A = 5 \times 2.1 \times 15 = 157.50 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Stage B** — Melting at 0 °C:

$$
Q_B = 5 \times 334 = 1670.00 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Stage C** — Water from 0 °C to 100 °C:

$$
Q_C = 5 \times 4.18 \times 100 = 2090.00 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Stage D** — Vaporization at 100 °C:

$$
Q_D = 5 \times 2260 = 11300.00 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Stage E** — Steam from 100 °C to 150 °C:

$$
Q_E = 5 \times 2.01 \times 50 = 502.50 \text{ kJ}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{total} = 157.50 + 1670 + 2090 + 11300 + 502.50 = 15720.00 \text{ kJ}}
$$

**(7 marks: 1 mark per stage + 2 marks for final summation)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the heating curve of water and identify the sensible and latent zones. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A **2 kg** aluminium vessel containing **5 kg** of water at **20 °C** is heated. The specific heat of aluminium is **0.9 kJ/(kg·K)**. Calculate the heat required to bring the water to boiling and then to convert **60%** of it into steam. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** A standard heating curve has five regions:

1. Sensible heating of ice (sloped line, ΔT ≠ 0).
2. Latent fusion plateau (horizontal line at 0 °C).
3. Sensible heating of water (sloped line).
4. Latent vaporization plateau (longest horizontal line at 100 °C).
5. Sensible heating of steam (sloped line).

**(7 marks: 3 marks for sketch + 2 marks for labeling + 2 marks for explanation)**

**(b)** Calculation:

**Stage 1** — Sensible heating of vessel + water from 20 °C to 100 °C:

Combined mass × specific heat × ΔT:

$$
Q_1 = \left[ (2 \times 0.9) + (5 \times 4.18) \right] \times (100 - 20)
$$

$$
Q_1 = [1.8 + 20.9] \times 80 = 22.7 \times 80 = 1816.00 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Stage 2** — Latent heat to vaporize 60% of water:

$$
m_{vaporized} = 0.6 \times 5 = 3 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
Q_2 = 3 \times 2260 = 6780.00 \text{ kJ}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{total} = 1816 + 6780 = 8596.00 \text{ kJ}}
$$

**(7 marks: 3 marks for Stage 1 logic + 2 marks for Stage 2 mass selection + 2 marks for final answer)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Sensible/Latent Heat Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the vessel/container mass** — Always check whether the problem specifies a container; its sensible heat must be added.
> 2. **Using °C instead of K for ΔT** — Numerically identical, but in theory must be Kelvin; KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark** if units are inconsistent in subsequent steps.
> 3. **Mixing specific heats** — $c_{ice} \neq c_{water} \neq c_{steam}$. A single value of 4.18 for everything will yield wrong answers.
> 4. **Applying $L_f$ or $L_v$ at wrong temperatures** — $L_f$ is defined at 0 °C, $L_v$ at 100 °C (1 atm). They change slightly at other pressures.
> 5. **Skipping intermediate stages** in a heating curve — Always show all five stages explicitly for full marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sensible heat** → temperature change only → $Q_s = m \, c \, \Delta T$.
- **Latent heat** → phase change only → $Q_L = m \, L$.
- **Latent heat is much larger in magnitude** than sensible heat for the same mass.
- For water: $c_{ice} = 2.093$, $c_{water} = 4.186$, $c_{steam} = 2.010$ kJ/(kg·K).
- For water: $L_f = 333.55$ kJ/kg at 0 °C; $L_v = 2260$ kJ/kg at 100 °C, 1 atm.
- **Five-stage heating curve**: ice sensible → fusion → water sensible → vaporization → steam sensible.
- In HVAC, total cooling load = **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)** × total load (sensible) + latent load.
- **Phase Change Materials (PCMs)** exploit latent heat for compact thermal energy storage.
- Always convert mass to **kg** and temperatures to **K (or °C for ΔT)** before substitution.
- In energy audits, **latent heat recovery from flue gas or process steam** is the highest-impact energy-saving intervention.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaporation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Evaporation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Evaporation** is the physical process by which molecules of a liquid (typically water, fuel, or refrigerant) transition from the liquid state to the gaseous/vapor state at temperatures **below their boiling point**, occurring exclusively at the free surface of the liquid.

In the context of **Energy Management and Conservation**, evaporation is critically important because every kilogram of liquid that evaporates absorbs a substantial quantity of energy from its surroundings in the form of **Latent Heat of Vaporization (h_fg)**. This represents an irreversible thermal loss from the system, which directly impacts the **First Law of Thermodynamics** energy balance of any industrial process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Evaporation is the surface phenomenon wherein molecules of a liquid, possessing sufficient kinetic energy to overcome intermolecular cohesive forces, escape from the liquid-vapor interface into the surrounding atmosphere, absorbing latent heat from the bulk liquid and reducing the system's thermal energy content."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Analogy — The Wet Clothes on a Clothesline:**
Imagine you hang wet clothes on a line on a hot, dry, breezy afternoon. Three observations:
1. The clothes dry faster (high temperature → high evaporation rate).
2. The clothes feel cold to touch (energy is being absorbed from the fabric as latent heat).
3. A strong wind makes them dry even faster (air velocity sweeps away vapor molecules, maintaining the concentration gradient).

This is **exactly** how evaporation works in:
- **Fuel storage tanks** (petrol/diesel loses mass due to evaporation)
- **Cooling ponds** (water evaporates and cools the remaining water)
- **Swimming pools** (significant thermal and mass losses)
- **Condensers and cooling towers** (heat rejection via evaporation)

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Latent Heat Values (Standard Atmospheric Pressure):**
> - Water: $h_{fg} = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$
> - Petrol/Gasoline: $h_{fg} \approx 350 \text{ kJ/kg}$
> - Diesel: $h_{fg} \approx 250 \text{ kJ/kg}$
> - Ammonia (NH₃): $h_{fg} = 1369 \text{ kJ/kg}$

The **Units of Evaporation Rate** are typically expressed in:
$$\text{kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{h)} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{mm/day (depth equivalent)}$$

### 1.4 Why Evaporation Matters in Energy Management

Evaporation causes three simultaneous losses that engineers must quantify:
1. **Mass Loss** — reduction in usable product (fuel, water, coolant).
2. **Thermal Loss** — extraction of latent heat from the bulk liquid, lowering its temperature.
3. **Environmental Loss** — release of vapors (VOCs) contributing to pollution and potential safety hazards.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Evaporation — Step-by-Step Logic

The evaporation process follows a sequential molecular and thermodynamic pathway:

- **Step 1 — Kinetic Energy Acquisition:** Liquid molecules near the surface continuously undergo random Brownian motion. Only those with kinetic energy exceeding the work function of surface tension escape the liquid surface.
- **Step 2 — Phase Transition (Energy Absorption):** Each escaping molecule carries away its latent heat of vaporization ($h_{fg}$). This energy is withdrawn from the remaining liquid (the liquid cools — **evaporative cooling**).
- **Step 3 — Vapor Diffusion:** The vapor molecules diffuse through the boundary layer of unsaturated air immediately above the liquid surface.
- **Step 4 — Convective Removal:** Air currents (natural or forced convection) carry the vapor away, preventing saturation and maintaining the concentration gradient.

### 2.2 The Driving Force — Partial Pressure Difference

Evaporation continues as long as the partial pressure of vapor at the liquid surface ($p_w$) **exceeds** the partial pressure of vapor in the surrounding air ($p_a$).

$$\Delta p = p_w - p_a \quad \text{(in kPa or mm of Hg)}$$

When $p_w = p_a$, the air is **saturated**, and net evaporation ceases (dynamic equilibrium).

### 2.3 Factors Affecting the Rate of Evaporation

The rate of evaporation depends on the following variables:

| # | Factor | Effect on Evaporation Rate |
|---|--------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Liquid temperature** ↑ | Increases $p_w$ exponentially → Higher rate |
| 2 | **Air temperature** ↑ | Reduces $\Delta p$ (as $p_a$ increases) → Lower rate (counter-intuitive but true) |
| 3 | **Humidity** ↑ | $p_a$ rises → Lower rate |
| 4 | **Wind speed (u)** ↑ | Sweeps saturated air → Higher rate |
| 5 | **Surface area (A)** ↑ | Proportionally higher rate |
| 6 | **Atmospheric pressure** ↓ | Increases volatility → Higher rate (high altitudes) |
| 7 | **Nature of liquid** | Lower $h_{fg}$ & lower surface tension → Higher rate |
| 8 | **Depth of liquid** | Very deep → negligible effect; shallow → bottom heating helps |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Evaporation analysis is critical in:
- **Petroleum refineries** — calculating breathing and standing losses in storage tanks.
- **Power plants** — sizing cooling ponds and cooling towers.
- **HVAC design** — selecting adiabatic cooling systems.
- **Agriculture** — irrigation reservoir design and water budgeting.
- **Chemical engineering** — design of evaporators (crystallizers, concentrating pans).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU University Exams**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | Dalton's Law of Evaporation | $m_e = C \cdot A \cdot (p_w - p_a)$ | $m_e$: mass evaporated (kg/h); $C$: empirical coefficient; $A$: surface area (m²); $p_w, p_a$: vapor & air partial pressure (mm Hg) |
| 2 | Meyer's Equation (with wind correction) | $m_e = C \cdot A \cdot (p_w - p_a) \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{u}{9}\right)$ | $u$: wind speed in km/h; constant 9 is empirical |
| 3 | Energy Lost in Evaporation | $Q_e = m_e \cdot h_{fg}$ | $Q_e$: heat lost (kJ/h); $h_{fg}$: latent heat (kJ/kg) |
| 4 | Equivalent Cooling Effect | $\Delta T = \dfrac{Q_e}{m_{\text{bulk}} \cdot c_p}$ | $m_{\text{bulk}}$: bulk liquid mass; $c_p$: specific heat |
| 5 | Evaporation Depth Equivalent | $d_e = \dfrac{m_e}{\rho_w \cdot A}$ | $d_e$: depth lost per unit time (mm/day); $\rho_w$: water density |
| 6 | Annual Evaporation Loss (Tank) | $L = \dfrac{V \cdot \rho}{1000} \cdot \left(\dfrac{p_w - p_a}}{p_{atm} - p_w}\right)$ | $L$: volumetric loss; $V$: tank volume |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The **Meyer's Equation** is the MOST FREQUENTLY asked formula. Always remember the constant $\frac{u}{9}$ where $u$ is in **km/h**.

### 2.6 The 'Why' Behind Dalton's Law

John Dalton (1802) observed that the rate of evaporation is directly proportional to the **vapor pressure deficit** $(p_w - p_a)$, the **exposed surface area** $A$, and an empirical constant $C$ that absorbs molecular properties, turbulence, and unit conversions. This is essentially a **mass transfer equation** analogous to Fourier's law for heat conduction, where the driving force is a concentration gradient rather than a temperature gradient.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Dalton's Law of Evaporation

**Starting Premise:**
The mass evaporation rate $\dot{m}_e$ is proportional to the partial pressure deficit $(p_w - p_a)$ and the surface area $A$.

$$\dot{m}_e \propto A \cdot (p_w - p_a)$$

**Step 1 — Introduce Proportionality Constant:**

$$\dot{m}_e = C \cdot A \cdot (p_w - p_a)$$

where $C$ is the empirical evaporation coefficient with units of $\text{kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{h} \cdot \text{mm Hg)}$.

**Step 2 — Incorporating Wind Correction (Meyer's Modification):**

Dalton's original equation did not account for wind. Meyer's experiments showed that for every km/h of wind speed, the evaporation rate increases by approximately $\frac{1}{9}$ of its still-air value. Therefore:

$$\dot{m}_e = C \cdot A \cdot (p_w - p_a) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{u}{9}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Multi-line Derivation of Energy Loss:**

Once mass evaporation rate is known, the rate of heat (energy) lost by the liquid can be computed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_e &= \dot{m}_e \cdot h_{fg} \\[6pt]
\dot{Q}_e &= C \cdot A \cdot (p_w - p_a) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{u}{9}\right) \cdot h_{fg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Derivation of Equivalent Temperature Drop:**

If the bulk liquid has mass $m_{\text{bulk}}$ and specific heat $c_p$, the equivalent cooling experienced by the bulk is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_e &= m_{\text{bulk}} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \\[6pt]
\Delta T &= \frac{\dot{Q}_e}{m_{\text{bulk}} \cdot c_p} \\[6pt]
\Delta T &= \frac{C \cdot A \cdot (p_w - p_a) \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{u}{9}\right) \cdot h_{fg}}{m_{\text{bulk}} \cdot c_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> An open water tank has a surface area of $A = 10 \text{ m}^2$. The water surface temperature is $30 \text{ °C}$ with corresponding saturation vapor pressure $p_w = 31.8 \text{ mm Hg}$. The surrounding air is at $25 \text{ °C}$ with relative humidity $60\%$, giving $p_a = 12.8 \text{ mm Hg}$. The wind speed is $u = 18 \text{ km/h}$. The empirical coefficient is $C = 0.0185 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{h} \cdot \text{mm Hg)}$. Compute: (a) the hourly mass evaporation rate, (b) the hourly energy loss, and (c) the equivalent cooling per hour for 50,000 kg of bulk water.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Mass evaporation rate using Meyer's Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_e &= C \cdot A \cdot (p_w - p_a) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{u}{9}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 0.0185 \cdot 10 \cdot (31.8 - 12.8) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{18}{9}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 0.0185 \cdot 10 \cdot 19.0 \cdot (1 + 2.0) \\[6pt]
&= 0.0185 \cdot 10 \cdot 19.0 \cdot 3.0 \\[6pt]
&= 10.545 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Energy loss per hour:**

Using $h_{fg}$ at $30 \text{ °C} \approx 2430 \text{ kJ/kg}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_e &= \dot{m}_e \cdot h_{fg} \\[6pt]
&= 10.545 \cdot 2430 \\[6pt]
&= 25{,}624.35 \text{ kJ/h} \\[6pt]
&\approx 7.118 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (c) — Equivalent temperature drop:**

Using $c_p = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ for water:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= \frac{\dot{Q}_e}{m_{\text{bulk}} \cdot c_p} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{25{,}624.35}{50{,}000 \cdot 4.186} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{25{,}624.35}{209{,}300} \\[6pt]
&= 0.1224 \text{ °C per hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Patterns

| Problem Pattern | Key Formula Used | Typical Twist |
|-----------------|------------------|---------------|
| Cooling pond sizing | Meyer's Equation | Subtract rainfall, add runoff |
| Fuel tank standing loss | $L = V \cdot \rho \cdot \left(\dfrac{p_w - p_a}{p_{atm} - p_w}\right)$ | Daily vs. annual loss |
| Cooling tower makeup water | $\dot{m}_{\text{makeup}} = \dot{m}_{\text{evap}} + \dot{m}_{\text{drift}} + \dot{m}_{\text{blowdown}}$ | Cycles of concentration |
| Refrigerant loss | $\dot{m}_e = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_e}{h_{fg}}$ | Convert kW to kg/s |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Block Diagram — Evaporation Mechanism in a Fuel Storage Tank

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Liquid Bulk in Storage Tank] --> B[Liquid Vapor Interface at Free Surface]
    B --> C{Molecules with KE > Surface Energy}
    C -->|Escape| D[Vapor Phase Above Surface]
    C -->|Return| A
    D --> E[Boundary Layer of Unsaturated Air]
    E --> F{Wind or Convection Present?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Sweep Away Saturated Air]
    F -->|No| H[Local Saturation Equilibrium Reached]
    G --> I[Fresh Dry Air Replaces Saturated Layer]
    H --> J[Net Evaporation Ceases]
    I --> K[Continued Mass Transfer from Liquid]
    K --> L[Mass Loss + Latent Heat Withdrawal]
    L --> M[Reduction in Liquid Temperature]
    L --> N[Reduction in Liquid Mass]

    style A fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    style D fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    style L fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    style M fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    style N fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Evaporation Loss Control Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Evaporation Loss Sources in Industry] --> S1[Tank Breathing Losses]
    R --> S2[Tank Standing Losses]
    R --> S3[Process Evaporation in Cooling]
    R --> S4[Uncovered Reservoir Losses]

    S1 --> M1a[Pressure Relief Valves Venting Vapors]
    S1 --> M1b[Use of Vapor Recovery Units VRU]

    S2 --> M2a[Fixed Roof with Insulation]
    S2 --> M2b[Internal Floating Roof]
    S2 --> M2c[External Floating Roof]
    S2 --> M2d[Pressure Tanks Sealed Systems]

    S3 --> M3a[Covered Cooling Towers]
    S3 --> M3b[Use of Drift Eliminators]
    S3 --> M3c[Lower Approach Temperature]

    S4 --> M4a[Floating Plastic Ball Covers]
    S4 --> M4b[Shade Nets to Reduce Solar Gain]
    S4 --> M4c[Wind Breaks Around Reservoir]
    S4 --> M4d[Chemical Monolayer Films]

    M1b --> OUT[Reduced HC Emission + Energy Saved]
    M2c --> OUT
    M3b --> OUT
    M4a --> OUT

    style R fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000
    style OUT fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Flow — Driving Force Behind Evaporation

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Saturation Vapor Pressure at Liquid Surface: pw] -->|Subtracted| DIFF
    P2[Actual Vapor Pressure in Ambient Air: pa] -->|Subtracted| DIFF
    DIFF{Is pw greater than pa?}
    DIFF -->|Yes, net driving force exists| FLOW[Evaporation Proceeds]
    DIFF -->|No, equilibrium or supersaturated| STOP[No Net Evaporation]
    FLOW --> Q[Rate = C times A times pw minus pa times wind factor]

    style P1 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    style FLOW fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    style STOP fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define evaporation. How is it different from boiling?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Evaporation is a surface phenomenon occurring at temperatures **below the boiling point** of the liquid, where molecules escape from the free liquid surface into the surrounding atmosphere. Boiling, in contrast, is a **bulk phenomenon** occurring throughout the liquid mass at a fixed temperature (the saturation temperature corresponding to the prevailing pressure). Evaporation is slow, diffusion-controlled, and produces cooling of the remaining liquid; boiling is rapid, bubble-driven, and occurs at constant temperature. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**State Dalton's law of evaporation and explain the significance of the term $(p_w - p_a)$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Dalton's law states that the rate of evaporation is directly proportional to the difference between the vapor pressure at the liquid surface ($p_w$) and the partial pressure of vapor in the surrounding air ($p_a$), multiplied by the surface area and an empirical constant. The term $(p_w - p_a)$ is the **vapor pressure deficit** — it represents the thermodynamic driving force for mass transfer. If this difference is zero (air saturated), no net evaporation occurs. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive Dalton's law of evaporation and modify it using Meyer's correction for wind speed. Explain the physical significance of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An open cooling pond of surface area $A = 50 \text{ m}^2$ contains water at $35 \text{ °C}$. The vapor pressure at this temperature is $p_w = 42.2 \text{ mm Hg}$ and the partial pressure of vapor in the surrounding air at $30 \text{ °C}$, $70\%$ RH is $p_a = 21.1 \text{ mm Hg}$. Wind speed is $u = 27 \text{ km/h}$. If $C = 0.022 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{h} \cdot \text{mm Hg)}$ and $h_{fg} = 2410 \text{ kJ/kg}$, compute:
- (i) The hourly evaporation rate in kg/h.
- (ii) The total energy lost per hour.
- (iii) The equivalent cooling in kW. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Answer for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of Dalton's Law (7 marks):**

**Statement:** The mass rate of evaporation $\dot{m}_e$ from a liquid surface is directly proportional to (i) the surface area $A$, and (ii) the vapor pressure deficit $(p_w - p_a)$.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$\dot{m}_e = C \cdot A \cdot (p_w - p_a)$$

where $C$ = empirical coefficient (depends on liquid properties, turbulence, units).

**Meyer's Wind Correction:**
Meyer found experimentally that evaporation rate increases by $\frac{1}{9}$ of its still-air value for every km/h increase in wind speed. Therefore:

$$\dot{m}_e = C \cdot A \cdot (p_w - p_a) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{u}{9}\right)$$

**Significance of each term:**
- $C$: lumped mass transfer coefficient.
- $A$: exposed free surface area (m²).
- $(p_w - p_a)$: driving force (vapor pressure deficit, mm Hg).
- $\left(1 + \frac{u}{9}\right)$: wind amplification factor (dimensionless), $u$ in km/h.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Hourly evaporation rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_e &= C \cdot A \cdot (p_w - p_a) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{u}{9}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 0.022 \cdot 50 \cdot (42.2 - 21.1) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{27}{9}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 0.022 \cdot 50 \cdot 21.1 \cdot (1 + 3.0) \\[6pt]
&= 0.022 \cdot 50 \cdot 21.1 \cdot 4.0 \\[6pt]
&= 92.84 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total energy lost per hour:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_e &= \dot{m}_e \cdot h_{fg} \\[6pt]
&= 92.84 \cdot 2410 \\[6pt]
&= 223{,}744.4 \text{ kJ/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Equivalent cooling in kW:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_e \text{ in kW} &= \frac{223{,}744.4}{3600} \\[6pt]
&\approx 62.15 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the various factors affecting the rate of evaporation from a liquid surface. Discuss how each factor alters the vapor pressure deficit $(p_w - p_a)$ or the mass transfer coefficient. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1000 m³ fuel storage tank contains gasoline with density $\rho = 750 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at an average temperature of $25 \text{ °C}$. The vapor pressure of gasoline at this temperature is $p_w = 200 \text{ mm Hg}$, ambient atmospheric pressure is $p_{atm} = 760 \text{ mm Hg}$, and the partial pressure of gasoline vapor in the air space of the tank is $p_a = 50 \text{ mm Hg}$. Estimate:
- (i) The fractional mass lost per day due to evaporation.
- (ii) The annual fuel loss in kg (assume 365 days).
- (iii) The economic loss if fuel costs ₹100/kg. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Answer for Question B:**

**(a) Factors affecting evaporation rate (7 marks):**

1. **Liquid Surface Temperature ($T_s$):** Increases $p_w$ exponentially (Clausius-Clapeyron), thus enlarging $(p_w - p_a)$ → increases $\dot{m}_e$.
2. **Air Temperature ($T_a$):** Higher $T_a$ → higher $p_a$ at the same RH → reduces driving force → decreases $\dot{m}_e$.
3. **Relative Humidity:** High RH → high $p_a$ → reduced deficit → slower evaporation.
4. **Wind Speed ($u$):** Enhances mass transfer coefficient by sweeping away saturated boundary layer → per Meyer's equation, rate increases by factor $(1 + u/9)$.
5. **Surface Area ($A$):** Direct proportionality — doubling area doubles evaporation rate.
6. **Atmospheric Pressure:** At higher altitudes, lower $p_{atm}$ means molecules escape more easily.
7. **Nature of Liquid:** Liquids with low $h_{fg}$ (e.g., petrol) evaporate faster than those with high $h_{fg}$ (e.g., water).
8. **Surface Tension:** Lower surface tension → easier molecular escape.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

The fractional evaporation loss formula for a closed tank is:

$$\frac{\Delta m}{m} = \frac{p_w - p_a}{p_{atm} - p_w}$$

**(i) Fractional mass lost per day:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta m}{m} &= \frac{p_w - p_a}{p_{atm} - p_w} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{200 - 50}{760 - 200} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{150}{560} \\[6pt]
&= 0.2679 \text{ (or } 26.79\%\text{ per day)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total initial mass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= V \cdot \rho \\[6pt]
&= 1000 \cdot 750 = 750{,}000 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Daily mass loss:**

$$
\Delta m_{\text{day}} = 0.2679 \cdot 750{,}000 = 200{,}925 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**(ii) Annual loss:**

$$
\Delta m_{\text{annual}} = 200{,}925 \cdot 365 = 73{,}337{,}625 \text{ kg/year}
$$

**(iii) Economic loss:**

$$
\text{Loss} = 73{,}337{,}625 \cdot ₹100 = ₹7{,}333{,}762{,}500 \approx ₹733.4 \text{ Crores/year}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** The wind speed $u$ in Meyer's equation MUST be in **km/h** (not m/s). Converting wrong → answer is off by a factor of 3.6.
> 2. **Partial Pressure vs Saturation Pressure:** $p_a$ must be the **actual** partial pressure of vapor in ambient air (RH × $p_{sat}$ at air temp), NOT the saturation pressure.
> 3. **Sign Convention:** $(p_w - p_a)$ must be **positive** — otherwise evaporation is zero or condensation occurs.
> 4. **Forgetting the wind correction factor:** Even when $u = 0$, the expression becomes 1, NOT 0. Many students incorrectly write 0.
> 5. **Time Unit Consistency:** If $C$ is in per-hour units, the resulting $\dot{m}_e$ is in **kg/h**, not kg/s. Convert using $\div 3600$ when needed.
> 6. **Forgetting to multiply by $h_{fg}$:** Energy loss requires multiplying mass loss by latent heat — a common step missed by students.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Evaporation is a SURFACE phenomenon** below boiling point; **boiling is a BULK phenomenon** at saturation temperature.
- [ ] **Dalton's Law:** $\dot{m}_e = C \cdot A \cdot (p_w - p_a)$
- [ ] **Meyer's Equation (Wind Correction):** $\dot{m}_e = C \cdot A \cdot (p_w - p_a) \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{u}{9}\right)$ — **$u$ is in km/h**.
- [ ] **Driving force = vapor pressure deficit** $(p_w - p_a)$. Zero deficit ⇒ no net evaporation.
- [ ] **Energy lost per unit mass** = latent heat of vaporization $h_{fg}$.
- [ ] Water's $h_{fg} = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (at 100 °C); varies with temperature.
- [ ] **Higher temperature** of liquid ⇒ higher $p_w$ ⇒ higher rate.
- [ ] **Higher humidity** of air ⇒ higher $p_a$ ⇒ lower rate.
- [ ] **Wind speed increases** rate by sweeping away saturated boundary layer.
- [ ] **Surface area is linearly proportional** to evaporation rate.
- [ ] **Evaporation causes cooling** — the basis of cooling towers, sweat, evaporative coolers.
- [ ] For **fuel storage tanks**, evaporation loss = standing loss + breathing loss.
- [ ] **Conservation methods:** floating roofs, vapor recovery units, pressure tanks, covers, shade nets, wind breaks.
- [ ] Convert $\text{kJ/h} \to \text{kW}$ by dividing by **3600**.
- [ ] **Partial pressure** $p_a = \text{Relative Humidity} \times p_{\text{sat}}(T_{\text{air}})$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Condensation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Condensation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Condensation** is the phase-change heat transfer process in which a vapor (commonly steam or a refrigerant) is converted into its liquid state by the removal of its latent heat of vaporization ($h_{fg}$) to a relatively cooler surface. It is one of the two principal modes of **phase-change heat transfer**, the other being boiling. In the context of **Energy Management and Conservation**, condensation plays a pivotal role in the **rejection of low-grade waste heat** to the environment, in **thermal power plant condensers**, in **HVAC & refrigeration cycles**, and in **industrial process heat recovery systems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *Condensation is the process of conversion of vapor into liquid by extracting its latent heat, accompanied by a release of energy equal to the enthalpy of vaporization at a constant saturation temperature and pressure.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cold Drink on a Humid Day"

Imagine pulling a chilled bottle of water out of your refrigerator on a hot, humid Kerala afternoon. Within seconds, the outer surface of the bottle becomes covered with water droplets. What happened?
