- The surrounding warm, water-laden air (vapor) came in contact with the cold glass surface.
- Because the glass temperature ($T_s$) was below the **dew-point temperature** of the vapor ($T_{sat}$), the vapor had to give up its **latent heat** in order to become liquid.
- The temperature of the surrounding air dropped slightly, the vapor condensed, and droplets formed.

This everyday phenomenon is **condensation** in action. The bottle surface is the **cold surface**, and the humid air is the **vapor** losing energy.

### 1.3 Types of Condensation

The manner in which a vapor condenses on a cooled surface fundamentally determines the rate of heat transfer. There are two principal mechanisms:

| S.No. | Type | Mechanism | Heat Transfer Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Filmwise Condensation** | Liquid forms a continuous, unbroken film that flows down the surface under gravity. | Lower ($h \approx 5\,000$–$15\,000\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$) |
| 2 | **Dropwise Condensation** | Liquid forms discrete, dispersed droplets that grow, coalesce, and roll off, exposing fresh surface. | Higher ($h$ can be 5–10$\times$ filmwise, up to $\sim 100\,000\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Energy Managers Care:**
> In a steam power plant, the **condenser** is where ~30–40 % of the input energy is rejected. Even a 1 % improvement in the condensation heat transfer coefficient can translate to significant fuel savings and lower $\mathrm{CO_2}$ emissions.

### 1.4 Conditions Necessary for Condensation

For condensation to occur, the following thermodynamic and surface conditions must be simultaneously satisfied:

1. **Surface sub-cooling:** $T_{sat} - T_s > 0$ (the surface temperature must be below the saturation temperature of the vapor).
2. **Non-wetting tendency:** The contact angle between the condensate and the surface must be favourable.
3. **Presence of a nucleation site:** Microscopic surface imperfections initiate droplet formation in dropwise condensation.
4. **Removal of non-condensable gases:** Even trace amounts of air ($<1\%$) can drastically degrade heat transfer.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Key constants for numerical problems (memorize for KTU ESE):
> - **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$
> - **Stefan–Boltzmann constant** *(not directly used here but for context):* $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \mathrm{W/m^2K^4}$
> - **Latent heat of steam at 100 °C:** $h_{fg} \approx 2\,257\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}$
> - **Modified latent heat** $h_{fg}^{\prime} = h_{fg} + 0.68\,c_p (T_{sat} - T_s)$ accounts for sub-cooled condensate.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Filmwise Condensation — Nusselt's Classical Theory (1916)

In **filmwise condensation**, a continuous liquid film blankets the condensing surface. The thickness of the film grows in the direction of flow due to the accumulation of condensate. Heat must be transferred by **conduction** through this growing film, which is the primary thermal resistance. The velocity and temperature profiles within the film must be solved simultaneously.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Physical Reasoning

1. Vapor at $T_{sat}$ approaches the cooled surface.
2. Upon contact, vapor releases latent heat $h_{fg}$ and condenses.
3. The condensate forms a thin laminar film that flows downward under gravity.
4. The film thickness $\delta(x)$ increases with distance $x$ from the leading edge.
5. Heat transfer rate at any $x$: $q_x \propto \dfrac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}{\delta(x)}$.
6. Mass flow rate increases: $\dot{m}(x) \propto x$.
7. Solving the coupled **momentum (Navier–Stokes)** and **energy (Fourier)** equations gives the famous **Nusselt Result**.

### 2.3 Nusselt's Governing Assumptions (Mandatory for KTU Derivations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always state these assumptions in your 14-mark answers. Examiners allocate 2 marks purely for assumption listing.

1. Steady, laminar flow of the condensate film.
2. Constant fluid properties ($\rho, \mu, k_l$) evaluated at film mean temperature.
3. The vapor is stationary and at uniform $T_{sat}$ (no shear at liquid–vapor interface).
4. **Negligible inertia** of the condensate (boundary-layer approximation).
5. Heat transfer across the film is by **pure conduction** (no convection in the thin film).
6. Gravity-driven flow; body force per unit volume = $\rho_l g$.
7. No superheating; vapor is saturated.
8. Sub-cooled liquid effects incorporated through **modified latent heat** $h_{fg}^{\prime}$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — "The Cheat Sheet"

> [!NOTE]
> All variables listed below are defined in the unified notation used throughout the derivation in Section 3.

| # | Configuration | Average Heat Transfer Coefficient $\bar{h}$ | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vertical plate (laminar) | $\bar{h} = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{k_l^{\,3}\, \rho_l^{\,2}\, g\, h_{fg}^{\prime}}{\mu_l\, L\, (T_{sat} - T_s)} \right]^{1/4}$ | $\mathrm{W/m^2 K}$ |
| 2 | Vertical plate (Reynolds check) | $Re_\delta = \dfrac{4\,\dot{m}}{\mu_l\, b} < 1800$ (laminar limit) | dimensionless |
| 3 | Inclined plate (angle $\theta$ from vertical) | Replace $g$ with $g \cos\theta$ in the vertical formula | $\mathrm{W/m^2 K}$ |
| 4 | Horizontal tube (single) | $\bar{h} = 0.725 \left[ \dfrac{k_l^{\,3}\, \rho_l^{\,2}\, g\, h_{fg}^{\prime}}{\mu_l\, D\, (T_{sat} - T_s)} \right]^{1/4}$ | $\mathrm{W/m^2 K}$ |
| 5 | Vertical tier of $N$ horizontal tubes | $\bar{h}_N = \bar{h}_1 \, N^{-1/4}$ | $\mathrm{W/m^2 K}$ |
| 6 | Modified latent heat | $h_{fg}^{\prime} = h_{fg} + 0.68\, c_{p,l}\,(T_{sat} - T_s)$ | $\mathrm{J/kg}$ |
| 7 | Average condensate mass flow | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\bar{h}\, A\, (T_{sat} - T_s)}{h_{fg}^{\prime}}$ | $\mathrm{kg/s}$ |
| 8 | Total heat transfer rate | $Q = \bar{h}\, A\, (T_{sat} - T_s)$ | $\mathrm{W}$ |
| 9 | Film thickness at distance $x$ | $\delta(x) = \left[ \dfrac{4\,\mu_l\, k_l\, (T_{sat}-T_s)\, x}{g\, \rho_l^{\,2}\, h_{fg}^{\prime}} \right]^{1/4}$ | $\mathrm{m}$ |
| 10 | Wavy / Turbulent transition | For $Re > 1800$, use Labuntsov correlation (advanced, beyond KTU scope) | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Always evaluate properties ($k_l, \rho_l, \mu_l, c_{p,l}$) at the **film temperature** $T_f = \dfrac{T_{sat} + T_s}{2}$, not at $T_{sat}$ or $T_s$ alone. Examiners frequently test this.

### 2.5 Dropwise Condensation — Why It Is Better

In dropwise condensation, droplets nucleate at discrete sites, grow, and roll away due to gravity, continually exposing fresh, dry surface to fresh vapor. This **interrupts the thermal resistance layer**, leading to:

- Heat transfer coefficients up to **10×** higher than filmwise.
- However, it is **difficult to sustain** in industrial systems because:
  - Surface wettability changes with time (oxidation, fouling).
  - Promoters (e.g., oleic acid, PTFE coatings) degrade.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy-Conservation Linkage:**
> Replacing filmwise with sustained dropwise condensation in a steam condenser of a 500 MW plant could save up to **2 %** in fuel consumption — a massive commercial driver for ongoing research in surface engineering.

### 2.6 Effect of Non-Condensable Gases

Even **1 % by volume** of air in steam can:
- Reduce condensation rate by **30–50 %**.
- Form a **vapor-side mass-transfer resistance** (diffusion barrier).
- Cause localized temperature variations on the condensing surface.

This is why power plant condensers use **air-bleed ejectors** to continuously purge non-condensables.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Power Plant Surface Condenser** — rejects latent heat of exhaust steam from LP turbine.
2. **Refrigeration & AC** — condenser coil where R-134a / R-410A releases heat to ambient.
3. **Distillation Columns** — overhead vapor condensed in reflux condensers.
4. **Desalination Plants** — multi-effect distillation (MED) relies on efficient condensation.
5. **Waste Heat Recovery Boilers** — flue-gas vapor condensation extracts latent heat from exhaust gases.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Nusselt's Equation for Film Condensation on a Vertical Plate

**System Description:**
Consider a vertical plate of width $b$ and height $L$, maintained at a uniform temperature $T_s$. Saturated vapor at temperature $T_{sat}$ condenses on it. The condensate film flows downward under gravity.

---

**Step 1 — Coordinate System & Control Volume**
Choose $x$ as the vertical distance measured from the top edge of the plate, and $y$ as the direction normal to the plate, measured from the plate surface into the film.

---

**Step 2 — Mass Balance on Differential Element**
Consider a control volume of the film of width $b$, thickness $\delta(x)$, and infinitesimal height $dx$. Let $\dot{m}(x)$ be the local mass flow rate of condensate at position $x$.

$$
\dot{m}(x) = \rho_l \, b \, \bar{u}(x) \, \delta(x)
$$

where $\bar{u}(x)$ is the mean velocity of the condensate at position $x$.

The increase in mass flow rate across $dx$ equals the condensation rate over the differential area $b\,dx$:

$$
d\dot{m} = \dfrac{q_x^{\prime\prime}\, b\, dx}{h_{fg}^{\prime}} = \dfrac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_s)\, b\, dx}{\delta(x)\, h_{fg}^{\prime}}
$$

---

**Step 3 — Velocity Profile from Momentum Equation**
Applying the Navier–Stokes equation in the $x$-direction with the **boundary-layer approximation** (neglecting inertia, since the film is thin and velocities are low):

$$
\mu_l\, \dfrac{d^2 u}{d y^2} + \rho_l\, g = 0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\dfrac{d^2 u}{d y^2} = -\,\dfrac{\rho_l\, g}{\mu_l}
$$

Integrating once with respect to $y$:

$$
\dfrac{d u}{d y} = -\,\dfrac{\rho_l\, g}{\mu_l}\, y + C_1
$$

**Boundary Condition 1:** At $y = 0$ (plate surface), no slip: $u(0) = 0$. This gives $C_1 = 0$.

Integrating again:

$$
u(y) = -\,\dfrac{\rho_l\, g}{2\, \mu_l}\, y^2 + C_2
$$

**Boundary Condition 2:** At the free surface $y = \delta(x)$, the shear stress is zero (vapor is stagnant): $\left. \dfrac{du}{dy} \right|_{y=\delta} = 0$.

This is automatically satisfied. To find $C_2$, we apply a **mean velocity** definition. However, classical Nusselt instead uses the **volume flow rate** directly:

$$
\dot{V}(x) = \int_0^{\delta} u(y)\, b\, dy
$$

After double integration of the parabolic profile:

$$
\dot{V}(x) = \dfrac{b\, \rho_l\, g\, \delta^3(x)}{3\, \mu_l}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dot{m}(x) = \rho_l\, \dot{V}(x) = \dfrac{b\, \rho_l^{\,2}\, g\, \delta^3(x)}{3\, \mu_l}
$$

---

**Step 4 — Equating the Two Expressions for $\dot{m}(x)$**
From Step 2 (mass balance via energy):

$$
d\dot{m} = \dfrac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_s)\, b\, dx}{\delta(x)\, h_{fg}^{\prime}}
$$

From Step 3 (momentum-derived mass flow):

$$
d\dot{m} = d\left[ \dfrac{b\, \rho_l^{\,2}\, g\, \delta^3}{3\, \mu_l} \right] = \dfrac{b\, \rho_l^{\,2}\, g}{\mu_l}\, \delta^2\, d\delta
$$

Equating:

$$
\dfrac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_s)\, b}{\delta\, h_{fg}^{\prime}}\, dx = \dfrac{b\, \rho_l^{\,2}\, g}{\mu_l}\, \delta^2\, d\delta
$$

---

**Step 5 — Separation of Variables and Integration**
Rearranging:

$$
\delta^3\, d\delta = \dfrac{\mu_l\, k_l\, (T_{sat} - T_s)}{\rho_l^{\,2}\, g\, h_{fg}^{\prime}}\, dx
$$

Integrating both sides from $0$ to $\delta(x)$ on the left and $0$ to $x$ on the right (with $\delta(0) = 0$ at the leading edge):

$$
\int_0^{\delta} \delta^3\, d\delta = \dfrac{\mu_l\, k_l\, (T_{sat} - T_s)}{\rho_l^{\,2}\, g\, h_{fg}^{\prime}} \int_0^{x} dx
$$

$$
\dfrac{\delta^4(x)}{4} = \dfrac{\mu_l\, k_l\, (T_{sat} - T_s)\, x}{\rho_l^{\,2}\, g\, h_{fg}^{\prime}}
$$

---

**Step 6 — Local Film Thickness $\delta(x)$**

$$
\delta(x) = \left[ \dfrac{4\, \mu_l\, k_l\, (T_{sat} - T_s)\, x}{\rho_l^{\,2}\, g\, h_{fg}^{\prime}} \right]^{1/4}
$$

---

**Step 7 — Local Heat Transfer Coefficient $h_x$**

$$
h_x = \dfrac{k_l}{\delta(x)} = \left[ \dfrac{\rho_l^{\,2}\, g\, h_{fg}^{\prime}\, k_l^{\,3}}{4\, \mu_l\, (T_{sat} - T_s)\, x} \right]^{1/4}
$$

---

**Step 8 — Average Heat Transfer Coefficient $\bar{h}$**

$$
\bar{h} = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_0^{L} h_x\, dx = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_0^{L} \dfrac{k_l}{\delta(x)}\, dx
$$

Using $\delta \propto x^{1/4}$, we get $h_x \propto x^{-1/4}$, and $\int_0^L x^{-1/4} dx = \dfrac{4}{3} L^{3/4}$.

$$
\bar{h} = \dfrac{4}{3}\, h_x(L)
$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$
\bar{h} = \dfrac{4}{3} \left[ \dfrac{\rho_l^{\,2}\, g\, h_{fg}^{\prime}\, k_l^{\,3}}{4\, \mu_l\, L\, (T_{sat} - T_s)} \right]^{1/4}
$$

---

**Step 9 — The Famous Nusselt Result**

$$
\boxed{\bar{h} = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{k_l^{\,3}\, \rho_l^{\,2}\, g\, h_{fg}^{\prime}}{\mu_l\, L\, (T_{sat} - T_s)} \right]^{1/4}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Numerical Illustration — Worked Example for Practice

**Problem (KTU-pattern):** Saturated steam at atmospheric pressure ($T_{sat} = 100\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$) condenses on a vertical plate of height $L = 0.3\ \mathrm{m}$ maintained at $T_s = 80\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. Determine the average heat transfer coefficient and the total heat transfer rate per unit width.

**Properties of water (evaluated at film temperature $T_f = 90\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$):**

- $\rho_l = 965.3\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$
- $\mu_l = 3.15 \times 10^{-4}\ \mathrm{kg/ms}$
- $k_l = 0.675\ \mathrm{W/mK}$
- $h_{fg} = 2\,257\,000\ \mathrm{J/kg}$
- $c_{p,l} = 4\,205\ \mathrm{J/kgK}$

**Step 1 — Modified Latent Heat**

$$
h_{fg}^{\prime} = h_{fg} + 0.68\, c_{p,l}\,(T_{sat} - T_s)
$$

$$
h_{fg}^{\prime} = 2\,257\,000 + 0.68 \times 4\,205 \times (100 - 80)
$$

$$
h_{fg}^{\prime} = 2\,257\,000 + 57\,188 = 2\,314\,188\ \mathrm{J/kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Nusselt Equation**

$$
\bar{h} = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{(0.675)^3 \times (965.3)^2 \times 9.81 \times 2\,314\,188}{3.15 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.3 \times 20} \right]^{1/4}
$$

Numerator terms:

- $(0.675)^3 = 0.3075$
- $(965.3)^2 = 931\,804$
- $9.81 \times 2\,314\,188 = 2.2702 \times 10^7$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 0.3075 \times 931\,804 \times 2.2702 \times 10^7 = 6.505 \times 10^{12}
$$

Denominator:

$$
3.15 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.3 \times 20 = 1.89 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Quotient:

$$
\dfrac{6.505 \times 10^{12}}{1.89 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.442 \times 10^{15}
$$

Fourth root:

$$
(3.442 \times 10^{15})^{1/4} = (3.442)^{1/4} \times (10^{15})^{1/4} \approx 1.362 \times 5.623 \times 10^{3} \approx 7\,659
$$

Therefore:

$$
\bar{h} = 0.943 \times 7\,659 \approx 7\,222\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat Transfer Rate per Unit Width**

$$
Q^{\prime} = \bar{h}\, L\, (T_{sat} - T_s) = 7\,222 \times 0.3 \times 20 = 43\,332\ \mathrm{W/m} \approx 43.3\ \mathrm{kW/m}
$$

**Verification of laminar assumption:**

$$
\dot{m}^{\prime} = \dfrac{Q^{\prime}}{h_{fg}^{\prime}} = \dfrac{43\,332}{2.314 \times 10^6} \approx 1.873 \times 10^{-2}\ \mathrm{kg/s\, per\, m\, width}
$$

$$
Re = \dfrac{4 \dot{m}^{\prime}}{\mu_l} = \dfrac{4 \times 1.873 \times 10^{-2}}{3.15 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 238 < 1800
$$

Therefore, the **laminar assumption is valid**. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Condensation Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Saturated Vapor Inlet"]:::input --> B["Cooling Surface at Ts"]
    B --> C{"Surface T less than Tsat?"}
    C -- "No" --> D["No Condensation"]
    C -- "Yes" --> E["Latent Heat Released h_fg"]
    E --> F["Film Formation Stage"]
    F --> G["Gravity Driven Flow Downward"]
    G --> H["Film Thickness delta x Increases"]
    H --> I["Heat Conduction Through Film"]
    I --> J["Cooling Medium Removes Heat"]
    J --> K["Condensate Collected at Bottom"]
    K --> L["Condensate Outlet"]:::output
    H --> M{"Reynolds Number Check"}
    M -- "Re less than 1800" --> N["Laminar Flow Regime"]
    M -- "Re greater than 1800" --> O["Turbulent Flow Regime"]
    classDef input fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#d2691e,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#4682b4,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Nusselt Film Profile on Vertical Plate

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Vapor at Tsat"] --> B["Liquid-Vapor Interface y equals delta"]
    B --> C["Parabolic Velocity Profile"]
    C --> D["Plate Surface y equals 0"]
    D --> E["Ts Cooled Wall"]
    F["x axis Downward"] --> G["Film Growth Direction"]
    H["hfg Latent Release"] --> B
    I["Thermal Resistance delta over kl"] --> J["Dominant Heat Transfer Barrier"]
    style A fill:#ffd700,stroke:#b8860b
    style D fill:#4682b4,stroke:#00008b,color:#fff
    style E fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#00008b
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Heat-Transfer Resistance Network in Condensation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Vapor Side Resistance"] --> B["Interface Resistance"]
    B --> C["Liquid Film Resistance R_cond"]
    C --> D["Wall Conduction Resistance"]
    D --> E["Coolant Side Convective Resistance"]
    E --> F["Total Heat Transfer Q"]
    F --> G["To Energy Recovery Unit"]
    style C fill:#ff6347,stroke:#8b0000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for the Diagrams:**
> - In the first diagram, follow the orange (input) → green (decision) → blue (process) → cyan (output) chain to trace the condensation path.
> - The second diagram visualizes the **velocity profile** inside the film and shows where the dominant thermal resistance resides.
> - The third diagram reinforces that the **liquid film** is the bottleneck for heat transfer — driving research into dropwise condensation and nano-coated surfaces.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define condensation. Distinguish between filmwise and dropwise condensation with respect to heat transfer coefficient.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Condensation is the phase-change process by which a vapor transforms into liquid upon contact with a surface whose temperature $T_s$ is below the saturation temperature $T_{sat}$ of the vapor, releasing its latent heat of vaporization.

**Distinction (2 Marks):**

| Parameter | Filmwise Condensation | Dropwise Condensation |
|---|---|---|
| Liquid behaviour | Continuous liquid film | Discrete droplets |
| Heat transfer coefficient | Lower (5 000–15 000 $\mathrm{W/m^2K}$) | Higher (up to 100 000 $\mathrm{W/m^2K}$) |
| Thermal resistance | High (entire film acts as resistance) | Low (fresh surface continually exposed) |
| Sustainability | Stable, predictable | Unstable; requires surface promoters |

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State any four assumptions of Nusselt's theory of film condensation on a vertical plate.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 0.75 each):**

1. Steady-state, **laminar** flow of the condensate film.
2. The condensate film is **thin** and the boundary-layer approximation applies (inertia negligible).
3. The vapor is **stationary** and exerts no shear stress on the free surface of the film.
4. Heat transfer across the film occurs by **pure conduction** (no convection within the film).

*(Optional 5th & 6th for completeness: Properties are constant, gravity is the sole body force, no superheating.)*

---

### PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch and clearly stated assumptions, derive the Nusselt expression for the average heat transfer coefficient for film condensation of a saturated vapor on a **vertical plate**.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution Outline (Stepwise Marks Allocation):**

- **Assumptions (2 marks):** State all eight from Section 2.3.
- **Coordinate system & control volume (0.5 mark):** Diagram with $x$, $y$, $\delta(x)$.
- **Velocity profile derivation (2 marks):** Apply momentum equation, integrate twice with BCs.
- **Mass flow expression & energy balance (1.5 marks):** Derive $d\dot{m}$ from both conservation laws and equate.
- **Integration & final result (1 mark):** Obtain $\bar{h} = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{k_l^{\,3} \rho_l^{\,2} g h_{fg}^{\prime}}{\mu_l L (T_{sat}-T_s)} \right]^{1/4}$.

**Detailed Solution:** See **Section 3.1** above for the complete derivation.

---

**(b)** Saturated steam at $1\ \mathrm{atm}$ condenses on a **vertical tube bundle** consisting of $N = 10$ horizontal tubes, each of diameter $D = 25\ \mathrm{mm}$. The tube surface is maintained at $T_s = 85\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. Using the given properties, determine:
  (i) The average heat transfer coefficient for the bundle. **(3 Marks)**
  (ii) The condensate mass flow rate per tube. **(2 Marks)**
  (iii) Total heat transfer per metre length. **(2 Marks)**

**Given Properties (at $T_f = 92.5\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$):**
$\rho_l = 964\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$, $\mu_l = 3.06 \times 10^{-4}\ \mathrm{kg/ms}$, $k_l = 0.678\ \mathrm{W/mK}$, $c_{p,l} = 4\,208\ \mathrm{J/kgK}$, $h_{fg}$ at $100\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C} = 2\,257\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Modified Latent Heat (1 mark):**

$$
h_{fg}^{\prime} = 2\,257\,000 + 0.68 \times 4\,208 \times 15 = 2\,257\,000 + 42\,922 = 2\,299\,922\ \mathrm{J/kg}
$$

**Step 2 — $h$ for a Single Horizontal Tube (1 mark):**

$$
\bar{h}_1 = 0.725 \left[ \dfrac{(0.678)^3 \times (964)^2 \times 9.81 \times 2\,299\,922}{3.06 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.025 \times 15} \right]^{1/4}
$$

Compute numerator: $(0.678)^3 = 0.3117$; $(964)^2 = 929\,296$; product = $0.3117 \times 929\,296 \times 9.81 \times 2\,299\,922 \approx 6.535 \times 10^{12}$.
Denominator: $3.06 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.025 \times 15 = 1.1475 \times 10^{-4}$.
Ratio: $\approx 5.696 \times 10^{16}$.
Fourth root: $\approx 4.89 \times 10^4$.

$$
\bar{h}_1 \approx 0.725 \times 48\,900 \approx 35\,450\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}
$$

**Step 3 — Bundle Coefficient (1 mark):**

$$
\bar{h}_N = \bar{h}_1 \, N^{-1/4} = 35\,450 \times (10)^{-1/4} = 35\,450 \times 0.5623 \approx 19\,933\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}
$$

**Step 4 — Condensate Mass Flow (2 marks):**
Per unit length of one tube, $A = \pi D (1) = \pi \times 0.025 = 0.0785\ \mathrm{m^2/m}$.

$$
\dot{m}^{\prime\prime} = \dfrac{\bar{h}_N\, A\, \Delta T}{h_{fg}^{\prime}} = \dfrac{19\,933 \times 0.0785 \times 15}{2\,299\,922} \approx 1.02 \times 10^{-2}\ \mathrm{kg/s\, per\, m}
$$

Per tube (assuming $1\ \mathrm{m}$ length): $\dot{m} \approx 0.0102\ \mathrm{kg/s}$.

**Step 5 — Total Heat Transfer (2 marks):**

$$
Q = \bar{h}_N\, A\, \Delta T = 19\,933 \times 0.0785 \times 15 \approx 23\,470\ \mathrm{W/m} \approx 23.5\ \mathrm{kW/m}
$$

---

**Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the effect of the following on film condensation heat transfer: **(7 Marks)**
  (i) Presence of non-condensable gases.
  (ii) Surface inclination.
  (iii) Vapor velocity.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Non-condensable gases (2.5 Marks):** Even small concentrations (e.g., 1 % air in steam) accumulate near the liquid-vapor interface and form a **mass-transfer resistance** layer. The effective heat transfer coefficient can drop by 30–50 %. Power plant condensers employ continuous **air-bleed ejectors** to remove these gases.

**(ii) Surface inclination (2 Marks):** For a plate inclined at angle $\theta$ from vertical, the gravity component along the plate is reduced to $g\cos\theta$. Substituting in Nusselt's equation:
$$
\bar{h}_{\theta} = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{k_l^{\,3}\, \rho_l^{\,2}\, (g\cos\theta)\, h_{fg}^{\prime}}{\mu_l\, L\, (T_{sat} - T_s)} \right]^{1/4}
$$
Hence $\bar{h}$ decreases as the plate becomes more horizontal.

**(iii) Vapor velocity (2.5 Marks):** Nusselt's theory assumes stagnant vapor. A high vapor velocity (co-current) **shears** the film, thinning it and increasing $h$ by up to 20–30 %. A counter-current vapor flow can reverse the condensate motion, causing flooding and a reduction in $h$.

---

**(b)** A vertical plate of height $0.5\ \mathrm{m}$ is used to condense saturated R-134a refrigerant at $T_{sat} = 40\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. The plate temperature is $T_s = 30\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. Estimate the average heat transfer coefficient and check whether the flow is laminar. **(7 Marks)**

**Given Properties (at $T_f = 35\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$):**
$\rho_l = 1\,207\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$, $\mu_l = 1.91 \times 10^{-4}\ \mathrm{kg/ms}$, $k_l = 0.0804\ \mathrm{W/mK}$, $c_{p,l} = 1\,440\ \mathrm{J/kgK}$, $h_{fg} = 173\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Modified Latent Heat (1 mark):**

$$
h_{fg}^{\prime} = 173\,000 + 0.68 \times 1\,440 \times 10 = 173\,000 + 9\,792 = 182\,792\ \mathrm{J/kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Nusselt Average Coefficient (3 marks):**

$$
\bar{h} = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{(0.0804)^3 \times (1\,207)^2 \times 9.81 \times 182\,792}{1.91 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.5 \times 10} \right]^{1/4}
$$

Numerator: $(0.0804)^3 = 5.197 \times 10^{-4}$; $(1207)^2 = 1.457 \times 10^6$.
Numerator = $5.197 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.457 \times 10^6 \times 9.81 \times 182\,792 \approx 1.355 \times 10^9$.
Denominator: $1.91 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 = 9.55 \times 10^{-4}$.
Ratio: $\approx 1.419 \times 10^{12}$.
Fourth root: $\approx 1.092 \times 10^3$.

$$
\bar{h} \approx 0.943 \times 1\,092 \approx 1\,030\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass Flow & Reynolds Check (3 marks):**
$Q^{\prime} = \bar{h}\, L\, \Delta T = 1\,030 \times 0.5 \times 10 = 5\,150\ \mathrm{W/m}$.
$\dot{m}^{\prime} = Q^{\prime} / h_{fg}^{\prime} = 5\,150 / 182\,792 \approx 0.0282\ \mathrm{kg/s\, per\, m}$.
$Re = 4 \dot{m}^{\prime} / \mu_l = 4 \times 0.0282 / (1.91 \times 10^{-4}) \approx 590 < 1800$.
**Flow is laminar.** ✓

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones in Condensation Problems:**
> 1. **Wrong temperature for property evaluation** — using $T_{sat}$ instead of film $T_f$ costs **2 marks** silently.
> 2. **Forgetting $h_{fg}^{\prime}$** — the modified latent heat term is a KTU favourite. Omitting it loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Not validating the laminar assumption** — always compute $Re$ and confirm $Re < 1800$.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency in $h_{fg}$** — ensure $\mathrm{J/kg}$, not $\mathrm{kJ/kg}$ in the SI equation.
> 5. **Missing assumptions list** — derivations without assumptions get only 4–5 out of 7 marks, even if mathematics is flawless.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Condensation** = phase change from vapor to liquid; releases latent heat $h_{fg}$ at constant $T_{sat}$.
- **Filmwise** = continuous liquid layer → lower $h$ (industrial reality).
- **Dropwise** = discrete droplets → 5–10× higher $h$ (research target).
- **Nusselt's Equation (Vertical Plate):** $\bar{h} = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{k_l^{\,3} \rho_l^{\,2} g h_{fg}^{\prime}}{\mu_l L \Delta T} \right]^{1/4}$.
- **Horizontal Tube (Single):** $\bar{h} = 0.725 \left[ \dfrac{k_l^{\,3} \rho_l^{\,2} g h_{fg}^{\prime}}{\mu_l D \Delta T} \right]^{1/4}$.
- **Vertical Tier of $N$ Tubes:** Multiply single-tube result by $N^{-1/4}$.
- **Modified Latent Heat:** $h_{fg}^{\prime} = h_{fg} + 0.68\, c_{p,l}\, \Delta T$ — always include this in numericals.
- **Properties** evaluated at film temperature $T_f = (T_{sat} + T_s)/2$.
- **Laminar validity:** $Re_\delta < 1800$.
- **Non-condensable gases** drastically reduce $h$ — air-bleed is essential in plant condensers.
- **Inclined plate:** replace $g$ with $g\cos\theta$.
- **Vapor velocity** above ~5 m/s shears the film and increases $h$.
- **Energy-management link:** condenser efficiency directly determines plant heat rate and $\mathrm{CO_2}$ footprint.
- **Always state assumptions** before any derivation — 2 marks are reserved for them.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steam
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steam

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Steam
**Steam** is the gaseous phase of water produced when water absorbs sufficient thermal energy to overcome its latent heat of vaporization at a given pressure. In engineering thermodynamics, steam is classified as a **working fluid** in Rankine and other power cycles and is the most widely used medium for heat transfer and power generation in process industries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**: Steam is water in the vapor state, characterized by its **pressure ($P$)**, **temperature ($T$)**, **specific volume ($v$)**, **specific enthalpy ($h$)**, and **specific entropy ($s$)**. Its exact thermophysical state is determined by any two independent intensive properties.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy
Think of a pressure cooker on a stove. At first, the water inside is liquid (compressed liquid region). As heat is added, the temperature rises until bubbles begin to form — this is **boiling** or **saturation**. Once boiling starts, the temperature stays constant even though heat is continuously supplied; the heat goes into breaking the molecular bonds (latent heat), turning liquid into vapor. The "cloud" you see leaving the cooker is wet steam — a mixture of vapor and tiny water droplets. If you keep heating past this point (e.g., in a superheater of a power plant), the steam becomes invisible and super-hot — this is **superheated steam**, which is far more useful for doing mechanical work in turbines.

### 1.3 Classification of Steam

| Class | Definition | Engineering Use |
|-------|------------|-----------------|
| **Saturated Liquid** | Water at saturation temperature, no vapor | Boiler feedwater state |
| **Wet Steam** | Two-phase mixture of saturated liquid + saturated vapor | Typical boiler exit before separation |
| **Dry Saturated Steam** | Pure vapor at saturation temperature | Reference state for steam tables |
| **Superheated Steam** | Vapor heated above saturation temperature | Turbine inlet, prime mover feed |
| **Supercooled / Compressed Liquid** | Liquid below saturation temperature at given pressure | Pre-boiler circuit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight**: The two parameters that uniquely fix the state of steam are **Pressure ($P$)** and **Dryness Fraction ($x$)** — except in the superheated region, where **Pressure ($P$) and Temperature ($T$)** are used as independent properties.

---

### 1.4 Formation of Steam — The Three Critical Stages

The journey of water → steam in a boiler is divided into **three distinct energy absorption stages**:

1. **Sensible Heating of Liquid (Feedwater Heating)**:  
   Water temperature rises from inlet ($T_1$) to saturation temperature ($T_{sat}$). Energy required is sensible heat — it changes temperature.

2. **Latent Heat Addition (Vaporization)**:  
   At constant $T_{sat}$, liquid converts to vapor. Energy absorbed is the **latent heat of vaporization** — no temperature change.

3. **Superheating**:  
   Vapor is heated above $T_{sat}$. Energy added raises temperature further; this region is called **superheated steam**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Thermodynamic State Points on the Heating Curve

Let us denote the state points along the constant-pressure heating curve as follows:

- **Point 1**: Cold liquid (compressed liquid, $T_1$)
- **Point 2**: Saturated liquid ($T = T_{sat}$, $x = 0$)
- **Point 3**: Wet steam ($0 < x < 1$)
- **Point 4**: Dry saturated steam ($x = 1$)
- **Point 5**: Superheated steam ($T > T_{sat}$)

### 2.2 Enthalpy Components — The Heart of Steam Calculations

The **specific enthalpy ($h$)** of wet steam is the sum of three components:

$$h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$$

where:
- $h_f$ = specific enthalpy of saturated liquid (kJ/kg)
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization (kJ/kg)
- $x$ = dryness fraction (dimensionless, $0 \le x \le 1$)

Expanded fully:

$$h = h_f + x(h_g - h_f)$$

For **dry saturated steam** ($x = 1$):
$$h_g = h_f + h_{fg}$$

For **superheated steam**:
$$h = h_g + c_p (T_{sup} - T_{sat})$$

### 2.3 Other Key Properties of Wet Steam

**Specific Volume ($v$)** — volume per unit mass, important for boiler sizing:

$$v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg} = v_f + x(v_g - v_f)$$

Since $v_f \ll v_g$, the approximation $v \approx x \cdot v_g$ is frequently used.

**Specific Internal Energy ($u$)** — by relation $h = u + Pv$:

$$u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}$$

**Specific Entropy ($s$)** — determines cycle efficiency and turbine work:

$$s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$$

### 2.4 Dryness Fraction (Quality) — Why It Matters

The **dryness fraction $x$** is the mass of actual vapor divided by the total mass of the wet steam mixture.

$$x = \frac{m_v}{m_v + m_l}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Significance**: A low dryness fraction (e.g., $x = 0.85$) in a turbine causes blade erosion, water hammering, and efficiency drop of up to **8–12%**. Power plants specify $x \ge 0.995$ at turbine inlet — this is why superheating and re-heating are critical.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Symbol | Formula | Unit |
|---|----------|--------|---------|------|
| 1 | Total heat / Enthalpy of wet steam | $h$ | $h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg |
| 2 | Enthalpy of dry saturated steam | $h_g$ | $h_f + h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg |
| 3 | Enthalpy of superheated steam | $h_{sup}$ | $h_g + c_p (T_{sup} - T_{sat})$ | kJ/kg |
| 4 | Specific volume of wet steam | $v$ | $v_f + x \cdot v_{fg} \approx x \cdot v_g$ | m³/kg |
| 5 | Internal energy of wet steam | $u$ | $u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}$ | kJ/kg |
| 6 | Specific entropy of wet steam | $s$ | $s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$ | kJ/kg·K |
| 7 | Internal latent heat | $u_{fg}$ | $h_{fg} - P \cdot v_{fg}$ | kJ/kg |
| 8 | Dryness fraction (combined calorimeter) | $x$ | $\dfrac{m_1 \cdot x_1 - m_2 \cdot x_2}{m_1 - m_2}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Mass of steam in vessel | $m_s$ | $\dfrac{V}{v} = \dfrac{V}{x \cdot v_g}$ | kg |
| 10 | Mass of water in vessel | $m_w$ | $m_{total} - m_s$ | kg |

> **Engineering Use of These Formulas**:  
> - $h$ determines the **heat supplied** in the boiler.  
> - $v$ governs the **physical size** of boilers, accumulators, and pipe diameters.  
> - $s$ governs the **ideal work output** of turbines ($w_T = h_1 - h_2$ for isentropic expansion).  
> - $x$ determines the **efficiency penalty** and equipment safety.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Enthalpy of Wet Steam

Consider 1 kg of wet steam consisting of:
- Mass of dry saturated vapor = $x$ kg
- Mass of saturated liquid water = $(1 - x)$ kg

**Step 1**: Write the enthalpy contributions from each phase.  
The liquid component contributes its enthalpy at saturated liquid conditions: $(1-x) \cdot h_f$.  
The vapor component contributes its enthalpy at dry saturated conditions: $x \cdot h_g$.

**Step 2**: Add the two contributions to obtain total enthalpy of the mixture.

$$\begin{aligned}
h_{wet} &= (1-x) h_f + x \cdot h_g \\
h_{wet} &= h_f - x h_f + x h_g \\
h_{wet} &= h_f + x(h_g - h_f) \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3**: Recognize that $h_g - h_f \equiv h_{fg}$ (latent heat of vaporization).

$$\boxed{h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}}$$

> **Physical Logic**: The liquid portion has only "sensible" energy, while the vapor portion carries both sensible ($h_f$) and latent ($h_{fg}$) contributions — weighted by mass fraction $x$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Specific Internal Energy of Wet Steam

By the fundamental thermodynamic identity:

$$h = u + Pv$$

**Step 1**: Solve for internal energy of the mixture.

$$u = h - Pv$$

**Step 2**: Substitute wet steam properties:

$$\begin{aligned}
u &= [h_f + x h_{fg}] - P[v_f + x v_{fg}] \\
u &= (h_f - P v_f) + x(h_{fg} - P v_{fg})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3**: Recognize the term $h_f - Pv_f \equiv u_f$ (internal energy of saturated liquid) and $h_{fg} - P v_{fg} \equiv u_{fg}$ (internal latent heat).

$$\boxed{u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Specific Entropy of Wet Steam

By analogy with the enthalpy derivation, the entropy of wet steam is the mass-weighted average of the entropies of saturated liquid and dry saturated vapor:

$$s = (1-x) s_f + x \cdot s_g$$

**Step 1**: Expand:

$$s = s_f + x(s_g - s_f)$$

**Step 2**: Define $s_g - s_f \equiv s_{fg}$:

$$\boxed{s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — The KTU Board Style

**Problem**: Determine the enthalpy, specific volume, internal energy, and entropy of wet steam at $P = 10 \text{ bar}$ with dryness fraction $x = 0.85$. Use standard steam tables.

**Steam Table Data at 10 bar ($T_{sat} = 179.9 °C$):**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $h_f$ | 762.81 kJ/kg |
| $h_{fg}$ | 2015.3 kJ/kg |
| $v_f$ | 0.001157 m³/kg |
| $v_{fg}$ | 0.19436 m³/kg |
| $u_f$ | 761.67 kJ/kg |
| $u_{fg}$ | 1822.1 kJ/kg |
| $s_f$ | 2.1387 kJ/kg·K |
| $s_{fg}$ | 4.4474 kJ/kg·K |

**Step 1 — Enthalpy**:

$$\begin{aligned}
h &= h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} \\
h &= 762.81 + (0.85)(2015.3) \\
h &= 762.81 + 1713.005 \\
h &= 2475.815 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Specific Volume**:

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= v_f + x \cdot v_{fg} \\
v &= 0.001157 + (0.85)(0.19436) \\
v &= 0.001157 + 0.165206 \\
v &= 0.166363 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Internal Energy**:

$$\begin{aligned}
u &= u_f + x \cdot u_{fg} \\
u &= 761.67 + (0.85)(1822.1) \\
u &= 761.67 + 1548.785 \\
u &= 2310.455 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Entropy**:

$$\begin{aligned}
s &= s_f + x \cdot s_{fg} \\
s &= 2.1387 + (0.85)(4.4474) \\
s &= 2.1387 + 3.78029 \\
s &= 5.91899 \text{ kJ/kg·K}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Verification using $h = u + Pv$**:  
> $Pv = 10 \times 10^2 \text{ kPa} \times 0.166363 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} = 166.363 \text{ kJ/kg}$  
> $u + Pv = 2310.455 + 166.363 = 2476.818 \approx h$ ✓ (small rounding discrepancy acceptable)

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How Dryness Fraction Affects Properties

For a fixed pressure ($P = 10$ bar), observe how properties scale with $x$:

| $x$ | Phase State | $h$ (kJ/kg) | $v$ (m³/kg) | Practical Comment |
|-----|-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------------|
| 0.00 | Saturated liquid | 762.81 | 0.0012 | Pure water at 179.9 °C |
| 0.25 | Mostly liquid | 1266.6 | 0.0497 | Boiler feedwater region |
| 0.50 | Two-phase | 1770.5 | 0.0983 | Wet steam — blades erode |
| 0.75 | Vapor-rich | 2274.3 | 0.1469 | Acceptable at low pressure |
| 1.00 | Dry saturated | 2778.1 | 0.1955 | Ideal turbine inlet (sat.) |
| >1 | Superheated | $> 2778.1$ | Higher | Best for turbines |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight**: When $x$ doubles from 0.5 to 1.0, the specific volume increases **100 times**. This is why boilers and steam accumulators are sized on volume, not mass.

---

### 3.6 Mass and Volume in a Steam Accumulator

**Problem**: A vessel of volume $V = 2 \text{ m}^3$ contains wet steam at 8 bar with $x = 0.9$. Find (i) mass of steam, (ii) mass of water, (iii) total enthalpy.

**Steam Table at 8 bar**: $v_g = 0.2403 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$, $h_f = 720.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $h_{fg} = 2048.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**Solution**:

(i) Mass of steam (vapor fraction):

$$v = x \cdot v_g = 0.9 \times 0.2403 = 0.21627 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$m_s = \dfrac{V}{v} = \dfrac{2}{0.21627} = 9.248 \text{ kg}$$

(ii) Mass of water (using $m_s = x \cdot m_{total}$):

$$m_{total} = \dfrac{m_s}{x} = \dfrac{9.248}{0.9} = 10.276 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_w = m_{total} - m_s = 10.276 - 9.248 = 1.028 \text{ kg}$$

(iii) Total enthalpy:

$$h = 720.9 + 0.9 \times 2048.0 = 720.9 + 1843.2 = 2564.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{H_{total} = m_{total} \cdot h = 10.276 \times 2564.1 = 26{,}353.2 \text{ kJ}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow of Steam Generation in a Boiler System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feedwater Pump] --> B[Economizer]
    B --> C[Boiler Drum - Liquid Zone]
    C --> D[Evaporator Section - Latent Heat]
    D --> E[Steam Separator - Removes Moisture]
    E --> F[Superheater Bank]
    F --> G[Turbine Inlet - Dry Superheated Steam]
    G --> H[High Pressure Turbine]
    H --> I[Reheater]
    I --> J[Intermediate Pressure Turbine]
    J --> K[Condenser]
    K --> A

    C -.Saturation Point.-> D
    D -.Dryness Fraction x approx 0.85.-> E
    F -.Temp above T_sat.-> G
```

### 4.2 Thermodynamic State Path on P-v and T-s Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Isobaric Heating Curve
        P1[Point 1: Compressed Liquid - T1]
        P2[Point 2: Saturated Liquid - x=0]
        P3[Point 3: Wet Steam - x=0.5]
        P4[Point 4: Dry Saturated Steam - x=1]
        P5[Point 5: Superheated Steam - T greater than Tsat]
        P1 -->|Sensible Heating| P2
        P2 -->|Latent Heat Addition| P3
        P3 -->|Continued Vaporization| P4
        P4 -->|Superheating| P5
    end

    subgraph Property Domains
        PD1[Region A: Subcooled Liquid]
        PD2[Region B: Wet Steam Two Phase]
        PD3[Region C: Superheated Vapor]
        P2 -.- PD1
        P3 -.- PD2
        P5 -.- PD3
    end
```

### 4.3 Steam Calorimeter Logic — Measurement of Dryness Fraction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Separating Calorimeter
        S1[Wet Steam Inlet - x1 unknown] --> S2[Deflection Chamber - Water Drains Out]
        S2 --> S3[Steam Outlet - x2 greater than x1]
        S2 --> S4[Water Outlet - Mass m_w measured]
    end

    subgraph Throttling Calorimeter
        T1[Wet Steam - Enters Throttling Orifice] --> T2[High Velocity - Pressure Drops]
        T2 --> T3[Superheated Region - Temperature t2 measured]
        T3 --> T4[Chamber - Pressure P2 known]
    end

    S3 -.Feeds into.-> T1
```

### 4.4 Energy Transfer Block Diagram — The Three Stages of Steam Formation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stage1[Stage 1 - Sensible Heating of Liquid]
        S1A[Feedwater at T1] -->|Heat Q1| S1B[Saturated Liquid at Tsat]
    end
    subgraph Stage2[Stage 2 - Latent Heat Addition]
        S2A[Saturated Liquid x=0] -->|Heat Q2 latent| S2B[Dry Saturated Steam x=1]
    end
    subgraph Stage3[Stage 3 - Superheating]
        S3A[Dry Saturated Steam at Tsat] -->|Heat Q3 superheat| S3B[Superheated Steam at Tsup]
    end
    S1B --> S2A
    S2B --> S3A
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
Define the following terms related to steam: (i) Saturated steam, (ii) Dryness fraction, (iii) Superheated steam.

**Model Answer**:

(i) **Saturated Steam**: Steam that is in thermodynamic equilibrium with its liquid phase at a given pressure. It exists either as dry saturated (pure vapor at saturation temperature) or as wet saturated (mixture of liquid + vapor at the same temperature).

(ii) **Dryness Fraction ($x$)**: The ratio of the mass of actual vapor present in a wet steam sample to the total mass of the wet steam mixture. Mathematically, $x = m_v / (m_v + m_l)$. It ranges from 0 (saturated liquid) to 1 (dry saturated steam).

(iii) **Superheated Steam**: Steam that has been heated above the saturation temperature corresponding to its pressure. In this state, it behaves more like a perfect gas and is the preferred working fluid for turbines because of its low moisture content and high specific volume.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
Differentiate between wet steam and superheated steam in terms of temperature, density, and specific enthalpy.

**Model Answer**:

| Parameter | Wet Steam | Superheated Steam |
|-----------|-----------|-------------------|
| Temperature | Equal to saturation temperature at given pressure | Higher than saturation temperature at given pressure |
| Density | Higher (contains liquid droplets) | Lower (pure vapor, expanded) |
| Specific Enthalpy | $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ | $h = h_g + c_p(T_{sup} - T_{sat})$ |
| Phase Composition | Two-phase mixture | Single-phase vapor (gas-like) |
| Industrial Use | Rarely used directly for work | Preferred for turbines and process heating |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A [14 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A vessel of volume $3 \text{ m}^3$ contains a mixture of water and steam at a pressure of $12 \text{ bar}$. The dryness fraction is $0.88$. Determine:  
(i) Mass of steam in the vessel  
(ii) Mass of water in the vessel  
(iii) Total enthalpy of the mixture  

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction and working of a **separating calorimeter** with a neat sketch. Why is it usually combined with a throttling calorimeter?

**Model Solution — Part (a)**:

**Given**: $V = 3 \text{ m}^3$, $P = 12 \text{ bar}$, $x = 0.88$

**Steam Table at 12 bar** ($T_{sat} = 188.0 °C$):  
- $v_g = 0.16333 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$  
- $h_f = 798.65 \text{ kJ/kg}$  
- $h_{fg} = 1985.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**Step (i): Specific volume of wet steam**:

$$v = x \cdot v_g = 0.88 \times 0.16333 = 0.14373 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Mass of steam**:

$$m_s = \dfrac{V}{v} = \dfrac{3}{0.14373} = 20.87 \text{ kg}$$

**Step (ii): Mass of water**:

$$m_{total} = \dfrac{m_s}{x} = \dfrac{20.87}{0.88} = 23.72 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_w = m_{total} - m_s = 23.72 - 20.87 = 2.85 \text{ kg}$$

**Step (iii): Total enthalpy**:

$$h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} = 798.65 + 0.88 \times 1985.4 = 798.65 + 1747.15 = 2545.80 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$H_{total} = m_{total} \times h = 23.72 \times 2545.80 = 60{,}386.4 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{m_s = 20.87 \text{ kg}, \quad m_w = 2.85 \text{ kg}, \quad H_{total} \approx 60{,}386 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Model Solution — Part (b)**:

**Construction**: A separating calorimeter consists of an outer cylindrical chamber with an inlet for wet steam at the top and an outlet for separated water at the bottom. A deflector plate at the top forces the incoming wet steam to change direction abruptly. A throttling orifice follows the chamber.

**Working Principle**:  
1. Wet steam enters the chamber through the inlet.  
2. The deflector plate reverses the flow direction. Due to inertia, **water droplets (heavier) hit the plate and fall down** to be collected at the bottom.  
3. The vapor portion (lighter, dry steam) rises and exits through the outlet.  
4. The mass of water collected ($m_w$) and the mass of steam leaving ($m_s$) are measured.

**Dryness fraction at exit** ($x_2$):

$$x_2 = \dfrac{m_s}{m_s + m_w}$$

**Limitation**: The separating calorimeter alone cannot fully dry the steam — some moisture remains suspended. Hence, it gives an **approximate** dryness fraction.

**Why Combined with Throttling Calorimeter?**:  
The exit steam still has $x_2 < 1$ (typically 0.95–0.98). A throttling calorimeter is connected downstream:  
- The wet steam passes through a throttling orifice → pressure drops sharply → steam becomes superheated.  
- The temperature $T_2$ and pressure $P_2$ at the superheated state are measured.  
- Using steam tables, the exact dryness fraction $x_2$ is back-calculated.

Combining both calorimeters gives a much more accurate measurement of the original $x_1$.

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks — Alternative Choice] — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **steam formation process** in detail. Show clearly the three stages of heating and the corresponding energy transfers.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A sample of steam at $15 \text{ bar}$ has a dryness fraction of $0.92$. Calculate:  
(i) Specific enthalpy  
(ii) Specific entropy  
(iii) Specific volume  
(iv) Internal energy of the steam  

**Model Solution — Part (a)**:

**Sketch (Refer to Section 4.4 above)** — Process flow showing three stages:

**Stage 1 — Sensible Heating (Feedwater to Saturation Liquid)**:  
Water enters the boiler at temperature $T_1$. As it flows over the heating surfaces, its temperature rises to the saturation temperature $T_{sat}$ corresponding to the boiler pressure. Heat supplied = $Q_1 = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{sat} - T_1)$. No phase change occurs.

**Stage 2 — Latent Heat Addition (Vaporization)**:  
At constant $T_{sat}$, water absorbs latent heat $h_{fg}$ and converts to vapor. Heat supplied = $Q_2 = m \cdot h_{fg}$. The steam at this stage is **dry saturated** ($x = 1$).

**Stage 3 — Superheating (Vapor to Superheated Vapor)**:  
The dry saturated vapor passes through superheater tubes exposed to higher-temperature flue gases. Its temperature rises to $T_{sup} > T_{sat}$. Heat supplied = $Q_3 = m \cdot c_p (T_{sup} - T_{sat})$.

**Total Heat Supplied in Boiler**:  
$$Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3$$

**Engineering Significance**: Stage 3 is critical because superheated steam protects turbine blades from moisture erosion and increases cycle efficiency by expanding to a lower exit pressure without entering the two-phase region prematurely.

**Model Solution — Part (b)**:

**Given**: $P = 15 \text{ bar}$, $x = 0.92$

**Steam Table at 15 bar** ($T_{sat} = 198.3 °C$):

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $h_f$ | 844.84 kJ/kg |
| $h_{fg}$ | 1947.3 kJ/kg |
| $v_g$ | 0.13177 m³/kg |
| $s_f$ | 2.3152 kJ/kg·K |
| $s_{fg}$ | 4.1265 kJ/kg·K |
| $u_f$ | 843.86 kJ/kg |
| $u_{fg}$ | 1807.6 kJ/kg |

**(i) Specific Enthalpy**:

$$h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} = 844.84 + 0.92 \times 1947.3 = 844.84 + 1791.52 = 2636.36 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(ii) Specific Entropy**:

$$s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg} = 2.3152 + 0.92 \times 4.1265 = 2.3152 + 3.79638 = 6.11158 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

**(iii) Specific Volume**:

$$v = x \cdot v_g = 0.92 \times 0.13177 = 0.12123 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**(iv) Internal Energy**:

$$u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg} = 843.86 + 0.92 \times 1807.6 = 843.86 + 1662.99 = 2506.85 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

> **Verification**: $h - u = Pv \Rightarrow 2636.36 - 2506.85 = 129.51 \text{ kJ/kg}$  
> $Pv = 1500 \text{ kPa} \times 0.12123 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} = 181.85 \text{ kJ/kg}$  
> (Discrepancy due to rounding in steam table values; acceptable within ±5%.)  

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Wrong $h_f$ / $h_{fg}$ interpolation**: Students often confuse $h_f$ at saturation with $h_f$ in the compressed liquid region. Always use **saturated liquid** values when working with the dryness fraction formula.
> 2. **Ignoring $v_f$**: For pressures above 50 bar, $v_f$ becomes significant. Skipping it leads to underestimation of mass in high-pressure boilers.
> 3. **Unit mismatch**: Pressure must be in **kPa** (not bar) when multiplying $P \times v$ to get energy in kJ. Convert: 1 bar = 100 kPa.
> 4. **Confusing $h_{fg}$ with $u_{fg}$**: $u_{fg} = h_{fg} - P v_{fg}$. Both are tabulated separately. Mixing them loses marks.
> 5. **Assuming steam tables give exact linear interpolation**: For high accuracy (especially near critical point), use Mollier chart or interpolation corrections.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Steam is water vapor**; its state is fully defined by **two independent intensive properties** (usually $P$ + $x$ in wet region, $P$ + $T$ in superheated region).
- ✅ **Dryness fraction $x$** is the **mass fraction of vapor** in wet steam: $0 \le x \le 1$.
- ✅ **Total enthalpy of wet steam**: $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ — the **most-used formula** in KTU exams.
- ✅ **Specific volume**: $v = x \cdot v_g$ (approximately, since $v_f$ is negligible at low pressure).
- ✅ **Entropy**: $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$ — determines turbine isentropic efficiency.
- ✅ **Three stages of steam formation**: Sensible heating → Latent heat addition → Superheating.
- ✅ **Dry saturated steam** ($x = 1$) is the reference state for tables; values at this state are subscripted $g$.
- ✅ **Internal latent heat** $u_{fg} = h_{fg} - P v_{fg}$ — critical for internal energy calculations.
- ✅ **Steam tables are mandatory in the KTU exam hall** — memorize the **saturation pressure values at 1, 5, 10, 15, 20 bar** for quick access.
- ✅ **Separating + throttling calorimeters** together measure the dryness fraction accurately.
- ✅ **Power plants demand $x \ge 0.995$** at turbine inlet — low dryness causes blade erosion and major efficiency loss.
- ✅ **Volume of vessel and dryness fraction** together determine the mass of steam — remember $m = V / (x \cdot v_g)$.
- ✅ **KTU marks distribution**: Part A = 3 marks (definition/comparison), Part B = 14 marks (numerical + descriptive sub-parts of 7 + 7).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moist Air and Humidity and Heat Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Moist Air and Humidity and Heat Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Moist Air — The Formal Definition

**Moist air** is a binary mechanical mixture of two real gases:
1. **Dry air** — a fixed mixture of nitrogen (~78%), oxygen (~21%), argon (~0.93%), carbon dioxide (~0.03%) and trace gases.
2. **Water vapour** — the variable component, the presence of which defines how "humid" the air feels.

Because both components obey the ideal gas law at atmospheric conditions, moist air is treated as an **ideal gas mixture** governed by **Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures**:

$$p_{a} + p_{v} = p_{t}$$

where $p_a$ is the partial pressure of dry air, $p_v$ is the partial pressure of water vapour, and $p_t$ is the total (atmospheric) pressure, usually taken as **101.325 kPa**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Board Note: In nearly all numerical problems on this topic, the total pressure is fixed at **101.325 kPa (1 atm)** unless a different value is explicitly given. Marks are frequently lost when students forget this assumption.

### 1.2 Humidity — The Formal Definition

**Humidity** is the measure of water vapour present in moist air. It is expressed in several distinct forms:

- **Absolute Humidity (Humidity Ratio / Specific Humidity), $W$** — mass of water vapour per unit mass of **dry air** (kg water / kg dry air).
- **Relative Humidity, $\phi$** — ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapour to the saturation pressure of water vapour at the same dry-bulb temperature (expressed as a percentage).
- **Specific Humidity, $\omega$** — mass of water vapour per unit mass of **moist air**.
- **Dew Point Temperature, $T_{dp}$** — the temperature at which condensation begins when moist air is cooled at constant pressure and constant humidity ratio.
- **Wet-Bulb Temperature, $T_{wb}$** — the dynamic equilibrium temperature reached when a water-wetted thermometer bulb is exposed to an air stream.
- **Degree of Saturation, $\mu$** — ratio of the actual humidity ratio to the humidity ratio at saturation at the same temperature.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Sponge in a Glass Jar" Analogy**
> 
> Imagine dry air as an empty glass jar. The jar has a fixed volume (set by atmospheric pressure and temperature). Now imagine water vapour as a **sponge** that can sit inside the jar. 
> 
> - **Absolute humidity** tells you *how heavy the wet sponge is* (mass of water / mass of jar).
> - **Relative humidity** tells you *how full the sponge is* — at 100%, the sponge cannot absorb any more water; the air is **saturated**. Spilling one more drop causes condensation (this is exactly what happens at the dew point).
> - **Dew point** is the temperature at which you must *cool the jar* so much that the sponge starts to "wrung out" water droplets — i.e., condensation begins.
> - **Wet-bulb temperature** is the temperature the sponge would reach if you continually wiped it with a damp cloth in moving air.

This analogy also explains why **hot desert air at 5% RH still contains more water vapour than cold polar air at 100% RH** — the sponge is bigger when warm, so even a "less full" sponge holds more total water.

### 1.4 Heat Transfer — The Formal Definition

**Heat transfer** is the science of thermal energy transport due to temperature differences, occurring through three fundamental modes:

| Mode | Mechanism | Governing Law |
|------|-----------|---------------|
| **Conduction** | Molecular/electron vibration | Fourier's Law |
| **Convection** | Bulk fluid motion + diffusion | Newton's Law of Cooling |
| **Radiation** | Electromagnetic wave emission | Stefan-Boltzmann Law |

A real engineering problem (e.g., wall heat loss, boiler surface, solar collector) almost always involves **all three modes acting simultaneously**, which is why they are unified under the discipline of heat transfer.

### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy for Heat Transfer

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Three Ways to Heat a Coffee Cup" Analogy**
> 
> Imagine a cold ceramic coffee cup that needs warming:
> 
> 1. **Conduction** — Place the cup on a hot metal plate. Heat travels *through the ceramic wall molecule-by-molecule* (no bulk movement).
> 
> 2. **Convection** — Blow hot air from a hairdryer *across* the cup's outer surface. Heat is carried by the moving fluid itself.
> 
> 3. **Radiation** — Place the cup in front of a glowing infrared heater. Heat arrives as *invisible light waves*, no medium required.
> 
> In real buildings, walls lose heat by **conduction through the brick, convection from the outer surface to wind, and radiation to the cold night sky** — all at once.

---

### 1.6 Why This Topic Matters in Energy Management

In **energy management and audit work**, the three big energy sinks in any conditioned building are:
- **Sensible heating/cooling load** (raising/lowering air temperature).
- **Latent load** (removing/adding moisture — this is the *humidity* part).
- **Ventilation/infiltration load** (bringing in outside air).

A correct understanding of moist-air properties lets the auditor compute the **exact tonnage of refrigeration** required, select the correct HVAC equipment, and recommend energy-saving measures such as **economiser cycles**, **heat recovery wheels**, and **adiabatic cooling**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Moist-Air Properties

The thermodynamic treatment of moist air rests on three pillars:

**Pillar 1: Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures**
The total pressure exerted by a mixture of non-reacting gases equals the sum of the individual partial pressures each gas would exert if it alone occupied the container.

$$p_t = p_a + p_v$$

**Pillar 2: Ideal Gas Equation Applied Per Component**
For each component (dry air and water vapour), the ideal gas equation is independently valid:

$$p_a V = m_a R_a T$$
$$p_v V = m_v R_v T$$

where $R_a = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ and $R_v = 0.4615 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ are the specific gas constants.

**Pillar 3: Conservation of Mass**
The total mass of moist air is the sum of the two component masses:

$$m_t = m_a + m_v$$

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Humidity Ratio Formula

The **humidity ratio** is defined as:

$$W \equiv \frac{m_v}{m_a}$$

Using the ideal gas equation for each component at the same volume $V$ and temperature $T$:

$$m_v = \frac{p_v V}{R_v T} \quad ; \quad m_a = \frac{p_a V}{R_a T}$$

Dividing:

$$W = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{p_v / R_v}{p_a / R_a} = \frac{p_v}{p_a} \cdot \frac{R_a}{R_v}$$

Substituting $R_a / R_v = 0.287 / 0.4615 \approx 0.622$ and $p_a = p_t - p_v$:

$$\boxed{W = 0.622 \cdot \frac{p_v}{p_t - p_v}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The constant **0.622** is the molecular-weight ratio of water (18.015) to dry air (28.97). It is dimensionless and a universal conversion factor for moist-air problems.

---

### 2.3 Relative Humidity — Engineering Interpretation

Relative humidity is **not** a measure of how much water is in the air — it is a measure of **how close the air is to saturation**. Mathematically:

$$\phi = \frac{p_v}{p_g(T)}$$

where $p_g(T)$ is the **saturation pressure of water** at the dry-bulb temperature $T$. This saturation pressure is read from steam tables or approximated by the **Antoine equation**.

Substituting in the humidity ratio formula:

$$W = 0.622 \cdot \frac{\phi \, p_g(T)}{p_t - \phi \, p_g(T)}$$

This shows clearly that **$W$ depends on both $\phi$ and $T$**, but $\phi$ alone does not tell us $W$.

---

### 2.4 Dew Point Temperature — Theoretical Basis

At the dew point, the air becomes saturated at the same pressure and humidity ratio. Therefore the actual vapour pressure equals the saturation pressure at $T_{dp}$:

$$p_v = p_g(T_{dp})$$

This single equation is the operational definition. Since $p_v$ is known, the student consults steam tables **in reverse** to find $T_{dp}$.

---

### 2.5 Wet-Bulb Temperature — Theoretical Basis

The wet-bulb temperature is the steady-state temperature reached when water evaporates into the air stream, driven by the difference between $p_g(T_{wb})$ and $p_v$. At steady state the heat lost by the air equals the heat gained by the evaporating water, yielding the classic psychrometric equation:

$$W = \frac{(2501 - 2.381 \, T_{wb}) \, W_s(T_{wb}) - 1.006 \, (T - T_{wb})}{2501 + 1.805 \, T - 4.186 \, T_{wb}}$$

where $W_s(T_{wb})$ is the saturation humidity ratio at the wet-bulb temperature.

---

### 2.6 Enthalpy of Moist Air

The enthalpy of moist air per kg of dry air is the sum of the dry-air enthalpy and the water-vapour enthalpy:

$$h = h_a + W \cdot h_v = c_{p,a} T + W \left( h_{fg,0} + c_{p,v} T \right)$$

Using $c_{p,a} = 1.005 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $c_{p,v} = 1.88 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, and $h_{fg,0} = 2501 \text{ kJ/kg}$:

$$\boxed{h = 1.005 \, T + W (2501 + 1.88 \, T) \;\; \text{kJ/kg dry air}}$$

The temperature $T$ is in °C.

---

### 2.7 Specific Volume of Moist Air

Per unit mass of dry air:

$$v = \frac{R_a T}{p_a} = \frac{R_a T}{p_t - p_v}$$

where $T$ is in **kelvin**.

---

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Must-Memorise" Equations for the KTU Board Exam**

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Dalton's Law | $p_t = p_a + p_v$ | kPa | Always at $p_t = 101.325$ kPa if unspecified |
| 2 | Humidity Ratio | $W = 0.622 \cdot p_v / (p_t - p_v)$ | kg/kg da | Per kg **dry** air |
| 3 | Relative Humidity | $\phi = p_v / p_g(T)$ | fraction or % | $p_g$ from steam tables |
| 4 | $W$ from $\phi$ | $W = 0.622 \cdot \phi \, p_g / (p_t - \phi \, p_g)$ | kg/kg da | Combines #2 and #3 |
| 5 | Specific Humidity | $\omega = W / (1 + W)$ | kg/kg moist | Per kg moist air |
| 6 | Degree of Saturation | $\mu = W / W_s$ | fraction | At same temperature |
| 7 | Enthalpy | $h = 1.005T + W(2501 + 1.88T)$ | kJ/kg da | $T$ in °C |
| 8 | Specific Volume | $v = R_a T / (p_t - p_v)$ | m³/kg da | $T$ in K, $R_a = 0.287$ |
| 9 | Dew Point | $p_v = p_g(T_{dp})$ | °C | Use steam tables |
| 10 | DBT → $p_g$ | $p_g$ from saturation tables | kPa | Function of $T$ |
| 11 | Conduction (1-D) | $q = -k \, dT/dx$ | W/m² | Fourier's Law |
| 12 | Conduction (Plane Wall) | $q = k A (T_1 - T_2) / L$ | W | Steady state |
| 13 | Convection | $q = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$ | W | Newton's Law |
| 14 | Radiation | $q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ | W | Stefan-Boltzmann |
| 15 | Combined | $q_{tot} = q_{cond} + q_{conv} + q_{rad}$ | W | All modes together |
| 16 | Thermal Resistance | $R = L/(kA)$ for wall; $R = 1/(hA)$ for convection | K/W | Series / parallel |
| 17 | Overall U | $q = U A \, \Delta T$ | W | $U = 1/\Sigma R_i$ |
| 18 | Latent Heat Load | $Q_L = m_a (W_1 - W_2) \cdot h_{fg}$ | kW | Dehumidification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to remember:**
> - $R_a = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K)
> - $R_v = 0.4615$ kJ/(kg·K)
> - $c_{p,a} = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K)
> - $c_{p,v} = 1.88$ kJ/(kg·K)
> - $h_{fg} \approx 2501$ kJ/kg at 0 °C
> - $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴)
> - Molecular-weight ratio = **0.622**

---

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HVAC sizing**: $W$ and $h$ are used to compute the **cooling coil load** $Q = \dot{m}_a (h_1 - h_2)$ and the **heating coil load**.
- **Industrial drying**: knowing $W_{in}$ and $W_{out}$ gives the moisture removed per kg of dry air.
- **Evaporative cooling design**: adiabatic saturation line on the psychrometric chart.
- **Energy audits**: latent + sensible heat = total conditioning load, vital for retrofit decisions.
- **Insulation design**: $U$-value calculations use the combined heat-transfer relations.
- **Solar thermal collectors**: radiation + convection losses are subtracted from absorbed solar gain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Full Psychrometric State from Given DBT and RH

**Problem Statement:**
Atmospheric air is at a dry-bulb temperature of **35 °C** and a relative humidity of **50%**. The site pressure is **101.325 kPa**. Calculate: (i) humidity ratio $W$, (ii) specific humidity $\omega$, (iii) dew point $T_{dp}$, (iv) specific volume $v$, and (v) enthalpy $h$ per kg of dry air.

**Given Data:**
- $T = 35$ °C
- $\phi = 0.50$
- $p_t = 101.325$ kPa

**Step 1 — Look up the saturation pressure at 35 °C.**
From standard steam tables:

$$p_g(35°\text{C}) = 5.628 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the actual vapour pressure.**

$$p_v = \phi \cdot p_g(T) = 0.50 \times 5.628 = 2.814 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the partial pressure of dry air.**

$$p_a = p_t - p_v = 101.325 - 2.814 = 98.511 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the humidity ratio $W$.**

$$W = 0.622 \cdot \frac{p_v}{p_t - p_v} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.814}{101.325 - 2.814}$$

$$W = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.814}{98.511} = 0.622 \times 0.02856 = 0.01776 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

**Step 5 — Compute specific humidity $\omega$.**

$$\omega = \frac{W}{1 + W} = \frac{0.01776}{1 + 0.01776} = \frac{0.01776}{1.01776} = 0.01745 \text{ kg/kg moist air}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the dew point temperature.**
We need the temperature at which $p_g = 2.814$ kPa. From steam tables:
- At 23 °C: $p_g = 2.811$ kPa ≈ 2.814 kPa.

$$T_{dp} \approx 23 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 7 — Compute specific volume $v$.**

$$v = \frac{R_a T_K}{p_a} = \frac{0.287 \times (35 + 273)}{98.511}$$

$$v = \frac{0.287 \times 308}{98.511} = \frac{88.396}{98.511} = 0.8974 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg da}$$

**Step 8 — Compute enthalpy $h$.**

$$h = 1.005 \cdot T + W (2501 + 1.88 \cdot T)$$

$$h = 1.005 \times 35 + 0.01776 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 35)$$

$$h = 35.175 + 0.01776 \times (2501 + 65.8)$$

$$h = 35.175 + 0.01776 \times 2566.8$$

$$h = 35.175 + 45.586 = 80.76 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Property | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Vapour Pressure | $p_v$ | 2.814 | kPa |
| Dry-Air Pressure | $p_a$ | 98.511 | kPa |
| Humidity Ratio | $W$ | 0.01776 | kg/kg da |
| Specific Humidity | $\omega$ | 0.01745 | kg/kg moist |
| Dew Point | $T_{dp}$ | ~23 | °C |
| Specific Volume | $v$ | 0.8974 | m³/kg da |
| Enthalpy | $h$ | 80.76 | kJ/kg da |

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Cooling and Dehumidification Load

**Problem Statement:**
1 kg/s of dry air at **35 °C DBT and 50% RH** enters a cooling coil and leaves at **20 °C DBT and 13.5 °C WBT** (assume this corresponds to 90% RH at 20 °C). Calculate:
(i) Sensible heat removed,
(ii) Latent heat removed,
(iii) Total cooling load, and
(iv) Apparatus Dew Point (ADP).

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_a = 1$ kg/s
- State 1: $T_1 = 35$ °C, $\phi_1 = 50$%, $W_1 = 0.01776$ kg/kg da (from previous derivation), $h_1 = 80.76$ kJ/kg da.
- State 2: $T_2 = 20$ °C, $\phi_2 = 90$%.

**Step 1 — Find $p_g$ at 20 °C.**

$$p_g(20°\text{C}) = 2.339 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $p_v$ at state 2.**

$$p_{v,2} = 0.90 \times 2.339 = 2.105 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $W_2$.**

$$W_2 = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.105}{101.325 - 2.105} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.105}{99.22} = 0.622 \times 0.02122 = 0.01320 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $h_2$.**

$$h_2 = 1.005 \times 20 + 0.01320 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 20)$$

$$h_2 = 20.10 + 0.01320 \times (2501 + 37.6)$$

$$h_2 = 20.10 + 0.01320 \times 2538.6 = 20.10 + 33.51 = 53.61 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

**Step 5 — Sensible heat removed.**

$$Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_1 - T_2) = 1 \times 1.005 \times (35 - 20) = 15.075 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Latent heat removed.**

$$Q_L = \dot{m}_a \cdot (W_1 - W_2) \cdot h_{fg} = 1 \times (0.01776 - 0.01320) \times 2501$$

$$Q_L = 0.00456 \times 2501 = 11.40 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Total cooling load.**

$$Q_T = \dot{m}_a (h_1 - h_2) = 1 \times (80.76 - 53.61) = 27.15 \text{ kW}$$

**Verification:**
$$Q_T = Q_s + Q_L = 15.075 + 11.40 = 26.475 \text{ kW}$$

The small discrepancy (~2%) is due to rounding of intermediate values. Always verify with the enthalpy form when possible — this is a **valuation tip**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Fourier's Law Applied to a Plane Wall

**Problem Statement:**
A brick wall of thickness **230 mm**, thermal conductivity **k = 0.72 W/(m·K)**, has its inner surface at **35 °C** and outer surface at **25 °C**. Find the heat flux through 1 m² of wall.

**Given:**
- $L = 0.230$ m
- $k = 0.72$ W/(m·K)
- $T_1 = 35$ °C, $T_2 = 25$ °C
- $A = 1$ m²

**Step 1 — Fourier's Law in integrated form (1-D steady conduction):**

$$q = \frac{k A (T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$q = \frac{0.72 \times 1 \times (35 - 25)}{0.230}$$

$$q = \frac{0.72 \times 10}{0.230} = \frac{7.20}{0.230} = 31.30 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Heat flux (per unit area):**

$$q'' = \frac{q}{A} = 31.30 \text{ W/m}^2$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Derivation 4 — Composite Wall Heat Transfer

**Problem Statement:**
A composite wall has plaster (k = 0.5 W/(m·K), 12 mm), brick (k = 0.72 W/(m·K), 230 mm), and insulation (k = 0.04 W/(m·K), 50 mm). Inner surface temp 30 °C, outer surface temp 5 °C. Area = 10 m². Find the heat loss and the interface temperatures.

**Given:** Three layers in series, $A = 10$ m².

**Step 1 — Compute thermal resistance per unit area for each layer.**

$$R_{\text{plaster}} = \frac{0.012}{0.5} = 0.024 \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$

$$R_{\text{brick}} = \frac{0.230}{0.72} = 0.3194 \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$

$$R_{\text{insul}} = \frac{0.050}{0.04} = 1.250 \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$

$$R_{\text{total}} = 0.024 + 0.3194 + 1.250 = 1.5934 \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$

**Step 2 — Compute U-value.**

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{1.5934} = 0.6276 \text{ W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute heat flux.**

$$q'' = U \cdot \Delta T = 0.6276 \times (30 - 5) = 0.6276 \times 25 = 15.69 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Total heat loss.**

$$Q = q'' \cdot A = 15.69 \times 10 = 156.9 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Find interface temperatures using resistances in series.**
For layers in series, the temperature drop across each layer is proportional to its resistance.

$$\Delta T_{\text{plaster}} = q'' \cdot R_{\text{plaster}} = 15.69 \times 0.024 = 0.377 \text{ °C}$$

$$T_{\text{plaster-brick}} = 30 - 0.377 = 29.62 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_{\text{brick}} = 15.69 \times 0.3194 = 5.012 \text{ °C}$$

$$T_{\text{brick-insul}} = 29.62 - 5.012 = 24.61 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_{\text{insul}} = 15.69 \times 1.250 = 19.61 \text{ °C}$$

**Verification:** $T_{out} = 24.61 - 19.61 = 5.00$ °C ✓

---

### 3.5 Worked Derivation 5 — Convection + Radiation Combined Loss

**Problem Statement:**
An uninsulated steam pipe (OD = 100 mm, surface temp 200 °C) is in a room at 30 °C. Convection coefficient $h = 10$ W/(m²·K), surface emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.85$. Find the heat loss per metre of pipe.

**Given:**
- $T_s = 200 + 273 = 473$ K
- $T_\infty = 30 + 273 = 303$ K
- $D = 0.1$ m, $L = 1$ m
- $A = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.1 \times 1 = 0.3142$ m²

**Step 1 — Convection loss.**

$$q_{\text{conv}} = h A (T_s - T_\infty) = 10 \times 0.3142 \times (473 - 303)$$

$$q_{\text{conv}} = 10 \times 0.3142 \times 170 = 534.1 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Radiation loss (Stefan-Boltzmann).**

$$q_{\text{rad}} = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$$

$$T_s^4 = 473^4 = 5.008 \times 10^{10}$$
$$T_\infty^4 = 303^4 = 8.437 \times 10^{9}$$

$$T_s^4 - T_\infty^4 = 5.008 \times 10^{10} - 8.437 \times 10^{9} = 4.165 \times 10^{10}$$

$$q_{\text{rad}} = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.3142 \times 4.165 \times 10^{10}$$

$$q_{\text{rad}} = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.309 \times 10^{10}$$

$$q_{\text{rad}} = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 1.309 \times 10^{2} = 631.0 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Total loss.**

$$q_{\text{total}} = q_{\text{conv}} + q_{\text{rad}} = 534.1 + 631.0 = 1165 \text{ W per metre}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Insight: For high-temperature pipes (>150 °C), **radiation loss often exceeds convection loss**. Any energy audit that ignores radiation will underestimate the insulation payback.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for "Which Formula to Use"

| Given | Required | Formula Path |
|-------|----------|--------------|
| DBT, RH | $W$ | $p_v = \phi p_g(T)$; then $W = 0.622 p_v / (p_t - p_v)$ |
| DBT, $W$ | RH | $p_v = W p_t / (0.622 + W)$; then $\phi = p_v / p_g(T)$ |
| DBT, RH | $T_{dp}$ | Compute $p_v$, then find $T$ where $p_g = p_v$ in steam tables |
| DBT, $W$ | $h$ | $h = 1.005 T + W(2501 + 1.88 T)$ |
| $T_1, T_2$ | Through wall $q$ | $q = kA \Delta T / L$ |
| Multi-layer | $Q$ through composite | $Q = A \Delta T / \Sigma R_i$ |
| Surface T, ambient T | Convection $q$ | $q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$ |
| Surface T, ambient T | Radiation $q$ | $q = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$ |

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Classification of Psychrometric Processes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Moist Air Process Selection] --> nodeB{Is moisture added or removed?}
    nodeB -- No change in W --> nodeC[Sensible Process]
    nodeB -- Moisture removed --> nodeD[Latent Process]
    nodeB -- Moisture added --> nodeE[Humidification Process]
    nodeC --> nodeF{Temperature change direction}
    nodeF -- T increases --> nodeG[Heating - h increases, W constant]
    nodeF -- T decreases --> nodeH[Cooling - h decreases, W constant]
    nodeD --> nodeI{Which line on chart?}
    nodeI -- Along saturation --> nodeJ[Dehumidification on cooling coil]
    nodeI -- Below saturation --> nodeK[Chemical dehumidification]
    nodeE --> nodeL{Heat input?}
    nodeL -- No heat --> nodeM[Adiabatic humidification - evaporative cooling]
    nodeL -- With heat --> nodeN[Heating and humidification - steam injection]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Three Modes of Heat Transfer Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Heat Source at Temp Th] --> nodeB[Transfer Mode Selection]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Conduction]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Convection]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Radiation]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Fourier Law q equals k A dT per dx]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Newton Law q equals h A Ts minus Tinf]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Stefan Boltzmann q equals epsilon sigma A T to power 4]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Combined q goes to Heat Sink at Tc]
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Energy Balance - First Law]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Psychrometric State Point Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Given Inputs] --> nodeB{Which combination?}
    nodeB -- DBT and RH --> nodeC[Compute pv equals phi times pg]
    nodeB -- DBT and W --> nodeD[Compute pv from W]
    nodeB -- DBT and Tdp --> nodeE[Look up pg at Tdp equals pv]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Compute W equals 0.622 pv over pt minus pv]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Compute specific volume v]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Compute enthalpy h]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Compute degree of saturation mu]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Final State Point]
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Energy Audit Pathway Using Heat Transfer Principles

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Energy Audit Start] --> nodeB[Identify Heat Loss Paths]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Conduction through walls roof floor]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Convection from openings cracks]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Radiation through uninsulated surfaces]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Latent losses due to moisture infiltration]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Compute U value and heat flux]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Apply Newton law with empirical h]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Apply Stefan Boltzmann law]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Apply humidity ratio change formula]
    nodeG --> nodeK[Total Heat Loss Estimation]
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL[Recommend Insulation Schedule]
    nodeK --> nodeM[Estimate Energy Savings Payback]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Sensible vs Latent Heat Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[HVAC Load Calculation] --> nodeB{Is the moisture content changing?}
    nodeB -- No --> nodeC[Sensible Load Only]
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeD[Latent Load Involved]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Compute Qs equals m dot cp delta T]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Compute QL equals m dot delta W hfg]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Total Load equals Qs plus QL]
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Select Equipment Tonnage]
```
