## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Conceptual)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**Define the following terms with one example each:**
(i) Absolute humidity, (ii) Relative humidity, (iii) Dew point temperature.

**Model Answer:**
- **(i) Absolute Humidity (Humidity Ratio, $W$)**: Mass of water vapour in kg per kg of dry air. *Example: W = 0.0176 kg/kg da for 35 °C, 50% RH air.*
- **(ii) Relative Humidity ($\phi$)**: Ratio of actual partial pressure of water vapour to saturation pressure at the same DBT, expressed as a percentage. *Example: $\phi = 50\%$ means the air holds half the moisture it could at saturation.*
- **(iii) Dew Point Temperature ($T_{dp}$)**: Temperature at which condensation begins when air is cooled at constant pressure and constant $W$. *Example: Air at 35 °C and 50% RH has $T_{dp} ≈ 23$ °C.*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**State Fourier's law of heat conduction, Newton's law of cooling, and Stefan-Boltzmann law. Mention the SI unit of thermal conductivity.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Fourier's Law**: Heat flux is proportional to the temperature gradient. $q'' = -k \, dT/dx$. The negative sign indicates heat flows from hot to cold.
- **Newton's Law of Cooling**: Heat transfer rate by convection is $q = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$ where $h$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient.
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**: Radiative heat transfer between surfaces is $q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ (for absolute temperature $T$ in K).
- **SI unit of $k$**: W/(m·K).

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]`**

**(a)** Moist air at a dry-bulb temperature of **38 °C** and relative humidity of **60%** enters an air-handling unit. The air passes through a cooling coil and leaves at **22 °C DBT** with a relative humidity of **85%**. Calculate:
1. Humidity ratio at inlet and outlet (4 marks)
2. Enthalpy at inlet and outlet (3 marks)

**(b)** If **2.5 m³/s** of air at the inlet conditions is being handled, find:
1. Mass flow rate of dry air (2 marks)
2. Total cooling load in kW (3 marks)
3. Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) — given that the cooling coil bypass factor (BPF) is 0.18, and ADP can be assumed at 90% RH (2 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)**

*Given:* $T_1 = 38$ °C, $\phi_1 = 60\%$, $T_2 = 22$ °C, $\phi_2 = 85\%$, $p_t = 101.325$ kPa.

**Step 1 — Look up saturation pressures:**
$p_g(38°\text{C}) = 6.633$ kPa (from steam tables).
$p_g(22°\text{C}) = 2.645$ kPa.

**Step 2 — Vapour pressures:**

$$p_{v,1} = 0.60 \times 6.633 = 3.980 \text{ kPa}$$
$$p_{v,2} = 0.85 \times 2.645 = 2.248 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Humidity ratios:**

$$W_1 = 0.622 \times \frac{3.980}{101.325 - 3.980} = 0.622 \times \frac{3.980}{97.345} = 0.02543 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

$$W_2 = 0.622 \times \frac{2.248}{101.325 - 2.248} = 0.622 \times \frac{2.248}{99.077} = 0.01411 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

**Step 4 — Enthalpies:**

$$h_1 = 1.005 \times 38 + 0.02543 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 38)$$
$$h_1 = 38.19 + 0.02543 \times (2501 + 71.44) = 38.19 + 0.02543 \times 2572.44 = 38.19 + 65.42 = 103.61 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

$$h_2 = 1.005 \times 22 + 0.01411 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 22)$$
$$h_2 = 22.11 + 0.01411 \times (2501 + 41.36) = 22.11 + 0.01411 \times 2542.36 = 22.11 + 35.87 = 57.98 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

**(b)**

**Step 5 — Specific volume at inlet.**

$$v_1 = \frac{R_a T_{K,1}}{p_{a,1}} = \frac{0.287 \times (38 + 273)}{97.345} = \frac{0.287 \times 311}{97.345} = \frac{89.257}{97.345} = 0.9170 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg da}$$

**Step 6 — Mass flow rate of dry air.**

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{\dot{V}}{v_1} = \frac{2.5}{0.9170} = 2.726 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 7 — Total cooling load.**

$$Q = \dot{m}_a (h_1 - h_2) = 2.726 \times (103.61 - 57.98) = 2.726 \times 45.63 = 124.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8 — Apparatus Dew Point.**
For a cooling coil with bypass factor BPF = 0.18:

$$T_{ADP} = T_2 - \text{BPF} \times (T_2 - T_1) = 22 - 0.18 \times (22 - 38) = 22 + 2.88 = 24.88 \text{ °C}$$

ADP is read at the saturation point on the coil line: $T_{ADP} \approx 24.9$ °C (at ~90% RH on saturation curve).

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks**
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]`**

**(a)** A composite wall consists of three layers:
- **Plaster**: $k_1 = 0.5$ W/(m·K), thickness $L_1 = 20$ mm
- **Brick**: $k_2 = 0.72$ W/(m·K), thickness $L_2 = 230$ mm
- **Foam insulation**: $k_3 = 0.04$ W/(m·K), thickness $L_3 = 50$ mm

The inner surface temperature is 35 °C and the outer surface temperature is 8 °C. For an area of **12 m²**, calculate:
1. Total thermal resistance (2 marks)
2. Overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ (2 marks)
3. Heat loss per hour through the wall (3 marks)

**(b)** A hot horizontal pipe of outer diameter **120 mm** and surface temperature **180 °C** is exposed to ambient air at **30 °C**. The combined convection and radiation heat transfer coefficient is **$h_{tot} = 18$ W/(m²·K)**. For a **5-metre** length of pipe, calculate:
1. Heat loss per metre due to combined convection and radiation (3 marks)
2. Annual energy loss in kWh, assuming continuous operation (2 marks)
3. Annual cost of energy loss at ₹8 per kWh (2 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)**

**Step 1 — Resistances per unit area:**

$$R_1 = \frac{0.020}{0.5} = 0.040 \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$
$$R_2 = \frac{0.230}{0.72} = 0.3194 \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$
$$R_3 = \frac{0.050}{0.04} = 1.250 \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$

$$R_{\text{tot}} = 0.040 + 0.3194 + 1.250 = 1.6094 \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$

**Step 2 — U-value:**

$$U = \frac{1}{1.6094} = 0.6213 \text{ W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K)}$$

**Step 3 — Heat loss:**

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.6213 \times 12 \times (35 - 8) = 0.6213 \times 12 \times 27 = 201.3 \text{ W}$$

$$Q_{\text{hour}} = 201.3 \times 3600 = 724{,}680 \text{ J/h} = 0.2013 \text{ kWh/h}$$

**(b)**

**Given:**
- $D = 0.120$ m
- $T_s = 180$ °C, $T_\infty = 30$ °C
- $L = 5$ m
- $h_{tot} = 18$ W/(m²·K)

**Step 1 — Surface area.**

$$A = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.120 \times 5 = 1.885 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Heat loss per metre.**
First total heat loss for 5 m:

$$Q_{5m} = h_{tot} \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) = 18 \times 1.885 \times (180 - 30)$$

$$Q_{5m} = 18 \times 1.885 \times 150 = 5089.5 \text{ W}$$

Per metre:

$$q_{1m} = \frac{5089.5}{5} = 1017.9 \text{ W/m}$$

**Step 3 — Annual energy loss (8760 hours):**

$$Q_{\text{annual}} = 5089.5 \text{ W} \times 8760 \text{ h} = 44{,}584{,}020 \text{ Wh} = 44{,}584 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Annual cost:**

$$\text{Cost} = 44{,}584 \times 8 = \text{₹ } 3{,}56{,}672$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in specific volume or radiation calculations. The vapour-pressure and $p_g$ formulas use °C; specific volume and Stefan-Boltzmann use **K**. Marks are lost for unit inconsistency.
> 
> 2. **Using 1.005 + 1.88W** as enthalpy — WRONG. The correct form is $h = 1.005T + W(2501 + 1.88T)$. Many students miss the latent term $2501 W$.
> 
> 3. **Using $R_a / R_v = 0.622$ but mixing with $p_a$ instead of $p_t - p_v$** in the denominator of $W$. Always recheck: $W = 0.622 \cdot p_v / (p_t - p_v)$, NOT $0.622 \cdot p_v / p_a$ (although numerically equal, the latter is harder to read off correctly when $p_v$ is large).
> 
> 4. **Neglecting radiation in heat-loss problems** for surfaces above 100 °C. For an exam question explicitly giving you a combined $h_{tot}$, use it directly; otherwise compute convection and radiation separately.
> 
> 5. **Apparatus Dew Point confusion**: BPF = (T₂ − T_ADP) / (T₁ − T_ADP), so $T_{ADP} = T_2 - \text{BPF}(T_2 - T_1)$ when $T_1 > T_2$ (cooling). 
> 
> 6. **Total pressure assumption**: If the problem states a non-standard pressure (e.g., Kochi at certain altitudes = 100.5 kPa), use that; otherwise, default **101.325 kPa**.
> 
> 7. **Latent heat value**: Use $h_{fg} = 2501$ kJ/kg only when referencing 0 °C; at higher coil temperatures (e.g., 10 °C ADP), use $h_{fg} ≈ 2477$ kJ/kg. For KTU problems, 2501 is acceptable unless specified.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Board Exam**

- **Moist air = dry air + water vapour**, treated as ideal gas mixture obeying Dalton's Law.
- **Humidity ratio $W$** is per kg **dry air**, not per kg moist air. Use the constant **0.622**.
- **Relative humidity $\phi$** is a ratio of pressures — a measure of "closeness to saturation," not water content.
- **Dew point** = temperature where $p_v = p_g$. Look up steam tables in reverse.
- **Wet-bulb temperature** = dynamic equilibrium from adiabatic saturation.
- **Enthalpy $h = 1.005T + W(2501 + 1.88T)$** in kJ/kg dry air, $T$ in °C.
- **Specific volume $v = R_a T_K / (p_t - p_v)$** in m³/kg dry air.
- **Fourier's Law**: $q = -k A \, dT/dx$; integrated form $q = kA \Delta T / L$ for plane wall.
- **Newton's Law of Cooling**: $q = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$.
- **Stefan-Boltzmann**: $q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ with $T$ in K, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴).
- **Composite walls** use series thermal resistance: $U = 1/\Sigma R_i$.
- **Cooling coil load**: $Q = \dot{m}_a (h_1 - h_2)$ = sensible + latent.
- **ADP** = apparatus dew point, computed from BPF and inlet/outlet DBT.
- **Energy audit applications**: insulation U-value, pipe heat loss, latent load from moisture removal.
- **Constants**: $R_a = 0.287$, $R_v = 0.4615$, $c_{p,a} = 1.005$, $c_{p,v} = 1.88$, $h_{fg,0} = 2501$, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$.
- **Atmospheric pressure default**: **101.325 kPa** unless stated.
- **Always specify the reference**: kg dry air, kJ/kg da, m³/kg da — never omit the "per kg dry air."

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Thermal Performance Evaluation?

**Thermal Performance Evaluation** is the systematic quantitative assessment of energy transfer (in the form of heat) across a building envelope, industrial furnace, insulated pipeline, or any thermodynamic system bounded by a hot and cold region. The primary purpose is to determine the **rate of heat loss (in winter/cooling season)** or **rate of heat gain (in summer/heating season)** so that energy conservation measures can be designed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Thermal Performance Evaluation is the analytical procedure of computing the energy exchange (Watts or kW) across the surfaces of a system by combining the effects of conduction, convection, radiation, infiltration, ventilation, and internal/solar loads, expressed through a single overall heat transfer coefficient (U-value in W/m²K).*

## 1.2 Heat Loss vs Heat Gain — The Fundamental Distinction

| Term | Direction of Heat Flow | Driving Potential | Typical Season |
|------|------------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Heat Loss** | Inside → Outside | $\Delta T = T_{in} - T_{out} > 0$ | Winter / Cold climates |
| **Heat Gain** | Outside → Inside | $\Delta T = T_{out} - T_{in} > 0$ + Solar Radiation | Summer / Hot climates |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy 🍵

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Thermos Flask vs. A Normal Steel Glass:**
> Imagine pouring hot tea into two cups — a **thermos flask (vacuum insulated, U ≈ 0.5 W/m²K)** and an **ordinary steel glass (U ≈ 50 W/m²K)**. After 30 minutes, the tea in the steel glass becomes cold because heat escapes rapidly (high heat loss), while the tea in the thermos remains hot (low heat loss).
>
> **In a building:** The walls, roof, and windows behave like the cup walls. The **U-value** tells us *how fast* the building loses its "heat tea" to the outside — a low U-value is a thermos, a high U-value is a steel glass.

## 1.4 Modes of Heat Transfer Involved

The total thermal load on a building is the **algebraic sum** of all energy exchange mechanisms:

$$
Q_{total} = \pm Q_{conduction} \pm Q_{convection} \pm Q_{radiation} \pm Q_{infiltration} \pm Q_{ventilation} \pm Q_{internal} \pm Q_{solar}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard SI Units Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - Heat flow rate ($Q$): **Watts (W)** or **kW**
> - Area ($A$): **m²**
> - Temperature difference ($\Delta T$): **Kelvin (K)** or **°C** (numerically identical for differences)
> - Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$): **W/m²K**
> - Thermal Resistance ($R$): **m²K/W**

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fourier's Law — The Foundation

Heat conduction through a plane surface is governed by **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction**:

$$
Q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{L} \quad \text{[Watts]}
$$

Where:
- $k$ = Thermal conductivity of material (W/mK)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area perpendicular to heat flow (m²)
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature difference across the material (K)
- $L$ = Thickness of material (m)

## 2.2 Thermal Resistance Concept (Electrical Analogy)

The thermal resistance of a single homogeneous layer is defined as:

$$
R = \frac{L}{k} \quad \text{[m²K/W]}
$$

For a **composite wall** consisting of multiple layers in **series**, the resistances add up — exactly like electrical resistors in series:

$$
R_{total} = R_{si} + R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \dots + R_{so}
$$

Where $R_{si}$ and $R_{so}$ are the **internal** and **external surface resistances** (convective + radiative combined).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Surface Resistances Matter (KTU Board Favourite):**
> The KTU examiner often tests whether students include $R_{si} \approx 0.13$ m²K/W and $R_{so} \approx 0.04$ m²K/W (for still air, vertical wall, winter conditions as per IS 3797 / ASHRAE). **Forgetting these is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.**

## 2.3 Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U-value)

The U-value is the **reciprocal of total thermal resistance**:

$$
U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{R_{si} + \sum \frac{L_i}{k_i} + R_{so}} \quad \text{[W/m²K]}
$$

The **simplified heat loss equation** used universally in KTU problems is:

$$
\boxed{Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \quad \text{[Watts]}}
$$

## 2.4 Components of Heat Loss in a Building

A complete heat loss audit must account for **five** distinct loss components:

### A. Transmission Loss (Conduction through envelope)
$$
Q_{trans} = U_w A_w \Delta T + U_r A_r \Delta T + U_f A_f \Delta T + U_g A_g \Delta T
$$
(Walls, Roof, Floor, Glass respectively)

### B. Infiltration Loss (Uncontrolled air leakage)
$$
Q_{inf} = \dot{V} \cdot \rho_a \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 0.33 \times n \times V \times \Delta T
$$

Where:
- $n$ = Air changes per hour (ACH) — typically **0.5 to 1.5** for modern homes, **2 to 5** for old leaky buildings
- $V$ = Volume of conditioned space (m³)
- $\rho_a \cdot c_p$ = Volumetric heat capacity of air ≈ **1200 J/m³K**

### C. Ventilation Loss (Controlled fresh air supply)
$$
Q_{vent} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

### D. Radiation Loss (from opaque surfaces at night)
Accounted within $R_{so}$ for opaque surfaces; for windows, via **Linear Heat Transmittance ($\psi$)** at edges.

## 2.5 Components of Heat Gain in a Building

### A. Conduction Heat Gain through Opaque Surfaces
$$
Q_{cond,wall} = U_w \cdot A_w \cdot CLTD
$$
Where **CLTD = Cooling Load Temperature Difference** (combines sol-air temperature and time lag effect).

### B. Solar Heat Gain through Glazing (Fenestration)
$$
Q_{solar} = A_g \cdot SHGC \cdot I_{solar} + U_g \cdot A_g \cdot \Delta T
$$

Where:
- $SHGC$ = Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (0 to 1)
- $I_{solar}$ = Incident solar radiation on the surface (W/m²)

### C. Internal Heat Gains
| Source | Typical Heat Output |
|--------|---------------------|
| Occupant (sedentary) | **75 W** per person |
| Occupant (active work) | **150–200 W** per person |
| Lighting (fluorescent) | **20–40 W/m²** of lit area |
| Computers / Office equipment | **100–200 W** per unit |
| Cooking (stove) | **1000–3000 W** |

## 2.6 📊 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = U A \Delta T$ | General transmission heat loss/gain | W |
| 2 | $U = \dfrac{1}{R_{total}}$ | Conversion: resistance ↔ transmittance | W/m²K |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{L}{k}$ | Resistance of single material layer | m²K/W |
| 4 | $R_{total} = \sum R_i$ (series) | Composite wall resistance | m²K/W |
| 5 | $Q_{inf} = 0.33 \, n \, V \, \Delta T$ | Infiltration loss | W |
| 6 | $q = \dfrac{Q}{A} = U \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat flux per unit area | W/m² |
| 7 | $Q_{solar} = A_g \cdot SHGC \cdot I$ | Solar gain through glass | W |
| 8 | $t_{lag} = \dfrac{\text{time of max indoor T} - \text{time of max outdoor T}}{\text{Time}}$ | Thermal lag of wall | hours |
| 9 | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{2k}{\rho c_p \omega}}$ | Decrement factor related depth | m |
| 10 | $f = e^{-L\sqrt{\pi \rho c / (86400 \, k)}}$ | Decrement factor (KTU standard) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility (Real-World):**
> - Used in **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code)** compliance audits in India.
> - Forms the basis of **HVAC load estimation** in M&E consultancy.
> - Essential for **Green Building Rating (GRIHA / LEED-India)** documentation.

# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Overall U-Value for a Composite Wall

Consider a composite wall made of **three layers** in series (e.g., plaster + brick + insulation) with surface films on both sides.

**Setup:**
- Layer 1 (internal plaster): $L_1$, $k_1$
- Layer 2 (brick): $L_2$, $k_2$
- Layer 3 (insulation): $L_3$, $k_3$
- Internal surface film: $R_{si}$
- External surface film: $R_{so}$

**Step 1:** Apply Fourier's law to each layer. In **steady state**, the heat flux $q$ (W/m²) is constant through every layer:

$$
q = \frac{T_i - T_1}{R_{si}} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{L_1/k_1} = \frac{T_2 - T_3}{L_2/k_2} = \frac{T_3 - T_4}{L_3/k_3} = \frac{T_4 - T_o}{R_{so}}
$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange each term to express temperature drops as a function of $q$:

$$
T_i - T_1 = q \cdot R_{si}
$$
$$
T_1 - T_2 = q \cdot \frac{L_1}{k_1}
$$
$$
T_2 - T_3 = q \cdot \frac{L_2}{k_2}
$$
$$
T_3 - T_4 = q \cdot \frac{L_3}{k_3}
$$
$$
T_4 - T_o = q \cdot R_{so}
$$

**Step 3:** Sum all five temperature drops. The intermediate temperatures cancel out, leaving only the overall temperature difference:

$$
T_i - T_o = q \left( R_{si} + \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3} + R_{so} \right)
$$

**Step 4:** By definition, the overall heat flux is $q = U \cdot (T_i - T_o)$. Substituting:

$$
T_i - T_o = U (T_i - T_o) \left( R_{si} + \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3} + R_{so} \right)
$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $(T_i - T_o)$ from both sides (valid when $T_i \neq T_o$):

$$
\boxed{U = \frac{1}{R_{si} + \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3} + R_{so}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalisation:** For a wall of $n$ layers, the general U-value formula is:
> $$U = \frac{1}{R_{si} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{k_i} + R_{so}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Infiltration Heat Loss Coefficient ($0.33 \, n \, V$)

The volumetric heat capacity of air at standard conditions ($20°C$) is:

$$
\rho_a c_p = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 1005 \text{ J/kgK} = 1206 \text{ J/m}^3\text{K} \approx 1200 \text{ J/m}^3\text{K}
$$

If the air changes $n$ times per hour, the volumetric flow rate of infiltrated air is:

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{n \cdot V}{3600} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/s]}
$$

The heat loss rate is:

$$
Q_{inf} = \dot{V} \cdot \rho_a c_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Substitute** $\dot{V}$:

$$
Q_{inf} = \frac{n \cdot V}{3600} \times 1200 \times \Delta T
$$

**Simplify** the numerical factor:

$$
\frac{1200}{3600} = \frac{1}{3} = 0.333
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{Q_{inf} = 0.33 \, n \, V \, \Delta T \quad \text{[Watts]}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Problem:** A 4 m × 3 m × 3 m room has a 200 mm brick wall ($k = 0.7$ W/mK) with 12 mm internal plaster ($k = 0.5$ W/mK) and a flat concrete roof ($k = 1.4$ W/mK) of thickness 100 mm. One wall has a window of 1.5 m × 1.2 m with single glass ($U_g = 5.8$ W/m²K). Inside temperature is 22°C; outside is 8°C. ACH = 1.0. Calculate total heat loss.

### Step 1: Compute U-values of opaque components

**Walls (4 walls, total window area must be excluded):**
- $L_{brick}/k_{brick} = 0.200/0.7 = 0.2857$ m²K/W
- $L_{plaster}/k_{plaster} = 0.012/0.5 = 0.0240$ m²K/W
- Total $R_{layer} = 0.3097$ m²K/W
- Add $R_{si} + R_{so} = 0.13 + 0.04 = 0.17$ m²K/W
- $R_{wall} = 0.3097 + 0.17 = 0.4797$ m²K/W

$$
U_{wall} = \frac{1}{0.4797} = 2.085 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

**Roof:**
- $L/k = 0.100/1.4 = 0.0714$ m²K/W
- $R_{roof} = 0.0714 + 0.17 = 0.2414$ m²K/W

$$
U_{roof} = \frac{1}{0.2414} = 4.143 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Areas

- Total wall area (gross) = $2(4 \times 3) + 2(3 \times 3) = 24 + 18 = 42$ m²
- Window area = $1.5 \times 1.2 = 1.8$ m²
- Net wall area = $42 - 1.8 = 40.2$ m²
- Roof area = $4 \times 3 = 12$ m²

### Step 3: Compute $\Delta T$

$$
\Delta T = T_{in} - T_{out} = 22 - 8 = 14 \text{ K}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Heat Loss Components

**Walls:**
$$
Q_{wall} = 2.085 \times 40.2 \times 14 = 1173.4 \text{ W}
$$

**Roof:**
$$
Q_{roof} = 4.143 \times 12 \times 14 = 696.0 \text{ W}
$$

**Window:**
$$
Q_{glass} = 5.8 \times 1.8 \times 14 = 146.2 \text{ W}
$$

**Infiltration:**
$$
Q_{inf} = 0.33 \times 1.0 \times (4 \times 3 \times 3) \times 14 = 0.33 \times 36 \times 14 = 166.3 \text{ W}
$$

### Step 5: Total Heat Loss

$$
Q_{total} = 1173.4 + 696.0 + 146.2 + 166.3 = 2181.9 \text{ W} \approx 2.18 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Roof accounts for **~32%** of losses despite being the smallest area — due to its high U-value. This is why KTU/ASHRAE standards mandate **insulation of roofs as the highest priority**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Heat Loss vs Heat Gain (Side-by-Side)

| Parameter | Heat Loss Calculation (Winter) | Heat Gain Calculation (Summer) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Primary equation** | $Q = U A (T_{in} - T_{out})$ | $Q = U A (T_{out} - T_{in}) + Q_{solar}$ |
| **Key area components** | Walls, roof, floor, glass | Walls, roof, glass, skylights |
| **Solar contribution** | Negligible (beneficial) | Dominant (uses SHGC) |
| **Infiltration** | Cold air entry = loss | Hot air entry = gain |
| **Internal gains** | Not added (heating season) | Must be added (lights, occupants) |
| **HVAC equipment** | Heater capacity sized | AC capacity sized |
| **Direction of $\Delta T$** | Always positive | May be negative on west walls after sunset |
| **Recommended U-value** | ECBC: ≤ 0.4 W/m²K (5-star) | ECBC: ≤ 0.4 W/m²K (5-star) |

# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the term "Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U-value)" and state its SI unit. Mention two factors on which the U-value of a wall depends.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U-value)** is defined as the rate of heat flow (in Watts) passing through one square metre of a building element for every 1 K (or 1 °C) difference in temperature between the inside and outside air, under steady-state conditions.

$$
U = \frac{1}{R_{si} + \sum \frac{L_i}{k_i} + R_{so}} \quad \text{[W/m}^2\text{K]}
$$

**SI Unit:** W/m²K

**Factors on which U-value depends:**
1. **Thermal conductivity ($k$)** of each material layer in the wall.
2. **Thickness ($L$)** of each material layer.
3. **Internal and external surface film resistances** ($R_{si}$, $R_{so}$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "U-value depends on area." This is **wrong** — U-value is an *intensive* property (per m²). Mark deduction: **-1 mark**.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supposed]**
**What is meant by the term "infiltration" in building heat loss estimation? Write the standard formula used to compute infiltration heat loss and define each term.**

**Model Answer:**

**Infiltration** is the uncontrolled leakage of outside air into a building through cracks, openings, doors, and gaps in the building envelope, driven by wind pressure and the **stack effect** (buoyancy due to temperature difference).

**Standard Formula:**

$$
Q_{inf} = 0.33 \, n \, V \, \Delta T
$$

Where:
- $Q_{inf}$ = Heat loss due to infiltration (W)
- $n$ = Air changes per hour (ACH) — dimensionless
- $V$ = Volume of the conditioned space (m³)
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature difference between inside and outside (K)
- $0.33$ = Numerical constant arising from $\rho_a c_p / 3600$ for air

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

---

### **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Model]**

**(a)** A composite wall of a refrigerated warehouse consists of the following layers (from inside to outside): 10 mm cement plaster, 200 mm brick, 50 mm cork insulation, and 20 mm external plaster. The thermal conductivities are: $k_{plaster} = 0.5$ W/mK, $k_{brick} = 0.7$ W/mK, $k_{cork} = 0.04$ W/mK. Inside temperature is 4°C; outside is 38°C. The wall has dimensions 10 m × 4 m. Take $R_{si} = 0.13$ m²K/W, $R_{so} = 0.04$ m²K/W. Compute the **U-value** of the wall and the **total heat gain** into the warehouse. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the difference between **steady-state** and **periodic** heat flow. Define **time lag** and **decrement factor** with reference to heat gain through roofs. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a): U-Value and Heat Gain Calculation**

**Step 1: Compute resistance of each layer**

$$
R_{plaster,in} = \frac{0.010}{0.5} = 0.0200 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
R_{brick} = \frac{0.200}{0.7} = 0.2857 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
R_{cork} = \frac{0.050}{0.04} = 1.2500 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
R_{plaster,out} = \frac{0.020}{0.5} = 0.0400 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

**Step 2: Sum all resistances in series**

$$
R_{total} = R_{si} + R_{plaster,in} + R_{brick} + R_{cork} + R_{plaster,out} + R_{so}
$$

$$
R_{total} = 0.13 + 0.0200 + 0.2857 + 1.2500 + 0.0400 + 0.04
$$

$$
R_{total} = 1.7657 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

**Step 3: Compute U-value**

$$
U = \frac{1}{1.7657} = 0.5664 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

**Step 4: Compute heat gain**

Area $A = 10 \times 4 = 40$ m²
Temperature difference $\Delta T = 38 - 4 = 34$ K

$$
Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.5664 \times 40 \times 34 = 770.3 \text{ W}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $U = 0.566$ W/m²K; $Q_{gain} = 770.3$ W $\approx 0.77$ kW

---

#### **Solution (b): Steady-State vs Periodic Heat Flow**

**Steady-State Heat Flow:**
- Temperature at every point in the wall does **not change with time**.
- Governed purely by Fourier's law: $Q = \frac{kA\Delta T}{L}$ or $Q = U A \Delta T$.
- Used for **winter design heat loss** calculations (constant indoor & outdoor temperatures assumed).
- Simple, conservative estimate.

**Periodic Heat Flow:**
- Outdoor temperature varies cyclically (day-night, seasonal).
- Heat wave penetrates the wall with **time lag** and is **attenuated (decrement)**.
- Used for **summer heat gain** through roofs and exposed walls.

**Time Lag ($\phi$):**
It is the time difference between the **peak outdoor temperature** and the **peak indoor surface temperature** of a building element.

$$
\phi = t_{T_{in,max}} - t_{T_{out,max}} \quad \text{[hours]}
$$

Higher thermal mass → longer time lag → better comfort (heat arrives indoors at night when ventilation can remove it).

**Decrement Factor ($\eta$):**
It is the ratio of the **amplitude of indoor surface temperature variation** to the **amplitude of outdoor air temperature variation**.

$$
\eta = \frac{(T_{in,max} - T_{in,min})}{(T_{out,max} - T_{out,min})}
$$

A **lower decrement factor** is desirable — it means the indoor temperature swing is dampened. Heavy walls (stone, concrete) have low decrement factors.

---

### **Question B [14 Marks] [Internal Choice – KTU Model]**

**(a)** A building has a gross wall area of 200 m², of which 25 m² is occupied by single-glazed windows ($U = 5.8$ W/m²K). The opaque wall is a 230 mm brick wall plastered on both sides ($k_{brick} = 0.7$ W/mK, $k_{plaster} = 0.5$ W/mK, plaster thickness = 12 mm each side). The roof is a 100 mm concrete slab ($k = 1.4$ W/mK). The floor is uninsulated ($U_f = 2.5$ W/m²K, area 100 m²). Indoor temperature = 24°C; outdoor = 14°C. The volume of the building is 600 m³, with ACH = 1.5. Calculate the **total heat loss** of the building in kW. Use $R_{si} = 0.13$ and $R_{so} = 0.04$ m²K/W. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A south-facing vertical window of area 3 m² receives solar radiation of 500 W/m². The SHGC of the glass is 0.55. The U-value of the glass is 3.0 W/m²K. The outdoor temperature is 32°C and indoor is 24°C. Calculate the **total instantaneous heat gain** through this window. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a): Total Building Heat Loss**

**Step 1: U-value of opaque wall**

$$
R_{brick} = \frac{0.230}{0.7} = 0.3286 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
R_{plaster} = 2 \times \frac{0.012}{0.5} = 0.0480 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
R_{total,wall} = 0.13 + 0.0480 + 0.3286 + 0.04 = 0.5466 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
U_{wall} = \frac{1}{0.5466} = 1.829 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

**Step 2: U-value of roof**

$$
R_{roof} = 0.13 + \frac{0.100}{1.4} + 0.04 = 0.13 + 0.0714 + 0.04 = 0.2414 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
U_{roof} = \frac{1}{0.2414} = 4.143 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

Assume roof area = 100 m² (same as floor).

**Step 3: Component heat losses**

- $\Delta T = 24 - 14 = 10$ K
- Opaque wall area = $200 - 25 = 175$ m²

$$
Q_{wall} = 1.829 \times 175 \times 10 = 3200.75 \text{ W}
$$

$$
Q_{glass} = 5.8 \times 25 \times 10 = 1450.0 \text{ W}
$$

$$
Q_{roof} = 4.143 \times 100 \times 10 = 4143.0 \text{ W}
$$

$$
Q_{floor} = 2.5 \times 100 \times 10 = 2500.0 \text{ W}
$$

$$
Q_{inf} = 0.33 \times 1.5 \times 600 \times 10 = 2970.0 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4: Total**

$$
Q_{total} = 3200.75 + 1450.0 + 4143.0 + 2500.0 + 2970.0 = 14263.75 \text{ W}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $Q_{total} \approx 14.26$ kW

---

#### **Solution (b): Total Window Heat Gain**

**Step 1: Solar transmitted component**

$$
Q_{solar} = A \cdot SHGC \cdot I = 3 \times 0.55 \times 500 = 825 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2: Conduction component (outdoor → indoor)**

$$
Q_{cond} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 3.0 \times 3 \times (32 - 24) = 3.0 \times 3 \times 8 = 72 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3: Total**

$$
Q_{total} = 825 + 72 = 897 \text{ W}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $Q_{total} = 897$ W $\approx 0.9$ kW

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Solar radiation accounts for **92%** of the heat gain through the window. This is why window shading, low-SHGC films, and external shading devices are the most effective energy conservation measures in tropical Indian climates.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting surface resistances ($R_{si}$, $R_{so}$):** Carries a penalty of **2 marks** in 14-mark problems.
> 2. **Using area in cm² or mm²:** Always convert to **m²** before applying $Q = U A \Delta T$. Board examiners are strict on unit consistency.
> 3. **Mixing up $k$ and $U$:** $k$ is a *material* property; $U$ is a *wall assembly* property. Writing $Q = kA\Delta T/L$ for a composite wall is **wrong** unless it is a single homogeneous layer.
> 4. **Neglecting infiltration:** Some students compute only transmission loss. KTU mandates inclusion of infiltration in **every** heat loss problem unless explicitly stated otherwise.
> 5. **Sign convention errors in heat gain problems:** Always write $\Delta T = T_{out} - T_{in}$ (positive) for heat gain. Reversing the sign gives a negative heat flow, which is logically inconsistent.
> 6. **No unit final answer:** Always report the final answer in **Watts or kW** with the unit written. Missing unit = **-0.5 to -1 mark** at KTU.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary):**
> - ✅ **Master formula:** $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ — works for both loss and gain.
> - ✅ **U-value formula:** $U = \frac{1}{R_{si} + \sum L_i/k_i + R_{so}}$
> - ✅ **Infiltration:** $Q_{inf} = 0.33 \, n \, V \, \Delta T$ (constant 0.33 comes from $\rho_a c_p / 3600$)
> - ✅ **Standard surface resistances:** $R_{si} = 0.13$, $R_{so} = 0.04$ m²K/W (winter, vertical wall, still air — verify for roof/wind conditions)
> - ✅ **SHGC × Area × Solar intensity** = dominant term for window heat gain.
> - ✅ **Time lag** = delay in heat reaching indoors; **Decrement factor** = reduction in amplitude. Both are critical for *periodic* (summer) heat flow analysis.
> - ✅ **Units always SI:** W, m², K, W/m²K, m²K/W. Convert °C differences to Kelvin (numerically equal).
> - ✅ **Steady-state** is used for winter design; **periodic** for summer design.
> - ✅ **ECBC reference U-values** for Indian climate: walls ≤ 0.4–0.8 W/m²K; roofs ≤ 0.4–0.5 W/m²K; windows ≤ 3.3 W/m²K (5-star).
> - ✅ **Infiltration and ventilation** together account for 25–40% of total cooling/heating load in typical Indian buildings — never ignore them.
> - ✅ **High U-value** = poor insulation (steel glass analogy). **Low U-value** = good insulation (thermos analogy).
> - ✅ Remember that $U$ and $R$ are **reciprocals** of each other, like conductance and resistance in electrical circuits.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Annual Heating and Cooling Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Estimation of Annual Heating and Cooling Loads # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Annual Heating and Cooling Load Estimation** is the systematic engineering procedure used to determine the total quantity of thermal energy (in Joules, kWh, or BTU) that must be supplied to or removed from a conditioned space over a one-year operating period, in order to maintain the desired indoor air temperature within a building envelope exposed to a variable outdoor climate. The estimation is primarily based on: - **Degree-Day Method** (HDD / CDD) - **Building Heat Transfer Coefficient (UA value)** - **Internal and Solar Heat Gains** - **HVAC System Performance Parameters** (Efficiency $\eta$ for heating, COP for cooling) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Heating Load** = Energy required to *add* heat to the building. > **Cooling Load** = Energy that must be *removed* from the building. > Both are functions of weather data, building envelope, occupancy, and HVAC efficiency. ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The "Yearly Fuel Budget" of a House** > > Imagine your home is a metal bucket with a small hole. During winter, the heat inside leaks out (like water leaking). To keep the bucket comfortably full, you must pour water (heating energy) at the same rate it leaks. The amount you pour in a year is your **Annual Heating Energy**. > > In summer, the reverse happens — the sun and the outdoor heat pour INTO the bucket, plus your body, lights, and gadgets (TV, fridge) also generate heat. To prevent overflow (overheating), you must constantly scoop heat out (cooling energy). The total amount scooped out in a year is your **Annual Cooling Energy**. > > The **Degree Day** is simply a clever weather-engineer's way of adding up *how cold* or *how hot* each day was compared to your comfort temperature — and multiplying it by the leakiness of your bucket to get the total energy needed. ## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics - **Degree Day (DD)** — Cumulative temperature deficit or excess over a base temperature, integrated over 24 hours. Unit: **°C-day** (SI) or **°F-day** (Imperial). - **Heating Degree Day (HDD)** — Base temperature typically **18°C (65°F)** for residential buildings. - **Cooling Degree Day (CDD)** — Base temperature typically **23°C to 26°C** for air-conditioned commercial buildings. - **UA Value (Heat Transfer Coefficient)** — Combined conductance of all envelope elements. Unit: **W/K** or **BTU/hr·°F**. - **Heating System Efficiency ($\eta$)** — Typically **0.6 to 0.9** for fuel-based heaters; **COP 2 to 4** for heat pumps. - **Cooling Coefficient of Performance (COP)** — Typically **2.5 to 4.0** for modern chillers and AC units. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Degree-Day Concept — Logic Breakdown The **Degree-Day Method** is a simplified, macro-level energy estimation technique that bypasses the need for hour-by-hour simulation by using long-term average weather statistics. The logic proceeds as follows: 1. **Establish a Base Temperature ($T_b$)**: This is the indoor setpoint at which neither heating nor cooling is required. 2. **Compute Daily Mean Outdoor Temperature ($T_{mean}$)**: Average of daily maximum and minimum outdoor temperature. 3. **Calculate Daily HDD or CDD**: - If $T_{mean} < T_b$ (winter day): $HDD = T_b - T_{mean}$ - If $T_{mean} > T_b$ (summer day): $CDD = T_{mean} - T_b$ 4. **Sum Daily Values Over the Year**: $\sum HDD$ and $\sum CDD$ give the **Annual Degree Days**. 5. **Multiply by Building Heat Loss/Gain Coefficient (UA)**: This converts thermal deficit into energy units. 6. **Divide by System Efficiency or COP**: Converts delivered energy into source energy (fuel or electricity). > [!NOTE] > **Why does Degree Day work?** > The heat loss/gain through a building envelope is approximately **linear with the temperature difference** between indoors and outdoors. So, instead of calculating $\Delta T$ for each of the 8760 hours in a year, we sum only the magnitude of that difference for the heating/cooling days, saving enormous computational effort. ## 2.2 Components of the Building Load A building's annual thermal load has **three main contributors**: | Component | Description | Proportional to ΔT? | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Transmission Load** | Heat flow through walls, roof, floor, windows | Yes | | **Infiltration/Ventilation Load** | Heat carried by outdoor air leakage | Yes | | **Internal/Solar Load** | Heat from occupants, lights, equipment, sun | No (for cooling) | For **heating**: Only the transmission and infiltration terms matter (solar/internal often offset heating). For **cooling**: All three contribute, and internal + solar loads dominate for commercial buildings. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Description | Unit (SI) | Unit (Imperial) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $HDD_{daily} = T_b - T_{mean}$ | Daily heating degree day | °C·day | °F·day | | 2 | $CDD_{daily} = T_{mean} - T_b$ | Daily cooling degree day | °C·day | °F·day | | 3 | $Q_{inst} = UA \cdot \Delta T$ | Instantaneous heat transfer | W | BTU/hr | | 4 | $Q_{annual,H} = \dfrac{UA \cdot HDD \cdot 24}{\eta_H}$ | Annual heating energy | Wh | BTU | | 5 | $Q_{annual,C} = \dfrac{UA \cdot CDD \cdot 24 + Q_{solar} + Q_{int}}{\text{COP}_C}$ | Annual cooling energy | Wh | BTU | | 6 | $Q_{solar,annual} = \sum_{i} I_i \cdot SC_i \cdot A_i \cdot CLF_i$ | Annual solar heat gain | Wh | BTU | | 7 | $Q_{int,annual} = (q_p N_p + q_l A_f + q_e) \cdot t_{occ}$ | Annual internal heat gain | Wh | BTU | | 8 | $UA = \sum_i U_i A_i + 0.33 \cdot n \cdot V \cdot c_v$ | Total heat transfer coeff. | W/K | BTU/hr·°F | | 9 | $\eta_H = \dfrac{Q_{delivered}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | Heating system efficiency | dimensionless | dimensionless | | 10 | $\text{COP}_C = \dfrac{Q_{cool}}{W_{input}}$ | Cooling system COP | dimensionless | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Conversion Notes**: > - $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.412 \text{ kBTU} = 3412 \text{ BTU}$ > - $1 \text{ W/K} = 1.896 \text{ BTU/hr·°F}$ (using R-value, $R = 1/U$) > - $1 \text{ °F-day} = 0.5556 \text{ °C-day}$ (multiply °C-day by $1.8$ to get °F-day) ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Energy Audits**: Auditors use degree-day data to benchmark actual utility bills against predicted consumption to identify waste. - **HVAC Sizing**: Preliminary sizing of boilers, chillers, and AHUs (Air Handling Units). - **Building Energy Ratings (BEE Star Rating, ECBC in India)**: Standardized load estimation is mandatory for green building certification. - **Solar Passive Design**: Architects use HDD/CDD to design appropriate insulation, glazing, and shading. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Annual Heating Energy from Degree Days We start from the steady-state heat loss equation for a building: $$Q(t) = UA \cdot [T_{in}(t) - T_{out}(t)]$$ **Step 1**: Recognize that $T_{in}$ is held constant at the setpoint $T_b$ during heating season. Thus, $\Delta T(t) = T_b - T_{out}(t)$. $$Q_{inst}(t) = UA \cdot \Delta T(t)$$ **Step 2**: The total energy delivered over a heating season is the time integral of the instantaneous power. $$Q_{season} = \int_{season} UA \cdot \Delta T(t) \, dt$$ **Step 3**: Define the **Heating Degree Day** as the integral of $\Delta T$ over 24 hours. $$HDD_{day} = \int_{day} \Delta T(t) \, dt \approx (T_b - T_{mean}) \cdot 24 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 4**: Sum daily HDD values over the entire year to obtain the **Annual HDD**. $$HDD_{annual} = \sum_{year} HDD_{day} = \sum_{i=1}^{365} (T_b - T_{i,mean})_+$$ where $(x)_+ = \max(0, x)$ ensures we only count heating days. **Step 5**: Substituting into the energy integral gives us: $$Q_{annual,H} = UA \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{365} (T_b - T_{i,mean})_+ \cdot 24 \text{ hr/day}$$ $$Q_{annual,H} = UA \cdot HDD_{annual} \cdot 24 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 6**: Adjust for heater efficiency $\eta_H$ (only a fraction of fuel energy becomes useful heat). $$\boxed{Q_{fuel,annual} = \frac{UA \cdot HDD \cdot 24}{\eta_H}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Annual Cooling Energy with Internal/Solar Loads Cooling load has two additive parts: a **temperature-dependent part** (transmission + infiltration) and a **temperature-independent part** (internal + solar). **Step 1**: Write the instantaneous cooling load. $$Q_{cool}(t) = UA \cdot [T_{out}(t) - T_b] + Q_{solar}(t) + Q_{int}(t)$$ **Step 2**: For the transmission part, integrate over the cooling season using CDD. $$Q_{trans,annual} = UA \cdot CDD_{annual} \cdot 24$$ **Step 3**: For internal loads, sum contributions from people, lights, and equipment over occupied hours. $$Q_{int,annual} = (q_p N_p + q_l A_f + q_e) \cdot t_{occ}$$ where $q_p$ is per-person heat dissipation, $N_p$ is number of occupants, $q_l$ is lighting power density, $A_f$ is floor area, $q_e$ is equipment load, and $t_{occ}$ is annual occupied hours. **Step 4**: For solar gains, use the solar cooling load factor (CLF) approach. $$Q_{solar,annual} = \sum_{i} SC_i \cdot A_i \cdot CLF_i \cdot t_{sun,i}$$ **Step 5**: Combine and divide by cooling system COP. $$\boxed{Q_{elec,annual} = \frac{UA \cdot CDD \cdot 24 + Q_{solar,annual} + Q_{int,annual}}{\text{COP}_C}}$$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example > [!NOTE] > **Problem**: A small office in Kochi has the following parameters: > - Total UA value = 850 W/K > - Annual HDD (base 18°C) = 0 (Kochi has no heating season) > - Annual CDD (base 24°C) = 2200 °C·day > - Internal heat gains = 25 kWh/day × 300 working days = 7500 kWh/year > - Solar heat gains = 4000 kWh/year > - Cooling COP = 2.8 **Step 1**: Calculate annual transmission cooling load. $$Q_{trans} = UA \cdot CDD \cdot 24 = 850 \times 2200 \times 24$$ $$Q_{trans} = 850 \times 52800 = 4.488 \times 10^7 \text{ Wh} = 44880 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 2**: Add internal and solar gains. $$Q_{total,thermal} = 44880 + 7500 + 4000 = 56380 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 3**: Divide by COP to get electricity consumed. $$Q_{elec} = \frac{56380}{2.8} = 20135.7 \text{ kWh/year}$$ **Step 4**: Convert to monetary value at ₹8/unit. $$\text{Annual Cost} = 20135.7 \times 8 = \text{₹}1{,}61{,}086$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a quick audit calculation. Note that in Kochi (tropical, coastal climate), **cooling loads dominate** and there is essentially **no heating load**, so the focus of energy conservation is on reducing UA (better insulation, double-glazing) and internal loads (LED lighting, efficient equipment). ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Analysis | Parameter | Effect on Heating Load | Effect on Cooling Load | Mitigation Strategy | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Increase in UA | Increases proportionally | Increases proportionally | Add insulation, double-glazing | | Increase in $T_b$ (heating) | Decreases | N/A | Lower thermostat setpoint | | Increase in $T_b$ (cooling) | N/A | Decreases | Raise thermostat setpoint (e.g., 26°C) | | Higher η (efficient heater) | Reduces fuel input | N/A | Use high-efficiency boiler/heat pump | | Higher COP | N/A | Reduces electricity | Use inverter AC, energy-efficient chiller | | More solar gain | Reduces heating load | Increases cooling load | External shading, low-SHGC glass | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Annual Load Estimation Methodology The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional architecture of estimating annual heating and cooling loads, from climate data ingestion to final energy demand output. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Climate Data Source
IMD / ASHRAE / Local Weather] --> B[Compute Daily Mean
Outdoor Temperature] B --> C{Tmean vs Tbase} C -->|Tmean less than Tbase| D[Calculate Daily HDD
HDD = Tbase minus Tmean] C -->|Tmean greater than Tbase| E[Calculate Daily CDD
CDD = Tmean minus Tbase] C -->|Tmean equals Tbase| F[No Load Day] D --> G[Sum Annual HDD] E --> H[Sum Annual CDD] G --> I[Building Heat Loss Coeff
UA = Sum Ui x Ai] H --> I I --> J[Multiply UA by Degree Days
by 24 hours] J --> K{Apply HVAC System
Performance} K -->|Heating| L[Divide by Efficiency eta] K -->|Cooling| M[Add Solar and Internal Loads
Divide by COP] L --> N[Annual Fuel Energy kWh] M --> O[Annual Electrical Energy kWh] N --> P[Final Energy Demand Report] O --> P ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow: Cooling Load Component Aggregation ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Envelope Conduction
UA x CDD x 24] --> D[Total Cooling Load Qcool] B[Infiltration Load
rho x cp x Vdot x DeltaT] --> D C[Internal Loads
People plus Lights plus Equipment] --> D E[Solar Heat Gain
Glass Area x SHGC x CLF] --> D D --> F[Divide by COP of AC] F --> G[Annual Electricity Bill kWh] ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Heat-Flow Visualization ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Indoor_Zone I1[Indoor Setpoint Tb] I2[Internal Heat Sources
Occupants, Equipment, Lights] end subgraph Outdoor_Zone O1[Outdoor Temp Tout] O2[Solar Radiation I_sol] end I1 -- Transmission UA -- O1 I2 -- Adds to Indoor -- I1 O2 -- Solar Gain through Windows -- I1 I1 -- Infiltration Airflow -- O1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram**: The indoor setpoint $T_b$ and the outdoor temperature $T_{out}$ are connected by a transmission link (UA), while internal sources and solar radiation *inject* heat into the indoor zone. The cooling system must remove the sum of all these heat inflows. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define Heating Degree Day (HDD) and Cooling Degree Day (CDD). Why is a base temperature of 18°C commonly used for residential heating calculations? **Model Answer**: **Heating Degree Day (HDD)**: A unit representing the cumulative extent to which the daily mean outdoor temperature falls below a specified base temperature $T_b$ over a 24-hour period. Mathematically, $$HDD_{daily} = T_b - T_{mean} \quad \text{when } T_{mean} < T_b$$ **Cooling Degree Day (CDD)**: The analogous unit when the outdoor temperature exceeds the base. $$CDD_{daily} = T_{mean} - T_b \quad \text{when } T_{mean} > T_b$$ A base temperature of **18°C (65°F)** is chosen for residential heating because indoor comfort studies have shown that human thermal neutrality in homes typically occurs around 20–22°C, and at 18°C ambient, internal gains (cooking, body heat) raise the indoor air close to comfort without active heating. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Distinguish between UA value and U-value. Why is the UA value preferred for whole-building energy estimation? **Model Answer**: - **U-value (Thermal Transmittance)**: Property of a *single* envelope element (wall, roof, window), measured in W/m²·K. It quantifies heat flow per unit area per unit temperature difference. - **UA value (Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient)**: Sum of (U × A) for *all* envelope elements plus infiltration/ventilation term, measured in W/K. It gives the **whole-building heat loss rate** per kelvin of temperature difference. $$UA_{total} = \sum_{i} U_i A_i + 0.33 \cdot n \cdot V \cdot c_v$$ The UA value is preferred for whole-building estimation because it aggregates the thermal performance of the entire envelope (walls, roof, floor, windows, doors) and air leakage paths into a **single coefficient**, which can be directly multiplied by the seasonal $\Delta T$ (represented as HDD or CDD) to give total annual energy. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Derive the expression for **Annual Heating Energy Consumption** of a building using the Degree Day Method. Clearly state all assumptions made. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A factory building located in Bengaluru has the following data: - UA value = 1200 W/K - Annual HDD (base 18°C) = 50 °C·day - Heating system efficiency = 0.75 - Cost of furnace oil = ₹75/litre; calorific value = 38 MJ/litre Calculate the **annual cost of heating**. What simple measure would you recommend to halve this cost? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to Question A: **Part (a) — Derivation**: The derivation proceeds in the following steps (assuming steady-state, constant indoor setpoint, and uniform building thermal properties): **Assumptions**: 1. The indoor temperature $T_{in}$ is maintained at a constant setpoint $T_b$ throughout the heating season. 2. The building heat loss coefficient $UA$ is constant (does not vary with temperature). 3. Heat flow through the envelope is quasi-steady and one-dimensional. 4. Internal heat gains and solar gains during winter are neglected (or subtracted as a constant offset). **Derivation**: The instantaneous heat loss rate is: $$Q(t) = UA \cdot [T_b - T_{out}(t)]$$ Energy delivered in a small time $dt$ is $dE = Q(t) \, dt$. Integrating over the heating season: $$E_{season} = \int_{season} UA \cdot [T_b - T_{out}(t)] \, dt$$ Recognizing that the integral of $[T_b - T_{out}(t)]$ over 24 hours equals the daily HDD, and summing over all heating days: $$E_{season} = UA \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} HDD_i \cdot 24 \text{ hr/day} = UA \cdot HDD_{annual} \cdot 24$$ To convert delivered (useful) heat to fuel energy: $$E_{fuel} = \frac{UA \cdot HDD_{annual} \cdot 24}{\eta_H}$$ **Part (b) — Numerical**: **Step 1**: Compute annual delivered heat energy. $$E = 1200 \text{ W/K} \times 50 \text{ K·day} \times 24 \text{ hr/day}$$ $$E = 1200 \times 1200 = 1{,}440{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 1440 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 2**: Convert Wh to MJ (since fuel is in MJ). $$1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}, \quad \text{so } E = 1440 \times 3.6 = 5184 \text{ MJ}$$ **Step 3**: Account for furnace efficiency. $$E_{fuel} = \frac{5184}{0.75} = 6912 \text{ MJ/year}$$ **Step 4**: Convert to litres of furnace oil. $$V_{oil} = \frac{6912}{38} = 181.9 \text{ litres/year}$$ **Step 5**: Compute annual cost. $$\text{Cost} = 181.9 \times 75 = \text{₹}13{,}643 \text{ per year}$$ **Step 6**: Recommended measure to halve cost — The simplest and most cost-effective measure is to **halve the UA value by adding roof and wall insulation** (e.g., 50 mm expanded polystyrene). This reduces UA to 600 W/K, halving the energy consumption to 90.9 litres/year and the cost to ₹6818/year. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the concept of **Cooling Load** and distinguish it from **Heat Gain**. Discuss the components that constitute the cooling load of a commercial building. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A commercial office in Chennai (hot-humid climate) has the following parameters: - Total UA value = 2000 W/K - Annual CDD (base 24°C) = 2800 °C·day - Annual solar heat gain = 8000 kWh - Annual internal heat gain (people, lights, equipment) = 12,000 kWh - Chiller COP = 3.0 Estimate the **annual electricity consumption for cooling** in kWh. Also compute the **annual electricity cost** at ₹9/unit and recommend one energy-saving measure. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to Question B: **Part (a) — Conceptual**: **Heat Gain** is the *instantaneous* rate at which heat enters or is generated inside a space (in W or BTU/hr). **Cooling Load** is the *rate at which heat must be removed* from the space to maintain the desired indoor temperature — a consequence, not the same as, the heat gain. The distinction matters because of **thermal storage effects**: heat gain through a sunlit concrete wall at 2 PM may not produce cooling load until 6 PM when the wall's mass releases heat into the room. This lag is captured by the **Cooling Load Factor (CLF)**. **Components of cooling load in a commercial building**: 1. **External Conduction** through walls, roof, floor exposed to outdoor temperature and solar radiation. 2. **Solar Radiation through Glazing** — often the largest single component in day-time cooling. 3. **Internal Loads**: - People (sensible + latent heat) - Lighting (especially incandescent/halogen; LED is much better) - Equipment (computers, printers, servers) 4. **Infiltration and Ventilation** of outdoor hot air. 5. **Reheat, Ductwork, and Fan Heat** — auxiliary loads from the HVAC system itself. **Part (b) — Numerical**: **Step 1**: Calculate annual transmission cooling load (temperature-dependent). $$Q_{trans} = UA \cdot CDD \cdot 24 = 2000 \text{ W/K} \times 2800 \text{ K·day} \times 24 \text{ hr/day}$$ $$Q_{trans} = 2000 \times 67200 = 1.344 \times 10^8 \text{ Wh} = 134{,}400 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 2**: Add temperature-independent internal and solar loads. $$Q_{thermal,total} = 134{,}400 + 8000 + 12{,}000 = 154{,}400 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 3**: Divide by COP of chiller. $$E_{elec} = \frac{154{,}400}{3.0} = 51{,}467 \text{ kWh/year}$$ **Step 4**: Compute annual cost. $$\text{Cost} = 51{,}467 \times 9 = \text{₹}4{,}63{,}200 \text{ per year}$$ **Step 5**: Recommended measure — **Raise the chiller setpoint to 24°C or higher** combined with **occupancy sensor-based AC zoning**. Each 1°C increase in setpoint reduces cooling load by ~6%, saving ~₹28,000/year. Alternatively, **install a high-efficiency chiller (COP upgrade from 3.0 to 4.0)** would reduce electricity consumption to 38,600 kWh, saving ~₹1,16,000/year. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation**: > 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 24 hours**: Many students write $Q = UA \cdot HDD$ and forget the hour-to-day conversion. This loses 2–3 marks. > 2. **Mixing up units**: Using $UA$ in W/K and HDD in °F-day without converting causes dimensional errors. Always convert HDD to °C-day for SI units. > 3. **Forgetting to divide by efficiency or COP**: The final energy demand is *source energy* (fuel or electricity), not delivered energy. Missing $\eta$ or COP loses 1–2 marks. > 4. **Adding solar/internal gains to heating load**: These are typically subtractions (offset heating) for HDD, not additions. Read the question carefully. > 5. **Not stating assumptions**: KTU examiners award marks for listing the assumptions of the degree-day method (constant indoor temp, constant UA, steady state). ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Degree Day = Base Temp − Mean Outdoor Temp** (for HDD), reversed for CDD. - **Annual HDD/CDD** are tabulated for each city (e.g., Chennai, Delhi, Bengaluru, Kochi) in ASHRAE handbooks and IMD publications. - **Heating Load** is temperature-driven; **Cooling Load** has both temperature-driven (UA·CDD·24) and temperature-independent (solar + internal) components. - The **UA value** is the single most important building parameter; reducing UA through insulation is the most cost-effective energy conservation measure. - The **degree-day method** is a screening/estimation tool — for final design, use detailed hour-by-hour simulation (e.g., EnergyPlus, eQuest). - Always include **system efficiency (η)** for heating and **COP** for cooling in final energy calculations. - **1 °C-day × 24 hr = 24 °C·hr** of energy deficit, so multiplying UA (W/K) by 24 converts degree-day to Wh. - For Indian climates, **cooling loads dominate**; heating is negligible in Kerala, Tamil Nadu, and most coastal regions. - **Sensible heat vs. Latent heat**: Sensible raises temperature; latent changes moisture content (humidification/dehumidification) — both matter for cooling. - **Conversion master key**: 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 3412 BTU; 1 BTU = 1055 J; 1 W/K = 1.896 BTU/hr·°F.

topics.title: Factors That Influence Thermal Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors That Influence Thermal Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Thermal Performance** is defined as the quantified ability of a building envelope, industrial equipment, or thermal system to resist, transfer, or retain heat energy under specified operating and environmental conditions, expressed through parameters such as **U-value (W/m²K)**, **R-value (m²K/W)**, **thermal admittance**, and **time-lag (thermal lag)**.

In the context of **Energy Management and Conservation**, thermal performance is evaluated to identify heat loss or heat gain pathways, quantify energy inefficiencies, and recommend conservation measures (ENCON) in:
- Building envelopes (walls, roofs, floors, glazing)
- Industrial furnaces, boilers, and kilns
- Heat exchangers and process piping
- HVAC ducts and chilled water lines
- Refrigeration and cold storage systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Thermal performance is the *single most critical input* in an energy audit (Level 1, 2, and 3) because every heat loss calculation, insulation retrofit decision, and payback analysis depends on it.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are wearing a **cotton shirt** versus a **thick woolen sweater** on a cold winter morning.

- The **woolen sweater** has *low thermal conductivity* and *trapped air pockets* — it resists heat flow from your body to the cold environment. **Good thermal performance.**
- The **cotton shirt** allows heat to escape easily — *high thermal conductivity*, *thin*, *no trapped air*. **Poor thermal performance.**

**Now apply the same logic to a building wall:**

| Clothing Analogy | Building Equivalent | Thermal Performance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Wool thickness | Wall thickness + insulation | Thicker = Better resistance |
| Wool fibre type | Material thermal conductivity (k) | Lower k = Better resistance |
| Wind cutting through fabric | Air infiltration / wind washing | More leaks = Worse performance |
| Bright vs dark surface | Surface emissivity (ε) | Lower ε = Less radiant loss |
| Wet vs dry fabric | Moisture content in insulation | Wet = Much worse performance |
| Tightly woven vs loose knit | Thermal bridges, gaps | Continuous barrier = Better |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Truth:** Thermal performance is **not** a property of a single material — it is the *combined outcome* of material properties, geometry, surface characteristics, environmental conditions, and operating practices.

### 1.3 Primary Classification of Influencing Factors

For KTU 2024 examination purposes, the factors influencing thermal performance are grouped into **five major categories**:

1. **Material Property Factors** — thermal conductivity, density, specific heat
2. **Geometric / Design Factors** — thickness, surface area, orientation
3. **Surface & Radiative Factors** — emissivity, absorptivity, reflectivity
4. **Environmental / Climatic Factors** — ambient temperature, wind, solar radiation
5. **Operational & Maintenance Factors** — load profile, fouling, moisture ingress, age

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All standard thermal performance metrics referenced in KTU papers use **SI units** unless explicitly stated otherwise. Standard reference temperature for U-value calculations is **23°C (296 K)** per **ISO 6946**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Heat Transfer Modes (Foundation Layer)

Every thermal performance calculation in energy management is built upon the **three modes of heat transfer**. A KTU student must recognize *which mode dominates* in a given scenario before applying any formula.

| Mode | Driving Force | Governing Law | Where It Dominates in Energy Audit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Conduction** | Temperature gradient (∇T) | Fourier's Law | Heat flow through walls, insulation, furnace walls |
| **Convection** | Fluid motion over surface | Newton's Law of Cooling | Heat loss from hot pipes, boiler surfaces, roofs |
| **Radiation** | Temperature⁴ difference | Stefan-Boltzmann Law | High-temp furnace losses, solar gain through glass |

### 2.2 Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction (The Backbone Equation)

Steady-state one-dimensional heat conduction through a homogeneous material:

$$q = -k \frac{dT}{dx}$$

For a plane wall of thickness L with surface temperatures T₁ (hot) and T₂ (cold):

$$Q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = heat transfer rate (**W**)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of material (**W/m·K**)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area normal to heat flow (**m²**)
- $L$ = thickness of material (**m**)
- $(T_1 - T_2)$ = temperature difference across material (**K** or **°C**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the negative sign?** Heat flows from hot to cold, *opposite* to the temperature gradient direction. The negative sign ensures $Q$ is positive when heat flows in the assumed positive x-direction.

### 2.3 Thermal Resistance Concept (R-Value) — The Engineer's Tool

**Thermal Resistance** is the *resistance* offered by a material or surface to heat flow. It is the *reciprocal* of conductance — directly analogous to electrical resistance (Ohm's Law analogy).

For conduction through a plane wall:

$$R_{cond} = \frac{L}{k \cdot A} \quad \text{(m}^2\text{K/W)}$$

For convection at a surface:

$$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{h \cdot A} \quad \text{(m}^2\text{K/W)}$$

For radiation between two surfaces:

$$R_{rad} = \frac{1}{h_r \cdot A} \quad \text{(m}^2\text{K/W)}$$

Where the **radiative heat transfer coefficient** is:

$$h_r = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot (T_s^2 + T_{sur}^2)(T_s + T_{sur})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** The thermal resistance **network** (analogous to electrical resistors) is the *fastest method* to solve composite wall, multilayer insulation, and overall heat transfer problems. Always draw the **thermal circuit** before writing equations.

### 2.4 Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U-Value) — The KTU Gold Standard

The **U-value** is the single most important metric in KTU energy management problems. It represents the **overall heat transfer per unit area per unit temperature difference** through a composite assembly.

$$\boxed{U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{h_i} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{L_j}{k_j} + \frac{1}{h_o}}}$$

Where:
- $h_i$ = inside (interior) convective heat transfer coefficient ≈ **9.30 W/m²K** (for still air, horizontal heat flow upward)
- $h_o$ = outside (exterior) convective heat transfer coefficient ≈ **23.30 W/m²K** (for 6.7 m/s wind)
- $L_j$ = thickness of j-th layer
- $k_j$ = thermal conductivity of j-th layer

The **total heat loss through a wall/roof** is then:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{overall}$$

### 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | When To Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Q = \frac{kA\Delta T}{L}$ | Fourier conduction (plane wall) | W | Single material, steady state |
| 2 | $Q = \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | Conduction through cylinder | W | Pipe insulation problems |
| 3 | $Q = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Newton's law of cooling | W | Convection loss from hot surface |
| 4 | $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A T_s^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann radiation | W | High-temp surfaces (furnaces) |
| 5 | $R = \frac{L}{kA}$ | Thermal resistance (conduction) | K/W | Resistance network method |
| 6 | $R = \frac{1}{hA}$ | Thermal resistance (convection) | K/W | Surface film resistance |
| 7 | $U = \frac{1}{\sum R_i}$ | Overall heat transfer coeff. | W/m²K | Composite wall / window U-value |
| 8 | $Q = U A \Delta T$ | Overall heat loss | W | Building energy audit |
| 9 | $\text{Decrement Factor} = \frac{T_{i,max}}{T_{o,max}}$ | Thermal wave damping | dimensionless | Roof thermal lag analysis |
| 10 | $\text{Time Lag} = \phi$ (hours) | Phase delay of heat wave | hours | Thermal mass evaluation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants (Commit to Memory):**
> - Stefan-Boltzmann constant: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ **W/m²K⁴**
> - Standard inside film resistance: $R_i = 0.13$ **m²K/W** (for heat flow up)
> - Standard outside film resistance: $R_o = 0.04$ **m²K/W** (for 6.7 m/s wind)
> - Brick thermal conductivity: $k \approx 0.70$ **W/m·K**
> - Concrete thermal conductivity: $k \approx 1.40$ **W/m·K**
> - Mineral wool insulation: $k \approx 0.040$ **W/m·K**
> - Polystyrene (EPS): $k \approx 0.038$ **W/m·K**
> - Air gap (sealed, 20mm): $R \approx 0.18$ **m²K/W**

### 2.6 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

A KTU-trained energy manager uses these formulas in:
- **Energy audits** to compute baseline heat loss of buildings
- **Payback calculations** for insulation retrofits (ENCON projects)
- **Boiler/furnace efficiency** analysis (direct + indirect method)
- **Pinch analysis** for process industries
- **HVAC load estimation** for cooling/heating equipment sizing
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: U-Value of a Composite Wall (Most Asked KTU Problem)

**Problem Setup (Standard KTU Pattern):**
A building wall consists of three layers:
- **Layer 1 (Outside):** 25 mm cement plaster, $k_1 = 0.72$ W/m·K
- **Layer 2 (Middle):** 200 mm brick, $k_2 = 0.70$ W/m·K
- **Layer 3 (Inside):** 15 mm gypsum plaster, $k_3 = 0.42$ W/m·K

Inside air temperature = **25°C**, outside air temperature = **5°C**.
Find: (a) U-value of wall (b) Heat loss per m² of wall.

**Step 1 — Identify the thermal resistance network**

The thermal circuit has 5 resistors in series:

$$R_{total} = R_{si} + R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_{so}$$

Where:
- $R_{si}$ = inside surface resistance = **0.13 m²K/W**
- $R_{so}$ = outside surface resistance = **0.04 m²K/W**

**Step 2 — Calculate each conduction resistance (per m²)**

For Layer 1 (cement plaster, L = 0.025 m):

$$R_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.025}{0.72} = 0.0347 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

For Layer 2 (brick, L = 0.200 m):

$$R_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.200}{0.70} = 0.2857 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

For Layer 3 (gypsum plaster, L = 0.015 m):

$$R_3 = \frac{L_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.015}{0.42} = 0.0357 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3 — Sum all resistances**

$$R_{total} = 0.13 + 0.0347 + 0.2857 + 0.0357 + 0.04$$

$$\boxed{R_{total} = 0.5261 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute U-value**

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{0.5261}$$

$$\boxed{U = 1.901 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute heat loss per m²**

$$\Delta T = T_i - T_o = 25 - 5 = 20 \text{ °C (or K)}$$

$$q = U \cdot \Delta T = 1.901 \times 20$$

$$\boxed{q = 38.02 \text{ W/m}^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The brick layer contributes **54%** of the total resistance. This is why adding insulation to brick walls is the *single highest-impact* ENCON measure in old Kerala buildings.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Radius of Insulation (Cylinder Problem)

For cylindrical insulation (e.g., steam pipe lagging), there exists a **critical radius** below which adding insulation *increases* heat loss. This is a frequently tested KTU concept.

**Derivation:**

For a cylinder of outer radius $r_2$ and insulation outer radius $r_3$, with inside temperature $T_1$ and ambient $T_\infty$:

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_\infty}{\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_1 L} + \frac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{2\pi k_{ins} L} + \frac{1}{h_o \cdot 2\pi r_3 L}}$$

To find the **critical radius** $r_{cr}$ at which heat loss is maximum, differentiate Q w.r.t. $r_3$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dQ}{dr_3} = 0 \implies \boxed{r_{cr} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_o}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:**
> - If $r_2 < r_{cr}$ → adding insulation **INCREASES** heat loss (counter-intuitive!)
> - If $r_2 > r_{cr}$ → adding insulation **DECREASES** heat loss (as expected)
> - For typical $h_o = 10$ W/m²K and $k_{ins} = 0.04$ W/m·K → $r_{cr} = 4$ mm
> - **All steam pipes with $r > 4$ mm** are safe to insulate for heat loss reduction.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Factors vs Performance Impact

| Factor | Effect on Thermal Performance | Magnitude of Impact | Mitigation in Energy Audit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ↑ Material thermal conductivity (k) | **Worsens** (more heat flows) | Very High | Use low-k insulation (PUF, EPS) |
| ↑ Insulation thickness | **Improves** (more resistance) | High, but diminishing | Optimize thickness economically |
| ↑ Surface emissivity (ε) | **Worsens** (more radiation) | High above 200°C | Apply aluminium foil cladding (ε ≈ 0.05) |
| ↑ Wind speed outside | **Worsens** (more convection) | Medium | Wind breaks, weatherproof cladding |
| ↑ Moisture content | **Worsens dramatically** (water k = 0.6) | Very High | Vapour barriers, proper sealing |
| ↑ Air infiltration rate | **Worsens** (direct leakage) | Very High | Caulking, weatherstripping |
| ↑ Thermal mass (ρc) | **Improves stability**, slows response | Medium | Use for night setback strategies |
| ↑ Solar absorptivity (α) | **Increases heat gain** (bad in summer) | High for roofs | Light-coloured paint, reflective coating |
| ↑ Surface temperature | **Worsens** (ΔT and T⁴) | Very High (non-linear) | Process optimization, insulation |
| ↑ Surface area | **Worsens** (direct proportionality) | Direct | Compact equipment design |
| ↑ Fouling/dirt on surface | **Worsens** (ε increases) | Medium | Regular cleaning schedule |
| ↑ Internal air movement | **Worsens** (high h_i) | Low-Medium | Static air design preference |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Thermal Performance Influence Factor Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thermal Performance] --> B[Material Property Factors]
    A --> C[Geometric Design Factors]
    A --> D[Surface Radiative Factors]
    A --> E[Environmental Climatic Factors]
    A --> F[Operational Maintenance Factors]

    B --> B1[Thermal Conductivity k]
    B --> B2[Density rho]
    B --> B3[Specific Heat c]
    B --> B4[Moisture Content]

    C --> C1[Wall Thickness L]
    C --> C2[Surface Area A]
    C --> C3[Shape Geometry]
    C --> C4[Orientation Tilt]

    D --> D1[Emissivity epsilon]
    D --> D2[Absorptivity alpha]
    D --> D3[Reflectivity rho]
    D --> D4[Surface Color Finish]

    E --> E1[Ambient Temperature]
    E --> E2[Wind Speed]
    E --> E3[Solar Radiation]
    E --> E4[Humidity]

    F --> F1[Process Load]
    F --> F2[Operating Hours]
    F --> F3[Fouling Aging]
    F --> F4[Maintenance Quality]

    B1 --> G[U Value R Value]
    C1 --> G
    D1 --> G
    E1 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Heat Loss or Gain W]
    H --> I[Energy Consumption kWh]
    I --> J[Energy Conservation Decision]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Thermal Resistance Network (Composite Wall)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inside Air Ti] --> B[Rsi Inside Film]
    B --> C[R1 Layer 1]
    C --> D[R2 Layer 2]
    D --> E[R3 Layer 3]
    E --> F[Rso Outside Film]
    F --> G[Outside Air To]

    B -.Heat Flow Q.-> G
    C -.Heat Flow Q.-> G
    D -.Heat Flow Q.-> G
    E -.Heat Flow Q.-> G

    A --> H[Delta T]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Q = Delta T divided by Sum of R]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Energy Audit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1 Identify System Boundary] --> P2[Step 2 Measure Surface Temps]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Determine k of Materials]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Calculate U Value]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Compute Heat Loss Q]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Compare with Benchmark]
    P6 --> P7{Performance Gap Exists}
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Step 7 Identify Dominant Factor]
    P7 -- No --> P9[System Optimal]
    P8 --> P10[Step 8 Recommend ENCON]
    P10 --> P11[Step 9 Payback Analysis]
    P11 --> P12[Step 10 Implementation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The first diagram shows that *thermal performance is a multi-factor output* — improving only one factor (e.g., adding insulation) without addressing others (e.g., sealing air leaks) will yield suboptimal results. The second diagram reinforces the **resistance-in-series** concept that the KTU examiner expects students to draw before solving composite wall problems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the term "Thermal Performance" of a building envelope. List any **four** factors that influence it.

**Model Answer:**

*Thermal Performance* is the ability of a building envelope to resist heat transfer between interior and exterior environments, quantified by parameters such as **U-value (W/m²K)**, **R-value (m²K/W)**, **thermal admittance**, and **time-lag**.

**Four influencing factors:**
1. Thermal conductivity of construction materials
2. Thickness of wall and insulation layers
3. Surface emissivity and absorptivity
4. Air infiltration rate through cracks and openings

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often list only material factors and forget **operational and environmental factors**. Always cover all five categories for full marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
State **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction** and explain the significance of the negative sign in its mathematical form.

**Model Answer:**

Fourier's Law states that the rate of heat transfer through a homogeneous material is directly proportional to the area normal to heat flow and the temperature gradient across it.

Mathematically:

$$Q = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

The **negative sign** indicates that heat flows in the direction of **decreasing temperature** — i.e., from the hot side to the cold side. Without the negative sign, the equation would predict heat flowing from cold to hot, violating the Second Law of Thermodynamics.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Failing to mention the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** connection costs the third mark. Examiners want physics reasoning, not just formula recall.

---

### PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** A composite wall of a cold storage facility consists of the following layers, arranged from outside to inside:
- 20 mm external cement plaster ($k = 0.72$ W/m·K)
- 220 mm brick masonry ($k = 0.70$ W/m·K)
- 50 mm expanded polystyrene insulation ($k = 0.038$ W/m·K)
- 15 mm internal gypsum plaster ($k = 0.42$ W/m·K)

If the inside air temperature is **2°C** and outside is **38°C**, calculate:
(i) The total thermal resistance of the wall per m²
(ii) The U-value of the wall
(iii) The heat gain per m² into the cold storage

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify all thermal resistances (per m² area)**

| Layer | L (m) | k (W/m·K) | R = L/k (m²K/W) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Inside film (Rsi) | — | — | 0.13 |
| Cement plaster | 0.020 | 0.72 | 0.0278 |
| Brick | 0.220 | 0.70 | 0.3143 |
| EPS insulation | 0.050 | 0.038 | 1.3158 |
| Gypsum plaster | 0.015 | 0.42 | 0.0357 |
| Outside film (Rso) | — | — | 0.04 |

**Step 2 — Sum all resistances**

$$R_{total} = 0.13 + 0.0278 + 0.3143 + 1.3158 + 0.0357 + 0.04$$

$$R_{total} = 1.8636 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**(i) Answer:** $\boxed{R_{total} = 1.8636 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}}$

**Step 3 — Compute U-value**

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{1.8636} = 0.5366 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**(ii) Answer:** $\boxed{U = 0.537 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}}$

**Step 4 — Compute heat gain per m²**

$$\Delta T = T_o - T_i = 38 - 2 = 36 \text{ K}$$

$$q = U \cdot \Delta T = 0.5366 \times 36 = 19.32 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**(iii) Answer:** $\boxed{q = 19.32 \text{ W/m}^2}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The 50 mm EPS layer contributes **70.6%** of the total thermal resistance — confirming why insulation thickness is the most cost-effective lever in cold storage design.

---

(b) **[7 Marks]** Explain any **six** factors that influence the thermal performance of an industrial furnace wall, with appropriate equations where applicable.

**Model Answer:**

The thermal performance of an industrial furnace wall is governed by:

1. **Refractory thermal conductivity (k):**
   Lower k reduces conductive loss.
   $$Q_{cond} = \frac{kA(T_i - T_o)}{L}$$

2. **Wall thickness (L):**
   Increased thickness raises resistance.
   $$R_{cond} = \frac{L}{kA}$$

3. **Surface emissivity (ε):**
   High ε increases radiative loss at high temperatures.
   $$Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma A T_s^4$$

4. **Internal hot-face temperature (T_i):**
   Heat loss is proportional to ΔT; radiative loss scales with T⁴ — small T increases cause large loss increases.

5. **Convective heat transfer coefficient (h_o) on outer surface:**
   $$Q_{conv} = h_o A (T_s - T_\infty)$$

6. **Fouling and slag accumulation on hot face:**
   Increases effective thermal resistance (sometimes beneficial) but creates hot spots, lowering refractory life and performance consistency.

7. **Operating hours and cyclic loading:**
   Repeated thermal cycling causes micro-cracks, raising effective k and degrading performance over time.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students commonly forget to mention the **T⁴ dependence of radiation** in furnace problems. Examiners specifically look for the statement *"radiative losses increase as the fourth power of absolute temperature"* — missing this loses 1–2 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 2]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **thermal resistance network analogy** for steady-state heat flow through a composite wall. Using this concept, derive the expression for the **overall heat transfer coefficient (U-value)**.

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch Description (to be drawn by student):**

The thermal circuit has resistors in series, just like an electrical circuit:

$$T_i \rightarrow R_{si} \rightarrow R_1 \rightarrow R_2 \rightarrow R_3 \rightarrow R_{so} \rightarrow T_o$$

**Analogy with Ohm's Law:**

$$I = \frac{V}{R_{electrical}} \quad \leftrightarrow \quad Q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{thermal}}$$

**Derivation of U-value:**

Heat flow through series resistance network:

$$Q = \frac{T_i - T_o}{R_{si} + R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_{so}}$$

Substituting $R_i = \frac{L_i}{k_i A}$ for conduction layers and $R_s = \frac{1}{hA}$ for surface films:

$$Q = \frac{T_i - T_o}{A \left[ \frac{1}{h_i} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{L_j}{k_j} + \frac{1}{h_o} \right]}$$
