For **unit area** (A = 1 m²):

$$q = \frac{\Delta T}{\frac{1}{h_i} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{L_j}{k_j} + \frac{1}{h_o}}$$

By definition, $U = \frac{q}{\Delta T}$:

$$\boxed{U = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{h_i} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{L_j}{k_j} + \frac{1}{h_o}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The *smallest resistance dominates the heat flow* (parallel of conductances). To significantly improve thermal performance, target the **layer with the lowest R-value**, not the thinnest one.

---

(b) **[7 Marks]** A steam pipeline of **100 mm outer diameter** carries saturated steam at **200°C**. The ambient temperature is **30°C**. The pipe is insulated with **75 mm thick mineral wool** ($k_{ins} = 0.045$ W/m·K). The outer surface convective heat transfer coefficient is **12 W/m²K**.

Calculate:
(i) Heat loss per metre length of insulated pipe
(ii) Heat loss per metre length of *bare* pipe (assume pipe wall k = 45 W/m·K, neglect pipe wall resistance)
(iii) Percentage reduction in heat loss due to insulation

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- $r_1$ (pipe outer) = 0.050 m
- $r_2$ (insulation outer) = 0.050 + 0.075 = 0.125 m
- $T_1$ = 200°C, $T_\infty$ = 30°C
- $k_{ins} = 0.045$ W/m·K, $h_o = 12$ W/m²K

**(i) Heat loss from insulated pipe (per metre):**

$$Q_{ins} = \frac{2\pi L (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_{ins}} + \frac{1}{h_o \cdot r_2}}$$

Numerator:

$$2\pi \times 1 \times (200 - 30) = 2\pi \times 170 = 1068.14 \text{ W·m/W}$$

Denominator:

$$\frac{\ln(0.125/0.050)}{0.045} + \frac{1}{12 \times 0.125}$$

$$= \frac{\ln(2.5)}{0.045} + \frac{1}{1.5}$$

$$= \frac{0.9163}{0.045} + 0.6667$$

$$= 20.362 + 0.667 = 21.029 \text{ m·K/W}$$

Therefore:

$$Q_{ins} = \frac{1068.14}{21.029} = 50.79 \text{ W/m}$$

**(i) Answer:** $\boxed{Q_{ins} \approx 50.8 \text{ W per metre of pipe}}$

**(ii) Heat loss from bare pipe (per metre):**

For the bare pipe, $r_2 = r_1 = 0.050$ m, and there is no insulation layer:

$$Q_{bare} = h_o \cdot (2\pi r_1 L) \cdot (T_1 - T_\infty)$$

$$= 12 \times 2\pi \times 0.050 \times 1 \times 170$$

$$= 12 \times 0.3142 \times 170 = 640.89 \text{ W/m}$$

**(ii) Answer:** $\boxed{Q_{bare} \approx 640.9 \text{ W per metre of pipe}}$

**(iii) Percentage reduction in heat loss:**

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{Q_{bare} - Q_{ins}}{Q_{bare}} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{640.89 - 50.79}{640.89} \times 100 = \frac{590.10}{640.89} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{ Reduction} \approx 92.1\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:**
> 1. Students often forget the **convective resistance of the outer surface** $\left(\frac{1}{h_o \cdot 2\pi r_2 L}\right)$ — this omission leads to a wrong answer of ~52.4 W/m, missing ~3 marks.
> 2. For **bare pipe**, the area used must be the **pipe outer area** $2\pi r_1 L$, not $2\pi r_2 L$ (since there is no insulation).
> 3. Always state **per metre length** explicitly — examiners look for the unit consistency.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Loss Errors in Thermal Performance Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting surface film resistances** $R_{si}$ and $R_{so}$ — typically costs 1–2 marks in U-value problems.
> 2. **Mixing up temperatures** in K and °C — K and °C have the *same magnitude* for differences, but not for use in Stefan-Boltzmann equation (which strictly needs **absolute temperature in Kelvin**).
> 3. **Wrong area in cylindrical problems** — pipe heat loss uses $2\pi r L$, not $\pi r^2$.
> 4. **Ignoring moisture** — water has $k = 0.60$ W/m·K, ~15× that of dry insulation. Wet insulation = failed insulation.
> 5. **Not drawing the thermal resistance network** before solving — examiners explicitly reward the **circuit diagram** with 1–2 marks even if numerical error occurs.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Thermal Performance = quantified ability of a system to resist/transfer heat** — measured by U-value, R-value, thermal admittance, time-lag.
- **Five Factor Categories:** Material, Geometric, Surface-Radiative, Environmental, Operational-Maintenance.
- **Three Heat Transfer Modes:** Conduction (Fourier), Convection (Newton), Radiation (Stefan-Boltzmann, T⁴ dependence).
- **Key Formula Trio:** $Q = \frac{kA\Delta T}{L}$, $R = \frac{L}{kA}$, $U = \frac{1}{\sum R_i}$ per unit area.
- **Critical Radius of Insulation:** $r_{cr} = k_{ins}/h_o$ — below this, insulation *increases* heat loss.
- **Standard Film Resistances (IS/ISO values):** $R_{si} = 0.13$, $R_{so} = 0.04$ m²K/W (per m²).
- **Most-Cited Constants:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴, EPS k ≈ 0.038, Mineral wool k ≈ 0.040 W/m·K.
- **Dominant Loss in Furnaces:** Above 600°C, **radiation dominates** — reduce ε with aluminium foil (ε ≈ 0.05).
- **Dominant Loss in Buildings:** **Air infiltration** — 30–50% of total heating/cooling load in leaky envelopes.
- **Moisture Effect:** Water in insulation raises k by 5–25× — always specify *dry* conditions.
- **KTU 2024 Standard SI Units:** W for heat, m² for area, K (or °C) for ΔT, W/m·K for conductivity.
- **Always draw the thermal resistance circuit** before solving composite problems — earns easy marks.
- **Optimization Rule:** Add insulation *up to the point where last 25 mm of thickness gives < 5% additional savings* — this is the economic insulation thickness used in **Pareto-optimal** ENCON design.
- **Building Envelope Rule of Thumb:** Old uninsulated walls in Kerala: U ≈ 2.5–3.5 W/m²K; ECBC-compliant: U ≤ 0.40 W/m²K.
- **Industrial Equipment Rule:** Bare steam pipe losses are ~10–15× higher than insulated pipes — always insulate above 60°C.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Mantra:** *"Know your R-values, draw your thermal circuit, watch your units, and always check for moisture."* A student who internalizes these four habits will secure full marks on any thermal performance problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Existing Buildings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Existing Buildings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Analysis of Existing Buildings** in the context of Energy Management and Conservation refers to the systematic, multi-disciplinary engineering assessment of an *already-constructed* commercial, institutional, industrial, or residential building to quantify its actual energy performance, identify losses, and recommend cost-effective energy conservation measures (ECMs). As per KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2, this process integrates on-site measurement, energy auditing protocols (per Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) guidelines), thermal imaging, building energy modeling (BEM), and benchmarking against standard baselines such as **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code, 2007/2017)** and **ASHRAE 90.1**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** Analysis of Existing Buildings forms the diagnostic backbone of any energy retrofit project. Without a rigorous baseline audit, ECMs cannot be prioritized, quantified, or verified under the **M&V (Measurement & Verification)** protocols of **ASHRAE Guideline 14** and **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol)**.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your **old motorcycle** that you have ridden for years. It consumes fuel inefficiently, but you cannot fix it blindly. Before opening the engine, you must:

1. **Measure** the current kilometers per litre (kilometer test ride) — this is the **Energy Baseline**.
2. **Diagnose** each sub-system: carburetor, spark plug, tyre pressure, air filter — these are the **End-Uses** (HVAC, Lighting, Walls).
3. **Identify** where the leaks are (using a stethoscope) — this is the **Thermographic Survey / Blower Door Test**.
4. **Compare** with a new, standard bike — this is the **Benchmarking against ECBC / ASHRAE 90.1**.
5. **Recommend** fixes (clean filter, retune carburetor) — these are the **Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)**.

The **"Analysis of Existing Buildings"** is exactly this diagnostic journey — except the engine is a 50-storey commercial complex, the fuel is electricity and thermal energy, and the stethoscope is replaced by thermal cameras, data loggers, and computer simulation software.

---

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold)

The following engineering metrics form the **vocabulary of building energy analysis**:

- **EUI (Energy Use Intensity):** $kWh/m^2/year$ — the universal yardstick of building energy performance.
- **CDD (Cooling Degree Days)** and **HDD (Heating Degree Days):** Degree-days based on a base temperature of **18.3 °C (65 °F)** for cooling and **18.3 °C (65 °F)** for heating (per ASHRAE).
- **U-value (Thermal Transmittance):** $W/m^2 \cdot K$ — rate of heat flow through a building element.
- **R-value (Thermal Resistance):** $m^2 \cdot K/W$ — resistance to conductive heat flow.
- **SHGC (Solar Heat Gain Coefficient):** Dimensionless (0 to 1) — fraction of incident solar radiation admitted through a window.
- **ACH (Air Changes per Hour):** Infiltration rate, dimensionless ratio.
- **Power Factor (PF):** Ratio of real power (kW) to apparent power (kVA); ideal = **1.0**.
- **CFL/W (Lumens per Watt):** Efficacy of lighting systems.
- **COP (Coefficient of Performance)** and **EER (Energy Efficiency Ratio):** HVAC performance metrics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these constants matter:** Every energy audit report for a KTU 2024 student must express findings using these standardized metrics. They allow comparison across buildings of different sizes, climates, and occupancies — making them the *lingua franca* of energy managers.

---

### Scope of Building Analysis (KTU 2024 Definition)

The analysis spans four major engineering domains simultaneously:

| Domain | Engineering Discipline | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Building Envelope** | Architecture + Heat Transfer | Walls, roof, glazing, insulation, air-tightness |
| **HVAC Systems** | Mechanical + Refrigeration | Chillers, boilers, AHUs, FCUs, VRF, ventilation |
| **Electrical Systems** | Electrical Engineering | Lighting, motors, transformers, power factor |
| **Service Systems** | Plumbing + Controls | Pumps, elevators, hot water, BMS controls |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

The **Analysis of Existing Buildings** follows a hierarchical five-stage methodology, standardized globally and adopted in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

#### Stage 1: Preliminary / Walk-Through Audit
- **Purpose:** Identify low-cost/no-cost opportunities with visual inspection only.
- **Tools:** Clipboard, flashlight, portable power meter, lux meter.
- **Duration:** 1–3 days.
- **Outcome:** Preliminary energy balance and short-list of "obvious" ECMs.

#### Stage 2: Detailed Energy Audit (Level 2 / Level 3 per ASHRAE)
- **Purpose:** Quantify energy use by every end-use with measured data.
- **Tools:** Power analyzers, ultrasonic flow meters, combustion analyzers, thermal cameras.
- **Duration:** 2–6 weeks.
- **Outcome:** Energy baseline, end-use breakdown, calibrated model.

#### Stage 3: Benchmarking
- Compare building's **EUI** against:
  - **CBECS (Commercial Buildings Energy Consumption Survey)** database (USA).
  - **ECBC compliant building** EUI benchmarks.
  - **CII-Godrej GBC** benchmarks (India).
- Identify if the building is an "underperformer."

#### Stage 4: Energy Modeling & Simulation
- Build a **digital twin** of the building using software like:
  - **EnergyPlus™** (Department of Energy, USA)
  - **eQUEST™**
  - **DesignBuilder™**
  - **IES VE™**
  - **TRACE 700™**
- Calibrate the model with actual utility bills (≥ 12 months of data) to within **±10 %** of measured energy use (per ASHRAE Guideline 14).

#### Stage 5: ECM Identification, Simulation & M&V Planning
- Model each proposed ECM in the calibrated simulation.
- Rank ECMs by **Simple Payback Period (SPP)**, **Net Present Value (NPV)**, and **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**.
- Develop an **M&V Plan** per IPMVP Option A, B, C, or D.

---

### The Why & The How — Engineering Logic

- **Why do we audit?** Because *every* building deviates from its design intent due to aging equipment, control overrides, occupancy changes, and poor maintenance.
- **How does the analysis integrate disciplines?** Heat transfer physics (envelope), thermodynamics (HVAC), electrical engineering (loads), and economics (payback) converge in a single energy model.

---

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $EUI = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{A_{floor}}$ | Energy Use Intensity | $kWh/m^2/yr$ or $MJ/m^2/yr$ |
| 2 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Conductive heat gain/loss through envelope | $Q$ in W; $U$ in $W/m^2K$; $A$ in $m^2$; $\Delta T$ in K |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{1}{U} = \sum_{i} \dfrac{d_i}{k_i}$ | Total thermal resistance of layered element | $m^2 K/W$ |
| 4 | $Q_{solar} = SHGC \cdot A_{glazing} \cdot I_{solar}$ | Solar heat gain through fenestration | $I_{solar}$ in $W/m^2$ |
| 5 | $Q_{inf} = \dot{V} \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Infiltration / ventilation heat loss | $\dot{V}$ in $m^3/s$; $\rho c_p \approx 1200\ J/m^3K$ |
| 6 | $ACH = \dfrac{\dot{V} \cdot 3600}{V_{room}}$ | Air changes per hour | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $COP_{cooling} = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{input}}$ | Chiller / AC efficiency | $COP_{cooling} \geq 1$ ideal; typical 2.5–6.0 |
| 8 | $EER = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}(Btu/hr)}{P(W)}$ | Energy Efficiency Ratio | $Btu/Wh$; typical 8–12 |
| 9 | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{input}} \times 100\%$ | Boiler / furnace thermal efficiency | Typical 70–95 % |
| 10 | $PF = \dfrac{kW}{kVA} = \cos \phi$ | Power factor | $0 \leq PF \leq 1$; target $\geq 0.95$ |
| 11 | $L = \dfrac{E_v \cdot A}{n \cdot F \cdot U \cdot MF}$ | Lumen method for lighting design | $E_v$ in lux; $n$ lamps; $F$ lumens/lamp; $MF$ maintenance factor $\approx 0.7$ |
| 12 | $SPP = \dfrac{Cost_{ECM}}{\Delta \$/yr}$ | Simple Payback Period | Years |
| 13 | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$ | Net Present Value | Currency; $r$ = discount rate |
| 14 | $CDD = \sum_{d=1}^{365} \max(0,\ T_{avg,d} - T_{base})$ | Cooling Degree Days | Degree-days; $T_{base} = 18.3\ ^\circ C$ |
| 15 | $HDD = \sum_{d=1}^{365} \max(0,\ T_{base} - T_{avg,d})$ | Heating Degree Days | Degree-days |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Boundary conditions and units are **mandatory** in every numerical answer. Marks are routinely deducted for missing units in formula sheets.

---

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Building analysis is the **diagnostic heart** of:

- **Green Building Certification** (LEED, IGBC, GRIHA, BREEAM) — requires documented baseline audit.
- **ESCO (Energy Service Company) Performance Contracts** — savings must be measured and verified.
- **Carbon Trading & ESG Reporting** — EUI is converted to $kg\ CO_2/m^2$ using grid emission factors.
- **Dispute Resolution in Construction** — forensic analysis of defective envelopes.
- **Insurance Risk Assessment** — fire load, electrical safety audits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Conductive Heat Transfer Through a Composite Wall

Consider a multi-layered wall composed of plaster, brick, insulation, and concrete. Heat flows from the warm interior to the cold exterior by conduction.

**Step 1 — Fourier's Law (1-D steady state):**

$$q = \dfrac{dQ}{dt} = -k \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{dT}{dx}$$

Here, $q$ is the heat transfer rate in W, $k$ is thermal conductivity in $W/(m \cdot K)$, $A$ is cross-sectional area in $m^2$, and $\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ is the temperature gradient in $K/m$.

**Step 2 — Integrate across a single layer of thickness $d$ and constant $k$:**

$$\int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT = -\dfrac{q}{kA} \int_0^d dx$$

$$\Delta T = T_1 - T_2 = \dfrac{q \cdot d}{k \cdot A}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the heat flow per unit area (heat flux):**

$$q^{\prime \prime} = \dfrac{q}{A} = \dfrac{k}{d} \cdot \Delta T$$

The term $\dfrac{k}{d}$ is the **conductance** of one layer. Its reciprocal $\dfrac{d}{k}$ is the **resistance** $R$ of one layer.

**Step 4 — For a wall with $n$ layers in series, resistances add:**

$$R_{total} = R_{si} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{d_i}{k_i} + R_{se}$$

$R_{si}$ = interior surface film resistance $\approx 0.13\ m^2K/W$; $R_{se}$ = exterior surface film resistance $\approx 0.04\ m^2K/W$ (per ASHRAE).

**Step 5 — Define the overall heat transfer coefficient (U-value):**

$$U = \dfrac{1}{R_{total}}$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form for steady-state conductive heat flow:**

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

**Conversion Logic:** The chain of equations transforms a differential law (Fourier) into an algebraic engineering tool. By integrating over each material layer and adding thermal resistances, we collapse a continuous problem into a simple lumped-parameter circuit — exactly analogous to electrical resistors in series, where temperature $T$ plays the role of voltage and heat flow $Q$ plays the role of current.

---

### Derivation 2: Simple Payback Period with Maintenance Savings

Many ECMs (e.g., LED retrofit) yield both energy savings and maintenance savings (longer lamp life).

**Step 1 — Define annual energy savings in currency:**

$$\Delta \$_{energy} = \Delta E_{annual} \times C_{elec}$$

$\Delta E_{annual}$ = energy saved per year in kWh; $C_{elec}$ = electricity tariff in $\$/kWh$.

**Step 2 — Define annual maintenance savings:**

$$\Delta \$_{maint} = (N_{old} - N_{new}) \times C_{labor} + N_{replaced} \times C_{lamp}$$

**Step 3 — Combine:**

$$\Delta \$_{total} = \Delta \$_{energy} + \Delta \$_{maint}$$

**Step 4 — Simple Payback Period:**

$$SPP = \dfrac{C_{ECM}}{\Delta \$_{total}}$$

where $C_{ECM}$ is the capital cost of the energy conservation measure.

**Conversion Logic:** The numerator is a one-time capital outflow; the denominator is an annual cash inflow. Dividing yields the time required to recover the investment in years. Engineers prefer $SPP \leq 3$–5 years for low-risk ECMs.

---

### Derivation 3: EUI Normalization for Cross-Building Comparison

Raw electricity bills cannot compare buildings of different sizes or climates. **Normalization** is required.

**Step 1 — Gross annual energy:**

$$E_{annual} = \sum_{m=1}^{12} E_m$$

**Step 2 — Energy Use Intensity:**

$$EUI = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{A_{floor}}\ [\text{in}\ kWh/m^2/yr]$$

**Step 3 — Weather normalization using CDD and HDD:**

$$EUI_{n} = EUI_{measured} \cdot \dfrac{(CDD_{std} + HDD_{std})}{(CDD_{actual} + HDD_{actual})}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Hotter summers and colder winters inflate measured EUI. By scaling with the ratio of standard degree-days (a 30-year climate normal) to actual degree-days of the audit year, we obtain a climate-fair EUI comparable across cities.

---

### Derivation 4: Heating Load from Infiltration

**Step 1 — Volumetric infiltration rate:**

$$\dot{V}_{inf} = \dfrac{ACH \cdot V_{room}}{3600}$$

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate of air:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho_{air} \cdot \dot{V}_{inf}$$

where $\rho_{air} \approx 1.2\ kg/m^3$ at standard conditions.

**Step 3 — Sensible heat load due to infiltration:**

$$Q_{inf,s} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = \rho \cdot \dot{V}_{inf} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

Using $\rho \cdot c_p \approx 1200\ J/(m^3 \cdot K)$:

$$Q_{inf,s} \approx 1200 \cdot \dot{V}_{inf} \cdot \Delta T \ [\text{in W}]$$

**Step 4 — Latent heat load (if applicable):**

$$Q_{inf,l} = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W$$

$h_{fg} \approx 2{,}500{,}000\ J/kg$ (latent heat of vaporization of water); $\Delta W$ = humidity ratio difference.

**Conversion Logic:** Each cubic metre of cold air that leaks into a heated building must be warmed to indoor temperature, costing energy proportional to the temperature difference. Sealing cracks (reducing $ACH$ from 1.5 to 0.5) can cut heating loads by more than half — a powerful, low-cost ECM.

---

### Analytical Matrix — Diagnostic Techniques vs. Building Defects

| Diagnostic Technique | Detects | Equipment | KTU-Mapped Module Topic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermography (IRT)** | Thermal bridges, moisture, insulation voids | Infrared camera (FLIR T-series) | Envelope analysis |
| **Blower Door Test** | Air leakage rate ($ACH_{50}$) | Blower door fan + manometer | Infiltration quantification |
| **Duct Blaster Test** | Dwork leakage | Duct blaster fan | HVAC distribution losses |
| **Power Logging** | Load profile, demand, PF | Three-phase power analyzer | Electrical baseline |
| **Combustion Analysis** | Boiler/flue efficiency, CO levels | Combustion gas analyzer | Boiler tuning |
| **Ultrasonic Flow Meter** | Chilled water flow rates | Ultrasonic clamp-on sensor | HVAC balance |
| **Lux Mapping** | Illumination uniformity | Calibrated lux meter | Lighting audit |
| **Data Loggers** | 24-h temperature/humidity trends | HOBO / Onset data loggers | Thermal comfort |
| **Thermal Comfort Survey** | Occupant perception | ASHRAE-55 questionnaire | IAQ / comfort |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Figure 1: Hierarchical Methodology of Building Energy Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Walk-Through Audit] --> B[Stage 2: Detailed Energy Audit]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Benchmarking vs ECBC and CBECS]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Energy Modeling and Calibration]
    D --> E[Stage 5: ECM Simulation and M and V Planning]
    E --> F[Final: Retrofit Implementation]
    F --> G[Post-Retrofit Verification per IPMVP]

    subgraph Inputs
        H[Utility Bills 12 months] --> B
        I[Building Drawings] --> B
        J[Equipment Nameplates] --> B
    end

    subgraph Tools
        K[Thermal Camera] --> B
        L[Power Analyzer] --> B
        M[Blower Door] --> B
        N[EnergyPlus eQUEST] --> D
    end
```

### Figure 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Energy Audit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EnergyInputs
        E1[Electricity Grid]
        E2[Diesel Generator]
        E3[Steam from Boiler]
        E4[Chilled Water Plant]
    end

    subgraph DistributionLayer
        D1[HT Panel and Transformer]
        D2[LT Switchgear]
        D3[Motor Control Centre]
        D4[BMS and Meters]
    end

    subgraph EndUseLayer
        U1[HVAC and Chillers]
        U2[Lighting Loads]
        U3[Motors and Pumps]
        U4[Plug Loads and IT]
        U5[Domestic Hot Water]
    end

    E1 --> D1
    E2 --> D2
    E3 --> D1
    E4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4
    D4 --> U1
    D4 --> U2
    D4 --> U3
    D4 --> U4
    D4 --> U5
```

### Figure 3: Thermal Resistance Circuit Analogy for a Composite Wall

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Indoor Air T_in] --> Rsi[R_si 0.13]
    Rsi --> R1[Plaster d1 over k1]
    R1 --> R2[Brick d2 over k2]
    R2 --> R3[Insulation d3 over k3]
    R3 --> R4[Concrete d4 over k4]
    R4 --> Rse[R_se 0.04]
    Rse --> T2[Outdoor Air T_out]
```

### Figure 4: IPMVP Options Decision Tree (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose IPMVP Option] --> B{Measure Key Parameter?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Option B: Retrofit Isolation]
    B -- No --> D{Measure Whole Facility?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Option C: Whole Facility]
    D -- No --> F{Measure All Parameters?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Option D: Calibrated Simulation]
    F -- No --> H[Option A: Partially Measured Retrofit Isolation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure Interpretation:** Each rectangular block represents an engineering sub-system. The arrows depict data/information flow (not physical heat flow), illustrating how audit data propagate from metering points through analysis layers to the final ECM decision.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Energy Use Intensity (EUI). Why is it considered the most important metric for benchmarking building energy performance?**

**Model Answer:**

EUI (Energy Use Intensity) is defined as the total annual energy consumed by a building divided by its total floor area. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$EUI = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{A_{floor}} \ [\text{in}\ kWh/m^2/\text{year}]$$

It is the most important benchmarking metric because (i) it normalizes energy use by floor area, allowing direct comparison between buildings of different sizes; (ii) it is independent of occupant count and operation hours to a first approximation; (iii) it enables comparison with national/international benchmarks (e.g., CBECS, ECBC); and (iv) it forms the baseline against which energy savings from ECMs are computed. Typical office EUI in India ranges from **120 to 250 kWh/m²/year**, while ECBC-compliant buildings target **< 100 kWh/m²/year**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Differentiate between a Walk-Through Audit and a Detailed Energy Audit.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Walk-Through Audit (Level 1) | Detailed Energy Audit (Level 2/3) |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Visual inspection only | Comprehensive measurement & analysis |
| **Duration** | 1–3 days | 2–6 weeks |
| **Instruments** | Lux meter, portable power meter | Power analyzer, thermal camera, blower door, flow meters |
| **Data** | Visual observations, spot readings | Hourly/daily logged data over weeks |
| **Outcome** | Identification of obvious ECMs | Calibrated energy model + investment-grade report |
| **Cost** | Low (₹20k–₹80k) | High (₹2 lakh–₹10 lakh) |
| **Accuracy** | ±30 % | ±10 % (per ASHRAE Guideline 14) |

The Walk-Through audit is a screening tool, while the Detailed audit is a quantitative diagnostic that feeds investment-grade decisions.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** *Explain in detail the step-by-step methodology for conducting a Detailed Energy Audit of an existing commercial building. Include the role of benchmarking against ECBC. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A 5,000 m² office building in Kerala consumes 750,000 kWh of electricity and 90,000 kg of LPG per year. Calculate the EUI in kWh/m²/year, using the standard calorific value of LPG as 42 MJ/kg and assuming 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ. Comment on whether the building is ECBC-compliant, given the ECBC benchmark of 120 kWh/m²/year (site EUI). (7 marks)*

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

The Detailed Energy Audit follows six structured steps:

**Step 1 — Pre-Audit Planning & Data Collection:** Gather architectural drawings, HVAC layouts, electrical single-line diagrams, occupancy schedules, and **at least 12 months of monthly utility bills**. Identify the audit scope, team composition (mechanical, electrical, civil engineers), and target benchmarks.

**Step 2 — Site Survey & Walk-Through Inspection:** Physically inspect every major end-use: lighting density, chiller plant, boiler, AHUs, transformers, motors. Note control strategies, setpoints, and operating hours.

**Step 3 — Instrumentation & Long-Term Monitoring:** Install portable power analyzers on LT feeders, ultrasonic flow meters on chilled water lines, combustion analyzers on boilers, and data loggers for temperature/humidity. Log continuously for 2–4 weeks to capture representative operating cycles including weekends.

**Step 4 — Energy Balance & End-Use Breakdown:** Tabulate the metered data into an energy balance table expressed in **kWh/day (or kWh/year)** for each end-use (lighting, fans, HVAC, plug loads). Compute the % share of each.

**Step 5 — Benchmarking against ECBC:** Compare the measured EUI against:
- **ECBC Prescriptive Requirements** for envelope (U-values, SHGC, visible light transmittance).
- **ECBC Prescriptive Requirements** for HVAC (chiller COP, duct insulation, ventilation).
- **ECBC Prescriptive Requirements** for lighting (LPD limits in W/m²).
- **ECBC Whole-Building Performance** option: simulated EUI ≤ 120 kWh/m²/yr (site EUI).

Identify the deviations — these are the audit findings.

**Step 6 — Energy Modeling & ECM Development:** Build a calibrated simulation model in **EnergyPlus / eQUEST** using the measured data. Calibrate to within ±10 % of actual energy use. Test proposed ECMs (LED retrofit, VFD on pumps, ECBC-grade insulation) and rank by simple payback.

**Conversion Logic:** The methodology transforms qualitative observations into quantitative, monetized, and prioritized actions. Each step has explicit deliverables that feed the next.

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Convert LPG energy to kWh equivalent:**

$$E_{LPG} = 90{,}000\ kg \times 42\ MJ/kg = 3{,}780{,}000\ MJ$$

Convert to kWh:

$$E_{LPG} = \dfrac{3{,}780{,}000}{3.6} = 1{,}050{,}000\ kWh$$

**Step 2 — Total annual energy in kWh equivalent:**

$$E_{total} = E_{electric} + E_{LPG} = 750{,}000 + 1{,}050{,}000 = 1{,}800{,}000\ kWh$$

**Step 3 — Compute EUI:**

$$EUI = \dfrac{1{,}800{,}000}{5{,}000} = 360\ kWh/m^2/\text{year}$$

**Step 4 — Compliance Check:**

$$EUI_{measured} = 360\ kWh/m^2/yr \quad \text{vs.} \quad EUI_{ECBC} = 120\ kWh/m^2/yr$$

**Step 5 — Comment:**

Since $360 \gg 120$, the building is **NOT ECBC-compliant**. It consumes **3 times** the benchmark energy. There is a **240 kWh/m²/yr savings potential**, equivalent to a **67 % energy reduction** opportunity if all cost-effective ECMs are implemented. The building would qualify as a priority candidate for an **ESCO-based Energy Performance Contract (EPC)**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** *With the help of neat sketches, explain the working principle of a Blower Door Test. Derive the expression for air leakage rate at 50 Pa. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A single-storey office has a 250 mm brick wall with k = 0.77 W/(m·K), 25 mm plaster (k = 0.72 W/(m·K)) on both sides, and 50 mm expanded polystyrene insulation (k = 0.038 W/(m·K)) on the exterior. Calculate the overall U-value and the heat loss through 200 m² of wall area when inside is 22 °C and outside is 14 °C. (7 marks)*

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Working Principle of Blower Door Test:**

A blower door is a powerful variable-speed fan mounted into an adjustable frame that is temporarily sealed into an exterior doorway of the building. A calibrated fan induces a controlled pressure difference between inside and outside. The airflow required to maintain a series of pressure differences (typically 10 to 60 Pa) is measured using a manometer and a flow-measuring orifice.

A thermography survey is often conducted simultaneously to identify the exact locations of air leaks (visible as cold streaks on the IR image at low indoor pressure).

The test follows **ASTM E779** and **EN 13829** standards.

**Derivation of Air Leakage Rate at 50 Pa ($ACH_{50}$):**

**Step 1 — Power-law leakage model:**

Air leakage through building cracks is governed by orifice flow, expressed empirically as:

$$Q = C \cdot (\Delta P)^n$$

where $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/h), $C$ = flow coefficient (m³/h·Pa⁻ⁿ), $\Delta P$ = pressure difference (Pa), and $n$ = flow exponent.

**Step 2 — At reference pressure $\Delta P_0 = 50\ Pa$:**

$$Q_{50} = C \cdot (50)^n$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $C$:**

$$C = \dfrac{Q_{50}}{(50)^n}$$

**Step 4 — Air changes per hour at 50 Pa:**

$$ACH_{50} = \dfrac{Q_{50}}{V_{building}} \times 1$$

where $V_{building}$ = conditioned volume of the building in m³.

**Step 5 — Convert $ACH_{50}$ to natural infiltration $ACH_{nat}$:**

Using the **Sherman-Grimsrud model** (typical residential assumption, $n = 0.65$, shielding coefficients):

$$ACH_{nat} = \dfrac{ACH_{50}}{20}$$

For commercial buildings with leakier envelopes, $ACH_{50}/10$ to $ACH_{50}/30$ are common conversion factors.

**Step 6 — Typical benchmark:** A tight office should have $ACH_{50} \leq 1.5$; leaky buildings can exceed $ACH_{50} > 5$.

**Conversion Logic:** The blower door test essentially "stress-tests" the building envelope by over-pressurizing it. The pressure-flow relationship is fitted to a power law (like a fluid mechanics orifice equation). The 50 Pa reference is chosen because it represents typical wind pressure on a building façade.

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — List all resistances in series:**

| Layer | Thickness $d$ (m) | Conductivity $k$ (W/(m·K)) | $R = d/k$ (m²K/W) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Interior surface film ($R_{si}$) | — | — | 0.13 |
| Plaster (inside) | 0.025 | 0.72 | 0.0347 |
| Brick | 0.250 | 0.77 | 0.3247 |
| EPS insulation | 0.050 | 0.038 | 1.3158 |
| Plaster (outside) | 0.025 | 0.72 | 0.0347 |
| Exterior surface film ($R_{se}$) | — | — | 0.04 |

**Step 2 — Total resistance:**

$$R_{total} = 0.13 + 0.0347 + 0.3247 + 1.3158 + 0.0347 + 0.04 = 1.8799\ m^2K/W$$

**Step 3 — Overall U-value:**

$$U = \dfrac{1}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{1}{1.8799} = 0.5319\ W/m^2K$$

**Step 4 — Temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = 22 - 14 = 8\ K$$

**Step 5 — Heat loss through 200 m² of wall:**

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.5319 \times 200 \times 8 = 850.97\ W \approx 0.851\ kW$$

**Step 6 — Daily heat loss:**

$$Q_{daily} = 0.851 \times 24 = 20.42\ kWh/day$$

**Step 7 — Comment:** Without insulation ($R$ would drop to ~0.56), heat loss would be ~2.85 kW. Insulation reduces wall heat loss by **~70 %**, validating the role of EPS in tropical-climate construction.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting units in EUI / U-value / Payback answers.** Always write $\text{kWh/m}^2/\text{yr}$, $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$, and $\text{years}$. A unit-less number is treated as physically meaningless in engineering valuation.
> 2. **Conflating Site EUI and Source EUI.** ECBC reports *site EUI*. Source EUI = site EUI × (3.6 / 0.30) ≈ 12× larger for Indian grid. Mixing them is a 2-mark loss.
> 3. **Ignoring surface film resistances ($R_{si}$, $R_{se}$).** These are not optional. Omitting them costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Using wall thickness in mm instead of m in U-value calculations.** A $k$ of 0.038 with $d = 50$ (mm) gives $R = 1.32$ instead of $R = 1316$ — a fatal error worth 3 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the "Comment" step in numerical answers.** Always interpret whether the result is ECBC-compliant, above/below benchmark, or technically feasible.
> 6. **No sketch in Q(a) of blower-door / audit methodology.** A clear block diagram or labeled sketch earns 1–2 easy marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Building Energy Analysis** is a multi-disciplinary diagnostic activity combining heat transfer, HVAC engineering, electrical engineering, and economics.
- The **EUI ($\text{kWh/m}^2/\text{yr}$)** is the universal metric — always cite it with units and floor area basis (conditioned vs gross).
- The methodology is **5-stage hierarchical**: Walk-through → Detailed Audit → Benchmarking → Modeling → ECM Simulation & M&V.
- **ASHRAE Guideline 14** mandates calibration to within **±10 %** of measured utility data for valid models.
- **IPMVP Options A–D** define how savings will be measured and verified post-retrofit.
- **ECBC (India)** sets prescriptive U-values, SHGC, LPD, COP thresholds — the audit compares actual building against these.
- **Degree-days (CDD/HDD)** with base **18.3 °C** are used for climate normalization.
- The **Blower Door Test** measures air-tightness via a power-law $Q = C \cdot (\Delta P)^n$, evaluated at the **50 Pa** reference.
- **Thermography** detects thermal bridges, missing insulation, and air leakage paths.
- **Composite wall heat transfer** uses series resistances — film resistances ($R_{si}$, $R_{se}$) must be included.
- **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** is the fastest screening tool; **NPV / IRR** are required for investment-grade decisions.
- Always express energy both as **delivered energy** and as **source energy** when comparing with codes.
- An audit report must close the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** loop with explicit M&V documentation.
- Common Indian benchmarks: offices **120 kWh/m²/yr**, malls **250–400 kWh/m²/yr**, hospitals **300–500 kWh/m²/yr**.
- ECMs must be ranked by **SPP ≤ 5 years** and **NPV > 0** at the prevailing discount rate (typically 12 % for India).
- Retrofit projects succeed only when **occupant behavior** and **Operations & Maintenance (O&M)** are addressed alongside hardware changes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Setting Up an Energy Management Programme and Use Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Setting Up an Energy Management Programme and Use Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Management Programme (EMP):** A structured, documented, top-management-driven framework of policies, procedures, organizational roles, baseline data, performance indicators, action plans, and review mechanisms designed to achieve continual improvement in energy performance within an organization. It is typically implemented in alignment with **ISO 50001** – the international standard for Energy Management Systems (EnMS).

**Use Management (UM):** The set of operational, maintenance, and behavioral practices applied at the point of *energy end-use* (lighting, motors, HVAC, processes) to ensure that energy is utilized efficiently, wastage is minimized, and consumption is matched to actual functional requirements without compromising output quality or safety.

Together, EMP provides the **strategic umbrella**, while Use Management provides the **tactical execution** at the equipment/process level.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The "Hospital Fitness Program":**
> Think of a factory as a patient, and the Energy Management Programme as a doctor's prescription:
>
> 1. **Initial Check-up** = Energy Audit & Baseline (finding the "BP and sugar levels")
> 2. **Prescription** = Energy Policy & Targets (the doctor writes goals)
> 3. **Daily Routine** = Use Management (eating right, walking, sleeping on time)
> 4. **Monthly Review** = Monitoring & Reporting (follow-up tests)
> 5. **Course Correction** = Management Review (changing medicines if results don't improve)
>
> Without Use Management, the EMP is just a written prescription nobody follows. Without EMP, Use Management is random — good habits without a goal.

---

## 1.3 Standard Units and Key Metrics (Highlighted)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Consumption | E | **kWh** or **MJ** | Base quantity |
| Tonne of Oil Equivalent | TOE | 1 TOE = 41.868 GJ | National-level aggregation |
| Specific Energy Consumption | SEC | **kWh/unit** or **MJ/kg** | Efficiency indicator |
| Energy Cost | EC | ₹/kWh (or $/kWh) | Financial impact |
| Payback Period | PB | **years** | Investment viability |
| Energy Performance Indicator | EnPI | varies | ISO 50001 metric |
| CUSUM | ΣΔE | kWh (cumulative) | Drift detection |
| Boiler Efficiency | η_b | **%** | Combustion quality |
| Electrical Efficiency | η_e | **%** | Motor/drive performance |
| Power Factor | cos φ | dimensionless (0–1) | Electrical quality |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant to Remember:** **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 860 kcal**. This is used in nearly every KTU numerical involving mixed-unit energy conversions.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 10-Step Framework for Setting Up an Energy Management Programme

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most frequently tested KTU structure**. Memorize the sequence — it appears in both Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

1. **Top Management Commitment & Energy Policy**
   - Formal declaration signed by CEO/MD
   - Includes commitment to continual improvement, legal compliance, and resource allocation
2. **Appointment of Energy Manager & Cross-Functional Team**
   - Energy Manager (BEE Certified, Energy Manager Examination by Bureau of Energy Efficiency, India)
   - Representatives from Production, Maintenance, Utilities, Finance, Purchase
3. **Initial Site Survey & Preliminary Energy Audit (Walk-through Audit)**
   - Identify energy-intensive areas
   - Quick-win opportunities
4. **Detailed Energy Audit (Level 2 / Level 3)**
   - Sub-metering, load profiling, end-use breakup
   - Identification of Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)
5. **Establishment of Energy Baseline (E_base)**
   - Historical, normalized baseline (correlated with production, weather, degree-days)
6. **Definition of Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)**
   - SEC, kWh/m², kWh/ton, kWh/passenger-km
7. **Goal & Target Setting**
   - SMART goals: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound
8. **Action Plan Development**
   - List ECMs with priority, budget, responsibility, timeline
9. **Implementation & Use Management**
   - Engineering changes, operational changes, behavioral changes
10. **Monitoring, Measurement, Verification (M&V) and Management Review**
    - M&V per **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol)**
    - PDCA cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act) — core to ISO 50001

---

## 2.2 Key Engineering & Managerial Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Relevant Equations)**

**Table 2.1 — Core Energy Management Equations**

| # | Equation | Description / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P_{output}}$ | Specific Energy Consumption — efficiency of production |
| 2 | $EnPI = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{E_{baseline}}$ | Energy Performance Indicator (ratio to baseline) |
| 3 | $\% Savings = \dfrac{E_{baseline} - E_{actual}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$ | Percent energy reduction |
| 4 | $AEB = E_{base} \times \dfrac{X_{actual}}{X_{base}}$ | Adjusted Energy Baseline (production-normalized) |
| 5 | $EC_{annual} = E_{annual} \times R_{unit}$ | Annual energy cost |
| 6 | $PB = \dfrac{I}{\Delta S}$ | Simple Payback Period (years) |
| 7 | $NPV = -I + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}$ | Net Present Value |
| 8 | $IRR : \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+IRR)^{t}} = 0$ | Internal Rate of Return |
| 9 | $CUSUM_{n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(E_{a,i} - E_{b,i})$ | CUSUM tracking of energy drift |
| 10 | $E_{saved} = SEC_{old} - SEC_{new}$ | Energy saved per unit output |
| 11 | $E_{CO_2} = E \times EF$ | CO₂ emissions (EF = emission factor) |
| 12 | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{E_{input}} \times 100$ | Equipment efficiency |
| 13 | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L} \cdot I_{L} \cdot \cos\phi$ | Three-phase electrical power |
| 14 | $E_{kWh} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L} \cdot I_{L} \cdot \cos\phi \cdot h \cdot 10^{-3}$ | Three-phase energy in kWh |

> Where:
> - $E_{total}$ = Total energy consumed (kWh, MJ, or TOE)
> - $P_{output}$ = Production output (units, tonnes, m³, etc.)
> - $AEB$ = Adjusted Energy Baseline
> - $X_{actual}, X_{base}$ = Actual vs. baseline production variable
> - $R_{unit}$ = Tariff rate (₹/kWh)
> - $I$ = Initial capital investment (₹)
> - $\Delta S$ = Annual monetary savings (₹/year)
> - $CF_t$ = Cash flow in year $t$
> - $r$ = Discount rate
> - $EF$ = Emission Factor (kg CO₂/kWh — India grid average ≈ **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh**)
> - $V_L, I_L$ = Line voltage, line current
> - $\cos\phi$ = Power factor

> [!WARNING]
> **Use `\\vert` or `\\mid` — never `\\vert x \\vert` with raw pipes — when writing modular/absolute notations in markdown tables to avoid breaking the renderer.**

---

## 2.3 Use Management — Tactical Pillars

Use Management operates on **four pillars**, each examined independently by KTU:

**Pillar A — Operations Management**
- Match energy supply to demand (avoid idling, throttling, partial loading penalties)
- Use of soft starters, VFDs (Variable Frequency Drives)
- Switching off idle equipment (no-load losses)
- Load scheduling to avoid peak demand charges

**Pillar B — Maintenance Management**
- Preventive Maintenance (PM) vs. Predictive Maintenance (PdM)
- Cleaning of heat exchangers, condenser tubes, filters
- Lubrication, alignment, balancing of rotating equipment
- Steam trap audit, compressed air leak survey (typically 20–30% leakage in Indian industry)

**Pillar C — Technology Upgrades**
- Replacement of standard efficiency motors with IE3/IE4 (International Efficiency class)
- LED replacement for incandescent/Halogen
- High-efficiency boilers, VAM (Vapor Absorption Machine) chillers
- Waste Heat Recovery (WHR)

**Pillar D — Behavioral / Human Aspects**
- Awareness training
- Display of SEC at operator stations
- "Switch-off" drives
- Incentive schemes, suggestion programs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked to "discuss Use Management," structure your answer along these four pillars — it is the cleanest, highest-scoring template.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Performance Index (EnPI) and Energy Baseline

### Conceptual Setup

The **Energy Baseline (E_base)** is the reference point that quantifies an organization's energy consumption under normalized operating conditions. Without a baseline, no comparison of "before vs. after" ECM (Energy Conservation Measure) implementation is valid.

The simplest form of a baseline (linear regression model):

$$E_{base} = a \cdot X + b$$

where:
- $E_{base}$ = Baseline energy (kWh)
- $X$ = Production output or driving variable (units/month)
- $a$ = Slope (kWh per unit) — energy intensity
- $b$ = Intercept (kWh) — fixed/auxiliary loads independent of production

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Collect historical monthly data pairs $(X_i, E_i)$ for at least 12 months.

**Step 2:** Apply **Least Squares Regression** to fit the linear model. The slope $a$ and intercept $b$ are obtained as:

$$a = \dfrac{n \sum X_i E_i - \sum X_i \sum E_i}{n \sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2}$$

$$b = \dfrac{\sum E_i - a \sum X_i}{n}$$

**Step 3:** For any new month with production $X_{new}$, the **Adjusted Energy Baseline (AEB)** is:

$$AEB = a \cdot X_{new} + b$$

**Step 4:** The **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** is computed as the ratio:

$$EnPI = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{AEB}$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation:
- $EnPI = 1.0$ → Performance equals baseline
- $EnPI < 1.0$ → Improvement (actual energy < baseline)
- $EnPI > 1.0$ → Degradation

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** Many 14-mark questions ask you to compute EnPI for 3–4 months and identify whether energy performance is improving. The CUSUM extension (next derivation) is often the follow-up.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CUSUM (Cumulative Sum) Drift Detection

### Conceptual Setup

A single EnPI value is a snapshot. To detect **sustained drift** (gradual degradation/improvement), we use CUSUM.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Compute the monthly deviation:

$$\Delta E_i = E_{actual,i} - AEB_i$$

**Step 2:** Cumulative sum:

$$CUSUM_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta E_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (E_{a,i} - E_{b,i})$$

**Step 3:** If $CUSUM_n$ is **positive and increasing** → sustained degradation
If $CUSUM_n$ is **negative and decreasing** → sustained improvement
If $CUSUM_n$ oscillates around zero → no significant trend

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Pattern)

### **Problem Statement**

A manufacturing plant recorded the following data for the first quarter of the financial year:

| Month | Production (units) | Actual Energy (kWh) | Tariff (₹/kWh) |
|---|---|---|---|
| January | 8,000 | 9,60,000 | 7.50 |
| February | 9,000 | 10,35,000 | 7.50 |
| March | 10,000 | 11,00,000 | 7.50 |

Historical baseline regression: $E_{base} = 100X + 1,00,000$ kWh (where $X$ = production in units).

**Tasks:**
(a) Compute the Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI) for each month and comment.
(b) Calculate the total energy cost saved (or excess) for the quarter.
(c) Compute the CUSUM at the end of March and interpret.
(d) If an ECM (Energy Conservation Measure) costs ₹15,00,000 and reduces SEC by 10 kWh/unit, calculate the simple payback period assuming 50,000 units/year post-implementation.

---

### **Solution — Part (a): EnPI for each month**

Using the baseline equation $E_{base} = 100X + 1,00,000$:

**January** ($X = 8,000$):
$$AEB_{Jan} = 100 \times 8,000 + 1,00,000 = 9,00,000 \text{ kWh}$$
$$EnPI_{Jan} = \dfrac{9,60,000}{9,00,000} = 1.0667$$

**February** ($X = 9,000$):
$$AEB_{Feb} = 100 \times 9,000 + 1,00,000 = 10,00,000 \text{ kWh}$$
$$EnPI_{Feb} = \dfrac{10,35,000}{10,00,000} = 1.0350$$

**March** ($X = 10,000$):
$$AEB_{Mar} = 100 \times 10,000 + 1,00,000 = 11,00,000 \text{ kWh}$$
$$EnPI_{Mar} = \dfrac{11,00,000}{11,00,000} = 1.0000$$

> **Commentary:** January shows the worst deviation (6.67% above baseline). February shows improvement. March matches the baseline exactly. This indicates a **recovery trend** following the EMP's awareness/training interventions.

---

### **Solution — Part (b): Energy Cost Comparison**

**Actual Cost:**
$$EC_{actual} = (9,60,000 + 10,35,000 + 11,00,000) \times 7.50$$
$$EC_{actual} = 30,95,000 \times 7.50 = \text{₹} 2,32,12,500$$

**Baseline Cost:**
$$EC_{baseline} = (9,00,000 + 10,00,000 + 11,00,000) \times 7.50$$
$$EC_{baseline} = 30,00,000 \times 7.50 = \text{₹} 2,25,00,000$$

**Excess Cost Incurred:**
$$\Delta EC = EC_{actual} - EC_{baseline} = \text{₹} 7,12,500$$

> **Commentary:** The plant overspent by ₹7,12,500 in Q1 due to inefficient operations. The EMP's primary goal must be to bring EnPI below 1.0.

---

### **Solution — Part (c): CUSUM at end of March**

$$\Delta E_{Jan} = 9,60,000 - 9,00,000 = +60,000 \text{ kWh}$$
$$\Delta E_{Feb} = 10,35,000 - 10,00,000 = +35,000 \text{ kWh}$$
$$\Delta E_{Mar} = 11,00,000 - 11,00,000 = 0 \text{ kWh}$$

$$CUSUM_{Mar} = 60,000 + 35,000 + 0 = +95,000 \text{ kWh}$$

> **Interpretation:** The positive CUSUM indicates **cumulative excess consumption** of 95,000 kWh over the quarter. Despite improvement within the quarter (60k → 35k → 0), the running balance has not yet crossed zero, signalling that more aggressive measures (e.g., ECM implementation) are required.

---

### **Solution — Part (d): Simple Payback Period**

**Given:**
- ECM cost (Investment) = ₹15,00,000
- Reduction in SEC = 10 kWh/unit
- Annual production post-implementation = 50,000 units
- Tariff = ₹7.50/kWh

**Step 1:** Annual energy saved:
$$E_{saved} = 10 \text{ kWh/unit} \times 50,000 \text{ units} = 5,00,000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 2:** Annual monetary savings:
$$\Delta S = 5,00,000 \times 7.50 = \text{₹} 37,50,000 \text{ /year}$$

**Step 3:** Simple Payback Period:
$$PB = \dfrac{I}{\Delta S} = \dfrac{15,00,000}{37,50,000} = 0.4 \text{ years}$$

Converting to months:
$$PB = 0.4 \times 12 = 4.8 \text{ months} \approx 5 \text{ months}$$

> **Conclusion:** The investment recovers in approximately **5 months** — extremely attractive. Any ECM with payback under 2 years is generally considered viable; under 6 months is excellent.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The 10-Step EMP Framework (PDCA-Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Top Management Commitment] --> B[Step 2: Energy Policy Declaration]
    B --> C[Step 3: Appoint Energy Manager and Team]
    C --> D[Step 4: Preliminary Walk-through Audit]
    D --> E[Step 5: Detailed Energy Audit Level 2 or 3]
    E --> F[Step 6: Establish Energy Baseline and EnPIs]
    F --> G[Step 7: Set SMART Goals and Targets]
    G --> H[Step 8: Develop Action Plan with ECMs]
    H --> I[Step 9: Implementation and Use Management]
    I --> J[Step 10: Monitoring Verification and Review]
    J --> K{Performance Improved?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Document and Standardize Best Practices]
    K -- No --> M[Root Cause Analysis and Corrective Action]
    L --> F
    M --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this in an exam:** The bidirectional arrows from `F` (Baseline) back into the loop represent the **PDCA cycle** mandated by ISO 50001. The decision diamond `K` is the **Act** phase. This is the most-likely KTU diagram question.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Use Management: Energy Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Energy Source: Grid / DG / Solar] --> C1[Conversion: Boiler / Compressor / Transformer]
    C1 --> D1[Distribution: Bus / Pipeline / Steam Network]
    D1 --> EU1[End Use: HVAC / Lighting / Motors / Process]
    EU1 --> W1[Wastage: Heat Loss / Leakage / Idle Running]
    EU1 --> P1[Useful Output: Production / Comfort]
    M1[Monitoring: Sub-meters / EMS / SCADA] -.-> C1
    M1 -.-> D1
    M1 -.-> EU1
    O1[Use Management Interventions] --> EU1
    O1 --> C1
    O1 --> D1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "explain Use Management," trace energy from source → conversion → distribution → end-use and identify intervention points at each stage. The dashed lines from `M1` (Monitoring) are the *feedback loops* — they distinguish Use Management from passive operation.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Energy Baseline vs. Actual Performance (CUSUM Visualization Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Historical Data: 12 plus months] --> R[Regression Analysis]
    R --> BL[Energy Baseline Equation: aX plus b]
    BL --> AEB[Adjusted Energy Baseline for current production]
    CUR[Current Month Actual Energy] --> CMP{EnPI comparison}
    AEB --> CMP
    CMP -->|EnPI less than 1| IMP[Performance Improved]
    CMP -->|EnPI equal to 1| STE[Performance Stable]
    CMP -->|EnPI greater than 1| DEG[Performance Degraded]
    IMP --> CUSUM[Add to CUSUM negative]
    STE --> CUSUM
    DEG --> CUSUM
    CUSUM --> ALERT{Trigger alarm if CUSUM exceeds threshold}
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**Define Energy Management Programme (EMP). List any four essential elements of an EMP.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** An Energy Management Programme is a structured, top-management-driven framework of policies, organizational roles, baseline data, action plans, and performance reviews designed to achieve continual improvement in energy performance. It is typically aligned with ISO 50001.
- **Four Essential Elements (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
  1. **Energy Policy** – Signed declaration by top management stating intent, compliance, and continual improvement.
  2. **Energy Baseline (E_base)** – Quantitative reference of past energy use normalized to production.
  3. **Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)** – Metrics like SEC, kWh/unit used to track performance.
  4. **Action Plan with Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)** – Identified projects with budget, responsibility, and timeline.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Energy Manager appointment, Energy Audit, Monitoring & Verification, Management Review.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]
**What is Use Management? State any two operational practices that fall under it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Use Management refers to operational, maintenance, and behavioral practices implemented at the point of energy end-use to ensure efficient utilization, minimize wastage, and align energy consumption with actual functional need.
- **Two Operational Practices (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**
  1. **Switching off idle equipment** to eliminate no-load losses in motors, transformers, and lighting.
  2. **Load scheduling** to shift consumption from peak tariff hours to off-peak hours.

*(Acceptable: steam trap maintenance, compressed air leak survey, VFD installation, operator training.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — EMP Setup and Baseline**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure for setting up an Energy Management Programme in an industrial organization. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The monthly energy consumption and production data of a plant for 4 months are given below. The baseline equation is $E_{base} = 80X + 50,000$ kWh. Compute the Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI) for each month and the CUSUM at the end of April. Comment on the trend. *(7 marks)*

| Month | Production (units) | Actual Energy (kWh) |
|---|---|---|
| January | 5,000 | 5,00,000 |
| February | 6,000 | 5,60,000 |
| March | 7,000 | 6,10,000 |
| April | 8,000 | 6,80,000 |

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

The step-by-step EMP setup is:

1. **Top Management Commitment (0.5 mark):** Formal energy policy signed by CEO; allocation of budget and personnel.
2. **Appointment of Energy Manager (0.5 mark):** BEE-certified (Bureau of Energy Efficiency) energy manager with cross-functional team.
3. **Preliminary Energy Audit (0.5 mark):** Walk-through to identify high-energy zones, quick wins, and data sources.
4. **Detailed Energy Audit (0.5 mark):** Sub-metering, load profiling, end-use breakup — identifies specific ECMs.
5. **Establishment of Baseline (0.5 mark):** Historical regression: $E_{base} = aX + b$, normalized to production, weather, degree-days.
6. **Define EnPIs (0.5 mark):** Specific Energy Consumption, kWh/ton, kWh/m², etc.
7. **Set SMART Goals (0.5 mark):** e.g., "Reduce SEC by 10% in 12 months."
8. **Action Plan (0.5 mark):** List of ECMs with priority, capital, payback, responsibility.
9. **Implementation and Use Management (0.5 mark):** Engineering, operational, behavioral changes.
10. **Monitoring, Verification & Management Review (0.5 mark):** CUSUM tracking, IPMVP-based M&V, quarterly reviews.
11. **Continual Improvement (0.5 mark):** PDCA cycle; setting revised baselines after major changes.
12. **Documentation and Training (0.5 mark):** Procedures, records, awareness programs, ISO 50001 alignment.
13. **Communication (0.5 mark):** Internal and external communication of energy performance.
14. **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** EMP is a continual, cyclical process, not a one-time project.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

Using $AEB_i = 80 \cdot X_i + 50,000$:

**January** ($X = 5,000$):
$$AEB_{Jan} = 80 \times 5,000 + 50,000 = 4,50,000 \text{ kWh}$$
$$EnPI_{Jan} = \dfrac{5,00,000}{4,50,000} = 1.1111$$

**February** ($X = 6,000$):
$$AEB_{Feb} = 80 \times 6,000 + 50,000 = 5,30,000 \text{ kWh}$$
$$EnPI_{Feb} = \dfrac{5,60,000}{5,30,000} = 1.0566$$

**March** ($X = 7,000$):
$$AEB_{Mar} = 80 \times 7,000 + 50,000 = 6,10,000 \text{ kWh}$$
$$EnPI_{Mar} = \dfrac{6,10,000}{6,10,000} = 1.0000$$

**April** ($X = 8,000$):
$$AEB_{Apr} = 80 \times 8,000 + 50,000 = 6,90,000 \text{ kWh}$$
$$EnPI_{Apr} = \dfrac{6,80,000}{6,90,000} = 0.9855$$

**CUSUM Calculation:**

$$\Delta E_{Jan} = 5,00,000 - 4,50,000 = +50,000$$
$$\Delta E_{Feb} = 5,60,000 - 5,30,000 = +30,000$$
$$\Delta E_{Mar} = 6,10,000 - 6,10,000 = 0$$
$$\Delta E_{Apr} = 6,80,000 - 6,90,000 = -10,000$$

$$CUSUM_{Apr} = 50,000 + 30,000 + 0 + (-10,000) = +70,000 \text{ kWh}$$

**Trend Commentary (1.5 marks for interpretation):**
- January had the worst performance (11.11% above baseline).
- Performance steadily improved from January through April.
- April is the first month with $EnPI < 1$, indicating **energy performance has crossed the baseline** — a positive milestone.
- CUSUM remains positive (+70,000 kWh) because the cumulative excess in the first three months is not yet fully offset by the April gain.
- **Recommendation:** Continue EMP interventions; within 1–2 more months of performance, CUSUM should turn negative, signalling a sustained improvement regime.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Use Management Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]**
**(a)** Explain the four pillars of Use Management with at least two examples under each pillar. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 50 HP (37.3 kW) induction motor operates for 6,000 hours/year. Currently it is operating at 80% loading with an efficiency of 88%. Replacement with a premium IE3 motor offers an efficiency of 93%. Electricity tariff is ₹8.00/kWh. Calculate: (i) annual energy consumption of both motors, (ii) annual cost saving, and (iii) simple payback period if the new motor costs ₹2,50,000 more than the existing one. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

The **four pillars of Use Management** are:

1. **Operations Management (2 marks):** Practices that optimize the *running* condition of equipment.
   - *Example 1:* Switching off idle motors, transformers, and lighting (no-load loss elimination).
   - *Example 2:* Use of Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) on pumps and fans to match speed to load (affinity law savings: $P \propto N^3$).

2. **Maintenance Management (2 marks):** Practices that preserve equipment efficiency over time.
   - *Example 1:* Periodic cleaning of condenser tubes and cooling coils in HVAC/chillers — a 1 mm scale layer can increase energy use by 5–10%.
   - *Example 2:* Compressed air leak survey and repair — leaks in typical Indian plants account for 20–30% of compressor energy.

3. **Technology Upgrades (1.5 marks):** Replacement of inefficient equipment.
   - *Example 1:* Replacement of standard efficiency motors with IE3/IE4 class motors.
   - *Example 2:* LED replacement for conventional lighting (50–70% reduction in lighting energy).

4. **Behavioral / Human Aspects (1.5 marks):** Training, awareness, motivation.
   - *Example 1:* Displaying real-time SEC at operator stations and running "switch-off" campaigns.
   - *Example 2:* Suggestion schemes and energy-efficiency incentive programs for employees.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- Motor rating = 50 HP = 37.3 kW (using 1 HP = 0.746 kW)
- Annual operating hours = 6,000 h
- Loading = 80% (so actual output power = 37.3 × 0.80 = 29.84 kW)
- Efficiency of existing motor = 88%
- Efficiency of IE3 motor = 93%
- Tariff = ₹8.00/kWh
- Extra cost of new motor = ₹2,50,000

**(i) Annual Energy Consumption**

**Existing Motor (88%):**
$$E_{existing} = \dfrac{P_{output}}{\eta_{old}} \times h = \dfrac{29.84}{0.88} \times 6,000$$
$$E_{existing} = 33.91 \times 6,000 = 2,03,454.5 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**IE3 Motor (93%):**
$$E_{new} = \dfrac{29.84}{0.93} \times 6,000 = 32.09 \times 6,000 = 1,92,516.1 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**(ii) Annual Cost Saving**

$$\Delta E = E_{existing} - E_{new} = 2,03,454.5 - 1,92,516.1 = 10,938.4 \text{ kWh/year}$$

$$\Delta S = 10,938.4 \times 8.00 = \text{₹} 87,507.2 \text{ /year}$$

**(iii) Simple Payback Period**

$$PB = \dfrac{I}{\Delta S} = \dfrac{2,50,000}{87,507.2} = 2.857 \text{ years}$$

$$\boxed{PB \approx 2 \text{ years and } 10 \text{ months}}$$

**Conclusion:** The IE3 motor upgrade pays back in less than 3 years — an excellent Use Management intervention. It also reduces CO₂ emissions by approximately:
$$\Delta CO_2 = 10,938.4 \times 0.82 = 8,969.5 \text{ kg CO}_2 \text{ /year} \approx 8.97 \text{ tCO}_2\text{/year}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing kW and HP:** 1 HP = 0.746 kW (not 0.75 kW, not 0.746 kW exactly, but in KTU exams, 0.746 is the accepted factor).
> 2. **Forgetting to apply loading:** Many students use 37.3 kW as the input directly — WRONG. Multiply by loading factor first.
> 3. **Unit confusion:** Energy is in kWh; input power formula is $P_{input} = P_{output} / \eta$.
> 4. **Mixing up ΔE and E_{saved}:** ΔE refers to the absolute difference; percentage savings = $\Delta E / E_{existing} \times 100$.
> 5. **Omitting units in final answer:** Always write kWh/year, ₹/year, years.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — 60-Second Summary**

- [x] **EMP is a framework, not a one-time project.** It follows the **PDCA cycle** of ISO 50001.
- [x] **10 key steps of EMP setup:** Commitment → Policy → Manager → Audit → Baseline → EnPIs → Goals → Action Plan → Implementation → M&V/Review.
- [x] **Baseline equation:** $E_{base} = aX + b$; obtained via least-squares regression on ≥12 months of data.
- [x] **EnPI** = $E_{actual} / AEB$; target EnPI < 1.0 means improvement.
- [x] **CUSUM** = cumulative sum of $(E_{actual} - AEB)$; positive = drifting worse, negative = improving.
- [x] **SEC** = $E_{total} / P_{output}$ — the most common efficiency metric in KTU problems.
- [x] **Simple Payback** = Investment / Annual Savings; <2 years = excellent, <5 years = good, >7 years = risky.
- [x] **Use Management pillars:** Operations, Maintenance, Technology, Behavioral (4 pillars).
- [x] **Key constant:** 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ; 1 HP = 0.746 kW; India grid EF ≈ 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh.
- [x] **ISO 50001 + IPMVP** are the international standards most often referenced in EMP answers.
- [x] **Behavior matters:** Studies show 5–15% savings are achievable purely through operator awareness — a free ECM.
- [x] **Compressed air leaks and steam trap failures** are the two most common "hidden" losses in Indian industry.
- [x] **Affinity laws:** $P \propto N^3$ — a 20% speed reduction saves nearly 50% power in fans/pumps. **This is a guaranteed 3-mark question in at least one KTU paper per year.**
- [x] **Three-phase power:** $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ — memorize it.
- [x] **BEE-certified Energy Manager** is a statutory requirement for designated energy-intensive industries in India (PAT scheme — Perform, Achieve, Trade).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electricity Saving Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electricity Saving Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electricity Saving Techniques** refer to a systematic set of engineering, operational, and managerial practices employed in industrial, commercial, and residential facilities to **reduce the consumption of electrical energy** (in kWh) and **lower the maximum demand** (in kVA or kW) drawn from the utility grid, without compromising the end-use utility, productivity, comfort, or safety of the installation.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, electricity saving techniques are classified under the broader umbrella of **Demand-Side Management (DSM)** and are implemented across four cardinal pillars:

1. **Energy Efficiency (EE)** – Doing the same work with less energy.
2. **Demand Response (DR)** – Shifting/shedding load to off-peak hours.
3. **Power Quality Improvement** – Minimizing wasteful reactive power.
4. **Load Factor Enhancement** – Smoothing the load curve to improve utilisation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your **monthly electricity bill as a water tap** that you cannot see. The water flowing out is energy in kWh, and the size of the pipe (maximum flow rate at any instant) is the maximum demand in kVA. If you let the tap run carelessly, your bucket (bill) fills quickly and you pay more. **Electricity saving techniques are the various "taps" you install** — such as fixing leaks (power factor correction), using a smaller pipe when possible (load scheduling), replacing the old, leaky tap with a new aerated one (energy-efficient motors/LEDs), and remembering to close the tap when not in use (occupancy sensors).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two parameters matter:** A consumer's electricity bill has two components in HT (High Tension) industrial connections — **Energy Charge (₹/kWh)** and **Demand Charge (₹/kVA)**. Effective electricity saving must address **both** components simultaneously.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Electricity Saving

- **Maximum Demand (MD)** – Highest average kVA drawn in any 15-minute (or 30-minute) window during the billing month. Unit: **kVA** or **kW**.
- **Power Factor (pf)** – Cosine of the phase angle between voltage and current. Unit: **dimensionless (0 to 1)**. The Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) penalises pf below **0.90** lag.
- **Load Factor (LF)** – Ratio of average load to peak load. Unit: **dimensionless**.
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** – Energy used per unit of production. Unit: **kWh/ton, kWh/m³, kWh/unit**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 expects students to compute **payback period**, **return on investment**, and quantify savings from at least three techniques: (i) Power factor correction, (ii) Maximum demand control, and (iii) Energy-efficient equipment replacement.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchy of Electricity Saving Techniques

The techniques are best understood in a layered hierarchy from **plant-level** (largest impact) to **equipment-level** (smallest, but most numerous):

**Layer 1 – System-Level Optimisation**
- Maximum Demand (MD) control
- Load scheduling / peak clipping
- Power factor improvement (capacitor banks)
- Transformer right-sizing
- Distribution cable loss reduction

**Layer 2 – Equipment-Level Replacement**
- High-efficiency motors (IE3/IE4 class)
- Variable Frequency Drives (VFD) for pumps and fans
- Energy-efficient transformers (amorphous core)
- LED / induction / plasma lighting
- Inverter-grade air conditioners

**Layer 3 – Operational & Behavioural**
- Switching off idle equipment
- Optimising HVAC set-points (each 1 °C ≈ 6 % saving)
- Daylight harvesting
- Energy audits and monitoring (ISO 50001)

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Major Technique

| Technique | Core Physical Principle | Why It Saves Electricity |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Factor Correction** | Capacitors supply reactive current locally, so the source current $I$ drops for the same real power $P$ | Reduces $I^2R$ line losses, frees up transformer capacity, avoids pf penalty |
| **Maximum Demand Control** | Distributes large loads over time | Lowers the highest 15-min kVA reading → lower demand charge |
| **Energy-Efficient Motors** | Lower copper and iron losses at the same shaft output | Every kW loss saved is a kW of billing saved |
| **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** | Pump/fan affinity laws: $P \propto N^3$ | A 20 % speed reduction yields ~50 % power saving |
| **LED Lighting** | Luminous efficacy ~100–150 lm/W vs 15 lm/W for incandescent | Same lumens at ~1/6 the wattage |
| **Transformer Optimisation** | Iron losses occur 24×7; copper losses scale with $S^2$ | Operating near 75 % load minimises total loss |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **frequently asked** in KTU university examinations. Memorise the variable meanings and units before the exam.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | Real power | kW | Power drawn |
| 2 | $S = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L$ | Apparent power | kVA | Demand charge basis |
| 3 | $Q = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \sin\phi$ | Reactive power | kVAR | Capacitor sizing |
| 4 | $\cos\phi_1 = \text{old pf},\ \cos\phi_2 = \text{new pf}$ | Power factor values | – | PF correction |
| 5 | $Q_c = P (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ | Required capacitor | kVAR | Capacitor bank rating |
| 6 | $kVAR = kW (\tan\cos^{-1}\text{pf}_1 - \tan\cos^{-1}\text{pf}_2)$ | Alternate form | kVAR | KSEB-type problem |
| 7 | $I^2R$ line loss $\propto \dfrac{1}{\cos^2\phi}$ | Loss relation | – | Quantifying PF loss savings |
| 8 | $\text{Line loss reduction} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}\right)^2$ | Fractional loss cut | – | % loss saved |
| 9 | $\text{Annual MD savings} = (MD_{\text{old}} - MD_{\text{new}}) \times C_d \times 12$ | MD charge saving | ₹/yr | Demand control economics |
| 10 | $\text{Energy saving} = kW \left(\dfrac{1}{\eta_{\text{old}}} - \dfrac{1}{\eta_{\text{new}}}\right) \times h \times C_e$ | Motor retrofit | ₹/yr | Motor replacement |
| 11 | $\text{Transformer loss} = P_{\text{iron}} + P_{\text{cu}} \left(\dfrac{S}{S_{\text{rated}}}\right)^2$ | Total loss | W or kW | Right-sizing transformer |
| 12 | $\text{Payback Period} = \dfrac{\text{Investment (₹)}}{\text{Annual Savings (₹/yr)}}$ | Years | yr | Financial viability |
| 13 | $\text{Load Factor} = \dfrac{\text{Average Load (kW)}}{\text{Peak Load (kW)}}$ | Smoothness index | – | Tariff optimisation |
| 14 | $P_{\text{saved}} = P_{\text{rated}} \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{N_{\text{new}}}{N_{\text{rated}}}\right)^3\right]$ | Affinity law | kW | VFD on pump/fan |
| 15 | $\text{Lighting saving} = (W_{\text{old}} - W_{\text{new}}) \times N \times h \times C_e$ | Per fitting | ₹/yr | LED retrofit |

**Boundary Conditions & Validity:**
- Formula 5 is valid only for **lagging** power factor correction.
- For the **affinity law** (Formula 14), it is valid for **variable torque loads** (centrifugal pumps, fans, blowers) — *not* for constant torque loads like conveyors and compressors.
- Payback Period < **2 years** is generally considered attractive for an industrial energy project.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** In a typical Indian HT industrial consumer (say, a Kerala rubber or coir factory), power factor correction from 0.80 to 0.95 can save **3–5 % of the total energy bill** *plus* eliminate the KSEB pf penalty. MD control through load staggering can cut the demand charge by another **10–15 %**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Required Capacitor kVAR for Power Factor Correction

**Problem Context:** A factory draws $P = 500 \text{ kW}$ at a lagging power factor of 0.80. The KSEB tariff penalises pf below 0.95. Determine the capacitor bank rating required to improve pf from 0.80 to 0.95, the resulting line-loss reduction, and the per-cycle reactive current relief.

**Step 1 – Convert power factors to phase angles.**

$$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.80) = 36.87^{\circ}$$

$$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19^{\circ}$$

**Step 2 – Evaluate tangent of both angles.**

$$\tan\phi_1 = \tan(36.87^{\circ}) = 0.7500$$

$$\tan\phi_2 = \tan(18.19^{\circ}) = 0.3287$$

**Step 3 – Apply the standard kVAR formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_c &= P \times (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2) \\
&= 500 \times (0.7500 - 0.3287) \\
&= 500 \times 0.4213 \\
&= 210.65 \text{ kVAR}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** A capacitor bank of approximately **211 kVAR** must be installed. Banks are typically selected in standard sizes — 200 kVAR + 12.5 kVAR switched stages is a common choice.

**Step 4 – Compute the line-loss reduction factor.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Loss Ratio} &= \left(\dfrac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}\right)^2 \\
&= \left(\dfrac{0.80}{0.95}\right)^2 \\
&= (0.8421)^2 \\
&= 0.7091
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the line losses after correction are **70.91 %** of the original losses, and the savings in $I^2R$ losses equal:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Loss reduction} &= 1 - 0.7091 \\
&= 0.2909 \\
&= 29.09\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A pf improvement from 0.80 to 0.95 reduces line current by ~16 % and copper losses by **~29 %**. This is why a one-time capacitor investment pays back in **6–14 months** in most Indian HT industries.

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — Maximum Demand Control Savings

**Problem Context:** A textile mill has a contract demand of 800 kVA. Its monthly billing MD is 750 kVA, charged at ₹450 per kVA per month. By staggering the operation of two 100 kW boilers (sequential instead of simultaneous), the recorded MD can be capped at 690 kVA. Compute the annual MD savings.

**Step 1 – Identify the reduction in MD.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta MD &= MD_{\text{old}} - MD_{\text{new}} \\
&= 750 - 690 \\
&= 60 \text{ kVA}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 – Apply the MD saving formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual MD Savings} &= \Delta MD \times C_d \times 12 \\
&= 60 \times 450 \times 12 \\
&= 3,24,000 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 – If the staggering required an automatic timer panel costing ₹2,00,000, calculate payback period.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Payback} &= \dfrac{2,00,000}{3,24,000} \\
&= 0.617 \text{ years} \\
&\approx 7.4 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Trick:** The KSEB (and most Indian DISCOMs) measure MD as the **highest 15-minute** (or sometimes 30-minute) integrated demand. Any load that runs for **less than 15 minutes** consecutively will *not* affect MD — useful for thermal loads.

---

### 3.3 Derivation C — Energy-Efficient Motor Replacement

**Problem Context:** A 15 kW standard-efficiency induction motor (η_old = 87 %) operates 16 hours/day, 300 days/year. It is to be replaced by an IE3 premium-efficiency motor (η_new = 92.5 %). Electricity cost is ₹7 per kWh. Compute annual energy and money savings, and payback if the new motor costs ₹45,000 extra.

**Step 1 – Input power drawn by the old motor.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{old, in}} &= \dfrac{P_{\text{shaft}}}{\eta_{\text{old}}} \\
&= \dfrac{15}{0.87} \\
&= 17.241 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 – Input power drawn by the new motor.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{new, in}} &= \dfrac{15}{0.925} \\
&= 16.216 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 – Power saved per hour of operation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= 17.241 - 16.216 \\
&= 1.025 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 – Annual energy saved.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= \Delta P \times h \times d \\
&= 1.025 \times 16 \times 300 \\
&= 4920 \text{ kWh/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 – Annual money saved.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= 4920 \times 7 \\
&= 34,440 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 – Simple payback period.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Payback} &= \dfrac{45,000}{34,440} \\
&= 1.307 \text{ years} \\
&\approx 15.7 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Numerical Pattern:** The university regularly sets a **two-part question** — one on **power factor correction** (7 marks) and one on **motor replacement payback** (7 marks). Memorise the three sub-steps: input power → energy saved → ₹ saved → payback.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Technique

| Selection Criterion | Best Technique | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Lowest capital, fastest payback | Power factor correction | Payback often 6–14 months |
| High kWh consumption, motors dominant | Premium-efficiency motor retrofit | 2–4 % energy cut per motor |
| Variable flow pumps / fans | VFD installation | Cubic saving through affinity law |
| Tariff has heavy demand charge | MD control via load shifting | Direct ₹/kVA reduction |
| Large lighting load (> 30 % of bill) | LED retrofit | 50–70 % lighting energy cut |
| Old distribution network | Re-conductoring, power factor correction | $I^2R$ loss reduction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** Always begin an energy audit with a **walk-through audit** to identify the largest kWh sinks, then move to **targeted retrofits**. A purely capital-driven approach (cheapest equipment first) often misses 80 % of the potential savings.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Electricity Saving Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electricity Saving Techniques] --> B[System Level Optimisation]
    A --> C[Equipment Level Retrofit]
    A --> D[Operational and Behavioural]

    B --> B1[Maximum Demand Control]
    B --> B2[Power Factor Improvement]
    B --> B3[Load Scheduling]
    B --> B4[Transformer Right Sizing]

    C --> C1[IE3 IE4 Efficient Motors]
```
