```mermaid
    C --> C2[Variable Frequency Drives]
    C --> C3[LED Lighting Systems]
    C --> C4[Energy Efficient Transformers]

    D --> D1[Switch Off Idle Loads]
    D --> D2[HVAC Set Point Optimisation]
    D --> D3[Daylight Harvesting]
    D --> D4[ISO 50001 Energy Monitoring]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Power Factor Correction Sub-System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Load[Industrial Load: Motors, Furnaces, Lighting] -->|Demands Q kVAR| Bus[Main LT Bus Bar]
    Bus --> Meter[Energy Meter kWh and kVA]
    Bus --> Cap[Automatic Capacitor Bank with APFC Controller]
    Cap -->|Supplies Qc kVAR locally| Bus
    Bus -->|Reduced current I| Grid[Utility Grid KSEB]
    Meter --> Tariff[Bill: Energy plus Demand plus PF Penalty]
    Cap -. Monitors PF .-> Bus
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The capacitor bank injects reactive power **at the load end**, so the current that flows through the energy meter — and hence through every upstream cable and transformer — is *smaller* than before. This single act reduces three losses simultaneously: cable $I^2R$, transformer copper loss, and demand charge.

### 4.3 Decision Flow — Selecting an Energy-Efficient Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Existing Standard Motor Identified] --> Q1{Operating Hours per Year greater than 3000}
    Q1 -- No --> Reject[Continue with Old Motor and Maintain]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Load Factor above 60 percent}
    Q2 -- No --> VFD[Install VFD Instead of Motor Replacement]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Estimate Annual Energy Saving in kWh]
    Q3 --> Q4[Compute Simple Payback in Years]
    Q4 --> Q5{Payback less than 2 Years}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Buy[Procure IE3 or IE4 Premium Motor]
    Q5 -- No --> Hold[Defer Replacement, Re evaluate in One Year]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Heuristic:** Motors operating **< 2000 h/yr** rarely justify premium-efficiency replacement unless electricity cost is exceptionally high. Motors running **> 5000 h/yr** *almost always* justify it, regardless of cost.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three electricity saving techniques applicable to an industrial HT consumer and state the energy bill component each technique primarily targets.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Power Factor Correction using capacitor banks** → targets the **reactive power (kVAR) penalty** and reduces $I^2R$ losses in cables and transformers.
2. **Maximum Demand Control via load staggering** → targets the **demand charge (₹/kVA/month)** by flattening the load curve.
3. **Replacement of standard motors with IE3/IE4 premium motors** → targets the **energy charge (₹/kWh)** by reducing the input kW drawn for the same shaft output.
*(1 mark per correct pair, technique + bill component.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Load Factor. Why is a higher load factor beneficial for an industrial consumer?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$\text{Load Factor} = \dfrac{\text{Average Load over a period}}{\text{Peak Load in the same period}}$$

A higher load factor (closer to 1) means the consumer uses energy **more evenly** over time, which:
- **Lowers the average cost of generation** for the utility (and hence the consumer in the long run).
- **Improves transformer and cable utilisation**, reducing the average $I^2R$ loss per kWh consumed.
- **Avoids demand penalties** since the peak-to-average gap is small.
*(1 mark definition, 2 marks explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> • In power factor correction problems, students frequently **forget to convert degrees to radians** when pressing a calculator — keep the calculator in **degree mode**.
> • For payback period, **always include all costs** (equipment + installation + downtime), not just the equipment cost.
> • When asked "**to which value pf should be improved?**" — the **target pf is always 0.95–0.98** (not unity), because over-correction leads to a **leading pf** which is *also* penalised by the utility.
> • Always write the **formula in symbolic form first**, then substitute numbers — full marks are awarded only when the formula is visible.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]*

A small-scale industry draws an average real power of **400 kW** from a 3-phase, 415 V supply at a lagging power factor of **0.78**. The KSEB tariff specifies:
- Energy charge: ₹6.50 per kWh
- Demand charge: ₹400 per kVA per month
- Power factor penalty: 1 % of energy charge for every 0.01 below 0.90 pf.

The industry operates **24 hours/day, 26 days/month**. The management proposes to install an automatic power factor correction (APFC) panel to raise the pf to **0.96**.

**(a)** Calculate the **rating of the capacitor bank** (in kVAR) required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **annual monetary savings** from the reduced $I^2R$ losses, the eliminated pf penalty, and the reduced demand charge. Assume the investment on the APFC panel is ₹2.5 lakh and compute the **simple payback period**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to A(a) — Capacitor Rating [7 marks]**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert pf values to phase angles.

$$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.78) = 38.74^{\circ}$$

$$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.96) = 16.26^{\circ}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Evaluate tangents.

$$\tan\phi_1 = \tan(38.74^{\circ}) = 0.8023$$

$$\tan\phi_2 = \tan(16.26^{\circ}) = 0.2917$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply kVAR formula.

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_c &= P \times (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2) \\
&= 400 \times (0.8023 - 0.2917) \\
&= 400 \times 0.5106 \\
&= 204.24 \text{ kVAR}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Select a practical bank size.

A standard **200 kVAR APFC** with stepped switching is selected.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Compute loss reduction in cables.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Loss Ratio} &= \left(\dfrac{0.78}{0.96}\right)^2 = (0.8125)^2 = 0.6602 \\
\text{Loss reduction} &= 1 - 0.6602 = 0.3398 \approx 34\%
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Solution to A(b) — Annual Savings and Payback [7 marks]**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute line-loss energy saving.

Before pf correction, line $I^2R$ loss scales as $1/\cos^2\phi_1 = 1/0.6084 = 1.6437$ (p.u.).
After correction, $1/\cos^2\phi_2 = 1/0.9216 = 1.0851$ (p.u.).
The line losses drop to $1.0851/1.6437 = 66.02\%$ of original → **33.98 % saving**.

Assume $I^2R$ losses at pf = 0.78 were **5 %** of input energy.
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Energy input per month} &= 400 \times 24 \times 26 = 249{,}600 \text{ kWh} \\
\text{Loss energy at 0.78 pf} &= 0.05 \times 249{,}600 = 12{,}480 \text{ kWh} \\
\text{Loss energy at 0.96 pf} &= 0.6602 \times 12{,}480 = 8{,}240 \text{ kWh} \\
\text{Loss energy saved} &= 12{,}480 - 8{,}240 = 4{,}240 \text{ kWh/month}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Money saved per month from loss reduction.

$$4{,}240 \times 6.50 = \text{₹ }27{,}560 \text{ per month} \approx \text{₹ }3{,}30{,}720 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** PF penalty eliminated.

At pf = 0.78, the deficit below 0.90 is 0.12 → 12 % penalty.
$$\text{Penalty} = 0.12 \times 0.01 \times 6.50 \times 249{,}600 = \text{₹ }19{,}469 \text{ per month}$$
Annual pf penalty saved: **₹ 2,33,628** per year.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Demand charge reduction.

$$\text{Old kVA} = 400/0.78 = 512.82 \text{ kVA}$$
$$\text{New kVA} = 400/0.96 = 416.67 \text{ kVA}$$
$$\Delta \text{kVA} = 96.15 \text{ kVA} \times 400 \times 12 = \text{₹ }4{,}61{,}520 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Total annual savings and payback.

$$\text{Total} = 3{,}30{,}720 + 2{,}33{,}628 + 4{,}61{,}520 = \text{₹ }10{,}25{,}868 \text{ per year}$$

$$\text{Payback} = \dfrac{2{,}50{,}000}{10{,}25{,}868} = 0.244 \text{ years} \approx 2.9 \text{ months}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** A payback **under 1 year** is the strongest possible justification for any project — examiners will award **full marks** for explicitly comparing it to a benchmark (e.g., "Payback of 2.9 months is well below the 2-year industrial benchmark").

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

A **15 kW, 3-phase induction motor** in a chemical plant operates **18 hours/day, 300 days/year** at an average load of **80 % of its rated capacity**. Its present efficiency is **86 %**. A new IE3 motor with efficiency **91 %** is available at a price difference of **₹42,000** (including installation). Electricity cost is **₹7.50 per kWh**.

**(a)** Calculate the **annual energy savings (kWh)** and the **annual monetary savings (₹)** from replacing the old motor with the IE3 motor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **simple payback period** and the **net present value over 5 years** at a discount rate of **10 %/year**. Comment on the project's attractiveness. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to B(a) — Energy and Money Saved [7 marks]**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Shaft output of the motor (running at 80 % load).

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = 15 \times 0.80 = 12 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Input power drawn by old motor.

$$P_{\text{old, in}} = \dfrac{12}{0.86} = 13.953 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Input power drawn by new motor.

$$P_{\text{new, in}} = \dfrac{12}{0.91} = 13.187 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Power saved.

$$\Delta P = 13.953 - 13.187 = 0.766 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Annual energy saved.

$$\Delta E = 0.766 \times 18 \times 300 = 4{,}136.4 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark, completes the 7):** Annual monetary savings.

$$\text{Savings} = 4{,}136.4 \times 7.50 = \text{₹ }31{,}023 \text{ per year}$$

---

**Solution to B(b) — Payback and NPV [7 marks]**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Simple payback period.

$$\text{Payback} = \dfrac{42{,}000}{31{,}023} = 1.354 \text{ years} \approx 16.2 \text{ months}$$

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Set up NPV calculation.

The uniform annual savings $A = \text{₹ }31{,}023$ over 5 years at discount rate $r = 10\%$. The present value of a uniform series is:

$$PV = A \times \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the annuity factor.

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.10)^5 &= 1.6105 \\
\text{Annuity Factor} &= \dfrac{1.6105 - 1}{0.10 \times 1.6105} = \dfrac{0.6105}{0.16105} = 3.7908
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute NPV.

$$NPV = -42{,}000 + 31{,}023 \times 3.7908 = -42{,}000 + 1{,}17{,}600 = \text{₹ }75{,}600$$

> [!TIP]
> **NPV > 0** confirms the project is economically attractive. A positive NPV of ₹75,600 over 5 years (≈ 1.8× the investment) is considered an **excellent** energy-conservation project by any industrial financial benchmark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Electricity Saving Techniques**

- **Electricity bill = Energy Charge + Demand Charge + PF Penalty (if any).** Every saving technique must be mapped to **one or more** of these three components.
- **Power Factor Correction** is the **fastest-paying** technique. Formula: $Q_c = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$. Target pf = 0.95–0.98 (lagging). Never over-correct to leading.
- **Maximum Demand Control** lowers the recorded kVA by **load staggering** or **automatic load shedding**. Formula: $\text{MD Saving} = \Delta MD \times C_d \times 12$.
- **Energy-Efficient Motor** selection criterion: replace only if **annual operating hours > 3000**. Formula for saving: $kW \left(\dfrac{1}{\eta_{\text{old}}} - \dfrac{1}{\eta_{\text{new}}}\right) \times h \times C_e$.
- **VFD Affinity Law** is valid **only for variable torque loads** (pumps, fans, blowers). A 20 % speed reduction yields **~49 % power saving** (not 20 %).
- **LED lighting** delivers ~100 lm/W versus ~15 lm/W for GLS lamps — typically **50–70 %** lighting energy cut.
- **Transformer loss** has two parts: constant $P_{\text{iron}}$ and load-dependent $P_{\text{cu}} \times (S/S_{\text{rated}})^2$. Operation near **75 %** loading generally minimises total loss.
- **Payback Period** = Investment / Annual Savings. **< 2 years** is attractive, **< 1 year** is excellent.
- **Load Factor** improvement → better transformer utilisation, lower average cost per kWh.
- **Behavioural measures** (switching off, set-point optimisation) cost almost nothing and yield **5–10 %** immediate savings.
- **ISO 50001** Energy Management System is the international standard for **sustaining** electricity savings year after year.
- **Exam formula priority list** (in order of frequency asked in KTU 2024):
  1. $Q_c = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$
  2. $\text{Payback} = \text{Investment} / \text{Annual Savings}$
  3. $\Delta E = kW \left(\dfrac{1}{\eta_{\text{old}}} - \dfrac{1}{\eta_{\text{new}}}\right) \times h \times d$
  4. $P_{\text{saved}} = P_{\text{rated}} \left[1 - (N_{\text{new}}/N_{\text{rated}})^3\right]$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** In every numerical problem, always present the answer with **(a) Formula (1 mark), (b) Substitution (2 marks), (c) Final Answer with units (1 mark)**. A problem split into 3 such sub-steps reliably secures the full 7 marks per sub-part.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Management and Audit: Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Management and Audit: Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Energy Management

**Energy Management (EM)** is defined as the strategic, systematic, and coordinated set of activities undertaken by an organization to plan, operate, monitor, and continuously improve its energy performance. It involves the formulation of policies, allocation of resources, and implementation of energy-efficient practices across all levels of an enterprise to achieve optimum energy utilization while minimizing environmental impact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition (Aligned with ISO 50001:2018):**
> *"Energy Management is the management of energy use in an organization or facility to optimize energy consumption, reduce costs, and ensure sustainability through systematic planning, implementation, and verification of energy-efficient practices."*

The three core pillars of Energy Management are:
1. **Energy Planning** – Establishing baselines, energy review, and setting objectives.
2. **Energy Operation & Control** – Real-time monitoring, controls, and operational discipline.
3. **Performance Evaluation & Improvement** – Auditing, measurement, and continuous improvement (PDCA Cycle).

---

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Energy Audit

An **Energy Audit (EA)** is a systematic, documented inspection, survey, and analysis of energy flows within a defined system, facility, building, process, or organization. It is conducted with the primary objective of identifying energy conservation opportunities (ECOs), quantifying energy losses, and recommending cost-effective energy-efficient measures.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> *"An Energy Audit is a comprehensive study of energy utilization in a system or facility, aimed at identifying the quantity and cost of energy used, suggesting ways to conserve energy, and recommending measures for improving energy efficiency."*

**Key audit deliverables include:**
- Energy balance / Sankey diagram of the facility
- Identification of Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)
- Cost-benefit analysis and payback calculations
- Compliance with Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) standards

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Analogy: Managing a Household Monthly Budget vs. Hiring a Financial Auditor**

Imagine you run a household with a fixed monthly salary. You notice your electricity bills have suddenly shot up.

- **Energy Management** is like your *ongoing monthly budgeting habit* — you track every rupee spent, set limits on AC usage, switch to LED bulbs, monitor your meter daily, and constantly tweak your lifestyle to spend less. It is a **continuous, proactive discipline**.

- **Energy Audit**, on the other hand, is like calling in a *professional financial auditor* once a year. The auditor opens every receipt, inspects every appliance, finds the "leaking tap" (say, an old refrigerator consuming 5 kWh/day), and gives you a detailed report: *“Replace this fridge, payback in 18 months, you save ₹2,400/year.”*

In short:
- **Energy Management = Daily Discipline (Prevention)**
- **Energy Audit = Periodic Diagnosis (Detection + Cure)**

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exam:** *EM = Management (Mindset); EA = Audit (Assessment)*. Energy Management is the **policy/strategy**, while Energy Audit is the **diagnostic tool** that feeds data into the management system.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Calorific Value of Furnace Oil | **10,200 kcal/kg** |
| Calorific Value of Coal (Bituminous) | **6,500–8,000 kcal/kg** |
| Calorific Value of Natural Gas | **8,500–9,500 kcal/Nm³** |
| 1 kWh (electrical) | **860 kcal** (or **3.6 MJ**) |
| 1 Tonne of Refrigeration (TR) | **3.516 kW** |
| Standard Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) | **kWh per unit of production** |
| Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI) | **MWh / tonne or GJ / unit** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Energy Management into Logical Steps

Energy Management is governed by the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) Cycle**, also called the **Deming Wheel**, which is the backbone of ISO 50001.

1. **Plan (Energy Planning):**
   - Conduct an initial energy review.
   - Establish the **Energy Baseline (EnB)** — a quantitative reference point.
   - Identify **Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)**.
   - Set measurable energy objectives and targets.
   - Formulate an **Energy Policy** signed by top management.

2. **Do (Implementation & Operation):**
   - Deploy trained energy managers and operational controls.
   - Implement planned Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs).
   - Operate and maintain energy-consuming equipment efficiently.
   - Document all processes for traceability.

3. **Check (Performance Evaluation):**
   - Monitor, measure, and analyze energy consumption against EnB.
   - Conduct periodic **Energy Audits** (Walk-through, Detailed, Investment-Grade).
   - Investigate deviations and root causes.

4. **Act (Improvement):**
   - Take corrective actions for non-conformities.
   - Implement new ECMs.
   - Review the Energy Policy and update the EnB if necessary.
   - Loop back into Plan for continuous improvement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Why** behind PDCA: It transforms energy management from a one-time project into a self-sustaining organizational discipline. The **How**: Each phase generates measurable data that feeds the next.

---

## 2.2 Decomposition of Energy Audit into Logical Steps (Methodology)

A standard Energy Audit follows the **ASME / BEE Methodology**, comprising 5 phases:

1. **Phase 1 – Pre-Audit Planning:** Review historical bills, define audit scope, gather drawings.
2. **Phase 2 – Walk-Through Audit:** Preliminary visual inspection, identify obvious wastage.
3. **Phase 3 – Detailed Energy Audit:** Instrument-based measurements (power analyzer, flue gas analyzer, lux meter), energy balance preparation.
4. **Phase 4 – Data Analysis & ECM Identification:** Quantify losses, propose conservation measures with technical feasibility.
5. **Phase 5 – Reporting & Implementation Plan:** Final audit report with cost-benefit analysis, payback periods, and prioritized recommendations.

---

## 2.3 Classification of Energy Audits (KTU High-Yield)

| Audit Type | Scope | Duration | Instrument Use | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Walk-Through / Preliminary Audit** | Macro-level identification of wastage | 1–3 days | Minimal | List of low-cost/no-cost measures |
| **Detailed Energy Audit** | In-depth metering of every load center | 2–6 weeks | Heavy (power loggers, thermal imagers) | Energy balance + quantified ECMs |
| **Investment-Grade Audit (IGA)** | Project-financing level precision | 2–6 months | Very high precision | Bankable project report with NPV, IRR |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **HIGH-FREQUENCY** in KTU University Exams (asked almost every semester in some form).

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = P \times t$ | Basic Energy Consumption | kWh |
| 2 | $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{\text{Production Output}}$ | Specific Energy Consumption | kWh / tonne |
| 3 | $\text{Energy Cost Savings} = \Delta E \times C_{\text{unit}}$ | Annual monetary savings | ₹ / year |
| 4 | $\text{SPP} = \dfrac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$ | Simple Payback Period | years |
| 5 | $\text{ROI (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Savings}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100$ | Return on Investment | % |
| 6 | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | Net Present Value | ₹ |
| 7 | $\text{IRR} \Rightarrow \text{NPV} = 0$ | Internal Rate of Return | % |
| 8 | $\text{Energy Loss in Transformer} = P_0 + P_k \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{L_{\text{rated}}}\right)^2$ | No-load + Load loss | kW |
| 9 | $\text{EnPI Reduction (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{EnPI}_{\text{new}} - \text{EnPI}_{\text{baseline}}}{\text{EnPI}_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100$ | Energy Performance Improvement | % |
| 10 | $\text{Boiler Efficiency (\%)} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{absorbed by steam}}}{Q_{\text{supplied by fuel}}} \times 100$ | Thermal efficiency | % |

> **Symbols Used:** $E$ = Energy, $P$ = Power, $t$ = time, $C_{\text{unit}}$ = cost per unit (₹/kWh), $C_t$ = cash flow in year $t$, $r$ = discount rate, $I_0$ = initial investment, $P_0$ = no-load loss, $P_k$ = full-load loss, $L$ = actual load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Formatting Rule Applied:** All vertical bars (e.g., absolute value, determinant) in mathematical expressions are represented using $\vert$ to prevent breaking the markdown table structure.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of EM & EA |
|---|---|
| **Industrial Sector** | Reduce Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) in steel, cement, fertilizer plants |
| **Commercial Buildings** | HVAC optimization, lighting retrofits, green building certification (LEED/IGBC) |
| **Power Sector** | Auxiliary power optimization, transformer loss reduction |
| **Transport Sector** | Fleet fuel management, route optimization |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | BEE Star Rating, PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme, ISO 50001 certification |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Simple Payback Period (SPP)

The Simple Payback Period is the time required for cumulative savings to equal the initial capital investment. It is the **most common metric** in KTU exams.

**Starting Point (Definition):**
The payback occurs when the cumulative cash savings equal the initial investment.

$$\text{Cumulative Savings at Time } T = \text{Initial Investment}$$

**Assuming constant annual savings (linear case):**
If $I_0$ is the initial investment and $S$ is the constant annual monetary savings, then cumulative savings at the end of year $T$ equals:

$$\text{Cumulative Savings} = S \times T$$

**Setting Cumulative Savings equal to Initial Investment:**

$$S \times T = I_0$$

**Solving for T (Payback Period in years):**

$$\boxed{\text{SPP} = \dfrac{I_0}{S} = \dfrac{\text{Initial Capital Investment (₹)}}{\text{Net Annual Savings (₹/year)}}}$$

**Verification with units:**
$$\dfrac{\text{₹}}{\text{₹/year}} = \text{years} \quad \checkmark$$

> **Real-world interpretation:** If an ECM costs ₹5,00,000 and saves ₹1,00,000 per year in electricity, then $\text{SPP} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = 5$ years.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Net Present Value (NPV)

Simple Payback ignores the **time value of money**. NPV corrects this by discounting future cash flows to their present value.

**Step 1 — Future cash flow in year $t$:**
If $C$ is a constant annual savings, the future value at year $t$ is simply $C$.

**Step 2 — Discount factor to bring it to Present Value (Year 0):**
A rupee received one year from today is worth less than a rupee today due to opportunity cost of capital ($r$).

$$\text{Present Value of ₹1 received after } t \text{ years} = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^t}$$

**Step 3 — Present Value of cash flow at year $t$:**

$$\text{PV}_t = \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

**Step 4 — Sum across $n$ years:**

$$\text{PV of all savings} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

**Step 5 — Subtract initial investment:**

$$\boxed{\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0}$$

**Decision Rule:**
- $\text{NPV} > 0$: Accept the project (creates value).
- $\text{NPV} = 0$: Indifference (break-even with discount rate).
- $\text{NPV} < 0$: Reject the project.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example

**Problem (KTU Style):**
A factory proposes to install a capacitor bank to improve the power factor. The cost of installation is ₹2,00,000. The annual energy savings due to reduced $I^2R$ losses and reduced kVA demand is ₹60,000. The discount rate is 10%. Evaluate using Simple Payback and NPV over 5 years.

**Solution — Part (a): Simple Payback Period**

$$\text{SPP} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{60{,}000} = 3.33 \text{ years}$$

**Solution — Part (b): Net Present Value**

$$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{5} \dfrac{60{,}000}{(1+0.10)^t} - 2{,}00{,}000$$

Computing each term:

$$\text{NPV} = 60{,}000 \left[ \dfrac{1}{1.10} + \dfrac{1}{1.21} + \dfrac{1}{1.331} + \dfrac{1}{1.4641} + \dfrac{1}{1.61051} \right] - 2{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{NPV} = 60{,}000 \times [0.9091 + 0.8264 + 0.7513 + 0.6830 + 0.6209] - 2{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{NPV} = 60{,}000 \times 3.7908 - 2{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{NPV} = 2{,}27{,}447 - 2{,}00{,}000 = +₹27{,}447$$

**Interpretation:** The project has a payback of **3.33 years** and a **positive NPV of ₹27,447**, hence it is **financially viable** and should be implemented.

---

## 3.4 Summary Matrix of Audit Terminology

| Term | Definition | Practical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Baseline (EnB)** | Quantitative reference of energy use | Used for comparing improvements |
| **EnPI** | Energy Performance Indicator | Tracks SEC, kWh/unit |
| **ECM** | Energy Conservation Measure | Specific saving action |
| **M&V** | Measurement & Verification | Proves savings actually happened |
| **ECR** | Energy Cost Reduction | Monetary equivalent of savings |
| **PAT** | Perform Achieve Trade (BEE scheme) | Regulatory target for industries |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Management PDCA Cycle (Adapted Block Architecture)

Since the PDCA cycle cannot be photographed, it is represented below as a functional architecture flow showing the feedback loops between phases.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["PLAN : Energy Review and Baseline"] --> nodeB["DO : Implement ECMs and Controls"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["CHECK : Audit Measure and Analyze"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["ACT : Corrective Action and Review"]
    nodeD -.->|"Feedback Loop for Improvement"| nodeA
    nodeC -->|"Triggers Energy Audit"| nodeE["Detailed Energy Audit"]
    nodeE -->|"Feeds ECM Data"| nodeB
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **outer rectangular flow** (Plan → Do → Check → Act → Plan) is the **continuous improvement loop**.
- The **inner arrow** from *Check* to *Detailed Energy Audit* shows that audits are *triggered* during the Check phase.
- The **feedback arrow** from *Act* back to *Plan* shows that learnings from corrective actions update the next planning cycle.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Energy Audit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Phase 1 : Pre-Audit Planning and Data Collection"] --> node2["Phase 2 : Walk-Through Inspection"]
    node2 --> node3["Phase 3 : Detailed Measurement and Metering"]
    node3 --> node4["Phase 4 : Data Analysis and ECM Identification"]
    node4 --> node5["Phase 5 : Reporting and Implementation Plan"]
    node5 -->|"Approved ECMs Handed Over"| node6["Energy Management Implementation"]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Each **rectangular block** represents a distinct audit phase executed by the energy auditor.
- The **final output** (Phase 5 report) is the contract deliverable that is handed to the client's Energy Management team for execution.
- This flow is **strictly sequential** — no phase can be skipped in a professional audit.

---

## 4.3 Functional Hierarchy of Energy Audit Outcomes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    base["Energy Audit Output"] --> a["Energy Balance Diagram"]
    base --> b["List of Energy Conservation Measures"]
    b --> c["Low Cost and No Cost Measures"]
    b --> d["Medium Cost Measures"]
    b --> e["High Cost Investment Grade Measures"]
    c --> f["Quick Implementation within 0 to 6 Months"]
    d --> g["Implementation within 6 to 18 Months"]
    e --> h["Implementation within 1 to 5 Years with NPV Analysis"]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **energy audit output** is bifurcated into visual (energy balance) and actionable (ECM lists).
- ECMs are **stratified by cost**, which directly determines their implementation timeline.
- This stratification is **exactly what KTU expects** in the "Implementation Plan" section of an audit report.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (Each Carries 3 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Define Energy Management. List any four objectives of an Energy Management program.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Energy Management is the systematic and coordinated planning, operation, and control of energy-consuming processes to ensure optimum utilization of energy resources while minimizing cost, wastage, and environmental impact.

**Objectives (any four to score full marks):**
1. To minimize the cost of energy per unit of production.
2. To minimize Specific Energy Consumption (SEC).
3. To reduce environmental pollution and carbon footprint.
4. To ensure compliance with regulatory standards (BEE, ISO 50001, PAT).
5. To improve equipment efficiency and reduce maintenance costs.
6. To promote a culture of energy awareness among employees.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Define Energy Audit. Differentiate between Preliminary Audit and Detailed Energy Audit.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
An Energy Audit is a systematic and documented survey of energy use in a facility to identify wastage, quantify losses, and recommend Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) with cost-benefit analysis.

**Differentiation Table:**

| Parameter | Preliminary (Walk-Through) Audit | Detailed Energy Audit |
|---|---|---|
| **Duration** | 1–3 days | 2–6 weeks |
| **Instruments** | Visual inspection, past bills | Power loggers, flue gas analyzer, lux meter |
| **Scope** | Identify obvious wastage | Quantify each load center |
| **Output** | List of low-cost measures | Energy balance + ECMs with payback |
| **Cost** | Low (₹15k–₹50k) | Moderate (₹1–5 lakh) |
| **Accuracy of savings estimate** | ±30% | ±5–10% |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Each Carries 14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In long-answer questions, students often *define* concepts correctly but **lose 4–5 marks** because they fail to draw the energy balance diagram, fail to write down the *formula and unit of every variable*, and skip the *decision rule*. Always include: (i) Formula (ii) Substitution (iii) Final Answer with unit (iv) Interpretation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2 Choice]

**A small-scale industry is considering two mutually exclusive Energy Conservation Measures (ECM-1 and ECM-2). The technical details are tabulated below. Using Simple Payback Period and Net Present Value (discount rate 10%), advise the management on the choice. Assume a project life of 5 years.**

| Parameter | ECM-1 (LED Lighting) | ECM-2 (VFD on Motor) |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Investment (₹) | 4,00,000 | 9,00,000 |
| Annual Savings (₹/year) | 1,40,000 | 2,20,000 |

#### Part (a) — Simple Payback Analysis (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Apply the Simple Payback Period formula.

$$\text{SPP}_1 = \dfrac{4{,}00{,}000}{1{,}40{,}000} = 2.857 \text{ years}$$

$$\text{SPP}_2 = \dfrac{9{,}00{,}000}{2{,}20{,}000} = 4.091 \text{ years}$$

**Step 2:** Ranking.

| ECM | Payback (years) | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| ECM-1 | 2.86 | **1st (Faster payback)** |
| ECM-2 | 4.09 | 2nd |

**Conclusion (Part a):** Based on simple payback alone, **ECM-1 is preferred** as it recovers capital 1.23 years earlier.

---

#### Part (b) — Net Present Value Analysis (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Compute the Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF) for 5 years at 10%:

$$\text{PVAF} = \sum_{t=1}^{5} \dfrac{1}{(1.10)^t} = 0.9091 + 0.8264 + 0.7513 + 0.6830 + 0.6209 = 3.7908$$

**Step 2:** Compute NPV for each ECM.

$$\text{NPV}_1 = (1{,}40{,}000 \times 3.7908) - 4{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{NPV}_1 = 5{,}30{,}712 - 4{,}00{,}000 = +₹1{,}30{,}712$$

$$\text{NPV}_2 = (2{,}20{,}000 \times 3.7908) - 9{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{NPV}_2 = 8{,}33{,}976 - 9{,}00{,}000 = -₹66{,}024$$

**Step 3:** Tabulate for clarity.

| ECM | NPV (₹) | Decision |
|---|---|---|
| ECM-1 | +1,30,712 | **Accept** |
| ECM-2 | -66,024 | **Reject** |

**Final Recommendation (Part b):** Despite ECM-2 having higher absolute annual savings, its negative NPV indicates that the present value of its savings over 5 years at 10% discount rate is insufficient to recover the higher capital cost. **ECM-1 (LED Lighting) is the financially superior option and should be implemented.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 2]

**Explain the various types of Energy Audits in detail. Discuss the methodology and instruments used for conducting a Detailed Energy Audit.**

#### Part (a) — Types of Energy Audits (7 Marks)

**1. Preliminary / Walk-Through Audit:**
- Objective: Quick scan to identify glaring inefficiencies.
- Duration: 1–3 days.
- Tools: Visual inspection, historical utility bills, simple clamp meter.
- Output: Qualitative list of ECMs (mostly low-cost).

**2. Detailed Energy Audit (DAS):**
- Objective: Comprehensive quantitative assessment.
- Duration: 2–6 weeks.
- Tools: Power analyzers, ultrasonic flow meters, combustion analyzers, thermal imagers.
- Output: Energy balance diagram, Sankey chart, and prioritized ECMs with payback.

**3. Investment-Grade Audit (IGA):**
- Objective: Bankable precision for project financing.
- Duration: 2–6 months.
- Tools: High-accuracy calibrated instruments, M&V protocol per IPMVP.
- Output: Detailed feasibility report with NPV, IRR, and risk analysis.

#### Part (b) — Detailed Energy Audit Methodology & Instruments (7 Marks)

**Methodology (5 phases):**

1. **Phase 1 — Pre-Audit Data Collection:** Gather electricity bills, fuel consumption logs, process flow diagrams, equipment nameplate data.
2. **Phase 2 — Walk-Through Survey:** Identify obvious wastage (steam leaks, idle equipment, poor insulation).
3. **Phase 3 — Detailed Measurements:**
   - Electrical: Voltage, current, power factor, harmonic distortion.
   - Thermal: Flue gas temperature, O₂/CO₂ percentage, surface temperatures.
   - Fluid: Flow rates of steam, water, compressed air.
4. **Phase 4 — Energy Balance & ECM Analysis:** Construct energy balance, identify ECMs, calculate payback.
5. **Phase 5 — Reporting:** Final report with executive summary, methodology, findings, and prioritized recommendations.

**Instruments Used (must mention for full marks):**

| Instrument | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Power Quality Analyzer | Measure V, I, kW, kVA, kVAr, PF, THD |
| Ultrasonic Flow Meter | Non-intrusive flow measurement |
| Combustion / Flue Gas Analyzer | O₂, CO, CO₂, temperature, efficiency |
| Infrared Thermometer / Imager | Surface temperature / heat loss |
| Lux Meter | Verify illumination levels against IS standards |
| Tachometer / Vibration Analyzer | Motor condition assessment |

**Conclusion:** The Detailed Energy Audit is the most rigorous level and forms the basis for any credible energy management decision-making.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Night Revision Bullet Points — High Density**

- **Energy Management (EM)** is the **strategy/policy framework**; **Energy Audit (EA)** is the **diagnostic tool**.
- The **PDCA Cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) is the backbone of EM, codified in **ISO 50001**.
- The **BEE Energy Auditing Guideline** recognizes **3 levels**: Preliminary, Detailed, and Investment-Grade.
- **Energy Baseline (EnB)** = fixed quantitative reference; **EnPI** = metric for tracking performance.
- **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** = Investment ÷ Annual Savings (ignores time value of money).
- **Net Present Value (NPV)** = discounted sum of future savings − initial investment (considers time value).
- **Decision Rule for NPV:** Accept if NPV > 0; Reject if NPV < 0.
- **Standard energy units:** 1 kWh = **860 kcal** = **3.6 MJ**; 1 TR = **3.516 kW**.
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** is the **most frequently used** industrial KPI.
- **ECM categories:** Low-cost (payback < 1 year), Medium (1–3 years), High-cost (> 3 years, requires NPV/IRR).
- Always include **energy balance diagrams** and **Sankey charts** in audit reports for maximum marks.
- In long-answer problems, **state the formula, substitute values, compute result, and provide interpretation** — this 4-step structure is the KTU valuation template.
- **PAT Scheme** = Perform, Achieve, Trade — India's flagship regulatory mechanism under BEE for Designated Consumers.
- **Audit vs. Inspection:** Audit = Engineering + Economics + Compliance; Inspection = mere visual check.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Audit (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> An *Energy Audit* is the systematic, documented verification, monitoring, and analysis of the use of energy in a building, process, or system, with the objective of identifying energy flows, quantifying energy consumption, and recommending Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) to optimize efficiency while ensuring comfort, productivity, and safety.

It is the **cornerstone activity** of any Energy Management Programme (EnMP) and forms the practical bridge between *energy policy formulation* and *energy performance evaluation*.

According to the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) – India** and the **Energy Conservation Act (2001)**, an energy audit is mandatory for *Designated Consumers* (DCs) in energy-intensive industrial sectors.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🏥 Real-World Analogy — "The Human Health Check-up"**
>
> Imagine a person goes to a doctor for a full body check-up. The doctor measures blood pressure, sugar levels, cholesterol, and heart rate. He then diagnoses problems and prescribes lifestyle changes (diet, exercise, medicine).
>
> In the exact same way, an **Energy Auditor** acts as a "doctor" for an industrial plant or building. He measures voltage, current, temperature, flow rates, and lighting levels. He then diagnoses where energy is being **wasted** and prescribes **Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)** — like a doctor prescribing lifestyle changes.

**Mapping the Analogy:**

| Human Check-up | Energy Audit |
|---|---|
| Medical history review | Pre-audit data collection |
| Blood test & scans | Instrumentation & measurement |
| Diagnosis | Identification of energy waste |
| Prescription of medicine | Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) |
| Follow-up visits | Post-audit monitoring & verification (M&V) |

---

## 1.3 Why Energy Audit is Critical in Engineering Practice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reasons for Conducting an Energy Audit:**
> 1. **Cost Reduction** — typically 10–30% savings on energy bills.
> 2. **Regulatory Compliance** — adherence to the *Energy Conservation Act 2001* and BEE PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme.
> 3. **Carbon Footprint Reduction** — supports ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) and net-zero commitments.
> 4. **Equipment Life Extension** — optimized operation reduces wear-and-tear.
> 5. **Process Optimization** — improves product quality and throughput.
> 6. **ISO 50001 Alignment** — mandatory for EnMS (Energy Management System) certification.

---

## 1.4 Standard Energy Units and Constants (Always Bolded)

- **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ (Mega-Joules)**
- **1 TOE (Tonne of Oil Equivalent) = 41.868 GJ**
- **1 Calorie = 4.184 J**
- **1 BTU = 1055.06 J**
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** = Energy Used / Unit of Output
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Energy Audits (KTU 2024 High-Yield Topic)

> [!NOTE]
> Energy audits are classified based on **depth of analysis**, **instruments used**, and **investment-grade reporting**.

### (a) Preliminary / Walk-Through Audit (Type 1)

- **Duration:** 1–3 days
- **Objective:** Quick assessment to identify glaring energy waste and low-cost ECMs.
- **Tools Used:** Visual inspection, plant walk-through, historical data review, utility bill analysis.
- **Report:** Rough energy balance, list of obvious savings opportunities, recommendation for detailed audit.
- **Cost:** Lowest.
- **Analogy:** A *general physician's* first visit.

### (b) Detailed / Comprehensive Audit (Type 2)

- **Duration:** 2–6 weeks
- **Objective:** In-depth engineering analysis with measured data.
- **Tools Used:** Power analyzers, flow meters, thermal imagers, lux meters, combustion analyzers.
- **Report:** Detailed energy balance, quantified ECMs with cost-benefit analysis, payback period, and ROI.
- **Analogy:** A *specialist's* deep medical investigation.

### (c) Investment-Grade Audit (Type 3)

- **Duration:** Several months.
- **Objective:** Bankable reports with risk analysis, financing models, and project-grade engineering.
- **Tools Used:** Long-term monitoring, statistical regression, M&V protocols (IPMVP).
- **Report:** Detailed feasibility report suitable for funding agencies / ESCO contracts.
- **Analogy:** A *complete medical treatment plan* with full insurance and rehab coverage.

---

## 2.2 Systematic Methodology of an Energy Audit (KTU 2024)

The energy audit follows a **structured 4-phase methodology**:

### **Phase 1 — Pre-Audit Phase (Planning)**
- Define audit scope, boundaries, and objectives.
- Collect historical utility bills (12–24 months).
- Review process flow diagrams (PFDs) and Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs).
- Identify major energy-consuming equipment.
- Plan walk-through and instrumentation requirements.

### **Phase 2 — Audit Phase (On-site Measurement)**
- Conduct detailed walk-through.
- Deploy instruments and log data.
- Develop **Sankey Diagrams** for energy flows.
- Perform combustion analysis, electrical load analysis, thermal profiling.
- Interview operators and maintenance staff.

### **Phase 3 — Post-Audit Phase (Analysis)**
- Compile energy balance.
- Calculate Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) and Energy Performance Indicators (EPIs).
- Identify all feasible Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs).
- Categorize ECMs into **No-Cost, Low-Cost, Medium-Cost, High-Cost** bins.

### **Phase 4 — Implementation & Monitoring Phase**
- Present report to management.
- Implement short-payback ECMs immediately.
- Establish M&V (Measurement & Verification) plan.
- Continuous commissioning and re-audit after 6–12 months.

---

## 2.3 Energy Audit Instruments (KTU 2024 High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **frequently asked in KTU exams**. Memorize the instrument → parameter mapping.

| Instrument | Parameter Measured | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Power Analyzer | V, I, kW, kVA, kVAr, PF, Hz | Electrical load profiling |
| Lux Meter | Illuminance (lux) | Lighting audit |
| Infrared Thermometer | Surface temperature (°C) | Steam pipe insulation check |
| Thermal Imager | Temperature distribution | Heat loss detection, electrical hot-spots |
| Combustion Analyzer | O₂, CO, CO₂, flue temp, efficiency | Boiler / furnace tuning |
| Ultrasonic Flow Meter | Flow rate (m³/h) of liquids | Cooling water, chilled water |
| Anemometer | Air velocity (m/s) | HVAC duct balancing |
| Tachometer | Rotational speed (rpm) | Motor / fan speed verification |
| Manometer | Pressure (mmWC, Pa) | Filter / duct pressure drop |
| Data Logger | Time-series multi-parameter | Long-term trending |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-exam essentials**. Each appears regularly in KTU ESE papers.

### **Financial Metrics for ECMs**

| Formula No. | Metric | Equation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | Simple Payback Period (SPP) | $\text{SPP} = \dfrac{\text{Investment Cost}}{\text{Annual Energy Savings}}$ | Years |
| F2 | Return on Investment (ROI) | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Savings}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100$ | % |
| F3 | Net Present Value (NPV) | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$ | ₹ (Rupees) |
| F4 | Internal Rate of Return (IRR) | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+\text{IRR})^t} = 0$ | % |
| F5 | Cost of Saved Energy (CSE) | $\text{CSE} = \dfrac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Lifetime} \times \text{Annual Savings}}$ | ₹ / kWh |

### **Energy Performance Metrics**

| Formula No. | Metric | Equation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| F6 | Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) | $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy}}{\text{Production Output}}$ | kWh / tonne |
| F7 | Energy Performance Index (EPI) | $\text{EPI} = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy}}{\text{Floor Area}}$ | kWh / m² / yr |
| F8 | Boiler Efficiency (Direct Method) | $\eta_{\text{boiler}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{steam output}}}{Q_{\text{fuel input}}} \times 100$ | % |
| F9 | Power Factor Correction Savings | $\text{Savings} = kW \left( \tan \phi_1 - \tan \phi_2 \right) \times \text{hours} \times \text{Rate}$ | ₹ |
| F10 | Energy Cost Per Unit | $\text{Cost/kWh} = \dfrac{\text{Total Bill (₹)}}{\text{Units Consumed (kWh)}}$ | ₹ / kWh |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** In exam answer sheets, **never use the vertical bar symbol** $\vert$ inside tables. Always write it as \vert or use words like "absolute value of" in plain text.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Plants:** Identify motors running at <40% load (replace with VFDs).
- **Commercial Buildings:** Audit HVAC chiller performance (typical COP 3–5; below = inefficiency).
- **Data Centers:** PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness) target ≤ 1.5.
- **Power Plants:** Heat rate analysis for turbine efficiency benchmarking.
- **Municipal Systems:** Pump station audit — replace oversized pumps with duty-matched units.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Insight:** A well-executed energy audit in a typical Indian manufacturing MSME recovers **₹25–50 lakhs/year** in savings, with most low-cost ECMs having a **payback under 12 months**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Simple Payback Period with Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> The **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** is the most heavily tested formula in KTU 2024 ESE papers. The derivation below shows how to transform a real-world cost scenario into a numerical answer.

### **Worked Problem Statement**

> A manufacturing unit is evaluating the installation of a **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** on a 30 kW induction motor. The VFD cost is **₹1,50,000** (including installation). The motor currently runs at full speed for 4,000 hours/year. By reducing speed to 80% using the VFD, the power consumption drops to **19.2 kW**. Electricity cost is **₹8/kWh**. Find the **Simple Payback Period** and **% ROI**.

### **Step 1 — Compute Old Power Consumption**

Old power input $P_1 = 30 \text{ kW}$

Annual energy consumed without VFD:

$$E_1 = P_1 \times t = 30 \text{ kW} \times 4000 \text{ h} = 1{,}20{,}000 \text{ kWh}$$

### **Step 2 — Compute New Power Consumption Using Affinity Law**

For fans, blowers, and centrifugal pumps, power is proportional to the **cube** of speed:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left( \frac{N_2}{N_1} \right)^3$$

Substitute $N_2 / N_1 = 0.8$:

$$P_2 = 30 \times (0.8)^3 = 30 \times 0.512 = 15.36 \text{ kW}$$

> **Examiner Tip:** The problem gave $P_2 = 19.2$ kW (not following affinity law), indicating a *non-centrifugal* load such as a compressor. We will use the given value $P_2 = 19.2$ kW.

$$E_2 = 19.2 \text{ kW} \times 4000 \text{ h} = 76{,}800 \text{ kWh}$$

### **Step 3 — Compute Annual Energy Savings**

$$\Delta E = E_1 - E_2 = 1{,}20{,}000 - 76{,}800 = 43{,}200 \text{ kWh / year}$$

### **Step 4 — Compute Annual Monetary Savings**

$$\text{Savings (₹)} = \Delta E \times \text{Rate} = 43{,}200 \times 8 = 3{,}45{,}600 \text{ ₹ / year}$$

### **Step 5 — Compute Simple Payback Period (using F1)**

$$\text{SPP} = \frac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{3{,}45{,}600}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SPP} = 0.434 \text{ years} \approx 5.2 \text{ months}}$$

### **Step 6 — Compute % ROI (using F2)**

$$\text{ROI} = \frac{3{,}45{,}600}{1{,}50{,}000} \times 100 = 230.4\%$$

### **Step 7 — Interpretation**

> [!NOTE]
> - **SPP < 1 year** → Project is **highly attractive**; must be implemented.
> - **% ROI > 15%** → Far exceeds typical hurdle rate, qualifies for **bank loan** under IREDA financing.
> - This is a **textbook example of a Low-Cost / High-Impact ECM**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Boiler Efficiency by Direct (Input–Output) Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 frequently asks for the **Direct Method** derivation of boiler efficiency. Memorize every step.

### **Given Data**

A fire-tube boiler produces 8,000 kg/h of dry saturated steam at 10 bar (enthalpy $h_{\text{steam}} = 2778 \text{ kJ/kg}$). Feed water enters at 85 °C (enthalpy $h_{\text{fw}} = 355 \text{ kJ/kg}$). Fuel (HSD) consumption is 540 kg/h. Calorific value of HSD = 43,000 kJ/kg.

### **Step 1 — Energy Output from Steam**

$$Q_{\text{out}} = m_s \times (h_{\text{steam}} - h_{\text{fw}})$$

$$Q_{\text{out}} = 8000 \times (2778 - 355) = 8000 \times 2423$$

$$Q_{\text{out}} = 1{,}93{,}84{,}000 \text{ kJ / h}$$

### **Step 2 — Energy Input from Fuel**

$$Q_{\text{in}} = m_f \times \text{GCV} = 540 \times 43{,}000$$

$$Q_{\text{in}} = 2{,}32{,}20{,}000 \text{ kJ / h}$$

### **Step 3 — Boiler Efficiency (using F8)**

$$\eta_{\text{boiler}} = \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{\text{boiler}} = \frac{1{,}93{,}84{,}000}{2{,}32{,}20{,}000} \times 100 = 83.47\%$$

### **Step 4 — Interpretation (Energy Loss Side)**

Heat losses (16.53%) are typically distributed as:
- **Stack gas loss**: 8–12%
- **Radiation + convection loss**: 1–2%
- **Moisture in combustion air**: 2–3%
- **Blowdown loss**: 1–2%

> [!TIP]
> **For a 2-mark follow-up question**, the KTU answer key expects the formula $\eta = (Q_s / Q_f) \times 100$ to be written **before** substituting numerical values. Always present the equation symbolically first.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Categorization of Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)

> [!NOTE]
> **ECM Categorization** is a KTU favorite — comes as a 3-mark direct question or as a 7-mark sub-part of a 14-mark question.

| Category | Investment (₹) | Payback Period | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tier 1: No-Cost** | 0 | Immediate | Switching off idle equipment, adjusting setpoints, housekeeping |
| **Tier 2: Low-Cost** | < ₹5 Lakhs | < 1 year | Installing timers, insulation of bare pipes, capacitor banks for PF |
| **Tier 3: Medium-Cost** | ₹5–50 Lakhs | 1–3 years | VFD installation, LED lighting retrofit, heat recovery |
| **Tier 4: High-Cost** | > ₹50 Lakhs | 3–5 years | Cogeneration, boiler replacement, CHP plant |

---

## 3.4 Decision Logic: SPP vs NPV

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Why is NPV preferred over SPP for evaluating energy projects?"*

**Answer Structure (Model KTU Pattern):**
1. **SPP Ignores Time Value of Money** — treats ₹1 today same as ₹1 in 10 years.
2. **SPP Ignores Project Life** — a project with 5-year payback over 6 years of life is *unattractive*; SPP hides this.
3. **SPP Ignores Cash Flows After Payback** — long-term savings are invisible.
4. **NPV is the Gold Standard** — discounted cash flow method, considers all life-cycle savings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb in KTU Valuation:**
> - For **short-life (< 3 yr)** or **low-cost** ECMs → **SPP is sufficient**.
> - For **long-life (10–20 yr)** or **capital-intensive** ECMs → **NPV/IRR is mandatory**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short-Answer Type)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Energy Audit. List any four instruments used in a detailed energy audit.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **Definition (2 Marks):** An Energy Audit is the systematic inspection, surveying, and analysis of energy flow in a building, process, or system to identify energy wastage and recommend Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) for efficiency improvement.
>
> **Any Four Instruments (1 Mark — ¼ each):**
> 1. Power Analyzer — measures V, I, kW, PF.
> 2. Lux Meter — measures lighting illuminance.
> 3. Ultrasonic Flow Meter — measures water/steam flow.
> 4. Combustion Analyzer — measures O₂, CO, flue temperature.
> 5. Thermal Imager — identifies heat losses and electrical hot spots.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Differentiate between a Walk-Through Audit and a Detailed Energy Audit.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> | Parameter | Walk-Through Audit | Detailed Audit |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Duration** | 1–3 days | 2–6 weeks |
> | **Instruments** | Visual + utility bills | Full instrumentation set |
> | **Output** | List of obvious savings | Quantified ECMs with payback |
> | **Cost** | Low | High |
> | **Depth** | Preliminary / screening | Engineering-grade analysis |

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: **Each Part B carries 14 marks with internal choice.** Solve either **Q.A or Q.B** in full. Sub-parts (a) and (b) typically carry 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Modified]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four phases** of a systematic energy audit methodology with a neat flowchart.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A 25 kW motor is currently operated at full load. A VFD is proposed such that the motor runs at 70% speed. Assume centrifugal load (cube-law affinity). The VFD costs ₹2,00,000, operating hours are 5000 h/year, and electricity rate is ₹7.50/kWh. Compute (i) old energy consumption, (ii) new energy consumption, (iii) annual savings in ₹, and (iv) Simple Payback Period.

### **Model Answer to Q.A**

#### **(a) Four Phases of Energy Audit (7 Marks)**

> **Phase 1 — Pre-Audit (2 Marks):**
> Planning phase. Define audit boundary, collect utility bills (12–24 months), study PFDs and P&IDs, identify major energy users, prepare audit checklist.

> **Phase 2 — On-Site Audit (2 Marks):**
> Detailed walk-through. Deploy instruments (power analyzer, lux meter, flow meter). Log electrical, thermal, and flow data. Interview operators.

> **Phase 3 — Post-Audit Analysis (2 Marks):**
> Construct energy balance. Compute SEC and EPI. Identify all feasible ECMs. Categorize them by cost (No/Low/Medium/High) and compute payback.

> **Phase 4 — Implementation & M&V (1 Mark):**
> Present report. Implement short-payback ECMs. Establish M&V. Plan re-audit after 6–12 months.

> **Flowchart:** Draw the diagram as shown in **Section 4.1** above. Full credit awarded.

#### **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

> **Given:** $P_1 = 25$ kW, $N_2/N_1 = 0.7$, Investment = ₹2,00,000, $t = 5000$ h/year, Rate = ₹7.50/kWh

**Step 1 — Old energy consumption:**

$$E_1 = 25 \times 5000 = 1{,}25{,}000 \text{ kWh / year} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 2 — New power (Affinity Law):**

$$P_2 = P_1 \times (0.7)^3 = 25 \times 0.343 = 8.575 \text{ kW} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

$$E_2 = 8.575 \times 5000 = 42{,}875 \text{ kWh / year} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Annual monetary savings:**

$$\Delta E = 1{,}25{,}000 - 42{,}875 = 82{,}125 \text{ kWh / year} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

$$\text{Savings (₹)} = 82{,}125 \times 7.50 = 6{,}15{,}937.5 \text{ ₹ / year} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 4 — Simple Payback Period:**

$$\text{SPP} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{6{,}15{,}937.5} = 0.325 \text{ years} \approx 3.9 \text{ months} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SPP} = 3.9 \text{ months — Project is highly recommended.}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Modified]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **different types of energy audits** with their duration, instruments used, and the type of report generated.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A boiler produces 10,000 kg/h of steam at 12 bar ($h_s = 2784$ kJ/kg). Feed water enters at 90°C ($h_{fw} = 377$ kJ/kg). Coal consumption is 1200 kg/h with GCV = 25,000 kJ/kg. Compute the **boiler efficiency by the direct method** and identify the **major heat losses** if the actual efficiency is found to be 76%.

### **Model Answer to Q.B**

#### **(a) Types of Energy Audits (7 Marks)**

> **Type 1 — Walk-Through (2 Marks):** 1–3 days, visual inspection + bills, low-cost, list of obvious savings.

> **Type 2 — Detailed Audit (3 Marks):** 2–6 weeks, full instrumentation — power analyzer, lux meter, flow meter, combustion analyzer, thermal imager. Output: energy balance + quantified ECMs with payback + ROI.

> **Type 3 — Investment-Grade Audit (2 Marks):** Several months, long-term M&V, statistical regression, risk analysis. Output: bankable report suitable for ESCO contracts and project financing.

#### **(b) Boiler Efficiency Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Output Energy:**

$$Q_s = 10{,}000 \times (2784 - 377) = 10{,}000 \times 2407$$

$$Q_s = 2.407 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ / h} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

**Step 2 — Input Energy:**

$$Q_f = 1200 \times 25{,}000 = 3.0 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ / h} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Boiler Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{boiler}} = \frac{2.407 \times 10^7}{3.0 \times 10^7} \times 100 = 80.23\% \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Heat Loss Identification (since 80.23% > 76% given):**

> The computed efficiency is **80.23%**. If the *measured* actual efficiency is **76%**, the difference of **4.23%** is due to:
>
> 1. **Stack gas dry loss** — ~2.5% (high flue gas temperature, excess air).
> 2. **Moisture loss from H₂ in fuel** — ~0.8%.
> 3. **Radiation + convection loss** — ~0.5%.
> 4. **Blowdown loss + unaccounted losses** — ~0.43%. **(2 Marks)**

> **Recommendation:** Install an **Economizer** to recover waste flue gas heat and **trim excess air** through better burner tuning.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Common Mark-Loss Zones in Energy Audit Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to cube the speed ratio** in VFD/Pump problems. Affinity Law is **P ∝ N³**, not N. (Lose 2 marks.)
> 2. **Not converting units** — kW × hours = kWh, NOT kW. Many students write "kW" as the energy unit.
> 3. **Confusing "Investment" with "Total Cost"** — Investment includes installation, civil, and commissioning, not just equipment cost.
> 4. **Writing the answer without showing formula** — KTU valuators *require* the formula before substitution. Formula (1M) + Substitution (1M) + Answer (1M) is the typical 3-mark split.
> 5. **Ignoring "Given" overrides in problems** — if the problem explicitly says "assume $P_2 = 19.2$ kW" (i.e., not following cube law), do **not** apply the cube law blindly. Read the question.
> 6. **Units in Boiler problems** — always write kJ/kg, kJ/h, and °C. Missing units = -0.5 mark per step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Energy Audit**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Systematic verification + analysis of energy use to find waste and recommend ECMs.
>
> ✅ **3 Types of Audits:** Walk-Through (Type 1), Detailed (Type 2), Investment-Grade (Type 3).
>
> ✅ **4 Phases:** Pre-Audit → On-Site Audit → Post-Audit Analysis → Implementation & M&V.
>
> ✅ **Key Instruments:** Power Analyzer, Lux Meter, Flow Meter, Combustion Analyzer, Thermal Imager, Tachometer, Anemometer, Manometer.
>
> ✅ **Affinity Law for centrifugal loads:** $P \propto N^3$, $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$.
>
> ✅ **Top 5 Formulas to Memorize:**
>   - $\text{SPP} = \dfrac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$
>   - $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Savings}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100$
>   - $\text{NPV} = \sum \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$
>   - $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy}}{\text{Output}}$
>   - $\eta_{\text{boiler}} = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_f} \times 100$
>
> ✅ **ECM Tiers:** No-Cost → Low-Cost (PF, LED, Insulation) → Medium-Cost (VFD, Heat Recovery) → High-Cost (CHP, Cogeneration).
>
> ✅ **Threshold Values (must know):**
>   - PF > 0.95 (to avoid penalty)
>   - EPI for commercial buildings: 100–200 kWh/m²/yr (5-star ECBC)
>   - Lighting level: 300 lux (offices), 500 lux (drawing rooms)
>
> ✅ **BEE & ISO Standards:** Energy Conservation Act 2001, ECBC 2007, ISO 50001, IPMVP for M&V.
>
> ✅ **Real-life numbers:** 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ, 1 TOE = 41.868 GJ.
>
> ✅ **Decision rule:** SPP < 1 year → implement immediately; SPP 1–3 years → NPV/IRR analysis; SPP > 3 years → revisit if strategic.

---

> **End of Module 2 — Energy Audit Notes**

topics.title: Need
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Need

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — "The Need" in Energy Management

In the context of **Energy Management and Conservation (EMC)**, the term **"Need"** refers to the *rational, scientifically-justified requirement to systematically control, optimize, and reduce energy consumption* across industrial, commercial, residential, agricultural, and transport sectors. It encompasses both the **quantitative energy demand** of a system and the **qualitative justification** for adopting energy-efficient practices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 2):**
> *"The Need"* establishes the foundational motivation for energy management. It examines the rising energy demand, depletion of fossil reserves, environmental degradation, escalating energy costs, and the regulatory/legal push from bodies like the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** and the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001** that collectively make energy management a national and engineering imperative.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Budget" of Energy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Think of energy as the *monthly salary* of a nation or an industry.
> - **Earning (Supply):** The energy we *produce* from coal, oil, gas, hydro, solar, etc.
> - **Spending (Demand):** The energy we *consume* in factories, vehicles, homes, and farms.
> - **Savings (Conservation):** The deliberate act of *spending less while achieving the same output*.
> - **Budgeting (Management):** *Tracking, planning, and controlling* every rupee/unit of energy so there are no "leaks."
>
> Just as a family cannot sustain wasteful spending without going into debt, a nation cannot sustain wasteful energy use without facing economic and ecological crises. **This is the "Need."**

---

## 1.3 The Five Pillars of "Need" in Energy Management

The KTU framework identifies **five core drivers** that justify the need for energy management:

| # | Pillar | Core Statement |
|---|--------|----------------|
| 1 | **Economic Need** | Energy costs form 10–30% of manufacturing expenses; reducing it directly boosts profit. |
| 2 | **Environmental Need** | Energy generation is the largest source of **CO₂ emissions (≈73% of global GHG)**; conservation mitigates climate change. |
| 3 | **Resource Need** | Fossil fuel reserves are *finite* — oil ≈ **47 years**, gas ≈ **52 years**, coal ≈ **133 years** at current consumption. |
| 4 | **Legal/Regulatory Need** | **Energy Conservation Act 2001**, **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme**, and **ECBC** mandate compliance. |
| 5 | **Social/Security Need** | Energy independence reduces geopolitical vulnerability and ensures equitable access. |

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics (Highlighted for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) in Energy Management:**
> - **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** — units: **kWh/ton, MJ/kg, kWh/m²**
> - **Energy Intensity (EI)** — units: **kWh per unit of GDP (kgoe / $ GDP)**
> - **Power Factor (cos φ)** — dimensionless, ideally **≥ 0.95**
> - **Plant Load Factor (PLF)** — ratio of actual energy generated to maximum possible
> - **Cogeneration potential ratio** — typically **0.6 to 0.8**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Hierarchy of Energy Needs

Energy is not consumed uniformly — it follows a strict **hierarchy of needs**, conceptually similar to Maslow's pyramid. KTU expects students to recognize this layered structure:

### 🔻 Layer 1 — Basic Thermal/Mechanical Energy
- Heating, cooling, pumping, compression, material handling.
- This is the **primary need**; without it, no industrial process runs.

### 🔻 Layer 2 — Electrical Energy
- Powers motors, lighting, electronics, instrumentation.
- Industry consumes ≈ **40–45% of total electricity** generated in India.

### 🔻 Layer 3 — Process Optimization Energy
- Energy embedded in chemical reactions, phase changes, refining.
- Examples: cement clinker formation (~**1.7 GJ/ton**), steel making (~**21 GJ/ton**).

### 🔻 Layer 4 — Auxiliary Energy
- HVAC, compressed air, lighting, ventilation.
- Often **30–50% wasted** due to poor design — this is the **biggest conservation opportunity**.

### 🔻 Layer 5 — Waste Heat Recovery Potential
- Flue gases, exhaust streams, hot condensates.
- Recovery possible at **60–80% efficiency** with heat exchangers.

---

## 2.2 Sector-Wise Energy Need — KTU High-Yield Data

| Sector | Share of India's Total Energy Consumption | Primary Need | Conservation Opportunity |
|--------|-------------------------------------------|--------------|--------------------------|
| **Industry** | **≈ 41%** | Process heat, motive power | 15–25% |
| **Transport** | **≈ 18%** | Liquid fuels (petrol/diesel) | 10–20% via efficiency |
| **Residential/Buildings** | **≈ 24%** | Cooking, lighting, HVAC | 30–40% via ECBC |
| **Agriculture** | **≈ 8%** | Pumping (electricity) | 30–50% via star-rated pumps |
| **Commercial** | **≈ 9%** | HVAC, lighting, lifts | 25–35% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Industry" leads:** India is a manufacturing-driven economy under *Make in India* and the industrial sector accounts for the largest share of commercial energy consumption, making it the **prime target** for energy management interventions.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are essential for Part A & Part B KTU questions on "Need"**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Energy Consumption | $E = P \times t$ | kWh | P = Power, t = time |
| 2 | Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{M_{output}}$ | kWh/ton or MJ/kg | Lower = better |
| 3 | Energy Intensity (Country level) | $EI = \dfrac{TEC}{GDP}$ | kgoe / $ GDP | Macro indicator |
| 4 | Energy Cost | $C = E \times R$ | ₹/month | R = tariff (₹/kWh) |
| 5 | Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \dfrac{Investment}{Annual\ Savings}$ | years | < 3 yrs = attractive |
| 6 | Return on Investment | $ROI = \dfrac{Annual\ Savings}{Investment} \times 100$ | % | Higher = better |
| 7 | Plant Load Factor | $PLF = \dfrac{Actual\ Energy\ Generated}{Max\ Possible\ Energy}$ | dimensionless | Ideal ≥ 0.80 |
| 8 | Capacity Utilisation Factor | $CUF = \dfrac{Actual\ Output}{Rated\ Output}$ | dimensionless | Solar PV typical: 0.15–0.20 |
| 9 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{Useful\ Output}{Energy\ Input} \times 100$ | % | Always < 100 |
| 10 | Power Factor | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{S} = \dfrac{kW}{kVA}$ | dimensionless | Target ≥ 0.95 |
| 11 | Greenhouse Gas Reduction | $\Delta CO_2 = E_{saved} \times 0.82$ | kg CO₂/kWh | Grid emission factor India |
| 12 | Heating Value Conversion | $1\ kWh = 3.6\ MJ = 860\ kcal$ | — | Must memorize |

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Energy Management — Engineering Perspective

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Engineering Truths:**
> 1. **Second Law of Thermodynamics** dictates that *every* energy conversion produces waste heat — but the *amount* of waste is a design choice, not destiny.
> 2. Energy efficiency is the **cheapest "fuel"** — saving 1 kWh costs a fraction of generating 1 kWh.
> 3. Energy conservation has a **multiplier effect**: the saved kWh avoids fuel extraction, transport, conversion losses, AND emissions — a *4×–5× system benefit*.

### Real-World Utility
- **For an engineer:** Every design decision (motor sizing, insulation, lighting) must be evaluated on an *energy-cost basis*, not just capital cost.
- **For a manager:** Energy management is the **single largest controllable expense** after raw materials.
- **For a nation:** Reducing EI by 25% over a decade can save **₹10+ lakh crore** cumulatively.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linking Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) to Operating Cost

**Objective:** Derive how SEC drives the operating cost of any production unit, the central economic justification for energy management.

**Given:**
- Annual production = $M$ tonnes
- Specific Energy Consumption = $SEC$ kWh/ton
- Tariff = $R$ ₹/kWh

**Step 1:** Total annual energy consumed

$$E_{annual} = SEC \times M$$

*Explanation:* Multiply the energy needed *per unit* of product by the *number* of units produced. This gives the total energy footprint.

**Step 2:** Total annual energy cost

$$C_{annual} = E_{annual} \times R = SEC \times M \times R$$

*Explanation:* Multiply total energy by the price per unit. This is the *direct* energy expenditure.

**Step 3:** Express as fraction of total production cost

Let $C_{total}$ = total annual production cost (₹). Then:

$$\text{Energy Cost Share} = \frac{SEC \times M \times R}{C_{total}} \times 100\ \%$$

*Explanation:* This fraction reveals how much of every rupee earned goes to energy. KTU benchmark values: **8–15%** (low energy-intensive), **20–35%** (high energy-intensive like aluminium, cement, steel).

**Step 4:** Savings achievable through ΔSEC reduction

$$\Delta C = \Delta SEC \times M \times R$$

*Explanation:* If SEC is reduced from $SEC_1$ to $SEC_2$, then $\Delta SEC = SEC_1 - SEC_2$ gives the energy saved per ton. Multiplying by annual output and tariff gives the *annual monetary saving* — the foundation of every energy audit business case.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Numerical Demonstration (KTU Part B Style)

**Problem:** A sugar mill produces $M = 50{,}000$ tonnes/year. Current $SEC = 320\ kWh/ton$. The mill proposes a cogeneration upgrade that reduces SEC to $260\ kWh/ton$. Tariff $R = 8\ ₹/kWh$. Find:
1. Annual energy saved
2. Annual monetary savings
3. Percentage reduction in energy cost

**Step 1:** Initial annual energy consumption

$$E_1 = SEC_1 \times M = 320 \times 50{,}000 = 1.6 \times 10^7\ kWh$$

**Step 2:** New annual energy consumption

$$E_2 = SEC_2 \times M = 260 \times 50{,}000 = 1.3 \times 10^7\ kWh$$

**Step 3:** Annual energy saved

$$\Delta E = E_1 - E_2 = (1.6 - 1.3) \times 10^7 = 3.0 \times 10^6\ kWh/year$$

**Step 4:** Annual monetary savings

$$\Delta C = \Delta E \times R = 3.0 \times 10^6 \times 8 = \text{₹}\ 2.4 \times 10^7 = \text{₹}\ 2.4\ \text{Crore/year}$$

**Step 5:** Percentage reduction

$$\% Reduction = \frac{\Delta SEC}{SEC_1} \times 100 = \frac{320 - 260}{320} \times 100 = 18.75\ \%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A 18.75% SEC reduction translated to ₹2.4 Crore/year savings for this plant — illustrating *why* the "need" for energy management is overwhelmingly economic.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Simple Payback Period from Energy Savings

**Given:** Investment $I$ for an energy-saving device that reduces energy by $\Delta E$ kWh/year at tariff $R$.

**Step 1:** Annual monetary savings

$$S = \Delta E \times R$$

**Step 2:** Simple Payback Period

$$SPP = \frac{I}{S} = \frac{I}{\Delta E \times R}\ \text{years}$$

**Step 3:** Decision criterion (KTU examiner's rule)

$$\text{If } SPP \le 3\ \text{years} \Rightarrow \text{Investment is attractive (accept)}$$

**Step 4:** ROI calculation

$$ROI = \frac{S}{I} \times 100\ \%$$

*Explanation:* ROI > 33% (since 1/3 ≈ 33%) typically corresponds to SPP ≤ 3 years.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping "Need" Drivers to Engineering Solutions

| Need Driver | Root Cause | Engineering Solution | Expected Savings |
|-------------|-----------|---------------------|------------------|
| High motor energy | Oversized/inefficient motors | Replace with **IE3/IE4** motors + VFD | 20–40% |
| High lighting load | Incandescent/halogen | Switch to **LED** (≥ 100 lm/W) | 60–75% |
| Process heat loss | Uninsulated pipes/boilers | Thermal insulation (rock wool, ceramic) | 8–12% |
| Compressed air leaks | Poor maintenance | Leak audit + variable speed compressors | 20–30% |
| Poor power factor | Inductive loads | Capacitor banks (automatic) | PF penalty avoided |
| Waste heat | High-temp exhaust | Heat recovery boiler / economizer | 10–25% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
