# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**"Explain the need for energy management in modern industries."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The need for energy management arises from four interlinked drivers:

1. **Economic need:** Energy accounts for 10–30% of manufacturing cost; systematic reduction directly improves profit margins. *(1 mark)*
2. **Environmental need:** Energy generation produces ≈ 73% of global CO₂ emissions; conservation mitigates climate change. *(1 mark)*
3. **Resource scarcity:** Fossil fuel reserves are finite (oil ~47 yrs, coal ~133 yrs), demanding efficient utilization. *(0.5 marks)*
4. **Regulatory compliance:** The Energy Conservation Act 2001, PAT scheme, and ECBC mandate energy performance standards. *(0.5 marks)*

Hence, energy management has evolved from being optional to a **strategic necessity** for industries to remain competitive and sustainable.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**"Define Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) and state its significance."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition:** Specific Energy Consumption is the energy consumed per unit of output, expressed as: $SEC = E_{total} / M_{output}$, with units such as kWh/ton, MJ/kg, or kWh/m². *(2 marks)*
- **Significance:** SEC is the most widely used KPI in energy management because it:
  - Allows benchmarking across plants and time periods.
  - Decouples energy use from production volume, revealing true efficiency.
  - Provides direct input for cost reduction and emission calculations. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Format)

---

### Question B — Choice A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various **drivers and justifications for the need of energy management**, covering economic, environmental, resource, and regulatory dimensions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A cement plant produces $4 \times 10^5$ tonnes of clinker per year. The present Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) is **$800\ kWh/ton$**. The plant plans to install a waste heat recovery system (WHRS) that reduces SEC to **$720\ kWh/ton$**. The tariff is **₹7/kWh**. Calculate:
1. Annual energy saved (kWh)
2. Annual monetary savings (₹)
3. Percentage reduction in SEC
4. If the WHRS costs ₹28 Crore, find the Simple Payback Period. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Choice A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
The need for energy management is justified by four major pillars:

1. **Economic Driver (2 marks):** Energy is a major operating expense in industries like cement, steel, aluminium, and fertilizers. Even a 10% SEC reduction translates to crores of rupees in annual savings. For example, a 1% reduction in Indian industrial energy use saves ≈ ₹10,000 crore/year nationally.

2. **Environmental Driver (2 marks):** As per IPCC, the energy sector contributes ≈ 73% of global GHG emissions. India is the third-largest CO₂ emitter. Energy conservation through efficient motors, lighting, and processes directly reduces emissions (≈ 0.82 kg CO₂ per kWh saved on the Indian grid).

3. **Resource Scarcity (1.5 marks):** Fossil fuel reserves are depleting fast — oil (~47 years), natural gas (~52 years). Conservation extends resource availability and shields economies from price shocks.

4. **Regulatory and Strategic Need (1.5 marks):** The **Energy Conservation Act 2001** established BEE; the **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme covers 478+ designated consumers; **ECBC** mandates building efficiency. Non-compliance attracts penalties and loss of market credibility.

Together, these make energy management a **strategic, ethical, and economic imperative**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks (Step-by-Step)

**Given:** $M = 4 \times 10^5$ tonnes/yr; $SEC_1 = 800$ kWh/ton; $SEC_2 = 720$ kWh/ton; $R = 7$ ₹/kWh; $I = 28$ Crore.

**Step 1:** Initial annual energy
$$E_1 = SEC_1 \times M = 800 \times 4 \times 10^5 = 3.2 \times 10^8\ kWh$$

**Step 2:** New annual energy
$$E_2 = 720 \times 4 \times 10^5 = 2.88 \times 10^8\ kWh$$

**Step 3:** Annual energy saved
$$\Delta E = E_1 - E_2 = (3.2 - 2.88) \times 10^8 = 3.2 \times 10^7\ kWh/year$$

**Step 4:** Annual monetary savings
$$\Delta C = \Delta E \times R = 3.2 \times 10^7 \times 7 = \text{₹}\ 2.24 \times 10^8 = \text{₹}\ 22.4\ \text{Crore/year}$$

**Step 5:** Percentage SEC reduction
$$\%\ Reduction = \frac{SEC_1 - SEC_2}{SEC_1} \times 100 = \frac{800 - 720}{800} \times 100 = 10\ \%$$

**Step 6:** Simple Payback Period
$$SPP = \frac{I}{\Delta C} = \frac{28\ \text{Crore}}{22.4\ \text{Crore/year}} = 1.25\ \text{years}$$

**Conclusion:** With SPP = 1.25 years (well below the 3-year benchmark), the WHRS is **highly attractive** and should be implemented.

---

### Question B — Choice B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **sector-wise distribution of energy consumption in India** and identify the sector with the **greatest conservation potential**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A textile mill has the following data:
- Annual production: 25,000 tonnes
- Annual electricity bill: ₹1.5 Crore
- Tariff: ₹6/kWh
- After installing capacitors, the energy consumption drops by 8%.

Calculate:
1. Annual energy consumption (kWh) before capacitor installation
2. Annual energy saved (kWh)
3. New annual energy bill (₹)
4. Annual monetary savings (₹) *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Choice B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
India's commercial energy consumption is distributed as follows:

| Sector | Share | Dominant Energy Use |
|--------|-------|---------------------|
| Industry | ≈ 41% | Motors, boilers, process heat |
| Residential | ≈ 24% | Cooking, lighting, HVAC |
| Transport | ≈ 18% | Petrol, diesel, aviation |
| Commercial | ≈ 9% | HVAC, lighting, lifts |
| Agriculture | ≈ 8% | Pump sets, diesel engines |

**Greatest conservation potential: Industrial sector** *(3 marks)* because:
- It consumes the largest share (41%) of total energy.
- It contains the most inefficient legacy equipment (motors, boilers, compressed air systems).
- Studies by BEE indicate 15–25% of industrial energy can be saved with mature technologies.
- High concentration of *Designated Consumers (DCs)* under PAT scheme provides measurable accountability.
- Auxiliary systems (HVAC, compressed air, lighting) within industry itself contribute 30–50% of waste.

*(Conclusion for 7 marks: tie back to actionable engineering solutions — IE3 motors, VFDs, WHR, LED retrofits.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** Bill = ₹1.5 Crore; Tariff = ₹6/kWh; Saving = 8%.

**Step 1:** Annual energy consumption (before)
$$E_1 = \frac{Bill}{Tariff} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^7}{6} = 2.5 \times 10^6\ kWh/year$$

**Step 2:** Energy saved (8% reduction)
$$\Delta E = 0.08 \times E_1 = 0.08 \times 2.5 \times 10^6 = 2.0 \times 10^5\ kWh/year$$

**Step 3:** New annual consumption
$$E_2 = E_1 - \Delta E = 2.5 \times 10^6 - 2.0 \times 10^5 = 2.3 \times 10^6\ kWh/year$$

**Step 4:** New annual bill
$$Bill_{new} = E_2 \times 6 = 2.3 \times 10^6 \times 6 = \text{₹}\ 1.38 \times 10^7 = \text{₹}\ 1.38\ \text{Crore}$$

**Step 5:** Annual monetary savings
$$\Delta C = Bill - Bill_{new} = 1.5 - 1.38 = \text{₹}\ 0.12\ \text{Crore} = \text{₹}\ 12\ \text{Lakh/year}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Pitfalls — "Need" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Need for Energy" with "Need for Energy Management."** The first is demand-side; the second is the strategic response. Always clarify the *management* aspect.
> 2. **Skipping units in numerical answers.** KTU examiners *strictly deduct 0.5 marks* if kWh, ₹, or tonnes are missing.
> 3. **Not stating the decision criterion** (SPP < 3 years, ROI > 33%) after payback calculations. This shows application-level thinking.
> 4. **Forgetting to cite the regulatory framework** (Energy Conservation Act 2001, BEE, PAT, ECBC) in descriptive answers. KTU's NEP 2020-aligned rubrics reward policy awareness.
> 5. **Mixing up SEC and Energy Intensity.** SEC is per unit of product; EI is per unit of GDP. This is a frequently tested distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Need" in Energy Management**
>
> ✅ The "Need" is justified by **5 pillars**: Economic, Environmental, Resource, Regulatory, Security.
>
> ✅ **Industry consumes ~41%** of India's commercial energy — the prime target for conservation.
>
> ✅ **SEC** (Specific Energy Consumption) = Energy per unit output — the master KPI.
>
> ✅ **Energy Intensity** = Energy per unit GDP — the macro-level national indicator.
>
> ✅ **Indian grid emission factor:** ≈ **0.82 kg CO₂ per kWh** (essential for emission reduction calculations).
>
> ✅ **Conversion to memorize:** $1\ kWh = 3.6\ MJ = 860\ kcal$.
>
> ✅ **Decision rule:** SPP ≤ 3 years ⇒ attractive; ROI > 33% ⇒ attractive.
>
> ✅ **BEE & PAT scheme** under the **Energy Conservation Act 2001** are the key regulatory anchors.
>
> ✅ **Auxiliary systems (HVAC, compressed air, lighting)** offer the largest *immediate* conservation opportunity (30–50%).
>
> ✅ **Second Law of Thermodynamics** underpins why waste heat exists — but design choice governs *how much* waste.
>
> ✅ Always present numerical answers with **units, formulas used, and final interpretation** — KTU valuation keys reward structured presentation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Energy Audit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Types of Energy Audit ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Energy Audit (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** > An **Energy Audit** is a systematic, documented verification process of energy use in a system, process, facility, or organization, designed to identify energy flows, quantify energy consumption, identify opportunities for energy conservation, and report on energy-saving recommendations with their associated costs and benefits. The **Types of Energy Audit** refer to the structured classification of audit methodologies based on the **depth of investigation**, **scope of analysis**, and the **level of investment-grade detail** required for decision-making. KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes them primarily by audit intensity. ### The Three Standard Types of Energy Audit | Type | Alternate Name | Depth | |---|---|---| | **Type 1** | Preliminary / Walk-through Audit | Low | | **Type 2** | Detailed / Comprehensive Audit | Medium to High | | **Type 3** | Investment-Grade Audit (IGA) | Very High | --- ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of Energy Audits like Medical Check-ups for a Factory:** > > 🔹 **Walk-through Audit** = A quick general check-up. The doctor listens to your heart, checks your weight, asks basic questions. No blood tests. Cost is low, and the doctor might say "you look healthy" or "I noticed something concerning." > > 🔹 **Detailed Audit** = A full-body check-up with blood tests, ECG, X-rays. The doctor measures everything precisely and recommends treatments with approximate costs. > > 🔹 **Investment-Grade Audit** = A pre-surgical evaluation. Every parameter is measured with hospital-grade precision, every risk analyzed, every cost calculated to the last rupee because a major financial commitment depends on it. ### Key Standards & Metrics (Highlighted) - The most widely accepted standard is **ASHRAE Level 1, 2, 3 audits** — globally benchmarked. - The **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), Government of India** classifies audits under the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001**. - The typical audit deliverable metric is the **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** measured in **kWh/tonne**, **MJ/kg**, or **TOE/year**. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** **"W-D-I"** → **W**alk-through → **D**etailed → **I**nvestment-Grade (increasing depth and cost). ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### Type 1 — Preliminary Energy Audit (Walk-Through Audit) **Definition:** A quick, low-cost assessment that identifies glaring inefficiencies and rough savings opportunities without detailed measurements. **Operational Logic Steps:** 1. **Walk-through survey** of the entire facility to observe operations. 2. **Review utility bills** (electricity, fuel, water) for the past 12–24 months. 3. **Identify obvious waste points** — leaks, idling equipment, poor insulation. 4. **Benchmark** specific energy consumption against industry standards. 5. **Prepare a preliminary report** with rough savings estimates and pointers for detailed study. **Characteristics:** - Duration: **2–5 days** - Cost: Low (typically ₹20,000 – ₹1,00,000) - Tools used: Visual inspection, bill analysis, simple data loggers - Accuracy of savings estimate: **± 30–40%** - Does **NOT** involve detailed instrumentation --- ### Type 2 — Detailed Energy Audit **Definition:** A comprehensive, data-intensive audit involving detailed measurements, monitoring, and engineering analysis of every major energy-using system. **Operational Logic Steps:** 1. **Detailed data collection** on all energy-consuming equipment (motors, boilers, HVAC, compressors, lighting). 2. **Instrumentation** using power analyzers, flue gas analyzers, thermometers, lux meters. 3. **Energy balance** across each subsystem — inputs vs useful outputs vs losses. 4. **Identification of Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)** with quantified savings. 5. **Economic analysis** — Simple Payback Period (SPP), Return on Investment (ROI), Net Present Value (NPV). 6. **Prioritization matrix** ranking ECMs by cost-effectiveness. **Characteristics:** - Duration: **2–6 weeks** - Cost: Medium to High (₹1,00,000 – ₹10,00,000+) - Tools used: Power loggers, flue gas analyzers, thermal imagers, ultrasonic flow meters - Accuracy of savings estimate: **± 5–10%** - Requires a **qualified energy auditor** (Certified Energy Auditor – CEA) --- ### Type 3 — Investment-Grade Audit (IGA) **Definition:** The most rigorous level of audit, providing bankable, project-finance-grade data with quantified uncertainties for major capital investments. **Operational Logic Steps:** 1. **Long-term monitoring** (3–12 months) with calibrated instruments. 2. **Statistical analysis** with confidence intervals. 3. **Engineering simulation** using software (e.g., EnergyPlus, RETScreen, DOE-2). 4. **Detailed engineering design** of proposed modifications. 5. **Risk analysis, Monte Carlo simulation** for uncertainty quantification. 6. **Bankable financial model** with sensitivity analysis, IRR, NPV, and risk-adjusted returns. **Characteristics:** - Duration: **3–12 months** - Cost: Very High (₹10,00,000 – ₹1,00,00,000+) - Used for: **ESCO projects**, large retrofits, project financing - Accuracy of savings estimate: **± 2–5%** - Report is suitable for **third-party financing and verification** --- ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following formulas are essential for KTU exam calculations involving energy audit economics. | Formula | Equation | Purpose / Use | |---|---|---| | **Specific Energy Consumption** | $SEC = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy Consumed}}{\text{Unit of Output}}$ | Benchmarking energy intensity | | **Simple Payback Period** | $SPP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Savings}}$ | Quick economic screening | | **Return on Investment** | $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Annual Savings}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage return measure | | **Net Present Value** | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$ | Long-term financial viability | | **Internal Rate of Return** | $0 = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t}$ | Discount rate where NPV = 0 | | **Energy Cost Reduction** | $\Delta C = E \times (P_1 - P_2)$ | Saving from unit price change | | **Cumulative Energy Savings** | $E_{saved} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i \times h_i$ | Total kWh saved over period | | **Power Factor Correction Savings** | $S_{PF} = kW \times \left(\dfrac{1}{\cos\phi_1} - \dfrac{1}{\cos\phi_2}\right) \times C$ | kVA charge reduction | | **Heat Loss through Walls** | $Q = U \times A \times \Delta T$ | Building thermal audit | | **Boiler Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Combustion efficiency | > [!TIP] > **Remember:** For KTU numerical problems, **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** is the most frequently tested formula. Always state the **assumed values of discount rate (r)** and **project life (n)** when using NPV/IRR. ### Real-World Utility - **Manufacturing Plants:** Type 2 audit helps identify motor and compressed air inefficiencies. - **Buildings (Commercial):** Type 1 audit suffices for quick benchmarking; Type 2 needed for green building certification (LEED, GRIHA). - **Power Plants:** Type 3 audit mandatory before issuing **Energy Performance Contracts (EPCs)**. - **Process Industries:** Continuous energy audit enables **ISO 50001** compliance. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Derivation 1: Simple Payback Period (SPP) from First Principles **Logical Foundation:** If an investment yields a constant annual saving, the payback period is simply how many years of saving it takes to recover the original investment. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Let } & C_0 = \text{Initial Capital Investment (₹)} \\ & S = \text{Annual Monetary Savings (₹/year)} \\ & n = \text{Payback Period (years)} \\ \text{At payback:} \quad & C_0 = S \times n \\ \text{Rearranging:} \quad & n = \frac{C_0}{S} \\ \text{Including O\&M cost } M: \quad & S_{net} = S - M \\ \text{Final form:} \quad & \boxed{SPP = \frac{C_0}{S_{net}}} \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion Logic:** The investment must equal the cumulative savings. When O&M (Operations & Maintenance) costs are non-zero, subtract them from gross savings to get net savings. --- ### Derivation 2: Net Present Value (NPV) for Energy Project **Logical Foundation:** Money received in the future is worth less than money today (time value of money). NPV discounts all future cash flows back to present value. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Future Value of Cash Flow in year } t: \quad & FV_t = CF_t \times (1+r)^t \\ \text{Present Value of Cash Flow in year } t: \quad & PV_t = \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} \\ \text{Sum of all PVs over project life } n: \quad & \sum_{t=1}^{n} PV_t = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} \\ \text{NPV (Net of initial investment):} \quad & \boxed{NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0} \end{aligned} $$ **Decision Rule:** If $NPV > 0$, the project is financially viable. KTU exam convention: $r = 10\%$ unless stated otherwise. --- ### Derivation 3: Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) Reduction Percentage **Logical Foundation:** Useful for showing the percentage improvement achieved after implementing audit recommendations. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Baseline SEC:} \quad & SEC_b = \frac{E_{baseline}}{P_{baseline}} \\ \text{Post-audit SEC:} \quad & SEC_a = \frac{E_{after}}{P_{after}} \\ \text{Reduction:} \quad & \Delta SEC = SEC_b - SEC_a \\ \text{Percentage Reduction:} \quad & \boxed{\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{SEC_b - SEC_a}{SEC_b} \times 100\%} \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion Logic:** Divide energy by production to normalize; the difference (numerator) is the improvement, normalized by baseline (denominator). --- ### Worked-Out KTU-Style Numerical Problem > **Problem:** A factory spends ₹ 8,00,000 on a Type 2 Energy Audit. The audit identifies measures that will save ₹ 2,40,000 per year in electricity costs. The annual O&M cost of implementing the recommendations is ₹ 40,000. Calculate (a) Simple Payback Period, (b) ROI, and (c) NPV assuming a project life of 5 years and discount rate of 10%. #### Solution: **Part (a) — Simple Payback Period:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net Annual Savings} &= \text{Gross Savings} - \text{O\&M Cost} \\ S_{net} &= 2{,}40{,}000 - 40{,}000 = ₹ 2{,}00{,}000 \\ \text{SPP} &= \frac{C_0}{S_{net}} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \\ \boxed{\text{SPP} &= 4 \text{ years}} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b) — Return on Investment:** $$ \begin{aligned} ROI &= \frac{\text{Annual Net Savings}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100\% \\ ROI &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% \\ \boxed{ROI &= 25\%} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (c) — Net Present Value:** $$ \begin{aligned} NPV &= \sum_{t=1}^{5} \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{(1+0.10)^t} - 8{,}00{,}000 \\ NPV &= 2{,}00{,}000 \times \left[\frac{1}{1.10} + \frac{1}{1.21} + \frac{1}{1.331} + \frac{1}{1.4641} + \frac{1}{1.61051}\right] - 8{,}00{,}000 \\ NPV &= 2{,}00{,}000 \times [0.9091 + 0.8264 + 0.7513 + 0.6830 + 0.6209] - 8{,}00{,}000 \\ NPV &= 2{,}00{,}000 \times 3.7908 - 8{,}00{,}000 \\ NPV &= 7{,}58{,}160 - 8{,}00{,}000 \\ \boxed{NPV &= -₹\,41{,}840 \text{ (Project NOT viable at 10\% discount)}} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Even though SPP and ROI look favorable, **NPV is negative**, meaning at a 10% discount rate, the project does not generate enough returns. The student must note this **contradiction** in KTU exams — SPP alone is insufficient! --- ### Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three Audit Types | Parameter | Type 1 (Walk-through) | Type 2 (Detailed) | Type 3 (Investment-Grade) | |---|---|---|---| | **Duration** | 2–5 days | 2–6 weeks | 3–12 months | | **Cost** | ₹ 20K – 1 L | ₹ 1 L – 10 L | ₹ 10 L – 1 Cr+ | | **Manpower** | 1–2 auditors | 3–8 specialists | 10+ multi-disciplinary team | | **Instrumentation** | Minimal | Extensive | High-precision, calibrated | | **Accuracy of Savings Estimate** | ± 30–40% | ± 5–10% | ± 2–5% | | **Suitable for** | Initial screening | ECM identification | ESCO / Project finance | | **Output Document** | Preliminary report | Detailed audit report | Bankable feasibility study | | **Standards followed** | BEE guidelines | IS 13940 / ISO 50002 | IPMVP / EVO protocols | | **KPIs Computed** | SEC, Energy cost ratio | SPP, ROI, NPV per ECM | IRR, Risk-adjusted NPV, Monte Carlo | | **Typical Client** | SME / First-time assessors | Medium / Large industries | Banks, ESCOs, PSUs | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Diagram 1: Energy Audit Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Type ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Start: Need Energy Assessment] --> nodeB{What is the
Decision Being Made?} nodeB -- Quick screening or
first-time assessment --> nodeC[Type 1
Walk-Through Audit] nodeB -- Identify specific
ECMs and quantify savings --> nodeD[Type 2
Detailed Audit] nodeB -- Major investment
or ESCO project --> nodeE[Type 3
Investment-Grade Audit] nodeC --> nodeF{Identified Major
Savings Potential?} nodeF -- Yes --> nodeD nodeF -- No --> nodeG[Implement Quick Wins
and Exit] nodeD --> nodeH{Investment Threshold
Greater Than 50 Lakhs?} nodeH -- Yes --> nodeE nodeH -- No --> nodeI[Implement ECMs
with Detailed Report] nodeE --> nodeJ[Execute Energy
Performance Contract] style nodeA fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff style nodeC fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff style nodeD fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff style nodeE fill:#d62728,color:#fff style nodeJ fill:#9467bd,color:#fff ``` --- ### Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Detailed Energy Audit ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeIn1[Utility Bill Data] --> nodeProc1 nodeIn2[Equipment Inventory] --> nodeProc1 nodeIn3[Process Flow Diagrams] --> nodeProc1 nodeIn4[Production Data] --> nodeProc1 nodeProc1[Data Acquisition
and Validation] --> nodeProc2[Instrumentation

Power, Temperature,

per System] nodeProc4 --> nodeProc5[Identification of

SPP, ROI, NPV] nodeProc6 --> nodeProc7[Prioritization

Report] nodeProc7 --> nodeOut2[Implementation
