List its key features.** **Model Answer:** A **Walk-through Energy Audit** (also called **Preliminary Energy Audit**) is the most basic level of energy assessment that involves a physical walk-through of the facility, review of utility bills, identification of obvious energy wastage, and rough estimation of savings potential — all without detailed instrumentation. **Key Features:** - **Duration:** Typically 2–5 days. - **Involves:** Visual inspection, utility bill analysis, benchmarking of specific energy consumption. - **Tools:** Limited instrumentation — only basic portable instruments. - **Output:** A preliminary report identifying major energy-saving opportunities for further detailed study. - **Accuracy:** Savings estimate accuracy is only **±30–40%**. - **Suitable for:** SMEs, first-time assessors, plants beginning their energy management journey. --- #### **Question 2:** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between Detailed Energy Audit and Investment-Grade Audit.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Detailed Energy Audit | Investment-Grade Audit (IGA) | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Identify ECMs with engineering analysis | Provide bankable data for ESCO/project financing | | **Duration** | 2–6 weeks | 3–12 months | | **Instrumentation** | Extensive (power analyzers, flue gas analyzer) | High-precision, calibrated, long-term | | **Accuracy** | ±5–10% | ±2–5% | | **Financial Analysis** | SPP, ROI, basic NPV | IRR, risk-adjusted NPV, Monte Carlo simulation | | **Output** | Detailed audit report with ECM list | Bankable feasibility study with risk analysis | | **Typical Client** | Manufacturing units | Banks, ESCOs, large retrofits | --- ### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **three types of energy audits** with their methodology, instruments used, duration, cost, and typical outcomes. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A Type 2 energy audit identifies an Energy Conservation Measure (ECM) requiring an investment of ₹ 12,00,000. The measure yields annual savings of ₹ 3,60,000 with an annual O&M cost of ₹ 60,000. Calculate **(i)** Simple Payback Period, **(ii)** ROI, and **(iii)** NPV at 10% discount rate over 5 years. Comment on the project viability. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Three Types of Energy Audit — Detailed Explanation:** **1. Walk-through (Preliminary) Energy Audit:** A rapid assessment conducted in 2–5 days. The audit team conducts a visual inspection of the facility, reviews utility bills for 1–2 years, benchmarks SEC against industry norms, and identifies grossly inefficient equipment. **Instruments used:** basic portable lux meter, clamp meter, thermometer. **Cost:** ₹ 20,000 – ₹ 1,00,000. **Output:** preliminary report identifying major savings opportunities. **Accuracy:** ±30–40%. **2. Detailed Energy Audit:** The most commonly conducted audit, lasting 2–6 weeks. It involves systematic measurement and analysis of every major energy-consuming system (motors, boilers, HVAC, compressed air, lighting). **Instruments used:** power analyzers, flue gas analyzers, ultrasonic flow meters, thermal imagers, data loggers. The auditor creates an **energy balance** for each subsystem, identifies ECMs, and evaluates them using **SPP, ROI, and NPV**. **Cost:** ₹ 1,00,000 – ₹ 10,00,000. **Accuracy:** ±5–10%. Output is a comprehensive audit report suitable for management decision-making. **3. Investment-Grade Audit (IGA):** A rigorous, long-duration audit (3–12 months) generating bankable data. Involves long-term calibrated monitoring, statistical analysis with confidence intervals, engineering simulation (EnergyPlus, RETScreen), and **Monte Carlo risk analysis**. **Instruments:** Class 0.2 power analyzers, calibrated flue gas analyzers, ISO 50002 compliant. **Cost:** ₹ 10 Lakh – ₹ 1 Cr+. **Accuracy:** ±2–5%. The output is a project-finance-grade feasibility report used by ESCOs and banks. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given Data:** - Initial Investment $C_0$ = ₹ 12,00,000 - Gross Annual Savings = ₹ 3,60,000 - Annual O&M Cost = ₹ 60,000 - Discount rate $r$ = 10% = 0.10 - Project Life $n$ = 5 years **Step 1: Net Annual Savings** $$ S_{net} = 3{,}60{,}000 - 60{,}000 = ₹ 3{,}00{,}000 $$ **(i) Simple Payback Period (SPP):** $$ SPP = \frac{C_0}{S_{net}} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = \boxed{4 \text{ years}} $$ **(ii) Return on Investment (ROI):** $$ ROI = \frac{S_{net}}{C_0} \times 100\% = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = \boxed{25\%} $$ **(iii) Net Present Value (NPV):** Discount factors for 10% over 5 years: | Year $t$ | $CF_t$ (₹) | $(1.10)^t$ | $PV_t = CF_t / (1.10)^t$ (₹) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 3,00,000 | 1.1000 | 2,72,727 | | 2 | 3,00,000 | 1.2100 | 2,47,934 | | 3 | 3,00,000 | 1.3310 | 2,25,394 | | 4 | 3,00,000 | 1.4641 | 2,04,904 | | 5 | 3,00,000 | 1.6105 | 1,86,277 | $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Sum of PVs} &= 2{,}72{,}727 + 2{,}47{,}934 + 2{,}25{,}394 + 2{,}04{,}904 + 1{,}86{,}277 \\ &= ₹ 11{,}37{,}236 \\ NPV &= 11{,}37{,}236 - 12{,}00{,}000 \\ \boxed{NPV &= -₹\,62{,}764} \end{aligned} $$ **Comment:** Since **NPV < 0**, the project is **not financially viable** at a 10% discount rate, even though SPP and ROI appear favorable. This demonstrates the **limitation of using SPP alone** — KTU exams expect students to recognize this trade-off. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]** **(a)** Discuss the **methodology of conducting a Detailed Energy Audit** in an industrial facility. List the instruments used and the deliverables expected. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A compressed air system audit reveals that a 50 HP compressor runs idle for 2 hours daily. The motor efficiency is 88% and electricity costs ₹ 8 per kWh. Calculate the **(i)** annual energy wasted (kWh), **(ii)** annual monetary loss, and **(iii)** recommend one technical solution with payback justification. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) Methodology of Detailed Energy Audit:** A detailed energy audit follows a structured 7-step methodology: **Step 1 — Planning and Pre-Audit Preparation:** Define scope, identify audit team, collect plant layout, P&ID drawings, utility bills, production data. **Step 2 — Walk-through Survey:** Conduct initial walk-through to understand process flow and identify obvious inefficiencies. **Step 3 — Detailed Data Collection:** Collect rated capacity, load patterns, operating hours of major equipment. Install temporary instruments. **Step 4 — Measurements and Monitoring:** Conduct measurements using calibrated instruments for at least 3–7 working days covering different operating conditions. **Step 5 — Data Analysis and Energy Balance:** Compute energy balance for each system. Calculate SEC. Compare with benchmarks. **Step 6 — Identification of Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs):** Propose ECMs such as variable frequency drives, waste heat recovery, efficient lighting, capacitor banks for PF correction, etc. **Step 7 — Economic Evaluation and Reporting:** Evaluate each ECM using SPP, ROI, NPV. Prioritize. Submit report with implementation schedule. **Instruments Used:** Power analyzer, clamp meter, flue gas analyzer, lux meter, ultrasonic flow meter, thermographic camera, manometer, tachometer. **Deliverables:** Audit report, energy balance diagrams, ECM list with costs and savings, implementation roadmap, M&V (Measurement & Verification) plan. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given Data:** - Motor rating = 50 HP = 50 × 0.746 = **37.3 kW** - Idle hours per day = 2 hours - Operating days per year = 300 days - Motor efficiency $\eta$ = 88% = 0.88 - Electricity cost = ₹ 8 / kWh **(i) Annual Energy Wasted During Idling:** Even when idle, a compressor motor draws approximately **15–30%** of full load power. Assuming 25%: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{idle} &= 0.25 \times 37.3 = 9.325 \text{ kW} \\ \text{Annual idle hours} &= 2 \times 300 = 600 \text{ hours} \\ E_{wasted} &= P_{idle} \times t = 9.325 \times 600 \\ \boxed{E_{wasted} &= 5{,}595 \text{ kWh/year}} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Annual Monetary Loss:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Loss} &= E_{wasted} \times \text{Rate} \\ &= 5{,}595 \times 8 \\ \boxed{\text{Annual Loss} &= ₹\,44{,}760/\text{year}} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Recommended Solution:** > **Recommendation:** Install **automatic idle shut-off control** or a **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** to stop the compressor during idle periods. - **Cost of VFD:** Approximately ₹ 1,50,000 - **Annual Saving:** ₹ 44,760 - **Simple Payback Period:** $$ \boxed{SPP = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{44{,}760} = 3.35 \text{ years}} $$ **Conclusion:** With a payback of ~3.35 years, the VFD installation is **economically justified** and should be implemented. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing SPP with Discounted Payback:** Many students compute SPP only — KTU examiners expect NPV analysis for full marks on financial sub-parts. > 2. **Forgetting to subtract O&M cost:** If the question gives both gross savings and O&M cost, you **must compute net savings** before SPP/ROI. Marks lost = 1 mark. > 3. **Not stating units:** Always write **kWh/year**, **₹/year**, **years** explicitly. Examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit. > 4. **Mixing up audit types:** Type 1, 2, 3 — students often swap features. Memorize the **W-D-I progression** with depth and cost increasing. > 5. **Missing the "Comment" line:** Numerical answers in KTU require a final **viability statement** (e.g., "Project is viable since NPV > 0"). Missing this = 1 mark lost. > 6. **Incorrect motor unit conversion:** $1 HP = 0.746 kW$ — not 1 kW. Common mistake. > 7. **Idle power assumption:** When not given, use **25% of full load** for compressor idle, not 0%. Many students assume 0 and lose 2 marks. --- ### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision — Types of Energy Audit:** - **Three Types:** Walk-through (Type 1), Detailed (Type 2), Investment-Grade (Type 3) — memorize in increasing order of depth, cost, accuracy. - **ASHRAE Equivalents:** Level 1 = Walk-through, Level 2 = Detailed, Level 3 = IGA. - **Duration Ranges:** 2–5 days | 2–6 weeks | 3–12 months. - **Accuracy Levels:** ±30–40% | ±5–10% | ±2–5%. - **Key Formulas:** SPP, ROI, NPV, IRR, SEC — must be memorized and applied correctly. - **PV Factor Table for 10%:** Year 1 = 0.909, Year 2 = 0.826, Year 3 = 0.751, Year 4 = 0.683, Year 5 = 0.621. - **1 HP = 0.746 kW** — universal conversion for motor problems. - **NPV Decision Rule:** NPV > 0 → Accept, NPV < 0 → Reject. - **Detailed audit always includes:** Instrumentation, energy balance, ECMs, economic analysis, prioritized recommendations. - **Investment-Grade Audit is mandatory for:** ESCO contracts, project finance, large retrofits exceeding ₹ 50 Lakhs. - **Standards:** IS 13940 (India), ISO 50002 (International), IPMVP (Verification). - **Typical Energy Savings from Audit:** Walk-through (5–10%), Detailed (10–20%), IGA-based projects (20–40%). - **Cross-Connection:** This topic links directly to **Module 3 (Energy Efficiency in Electrical Systems)** and **Module 4 (Economic Analysis)**. - **Examiner's Favourite Question:** "Differentiate Detailed and Investment-Grade Audit" — appears almost every semester.

topics.title: Energy Management (Audit) Approach - Understanding Energy Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Management (Audit) Approach - Understanding Energy Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Energy Management** is the systematic, proactive, and well-documented process of controlling, monitoring, and conserving energy in a building, organization, or industrial facility. It combines technical, managerial, and financial strategies to optimize energy use while maintaining comfort, productivity, and safety.

The **Energy Audit Approach** is the foundational methodology used to understand, quantify, and manage energy consumption within a facility. It is a structured, step-by-step investigation that identifies *how*, *where*, *when*, and *why* energy is being used, and pinpoints opportunities for improvement.

**Understanding Energy Costs** refers to the systematic decomposition, measurement, and analysis of every monetary charge associated with the purchase, conversion, distribution, and use of energy (electricity, fuel, steam, compressed air, etc.) within a facility. It is the financial backbone of any energy audit because **no conservation project can be justified without a clear knowledge of what is being spent, where it is being spent, and how the cost is structured.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students must be able to describe the structured energy audit methodology, classify the components of energy cost (fixed vs. variable), and compute the cost of energy waste using tariff structures and specific energy consumption metrics.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of the Energy Audit Approach

| Pillar | Focus | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Consumption Analysis** | *How much* energy is used, *where*, *when* | Energy balance diagram |
| **Energy Cost Analysis** | *How much* money is spent, on *what*, under *which tariff* | Cost breakdown sheet |
| **Energy Conservation Opportunities (ECOs)** | *How* to reduce waste economically | Ranked project list with payback |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Family Grocery Budget"

Imagine a family that realizes their monthly grocery bill has become unmanageable. Before they can cut costs, they must first **understand their spending pattern**.

- **Step 1 — Track the Bill:** Just like reading an electricity bill, the family records *every* expense (milk, vegetables, snacks, fuel).
- **Step 2 — Categorize:** They split expenses into **fixed** (rent-like essentials: milk, LPG) and **variable** (luxuries: chocolates, restaurant meals). For electricity, fixed charges are like demand charges, variable charges are like per-kWh consumption charges.
- **Step 3 — Find the Leak:** They discover that 30% of their food is being thrown away — equivalent to **identifying energy waste** in a factory (leaking compressed air, idle motors, poor insulation).
- **Step 4 — Act:** They buy a fridge with better insulation (like replacing an old motor with an energy-efficient one) — this is the **Energy Conservation Opportunity (ECO)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** An energy audit is to an industry what a household budget review is to a family. You cannot reduce what you do not measure, and you cannot justify investment without knowing what is being wasted.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used Throughout the Energy Cost Analysis

The following **industry-standard metrics** form the vocabulary of every energy auditor:

- **kWh** (kilowatt-hour) — Unit of electrical energy; **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 860 kcal**.
- **kVA** (kilovolt-ampere) — Apparent power drawn from the grid.
- **kVArh** (kilovolt-ampere-reactive-hour) — Reactive energy consumed.
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** — Energy used per unit of production (e.g., kWh/ton, MJ/kg).
- **Power Factor (cos φ)** — Ratio of real power (kW) to apparent power (kVA).
- **Maximum Demand (MD)** — Highest kVA recorded in a billing cycle, usually over a 15- or 30-minute window.
- **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** — **1 TR = 3.516 kW** of cooling effect.
- **SCE (Standard Coal Equivalent)** — **1 kWh = 0.1229 kg of SCE** for energy benchmarking.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Structured Energy Audit Approach — Step-by-Step Logic

The energy audit is **not a one-time event**; it is a **cyclical, PDCA-style management process**. Below is the universally accepted KTU-aligned audit methodology:

### **Phase 1 — Pre-Audit Planning**
- Define audit boundary (single plant, single process, or entire facility).
- Gather historical data: electricity bills (12–24 months), fuel bills, production data.
- Form the cross-functional audit team (electrical, mechanical, process, finance).

### **Phase 2 — Walk-Through Audit (Preliminary Audit)**
- A qualitative, low-cost visual inspection of the entire facility.
- Identify obvious waste: leaking steam traps, open windows next to heaters, oversized motors, lights in unoccupied zones.
- **Goal:** Build a preliminary energy balance and shortlist high-priority areas for detailed study.

### **Phase 3 — Detailed Energy Audit**
- **Quantitative** measurement campaign using instruments: power analyzers, flue gas analyzers, thermographic cameras, ultrasonic flow meters, lux meters.
- Measurements are taken at major energy-consuming equipment (motors, boilers, compressors, chillers, furnaces).
- **Goal:** Produce a verified energy balance with ±5–10 % accuracy.

### **Phase 4 — Identification of Energy Conservation Opportunities (ECOs)**
- Each ECO is documented with:
  - Baseline energy use
  - Proposed saving
  - Capital investment
  - Annual monetary saving
  - Simple Payback Period (SPP)

### **Phase 5 — Implementation & Monitoring**
- Execute approved ECOs.
- Install sub-metering, EMS (Energy Management System), or SCADA-based monitoring.
- Verify savings using **M&V (Measurement & Verification)** protocols like IPMVP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Understanding Energy Costs" is Step Zero:**
> Every ECO is justified by its **cost saving**, not by energy saving alone. Energy saved at ₹0/unit is worthless; energy saved at ₹12/unit is a goldmine. Therefore, understanding the tariff is the *first* analytical task of any auditor.

## 2.2 Anatomy of an Electricity Bill — How Energy Cost is Structured

A typical industrial electricity bill contains the **following components**, each of which must be understood before any cost-reduction strategy can be designed:

1. **Fixed Charges (Demand-Based):**
   - Charged on **sanctioned load** or **recorded maximum demand**, whichever is higher.
   - Paid even if the factory is closed — equivalent to a "standing fee" for grid connection.

2. **Energy Charges (Variable):**
   - Charged per **kWh consumed** during the billing period.
   - May be **flat rate**, **slab-based** (e.g., ₹7 for first 1000 units, ₹8.5 thereafter), or **time-of-use (ToU)** (peak vs. off-peak).

3. **Demand Penalty (Excess Demand Charge):**
   - Levied if **recorded MD > contracted MD**. Usually **1–2× the normal demand rate**.
   - Encourages demand management through load shifting and peak clipping.

4. **Power Factor Penalty / Incentive:**
   - If PF < 0.90 (or 0.95, depending on the utility), a penalty is imposed.
   - If PF > threshold, an incentive may be paid.
   - **Critical for understanding capacitor bank economics.**

5. **Fuel Adjustment Charge (FAC) / Fuel Cost Adjustment (FCA):**
   - Variable surcharge tied to international fuel prices — changes monthly.

6. **Taxes & Duties:**
   - Electricity duty, GST, municipal taxes, surcharges — typically 15–30 % of base bill.

7. **Time-of-Use (ToU) Differential:**
   - Peak hours (e.g., 6 PM – 10 PM) may cost **₹10–12/unit**, while off-peak hours (e.g., 10 PM – 6 AM) may cost only **₹4–5/unit**.
   - **This single factor often drives the economics of load-shifting projects.**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat-Sheet Table:** Memorize these equations. Every KTU numerical problem on energy costs is built from one or more of them.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Electrical Energy | $E = P \times t$ | kWh | P in kW, t in hours |
| 2 | Real Power (3-φ) | $P = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \cos\varphi$ | kW | $V_L$ = Line voltage (kV), $I_L$ = Line current (A) |
| 3 | Apparent Power | $S = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L$ | kVA | |
| 4 | Reactive Power | $Q = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \sin\varphi$ | kVAr | |
| 5 | Power Factor | $\cos\varphi = \dfrac{P}{S} = \dfrac{\text{kW}}{\text{kVA}}$ | dimensionless | Must be $\geq 0.90$ to avoid penalty |
| 6 | Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{consumed}}{\text{Production output}}$ | kWh/ton, MJ/kg | Lower SEC = higher efficiency |
| 7 | Cost of Energy | $C_E = E \times r$ | ₹ | $r$ = tariff rate (₹/kWh) |
| 8 | Total Electricity Bill | $C_T = C_{fixed} + C_{energy} + C_{PF\,penalty} + C_{FAC} + \text{Taxes}$ | ₹ | |
| 9 | Cost of Wasted Energy | $C_{waste} = W \times r \times T$ | ₹/year | W = wasted power (kW), T = operating hours/yr |
| 10 | Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \dfrac{\text{Capital Investment}}{\text{Annual Monetary Saving}}$ | years | SPP $<$ 2 years = excellent project |
| 11 | Annual Energy Bill Equivalent | $C_{annual} = \sum (E_{month} \times r_{month})$ | ₹/year | Used for annual savings calculations |
| 12 | Maximum Demand Charge | $C_{MD} = MD_{recorded} \times r_{demand}$ | ₹ | Charged in ₹/kVA |
| 13 | PF Penalty | $C_{PF\,pen} = \left(0.90 - \cos\varphi\right) \times E \times r \times k$ | ₹ | k = utility-defined penalty factor |
| 14 | Energy in SCE | $E_{SCE} = E_{kWh} \times 0.1229$ | kg of SCE | For national benchmarking |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **In Industrial Plants:** Helps identify whether to invest in capacitor banks (PF correction), VFDs (variable frequency drives), or waste-heat recovery based on the *tariff structure* applicable.
- **In Commercial Buildings:** HVAC-related ToU shifting can deliver 15–25 % bill reduction without capital-intensive retrofits.
- **In Power Utilities:** Demand-side management (DSM) programs rely entirely on accurate customer-side energy cost models.
- **In ESG Reporting:** Carbon cost ($/tCO₂) is increasingly being internalized in energy bills through carbon taxes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 1 % improvement in plant power factor can reduce the apparent power drawn from the grid by 1–2 %, directly lowering the **maximum demand charge** — a saving that is often missed by auditors who focus only on kWh reduction.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Electrical Energy Cost from a Tariff Bill

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the **total annual electricity bill** for a small industry given its monthly consumption profile and a two-part tariff.

### Given Data (KTU-Style Numerical Setup)
- Contracted Maximum Demand: $MD_c = 500 \text{ kVA}$
- Recorded Maximum Demand: $MD_r = 480 \text{ kVA}$
- Monthly Energy Consumption: $E = 90{,}000 \text{ kWh}$
- Average Power Factor: $\cos\varphi = 0.88$
- Tariff structure:
  - Fixed (Demand) Charge: $r_d = ₹450 \text{ per kVA per month}$
  - Energy Charge: $r_e = ₹8.50 \text{ per kWh}$
  - PF Penalty: levied at ₹0.50 per kWh for every 0.01 fall below 0.90
  - Electricity Duty: 10 % of (Demand + Energy charges)

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Compute the monthly **Fixed / Demand Charge**.

$$
C_{fixed} = MD_c \times r_d
$$

$$
C_{fixed} = 500 \times 450 = ₹2{,}25{,}000 \text{ per month}
$$

*Logic:* The utility charges on the *contracted* demand (500 kVA), even though the recorded demand was only 480 kVA. This is to penalize over-subscription of capacity.

**Step 2:** Compute the monthly **Energy Charge**.

$$
C_{energy} = E \times r_e
$$

$$
C_{energy} = 90{,}000 \times 8.50 = ₹7{,}65{,}000 \text{ per month}
$$

*Logic:* Direct multiplication of total units consumed by the per-unit rate.

**Step 3:** Compute the **Power Factor Penalty**.

The PF is 0.88, which is 0.02 below the threshold of 0.90.

$$
\Delta PF = 0.90 - 0.88 = 0.02
$$

$$
\text{Number of penalty units} = \frac{\Delta PF}{0.01} = 2
$$

$$
C_{PF\,pen} = E \times r_{pen} \times \text{units} = 90{,}000 \times 0.50 \times 2 = ₹90{,}000 \text{ per month}
$$

*Logic:* Two blocks of ₹0.50/unit for every 0.01 drop. PF correction to 0.95 would eliminate this completely.

**Step 4:** Compute the **Electricity Duty**.

$$
C_{duty} = 0.10 \times (C_{fixed} + C_{energy})
$$

$$
C_{duty} = 0.10 \times (2{,}25{,}000 + 7{,}65{,}000) = ₹99{,}000 \text{ per month}
$$

*Logic:* Duty is typically applied to the *pre-penalty* base to avoid double taxation.

**Step 5:** Total Monthly Bill.

$$
C_{month} = C_{fixed} + C_{energy} + C_{PF\,pen} + C_{duty}
$$

$$
C_{month} = 2{,}25{,}000 + 7{,}65{,}000 + 90{,}000 + 99{,}000 = ₹11{,}79{,}000
$$

**Step 6:** Total **Annual Bill**.

$$
C_{annual} = C_{month} \times 12
$$

$$
C_{annual} = 11{,}79{,}000 \times 12 = ₹1{,}41{,}48{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Of this annual bill, the **demand charge alone is ₹27,00,000 (~19 %)** and the **PF penalty is ₹10,80,000 (~7.6 %)**. Together they represent **₹37.8 lakhs of "avoidable" annual cost** — enough to fund a ₹30-lakh capacitor bank with payback under 1 year.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cost of Energy Waste (Compressed Air Leak)

**Statement:** A compressed air system has 8 bar pressure with multiple leaks. Estimate the annual cost of compressed air leakage.

### Given Data
- Compressor motor rating: $P_{comp} = 75 \text{ kW}$
- Motor loading (specific power input): $SPC = 7.5 \text{ kW per 100 cfm}$
- Measured leak rate (equivalent orifice flow): $Q_{leak} = 60 \text{ cfm}$
- Compressor annual operating hours: $T = 6000 \text{ hr/yr}$
- Electricity tariff: $r = ₹9/\text{kWh}$

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Compute the **power wasted** due to leaks.

$$
P_{leak} = \frac{Q_{leak}}{100} \times SPC
$$

$$
P_{leak} = \frac{60}{100} \times 7.5 = 4.5 \text{ kW}
$$

*Logic:* Every 100 cfm of compressed air requires 7.5 kW of input power; therefore 60 cfm requires 4.5 kW.

**Step 2:** Compute the **annual energy wasted**.

$$
E_{leak} = P_{leak} \times T
$$

$$
E_{leak} = 4.5 \times 6000 = 27{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **annual monetary cost of the leak**.

$$
C_{leak} = E_{leak} \times r
$$

$$
C_{leak} = 27{,}000 \times 9 = ₹2{,}43{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Audit Insight:** A single small leak of 60 cfm costs the plant **₹2.43 lakh/year**. Multiply this by 20–30 such leaks typically found in a medium plant, and the "invisible" wastage easily crosses ₹50 lakh/year — a textbook example of why energy cost understanding must precede conservation analysis.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) Benchmarking

**Statement:** A cement plant produces 4500 tonnes of clinker per day. Determine the SEC and benchmark it against the national best-practice value.

### Given Data
- Daily Clinker Production: $M = 4500 \text{ t/day}$
- Daily Electrical Energy Consumption: $E_d = 2{,}70{,}000 \text{ kWh}$
- Daily Thermal Energy (coal) Consumption: $H_d = 1.2 \times 10^9 \text{ kcal}$

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Compute the **electrical SEC**.

$$
SEC_e = \frac{E_d}{M}
$$

$$
SEC_e = \frac{2{,}70{,}000}{4500} = 60 \text{ kWh/tonne of clinker}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the **thermal SEC** in kcal/tonne.

$$
SEC_t = \frac{H_d}{M}
$$

$$
SEC_t = \frac{1.2 \times 10^9}{4500} = 2{,}66{,}667 \text{ kcal/tonne}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert electrical SEC to **kcal/tonne** for combined benchmarking.

Using **1 kWh = 860 kcal**:

$$
SEC_{e,\,kcal} = 60 \times 860 = 51{,}600 \text{ kcal/tonne}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the **Total SEC**.

$$
SEC_{total} = SEC_t + SEC_{e,\,kcal}
$$

$$
SEC_{total} = 2{,}66{,}667 + 51{,}600 = 3{,}18{,}267 \text{ kcal/tonne}
$$

$$
SEC_{total} = 3{,}18{,}267 \text{ kcal/tonne} = 3.18 \text{ GCal/tonne}
$$

**Step 5:** Benchmark against **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** norms.

- **Indian Best Practice:** ~3.0 GCal/tonne
- **World Best Practice:** ~2.8 GCal/tonne
- **Our Plant:** 3.18 GCal/tonne → **6 % above best practice** → scope for improvement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** SEC is the **single most important metric** in any energy audit. It translates raw kWh and tonnes of coal into a normalized, comparable, technology-agnostic number that survives across plant sizes and product mixes.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Tariff Components to Conservation Strategies

| Tariff Component | Formula Basis | Typical % of Bill | Best Conservation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Demand Charge | $MD \times r_d$ | 15–25 % | Load scheduling, peak clipping, VFDs |
| Energy Charge | $E \times r_e$ | 50–65 % | Efficient equipment, insulation, process optimization |
| PF Penalty | $f(PF)$ | 2–10 % | Automatic capacitor banks (APFC panels) |
| Fuel Adjustment | $\alpha \times E$ | 5–15 % | Reduce kWh consumption (same effect) |
| Time-of-Use Surcharge | $r_{peak} \times E_{peak}$ | 0–20 % | Shift load to off-peak hours |
| Taxes & Duties | $\% \times \text{Base}$ | 15–30 % | Reduces automatically when base reduces |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** When asked in KTU exams *"Which component gives the largest saving opportunity?"*, the answer is **never "energy charge" alone** — it is always the **interaction** between energy charge and demand/ToU penalties that defines true ROI.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Five-Phase Energy Audit Approach

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1: Pre-Audit Planning] --> B[Phase 2: Walk-Through Audit]
    B --> C[Phase 3: Detailed Measurements]
    C --> D[Phase 4: Identify ECOs]
    D --> E[Phase 5: Implementation and M and V]
    E --> A

    A1[Collect 12-24 month bills] --> A
    A2[Map energy sources] --> A
    A3[Form audit team] --> A

    B1[Visual inspection] --> B
    B2[Identify obvious waste] --> B
    B3[Preliminary energy balance] --> B

    C1[Power analyzer data] --> C
    C2[Flue gas analysis] --> C
    C3[Ultrasonic leak survey] --> C
    C4[Verified energy balance] --> C

    D1[Baseline kWh and cost] --> D
    D2[Estimate savings] --> D
    D3[Compute Simple Payback] --> D

    E1[Install EMS and submeters] --> E
    E2[Verify savings vs baseline] --> E
    E3[Document for ISO 50001] --> E
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Anatomy of an Industrial Energy Cost

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TC[Total Annual Energy Bill] --> FC[Fixed Charges]
    TC --> VC[Variable Charges]
    TC --> PEN[Penalties and Incentives]
    TC --> TX[Taxes and Duties]

    FC --> F1[Demand Charge: MD x rate]
    FC --> F2[Service Connection Fee]

    VC --> V1[Energy Charge: kWh x rate]
    VC --> V2[Time of Use Differential]
    VC --> V3[Fuel Adjustment Charge]

    PEN --> P1[Power Factor Penalty]
    PEN --> P2[Excess Demand Penalty]

    TX --> T1[Electricity Duty]
    TX --> T2[GST]
    TX --> T3[Municipal Surcharge]

    ECO1[Capacitor Bank] -.addresses.-> P1
    ECO2[Load Scheduling] -.addresses.-> F1
    ECO3[Efficient Motors] -.addresses.-> V1
    ECO4[Load Shifting] -.addresses.-> V2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Cost Flow During an Audit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Energy Sources: Grid, Diesel, Coal, Biomass] --> CON[Conversion Equipment: Boiler, Compressor, Chiller]
    CON --> END[End Use: Lighting, HVAC, Process, Motors]
    END --> PROD[Useful Output: tonnes, units, services]

    SRC -.wasted cost.-> WS1[Standby Losses]
    CON -.wasted cost.-> WS2[Conversion Losses]
    END -.wasted cost.-> WS3[Distribution Losses]
    END -.wasted cost.-> WS4[Usage Waste: idle, leaks, poor controls]

    WS1 --> AUDIT[Energy Audit Quantifies Every Loss]
    WS2 --> AUDIT
    WS3 --> AUDIT
    WS4 --> AUDIT
    AUDIT --> ECO[Ranked Energy Conservation Opportunities]
    ECO --> SAVE[Annual Monetary Saving in Rupees]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**List and briefly explain the three main components of an industrial electricity tariff. Why is each component important from an energy auditor's perspective?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — allocate ~1 mark per component):**

The three main components of an industrial electricity tariff are:

1. **Fixed / Demand Charge (1 mark):** Levied on the *contracted* or *recorded maximum demand* (in kVA), independent of energy actually consumed. **Audit importance:** Even zero-consumption months attract this charge, so demand management (load shifting, peak clipping) yields direct savings.

2. **Energy Charge (1 mark):** A variable charge based on *kWh units consumed* during the billing period. **Audit importance:** Forms the bulk of the bill (50–65 %); any kWh reduction through efficiency directly lowers this component.

3. **Power Factor Penalty / Incentive (1 mark):** A penalty for operating below the utility-mandated PF threshold (typically 0.90). **Audit importance:** PF correction via APFC panels is one of the fastest-payback ECOs in any plant.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Specific Energy Consumption (SEC). Explain why it is considered the most important metric in an energy audit.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Specific Energy Consumption is the energy consumed **per unit of production output**. Mathematically,
$$SEC = \frac{E_{consumed}}{\text{Production output}}$$
Common units include kWh/tonne, MJ/kg, and GCal/tonne.

- **Why it is the most important metric (1.5 marks):**
  (i) It **normalizes** energy use across plants of different sizes.
  (ii) It enables **direct benchmarking** against national/international best-practice values.
  (iii) It is **technology-agnostic** — improvements can be tracked over years.
  (iv) It is the basis for **BEE PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme compliance in India.

---

## **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Each Module carries internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** in the End-Semester Exam (ESE). Either may appear; attempt the one you can answer fully.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step methodology of conducting a detailed energy audit** in an industrial plant. Describe the data collected, instruments used, and typical deliverables at each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing unit consumes **1,20,000 kWh per month** at an average power factor of **0.82 lagging**. The utility tariff is:
- Demand charge: ₹400/kVA/month
- Energy charge: ₹7.50/kWh
- PF penalty: ₹0.60 per kWh for every 0.01 below 0.90

Calculate the **monthly electricity bill**, the **PF penalty component**, and the **annual saving** if the power factor is improved to **0.96** using a suitable capacitor bank. Assume the recorded maximum demand remains **350 kVA**. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Detailed Energy Audit Methodology (7 Marks)**

The detailed energy audit follows a **five-phase structured methodology**:

**Phase 1 — Pre-Audit Planning (1 Mark)**
- Define audit boundary, gather 12–24 months of electricity and fuel bills.
- Collect production data, plant layout, single-line diagrams.
- Form a cross-functional audit team (electrical, mechanical, process, finance).

**Phase 2 — Walk-Through Audit (1 Mark)**
- Visual inspection of all energy-consuming areas.
- Identify obvious inefficiencies: leaking steam traps, idle motors, poor insulation, lights in unoccupied zones.
- Deliverable: Preliminary energy balance and prioritized list of detailed study areas.

**Phase 3 — Detailed Measurements (2 Marks)**
- **Instruments used:**
  - Power analyzer (for kW, kVA, PF, harmonics)
  - Flue gas analyzer (for boiler efficiency)
  - Ultrasonic flow meter (for steam, water, air)
  - Thermographic camera (for insulation losses)
  - Lux meter, tachometer, vibration analyzer

**Phase 4 — Identification of ECOs (2 Marks)**
- Quantify each saving opportunity with baseline data.
- Estimate capital cost, annual monetary saving, and Simple Payback Period.
- Rank ECOs by SPP and Net Present Value (NPV).

**Phase 5 — Implementation & M&V (1 Mark)**
- Execute approved projects.
- Install Energy Management System (EMS), sub-meters, APFC panels.
- Verify savings using **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement & Verification Protocol)**.
- Document for **ISO 50001** certification.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Identify base PF and compute penalty blocks.**

Initial PF: $\cos\varphi_1 = 0.82$; Target PF: $\cos\varphi_2 = 0.96$.

$$
\Delta PF_{\text{initial}} = 0.90 - 0.82 = 0.08
$$

$$
\text{Penalty blocks (initial)} = \frac{0.08}{0.01} = 8
$$

After correction, PF = 0.96 > 0.90 → **No penalty.**

**Step 2: Compute monthly energy charge.**

$$
C_{energy} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 7.50 = ₹9{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Compute demand charge.**

$$
C_{demand} = 350 \times 400 = ₹1{,}40{,}000
$$

**Step 4: Compute PF penalty (initial).**

$$
C_{PF,1} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 0.60 \times 8 = ₹5{,}76{,}000
$$

**Step 5: Total monthly bill at PF = 0.82.**

$$
C_{T,1} = 9{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}40{,}000 + 5{,}76{,}000 = ₹16{,}16{,}000
$$

**Step 6: Total monthly bill at PF = 0.96 (no penalty).**

$$
C_{T,2} = 9{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}40{,}000 + 0 = ₹10{,}40{,}000
$$

**Step 7: Monthly saving.**

$$
\Delta C = C_{T,1} - C_{T,2} = 16{,}16{,}000 - 10{,}40{,}000 = ₹5{,}76{,}000 \text{ per month}
$$

**Step 8: Annual saving.**

$$
C_{annual\,save} = 5{,}76{,}000 \times 12 = ₹69{,}12{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> **Common mistakes:** (i) Forgetting to convert PF penalty into "blocks of 0.01" — students often write penalty as just $0.08 \times E \times r$ instead of $8 \times 0.60 \times E$. This costs 1–2 marks. (ii) Assuming demand charge also changes with PF correction — in most utility tariffs, the demand charge is on **kVA**, so if kW stays the same and PF rises, kVA *decreases* slightly. However, in this problem, the question states MD = 350 kVA remains constant, so demand charge is unchanged. Read the question carefully!

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2 Alternate]**

**(a)** Describe the **components of an electricity bill** in detail. Explain how each component influences the design of an energy conservation strategy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A compressed air system delivers air at **7 bar** with a measured leak rate of **120 cfm**. The compressor motor is rated **90 kW** with a **specific power consumption of 8.0 kW per 100 cfm**. The plant operates **5500 hours per year** at an electricity cost of **₹9.50/kWh**.
Compute:
(i) The **power wasted** due to leaks.
(ii) The **annual energy loss** in kWh.
(iii) The **annual monetary loss** in ₹.
(iv) The **maximum justifiable investment** for a leak-sealing project if the company requires a **Simple Payback Period ≤ 1.5 years**. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Components of an Electricity Bill (7 Marks)**

A typical HT industrial electricity bill contains the following components:

1. **Fixed / Demand Charges (~15–25 % of bill) (1.5 Marks):** Based on contracted or recorded maximum demand. **Strategy:** Schedule loads to avoid simultaneous peaks, install VFDs, downsize contract if consistently under-utilized.

2. **Energy / Variable Charges (~50–65 %) (1.5 Marks):** Per-kWh charge. **Strategy:** Use efficient motors (IE3/IE4), LED lighting, VFDs, heat recovery, insulation upgrades.

3. **Power Factor Penalty (~2–10 %) (1.5 Marks):** Penalty if PF < 0.90. **Strategy:** Install Automatic Power Factor Correction (APFC) panels — fastest ROI project in most plants.

4. **Fuel Adjustment Charge (~5–15 %) (1 Mark):** Pass-through based on fuel prices. **Strategy:** Reduce kWh — directly cuts this component.

5. **Time-of-Use Surcharge (0–20 %) (1 Mark):** Higher rates during peak hours. **Strategy:** Shift flexible loads (compressor charging, pre-heating, charging batteries) to off-peak hours.

6. **Taxes & Duties (~15–30 %) (0.5 Mark):** Applied on base bill. **Strategy:** All base-cost reductions automatically cascade into tax savings.

**Part (b) — Compressed Air Leak Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
$Q_{leak} = 120 \text{ cfm}$
$P_{motor} = 90 \text{ kW}$
$SPC = 8.0 \text{ kW/100 cfm}$
$T = 5500 \text{ hr/yr}$
$r = ₹9.50/\text{kWh}$

**Step (i): Power wasted.**

$$
P_{leak} = \frac{Q_{leak}}{100} \times SPC = \frac{120}{100} \times 8.0 = 9.6 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step (ii): Annual energy loss.**

$$
E_{leak} = P_{leak} \times T = 9.6 \times 5500 = 52{,}800 \text{ kWh/yr}
$$

**Step (iii): Annual monetary loss.**

$$
C_{leak} = E_{leak} \times r = 52{,}800 \times 9.50 = ₹5{,}01{,}600 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step (iv): Maximum justifiable investment.**

For SPP ≤ 1.5 years:

$$
\text{Max Investment} = \text{Annual Saving} \times \text{Max SPP}
$$

$$
I_{max} = 5{,}01{,}600 \times 1.5 = ₹7{,}52{,}400
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> **Common mistakes:** (i) Students often use **full motor rating (90 kW)** for the leak calculation — this is wrong; the *specific power* method (8.0 kW/100 cfm) must be applied. (ii) Many forget to multiply the SPC ratio correctly — the SPC already accounts for part-load efficiency. Always use $\frac{Q}{100} \times SPC$, never $P \times \frac{Q}{Q_{full}}$. Each mistake costs 1–2 marks.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Energy Management (Audit) Approach — Understanding Energy Costs:**

- ✅ **Energy Audit** = systematic study to identify, quantify, and reduce energy waste; not a one-time activity.
- ✅ **Three Types of Audits:** Walk-through (qualitative), Preliminary (semi-quantitative), Detailed (quantitative with instruments).
- ✅ **Five Audit Phases:** Pre-audit planning → Walk-through → Detailed measurements → ECO identification → Implementation & M&V.
- ✅ **Electricity Bill Components:** Fixed (demand) + Variable (energy) + PF penalty + Fuel adjustment + ToU + Taxes.
- ✅ **Tariff Structures to Memorize:** Flat rate, slab rate, two-part tariff (demand + energy), time-of-use tariff.
- ✅ **Key Formula:** $C_T = C_{fixed} + C_{energy} + C_{PF\,pen} + C_{FCA} + C_{duty}$
- ✅ **Power Factor Penalty Formula:** ₹ per block of 0.01 below 0.90 × Number of blocks × kWh.
- ✅ **SEC** is the single most important benchmarking metric — **lower is better**.
- ✅ **Cost of Waste Formula:** $C_{waste} = W \times r \times T$ — applies to leaks, idle loads, standby losses.
- ✅ **Simple Payback Period:** $SPP = \dfrac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Saving}}$ — **SPP < 2 yrs = excellent project**.
- ✅ **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 860 kcal = 0.1229 kg SCE** — memorize for unit conversions.
- ✅ **Maximum Demand** is recorded over a 15- or 30-minute sliding window — even a brief peak can spike the entire month's bill.
- ✅ **APFC (Automatic Power Factor Correction) panels** are typically the first ECO recommended in any plant.
- ✅ **Time-of-Use shifting** can deliver 15–25 % bill reduction in commercial buildings without major capital outlay.
- ✅ **M&V Protocol:** IPMVP (International Performance Measurement & Verification Protocol) is the global standard.
- ✅ **Standards to Know:** ISO 50001 (Energy Management System), BEE PAT Scheme, ISO 14001 (Environmental).
- ✅ **Audit Deliverable Hierarchy:** Energy Balance Diagram → Ranked ECO List → Implementation Roadmap → M&V Reports.
- ✅ **Always quote savings in ₹/year AND kWh/year** — auditors who quote only kWh miss the financial story.
- ✅ **In KTU exams,** draw **mermaid/flowchart-style energy audit phase diagrams** wherever the question asks for "explain the methodology" — visual answers earn extra credit.
- ✅ **Conversion factor for combined SEC (electrical + thermal):** Convert electrical kWh to kcal using **860 kcal/kWh** before summing.
- ✅ **Final memory hook:** *"Measure first, then manage. The energy cost tells you where the money is leaking — chase the leak, not the noise."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Benchmarking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Benchmarking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Benchmarking (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> Benchmarking is a **systematic, continuous, and structured process** of measuring and comparing an organization's energy performance (energy use, energy intensity, specific energy consumption, and energy efficiency practices) against **internally established baselines, industry peers, best-in-class performers, or recognized engineering/theoretical standards**, in order to identify performance gaps, prioritize improvement opportunities, and implement corrective actions that reduce energy consumption and costs.

In the context of **Energy Management and Conservation (PESFT741)**, benchmarking is recognized as the **third critical pillar** of the Energy Management cycle (after Energy Audit and Energy Monitoring & Targeting). It is formally codified under **ISO 50015:2014** ("Measurement and verification of energy performance of organizations — General principles and guidance") and forms the quantitative backbone of the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India** — Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT) scheme.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — EnPI (Energy Performance Indicator):**
> An EnPI is a **quantitative metric** (e.g., kWh/tonne of product, GJ/m², TOE/revenue) that reflects the energy performance of a facility, system, or organization. Benchmarking operates by comparing the EnPIs of the subject organization with reference values (benchmarks).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The School Report Card"**
> Imagine you are a student in Class 10. Your teacher doesn't just look at your marks alone; she **compares** your marks with:
> 1. **Your own past performance** (Internal Benchmark) — "Last year you scored 72%, now 81%. You improved!"
> 2. **Class average** (Industry/Peer Benchmark) — "Class average is 68%. You are doing well."
> 3. **Topper of the class** (Best-in-Class Benchmark) — "Topper scored 95%. That's the target."
>
> **Benchmarking in Energy Management works identically.** An industry does not just monitor its own electricity bill — it compares its energy use (per tonne of cement, per m² of built area, per ₹ of revenue) with itself last year, with peer industries, and with the best-in-class factory worldwide. The **gap** between where you are and where the best is, is the **improvement opportunity**.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics & Constants

The following physical and economic constants/metrics are **mandatory for KTU board examination answers**:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit |
|:-------|:---------|:---------------------|
| **TOE** | Tonne of Oil Equivalent | **1 TOE = 41.868 GJ = 11630 kWh** |
| **GJ** | Gigajoule | **1 GJ = 277.78 kWh** |
| **SEC** | Specific Energy Consumption | **kWh/unit product** or **GJ/tonne** |
| **EUI** | Energy Use Intensity (Index) | **kWh/m²/year** (buildings) |
| **kgCO₂/kWh** | Grid Emission Factor (India avg.) | **≈ 0.72 kg CO₂/kWh** |
| **PEC** | Primary Energy Consumption | **TOE/year** |

---

### 1.4 Why Benchmarking is Essential (KTU Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Undeniable Reasons (frequently asked in KTU ESE):**
> 1. **Quantifies improvement potential** — converts abstract "save energy" into ₹-per-year figures.
> 2. **Justifies capital investment** — management approves projects only when a credible benchmark gap exists.
> 3. **Enables regulatory compliance** — BEE-PAT scheme mandates industry-level benchmarking against sectoral SEC targets.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Recognized Types of Benchmarking

Benchmarking is **not a single technique** but a *family* of techniques. The KTU syllabus expects students to classify any benchmarking example correctly.

#### A. Internal Benchmarking
- **Definition:** Comparison of energy performance **between similar units within the same organization** (e.g., Plant A vs. Plant B of the same company; or Line 1 vs. Line 2 within a plant).
- **Why use it?** Data is freely available, comparison is fair (same processes, management, climate), and it rapidly identifies the *best internal practice* that can be replicated.
- **Limitation:** Does not capture external best practice; ceiling effect.

#### B. Competitive (External) Benchmarking
- **Definition:** Comparison against **direct industry competitors** in the same sector.
- **Example:** Indian cement plant A vs. cement plant B — comparing kWh/tonne of clinker.
- **Data sources:** BEE-PAT database, CII energy efficiency awards, industry associations (e.g., Indian Sugar Mills Association).

#### C. Generic / Process Benchmarking
- **Definition:** Comparison of a **specific energy-using process** (e.g., compressed air generation, HVAC, pumping) against the best-in-class *process* performance, regardless of industry.
- **Example:** A textile mill compares its boiler efficiency with a paper mill's boiler — both use the same process.

#### D. Strategic Benchmarking
- **Definition:** Comparison of **long-term strategic energy practices** (e.g., integration of renewable energy, ISO 50001 certification level) rather than operational numbers.
- **Strategic energy vision**: 100% renewable, net-zero by 2050.

#### E. Performance (Energy Performance Benchmarking)
- **Definition:** The most common form — comparing **EnPIs** (kWh/product, GJ/m²) with published standards, codes, or peers.

### 2.2 The Five-Phase Benchmarking Methodology (KTU High-Yield)

The **Energy Benchmarking Process** is conventionally presented in 5 sequential phases:

> **Phase 1 — Plan:**
> - Define scope (plant, process, system).
> - Select EnPI(s).
> - Identify benchmarking partners.
> - Set baseline year.

> **Phase 2 — Collect Data:**
> - Gather 12–36 months of historical utility bills (electricity, fuel, steam).
> - Collect production data, operating hours, weather data.
> - Normalize data (e.g., correct for degree-days, product mix).

> **Phase 3 — Analyze:**
> - Compute SEC, EUI, Energy Intensity.
> - Compare with baseline, peers, best-in-class.
> - Identify **Performance Gap** = (Benchmark EnPI) − (Your EnPI).

> **Phase 4 — Act (Implement):**
> - Prioritize projects by ROI, payback, and energy savings potential.
> - Implement Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs).
> - Establish new (tighter) benchmarks.

> **Phase 5 — Monitor & Re-Benchmark:**
> - Track EnPIs continuously.
> - Repeat annually (or quarterly) — **continuous improvement**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam ready. Memorize the formula and the units — examiners award marks for both.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units | Engineering Use |
|:-:|:------------------|:--------------|:------|:----------------|
| 1 | **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | $SEC = \dfrac{E}{P}$ | **kWh/tonne** or **GJ/tonne** | Most widely used EnPI in process industries |
| 2 | **Energy Use Intensity (EUI)** | $EUI = \dfrac{E}{A}$ | **kWh/m²/year** | Buildings (commercial, institutional) |
| 3 | **Energy Intensity (Economic)** | $EI = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | **TOE/₹ Crore** | Cross-sector comparison |
| 4 | **Total Energy Consumption** | $E = \sum(E_{elec} + E_{fuel} + E_{steam})$ | **kWh or TOE/year** | Aggregate baseline |
| 5 | **Performance Gap** | $\Delta = SEC_{bench} - SEC_{actual}$ | **kWh/tonne** | Identifies saving potential |
| 6 | **% Improvement** | $\%I = \dfrac{SEC_{old} - SEC_{new}}{SEC_{old}} \times 100$ | **%** | Quantifies ECM impact |
| 7 | **CO₂ Emissions** | $CO_2 = E \times EF$ | **kg CO₂** | Carbon footprint; $EF \approx \mathbf{0.72 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kWh}}$ (India) |
| 8 | **Energy Cost per Unit** | $C_u = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy Cost}}{\text{Total Production}}$ | **₹/tonne** | Financial benchmarking |
| 9 | **Boiler Efficiency** | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{(H_g - H_f)}{F \times GCV}$ | **%** | Thermal benchmark |
| 10 | **Normalization Factor** | $E_{norm} = E_{actual} \times \dfrac{P_{ref}}{P_{actual}}$ | **kWh** | Compare across production levels |

> **Note on Table Notation:** All absolute-value and division bars are rendered using `\vert`/`\mid` LaTeX commands to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Applications of Benchmarking:**
> - **Cement:** kWh/tonne of cement (Indian best-in-class ≈ 65 kWh/t; global best ≈ 50 kWh/t).
> - **Steel:** GJ/tonne of crude steel (Indian average ≈ 28 GJ/t; best-in-class ≈ 18 GJ/t).
> - **Buildings:** kWh/m²/year (ECBC SuperECBC reference values).
> - **PAT Scheme:** Sectoral SEC targets notified by BEE every 3-year cycle.
> - **ISO 50001:** Clause 6.5 mandates *Energy Performance Evaluation* through EnPI benchmarking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Benchmarking Two Cement Plants

> **Problem Statement (KTU ESE-Style):**
> Two cement plants — Plant **X** (Your Plant) and Plant **Y** (Best-in-Class Peer) — have reported the following annual energy data. You are required to:
> 1. Compute the **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** for both plants.
> 2. Calculate the **Performance Gap** and **% improvement potential**.
> 3. Estimate the **annual monetary savings** if Plant X closes the gap to Plant Y's level, given electricity tariff = **₹8/kWh** and thermal fuel cost = **₹6/GJ**.
> 4. Estimate the **CO₂ emission reduction** achievable.

| Parameter | Plant X (Your Plant) | Plant Y (Best-in-Class) |
|:----------|:--------------------:|:-----------------------:|
| Electricity consumption | **12,00,000 kWh/year** | **9,50,000 kWh/year** |
| Thermal energy (fuel) | **18,000 GJ/year** | **13,500 GJ/year** |
| Cement production | **80,000 tonnes/year** | **75,000 tonnes/year** |
| Operating hours | 7,200 hr | 7,200 hr |

---

### 3.2 Step 1 — Compute Electricity SEC

$$SEC_{elec,X} = \dfrac{E_{elec,X}}{P_X} = \dfrac{1{,}200{,}000 \text{ kWh}}{80{,}000 \text{ t}} = 15.00 \text{ kWh/t}$$

$$SEC_{elec,Y} = \dfrac{E_{elec,Y}}{P_Y} = \dfrac{950{,}000 \text{ kWh}}{75{,}000 \text{ t}} = 12.67 \text{ kWh/t}$$

> **Interpretation:** Plant X uses 15.00 kWh to produce 1 tonne of cement; Plant Y uses only 12.67 kWh. **Plant X is electrically less efficient.**

---

### 3.3 Step 2 — Compute Thermal SEC (GJ/tonne)

$$SEC_{th,X} = \dfrac{E_{th,X}}{P_X} = \dfrac{18{,}000 \text{ GJ}}{80{,}000 \text{ t}} = 0.2250 \text{ GJ/t}$$

$$SEC_{th,Y} = \dfrac{E_{th,Y}}{P_Y} = \dfrac{13{,}500 \text{ GJ}}{75{,}000 \text{ t}} = 0.1800 \text{ GJ/t}$$

> **Interpretation:** Plant X uses 0.225 GJ of thermal energy per tonne; Plant Y uses only 0.180 GJ. **Plant X has a thermal efficiency gap.**

---

### 3.4 Step 3 — Convert All SEC to a Common Unit (kWh/tonne)

Using the conversion $1 \text{ GJ} = 277.78 \text{ kWh}$:

$$SEC_{th,X} = 0.2250 \times 277.78 = 62.50 \text{ kWh/t}$$

$$SEC_{th,Y} = 0.1800 \times 277.78 = 50.00 \text{ kWh/t}$$

> **Interpretation:** This conversion enables direct addition of electricity and thermal SECs.

---

### 3.5 Step 4 — Compute Total (Combined) SEC

$$SEC_{tot,X} = SEC_{elec,X} + SEC_{th,X} = 15.00 + 62.50 = 77.50 \text{ kWh/t}$$

$$SEC_{tot,Y} = SEC_{elec,Y} + SEC_{th,Y} = 12.67 + 50.00 = 62.67 \text{ kWh/t}$$

> **Interpretation:** Plant X's total energy footprint is 77.50 kWh/t; the benchmark is 62.67 kWh/t.

---

### 3.6 Step 5 — Performance Gap and % Improvement Potential

$$\Delta SEC = SEC_{tot,X} - SEC_{tot,Y} = 77.50 - 62.67 = 14.83 \text{ kWh/t}$$

$$\% \text{ Improvement} = \dfrac{\Delta SEC}{SEC_{tot,X}} \times 100 = \dfrac{14.83}{77.50} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Improvement} = 19.13\%$$

> **Interpretation:** If Plant X matches Plant Y, it can save **~19.13% of total energy**.

---

### 3.7 Step 6 — Annual Monetary Savings

Energy saved per year (in kWh equivalent):

$$\Delta E_{saved} = \Delta SEC \times P_X = 14.83 \text{ kWh/t} \times 80{,}000 \text{ t} = 11{,}86{,}400 \text{ kWh/year}$$

Splitting into electricity and thermal savings using the ratio 15.00 : 62.50 = 0.1935 : 0.8065:

$$\Delta E_{elec} = 11{,}86{,}400 \times \dfrac{15.00}{77.50} = 2{,}29{,}600 \text{ kWh/year}$$

$$\Delta E_{th} = 11{,}86{,}400 \times \dfrac{62.50}{77.50} = 9{,}56{,}800 \text{ kWh/year}$$

Convert thermal back to GJ: $9{,}56{,}800 \text{ kWh} \div 277.78 = 3{,}444 \text{ GJ/year}$

Monetary savings:

$$S_{elec} = 2{,}29{,}600 \text{ kWh} \times ₹8/\text{kWh} = ₹18{,}36{,}800/\text{year}$$

$$S_{th} = 3{,}444 \text{ GJ} \times ₹6/\text{GJ} = ₹20{,}664/\text{year}$$

$$\boxed{S_{total} = ₹18{,}57{,}464/\text{year} \approx ₹18.57 \text{ Lakhs/year}}$$

---

### 3.8 Step 7 — CO₂ Emission Reduction

Using India's grid emission factor **$EF = 0.72 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kWh}$**:

For electricity savings:
$$CO_{2,e} = 2{,}29{,}600 \times 0.72 = 1{,}65{,}312 \text{ kg CO}_2 = 165.31 \text{ tCO}_2$$

For thermal savings (assume fuel is coal with EF ≈ 0.10 kg CO₂/MJ):
$$CO_{2,t} = 3{,}444 \text{ GJ} \times 1000 \text{ MJ/GJ} \times 0.10 \text{ kg/MJ} = 3{,}44{,}400 \text{ kg} = 344.40 \text{ tCO}_2$$

$$\boxed{CO_{2,total} = 165.31 + 344.40 = 509.71 \text{ tCO}_2/\text{year}}$$

> **Interpretation:** Benchmarking reveals a **₹18.57 lakh/year** opportunity **and** a **509.71 tCO₂/year** decarbonization opportunity.

---

### 3.9 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Findings

| Metric | Plant X | Plant Y (Bench) | Gap | % Improvement |
|:-------|:-------:|:---------------:|:---:|:-------------:|
| Electrical SEC | 15.00 kWh/t | 12.67 kWh/t | 2.33 kWh/t | 15.55% |
| Thermal SEC | 62.50 kWh/t | 50.00 kWh/t | 12.50 kWh/t | 20.00% |
| **Total SEC** | **77.50 kWh/t** | **62.67 kWh/t** | **14.83 kWh/t** | **19.13%** |
| Annual Saving | — | — | — | **₹18.57 Lakh** |
| CO₂ Avoided | — | — | — | **509.71 tCO₂/yr** |

> **Engineering Insight:** The thermal gap (20%) is larger than the electrical gap (15.5%) — benchmarking correctly identifies **where** to invest first (boiler/combustion optimization before motor upgrade).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Five-Phase Benchmarking Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([START: Plant Energy Review]) --> nodeB[Phase 1: PLAN]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["Define scope and EnPIs"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2["Identify benchmark partners"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3["Set baseline year"]
    nodeB3 --> nodeC[Phase 2: COLLECT DATA]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["Utility bills 12-36 months"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC2["Production and operating data"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC3["Normalize variables"]
    nodeC3 --> nodeD[Phase 3: ANALYZE]
    nodeD --> nodeD1["Compute SEC and EUI"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD2["Compare with baseline and peers"]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD3["Identify Performance Gap"]
    nodeD3 --> nodeE{Performance Gap significant?}
    nodeE -->|YES| nodeF[Phase 4: ACT]
    nodeE -->|NO| nodeH[Continue Monitoring]
    nodeF --> nodeF1["Prioritize ECMs by ROI"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF2["Implement projects"]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF3["Update benchmarks tighter"]
    nodeF3 --> nodeG[Phase 5: MONITOR]
    nodeG --> nodeG1["Track EnPIs continuously"]
    nodeG1 --> nodeG2["Re-benchmark annually"]
    nodeG2 --> nodeD
    nodeH --> nodeG1
    nodeG --> nodeZ([CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT])
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is a **closed-loop PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle — the very backbone of **ISO 50001** and **BEE-PAT** compliance. Notice the feedback arrow from "Monitor" back to "Analyze" — benchmarking is **not a one-time activity**.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Types of Benchmarking Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeRoot[BENCHMARKING TYPES] --> nodeT1[Internal Benchmarking]
    nodeRoot --> nodeT2[Competitive Benchmarking]
    nodeRoot --> nodeT3[Generic Benchmarking]
    nodeRoot --> nodeT4[Strategic Benchmarking]
    nodeRoot --> nodeT5[Performance Benchmarking]
    nodeT1 --> nodeT1a["Compare similar units within own company"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT2a["Compare with direct industry competitors"]
    nodeT3 --> nodeT3a["Compare specific energy-using processes across industries"]
    nodeT4 --> nodeT4a["Compare long-term energy strategy and policies"]
    nodeT5 --> nodeT5a["Compare EnPI metrics with standards"]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture — EnPI Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Select Energy Performance Indicator]) --> q1{Process or Building?}
    q1 -->|Process Industry| p1[SEC: kWh per tonne]
    q1 -->|Commercial Building| p2[EUI: kWh per m2 per year]
    p1 --> p2a{Continuous or Batch?}
    p2a -->|Continuous| p3[kWh per unit product]
    p2a -->|Batch| p4[GJ per batch]
    p2 --> p3a{Climate Sensitive?}
    p3a -->|YES| p5[kWh per m2 per degree-day]
    p3a -->|NO| p6[kWh per m2 per year]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style, Module 2]**

#### **Q1. Define Energy Benchmarking. List any four types of benchmarking.**
*(3 Marks — Cognitive Level: Remember / Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
Energy Benchmarking is the systematic process of comparing an organization's energy performance against **its own historical performance**, **peer industries**, or **best-in-class standards**, using quantified Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs).

Four types:
1. **Internal Benchmarking** — comparison among similar units within the same organization.
2. **Competitive Benchmarking** — comparison with direct industry competitors.
3. **Generic/Process Benchmarking** — comparison of a specific process (e.g., boiler) across industries.
4. **Strategic Benchmarking** — comparison of long-term energy strategies and policies.

---

#### **Q2. What is Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)? Write its formula and state two industry examples of SEC units.**
*(3 Marks — Cognitive Level: Remember / Apply)*

**Model Answer:**
SEC is the energy consumed per unit of product or output — the most widely used EnPI in process industries.

$$SEC = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy Consumed}}{\text{Quantity of Product Produced}}$$

**Industry Examples:**
- **Cement:** $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{E \text{ (kWh)}}{P \text{ (tonnes of cement)}}$ — unit: **kWh/tonne**.
- **Steel:** $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{E \text{ (GJ)}}{P \text{ (tonnes of crude steel)}}$ — unit: **GJ/tonne**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** *(Option 1)*
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **five-phase methodology** of energy benchmarking in detail with a neat flowchart. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A textile dyeing unit consumes **8,00,000 kWh of electricity** and **6,500 GJ of thermal energy** per year, producing **1,000 tonnes** of dyed fabric. The industry benchmark SEC is **700 kWh/tonne** (combined). Compute:
- (i) The actual SEC of the unit (in kWh/tonne).
- (ii) The performance gap in kWh/tonne.
- (iii) The percentage improvement potential.
- (iv) The annual monetary saving at **₹9/kWh** electricity and **₹7/GJ** fuel. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** *(Option 2 — Alternative Choice)*
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Internal Benchmarking** and **Competitive Benchmarking**. Discuss their respective advantages and limitations with industry examples. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A commercial office building has an annual electricity consumption of **4,50,000 kWh** and a built-up area of **3,000 m²**. The benchmark Energy Use Intensity (EUI) for similar ECBC-rated buildings is **130 kWh/m²/year**. Compute:
- (i) The actual EUI of the building.
- (ii) The performance gap (in kWh/m²/year).
- (iii) The annual energy saving potential (in kWh).
- (iv) The annual monetary saving at ₹10/kWh, and the corresponding CO₂ reduction at 0.72 kg CO₂/kWh. *(7 Marks)*

---

### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

#### **Solution to Question A(b):**

**Step 1 — Convert thermal energy to kWh:**
$$E_{th} = 6{,}500 \text{ GJ} \times 277.78 \text{ kWh/GJ} = 18{,}05{,}570 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Total energy in kWh:**
$$E_{tot} = 8{,}00{,}000 + 18{,}05{,}570 = 26{,}05{,}570 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Actual SEC:**
$$SEC_{actual} = \dfrac{26{,}05{,}570}{1{,}000} = 2605.57 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

**Step 4 — Performance Gap:**
$$\Delta SEC = SEC_{actual} - SEC_{bench} = 2605.57 - 700 = 1905.57 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

> **Note to student:** A SEC of 2605 kWh/tonne is unusually high. Verify: typically textile dyeing SEC is ~5–15 GJ/tonne ≈ 1,400–4,200 kWh/tonne. The benchmark value of 700 kWh/tonne may have been set by an aggressive peer. The calculation methodology is what examiners reward.

**Step 5 — % Improvement Potential:**
$$\%I = \dfrac{1905.57}{2605.57} \times 100 = 73.13\%$$

**Step 6 — Annual Energy Saving:**
$$\Delta E = 1905.57 \times 1{,}000 = 19{,}05{,}570 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 7 — Allocate to electricity and thermal (ratio 8,00,000 : 18,05,570 = 0.3071 : 0.6929):**
$$\Delta E_{elec} = 19{,}05{,}570 \times 0.3071 = 5{,}85{,}260 \text{ kWh}$$
$$\Delta E_{th} = 19{,}05{,}570 \times 0.6929 = 13{,}20{,}310 \text{ kWh} = 4{,}753 \text{ GJ}$$

**Step 8 — Monetary Saving:**
$$S_{elec} = 5{,}85{,}260 \times 9 = ₹52{,}67{,}340$$
$$S_{th} = 4{,}753 \times 7 = ₹33{,}271$$
$$\boxed{S_{total} = ₹53{,}00{,}611 \approx ₹53.0 \text{ Lakhs/year}}$$

---

#### **Solution to Question B(b):**

**Step 1 — Actual EUI:**
$$EUI_{actual} = \dfrac{4{,}50{,}000}{3{,}000} = 150 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{year}$$

**Step 2 — Performance Gap:**
$$\Delta EUI = 150 - 130 = 20 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{year}$$

**Step 3 — Annual Energy Saving:**
$$\Delta E = 20 \times 3{,}000 = 60{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 4 — Monetary Saving:**
$$S = 60{,}000 \times 10 = ₹6{,}00{,}000/\text{year}$$

**Step 5 — CO₂ Reduction:**
$$CO_2 = 60{,}000 \times 0.72 = 43{,}200 \text{ kg CO}_2 = 43.2 \text{ tCO}_2/\text{year}$$

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Benchmarking Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting unit consistency** — mixing kWh with GJ in the same SEC calculation. **Always convert** all energy to a common unit (kWh or GJ) BEFORE dividing.
> 2. **Wrong benchmark identification** — comparing a textile plant's SEC with a chemical plant's SEC. The benchmark must be **sector-appropriate**.
> 3. **Skipping the % Improvement step** — KTU examiners award 2–3 marks specifically for the percentage calculation. Even if the absolute gap looks small, the % improvement reveals priority.
> 4. **No mention of normalization** — failing to note that data should be normalized for production volume, weather, product mix. Examiners penalize "raw bill" comparisons.
> 5. **Ignoring CO₂ accounting** — in 2024 scheme, every energy problem should mention the carbon footprint linkage (₹/tCO₂).
> 6. **Skipping the flow-diagram / block-diagram** — in 7-mark sub-parts, a labelled diagram earns **at least 2 marks** even if the written content is brief.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — "Benchmarking" (Module 2, PESFT741)**

- [ ] **Benchmarking** = systematic comparison of EnPIs across time / peers / best-in-class.
- [ ] **Five Types**: Internal, Competitive, Generic, Strategic, Performance.
- [ ] **Five-Phase Process**: Plan → Collect → Analyze → Act → Monitor (PDCA cycle).
- [ ] **EnPI** = Energy Performance Indicator (the metric being compared).
- [ ] **SEC Formula**: $SEC = E / P$ (kWh/tonne or GJ/tonne).
- [ ] **EUI Formula**: $EUI = E / A$ (kWh/m²/year).
- [ ] **% Improvement Formula**: $\%I = [(SEC_{old} - SEC_{new}) / SEC_{old}] \times 100$.
- [ ] **Conversion Constants**: $1 \text{ TOE} = 41.868 \text{ GJ} = 11{,}630 \text{ kWh}$.
- [ ] **CO₂ Emission Factor (India)**: $\approx 0.72 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kWh}$.
- [ ] **Performance Gap** $\Delta = \text{Your EnPI} - \text{Benchmark EnPI}$ — must be positive for savings.
- [ ] **BEE-PAT Scheme**: India's sectoral benchmarking and tradable energy savings mechanism.
- [ ] **ISO 50015**:2014**: International standard for M&V and benchmarking of organizations.
- [ ] **Always normalize** before comparing (production volume, weather, product mix).
- [ ] **Drawing the PDCA flowchart** earns easy marks in 7-mark sub-questions.
- [ ] **Mention ₹-savings AND tCO₂-reduction** in every numerical answer (2024 scheme emphasis).
- [ ] **Benchmarking is continuous**, not one-time — re-benchmark every cycle.
- [ ] **Top reason for benchmarking**: convert abstract "save energy" into defensible ₹/year business cases.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Energy Performance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Performance (EnP)** is the measurable result of energy efficiency, energy use, and energy consumption of an organization, building, process, equipment, or system, expressed in quantified terms through specific metrics that allow evaluation, comparison, and continuous improvement over a defined period.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT741)**, Energy Performance refers to a structured assessment framework that quantifies *how efficiently* a system converts, uses, or conserves energy relative to a defined output, service, or production unit. It is not just the amount of energy consumed, but **energy consumed per unit of useful output**.

### 1.1.1 Standardized Definitions (ISO 50001 / ISO 50015 Alignment)

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Performance** per ISO 50001:2018: The measurable results related to **energy efficiency**, **energy use**, and **energy consumption** of an organization or a system.

**Energy Efficiency (η)** — The ratio of useful output to energy input. A dimensionless or percentage quantity.

**Energy Use** — The amount of energy actually applied to deliver a product or service (in kWh, MJ, TOE, etc.).

**Energy Consumption** — The total energy drawn by a system boundary, including all losses.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Fuel Economy of a Car:**
> Think of Energy Performance like the **kilometers per litre (kmpl)** rating of your car.
> - A car consuming **20 kmpl** is energy-efficient compared to one at **10 kmpl**, even if both travel the same 200 km.
> - The **total fuel burnt** is "Energy Consumption".
> - The **distance covered per litre** is "Energy Performance".
> - When you compare **your** car's kmpl with the **industry-best** kmpl, you are doing **benchmarking**.
> - When you record today's kmpl as your **baseline** and track next month's kmpl, you are **monitoring Energy Performance**.

**Translating to Industry:**
- "kmpl" in a factory = **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** — energy per unit production.
- "Industry best kmpl" = **Benchmark**.
- "Today's kmpl" = **Baseline**.
- Tracking improvements = **Energy Performance Monitoring & Verification (M&V)**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are central to quantifying Energy Performance (bolded for syllabus emphasis):

- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** — kWh/unit, MJ/tonne, kWh/m², etc.
- **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** — Ratio of energy use to a relevant variable (production, degree-days, occupancy).
- **Energy Performance Index (EPI)** — Total energy consumed per unit floor area per year (kWh/m²/year).
- **Energy Intensity (EI)** — Energy per unit of economic output (TOE/million ₹).
- **Baseline Energy Performance (BEP)** — Reference quantitative measure serving as comparison.
- **Baseline Energy Consumption (BEC)** — Reference consumption before any ECM implementation.
- **M&V (Measurement & Verification)** — IPMVP Protocol.
- **TOE (Tonne of Oil Equivalent)** = **41.868 GJ** = **11,630 kWh**.
- **1 kWh** = **3.6 MJ** = **860 kcal**.
- **1 BTU** = **1055 J** = **0.293 Wh**.
- **Energy Efficiency (η)** is dimensionless, expressed as **%** when multiplied by 100.

---

## 1.4 Why Energy Performance Matters (KTU 2024 Emphasis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Regulatory Compliance**: Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) standards in India mandate EPI ratings.
> - **Cost Reduction**: Better Energy Performance directly lowers operational expenditure (OPEX).
> - **Carbon Accounting**: Energy Performance is the foundation for GHG (Greenhouse Gas) Scope 1, 2, and 3 reporting.
> - **Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 7**: Affordable and Clean Energy.
> - **ISO 50001 EnMS**: Energy Performance is the core audit metric.
> - **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme**: Energy Performance determines the Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts).

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Energy Performance

Energy Performance is a **multi-dimensional construct**. It comprises three measurable elements:

1. **Energy Efficiency (η)** — How well energy is converted to useful work.
2. **Energy Use (E_use)** — The deliberate energy applied toward a service/output.
3. **Energy Consumption (E_cons)** — The total energy drawn by the system, including all losses.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A typical 14-mark exam question tests the inter-relation between these three, often asking the student to compute **Energy Savings** using **Baseline vs. Reporting Period** data.

---

## 2.2 Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)

**SEC is the workhorse metric of Energy Performance.** It expresses energy used per unit of meaningful output.

$$
\text{SEC} = \frac{\text{Total Energy Consumed (kWh, MJ, TOE)}}{\text{Quantity of Output Produced (units, tonnes, m}^2\text{)}}
$$

### 2.2.1 Variants of SEC

| Sector | Typical SEC Unit |
|---|---|
| Cement Industry | kWh/tonne of clinker |
| Steel Industry | GJ/tonne of crude steel |
| Textile Industry | kWh/kg of fabric |
| Buildings | kWh/m²/year (EPI) |
| Transport | MJ/tonne-km |
| DG Set | kWh/litre of diesel |
| Compressed Air | kWh/m³ of free air delivered |

---

## 2.3 Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EnPI (Energy Performance Indicator)** is the metric chosen by an organization to track its Energy Performance over time. It is *not* fixed — it must be relevant to the organization's primary energy-consuming variable.

**General Form:**

$$
\text{EnPI} = \frac{\text{Energy Use (E)}}{\text{Energy Influencing Variable (X)}}
$$

**Example:** In an HVAC-dominated building:
- E = Annual electricity (kWh)
- X = Degree-Days (DD) or Occupancy × Hours
- EnPI = kWh/DD

### 2.3.1 Types of EnPIs

| Type | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Simple Ratio | E ÷ X | Single-variable production |
| Regression Model | E = a·X + b | Multi-variable statistical baseline |
| Specific Energy | E ÷ Output (kg, m²) | Manufacturing |
| Composite Index | Σ(weight_i × EnPI_i) | Multi-process plants |

---

## 2.4 Baseline Energy Performance (BEP)

**Baseline** is the reference quantitative measure against which subsequent performance is compared.

> [!NOTE]
> A baseline is established **before** implementing any Energy Conservation Measure (ECM). It can be:
> - **Static Baseline** — Fixed reference (e.g., last year's SEC).
> - **Dynamic Baseline** — Adjusted for production/weather using regression.

**Static Baseline Equation:**

$$
\text{BEP} = \text{SEC}_{\text{baseline period}} = \frac{E_{\text{baseline}}}{Q_{\text{baseline}}}
$$

**Dynamic Baseline Equation (Linear Regression):**

$$
E_{\text{baseline}} = a + b \cdot X
$$

Where:
- $a$ = Baseline fixed energy (kWh)
- $b$ = Baseline variable energy coefficient (kWh/unit)
- $X$ = Production / Degree-Days / Output

---

## 2.5 Energy Savings Calculation

Once the baseline and reporting period are defined:

$$
\text{Energy Savings} = E_{\text{baseline}} - E_{\text{reporting}}
$$

**Adjusted Baseline Savings (for production variation):**

$$
\text{Adjusted Savings} = (a + b \cdot X_{\text{rep}}) - E_{\text{reporting}}
$$

Where $X_{\text{rep}}$ is the production in the reporting period.

**Percentage Energy Savings:**

$$
\% \text{Savings} = \frac{E_{\text{baseline}} - E_{\text{reporting}}}{E_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100
$$

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every PESFT741 Module 2 question.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $SEC = \dfrac{E}{Q}$ | Specific Energy Consumption | kWh/unit |
| 2 | $EnPI = \dfrac{E}{X}$ | Energy Performance Indicator | kWh/X |
| 3 | $EPI = \dfrac{E_{\text{annual}}}{A_{\text{floor}}}$ | Energy Performance Index (Buildings) | kWh/m²/yr |
| 4 | $E_{\text{baseline}} = a + bX$ | Linear regression baseline | kWh |
| 5 | $\text{Savings} = E_b - E_r$ | Energy Savings | kWh |
| 6 | $\% \text{Savings} = \dfrac{E_b - E_r}{E_b} \times 100$ | Percentage savings | % |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{\text{output}}}{E_{\text{input}}} \times 100$ | Energy Efficiency | % |
| 8 | $\text{EI} = \dfrac{E}{\text{GDP or Revenue}}$ | Energy Intensity | TOE/₹ |
| 9 | $1 \text{ TOE} = 41.868 \text{ GJ}$ | Conversion | — |
| 10 | $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$ | Conversion | — |

---

## 2.7 Energy Performance Assessment Methodology (KTU 2024 Process Flow)

The Energy Performance Assessment follows the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle under ISO 50001:

### Phase 1 — Plan
- Define energy boundaries.
- Identify Energy Review baseline.
- Select EnPIs.

### Phase 2 — Do
- Implement measurement plan.
- Collect data (energy, production, ambient conditions).

### Phase 3 — Check
- Compare reporting period vs. baseline.
- Compute SEC, EnPI, savings.

### Phase 4 — Act
- Implement Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs).
- Verify savings through M&V (IPMVP).

---

## 2.8 IPMVP (International Performance Measurement & Verification Protocol) — KTU Highlight

> [!NOTE]
> **IPMVP** is the global standard for M&V. KTU Module 2 questions frequently ask the student to *name* the four M&V options.

| Option | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Option A** | Retrofit Isolation (key parameter measurement) | Lighting retrofit |
| **Option B** | Retrofit Isolation (all parameter measurement) | Compressed air system |
| **Option C** | Whole Facility (utility bill analysis) | Building-level savings |
| **Option D** | Calibrated Simulation | HVAC, new construction |

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industry:** SEC is the primary KPI tracked in cement, steel, paper, and textile plants.
- **Buildings:** EPI is mandated by **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code)** in India.
- **Utility:** Energy intensity (TOE/million ₹) is a national-level SDG tracker.
- **PAT Scheme:** Designated Consumers (DCs) under the BEE PAT must reduce their SEC.
- **ESG Reporting:** EnPIs are part of GRI 302 disclosures.
- **Carbon Markets:** Energy savings × Emission factor → Carbon credits (CCs).
