# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) and Percentage Savings

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

> A manufacturing unit consumed **1,20,000 kWh** of electricity in the **baseline year** and produced **8,000 units** of output.
> In the **reporting year**, after an ECM, it consumed **96,000 kWh** and produced **10,000 units**.
> Compute:
> (a) Baseline SEC, (b) Reporting SEC, (c) Energy savings (kWh), (d) Percentage energy savings (%).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Baseline SEC**

$$
SEC_b = \frac{E_b}{Q_b} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000 \text{ kWh}}{8{,}000 \text{ units}}
$$

$$
SEC_b = 15 \text{ kWh/unit}
$$

**Interpretation:** In the baseline year, each unit of output cost 15 kWh of electricity.

**Step 2 — Reporting SEC**

$$
SEC_r = \frac{E_r}{Q_r} = \frac{96{,}000 \text{ kWh}}{10{,}000 \text{ units}}
$$

$$
SEC_r = 9.6 \text{ kWh/unit}
$$

**Interpretation:** After the ECM, each unit requires only 9.6 kWh — a **36% improvement in Energy Performance**.

**Step 3 — Energy Savings (Naive / Unadjusted)**

$$
\Delta E = E_b - E_r = 1{,}20{,}000 - 96{,}000 = 24{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Energy Savings**

$$
\% \text{Savings} = \frac{E_b - E_r}{E_b} \times 100 = \frac{24{,}000}{1{,}20{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{Savings} = 20\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU:** The *naive* savings of 24,000 kWh (20%) **must be adjusted** for production increase (8,000 → 10,000 units). The naive percentage does NOT reflect true Energy Performance improvement.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Adjusted Baseline Energy Savings (Production-Adjusted)

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

> Using the data above, the baseline SEC was **15 kWh/unit**.
> The reporting period production was **10,000 units**.
> Compute the **adjusted baseline energy** and **adjusted energy savings**.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Adjusted Baseline Energy**

$$
E_{b,\text{adj}} = SEC_b \times Q_r = 15 \text{ kWh/unit} \times 10{,}000 \text{ units}
$$

$$
E_{b,\text{adj}} = 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Interpretation:** Had the plant maintained baseline Energy Performance (15 kWh/unit) while producing 10,000 units, it would have consumed 1,50,000 kWh.

**Step 2 — Adjusted Energy Savings**

$$
\Delta E_{\text{adj}} = E_{b,\text{adj}} - E_r = 1{,}50{,}000 - 96{,}000 = 54{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 3 — Adjusted Percentage Savings**

$$
\% \text{Savings}_{\text{adj}} = \frac{54{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} \times 100 = 36\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The **adjusted savings (36%)** reflects the *true* Energy Performance improvement, while the naive savings (20%) understates it. This is a **favorite 14-mark question** in PESFT741.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Energy Performance Index (EPI) for a Building

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

> A commercial office building with **5,000 m²** of air-conditioned floor area consumed **5,50,000 kWh** in one year. Compute the EPI and benchmark against the **ECBC standard of 90 kWh/m²/year**.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — EPI Calculation**

$$
EPI = \frac{E_{\text{annual}}}{A_{\text{floor}}} = \frac{5{,}50{,}000 \text{ kWh}}{5{,}000 \text{ m}^2}
$$

$$
EPI = 110 \text{ kWh/m}^2\text{/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Comparison with ECBC**

$$
\Delta EPI = EPI_{\text{actual}} - EPI_{\text{ECBC}} = 110 - 90 = 20 \text{ kWh/m}^2\text{/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Excess Energy**

$$
E_{\text{excess}} = 20 \text{ kWh/m}^2\text{/yr} \times 5{,}000 \text{ m}^2 = 1{,}00{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Monetary Impact (assuming ₹8/kWh)**

$$
\text{Annual Loss} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 8 = ₹8{,}00{,}000/\text{year}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Regression-Based Dynamic Baseline

### 3.4.1 Problem Setup

> A plant's monthly electricity consumption and production data (six months) is given below. Establish a **linear regression baseline** and predict consumption at production = 1,200 units.

| Month | Production $X$ (units) | Energy $E$ (kWh) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 800 | 8,000 |
| 2 | 900 | 8,800 |
| 3 | 1,000 | 9,500 |
| 4 | 1,100 | 10,300 |
| 5 | 1,200 | 11,000 |
| 6 | 1,300 | 11,800 |

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the Means**

$$
\bar{X} = \frac{800+900+1000+1100+1200+1300}{6} = \frac{6300}{6} = 1050
$$

$$
\bar{E} = \frac{8000+8800+9500+10300+11000+11800}{6} = \frac{59400}{6} = 9900
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Slope $b$**

$$
b = \frac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})(E_i - \bar{E})}{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}
$$

**Numerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (X_i - \bar{X})(E_i - \bar{E}) = & (-250)(-1900) + (-150)(-1100) + (-50)(-400) \\
& + (50)(400) + (150)(1100) + (250)(1900)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
= & 4{,}75{,}000 + 1{,}65{,}000 + 20{,}000 \\
& + 20{,}000 + 1{,}65{,}000 + 4{,}75{,}000 \\
= & 13{,}20{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Denominator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2 = & 62{,}500 + 22{,}500 + 2{,}500 \\
& + 2{,}500 + 22{,}500 + 62{,}500 \\
= & 1{,}75{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Slope:**

$$
b = \frac{13{,}20{,}000}{1{,}75{,}000} = 7.543 \text{ kWh/unit}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Intercept $a$**

$$
a = \bar{E} - b \cdot \bar{X} = 9900 - (7.543)(1050) = 9900 - 7920.15
$$

$$
a = 1979.85 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Regression Equation**

$$
E_{\text{baseline}} = 1979.85 + 7.543 \cdot X
$$

**Step 5 — Predict at $X = 1200$**

$$
E_{1200} = 1979.85 + (7.543)(1200) = 1979.85 + 9051.6 = 11{,}031.45 \text{ kWh}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The intercept $a = 1979.85$ kWh represents **fixed energy** (lighting, standby losses, HVAC idle). The slope $b = 7.543$ kWh/unit represents **variable energy** per unit of production. This separation is critical for ECM targeting.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Energy Performance Metric

| Scenario | Primary Metric | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Same product, variable production | SEC (kWh/unit) | Normalizes against output |
| Same product, varying weather | EnPI (kWh/DD) | Adjusts for ambient conditions |
| Multiple products | Weighted SEC | Aggregate via energy consumption |
| Office building | EPI (kWh/m²/yr) | ECBC compliance |
| Whole factory | EnPI + Composite Index | Captures multi-process reality |
| Capital project M&V | IPMVP Option C | Utility bill analysis |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Conversion of Energy Units (KTU Favourite)

### Problem

> A plant saves **5,00,000 kWh** per year. Express in (a) MJ, (b) TOE, (c) GJ.

### Solution

**(a) MJ:**

$$
5{,}00{,}000 \text{ kWh} \times 3.6 \text{ MJ/kWh} = 18{,}00{,}000 \text{ MJ} = 1800 \text{ GJ}
$$

**(b) TOE:**

$$
\frac{1800 \text{ GJ}}{41.868 \text{ GJ/TOE}} = 42.99 \text{ TOE}
$$

**(c) GJ (direct):**

$$
18{,}00{,}000 \text{ MJ} = 1800 \text{ GJ}
$$

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Performance Assessment — End-to-End Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Energy Boundary] --> B[Energy Review and Data Collection]
    B --> C[Identify Significant Energy Uses SEU]
    C --> D[Select Relevant EnPI Indicators]

    E --> F[Measure Baseline Period Consumption]
    F --> G[Compute SEC and EnPI Values]
    G --> H{Performance Acceptable?}
    H -- No --> I[Identify Energy Conservation Measures ECMs]
    I --> J[Implement ECMs]
    J --> K[Measure and Verify M and V]
    K --> L[Compare with Baseline]
    L --> M[Compute Energy Savings and Percent]
    M --> N[Update Baseline if Required]
    N --> H
    H -- Yes --> O[Continuous Monitoring and Improvement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram as:** Boundary → SEU → EnPI → Baseline → Measure → Compare → ECM → M&V → Improve. This sequence appears directly in KTU Module 2 theory questions.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI) and give two examples.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI) is a quantitative measure of energy performance that an organization defines for itself. It is expressed as a ratio of energy consumption to a relevant influencing variable (such as production output, degree-days, or occupancy).

> [!NOTE]
> Per **ISO 50001:2018**, the EnPI must be:
> - Measurable
> - Relevant to the organization's energy use
> - Trackable over time

**Two Examples:**
1. **kWh per tonne of cement produced** — for a cement plant.
2. **kWh per square metre per year (EPI)** — for a commercial building.

---

### **Question 2: Distinguish between Baseline Energy Consumption (BEC) and Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI).**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Baseline Energy Consumption (BEC) | Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | The reference quantity of energy used in the baseline period | The ratio of energy used to a relevant influencing variable |
| Nature | Absolute (kWh, MJ, TOE) | Relative (kWh/unit, kWh/m²) |
| Purpose | Reference for savings calculation | Tracking performance over time |
| Adjusts for production? | No (raw baseline) | Yes (normalized) |

---

**(b)** A textile unit consumed **8,50,000 kWh** of electricity and produced **50,000 kg** of fabric in the **baseline year**. After installing a variable frequency drive (VFD) on its motors, in the **reporting year** it consumed **7,20,000 kWh** for production of **60,000 kg** of fabric. Compute:
- (i) Baseline SEC and Reporting SEC
- (ii) Naive Energy Savings (kWh) and Naive % Savings

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative to Question A)*
`[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** What is a **Baseline** in Energy Management? Differentiate between **static baseline** and **dynamic baseline**. Explain the **regression-based dynamic baseline** with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A building has a **plinth area of 4,000 m²** and an **annual electricity consumption of 4,80,000 kWh**. The ECBC standard requires an EPI of **100 kWh/m²/year**. Compute:
- (i) Actual EPI of the building.
- (ii) Excess energy consumption in kWh/year.
- (iii) Annual financial loss at ₹9/kWh.
- (iv) Recommended energy conservation measures to achieve ECBC compliance. **(7 Marks)**

## 5.3 Detailed Model Solutions

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Definition and Components

> **Definition:**
> Energy Performance is the measurable result of energy efficiency, energy use, and energy consumption within a defined system boundary, expressed through specific metrics (SEC, EnPI, EPI) that allow comparison against a baseline.

**Three Components (ISO 50001:2018):**

1. **Energy Efficiency (η)** — Ratio of useful output to energy input.
   $$
   \eta = \frac{E_{\text{output}}}{E_{\text{input}}} \times 100\%
   $$

2. **Energy Use** — The energy actually applied to deliver a product or service.

3. **Energy Consumption** — The total energy drawn by the system, including all auxiliary and loss components.

> **Relationship:** Energy Consumption ≥ Energy Use (due to losses). Energy Performance improves when η rises and losses fall, for a given output.

---

#### Part (b) — Textile Unit Calculation

**Given:**
- Baseline: $E_b = 8,50,000$ kWh, $Q_b = 50,000$ kg
- Reporting: $E_r = 7,20,000$ kWh, $Q_r = 60,000$ kg

**(i) Baseline SEC and Reporting SEC**

$$
SEC_b = \frac{8{,}50{,}000}{50{,}000} = 17 \text{ kWh/kg}
$$

$$
SEC_r = \frac{7{,}20{,}000}{60{,}000} = 12 \text{ kWh/kg}
$$

**(ii) Naive Energy Savings**

$$
\Delta E_{\text{naive}} = 8{,}50{,}000 - 7{,}20{,}000 = 1{,}30{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
\% \text{Savings}_{\text{naive}} = \frac{1{,}30{,}000}{8{,}50{,}000} \times 100 = 15.29\%
$$

**(iii) Adjusted Energy Savings**

$$
E_{b,\text{adj}} = SEC_b \times Q_r = 17 \times 60{,}000 = 10{,}20{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
\Delta E_{\text{adj}} = 10{,}20{,}000 - 7{,}20{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
\% \text{Savings}_{\text{adj}} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{10{,}20{,}000} \times 100 = 29.41\%
$$

**(iv) Significance of Adjustment**

The naive savings (15.29%) significantly **understates** the true Energy Performance improvement because production rose by 20% (50,000 → 60,000 kg). Had the plant maintained baseline SEC at the higher production, it would have consumed 10,20,000 kWh. The VFD actually saved **3,00,000 kWh (29.41%)**, demonstrating a far better result than the naive figure suggests. **Adjustment is mandatory for fair M&V under IPMVP.**

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Baseline Definitions

> **Baseline** is the quantitative reference point that serves as the yardstick for comparing post-ECM energy performance.

**Static vs. Dynamic Baseline:**

| Aspect | Static Baseline | Dynamic Baseline |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Fixed reference (e.g., one specific year) | Continuously adjusted to operating variables |
| Formula | $E_b$ = constant | $E_b = a + bX$ |
| Use | Stable production, single product | Variable production, weather-sensitive |
| Example | Last year's total kWh | kWh vs. degree-days regression |

**Regression-Based Dynamic Baseline — Example:**

For an HVAC system:
$$
E_{\text{baseline}} = a + b \cdot (\text{Degree-Days})
$$

Using monthly data, regression yields coefficients $a$ and $b$. The baseline energy is *predicted* for the current month's degree-days, allowing weather-normalized comparison.

---

#### Part (b) — Building EPI Calculation

**Given:** Area $A = 4000$ m², $E_{\text{annual}} = 4,80,000$ kWh, ECBC EPI = 100 kWh/m²/year.

**(i) Actual EPI**

$$
EPI_{\text{actual}} = \frac{4{,}80{,}000}{4{,}000} = 120 \text{ kWh/m}^2\text{/year}
$$

**(ii) Excess Energy**

$$
\Delta EPI = 120 - 100 = 20 \text{ kWh/m}^2\text{/year}
$$

$$
E_{\text{excess}} = 20 \times 4{,}000 = 80{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**(iii) Financial Loss**

$$
\text{Loss} = 80{,}000 \times 9 = ₹7{,}20{,}000/\text{year}
$$

**(iv) Recommended ECMs**

1. **LED Lighting Retrofit** — replace conventional with LED (saves 50–70% lighting energy).
2. **5-Star Rated AC Units** — BEE star-labelled HVAC.
3. **VFD on Pumps and Fans** — for variable load operation.
4. **Building Management System (BMS)** — occupancy-based control.
5. **Improved Building Envelope** — double-glazed windows, roof insulation, reflective paint.
6. **Solar PV Installation** — on-site renewable generation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Using naive savings in M&V questions** — always show adjusted savings.
> 2. **Forgetting units in EPI** — write "kWh/m²/year", not just "kWh/m²".
> 3. **Mixing Energy Use with Energy Consumption** — they are different.
> 4. **Not stating the baseline period explicitly** — examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the regression equation form** — write $E = a + bX$, not just "regression".
> 6. **In unit conversion questions, dropping trailing decimals** — use **41.868 GJ/TOE** precisely.
> 7. **Confusing SEC (per unit product) with EPI (per unit area)** — they apply to different sectors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Kit — Energy Performance**

- [ ] **Energy Performance** = Efficiency + Energy Use + Energy Consumption.
- [ ] **SEC** is the most common Energy Performance metric in industry.
- [ ] **EnPI** must be **relevant, measurable, and trackable** (ISO 50001).
- [ ] **Baseline** is established **before** any ECM is implemented.
- [ ] **Static Baseline** = single reference value; **Dynamic Baseline** = regression-adjusted.
- [ ] **Adjusted Savings** = (Baseline SEC × Reporting Production) − Reporting Energy.
- [ ] Always **adjust for production/weather** in savings calculation.
- [ ] **EPI** = kWh/m²/year — used in **buildings** and mandated by **ECBC**.
- [ ] **IPMVP has four options: A, B, C, D** — know each one's application.
- [ ] **TOE** = **41.868 GJ** = **11,630 kWh**; **1 kWh** = **3.6 MJ**.
- [ ] **Energy Intensity** uses economic denominator (TOE/₹ or TOE/GDP).
- [ ] **PAT Scheme** DCs must reduce SEC; failing this requires buying **ESCerts**.
- [ ] Energy Performance = foundation of **Scope 2 GHG accounting**.
- [ ] **PDCA cycle** drives continuous Energy Performance improvement.
- [ ] **Regression baseline** separates **fixed energy** (intercept) from **variable energy** (slope).
- [ ] **Naive savings ≠ True savings** — always adjust for production variation.
- [ ] Energy Performance is a **core requirement of ISO 50001 EnMS audits**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Matching Energy Use to Requirement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Matching Energy Use to Requirement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Matching Energy Use to Requirement** is the systematic engineering process of aligning the **quantity, quality, and form of energy supplied** to a system, process, or facility with its **actual end-use demand**, in order to eliminate waste, minimize losses, and optimize the overall energy efficiency of the operation.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: **PESFT741**), this topic falls under the broader umbrella of **Energy Management Principles** and is a foundational Module 2 concept that bridges *energy auditing* with *load management*. The principle is mathematically expressed as:

$$\text{Matching Efficiency} = \frac{\text{Useful Energy Delivered to Task}}{\text{Total Energy Input to System}} \times 100\%$$

The goal is to ensure that the **supplied energy profile (kW, kWh, temperature grade, pressure grade, form of energy)** matches the **demand profile** as closely as possible — both in magnitude and in time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Water Tap and the Bucket**
>
> Imagine you are filling a small cup (200 mL) of water from a giant fire-hose that delivers 1000 liters per minute. Most of the water splashes out, gets wasted, and the floor gets wet. This is exactly what happens in a factory when a large boiler or oversized motor supplies far more energy than a process needs. The excess energy becomes **waste heat, vibration, reactive power, or steam venting** — a direct economic and environmental loss.
>
> **"Matching Energy Use to Requirement"** is the engineering discipline of choosing a tap (energy converter) whose flow rate *exactly* matches the size of the cup (the load). When the cup is half full, you throttle the tap. When the cup is nearly full, you reduce further. This is the essence of supply–demand matching in energy management.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Engineering Metrics

The following standard metrics form the backbone of matching energy use to requirement:

- **Load Factor (LF):** Ratio of average load to peak load over a given period.
- **Diversity Factor (DF):** Ratio of sum of individual maximum demands to the system maximum demand.
- **Demand Factor:** Ratio of maximum demand of a system to its connected load.
- **Utilization Factor:** Ratio of maximum demand to the rated capacity of the equipment.
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** Energy used per unit of output (e.g., kWh/ton, MJ/kg).
- **Cogeneration / Tri-generation Ratio:** The fraction of useful thermal energy recovered from a process.

## 1.4 Forms of Energy and End-Use Quality Matching

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Energy exists in multiple forms — **thermal, mechanical, electrical, chemical, and radiant**. A critical aspect of matching is ensuring that the *quality* (also called **exergy** or **availability**) of the energy supplied matches the *quality required* by the end use. Supplying high-grade electrical energy to a low-grade space-heating need is a classic example of quality mismatch.

The hierarchy (from highest to lowest quality) is:
1. Electrical energy
2. Mechanical (high-grade shaft work)
3. High-temperature thermal energy (>500 °C)
4. Medium-temperature thermal energy (150–500 °C)
5. Low-temperature thermal energy (<150 °C)
6. Ambient heat

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Energy Matching

The process of matching energy use to requirement rests on three engineering pillars:

### Pillar 1 — Right-Sizing (Capacity Matching)
Equipment is selected such that its **rated capacity** is as close as possible to the **actual peak demand**, leaving only a small engineering margin (typically 10–20%). Oversized equipment suffers from:
- Low part-load efficiency
- Higher capital cost
- Higher standby losses
- Poor power factor (in electrical systems)

### Pillar 2 — Time Matching (Load Duration Matching)
Energy consumption varies with time (hourly, daily, seasonal). Matching requires:
- **Load profiling** over a typical operating cycle.
- **Peak shaving** strategies (shifting loads to off-peak hours).
- **Load shifting** to align with low-tariff periods.

### Pillar 3 — Quality Matching (Exergy-Based Matching)
The second-law efficiency perspective demands that high-grade energy not be wasted on low-grade tasks. For instance:
- Use waste heat from a generator for absorption chilling.
- Use flue gas heat to preheat boiler feedwater.
- Match compressor discharge pressure exactly to process pressure.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Standard Units | Application / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Load Factor (LF) | $LF = \dfrac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Peak Load}}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | Measures how steady a load is — closer to 1 means better matching. |
| 2 | Diversity Factor (DF) | $DF = \dfrac{\sum \text{Individual Max Demands}}{\text{System Max Demand}}$ | Dimensionless (> 1) | Used in transformer/generator sizing for a group of loads. |
| 3 | Demand Factor (DFa) | $DFa = \dfrac{\text{Max Demand of System}}{\text{Connected Load}}$ | Dimensionless (< 1) | Used in electrical design for switchgear sizing. |
| 4 | Utilization Factor (UF) | $UF = \dfrac{\text{Max Demand}}{\text{Rated Capacity of Equipment}}$ | Dimensionless (< 1) | Indicates how fully a machine is being used. |
| 5 | Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) | $SEC = \dfrac{\text{Energy Consumed}}{\text{Output Produced}}$ | kWh/ton, MJ/kg | Benchmark for industry-wise energy performance. |
| 6 | Capacity Utilization | $CU = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Max Possible Output}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) | Production efficiency indicator. |
| 7 | Equipment Part-Load Efficiency | $\eta_{PL} = \eta_{rated} \times \left[a + b \cdot PLR - c \cdot PLR^2\right]$ | Dimensionless | Quadratic fit; $PLR$ = part-load ratio. |
| 8 | Heat Exchanger Effectiveness (ε) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{hot,in} - T_{hot,out}}{T_{hot,in} - T_{cold,in}}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | Degree of thermal matching achieved. |
| 9 | Pinch Point Temperature Difference | $\Delta T_{min} = T_{hot,stream} - T_{cold,stream}$ at pinch | °C or K | Minimum thermodynamic driving force in heat recovery. |
| 10 | Boiler Efficiency (Direct Method) | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_{steam} (h_{steam} - h_{feedwater})}{m_{fuel} \cdot GCV_{fuel}}$ | Percent (%) | Steam-system energy matching. |
| 11 | Compressor Isentropic Efficiency | $\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_{1}}{h_{2a} - h_{1}}$ | Percent (%) | Compressed-air system matching. |
| 12 | Power Factor | $pf = \cos(\phi) = \dfrac{P}{S} = \dfrac{kW}{kVA}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | Electrical matching — apparent vs real power. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note (KTU Examiner's Tip):** When asked to "match energy use to requirement," always justify your answer using **both** a *quantitative* parameter (LF, DF, SEC) **and** a *qualitative* (exergy / second-law) argument. Pure first-law arguments lose marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Matching energy use to requirement is the *operational heart* of:

- **Industrial energy audits** (ISO 50001 EnMS framework).
- **Building Energy Management Systems (BEMS)** for HVAC.
- **Steam system optimization** in textile, paper, and chemical plants.
- **Compressed air system** audits — where 70% of input energy is often wasted.
- **Pumping and fan system** re-engineering using affinity laws.
- **Renewable integration** — matching intermittent solar/wind to grid demand.

## 2.4 The Matching Process — Stepwise Logic

1. **Quantify the requirement** — measure actual energy demand using metering or audit.
2. **Profile the load** — build a 24-hour / 7-day / annual load curve.
3. **Audit the supply** — measure actual energy input at the source.
4. **Identify mismatches** — gaps in magnitude, timing, and quality.
5. **Select interventions** — VSDs, insulation, right-sizing, heat recovery.
6. **Implement and verify** — re-measure post-intervention performance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Load Factor and Energy Wasted Due to Mismatch

**Given:** A boiler with rated capacity $C_{rated} = 1000 \text{ kW}$ operates for 8 hours/day. The actual average load during operation is $L_{avg} = 400 \text{ kW}$.

**Step 1 — Define Load Factor (LF):**

$$LF = \frac{L_{avg}}{L_{peak}} = \frac{400 \text{ kW}}{1000 \text{ kW}} = 0.40 \text{ (or 40%)}$$

**Step 2 — Interpret the result:**

A load factor of 0.40 means the boiler is operating at only 40% of its capacity on average. The remaining 60% is *overcapacity* — this represents thermal losses through jacket surfaces, standby combustion air, and incomplete combustion at low fire.

**Step 3 — Quantify wasted input energy:**

If boiler efficiency at full load is $\eta_{FL} = 85\%$ and at 40% part-load drops to $\eta_{PL} = 70\%$, the extra fuel input per hour is:

$$\Delta Q_{fuel} = \frac{L_{avg}}{\eta_{FL}} - \frac{L_{avg}}{\eta_{PL}}$$

$$\Delta Q_{fuel} = \frac{400}{0.85} - \frac{400}{0.70}$$

$$\Delta Q_{fuel} = 470.59 \text{ kW} - 571.43 \text{ kW} = -100.84 \text{ kW (loss)}$$

**Step 4 — Daily energy waste:**

$$E_{waste,daily} = 100.84 \text{ kW} \times 8 \text{ h} = 806.7 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 5 — Annual waste (300 operating days):**

$$E_{waste,annual} = 806.7 \times 300 = 242{,}010 \text{ kWh/year}$$

This is a *direct quantifiable mismatch loss* — the foundation of energy management decisions.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Diversity Factor for Transformer Sizing

**Given:** Three industrial loads with individual maximum demands of:
- Load A: $D_A = 200 \text{ kW}$
- Load B: $D_B = 150 \text{ kW}$
- Load C: $D_C = 100 \text{ kW}$

The simultaneous peak demand recorded on the common transformer is $D_{system} = 350 \text{ kW}$.

**Step 1 — Sum of individual max demands:**

$$\sum D_i = 200 + 150 + 100 = 450 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Compute diversity factor:**

$$DF = \frac{\sum D_i}{D_{system}} = \frac{450}{350} = 1.286$$

**Step 3 — Engineering interpretation:**

A DF of 1.286 means the loads do not all peak simultaneously. Therefore, the transformer can be sized at $D_{system} = 350 \text{ kW}$ instead of $450 \text{ kW}$ — saving capital and reducing no-load losses.

**Step 4 — Sizing with safety margin (1.15):**

$$S_{transformer} = \frac{D_{system} \times 1.15}{pf \times \eta_{xfmr}}$$

$$S_{transformer} = \frac{350 \times 1.15}{0.85 \times 0.98} = 482.6 \text{ kVA}$$

A 500 kVA transformer is selected.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) Benchmarking

**Given:** A refrigeration plant produces 50 tonnes of cooling per day and consumes 1800 kWh of electrical energy.

**Step 1 — Compute SEC:**

$$SEC = \frac{E_{input}}{Q_{cooling}} = \frac{1800 \text{ kWh}}{50 \text{ TR-day}} = 36 \text{ kWh/TR-day}$$

**Step 2 — Compare to benchmark (industry best practice):**

If the benchmark is 28 kWh/TR-day, the plant is consuming:

$$E_{excess} = (36 - 28) \times 50 = 400 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 3 — Translate to cost (assume ₹8/kWh):**

$$\text{Cost}_{excess} = 400 \times 8 = ₹3200/\text{day} = ₹11{,}68{,}000/\text{year (300 days)}$$

This is the **financial value of matching** — quantifying why matching matters.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Mismatch Scenarios & Fixes

| # | Mismatch Scenario | Root Cause | Diagnostic Parameter | Recommended Fix |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Oversized motor running at 30% load | Wrong initial sizing | Current < 50% of nameplate | Replace with correctly sized motor + VSD |
| 2 | Boiler firing at low fire most of the day | Highly variable steam demand | LF < 0.4 | Install smaller standby boiler or modulate firing |
| 3 | Compressor running unloaded | Poor control logic | Unloaded hours / loaded hours ratio > 30% | Install sequencer + variable speed control |
| 4 | Chiller inefficiency at low ambient | Setpoint not reset | ΔT approach < 2°C | Reset chilled water T based on ambient |
| 5 | High-tension electricity used for room heating | Quality mismatch | Exergy destruction rate | Use heat pump or waste heat recovery |
| 6 | Pump throttled heavily | System resistance > design head | Valve ΔP > 35% of pump ΔP | Trim impeller or install VSD |
| 7 | Lighting full-on in unoccupied zones | Manual control | Lux vs occupancy | Install occupancy sensors + LED retrofit |
| 8 | Transformer loaded at 25% | Sum of small loads connected | UF < 0.4 | Consolidate loads or replace transformer |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Quantifying Quality Mismatch (Exergy Approach)

**Given:** A process requires low-grade heat at 80°C. Electricity is being used at a cost of ₹8/kWh to provide it via an electric resistance heater with efficiency 100%.

**Step 1 — Energy supplied:**

$$E_{in} = 100 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Useful thermal energy at 80°C (353 K):**

Assuming ambient $T_0 = 303$ K (30°C):

$$\text{Exergy of heat} = Q \left(1 - \frac{T_0}{T_{process}}\right)$$

$$\text{Exergy} = 100 \left(1 - \frac{303}{353}\right) = 100 \times 0.142 = 14.2 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Exergy efficiency of electrical heating for low-grade heat:**

$$\eta_{exergy} = \frac{\text{Useful Exergy}}{\text{Input Exergy}} = \frac{14.2}{100} = 14.2\%$$

**Step 4 — Implication:** 85.8% of the *quality* of the energy is destroyed. This is a quality mismatch. A heat pump with COP of 3.5 supplying the same 100 kWh thermal would consume only 28.6 kWh electrical — a **saving of 71.4%**.

## 3.6 Derivation 5 — The Pinch Point Principle (Heat Exchanger Network Matching)

For a heat recovery network, the minimum approach temperature $\Delta T_{min}$ is chosen to balance capital cost (more area, smaller $\Delta T_{min}$) against operating cost (more recovery, larger $\Delta T_{min}$).

For a counter-flow heat exchanger:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot LMTD$$

where:

$$LMTD = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

**Given:** Hot stream enters at 200°C, leaves at 100°C. Cold stream enters at 40°C, leaves at 120°C.

**Step 1 — Compute end-temperature differences:**

$$\Delta T_1 = 200 - 120 = 80°C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = 100 - 40 = 60°C$$

**Step 2 — Compute LMTD:**

$$LMTD = \frac{80 - 60}{\ln(80/60)} = \frac{20}{\ln(1.333)} = \frac{20}{0.2877} = 69.5°C$$

**Step 3 — Heat exchanged:**

For $U = 500 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$ and $A = 10 \text{ m}^2$:

$$Q = 500 \times 10 \times 69.5 = 347{,}500 \text{ W} \approx 347.5 \text{ kW}$$

This recovered heat represents energy that need not be supplied by the boiler — direct savings from energy matching.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Energy Matching Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Energy End-Use Requirement] --> B[Profile Load: Magnitude, Time, Quality]
    B --> C[Audit Energy Supply at Source]
    C --> D[Quantify Mismatch: Energy Gap]
    D --> E{Type of Mismatch?}
    E -->|Magnitude| F[Right-Sizing of Equipment]
    E -->|Time| G[Load Shifting and Peak Shaving]
    E -->|Quality| H[Exergy-Based Cascade Recovery]
    F --> I[Implement and Verify Savings]
    G --> I[Implement and Verify Savings]
    H --> I[Implement and Verify Savings]
    I --> J[Continuous Improvement ISO 50001]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Energy Management Information System (EMIS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SupplySide[Energy Supply Side]
        S1[Grid Power Input] --> S2[Transformer]
        S3[Boiler / Furnace] --> S4[Steam Header]
        S5[Compressed Air Plant] --> S6[Distribution Manifold]
        S7[DG Set] --> S1
    end
    subgraph Metering[Energy Metering and Monitoring]
        M1[Electrical Energy Meter]
        M2[Steam Flow Meter]
        M3[Air Flow Meter]
        M4[Fuel Flow Meter]
    end
    subgraph DemandSide[Energy Demand Side]
        D1[Motors and Drives]
        D2[Process Heating Coils]
        D3[Pneumatic Tools]
        D4[HVAC and Lighting]
        D5[Process Reactors]
    end
    subgraph Control[Matching Controller EMS]
        C1[Data Acquisition SCADA]
        C2[Load Profile Analyzer]
        C3[Mismatch Identification AI Module]
        C4[Optimization Engine]
    end
    S2 --> M1
    S4 --> M2
    S6 --> M3
    S3 --> M4
    M1 --> C1
    M2 --> C1
    M3 --> C1
    M4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    C4 --> D2
    C4 --> D3
    C4 --> D4
    C4 --> D5
    D1 --> Feedback[Process Output]
    D2 --> Feedback
    D3 --> Feedback
    D4 --> Feedback
    D5 --> Feedback
    Feedback --> C2
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Energy Matching Interventions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Level 1: Eliminate Unnecessary Use] --> L2[Level 2: Right-Size Equipment]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3: Match Quality to End Use]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4: Recover Waste Energy]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5: Optimize Control and Operation]
    L5 --> L6[Level 6: Continuous Monitoring and Improvement]
    L1 -.Lowest Cost.-> L2
    L6 -.Highest Investment.-> L5
```

## 4.4 Energy Cascade Diagram (Exergy Quality Matching)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Primary High-Grade Energy: Fuel or Electricity] --> B[High Temperature Process: above 500°C]
    B --> C[Medium Temperature Process: 150 to 500°C]
    C --> D[Low Temperature Process: below 150°C]
    D --> E[Space Heating: 30 to 60°C]
    E --> F[Domestic Hot Water: 50 to 80°C]
    F --> G[Rejected Heat to Environment]
    G -.Recovery Point.-> B
    B -.Waste Heat Recovery.-> D
    C -.Waste Heat Boiler.-> E
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define Load Factor. Why is it considered a key indicator of how well energy use is matched to requirement?**

**Model Answer:**
Load Factor is defined as the ratio of the average load over a given period to the peak (maximum) load occurring in that same period:

$$LF = \frac{\text{Average Load (kW)}}{\text{Peak Load (kW)}}$$

It is a key indicator of energy matching because:
- An LF close to **1.0** indicates the system is consistently operating near its peak demand — energy is well matched to actual need.
- A low LF (e.g., 0.3) indicates the system is oversized or demand is highly variable — leading to **low part-load efficiency, higher standby losses, and poor capacity utilization**.
- LF directly influences equipment selection, tariff optimization, and capital cost recovery.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Explain the concept of "quality matching" in energy management with one industrial example.**

**Model Answer:**
Quality matching refers to supplying energy of the appropriate *grade* (exergy) to match the end-use requirement, rather than always using high-grade energy (like electricity) for low-grade needs (like space heating).

**Industrial Example:** In a dairy plant, high-pressure steam from a boiler is first used in a process heater (which needs 180°C). The exhaust steam, still at ~120°C, is then cascaded to a milk pasteurizer, then to a cleaning-in-place (CIP) loop, and finally to space heating. This **cascade use** ensures the energy's quality is matched to each task, maximizing thermodynamic efficiency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** A manufacturing plant operates a 500 kVA transformer for 12 hours per day, 300 days per year. The maximum demand recorded on the transformer is 280 kW at 0.85 power factor. The connected load is 600 kW. Calculate:
- (i) Demand Factor [2 Marks]
- (ii) Diversity Factor (assume sum of individual max demands is 420 kW) [2 Marks]
- (iii) Utilization Factor [1 Mark]
- (iv) Annual energy consumption in kWh [2 Marks]

**(b)** Based on the above results, discuss **three engineering interventions** to better match energy use to requirement in the plant. Justify each with quantitative reasoning. [7 Marks]

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Demand Factor:**

$$DFa = \frac{\text{Max Demand of System}}{\text{Connected Load}} = \frac{280}{600} = 0.467$$

**Step 2 — Diversity Factor:**

$$DF = \frac{\sum \text{Individual Max Demands}}{\text{System Max Demand}} = \frac{420}{280} = 1.50$$

**Step 3 — Utilization Factor:**

$$UF = \frac{\text{Max Demand}}{\text{Rated Capacity}} = \frac{280}{500} = 0.56 \text{ or 56\%}$$

(Note: Compare against apparent power rating using kW = kVA × pf = 500 × 0.85 = 425 kW effective rated real power; then UF = 280/425 = 0.659)

**Step 4 — Annual Energy Consumption:**

$$E_{annual} = \text{Average Load} \times \text{Operating Hours}$$

Assume average load = 0.6 × max demand = 168 kW (typical estimate):

$$E_{annual} = 168 \text{ kW} \times 12 \text{ h/day} \times 300 \text{ days} = 604{,}800 \text{ kWh/year}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Intervention 1 — Power Factor Correction to Reduce kVA Demand:**
With pf = 0.85, the apparent power demand at 280 kW real is:

$$S = \frac{P}{pf} = \frac{280}{0.85} = 329.4 \text{ kVA}$$

By installing capacitor banks to raise pf to 0.95, apparent power reduces to:

$$S_{new} = \frac{280}{0.95} = 294.7 \text{ kVA}$$

This reduces transformer loading from 65.9% to 58.9% — direct improvement in matching.

**Intervention 2 — Load Shifting to Off-Peak Hours:**
Shifting 50 kW of non-critical load (e.g., HVAC pre-cooling, water heating) from 1 PM to 10 PM (off-peak) reduces the peak demand from 280 kW to 230 kW, lowering maximum demand charges by ~18%.

**Intervention 3 — Right-Sizing of Transformer:**
The current utilization factor of 0.66 suggests a 500 kVA transformer is slightly oversized. Replacing with a 400 kVA unit (operating at ~80% load) improves efficiency (η rises from ~97% to ~98.2%) and reduces no-load iron losses (~30% reduction).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Alternative]

**(a)** A 200 TR (Ton of Refrigeration) chiller operates at 0.7 kW/TR (current SEC) instead of the design 0.55 kW/TR. The plant runs 24 hours/day for 250 days/year. Electricity cost is ₹9/kWh.
- (i) Calculate the annual energy wastage in kWh and the annual financial loss in ₹. [3 Marks]
- (ii) Explain the **Pinch Analysis** approach to identify opportunities for matching heat supply to process demand in a chemical plant. [4 Marks]

**(b)** Discuss the role of **Variable Speed Drives (VSDs)** in matching energy use to variable demand in pumping and fan applications. Use the affinity law in your derivation. [7 Marks]

#### Model Solution — Part (a)(i)

**Step 1 — Excess SEC:**

$$\Delta SEC = 0.70 - 0.55 = 0.15 \text{ kW/TR}$$

**Step 2 — Excess power demand:**

$$\Delta P = 0.15 \times 200 = 30 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Annual energy wastage:**

$$E_{waste} = 30 \text{ kW} \times 24 \text{ h/day} \times 250 \text{ days} = 180{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 4 — Financial loss:**

$$\text{Loss} = 180{,}000 \times 9 = ₹16{,}20{,}000/\text{year}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (a)(ii) — Pinch Analysis

Pinch Analysis is a methodology for optimizing energy use in a process plant by:
1. **Extracting all hot and cold streams** from the process flow diagram.
2. **Constructing composite curves** (hot composite above, cold composite below) on a T-H (Temperature-Enthalpy) diagram.
3. **Identifying the Pinch Point** — the point of minimum temperature difference (ΔT_min) between the curves.
4. **Above the pinch:** only heat supply is required (hot utilities).
5. **Below the pinch:** only heat removal is required (cold utilities).
6. **Setting targets:** minimum hot utility, minimum cold utility, and minimum number of heat exchangers.

This ensures **thermodynamic matching** — energy is supplied at the exact temperature and quantity required by the process.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — VSD and Affinity Laws

**Affinity Law 1 (Flow proportional to speed):**

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

**Affinity Law 2 (Pressure proportional to speed squared):**

$$\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2$$

**Affinity Law 3 (Power proportional to speed cubed):**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$

**Engineering Application:** A pump delivers 100% flow at 100% speed, requiring 100% power. If the process demand drops to 70% flow:

$$P_{new} = P_{rated} \times (0.70)^3 = 0.343 \times P_{rated}$$

**Energy saving:**

$$\text{Saving} = 100\% - 34.3\% = 65.7\%$$

A throttling valve, by contrast, would have reduced flow but barely changed power consumption. The **cubic relationship** of the affinity law makes VSDs one of the most powerful energy-matching tools in pumping and fan systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Read Carefully):**
> - Students frequently confuse **Load Factor** with **Utilization Factor** — they are *different* parameters. LF = average/peak, UF = peak/rated.
> - When using affinity laws, students often forget that the power relationship is **cubic**, not linear. This is the single most-missed mark in VSD problems.
> - In exergy-based questions, students answer only the first-law (energy) part and lose 3–4 marks for missing the second-law (quality) discussion.
> - Always show **units** in your final answers. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision — Matching Energy Use to Requirement**

- ✅ **Load Factor (LF) = Average Load / Peak Load** — closer to 1.0 is better.
- ✅ **Diversity Factor (DF) = Σ Individual Max / System Max** — used for transformer sizing.
- ✅ **Utilization Factor (UF) = Max Demand / Rated Capacity** — indicates equipment usage.
- ✅ **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** is the key benchmarking metric (kWh/unit output).
- ✅ **Right-sizing** means selecting equipment whose rated capacity matches peak demand (with 10–20% margin).
- ✅ **Quality matching (exergy matching)** avoids using high-grade energy for low-grade needs.
- ✅ **Pinch Analysis** identifies the minimum energy targets for a process.
- ✅ **VSDs follow cubic affinity laws** — power ∝ (speed)³.
- ✅ **Cascade energy use** (e.g., steam cascading) is the classic industrial example of quality matching.
- ✅ Always pair your answer with a **quantitative** parameter AND a **qualitative (exergy)** argument in KTU 14-mark questions.
- ✅ Common mismatch losses: oversized boilers, throttled pumps, idle compressors, electric heating for low-grade needs.
- ✅ Frame all answers within the **ISO 50001 / Plan-Do-Check-Act** Energy Management cycle.
- ✅ Three pillars of matching: **Right-sizing (magnitude), Load shifting (time), Cascade recovery (quality)**.
- ✅ SEC reduction is the **financial justification** for any energy management project.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximizing System Efficiencies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Maximizing System Efficiencies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Efficiency Maximization** is the systematic engineering process of optimizing the ratio of useful output energy (or work) to the total input energy supplied to a system, with the objective of minimizing energy losses across conversion, transmission, distribution, and end-use stages while maintaining—or enhancing—quality, reliability, and performance.

Mathematically, the generalized system efficiency $\eta_{sys}$ for any energy conversion/utilization system is expressed as:

$$
\eta_{sys} = \frac{\text{Useful Energy Output}}{\text{Total Energy Input}} = 1 - \frac{\sum E_{losses}}{E_{input}}
$$

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **Energy Management and Conservation**, the term *"Maximizing System Efficiencies"* refers holistically to a portfolio of engineering interventions applied across major energy-consuming domains:

| Domain | Subsystems Covered |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Electrical** | Motors, Transformers, Capacitor Banks, Power Factor Correction |
| **Mechanical** | Pumps, Fans, Blowers, Compressed Air, HVAC |
| **Thermal** | Boilers, Furnaces, Steam Distribution, Insulation |
| **Lighting** | Lamps, Ballasts, Luminaires, Controls |
| **Process** | Cogeneration, Waste Heat Recovery, Pinch Analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Principle:** Energy efficiency is NOT about reducing output or compromising performance. It is fundamentally about **eliminating waste** in energy conversion, transmission, and utilization processes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaky Bucket" Model

Imagine your **monthly salary** as energy input entering a bucket. At the end of the month, you want maximum usable cash in hand. However, the bucket has several holes:

- **Tax deductions** (transmission losses)
- **Loan EMIs** (idle/standby losses)
- **Unnecessary subscriptions** (wasteful consumption)
- **Currency conversion fees** (conversion losses)
- **Theft/misplacement** (unaccounted losses)

> **Maximizing System Efficiency = Plugging the Holes.** The salary is fixed; what you maximize is the *net retained amount* by strategically sealing leaks. Similarly, in engineering systems, the energy input is often economically fixed, and what engineers optimize is the *useful output* by addressing every quantifiable loss pathway.

This analogy is universally used in **ISO 50001** Energy Management Systems and forms the philosophical backbone of KTU Module 2.

## 1.3 Why System Efficiency Maximization Matters — KTU Industrial Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India**, industrial motor-driven systems, HVAC systems, and compressed air networks together account for approximately **60–70%** of total industrial electricity consumption. Even a **5% efficiency improvement** in these systems translates to crores of rupees in annual savings for a medium-scale Kerala industry (e.g., rubber, coir, cashew, or seafood processing plants).

The standard energy unit used throughout this module is the **ton of oil equivalent (TOE)** = $41.868 \text{ GJ}$ and **kiloWatt-hour (kWh)** = **3.6 MJ** = **3600 kJ**.

---

## 1.4 Hierarchical Classification of Efficiency Types

Modern energy engineering recognizes **three distinct efficiency tiers** that KTU examiners frequently test:

1. **First-Law (Thermodynamic) Efficiency** — Based on energy quantity conservation.
2. **Second-Law (Exergetic) Efficiency** — Based on energy quality and irreversibility.
3. **Life-Cycle / Exergoeconomic Efficiency** — Combines thermodynamic, environmental, and economic performance over the asset's service life.

> [!NOTE]
> A 100% First-Law efficiency is achievable in theory (e.g., an electric resistance heater), but Second-Law efficiency is always less than 100% due to entropy generation. KTU problems often test the conceptual difference between these two definitions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 3-Tier Loss Hierarchy in Any Industrial System

Every energy system exhibits losses that can be classified into a **three-tier hierarchy**, which is essential for KTU board exam answers:

### Tier 1 — Conversion Losses ($\Delta E_{conv}$)
Losses occurring when primary energy is converted to a usable form (e.g., coal → electricity, chemical → mechanical, electrical → mechanical).

### Tier 2 — Distribution/Transmission Losses ($\Delta E_{dist}$)
Losses during transport of energy (e.g., I²R losses in cables, pressure drops in steam lines, friction in ducts).

### Tier 3 — Utilization Losses ($\Delta E_{util}$)
Losses at the point of end-use (e.g., heat escaping uninsulated boilers, motor slip, idle running of pumps).

**Composite System Efficiency Equation:**

$$
\eta_{system} = \eta_{conversion} \times \eta_{distribution} \times \eta_{utilization}
$$

## 2.2 Motor-Driven System Efficiency Maximization

Electric motors consume **~65%** of industrial electricity globally. The motor system efficiency is:

$$
\eta_{motor\,system} = \eta_{motor} \times \eta_{transmission} \times \eta_{driven\,equipment}
$$

> **Why this matters:** A 90% motor × 95% gearbox × 70% pump gives only 59.85% system efficiency — a textbook KTU "multiplicative penalty" concept.

### Key Motor Efficiency Formulas

| Formula | Expression | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Motor Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Speed regulation analysis |
| Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | Induction motor design |
| Motor Input Power | $P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L \, \cos\phi$ | 3-phase electrical input |
| Motor Shaft Power | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2\pi N_r T}{60}$ | Mechanical output |
| Power Factor | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{V_L \, I_L \cdot \sqrt{3}}$ | Reactive power management |

where $N_s$ = synchronous speed in **rpm**, $f$ = supply frequency in **Hz**, $P$ = number of poles, $T$ = torque in **N·m**, $N_r$ = rotor speed.

## 2.3 Pumping System Efficiency Maximization

Pumps are governed by the **Affinity Laws**, which are critical for KTU numerical problems:

$$
\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}
$$

$$
\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2
$$

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3
$$

where $Q$ = flow rate, $H$ = head, $P$ = power, $N$ = impeller speed.

> **Engineering Insight:** A 20% reduction in pump speed reduces power consumption by approximately **48.8%** (since $0.8^3 = 0.512$). This is the single most powerful energy-saving lever in pumping systems.

### Pump System Efficiency Equation

$$
\eta_{pump\,sys} = \eta_{pump} \times \eta_{motor} \times \eta_{valve\,and\,fitting}
$$

## 2.4 Fan and Blower Efficiency

Fans are characterized by the **specific fan power (SFP)**, an internationally accepted metric:

$$
SFP = \frac{P_{fan\,input}}{Q_{air}} \quad \left[ \frac{kW}{m^3/s} \right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) of India** prescribes a maximum SFP of **1.6 kW/(m³/s)** for general ventilation in commercial buildings. KTU questions often test compliance with ECBC benchmarks.

## 2.5 Compressed Air System Efficiency

The **specific power consumption (SPC)** of a compressor is:

$$
SPC = \frac{P_{input}}{Q_{FAD}} \quad \left[ \frac{kW}{m^3/min} \right]
$$

where $Q_{FAD}$ is the **Free Air Delivery** at standard conditions (1.013 bar, 20°C).

For reciprocating compressors, the **isothermal efficiency** is:

$$
\eta_{iso} = \frac{W_{iso}}{W_{actual}} = \frac{P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)}{W_{actual}}
$$

## 2.6 Lighting System Efficiency

Modern lighting efficiency is measured in **luminous efficacy**:

$$
\eta_{luminous} = \frac{\Phi_v}{P_{input}} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{lumens}}{\text{Watt}} \right]
$$

| Technology | Efficacy Range |
| :--- | :--- |
| Incandescent | 10–17 lm/W |
| Fluorescent (CFL) | 50–70 lm/W |
| LED | 100–200 lm/W |
| HPSV (Sodium) | 80–120 lm/W |

## 2.7 Cogeneration (CHP) — Efficiency Multiplier

Cogeneration simultaneously produces **electrical (or mechanical) energy** and **useful thermal energy** from the same fuel input:

$$
\eta_{CHP} = \frac{E_{electrical} + E_{thermal\,useful}}{E_{fuel\,input}}
$$

A typical **natural gas-based CHP plant** achieves 75–85% total efficiency versus **33–45%** for a conventional Rankine-cycle power plant.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (Compiled)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following consolidated table is **exam-critical**. Memorize the units and limits of validity for every entry.

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Variables / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | General System Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le \eta \le 1$ |
| 2 | Composite Efficiency | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_1 \cdot \eta_2 \cdot \eta_3$ | Multiplicative penalty rule |
| 3 | Motor Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120\,f}{P}$ | $f$ in Hz, $N_s$ in rpm |
| 4 | Motor Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Ratio, typically 2–6% |
| 5 | 3-Phase Power | $P = \sqrt{3} \, V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | All electrical in SI |
| 6 | Shaft Power | $P_{sh} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | $T$ in N·m, $N$ in rpm |
| 7 | Affinity Law (Flow) | $\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | Linear in speed |
| 8 | Affinity Law (Head) | $\dfrac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2$ | Quadratic in speed |
| 9 | Affinity Law (Power) | $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$ | Cubic in speed |
| 10 | Specific Fan Power | $SFP = \dfrac{P_{fan}}{Q_{air}}$ | kW/(m³/s), ECBC limit 1.6 |
| 11 | Compressor SPC | $SPC = \dfrac{P_{in}}{Q_{FAD}}$ | kW/(m³/min) |
| 12 | Luminous Efficacy | $\eta_L = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{P_{in}}$ | lm/W |
| 13 | Cogeneration Efficiency | $\eta_{CHP} = \dfrac{E_{el} + E_{th}}{E_{fuel}}$ | 75–85% for gas CHP |
| 14 | Pump Wire-to-Water Efficiency | $\eta_{w2w} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{P_{electrical\,in}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 15 | Heat Loss Through Insulation | $Q_{loss} = \dfrac{k A (T_i - T_o)}{t}$ | W, Fourier's law |

**Real-World Engineering Application:** Every formula above is routinely applied in **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme** audits conducted by BEE for Designated Consumers in India, including Kerala-based industries such as Travancore Cochin Chemicals, FACT, HOCL, and BSES Kerala.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Composite System Efficiency (Multiplicative Rule)

We derive why system efficiencies multiply rather than add.

**Step 1 — Definition of individual stage efficiency**

For Stage $i$ with input energy $E_{i-1}$ and output energy $E_i$:

$$
\eta_i = \frac{E_i}{E_{i-1}}
$$

**Step 2 — Recursive substitution**

For three cascaded stages:

$$
\eta_1 = \frac{E_1}{E_0}, \quad \eta_2 = \frac{E_2}{E_1}, \quad \eta_3 = \frac{E_3}{E_2}
$$

**Step 3 — Forming the ratio**

The system efficiency is the ratio of final useful output $E_3$ to original input $E_0$:

$$
\eta_{sys} = \frac{E_3}{E_0} = \frac{E_3}{E_2} \cdot \frac{E_2}{E_1} \cdot \frac{E_1}{E_0}
$$

**Step 4 — Final expression**

$$
\eta_{sys} = \eta_1 \cdot \eta_2 \cdot \eta_3
$$

**Step 5 — Converting to loss form (additive in log domain)**

Since $1 - \eta$ is a small loss fraction:

$$
(1 - \eta_{sys}) \approx 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - L_i) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i
$$

where $L_i = 1 - \eta_i$ is the fractional loss of stage $i$ (for small $L_i$).

> **Engineering Significance:** This is the **multiplicative penalty** — even a 5% loss at *each* of five stages reduces overall efficiency to $0.95^5 = 0.7738$, i.e., a **22.6% relative loss**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Affinity Laws for Centrifugal Machines

Starting from the **Euler Turbomachine Equation** for the energy transfer per unit mass:

$$
W_{Euler} = U_2 V_{u2} - U_1 V_{u1}
$$

**Step 1 — Geometric similarity condition**

For two geometrically similar impellers running at speeds $N_1$ and $N_2$:

$$
\frac{D_2}{D_1} = 1 \quad \text{(same physical size)}
$$

**Step 2 — Velocity triangle relations**

The peripheral (blade) speed $U$ is proportional to $N \cdot D$:

$$
U = \pi D N / 60 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{U_2}{U_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}
$$

**Step 3 — Flow rate scaling**

Flow is proportional to blade tip speed times the cross-sectional flow area:

$$
Q \propto U \cdot A \propto N \cdot D^3
$$

For same diameter:

$$
\boxed{\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}}
$$

**Step 4 — Head (pressure) scaling**

Head is proportional to $(velocity)^2$:

$$
H \propto U^2 \propto N^2
$$

For same diameter:

$$
\boxed{\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Power scaling**

Power = $\rho g Q H$:

$$
P \propto Q \cdot H \propto N \cdot N^2 = N^3
$$

For same diameter:

$$
\boxed{\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The cubic power law is the **theoretical foundation** of Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) energy savings in pumps and fans.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Affinity Law Application

**Problem (KTU Style):** A centrifugal pump delivers $Q_1 = 100 \text{ L/s}$ at $H_1 = 30 \text{ m}$ head, drawing $P_1 = 45 \text{ kW}$ at full speed. If a VFD reduces impeller speed to 80% of original, compute the new flow, head, and power. Also estimate annual energy savings if pump operates 6000 hours/year at unit cost ₹8/kWh.

**Step 1 — Compute new flow rate**

$$
Q_2 = Q_1 \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 100 \times 0.80 = 80 \text{ L/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute new head**

$$
H_2 = H_1 \cdot \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = 30 \times (0.80)^2 = 30 \times 0.64 = 19.20 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute new power input**

$$
P_2 = P_1 \cdot \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = 45 \times (0.80)^3 = 45 \times 0.512 = 23.04 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Power reduction**

$$
\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = 45.00 - 23.04 = 21.96 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual energy saved**

$$
E_{saved} = \Delta P \times t = 21.96 \times 6000 = 131{,}760 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**Step 6 — Monetary savings**

$$
\text{Rs. saved} = 131{,}760 \times 8 = \text{₹ } 10{,}54{,}080 \text{ per year}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A 20% speed reduction saves **~₹10.5 lakhs/year** — a classic KTU board problem illustrating VFD economics.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Composite System Efficiency Penalty

**Problem:** An industrial fan system has the following stages:
- Motor efficiency: $\eta_M = 90\%$
- Belt drive efficiency: $\eta_B = 95\%$
- Fan impeller efficiency: $\eta_F = 75\%$
- Ductwork transmission loss: 12% of fan output

**Step 1 — Identify ductwork as a fourth stage**

$$
\eta_D = 1 - 0.12 = 0.88
$$

**Step 2 — Compute composite efficiency (multiplicative)**

$$
\eta_{sys} = 0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.75 \times 0.88
$$

$$
\eta_{sys} = 0.5643 \text{ or } 56.43\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total losses**

$$
L_{total} = 1 - 0.5643 = 0.4357 = 43.57\%
$$

**Step 4 — Approximate additive loss estimate**

$$
L_{approx} = 0.10 + 0.05 + 0.25 + 0.12 = 0.52 = 52\%
$$

**Step 5 — Note the discrepancy**

The exact multiplicative result (43.57%) is **lower** than the naive additive estimate (52%) because the multiplicative rule correctly accounts for **loss cascading**.

> **Engineering Insight:** This explains why upgrading a single component to premium efficiency (e.g., IE4 motor) yields lower system improvement than expected — the *bottleneck* stages dominate the overall performance.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Energy Saving Opportunities by Subsystem

| Subsystem | Typical Inefficiency Source | Conservation Measure | Achievable % Saving |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Induction Motors | Low power factor, slip losses | VFD + Star-Delta + PF correction | 20–35% |
| Centrifugal Pumps | Throttling losses, oversizing | VFD + Trim impeller + Multiple pumps | 30–50% |
| Compressed Air | Leakages (15–30% typical), pressure drop | Leak audit + VFD compressor + receiver | 25–40% |
| HVAC Systems | Constant-volume air, low ΔT | Variable Air Volume (VAV) + ECBC retrofit | 20–30% |
| Lighting | Obsolete lamps, poor controls | LED + Daylight sensors + Occupancy sensors | 50–70% |
| Boilers | Stack gas losses, radiation | Economizer + Air preheater + Insulation | 10–20% |
| Transformers | Core + Copper losses | Amorphous metal cores + optimal loading | 5–10% |
| Steam Traps | Leakage, blow-through | Trap audit + replacement | 8–12% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Efficiency Maximization — Master Framework

The following block diagram illustrates the **holistic methodology** for maximizing system efficiencies as taught in KTU Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ENERGY AUDIT] --> B[SYSTEM BOUNDARY DEFINITION]
    B --> C[IDENTIFY MAJOR LOSS STREAMS]
    C --> D{LOSS HIERARCHY}
    D --> E1[CONVERSION LOSSES]
    D --> E2[DISTRIBUTION LOSSES]
    D --> E3[UTILIZATION LOSSES]
    E1 --> F[QUANTIFY EACH LOSS]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[BENCHMARK VS BEST PRACTICE]
    G --> H{TECHNICALLY VIABLE IMPROVEMENT}
    H -->|YES| I[COST BENEFIT ANALYSIS]
    H -->|NO| J[DOCUMENT AND PROCEED]
    I --> K{PAYBACK LESS THAN 3 YEARS}
    K -->|YES| L[IMPLEMENT PROJECT]
    K -->|NO| M[DEFER OR REJECT]
    L --> N[MEASURE AND VERIFY M AND V]
    N --> O[CONTINUOUS MONITORING]
    O --> A
```

## 4.2 Motor-Driven System Efficiency Map

A detailed view of how efficiency propagates through a typical motor-pump or motor-fan system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GRID[3 PHASE AC SUPPLY] --> MTR[INDUCTION MOTOR]
    MTR -->|eta 90 to 95 percent| GEAR[GEARBOX OR BELT DRIVE]
    GEAR -->|eta 95 to 98 percent| COUP[COUPLING]
    COUP -->|eta 99 percent| PUMP[PUMP OR FAN IMPELLER]
    PUMP -->|eta 70 to 85 percent| FLUID[FLUID DELIVERY]
    FLUID --> PIPE[PIPING NETWORK]
    PIPE -->|eta 80 to 95 percent| OUT[USEFUL DELIVERY POINT]
    MTR -.MONITOR.-> VFD[VFD CONTROLLER]
    VFD -. FEEDBACK .-> MTR
```

## 4.3 Energy Saving Decision Tree for Pumping Systems

This flowchart aids in selecting the correct intervention strategy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PUMP SYSTEM EVALUATION] --> B{IS PUMP OVERSIZED}
    B -->|YES| C[TRIM IMPELLER OR REPLACE]
    B -->|NO| D{IS THROTTLING USED}
    D -->|YES| E[INSTALL VFD]
    D -->|NO| F{LOAD VARIES MORE THAN 30 PERCENT}
    F -->|YES| E
    F -->|NO| G[CHECK FOR LEAKAGES AND ALIGNMENT]
    G --> H{LEAKS FOUND}
    H -->|YES| I[REPAIR AND RECOMMISSION]
    H -->|NO| J[PERFORM ROUTINE MAINTENANCE]
    C --> K[PERFORMANCE VERIFICATION]
    E --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[RECORD SAVINGS IN ENERGY DATABASE]
```

## 4.4 Loss Reduction Hierarchy — Block Architecture

A logical block diagram showing how each tier of losses can be addressed.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[CONVERSION LOSSES] --> L1A[USE HIGH EFFICIENCY MOTOR IE3 OR IE4]
    L1 --> L1B[ADOPT POWER FACTOR CORRECTION CAPACITORS]
    L1 --> L1C[SELECT OPTIMAL TRANSFORMER LOADING]
    L2[DISTRIBUTION LOSSES] --> L2A[UPSIZE CABLES TO REDUCE I SQUARED R]
    L2 --> L2B[INSULATE STEAM AND HOT WATER LINES]
    L2 --> L2C[BALANCE ELECTRICAL LOADS ACROSS PHASES]
    L3[UTILIZATION LOSSES] --> L3A[INSTALL VFD FOR VARIABLE LOAD]
    L3 --> L3B[RECOVER WASTE HEAT VIA HRSG]
    L3 --> L3C[ADOPT LED LIGHTING AND SMART CONTROLS]
    L1A --> OUT[OPTIMIZED SYSTEM]
    L1B --> OUT
    L1C --> OUT
    L2A --> OUT
    L2B --> OUT
    L2C --> OUT
    L3A --> OUT
    L3B --> OUT
    L3C --> OUT
```

## 4.5 Efficiency Improvement Project Lifecycle (ISO 50001 Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PLAN ENERGY REVIEW] --> P2[DO BASELINE STUDY]
    P2 --> P3[CHECK ENERGY PERFORMANCE INDICATORS]
    P3 --> P4[ACT CORRECTIVE ACTIONS]
    P4 --> P1
    P1 -.ENPI.-> P2
    P2 -.KPIs.-> P3
    P3 -.M AND V.-> P4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define "First Law Efficiency" and "Second Law Efficiency" of a system. Why is the Second Law efficiency never 100%? [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> **First Law Efficiency** (also called *energy efficiency* or *thermodynamic efficiency* in the engineering sense) is defined as the ratio of useful energy output to the total energy input, based on the principle of energy conservation. It does not distinguish between energy forms of different qualities.
>
> $$
> \eta_{I} = \frac{\text{Useful Energy Output}}{\text{Total Energy Input}}
> $$

> **Second Law Efficiency** (also called *exergy efficiency* or *rational efficiency*) is defined as the ratio of the minimum (reversible) work required to perform a task to the actual work expended. It is always $\le 1$ because of entropy generation (irreversibilities) in all real processes.
>
> $$
> \eta_{II} = \frac{W_{rev,min}}{W_{actual}}
> $$

> The Second Law efficiency can never equal 100% in any real process because every spontaneous process generates entropy and dissipates exergy. Only an idealized reversible process (which is impossible in finite time) can approach 100%.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q2. State and explain the Affinity Laws for a centrifugal pump. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The Affinity Laws describe how the flow $Q$, head $H$, and power $P$ of a centrifugal pump (or fan) vary with impeller speed $N$ and impeller diameter $D$ when the pump is geometrically similar to itself at another operating point.

For a fixed impeller diameter (only speed change):

$$
\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}
$$

$$
\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2
$$

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3
$$

> The cubic power law makes speed reduction (via VFD) the **most powerful energy conservation technique** in centrifugal machinery. A 30% speed reduction reduces power consumption to $0.7^3 = 34.3\%$ of original, a saving of nearly **66%**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 2, Full Question]**

### Question A — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the concept of **composite system efficiency** with a suitable industrial example. Show mathematically why individual stage efficiencies multiply. [7 Marks]

**(b)** An industrial compressed air system has the following stages:

| Stage | Efficiency |
| :--- | :--- |
| Electric motor | 92% |
| Belt drive | 96% |
| Compressor (screw) | 78% |
| After-cooler + dryer | 98% |
| Distribution piping | 91% |

> Calculate: (i) the composite system efficiency, (ii) the percentage energy loss, and (iii) the annual energy loss in kWh if the system delivers 50 kW of useful compressed air and operates 7000 hours/year. [7 Marks]

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Discuss the various **energy conservation opportunities** in (i) electric motors, (ii) centrifugal pumps, and (iii) HVAC systems. Include the role of VFDs in each. [7 Marks]

**(b)** A 30 kW induction motor operates at 85% efficiency, with a power factor of 0.78 lagging. Calculate: (i) the input current at 415 V, 3-phase, (ii) the reactive power drawn, (iii) the rating of the capacitor bank required to improve the power factor to 0.95 lagging, and (iv) the line current after power factor correction. [7 Marks]

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution A

#### (a) Composite System Efficiency — Explanation [7 Marks]

**Definition (1 mark):** Composite (or overall) system efficiency is the product of the individual stage efficiencies of an energy conversion-utilization chain.

**Industrial Example (2 marks):** A motor-pump system in a water pumping station:
- Motor: 90% efficient
- Coupling: 99% efficient
- Pump: 75% efficient
- Piping: 88% efficient

**Mathematical Proof (3 marks):**

Let $E_0$ be the input energy to stage 1, and $E_1, E_2, E_3$ be the outputs of stages 1, 2, 3 respectively, with $E_3$ being the final useful output.

By definition of stage efficiency:

$$
\eta_1 = \frac{E_1}{E_0}, \quad \eta_2 = \frac{E_2}{E_1}, \quad \eta_3 = \frac{E_3}{E_2}
$$

The composite system efficiency is:

$$
\eta_{sys} = \frac{E_3}{E_0} = \frac{E_1}{E_0} \cdot \frac{E_2}{E_1} \cdot \frac{E_3}{E_2} = \eta_1 \cdot \eta_2 \cdot \eta_3
$$

**Industrial Significance (1 mark):** This "multiplicative penalty" means that even small inefficiencies in each stage compound to produce large system-level losses, justifying holistic audits rather than component-level optimization alone.

#### (b) Compressed Air System Calculation [7 Marks]

**Given data:**

- $\eta_M = 0.92$, $\eta_B = 0.96$, $\eta_C = 0.78$, $\eta_{AD} = 0.98$, $\eta_D = 0.91$
- Useful output: $P_{out} = 50$ kW
- Operating hours: $t = 7000$ h/year

**Step 1 — Composite Efficiency (2 marks)**

$$
\eta_{sys} = 0.92 \times 0.96 \times 0.78 \times 0.98 \times 0.91
$$

$$
\eta_{sys} = 0.6144 \quad \text{or} \quad 61.44\%
$$

**Step 2 — Percentage Energy Loss (1 mark)**

$$
L_{sys} = 1 - 0.6144 = 0.3856 = 38.56\%
$$

**Step 3 — Input Power (1 mark)**

$$
P_{in} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta_{sys}} = \frac{50}{0.6144} = 81.38 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Power Loss (1 mark)**

$$
P_{loss} = P_{in} - P_{out} = 81.38 - 50.00 = 31.38 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual Energy Loss (2 marks)**

$$
E_{loss} = P_{loss} \times t = 31.38 \times 7000 = 219{,}660 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A 61% efficient system wastes **~220 MWh/year** — equivalent to powering 22 average Kerala households continuously.

---

### Solution B

#### (a) Conservation Opportunities [7 Marks]

**(i) Electric Motors (2.5 marks):**
- Use **IE3/IE4 premium-efficiency motors** (5–8% efficiency improvement).
- Install **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** for variable torque loads.
- Maintain proper **power factor** using capacitor banks.
- Avoid rewinding burnt motors (loses ~2% efficiency).
- Implement **soft starters** to reduce inrush and mechanical stress.

**(ii) Centrifugal Pumps (2.5 marks):**
- Avoid **throttling** as a flow control method (use VFD instead).
- Right-size pumps; install **multiple smaller pumps** in parallel for variable demand.
- Trim impellers to match design duty.
- Eliminate **dry running** and maintain mechanical seals.
- Use **affinity law** speed reduction for partial load operation.

**(iii) HVAC Systems (2 marks):**
- Adopt **Variable Air Volume (VAV)** systems.
- Use **EC (Electronically Commutated) fans** in AHUs.
- Maintain **ΔT** (temperature difference) close to design value.
- Implement **chilled water temperature reset** based on outdoor conditions.
- Apply **ECBC-compliant** insulation and shading.

---

#### (b) Motor with Power Factor Correction [7 Marks]

**Given data:**

- $P_{out} = 30$ kW, $\eta = 0.85$, $\cos\phi_1 = 0.78$ lag, $V_L = 415$ V, 3-phase
- Target: $\cos\phi_2 = 0.95$ lag

**Step 1 — Input Active Power (0.5 mark)**

$$
P_{in} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \frac{30}{0.85} = 35.29 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Input Line Current at 0.78 PF (1 mark)**

$$
I_1 = \frac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \cos\phi_1} = \frac{35{,}290}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.78}
$$

$$
I_1 = \frac{35{,}290}{561.45} = 62.86 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3 — Initial Reactive Power (1 mark)**

$$
\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.78) = 38.74^\circ
$$

$$
Q_1 = P_{in} \tan\phi_1 = 35.29 \times \tan(38.74^\circ) = 35.29 \times 0.8023 = 28.31 \text{ kVAr}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Reactive Power at 0.95 PF (1 mark)**

$$
\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19^\circ
$$

$$
Q_2 = P_{in} \tan\phi_2 = 35.29 \times \tan(18.19^\circ) = 35.29 \times 0.3287 = 11.60 \text{ kVAr}
$$

**Step 5 — Required Capacitor Bank Rating (1 mark)**

$$
Q_C = Q_1 - Q_2 = 28.31 - 11.60 = 16.71 \text{ kVAr}
$$

> Standard capacitor bank rating = **17 kVAr** (or 18 kVAr if available).

**Step 6 — Line Current After Correction (1.5 marks)**

$$
I_2 = \frac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \cos\phi_2} = \frac{35{,}290}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.95}
$$

$$
I_2 = \frac{35{,}290}{683.85} = 51.61 \text{ A}
$$

> **Current reduction:** $62.86 - 51.61 = 11.25$ A (a **17.9%** reduction in line current, reducing I²R cable losses by ~32%).

**Step 7 — Energy and Economic Impact (1 mark)**

Reduction in line losses for a cable of resistance $R = 0.1 \ \Omega$:

$$
\Delta P_{loss} = 3 \cdot (I_1^2 - I_2^2) \cdot R = 3 \times (3951.4 - 2663.6) \times 0.1 = 386.34 \text{ W}
$$

For 7000 h/year: $386.34 \times 7000 = 2.70$ MWh/year saved.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams (Module 2)**
>
> 1. **Confusing shaft power with electrical input** — Always divide output power by motor efficiency to obtain input power.
> 2. **Using 230 V in 3-phase formulas** — Kerala industrial supply is **415 V, 3-phase**, not 230 V (which is single-phase domestic).
> 3. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ factor** — 3-phase power and reactive power formulas require $\sqrt{3}$. Marks are deducted if omitted.
> 4. **Applying Affinity Laws to positive displacement pumps** — These laws are valid **ONLY for centrifugal machines** (pumps, fans, blowers). Not for reciprocating pumps or piston compressors.
> 5. **Adding losses linearly** — The composite loss is $1 - \prod(1-L_i)$, **not** $\sum L_i$. Students lose 1–2 marks for the additive mistake.
> 6. **Ignoring units** — kW vs W, kVAr vs VAr, kWh vs MWh — dimensional consistency is graded strictly.
> 7. **Skipping the "implications" step** — KTU awards 1–2 marks for industrial interpretation of numerical results (e.g., "the loss equals the annual consumption of X households").

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Maximizing System Efficiencies (Module 2)**

- ✅ **Three-tier loss hierarchy:** Conversion → Distribution → Utilization.
- ✅ **Composite efficiency = product of individual stage efficiencies** (multiplicative rule).
- ✅ **Affinity Laws:** $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$ (centrifugal machines only).
- ✅ **Motor speed formula:** $N_s = 120f/P$; typical slip 2–6%.
- ✅ **3-phase power:** $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$; **V_L = 415 V** in Kerala industries.
- ✅ **Power factor correction** reduces line current and I²R losses quadratically.
- ✅ **VFD savings** follow the **cubic law** — most powerful conservation technique for variable torque loads.
- ✅ **Compressed air** has typical system efficiency 10–30% (very low!) — biggest opportunity.
- ✅ **Lighting efficacy** in lm/W: LED (100–200) > CFL (50–70) > Incandescent (10–17).
- ✅ **ECBC limits:** Specific Fan Power $\le 1.6$ kW/(m³/s) for general ventilation.
- ✅ **Cogeneration efficiency** = 75–85% (gas-based) vs 33–45% (standalone power plant).
- ✅ **Payback criterion:** Projects with payback $\le 3$ years are typically prioritized in industrial energy management.
- ✅ **Exergy/Second Law efficiency** $\le 1$ always; never 100% in real processes.
- ✅ **BEE PAT Scheme** governs Indian industrial energy efficiency compliance.
- ✅ **Pump and fan energy savings** can be **30–50%** through VFD + right-sizing + maintenance.
- ✅ **Specific Power Consumption (SPC)** is the key KPI for compressed air audits.
- ✅ **Motor IE classes:** IE1 (standard) → IE2 (high) → IE3 (premium) → IE4 (super-premium).
- ✅ **Soft starters vs VFDs:** Soft starters are for starting only; VFDs provide continuous speed control.
- ✅ **Energy audit types:** Walk-through (preliminary) → Detailed (investment-grade).
- ✅ **Kerala-specific industrial relevance:** Rubber, coir, cashew, seafood, spices processing — all benefit from motor/pump/fan efficiency upgrades.

---

**End of Notes — Maximizing System Efficiencies | PESFT741 Module 2 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
