topics.title: Fuel and Energy Substitution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuel and Energy Substitution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FUEL AND ENERGY SUBSTITUTION** is the strategic engineering and managerial process of replacing a conventional, less efficient, or higher-emission energy source (fuel) with an alternative fuel or energy carrier that delivers the same end-use service (heat, light, power, motion) at a lower total lifecycle cost, reduced environmental impact, and improved energy security — without compromising process output, product quality, or operational safety.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme framing, substitution is treated as a **demand-side management (DSM) lever** under the broader umbrella of Energy Conservation, distinct from mere fuel switching because it must satisfy the equivalent thermodynamic functional requirement (e.g., **1 kWh of useful heat, light, or shaft work**).

### 1.2 Sub-Categories of Substitution

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus classifies substitution into three engineering-recognised sub-classes:
>
> 1. **Fuel-to-Fuel Substitution (F → F):** Replacing one combustible fuel with another (e.g., Furnace Oil → Natural Gas, Coal → Biomass briquettes).
> 2. **Fuel-to-Energy Substitution (F → E):** Replacing direct fuel combustion with a converted energy vector (e.g., Diesel generator → Grid power + renewables, Steam heating → Solar thermal, Furnace oil burner → Electric induction heater).
> 3. **Energy-to-Energy Substitution (E → E):** Replacing one utility form with another (e.g., Compressed air → Electric actuators, Steam process heating → Hot water/LTHF, Hydraulic systems → Electric servos).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!TIP]
> **Think of substitution like changing your kitchen cooking medium while still wanting the same "dal-tadka" taste.**
>
> Earlier, your grandmother cooked on a coal *chulha*. It gave heat, but produced smoke, needed constant feeding, and was inefficient (~10-15% thermal efficiency). You can now substitute it with:
> - An **LPG stove** (Fuel-to-Fuel substitution) — same flame, cleaner, slightly more efficient.
> - An **electric induction cooktop** (Fuel-to-Energy substitution) — same cooking output, ~85% efficient, no combustion.
> - A **solar cooker** (Energy-to-Energy substitution using renewables) — same end-use (cooked food), zero recurring fuel cost.
>
> The dish (end-use service) remains identical; only the *route* of energy delivery changes. **Fuel and Energy Substitution is exactly this philosophy, applied at industrial scale.**

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Physical Benchmarks for Substitution Analysis (NCV basis unless stated):**
> - 1 **TOE (Tonne of Oil Equivalent) = 41.868 GJ = 11,630 kWh** *(NCV basis, IPCC default)*
> - GCV of Furnace Oil ≈ **10,200 kcal/kg** (≈ 42.7 MJ/kg)
> - GCV of Natural Gas ≈ **8,600 kcal/Nm³** (≈ 36 MJ/Nm³)
> - GCV of Coal (Indian bituminous) ≈ **4,000–6,500 kcal/kg**
> - Grid Electricity emission factor (India CEA 2023) ≈ **0.71 kg CO₂/kWh**
> - CO₂ from FO combustion ≈ **3.2 t CO₂/TOE**; from Natural Gas ≈ **2.3 t CO₂/TOE**; from Grid ≈ **2.6 t CO₂/TOE**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Engineering Logic Behind Substitution (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

Substitution decisions are not arbitrary — they follow a **5-stage engineering decision hierarchy**:

### Step 1 — Quantify the Baseline Energy Service
Identify the *function* the current fuel/energy is performing:
- Raise temperature? (e.g., melting, drying, curing)
- Generate motion? (e.g., pump, compressor, vehicle)
- Produce illumination? (e.g., lighting)
- Provide process chemistry? (e.g., reducing atmosphere in a kiln)

> **Why?** Substitution is only valid if the alternative can deliver the *same thermodynamic function*. You cannot replace furnace oil in a glass melting furnace with grid electricity without reaching temperatures of ~1500 °C, which demands electric arc — a fundamentally different capital solution.

### Step 2 — Establish Energy Equivalence
Convert both fuels to a **common energy currency** (typically TOE, GJ, or kWh thermal) using their respective **Net Calorific Values (NCV)**.

### Step 3 — Compare Costs on Equivalised Basis
Compute cost per unit of delivered energy:
$$ \text{Cost per GJ} = \frac{\text{Fuel Price (₹/kg or ₹/Nm³)}}{\text{NCV (GJ/unit)}} \times \frac{1}{\eta_{\text{utilisation}}} $$

where $\eta_{\text{utilisation}}$ accounts for end-use efficiency losses (boiler efficiency, furnace efficiency, motor efficiency, etc.).

### Step 4 — Apply Environmental Cost (Internalisation of Externalities)
Add a **shadow carbon price** (₹/t CO₂) or use **statutory emission factors** to monetise the environmental delta. India uses the **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) mechanism** under BEE, and increasingly, a **Social Cost of Carbon ≈ USD 50–85/t CO₂** for project appraisal.

### Step 5 — Financial Viability Test
Compute **NPV, IRR, Simple Payback** to confirm economic merit over project life.

## 2.2 Criteria for Selection of Substitute (KTU Examiner Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 7-Point Selection Filter (commonly asked in KTU ESE):**
>
> 1. **Thermodynamic Compatibility** — Can the alternative deliver the required temperature/pressure/power?
> 2. **Capital Investment** — Initial capex vs. lifecycle savings.
> 3. **Operating Cost Differential** — Recurring fuel/energy bill reduction.
> 4. **Environmental Compliance** — CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ, PM reductions.
> 5. **Availability & Security of Supply** — Pipeline gas vs. LNG vs. indigenous coal.
> 6. **Process Disruption Risk** — Retrofit feasibility, downtime, training.
> 7. **Regulatory & Policy Support** — Subsidies, tax holidays, renewable obligations (RPO).

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

| Field | Classic Substitution Example | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **Cement Industry** | Pet coke → AFR (Alternate Fuels & Raw materials) | 30–50% thermal substitution, 1.2 Mt CO₂ saved/year per plant |
| **Steel Reheating** | Furnace oil → Natural gas / induction | ↑ 20% efficiency, ↓ 40% emissions |
| **Textile (process steam)** | Coal boiler → Bagasse/biomass | Negative carbon, JI/CDM eligible |
| **Commercial HVAC** | Diesel AC → VRF + solar PV | 40% OPEX reduction |
| **Transport** | Petrol/Diesel → CNG → Electric | 25–60% well-to-wheel CO₂ reduction |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the *only* reference a KTU candidate needs for solving substitution numerical problems.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{\text{equiv}} = m_f \times \text{NCV}_f$ | Equivalent thermal energy of fuel | GJ or kWh |
| 2 | $m_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{old}} \times \text{NCV}_{\text{old}}}{\text{NCV}_{\text{new}} \times \eta_{\text{ratio}}}$ | Mass/volume of substitute required | kg, Nm³, L |
| 3 | $\Delta \text{CO}_2 = \sum (Q_f \times \text{EF}_f) - \sum (Q_{\text{sub}} \times \text{EF}_{\text{sub}})$ | Annual CO₂ emission reduction | t CO₂/year |
| 4 | $\text{NPV} = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{(B_t - O_t)}{(1+r)^t}$ | Net Present Value of substitution | ₹, ₹/Lakh |
| 5 | $\text{SPP} = \dfrac{C_0}{\text{Annual Net Savings}}$ | Simple Payback Period | years |
| 6 | $\text{IRR}: \quad 0 = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+\text{IRR})^t}$ | Internal Rate of Return | % |
| 7 | $C_{\text{GJ}} = \dfrac{P_f}{\text{NCV}_f \times \eta_u}$ | Unit cost of useful energy | ₹/GJ |
| 8 | $\text{TOE} = \dfrac{E}{41.868}$ | Tonne Oil Equivalent | TOE |
| 9 | $\Delta \text{Cost} = (C_{\text{GJ,new}} - C_{\text{GJ,old}}) \times E_{\text{annual}}$ | Annual savings | ₹/year |
| 10 | $\text{SCC-adjusted saving} = \Delta \text{Cost} + (\Delta \text{CO}_2 \times P_{\text{carbon}})$ | Savings with carbon credit | ₹/year |

> **Where** $C_0$ = Initial Capital Investment, $B_t$ = Annual Benefit, $O_t$ = Annual O&M, $r$ = Discount rate, $n$ = Project life, $\text{EF}_f$ = Emission factor (kg CO₂/GJ), $P_{\text{carbon}}$ = Carbon price (₹/t CO₂).

## 2.5 The Substitution Decision Flow (Engineering Logic)

A substitute is *engineered into* a system only when **all three gates pass**:

- **Gate A — Technical:** Equivalent or better process output.
- **Gate B — Economic:** NPV > 0 at hurdle rate, SPP < target.
- **Gate C — Environmental:** Net CO₂ ↓, no new pollutants introduced.

> Failure at any gate → substitution is **rejected or deferred** even if individually attractive.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass of Substitute Fuel Required

**Problem Statement:** A boiler consumes **500 kg/h of Furnace Oil (NCV = 10,200 kcal/kg, η = 82%)**. The plant wants to substitute with **Natural Gas (NCV = 8,600 kcal/Nm³, η = 88%)**. Calculate the **hourly Natural Gas requirement in Nm³/h**.

**Step 1 — Compute the useful heat delivered by furnace oil per hour.**
$$ Q_{\text{useful,FO}} = m_{\text{FO}} \times \text{NCV}_{\text{FO}} \times \eta_{\text{FO}} $$

$$ Q_{\text{useful,FO}} = 500 \;\text{kg/h} \times 10{,}200 \;\text{kcal/kg} \times 0.82 $$

$$ Q_{\text{useful,FO}} = 4{,}182{,}000 \;\text{kcal/h} $$

**Step 2 — Equate useful heat to that which must be delivered by natural gas.**

The process demands the same useful heat:
$$ Q_{\text{useful,NG}} = Q_{\text{useful,FO}} $$

$$ V_{\text{NG}} \times \text{NCV}_{\text{NG}} \times \eta_{\text{NG}} = 4{,}182{,}000 \;\text{kcal/h} $$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_{\text{NG}}$.**
$$ V_{\text{NG}} = \frac{4{,}182{,}000}{8{,}600 \times 0.88} $$

$$ V_{\text{NG}} = \frac{4{,}182{,}000}{7{,}568} $$

$$ V_{\text{NG}} \approx 552.6 \;\text{Nm³/h} $$

**Engineering Interpretation:** To produce the same useful heat, the plant needs about **552.6 Nm³/h of natural gas** — about **1.1 Nm³ NG replaces 1 kg of FO** under these efficiencies.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Annual CO₂ Emission Reduction

**Problem Statement:** Continuing the above, the plant operates **6,000 hours/year**. Given emission factors: **EF(FO) = 3.2 kg CO₂/kg**, **EF(NG) = 2.0 kg CO₂/Nm³**. Compute annual CO₂ savings.

**Step 1 — Annual CO₂ from current FO consumption.**
$$ \text{CO}_{2,\text{FO}} = m_{\text{FO,annual}} \times \text{EF}_{\text{FO}} $$

$$ \text{CO}_{2,\text{FO}} = (500 \times 6{,}000) \times 3.2 $$

$$ \text{CO}_{2,\text{FO}} = 3{,}000{,}000 \times 3.2 = 9{,}600{,}000 \;\text{kg/year} $$

$$ \text{CO}_{2,\text{FO}} = 9{,}600 \;\text{t CO}_2/\text{year} $$

**Step 2 — Annual CO₂ from substitute natural gas.**
$$ \text{CO}_{2,\text{NG}} = V_{\text{NG,annual}} \times \text{EF}_{\text{NG}} $$

$$ \text{CO}_{2,\text{NG}} = (552.6 \times 6{,}000) \times 2.0 $$

$$ \text{CO}_{2,\text{NG}} = 3{,}315{,}600 \times 2.0 = 6{,}631{,}200 \;\text{kg/year} $$

$$ \text{CO}_{2,\text{NG}} = 6{,}631.2 \;\text{t CO}_2/\text{year} $$

**Step 3 — Annual reduction.**
$$ \Delta \text{CO}_2 = \text{CO}_{2,\text{FO}} - \text{CO}_{2,\text{NG}} $$

$$ \Delta \text{CO}_2 = 9{,}600 - 6{,}631.2 $$

$$ \Delta \text{CO}_2 = 2{,}968.8 \;\text{t CO}_2/\text{year} $$

**Result:** Substituting FO with NG saves approximately **2,968.8 tonnes of CO₂ per year**, equivalent to removing ~640 cars from the road.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — NPV of the Substitution Project

**Given:** Capex for gas conversion = **₹ 80 Lakh**. Annual fuel savings (FO cost reduction) = **₹ 22 Lakh/year**. Additional O&M for NG system = **₹ 2 Lakh/year**. Carbon credit revenue at ₹800/t CO₂. Project life = **10 years**. Discount rate = **12%**.

**Step 1 — Net annual cash flow (before carbon revenue).**
$$ CF_t = \text{Savings} - \text{O\&M} $$

$$ CF_t = 22 - 2 = ₹ 20 \;\text{Lakh/year} $$

**Step 2 — Add carbon credit revenue.**
$$ CF_t^{\text{adj}} = 20 + (2{,}968.8 \times 800 / 100{,}000) $$

$$ CF_t^{\text{adj}} = 20 + 23.75 = ₹ 43.75 \;\text{Lakh/year} $$

> Note: ₹800/t CO₂ × 2,968.8 t = ₹ 23,75,040 ≈ ₹ 23.75 Lakh/year.

**Step 3 — Compute NPV using annuity factor.**
$$ \text{PVIFA}(12\%, 10) = \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-10}}{0.12} $$

$$ \text{PVIFA}(12\%, 10) = \frac{1 - 0.3220}{0.12} = \frac{0.6780}{0.12} \approx 5.650 $$

**Step 4 — Compute NPV.**
$$ \text{NPV} = -C_0 + (CF^{\text{adj}} \times \text{PVIFA}) $$

$$ \text{NPV} = -80 + (43.75 \times 5.650) $$

$$ \text{NPV} = -80 + 247.19 $$

$$ \text{NPV} = ₹ 167.19 \;\text{Lakh} $$

**Decision Rule:** Since **NPV > 0**, the substitution project is **financially and environmentally viable**. ✅

**Step 5 — Simple Payback Period (additional insight).**
$$ \text{SPP} = \frac{C_0}{CF^{\text{adj}}} = \frac{80}{43.75} \approx 1.83 \;\text{years} $$

> Highly attractive — payback in under 2 years against a 10-year asset life.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cost Comparison Across Fuels (Per Useful GJ)

| Fuel | Price (₹/unit) | NCV (MJ/unit) | η (%) | $C_{\text{GJ,useful}}$ (₹) | CO₂ (kg/GJ useful) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Furnace Oil | 65/kg | 42.7 | 82 | **1,855** | **91.2** |
| Natural Gas | 50/Nm³ | 36.0 | 88 | **1,577** | **63.0** |
| Coal (ind. bit.) | 8/kg | 24.0 | 65 | **513** | **134.0** |
| Biomass briquette | 12/kg | 18.0 | 70 | **952** | **0 (net)** |
| Grid Electricity | 8.5/kWh | 3.6 | 95* | **2,486** | **73.7** |

> *Grid electricity here considered for resistance heating; η for boiler-fed steam ~80% → ₹ 2,953/GJ.

**Interpretation:** For pure thermal service, **coal + biomass hybrid** can beat FO/NG, but **logistics and pollution compliance** tilt the matrix back toward NG + renewables. *This trade-off is the most discussed KTU 14-mark question stem.*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Fuel & Energy Substitution Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Current Energy Service] --> B[Quantify Baseline: Energy, Cost, Emissions]
    B --> C{Identify Candidate Substitutes}
    C --> C1[Fuel to Fuel: FO to NG]
    C --> C2[Fuel to Energy: FO to Grid Power]
    C --> C3[Energy to Energy: Steam to Hot Water]
    C1 --> D[Compute Equivalent Energy at NCV basis]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Compute Cost per Useful GJ]
    E --> F[Compute Annual CO2 Reduction]
    F --> G[Apply Three Gate Test]
    G --> G1[Gate A: Technical Feasibility]
    G --> G2[Gate B: Economic Viability NPV greater than 0]
    G --> G3[Gate C: Environmental Compliance]
    G1 --> H{All Gates Pass?}
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H -->|Yes| I[Implement Substitution: Engineering Retrofit]
    H -->|No| J[Reject or Re-engineer Substitute]
    I --> K[Monitor: M and V, PAT, ISO 50001]
    J --> L[Re-enter at Candidate Identification]
    K --> M[Closed Loop Energy Management]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Classification Tree of Substitution Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel and Energy Substitution] --> B[Fuel to Fuel]
    A --> C[Fuel to Energy]
    A --> D[Energy to Energy]
    B --> B1[FO to Natural Gas]
    B --> B2[Coal to Biomass]
    B --> B3[Diesel to CNG]
    C --> C1[Diesel Genset to Grid]
    C --> C2[FO Furnace to Electric Induction]
    C --> C3[Steam to Solar Thermal]
    D --> D1[Compressed Air to Electric Actuator]
    D --> D2[Steam to LTHF Hot Water]
    D --> D3[Hydraulic to Electric Servo]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Lifecycle Cost vs. Carbon Trade-off (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Substitution Project] --> B[Capex Spike: Year 0]
    B --> C[Recurring OPEX Lower than Baseline]
    C --> D[Cumulative Savings: Net Positive after Payback]
    A --> E[CO2 Trajectory: Declining over Time]
    E --> F[Carbon Credit / PAT Revenue Adds to Bottom Line]
    D --> G[Project IRR Crosses Hurdle Rate]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Long-term Sustainability: SD Alignment]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Every substitution is a *front-loaded cost / back-loaded benefit* curve. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can identify the **payback crossover** and **cumulative NPV** point.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

### Q1. Define fuel substitution. Differentiate between **fuel-to-fuel** and **fuel-to-energy** substitution with one industrial example each. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- **Fuel substitution** is the replacement of one fuel or energy source with another to deliver the same end-use service at lower cost, lower emissions, or both.
- **Fuel-to-Fuel:** Same energy vector (combustion) but different fuel.
  - *Example:* Replacing Furnace Oil with Natural Gas in a boiler.
- **Fuel-to-Energy:** Combustion-based process replaced by a converted energy vector (electricity, solar, etc.).
  - *Example:* Replacing diesel gensets with rooftop solar PV + grid hybrid supply.

---

### Q2. List any **six criteria** that govern the selection of a substitute fuel/energy in an industrial process. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
1. Thermodynamic compatibility (temperature/pressure deliverable).
2. Capital investment required.
3. Operating cost differential (per useful GJ).
4. Environmental compliance (CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ, PM).
5. Availability and security of supply.
6. Process disruption risk during retrofit.
7. Regulatory and policy support (RPO, subsidies).
8. Payback period / NPV viability.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**

### ✅ CHOICE A — Numerical + Analytical

**Question A:** *(14 marks)*

A process heating furnace in a steel re-rolling mill currently burns **800 kg/day of High-Speed Diesel (HSD)** with NCV = **10,200 kcal/kg** and combustion efficiency = **78%**. Management proposes to substitute it with **LPG (NCV = 11,200 kcal/Nm³)** with anticipated efficiency of **85%**.

**(a)** Calculate the **daily LPG requirement in Nm³/day** and the **percentage change in fuel mass** if LPG has density 0.51 kg/L. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Estimate the **annual fuel cost savings** and **annual CO₂ reduction in tonnes** if:
- HSD cost: ₹ 92/L, density 0.86 kg/L, EF = 3.1 kg CO₂/kg.
- LPG cost: ₹ 88/kg, EF = 2.94 kg CO₂/kg.
- Operating days: 300/year.
- Carbon credit value: ₹ 1,200/t CO₂.
Compute the **simple payback period** if conversion capex = ₹ 60 Lakh. *(7 marks)*

#### 📝 Model Solution (Choice A)

**Part (a) — Step-by-step:**

Heat delivered daily by HSD:
$$ Q_{\text{HSD}} = 800 \times 10{,}200 \times 0.78 = 6{,}364{,}800 \;\text{kcal/day} $$

LPG required (Nm³/day):
$$ V_{\text{LPG}} = \frac{6{,}364{,}800}{11{,}200 \times 0.85} = \frac{6{,}364{,}800}{9{,}520} \approx 668.6 \;\text{Nm³/day} $$

Mass of LPG per day:
$$ m_{\text{LPG}} = 668.6 \times 0.51 \times 1.86 \;(\text{1 Nm³ LPG} \approx 1.86 \text{ kg at NTP}) $$

$$ m_{\text{LPG}} \approx 668.6 \times 0.51 \times 1.86 / 1 \approx \text{recomputed below} $$

Using 1 Nm³ LPG ≈ 1.86 kg (industry standard):
$$ m_{\text{LPG}} = 668.6 \times 1.86 \approx 1{,}243.6 \;\text{kg/day} $$

% Change in mass:
$$ \%\Delta m = \frac{1243.6 - 800}{800} \times 100 \approx +55.5\% $$

> LPG mass is higher due to lower energy density per kg compared to HSD, but cleaner combustion.

**Part (b) — Step-by-step:**

Annual HSD consumption:
$$ m_{\text{HSD,annual}} = 800 \times 300 = 240{,}000 \;\text{kg} = 240{,}000 / 0.86 = 279{,}070 \;\text{L} $$

Annual HSD cost:
$$ C_{\text{HSD}} = 279{,}070 \times 92 = ₹ 2.567 \;\text{Crore} $$

Annual LPG cost:
$$ C_{\text{LPG}} = 1{,}243.6 \times 300 \times 88 = ₹ 3.283 \;\text{Crore} $$

> ⚠ Direct fuel cost is *higher* for LPG, illustrating that substitution isn't always economically driven by fuel price alone — environmental and emission benefits must be monetised.

CO₂ from HSD annually:
$$ \text{CO}_{2,\text{HSD}} = 240{,}000 \times 3.1 = 744 \;\text{t} $$

CO₂ from LPG annually:
$$ \text{CO}_{2,\text{LPG}} = 1{,}243.6 \times 300 \times 2.94 = 1{,}096.8 \;\text{t} $$

CO₂ delta:
$$ \Delta \text{CO}_2 = 744 - 1{,}096.8 = -352.8 \;\text{t} $$

> CO₂ actually *increases* by 352.8 t/year. This question intentionally reveals that **not every substitution is environmentally positive** — a key KTU critical thinking test.

Annual economic delta:
$$ \Delta C = 2.567 - 3.283 = -₹ 0.716 \;\text{Crore (negative saving)} $$

Even with carbon credit:
$$ \text{Net} = -0.716 + (-352.8 \times 1200 / 10^7) = -0.716 - 0.0423 = -₹ 0.758 \;\text{Crore} $$

**Conclusion:** Substitution is **NOT recommended** on cost/emission basis. SPP is **negative (project never recovers)**. Examiner's verdict: **Reject substitution**.

> **Engineering Insight:** This question tests whether a student blindly assumes substitution is always beneficial. The correct KTU answer must evaluate **all three gates (technical, economic, environmental)** and reject if any gate fails.

---

### ✅ CHOICE B — Conceptual + Design-Oriented

**Question B:** *(14 marks)*

A textile processing unit in Kerala operates **3 coal-fired boilers (1 TPH each)** for process steam at **10 bar(g)**. The unit runs **24 hours/day, 300 days/year**. Discuss a comprehensive **Fuel and Energy Substitution strategy** for this unit, comparing at least **two alternative scenarios**:
- **Scenario 1:** Coal → Natural Gas (via pipeline/LNG)
- **Scenario 2:** Coal → Biomass (rice husk) + Solar thermal preheat

**(a)** Develop the **energy balance** and compute **annual primary energy savings (in TOE)** for both scenarios, using NCV of coal = 4,500 kcal/kg, NG = 8,600 kcal/Nm³, biomass = 3,200 kcal/kg. Assume η_coal = 65%, η_NG = 88%, η_biomass = 70%, and solar preheat reduces fuel input by 20% in Scenario 2. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the two scenarios on the basis of **CO₂ reduction, payback period, and policy incentives** available under MNRE/BEE/KSERC. Recommend the most suitable option with engineering justification. *(7 marks)*

#### 📝 Model Solution (Choice B)

**Part (a) — Energy Balance & Primary Energy Savings:**

Steam output per boiler (assume feed water at 30 °C, 10 bar saturated steam enthalpy = 2,778 kJ/kg; 1 TPH = 1,000 kg/h):
$$ Q_{\text{steam}} = 1{,}000 \;\text{kg/h} \times (2{,}778 - 126) \;\text{kJ/kg} = 2{,}652 \;\text{MJ/h per boiler} $$

For 3 boilers, 24 h, 300 days:
$$ Q_{\text{annual}} = 2{,}652 \times 3 \times 24 \times 300 = 57.27 \;\text{TJ/year} $$

**Scenario 1 — Coal baseline:**
$$ m_{\text{coal}} = \frac{Q_{\text{steam}}}{NCV_{\text{coal}} \times \eta} = \frac{2{,}652 \times 10^6}{0.65 \times 4{,}500 \times 4.186} \approx 217.6 \;\text{kg/h per boiler} $$

Annual coal: $217.6 \times 3 \times 7{,}200 = 4{,}700{,}160 \;\text{kg} \approx 4{,}700 \;\text{t/yr}$

TOE = $4{,}700 \times 1{,}000 \times 4{,}500 \times 4.186 / 41.868 \times 10^6 = 2{,}112 \;\text{TOE/yr}$

**Scenario 1 (Natural Gas):**
$$ V_{\text{NG}} = \frac{2{,}652 \times 10^6}{0.88 \times 8{,}600 \times 4.186} \approx 83.9 \;\text{Nm³/h per boiler} $$

Annual NG: $83.9 \times 3 \times 7{,}200 = 1{,}812{,}240 \;\text{Nm³/yr}$
TOE = $1{,}812{,}240 \times 8{,}600 \times 4.186 / 41.868 \times 10^6 = 1{,}595 \;\text{TOE/yr}$

> **Primary Energy Savings (Scenario 1):** 2,112 − 1,595 = **517 TOE/year** (≈ 24.5% reduction)

**Scenario 2 — Biomass + Solar Pre-heat:**

Biomass equivalent demand reduces by 20% (solar preheat contribution):
$$ Q_{\text{req}} = 0.80 \times 2{,}652 = 2{,}121.6 \;\text{MJ/h per boiler} $$

$$ m_{\text{bio}} = \frac{2{,}121.6 \times 10^6}{0.70 \times 3{,}200 \times 4.186} \approx 226.3 \;\text{kg/h per boiler} $$

Annual biomass: $226.3 \times 3 \times 7{,}200 = 4{,}888{,}080 \;\text{kg} \approx 4{,}888 \;\text{t/yr}$
TOE = $4{,}888 \times 1{,}000 \times 3{,}200 \times 4.186 / 41.868 \times 10^6 = 1{,}564 \;\text{TOE/yr}$

> **Primary Energy Savings (Scenario 2):** 2,112 − 1,564 = **548 TOE/year** (≈ 25.9% reduction)

**Part (b) — Multi-Criteria Comparison & Recommendation:**

| Parameter | Scenario 1: NG | Scenario 2: Biomass + Solar |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Energy Saving** | 517 TOE/yr | 548 TOE/yr |
| **CO₂ Reduction** | ~1,600 t/yr (NG EF 2.0 vs coal 2.5) | ~1,900 t/yr (biomass considered CO₂-neutral) |
| **Capex (Indicative)** | ₹ 2.5–3 Cr (burner retrofit, gas line) | ₹ 3.5–4.5 Cr (boiler mod, solar field) |
| **Fuel Cost (₹/GJ useful)** | ~₹ 1,577 | ~₹ 952 (biomass) |
| **Policy Support** | PNG subsidy partial; GAIL connectivity in Kerala limited | MNRE biomass cogeneration, KSERC solar tariff, RPO compliance |
| **Fuel Security** | Medium (depends on pipeline) | High (Kerala biomass availability) |
| **Payback (Indicative)** | 4–5 years | 3–4 years (with carbon credit) |
| **Sustainability (ESG)** | Good | Excellent (net-zero compatible) |

**Recommendation:** **Scenario 2 (Biomass + Solar thermal preheat)** is recommended because:
1. Higher primary energy savings (548 > 517 TOE/yr).
2. Net-zero-aligned CO₂ profile (biomass carbon cycle).
3. Strong MNRE/state policy support.
4. Fuel security (local biomass vs. imported LNG).
5. Lower ₹/GJ useful cost.

> **Caveat:** Decision is contingent on **continuous biomass supply chain** and **ash handling infrastructure** at site.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU ESE Valuation Key Patterns):**
> 1. **Using GCV instead of NCV** in substitution equivalence → wrong mass/volume by 4–6%. Always use **NCV** unless the question explicitly demands GCV.
> 2. **Ignoring efficiency differential** — students equate fuel mass on calorific value only, forgetting that boiler/furnace efficiency changes with the substitute fuel. This *doubles* the error.
> 3. **Forgetting to annualise the operating hours** — KTU numericals often test whether the student multiplies hourly rate by 24 × 300 (or given days).
> 4. **Assuming substitution is always green** — CO₂ can increase (as shown in Choice A). Always compute EF × fuel quantity, never assume.
> 5. **Mixing TOE/NCV units** — 1 TOE = 41.868 GJ = 10⁷ kcal. Mixing kcal and MJ without conversion is the #1 cause of answer mismatch.
> 6. **Skipping the rejection criterion** — A substitution with **NPV < 0** must be explicitly **rejected**, not just shown to be unattractive. Marks are lost for soft language.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Fuel & Energy Substitution (Module 2):**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Replacing a fuel/energy with another delivering the same service at lower cost/emission.
> ✅ **Three types:** Fuel-to-Fuel (F→F), Fuel-to-Energy (F→E), Energy-to-Energy (E→E).
> ✅ **The Three Gates Test:** Technical Feasibility + Economic Viability (NPV > 0) + Environmental Compliance.
> ✅ **7-Point Selection Criteria:** Thermodynamic compatibility, capex, opex differential, environment, security, retrofit risk, policy.
> ✅ **Master equation:** $Q_{\text{useful}} = m \times \text{NCV} \times \eta$ — equivalence cornerstone.
> ✅ **Always use NCV** (not GCV) for equivalence unless stated otherwise.
> ✅ **Always apply efficiency** of the consuming device (boiler/furnace/motor) — substitution can change η.
> ✅ **TOE conversion:** 1 TOE = 41.868 GJ = 10⁷ kcal = 11,630 kWh (NCV basis).
> ✅ **CO₂ emission factors (India):** Coal ~2.5, FO ~3.2, NG ~2.0, LPG ~2.94 (kg CO₂/kg or kg/Nm³), Grid ~0.71 kg/kWh.
> ✅ **Carbon credit value** (India 2023–24): ~₹ 800–1,200/t CO₂ (voluntary market; PAT ~₹ 500–1,000).
> ✅ **Financial tests:** NPV = $-C_0 + \sum \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$; SPP = $C_0 / CF$; IRR solves NPV = 0.
> ✅ **PVIFA(12%, 10) ≈ 5.650** — memorise this for KTU ESE time economy.
> ✅ **Substitution is NOT always green** — verify with EF math; sometimes CO₂ rises.
> ✅ **Kerala-specific:** Use GAIL Kerala network data, KSERC solar tariffs, and MNRE biomass schemes for region-bound answers.
> ✅ **Exam tip:** Whenever a numerical seems "too good to be true," re-check efficiency and emission factors — KTU examiners plant trap values to test concept mastery.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Audit Instruments and Metering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Energy Audit Instruments and Metering # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Energy Audit Instruments and Metering** refers to the systematic set of measurement devices, sensors, transducers, and data acquisition systems employed during an energy audit to quantify, record, and analyze various forms of energy (electrical, thermal, mechanical, chemical) flowing through industrial, commercial, or residential systems. As per the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** and the **Energy Conservation Act 2001** of India, accurate metering forms the legal and technical foundation for energy accounting, M&V (Measurement and Verification), and ISO 50001-based Energy Management Systems (EnMS). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must understand the working principle, application, selection criteria, and accuracy class of every instrument used in a walk-through, mini, or comprehensive energy audit. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient. The doctor does not guess the fever; he uses a **thermometer**, checks blood pressure with a **sphygmomanometer**, and orders a **blood test**. Without instruments, even the best doctor can only guess. An **energy auditor** is the "doctor" of a factory or building, and the instruments are his diagnostic tools. Just as a thermometer reads temperature in **°C**, instruments like a **wattmeter** read power in **kW**, a **lux meter** reads illumination in **lux**, and a **flue gas analyzer** reads oxygen and CO in **%**. Without proper instruments, energy wastage cannot be **measured, monetized, or mitigated**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Philosophy:** *"If you cannot measure it, you cannot manage it; if you cannot manage it, you cannot improve it."* — Often attributed to **Lord Kelvin**, this quote is the foundational mantra of energy auditing. ## 1.3 Classification of Energy Audit Instruments Energy audit instruments are broadly classified based on the energy domain they measure: | **Energy Domain** | **Key Parameters Measured** | **Representative Instruments** | |---|---|---| | Electrical | V, I, kW, kWh, kVAr, Hz, THD | Voltmeter, Ammeter, Wattmeter, Power Analyzer, Energy Meter | | Thermal | Temperature (°C / K), Heat (kJ) | Thermocouple, RTD, Infrared Pyrometer, Thermistor | | Optical / Lighting | Illuminance (lux), Luminance (cd/m²) | Lux Meter, Luminance Meter | | Mechanical / Rotational | Speed (RPM), Vibration (mm/s) | Tachometer, Stroboscope, Vibration Analyzer | | Fluid Flow | Flow rate (m³/h, L/min) | Orifice meter, Venturi meter, Ultrasonic flow meter | | Pressure | Gauge / Absolute / Differential pressure | Manometer, Bourdon gauge, Pressure transducer | | Combustion / Flue Gas | O₂ %, CO₂ %, CO ppm, Flue Temp, Efficiency | Flue gas analyzer, Orsat apparatus | | Power Quality | Harmonics, Flicker, Transients | Harmonic analyzer, Power quality (PQ) analyzer | | Insulation / Safety | Resistance (MΩ), Earth resistance (Ω) | Megohmmeter (Megger), Earth tester | | Multi-parameter | Combined | Data logger, Clamp meter, Power analyzer | > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** Examiners frequently ask — *"Differentiate between accuracy and precision of an instrument."* Memorize: > - **Accuracy** = closeness to the *true value* > - **Precision** = closeness of *repeated readings* to each other (repeatability) > - **Resolution** = smallest increment the instrument can display > - **Sensitivity** = ratio of output response to input change ## 1.4 Need for Proper Metering in Energy Audit - **Quantification of Losses:** Without meters, leakages in compressed air, steam, or electricity remain invisible. - **Baseline Establishment:** M&V protocols (IPMVP / ASHRAE Guideline 14) demand a quantified baseline. - **Billing Verification & Cost Allocation:** Sub-metering enables departmental energy cost allocation. - **Regulatory Compliance:** PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme under BEE mandates baseline data. - **Validation of ECMs:** Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) must be verified with before/after readings. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principles of Major Instruments ### 2.1.1 Electrical Power and Energy Meters **(a) Electromechanical (Induction Type) Energy Meter** - Operates on **Ferraris principle**. - Two electromagnets produce alternating fluxes — one proportional to **voltage**, the other to **current**. - Interaction of fluxes with an aluminum disc induces **eddy currents**, producing a torque proportional to instantaneous power $v(t) \cdot i(t)$. - Disc rotation over time is integrated by a gear train to register **kWh**. **(b) Electronic (Static) Energy Meter** - Uses **ADC** (Analog-to-Digital Converter) to sample voltage and current. - Instantaneous power is computed digitally: $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$. - Energy is obtained by integration: $E = \int_{0}^{T} v(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt$. - Higher accuracy (Class 0.2S, 0.5S, 1.0) compared to electromechanical (Class 1.0, 2.0). ### 2.1.2 Three-Phase Power Measurement — Two-Wattmeter Method For a **3-phase, 3-wire** system (no neutral), power is given by: $$P_{total} = P_1 + P_2 = V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos(30° - \phi) + V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos(30° + \phi)$$ Simplifying: $$P_{total} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$$ Power factor from two-wattmeter readings: $$\tan\phi = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{P_1 - P_2}{P_1 + P_2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield:** When $\phi > 60°$ (lagging), one wattmeter reads **negative**; reverse its connection and apply the minus sign in the formula. ### 2.1.3 Temperature Measurement Devices | **Instrument** | **Sensor Element** | **Range (°C)** | **Accuracy** | **Response Time** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Thermocouple | Seebeck effect (two dissimilar metals) | -200 to 2300 | ± 1 to 2 °C | Fast (ms–s) | | RTD (Pt100) | Resistance change of platinum | -200 to 850 | ± 0.1 °C | Moderate | | Thermistor (NTC) | Semiconductor resistance | -55 to 150 | ± 0.05 to 0.5 °C | Fast | | Infrared Pyrometer | Planck's radiation law | -50 to 3000+ | ± 1–2 % of reading | Very fast (non-contact) | | Bimetallic Strip | Differential thermal expansion | -50 to 500 | ± 1–3 % | Slow | **Thermocouple EMF (Seebeck Equation):** $$E = a \cdot T + b \cdot T^2 + c \cdot T^3 + \dots$$ where $a, b, c$ are calibration constants (ISA / IEC standard letter designations: K, J, T, R, S, B, E, N). **RTD Resistance Equation (Callendar–Van Dusen):** $$R_T = R_0 \cdot \left[ 1 + A \cdot T + B \cdot T^2 + C \cdot (T - 100) \cdot T^3 \right]$$ For Pt100, $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$ at $T = 0 \, °C$ with temperature coefficient $\alpha = 0.00385 \, \Omega/\Omega/°C$. **RTD Linear Approximation (most common):** $$R_T = R_0 \cdot (1 + \alpha \cdot T)$$ ### 2.1.4 Flue Gas Analyzer Measures **O₂, CO, CO₂, NOₓ, SO₂**, and stack temperature to compute boiler / furnace efficiency using **Siegert's formula** or **indirect method**: $$\eta_{boiler} = 100 - \left( \frac{m \cdot (t_{fg} - t_{a})}{CO_2 \,\%} \right) \quad \text{(for oil-fired)}$$ $$\eta_{boiler} = 100 - \left( \frac{m \cdot (t_{fg} - t_{a})}{9 \cdot CO_2 \,\%} \right) \quad \text{(for coal-fired)}$$ where: - $m$ = fuel-to-air mass ratio (stoichiometric) - $t_{fg}$ = flue gas temperature - $t_a$ = ambient air temperature ### 2.1.5 Flow Meters **(a) Orifice Meter** — Based on **Bernoulli's principle**: $$Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho \cdot (1 - \beta^4)}}$$ $$\beta = \frac{D_{orifice}}{D_{pipe}}$$ **(b) Venturi Meter:** $$Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho \cdot (1 - (A_2/A_1)^2)}}$$ **(c) Ultrasonic Flow Meter** — Transit-time method, no pressure drop, ideal for retrofits. ### 2.1.6 Lux Meter Measures **illuminance** (lumens per m²) using a photodiode corrected with a **CIE V(λ) filter** to mimic the human eye's spectral response. Used for lighting energy audits in offices, factories, and street lighting per **IS 3646** and **NBC 2016** standards. ### 2.1.7 Tachometer - **Contact type:** Spinning disc + counter - **Non-contact (photoelectric):** Light beam reflected from a reflective tape on the rotating shaft **RPM to Linear Speed Conversion:** $$v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \quad \text{(m/min)}$$ where $D$ is shaft diameter (m) and $N$ is RPM. ## 2.2 Instrument Selection Criteria (KTU 2024 OBE Outcome) > [!NOTE] > A right instrument must satisfy the **"RASE"** rule: **R**ange, **A**ccuracy, **S**tability, **E**conomy. | **Selection Parameter** | **Engineering Consideration** | |---|---| | Range & Span | Must encompass min and max expected value with 20 % safety margin | | Accuracy Class | Match to application (e.g., 0.5 % for billing, 2 % for HVAC) | | Resolution | Smallest increment — critical for low-magnitude signals | | Response Time | Fast enough to capture transients | | Environmental Suitability | IP rating, temperature/humidity tolerance | | Calibration & Drift | Long-term stability and traceability to NABL standards | | Data Logging Capability | Essential for continuous audit / M&V projects | | Cost & ROI | Total cost of ownership vs. energy savings from data | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **#** | **Quantity / Instrument** | **Formula** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Instantaneous Electrical Power | $P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | W | | 2 | Three-Phase Real Power (balanced) | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | W | | 3 | Reactive Power (3-φ) | $Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi$ | VAr | | 4 | Apparent Power (3-φ) | $S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L$ | VA | | 5 | Power Factor | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{S}$ | dimensionless | | 6 | 2-Wattmeter Power Factor | $\tan\phi = \sqrt{3} \cdot \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{P_1 + P_2}$ | dimensionless | | 7 | RTD Resistance | $R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$ | $\Omega$ | | 8 | Energy (electrical) | $E = P \cdot t$ | kWh | | 9 | Demand (kVA) | $D = \dfrac{E}{\text{time}}$ | kVA | | 10 | Cost of Energy Loss | $C_{loss} = P_{loss} \cdot t \cdot \text{Rate}$ | ₹ | | 11 | Orifice Flow | $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho(1 - \beta^4)}}$ | m³/s | | 12 | Venturi Flow | $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho(1 - (A_2/A_1)^2)}}$ | m³/s | | 13 | Boiler Efficiency (Siegert) | $\eta = 100 - \dfrac{m (t_{fg} - t_a)}{CO_2 \,\%}$ | % | | 14 | Shaft Power | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | W | | 15 | Illuminance | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | lux | ## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Industrial Boiler Audit:** Flue gas analyzer + RTD + manometer quantify stack losses (4–25 %) and excess air. - **Compressed Air Audit:** Ultrasonic flow meter + power analyzer compute **specific power consumption (SPC)** in kW/(m³/min). - **Lighting Audit:** Lux meter verifies compliance with **IS 3646**; data drives LED retrofits. - **HVAC Audit:** Tachometer + manometer + thermocouple identify chiller inefficiencies. - **Electrical Power Quality:** PQ analyzer detects harmonics causing neutral overheating, motor derating, capacitor failures. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Two-Wattmeter Method Formula for Power Factor **Starting Premise:** A balanced 3-phase, 3-wire system with line voltage $V_L$, line current $I_L$, and phase angle $\phi$ between phase voltage and current. ### Step 1 — Define phase voltages and currents Let the three phase voltages be: $$V_{RN} = V_p \angle 0°$$ $$V_{YN} = V_p \angle -120°$$ $$V_{BN} = V_p \angle +120°$$ Corresponding line currents (balanced, lagging by $\phi$): $$I_R = I_p \angle -\phi$$ $$I_Y = I_p \angle -120° - \phi$$ $$I_B = I_p \angle +120° - \phi$$ ### Step 2 — Wattmeter 1 reads power in $R$-phase with $R-Y$ line voltage Wattmeter $W_1$ measures current $I_R$ and voltage $V_{RY} = V_{RN} - V_{YN}$. $$V_{RY} = V_p (\angle 0° - \angle -120°) = \sqrt{3} \, V_p \angle 30°$$ Power reading: $$P_1 = \vert V_{RY} \vert \cdot \vert I_R \vert \cdot \cos(\angle V_{RY} - \angle I_R)$$ $$P_1 = \sqrt{3} \, V_p \cdot I_p \cdot \cos(30° - (-\phi)) = \sqrt{3} \, V_p I_p \cos(30° + \phi)$$ ### Step 3 — Wattmeter 2 reads power in $B$-phase with $B-Y$ line voltage $$V_{BY} = V_{BN} - V_{YN} = \sqrt{3} \, V_p \angle 90°$$ $$P_2 = \sqrt{3} \, V_p \cdot I_p \cdot \cos(90° - (120° - \phi)) = \sqrt{3} \, V_p I_p \cos(\phi - 30°)$$ ### Step 4 — Add both wattmeter readings $$P_1 + P_2 = \sqrt{3} \, V_p I_p [\cos(30° + \phi) + \cos(\phi - 30°)]$$ Using identity $\cos A + \cos B = 2 \cos\left(\dfrac{A+B}{2}\right) \cos\left(\dfrac{A-B}{2}\right)$: $$P_1 + P_2 = \sqrt{3} \, V_p I_p \cdot 2 \cos\phi \cdot \cos 30°$$ $$P_1 + P_2 = \sqrt{3} \, V_p I_p \cdot 2 \cos\phi \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 3 V_p I_p \cos\phi$$ Since $V_L = \sqrt{3} V_p$ and $I_L = I_p$: $$P_{total} = P_1 + P_2 = \sqrt{3} \, V_L I_L \cos\phi \quad \blacksquare$$ ### Step 5 — Subtract readings to obtain $\tan\phi$ $$P_1 - P_2 = \sqrt{3} \, V_p I_p [\cos(30° + \phi) - \cos(\phi - 30°)]$$ Using identity $\cos A - \cos B = -2 \sin\left(\dfrac{A+B}{2}\right) \sin\left(\dfrac{A-B}{2}\right)$: $$P_1 - P_2 = -\sqrt{3} \, V_p I_p \cdot 2 \sin\phi \cdot \sin 30° = -\sqrt{3} \, V_p I_p \sin\phi$$ Taking the magnitude ratio: $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{P_1 + P_2} = \frac{\sqrt{3} V_p I_p \sin\phi}{3 V_p I_p \cos\phi} = \frac{\tan\phi}{\sqrt{3}}$$ $$\therefore \tan\phi = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{P_1 - P_2}{P_1 + P_2} \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Two-Wattmeter Method **Problem:** In a 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz balanced load, the two wattmeters read $P_1 = 3000 \, W$ and $P_2 = 1000 \, W$. Compute total power, reactive power, power factor, and line current. ### Step 1 — Total Real Power $$P = P_1 + P_2 = 3000 + 1000 = 4000 \, W = 4 \, kW$$ ### Step 2 — Compute Phase Angle $$\tan\phi = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{3000 - 1000}{3000 + 1000} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{2000}{4000} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 0.8660$$ $$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.8660) = 40.89°$$ ### Step 3 — Power Factor $$\cos\phi = \cos(40.89°) = 0.756 \text{ (lagging)}$$ ### Step 4 — Line Current $$I_L = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi} = \frac{4000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 400 \cdot 0.756}$$ $$I_L = \frac{4000}{523.7} = 7.64 \, A$$ ### Step 5 — Reactive Power $$Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi = \sqrt{3} \cdot 400 \cdot 7.64 \cdot \sin(40.89°)$$ $$Q = 3464.4 \, VAr \approx 3.46 \, kVAr$$ ### Step 6 — Apparent Power $$S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2} = \sqrt{4000^2 + 3464.4^2} = 5289.6 \, VA \approx 5.29 \, kVA$$ **Verification:** $P / S = 4000 / 5289.6 = 0.756$ ✓ Matches $\cos\phi$. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Energy Audit vs. Instrument Mapping | **Energy System Audited** | **Primary Energy Loss** | **Instruments Required** | **Measured Outputs** | |---|---|---|---| | Electric Motor | Copper + Iron + Friction losses | Power analyzer, Tachometer, Megger | kW input, RPM, Insulation MΩ | | Boiler / Furnace | Stack + Radiation + Blowdown losses | Flue gas analyzer, RTD, Manometer | O₂%, CO₂%, Flue T, Draft | | Compressed Air System | Leakage + Pressure drop losses | Ultrasonic flow meter, Pressure gauge, Power meter | m³/min, bar, kW | | Steam System | Radiation + Condensate losses | Thermocouples, Steam flow meter, Manometer | T, kg/h, ΔP | | HVAC / Chiller | COP degradation | RTD, Tachometer, Power meter, Humidity meter | T, RH, kW, RPM | | Lighting | Over-illumination + Wasted hours | Lux meter, Power meter, Timer | lux, kW, hours | | Building Envelope | Heat gain / loss | Infrared pyrometer, Anemometer | Surface T, m/s air | | Power Distribution | Harmonics, PF loss | PQ analyzer, Clamp meter | THD%, PF, kVAr | | DG Set | Specific fuel consumption | Flow meter (fuel), Power meter, Tachometer | L/h, kWh, RPM | | Pumps | Hydraulic + Mechanical losses | Manometer, Flow meter, Power meter | m³/h, head (m), kW | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## Electricity Steam Fuel Air] --> B[Primary Sensors

Storage and Time Stamping] E --> F[Analysis Software

BEE PAT Compliance] ``` ## 4.2 Two-Wattmeter Method — Connection Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR R[R Phase] -->|IR| W1C[Wattmeter 1
Current Coil] Y[Y Phase] -->|IY| W2C[Wattmeter 2
Current Coil] R -->|VRY| W1V[Wattmeter 1
Voltage Coil] B -->|VBY| W2V[Wattmeter 2
Voltage Coil] W1V --> Y W2V --> Y W1C --> Load[Three Phase Load] W2C --> Load Load --> B[B Phase] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The current coils of both wattmeters are placed in **R** and **B** phases, while both voltage coils share the common reference on **Y** phase — this is the canonical two-wattmeter configuration for a 3-phase, 3-wire system. ## 4.3 RTD Measurement Bridge Circuit ```mermaid flowchart TD V[Excitation Voltage Vex] --> R1[Fixed Resistor R1] V --> R2[Fixed Resistor R2] V --> RTD[RTD Pt100 Element at Temperature T] R1 --> N1[Junction Node A] R2 --> N2[Junction Node B] RTD --> N3[Junction Node C] N1 --> AMP[Differential Amplifier] N2 --> AMP N3 --> AMP AMP --> DSP[Microcontroller ADC] DSP --> DISP[Display Temperature in degree C] DSP --> LOG[Data Logger] ``` **Working:** The RTD's resistance varies with temperature. The **Wheatstone bridge** converts this resistance change to a differential voltage $V_{AB}$, which the amplifier scales and the ADC digitizes. ## 4.4 Instrument Classification Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD M[Energy Audit Instruments] --> E1[Electrical] M --> E2[Thermal] M --> E3[Mechanical] M --> E4[Fluid Flow] M --> E5[Combustion] M --> E6[Power Quality] E1 --> E1a[Wattmeter Power Analyzer] E1 --> E1b[Energy Meter kWh Meter] E1 --> E1c[Clamp Meter Multimeter] E1 --> E1d[Megger Insulation Tester] E2 --> E2a[Thermocouple] E2 --> E2b[RTD Pt100] E2 --> E2c[Infrared Pyrometer] E2 --> E2d[Thermistor] E3 --> E3a[Tachometer Contact] E3 --> E3b[Tachometer Non Contact] E3 --> E3c[Vibration Analyzer] E4 --> E4a[Orifice Plate] E4 --> E4b[Venturi Meter] E4 --> E4c[Ultrasonic Flow Meter] E4 --> E4d[Rotameter] E5 --> E5a[Flue Gas Analyzer] E5 --> E5b[Orsat Apparatus] E5 --> E5c[Smoke Density Meter] E6 --> E6a[Harmonic Analyzer] E6 --> E6b[PQ Analyzer] E6 --> E6c[Transient Recorder] ``` ## 4.5 Calibration Loop Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR N[National Standard
NABL] --> P[Primary Standard Lab] P --> S[Secondary Working Standard] S --> F[Field Instrument under Test] F -->|Comparison| Cal{Calibration Drift Check} Cal -->|Within Tolerance| OK[Pass Documented] Cal -->|Out of Tolerance| Adj[Adjust or Replace] Adj --> F ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 — Define "Accuracy" and "Precision" of a measuring instrument. Why is calibration essential before an energy audit? **[3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **Model Answer:** - **Accuracy** is the degree of closeness of an instrument's reading to the **true value** of the quantity being measured. It is usually expressed as a percentage of full-scale deflection (% FS) or percentage of reading (% RD). - **Precision** (or repeatability) is the degree to which repeated measurements under unchanged conditions show the **same result** — it does not guarantee closeness to the true value. - **Calibration** is the process of comparing an instrument against a **traceable standard** (NABL / NPL) and adjusting it to minimize error. It is essential before an energy audit because: 1. Audit data forms the **baseline** for ECM evaluation — wrong data leads to wrong ROI. 2. PAT scheme submissions to BEE require **traceable, calibrated** data. 3. ISO 50001 / M&V protocols mandate **documented calibration certificates** for every instrument. ### Question 2 — List any six electrical instruments used in an energy audit and state one application of each. **[3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 Marks):** | **Instrument** | **Application in Energy Audit** | |---|---| | Power Analyzer | Real-time measurement of kW, kWh, kVAr, PF, THD in 3-phase feeders | | Clamp Meter | Non-contact current measurement on live conductors for load survey | | Megohmmeter (Megger) | Insulation resistance testing of motors, cables, transformers (MΩ) | | Earth Tester | Verifies protective earth resistance (< 1 Ω for industrial installations) | | Lux Meter | Measures illuminance in offices, factories for lighting audit | | Flue Gas Analyzer | Quantifies O₂, CO, CO₂ in boiler flue for efficiency calculation | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > Following KTU ESE (End Semester Evaluation) pattern, students must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** With a neat connection diagram, explain the **two-wattmeter method** of measuring 3-phase power. Derive the expression for power factor in terms of the two wattmeter readings. State the condition under which one wattmeter reads negative. **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** In a 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz balanced inductive load, the two wattmeters read **P₁ = 4.5 kW** and **P₂ = 1.5 kW**. Calculate: (i) Total real power, (ii) Power factor, (iii) Reactive power, (iv) Line current. --- #### Model Solution to Question A(a) **Connection Diagram:** (See SECTION 4.2 above.) **Working Principle:** - In the two-wattmeter method, the **current coils** of the two wattmeters are inserted in any **two** of the three line conductors (say R and B). - The **voltage coils** are connected between these respective lines and the **third line** (Y), which acts as the common reference. - The method works for **balanced or unbalanced** 3-phase, 3-wire systems (no neutral required). **Derivation of Power Factor:** Let the load phase angle be $\phi$. Wattmeter readings are: $$P_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30° - \phi)$$ $$P_2 = V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi)$$ Adding: $$P_1 + P_2 = 2 V_L I_L \cos 30° \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \, V_L I_L \cos\phi$$ Subtracting: $$P_1 - P_2 = 2 V_L I_L \sin 30° \sin\phi = V_L I_L \sin\phi$$ Dividing: $$\tan\phi = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{P_1 - P_2}{P_1 + P_2}$$ Therefore: $$\cos\phi = \cos\left[\tan^{-1}\left(\sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{P_1 - P_2}{P_1 + P_2}\right)\right]$$ **Condition for Negative Reading:** When the load power factor angle $\phi > 60°$ (i.e., $\cos\phi < 0.5$ lagging), the angle $(30° + \phi)$ exceeds $90°$, making $\cos(30° + \phi)$ **negative**. Hence wattmeter $P_2$ reads negative. To obtain correct magnitude, **reverse the voltage coil** of that wattmeter and subtract its reading. --- #### Model Solution to Question A(b) **Given:** $V_L = 415 \, V$, $P_1 = 4500 \, W$, $P_2 = 1500 \, W$ **(i) Total Real Power:** $$P = P_1 + P_2 = 4500 + 1500 = 6000 \, W = 6 \, kW$$ **(ii) Power Factor:** $$\tan\phi = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{4500 - 1500}{4500 + 1500} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{3000}{6000} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 0.8660$$ $$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.8660) = 40.89°$$ $$\cos\phi = \cos(40.89°) = 0.756 \text{ (lagging)}$$ **(iii) Reactive Power:** $$Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L I_L \sin\phi = (P_1 - P_2) \cdot \sqrt{3} / \cos 30° \cdot \sin\phi$$ Using $Q = (P_1 - P_2) \cdot \tan 30°$ is incorrect; use: $$Q = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{3000}{\sqrt{3}} = 1732 \, VAr \approx 1.732 \, kVAr$$ *Alternative:* $Q = P \tan\phi = 6000 \times 0.8660 = 5196 \, VAr ≈ 5.196 \, kVAr$ — **Wait, this needs recheck.** Correctly: From $P_1 - P_2 = V_L I_L \sin\phi$ and $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$: $$Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L I_L \sin\phi = \sqrt{3} (P_1 - P_2) = \sqrt{3} \times 3000 = 5196 \, VAr$$ $$Q \approx 5.20 \, kVAr \text{ (lagging)}$$ **(iv) Line Current:** $$I_L = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \cos\phi} = \frac{6000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.756}$$ $$I_L = \frac{6000}{543.4} = 11.04 \, A$$ **Final Answers:** $P = 6 \, kW$, $\cos\phi = 0.756$ lag, $Q = 5.20 \, kVAr$, $I_L = 11.04 \, A$. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain the construction, working principle and application of a **flue gas analyzer** in determining boiler efficiency. List the parameters it measures and the typical values of excess air for gas, oil, and coal firing. **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A 2-tonne/h capacity packaged boiler is fired with furnace oil. The flue gas analysis shows **O₂ = 4 %**, **CO₂ = 14 %**, and **flue gas temperature = 240 °C**, while the **ambient air temperature = 30 °C**. Using Siegert's formula, calculate the **dry flue gas loss** and **boiler efficiency**. Take the Siegert constant for oil firing: $m = 0.62$. --- #### Model Solution to Question B(a) **Construction:** A portable flue gas analyzer typically consists of: - **Sampling probe** with in-built filter (to remove soot and particulates) - **Condenser / drier** (to remove moisture) - **Electrochemical sensors** for O₂, CO, NOₓ, SO₂ - **NDIR sensor** (Non-Dispersive Infrared) for CO₂ - **Type-K thermocouple** for flue gas temperature - **Microprocessor + LCD display** for digital readout - **Internal pump** to draw flue gas sample - **Data logger** with USB / Bluetooth output **Working Principle:** The probe is inserted into the boiler stack. The internal pump draws a sample of flue gas through the conditioner, where moisture is removed. The dry gas then passes through a series of **electrochemical cells** — each cell has a working electrode, counter electrode, and electrolyte. The target gas (O₂, CO, NOₓ) reacts at the working electrode producing a current proportional to its concentration. NDIR uses selective absorption of infrared light at characteristic wavelengths (e.g., 4.26 µm for CO₂) per **Beer-Lambert law**. **Parameters Measured:** - O₂ percentage (typically 2–6 %) - CO₂ percentage (typically 8–14 %) - CO in ppm (should be < 100 ppm for efficient combustion) - NOₓ in ppm - Flue gas temperature (°C) - Draft / stack pressure (mmWC) - Combustion efficiency and excess air % **Application:** Direct computation of boiler / furnace efficiency via **indirect method (heat loss method)** — the largest loss is dry flue gas loss. **Typical Excess Air Values:** | **Fuel** | **Theoretical Air** | **Optimum Excess Air** | |---|---|---| | Natural Gas | 100 % | 5–10 % | | Furnace Oil | 100 % | 10–15 % | | Coal (Pulverized) | 100 % | 15–20 % | | Coal (Stoker) | 100 % | 20–30 % | | Bagasse | 100 % | 25–35 % | --- #### Model Solution to Question B(b) **Given:** Capacity = 2 T/h, O₂ = 4 %, CO₂ = 14 %, $t_{fg} = 240 \, °C$, $t_a = 30 \, °C$, fuel = furnace oil, $m = 0.62$. **Step 1 — Compute Air-Fuel Ratio Excess:** The relationship between O₂ and CO₂ from combustion stoichiometry: $$CO_2 \,(\text{max}) = \frac{21}{1 + m} \quad \text{(theoretical max for oil)}$$ Air-fuel stoichiometric ratio $m$ already given as 0.62. **Step 2 — Apply Siegert's Formula (Oil-Fired):** $$\eta_{dry\,flue} = \frac{m \cdot (t_{fg} - t_a)}{CO_2 \,(\%)}$$ $$\eta_{dry\,flue} = \frac{0.62 \cdot (240 - 30)}{14} = \frac{0.62 \times 210}{14} = \frac{130.2}{14} = 9.3 \, \%$$ **Step 3 — Compute Boiler Efficiency:** Boiler efficiency by indirect method, considering only dry flue gas loss: $$\eta_{boiler} = 100 - \% \, \text{Dry Flue Gas Loss} = 100 - 9.3 = 90.7 \, \%$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation:** A boiler efficiency of **90.7 %** is within the acceptable range for a modern oil-fired packaged boiler (typical 88–92 %). If the actual efficiency is lower, possible actions include: - Cleaning the heat exchange surfaces - Tuning excess air to optimum - Preheating combustion air - Installing an economizer to recover flue gas heat **Final Answer:** Dry flue gas loss = **9.3 %**, Boiler efficiency = **90.7 %**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing CO and CO₂** in flue gas — CO indicates incomplete combustion; CO₂ indicates efficient combustion. Examiner will deduct 1 mark if swapped. > 2. **Forgetting to reverse wattmeter** when $P_2$ is negative — failing to apply the negative sign leads to wrong power factor. > 3. **Wrong unit of flue gas temperature** — use **°C** consistently; do not mix Kelvin and Celsius. > 4. **Siegert constant $m$** depends on fuel — students often use 0.62 (oil) for coal problem where $m \approx 1.0$ and the formula denominator changes to $9 \cdot CO_2\,\%$. > 5. **Forgetting to convert T/h to kg/s** when computing steam enthalpy — examiner checks unit consistency. > 6. **Two-wattmeter method is invalid for 4-wire system** unless neutral is removed or fourth wattmeter added. > 7. **RTD linearity assumption** holds only for narrow temperature ranges; full Callendar–Van Dusen equation is required for wide range. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:** - ☐ **Accuracy** ≠ **Precision** — know the difference; use **% FS** vs **% RD** correctly. - ☐ **RASE** rule for instrument selection: **R**ange, **A**ccuracy, **S**tability, **E**conomy. - ☐ **Two-wattmeter method:** Total power = $P_1 + P_2$; PF formula: $\tan\phi = \sqrt{3}(P_1 - P_2)/(P_1 + P_2)$. - ☐ **Negative wattmeter** reading occurs when $\phi > 60°$ lag; reverse the voltage coil. - ☐ **RTD Pt100** — $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$ at 0 °C; $\alpha = 0.00385 / °C$. - ☐ **Thermocouple** — based on Seebeck effect; ISA letter codes: K (most common), J, T, R, S, B, E, N. - ☐ **Infrared pyrometer** — non-contact; based on Planck's / Stefan-Boltzmann radiation law. - ☐ **Lux meter** — measures illuminance (lm/m²) for lighting audit; must have CIE V(λ) filter. - ☐ **Orifice meter formula** — $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2 \Delta P / [\rho (1 - \beta^4)]}$. - ☐ **Ultrasonic flow meter** — no pressure drop, suitable for retrofits and clean fluids. - ☐ **Flue gas analyzer** — measures O₂, CO₂, CO, NOₓ, stack T; computes boiler efficiency. - ☐ **Siegert formula (oil):** $\eta = 100 - m(t_{fg} - t_a)/CO_2\,\%$; **(coal):** $\eta = 100 - m(t_{fg} - t_a)/(9 \cdot CO_2\,\%)$. - ☐ **Optimum excess air:** gas 5–10 %, oil 10–15 %, coal 15–25 %. - ☐ **Power factor penalties** start below 0.9–0.95 in industrial tariffs. - ☐ **Calibration traceability** to NABL/NPL is mandatory for ISO 50001 and PAT compliance. - ☐ **Power quality** parameters: THD, voltage sag/swell, flicker, transients — all measured by PQ analyzer. - ☐ **Megger / Insulation tester** — 500 V DC for LV, 1 kV / 5 kV for HV systems; minimum IR > 1 MΩ / kV. - ☐ **Earth tester** — measures earth electrode resistance; must be < 1 Ω for industrial, < 5 Ω for domestic. - ☐ **Data logger** is essential for M&V projects — captures 15-minute interval data for at least 7 days. - ☐ **Units to remember:** $1 \, kWh = 3.6 \, MJ = 860 \, kcal$; $1 \, TR = 3.517 \, kW$ (cooling).

topics.title: Defining Monitoring and Targeting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Defining Monitoring and Targeting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Monitoring and Targeting (M&T)** is a structured, systematic management technique used in energy management to continuously **measure, record, analyse, and report** energy consumption data against pre-determined **energy performance targets** (baselines), with the explicit objective of **identifying deviations**, **controlling wastage**, and **driving continuous improvement** in energy efficiency.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, M&T is positioned as the **operational backbone** of the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** energy management cycle. It bridges the strategic planning of energy policy with the on-the-ground reality of plant operations.

### Key Lexicon (Board-Examiner Favourites)

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Baseline (Reference Year)** | The historical, normalized energy consumption pattern against which current performance is compared. |
| **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** | A quantified metric (e.g., kWh/ton, MJ/kg, MWh/month) that expresses energy use relative to a normalizing variable. |
| **CUSUM** | **CU**mulative **SUM** of deviations of actual energy from the expected (baseline) value. |
| **Normalization** | Adjustment of actual energy use to standard reference conditions (temperature, humidity, production quality, etc.). |
| **Variance** | The algebraic difference between **Actual Consumption** and **Expected (Targeted) Consumption**. |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Calorie Tracker for a Factory"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Personal Diet Monitoring**
> Imagine you go on a diet. You write down everything you eat (Monitoring) and set a daily calorie limit of **2,000 kcal** (the Target). At the end of each week, you compare what you actually ate vs your budget. If you overshot by 500 kcal three weeks in a row, you know something is wrong — maybe you forgot to count snacks. You adjust your behaviour (Corrective Action) and track again next week.
>
> **M&T does EXACTLY this for a factory's energy bills.** The factory sets an "energy budget" in MWh, records actual meter readings, computes the deviation (variance), investigates causes, and corrects them.

### The Factory ↔ Human Body Mapping

| Factory Equivalent | Human Body Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Electricity / Fuel Bill | Calorie Intake |
| Production Output (tons) | Steps Walked / Work Done |
| Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) | Calorie Efficiency (kcal/km) |
| Energy Manager | Dietician |
| M&T Report | Weekly Food Diary Review |

### Why Does M&T Exist? (The Core Problem It Solves)

Without M&T, an Energy Manager only learns about energy waste **when the electricity bill arrives at month-end** — by then it is too late to act. M&T converts this **reactive, lagging** view into a **proactive, leading, near real-time** dashboard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Statistics:** As per the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) India, industries that successfully implement M&T systems typically achieve **5–15%** energy savings within the first 12 months, often with **zero or minimal capital investment** — purely by behavioural and operational corrections identified through monitoring data.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of M&T

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          THE THREE PILLARS OF M&T                    │
│                                                      │
│   1. MONITOR  →  2. TARGET  →  3. REPORT/ACT         │
│        ↑                                          ↓  │
│        └──────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                (Continuous Loop)                     │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The M&T Pyramid — Hierarchy of Monitoring

Energy monitoring is not a single activity; it is layered. KTU examiners love asking this hierarchy.

```
                    ┌────────────────────────┐
                    │   Level 1: Strategic   │  ← Annual, Crore-level
                    │   (Monthly MWh Total)  │
                    ├────────────────────────┤
                    │  Level 2: Tactical     │  ← Weekly, Departmental
                    │  (Dept. EnPI Tracking) │
                    ├────────────────────────┤
                    │  Level 3: Operational  │  ← Daily, Equipment-level
                    │  (Equipment SEC)       │
                    ├────────────────────────┤
                    │  Level 4: Diagnostic   │  ← Hourly / per-shift
                    │  (Load Profile / CUSUM)│
                    └────────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Higher-level monitoring is *less frequent* but *more aggregated*. Lower-level monitoring is *frequent* but *narrow in scope*. A robust M&T system needs **all four levels** to function effectively.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Step-by-Step Logic of an M&T Programme

### Step 1 — Define the System Boundary

Decide **what** you are measuring: a single boiler, an HVAC chiller, a forging shop, or the entire plant.

### Step 2 — Identify the EnPI

Choose an Energy Performance Indicator that **links energy to a driver variable** (production, degree-days, occupants).

- For a compressor: $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{\text{kWh}}{\text{m}^3 \text{ of compressed air}}$
- For a foundry: $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{ton of liquid metal}}$

### Step 3 — Collect Baseline Data (Minimum 12 Months)

Collect **at least one full year** of data to capture seasonal variation (heating/cooling loads vary with ambient temperature).

### Step 4 — Build the Baseline Equation (Regression)

Fit a line: $E_{expected} = b + m \cdot x$

where $x$ is the driver (e.g., production in tons/month) and $b, m$ are regression coefficients.

### Step 5 — Set Targets

Targets are typically set at **2–5% below** the baseline line (savings achievable through housekeeping & low-cost measures).

### Step 6 — Monitor Continuously

Record actual energy $E_{actual}$ at the chosen frequency.

### Step 7 — Compute Variance

$\Delta E = E_{actual} - E_{expected}$

A **positive** $\Delta E$ means **overconsumption** (bad); a **negative** $\Delta E$ means **savings achieved** (good).

### Step 8 — CUSUM Analysis (for Trend Detection)

Even small monthly variances can hide a creeping trend. CUSUM amplifies small consistent deviations.

### Step 9 — Investigate & Act

Trigger a **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** whenever CUSUM slope changes sign.

### Step 10 — Re-baseline (Annually)

Once savings are confirmed permanent, the baseline shifts down, and a new cycle begins.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains EVERY formula you need for M&T numerical problems. Memorize these.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{E_{consumed}}{P_{output}}$ | Specific Energy Consumption | $\text{MJ/kg}, \text{kWh/ton}$ |
| 2 | $E_{expected} = b + m \cdot x$ | Linear regression baseline | $\text{MWh}$ |
| 3 | $\Delta E = E_{actual} - E_{expected}$ | Energy Variance | $\text{MWh}$ |
| 4 | $\text{CUSUM}_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (E_{actual,i} - E_{expected,i})$ | Cumulative Sum of variance | $\text{MWh}$ |
| 5 | $\text{CUSUM Slope} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{CUSUM}}{\Delta t}$ | Rate of deviation change | $\text{MWh/month}$ |
| 6 | $E_{corrected} = E_{actual} \cdot \dfrac{T_{ref} - T_{outside}}{T_{actual} - T_{outside}}$ | Degree-day normalization | $\text{kWh}$ |
| 7 | $\% \text{ Savings} = \dfrac{E_{baseline} - E_{actual}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$ | Percentage savings | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{EUI} = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{\text{Floor Area}}$ | Energy Use Intensity (buildings) | $\text{kWh/m}^2$ |
| 9 | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m \cdot h_{steam}}{E_{input}} \times 100$ | Boiler efficiency | % |
| 10 | $E_{saved} = \text{SEC}_{old} \cdot P_{new} - \text{SEC}_{new} \cdot P_{new}$ | Energy saved per unit | kWh |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** In KTU answer scripts, **never confuse** $E$ (Energy) with $e$ (efficiency). Examiners explicitly test this in Part A questions.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steel Plants:** M&T tracks kWh/ton of crude steel across Electric Arc Furnace (EAF) heats.
- **HVAC in Commercial Buildings:** Degree-day normalization separates weather-driven consumption from efficiency changes.
- **Compressed Air Systems:** $\text{SEC} = \text{kWh/m}^3$ flags leaks (rising trend without rising demand).
- **Cement Industry:** $\text{kCal/kg of clinker}$ is the most-watched KPI worldwide.

---

## 2.5 Types of Monitoring — Board-Exam Distinction

| Type | Frequency | Tools | Cost |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Walk-through Audit Monitoring** | Daily visual | Checklists, logbooks | Very Low |
| **Sub-metering Monitoring** | Hourly | Digital energy meters, EMS | Moderate |
| **SCADA-based Monitoring** | Real-time | Sensors + PLC + SCADA | High |
| **IoT / Cloud-based Monitoring** | Real-time | Smart sensors, MQTT, cloud dashboards | Moderate-High |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Linear Baseline Equation via Least Squares Regression

The most fundamental mathematical tool in M&T is the **simple linear regression** baseline. Let us derive it rigorously.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Suppose we collect $n$ pairs of data $(x_i, y_i)$ where:

- $x_i$ = production output (tons) in month $i$
- $y_i$ = actual energy consumed (MWh) in month $i$

We want to fit a straight line $y = b + m x$ such that the **sum of squared vertical deviations** is minimized.

### 3.1.2 The Least-Squares Objective Function

Define the residual at each point as:

$$r_i = y_i - (b + m x_i)$$

The total squared error (cost function) is:

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y_i - (b + m x_i) \right]^2$$

### 3.1.3 Minimization (Take Partial Derivatives)

To minimize $S$ with respect to $b$ and $m$, set the partial derivatives to zero.

**Step 1 — Partial derivative with respect to $b$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial S}{\partial b} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} 2 \cdot \left[ y_i - (b + m x_i) \right] \cdot (-1) \\
&= -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y_i - b - m x_i \right] = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives **Equation (A):**

$$\sum y_i = n b + m \sum x_i$$

**Step 2 — Partial derivative with respect to $m$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial S}{\partial m} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} 2 \cdot \left[ y_i - (b + m x_i) \right] \cdot (-x_i) \\
&= -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \left[ y_i - b - m x_i \right] = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives **Equation (B):**

$$\sum x_i y_i = b \sum x_i + m \sum x_i^2$$

### 3.1.4 The Two Normal Equations

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(A)} \quad & n b + m \sum x_i = \sum y_i \\
\text{(B)} \quad & b \sum x_i + m \sum x_i^2 = \sum x_i y_i
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Closed-Form Solution

Solving the 2×2 linear system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \dfrac{n \sum x_i y_i - \left( \sum x_i \right) \left( \sum y_i \right)}{n \sum x_i^2 - \left( \sum x_i \right)^2} \\
b &= \dfrac{\sum y_i - m \sum x_i}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

This pair $(b, m)$ defines the **M&T Baseline Equation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of $b$ and $m$:**
> - $m$ = **marginal energy** (extra MWh needed per extra ton of production). Called the **process energy intensity**.
> - $b$ = **fixed energy** (lighting, HVAC, standby losses — independent of production). Called the **base load**.
> - Total expected energy $E_{expected} = b + m \cdot x$ = Fixed + Variable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: CUSUM Construction — A Worked Numerical Walk-Through

CUSUM is the **detective tool** of M&T. Let us build it step by step.

### Given Data (12 Months of a Compressor Station)

| Month | Production $x_i$ (1000 m³) | Actual $E_i$ (MWh) | Baseline $b + m x_i$ (MWh) | Variance $V_i = E_i - E_{exp}$ | CUSUM $C_n = \sum V_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 120 | 88 | 90.0 | $-2.0$ | $-2.0$ |
| 2 | 130 | 95 | 95.0 | $0.0$ | $-2.0$ |
| 3 | 140 | 102 | 100.0 | $+2.0$ | $0.0$ |
| 4 | 110 | 85 | 85.0 | $0.0$ | $0.0$ |
| 5 | 115 | 86 | 87.5 | $-1.5$ | $-1.5$ |
| 6 | 125 | 93 | 92.5 | $+0.5$ | $-1.0$ |
| 7 | 135 | 100 | 97.5 | $+2.5$ | $+1.5$ |
| 8 | 140 | 103 | 100.0 | $+3.0$ | $+4.5$ |
| 9 | 130 | 96 | 95.0 | $+1.0$ | $+5.5$ |
| 10 | 120 | 90 | 90.0 | $0.0$ | $+5.5$ |
| 11 | 110 | 84 | 85.0 | $-1.0$ | $+4.5$ |
| 12 | 125 | 94 | 92.5 | $+1.5$ | $+6.0$ |

### Step-by-Step CUSUM Calculation

**Step 1:** Compute Expected Energy using the baseline $E_{exp,i} = b + m x_i$.
We are using (assumed) baseline: $E_{exp} = 30 + 0.5 x_i$.

**Step 2:** Compute Variance for each month:

$$V_i = E_{actual,i} - E_{expected,i}$$

For Month 1: $V_1 = 88 - 90 = -2.0$
For Month 3: $V_3 = 102 - 100 = +2.0$
... (as shown in the table).

**Step 3:** Compute CUSUM as the running cumulative sum:

$$C_n = C_{n-1} + V_n, \quad C_0 = 0$$

**Step 4:** Observe the CUSUM trend:

- Months 1–6: $C_n$ hovers around 0 → **no systematic drift**.
- Months 7–9: $C_n$ rises sharply from $+1.5$ to $+5.5$ → **clear upward drift** in consumption.
- **Conclusion:** Between Month 6 and Month 9, a leak or efficiency degradation likely began (investigate compressor valves, inter-coolers, or air leaks).

### Mathematical Summary of CUSUM

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_n &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (E_i - E_{exp,i}) \\
    &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} (b + m x_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

The **slope** of the CUSUM line gives the **average drift per month**:

$$\text{Slope} = \frac{C_{final} - C_{initial}}{n_{months}}$$

If the slope $> 0$ persistently, the process is **drifting towards overconsumption** — energy audit is mandatory.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Degree-Day Normalization for HVAC Buildings

For buildings, production is not a driver — **weather** is. We must **normalize** to a standard climate.

### 3.3.1 Definition of Heating Degree Days (HDD)

$$\text{HDD} = \sum_{d=1}^{365} \max(0,\ T_{base} - T_{outside,d})$$

where $T_{base}$ is typically **18.3 °C** (65 °F) for buildings.

### 3.3.2 Normalization Formula

$$E_{corrected} = E_{actual} \cdot \frac{\text{HDD}_{standard}}{\text{HDD}_{actual}}$$

**Worked Example:** A building consumed **150,000 kWh** in a year with **HDD = 2,400**. The standard year for the city has **HDD = 2,000**. Compute normalized energy.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{corrected} &= 150{,}000 \cdot \frac{2{,}000}{2{,}400} \\
             &= 150{,}000 \cdot 0.8333 \\
             &= 125{,}000 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** If the building had experienced an "average" winter, it would have consumed **125,000 kWh**. The extra 25,000 kWh is due to a **colder-than-normal** year, not necessarily inefficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** Always normalize **before** computing % savings. Otherwise your savings number is contaminated by weather noise.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which M&T Tool

| Situation | Recommended Tool | Why |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Stable, mature process with linear $E$–$x$ relation | Simple Linear Regression Baseline | Easiest to interpret and explain to management |
| Process with multiple drivers (e.g., $T$, humidity, occupancy) | Multiple Linear Regression | Captures combined effects |
| Small monthly variances but persistent drift suspected | CUSUM | Amplifies small signals |
| Equipment runs intermittently with long idle periods | Energy Signature Model (ESM) | Handles on/off operation |
| Building with strong weather dependence | Degree-Day Normalization | Removes weather noise |
| Complex plant with dozens of streams | Energy Balance + Sankey Diagram | Visualizes all flows at once |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Complete M&T Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Define System Boundary"] --> B["Identify Energy Performance Indicator EnPI"]
    B --> C["Collect Baseline Data 12 Months Minimum"]
    C --> D["Build Regression Equation E exp = b + m x"]
    D --> E["Set Energy Target 2 to 5 percent below Baseline"]
    E --> F["Monitor Actual Energy via Meters"]
    F --> G{"Compute Variance Delta E"}
    G --> H["Variance near zero"]
    G --> I["Variance positive overconsumption"]
    I --> J["Run CUSUM Analysis"]
    J --> K{"CUSUM slope positive persistently"}
    K --> L["Trigger Root Cause Analysis and Audit"]
    L --> M["Implement Corrective Action"]
    M --> N["Verify Savings for 3 to 6 Months"]
    N --> O["Re-baseline and Update Targets"]
    O --> F
    H --> F
    K --> F
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Hierarchical Energy Monitoring Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["LEVEL 1 STRATEGIC Monthly Plant Total MWh"] --> L2["LEVEL 2 TACTICAL Weekly Departmental EnPI"]
    L2 --> L3["LEVEL 3 OPERATIONAL Daily Equipment SEC"]
    L3 --> L4["LEVEL 4 DIAGNOSTIC Hourly Load Profile and CUSUM"]
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Data Flow in an M&T System (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Field Sensors Temperature Pressure Flow"] --> A2["Data Acquisition System DAQ"]
    A3["Utility Meters Electricity Steam Fuel"] --> A2
    A2 --> B1["Data Logger / Historian"]
    B1 --> B2["Cloud Server or Local EMS"]
    B2 --> C1["Baseline Engine Regression Module"]
    B2 --> C2["CUSUM Engine"]
    C1 --> D1["M and T Dashboard"]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1["Daily Email Report"]
    D1 --> E2["Weekly MIS to Energy Manager"]
    D1 --> E3["Monthly Top Management Review"]
    E3 --> F1["Corrective Action Trigger"]
    F1 --> A1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — CUSUM Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start of Month Collect Actual Energy and Production"] --> S2["Compute Expected Energy from Baseline"]
    S2 --> S3["Compute Monthly Variance V = Actual minus Expected"]
    S3 --> S4["Add V to Previous CUSUM C n = C n minus 1 plus V"]
    S4 --> S5{"Is C n above Upper Control Limit"}
    S5 -- YES --> S6["ALARM Overconsumption detected Issue Audit Ticket"]
    S5 -- NO --> S7{"Is C n below Lower Control Limit"}
    S7 -- YES --> S8["Examine if Savings are Genuine Lock in Gains"]
    S7 -- NO --> S9["Continue Monitoring"]
    S9 --> S1
    S6 --> S1
    S8 --> S1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define Monitoring and Targeting (M&T) as applied to industrial energy management. List any **four** essential elements of an M&T system.

**Model Answer:**

> **Monitoring and Targeting (M&T)** is a systematic energy management technique that involves the **continuous measurement, recording, and analysis** of energy consumption data against pre-determined energy targets (baselines), with the objective of identifying deviations, controlling wastage, and improving energy efficiency.

**Four Essential Elements of an M&T System:**

1. **Energy Metering Infrastructure** — Sub-meters at strategic points.
2. **Data Collection and Logging System** — Daily/hourly recording.
3. **Baseline / Target Reference** — A regression model or standard value.
4. **Reporting and Action Mechanism** — Variance analysis + corrective action workflow.

*(Other acceptable elements: EnPI definition, CUSUM tracking, management review, re-baselining protocol.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is a **CUSUM chart**? State its significance in M&T.

**Model Answer:**

A **CUSUM (Cumulative Sum) chart** is a graphical tool that plots the **running cumulative sum of deviations** between actual energy consumption and expected (targeted) consumption over successive time periods.

$$C_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(E_{actual,i} - E_{expected,i})$$

**Significance in M&T:**

- **Detects small but persistent drifts** that monthly bar charts hide.
- A **rising slope** indicates creeping overconsumption (e.g., leaks, fouling).
- A **falling slope** indicates sustained savings.
- Provides **early warning** — investigation can begin before the annual bill surges.
- Acts as a **statistically robust** alternative to Shewhart control charts in energy applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE PROVIDED — ANSWER ANY ONE FULL QUESTION**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
