<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boiler (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A boiler is a closed pressure vessel in which a fluid (commonly water) is heated under pressure by the combustion of fuel or by the application of heat from a hot fluid. The resulting steam or hot vapor is then used externally to drive work in industrial, commercial, or power generation processes.

From an **Energy Management** standpoint, a boiler is classified as a **Thermal Utility** because it is a centralized equipment unit that converts chemical energy of a fuel into thermal energy (in the form of steam or hot water) for distribution to multiple end-use points. The efficiency of this conversion is the single largest determinant of plant-wide fuel economy in energy-intensive industries such as textiles, paper, chemicals, refineries, food processing, and captive power.

**Boiler Efficiency ($\eta_{boiler}$)** is formally defined as the ratio of the useful heat absorbed by the working fluid (water/steam) to the heat released by the fuel combustion, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta_{boiler} = \frac{Q_{absorbed\,by\,fluid}}{Q_{released\,by\,fuel}} \times 100\,\%$$

Two universally accepted measurement methodologies exist:
1. **Direct (Input-Output) Method** — also called the Heat Output Method
2. **Indirect (Heat Loss) Method** — also called the Energy-Loss Method

> [!NOTE]
> As per the **ASME PTC 4 (Performance Test Code)** standard and the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) – India** guidelines adopted in KTU references, both methods must agree within **±0.5 %** during a valid boiler performance test.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Pressure Cooker Model

Imagine boiling rice in a household pressure cooker on a gas stove. The **burner (combustion zone)** releases chemical energy from LPG. Of this energy, the rice and water absorb some (useful heat), some escapes through the lid handle and outer surface (radiation and surface loss), and the remainder is lost when the whistle releases steam (flue/stack loss analogue). A good pressure cooker (akin to a well-tuned boiler) extracts a higher share of energy into the rice — that extracted fraction is precisely the **boiler efficiency**.

Now scale this thought experiment to industrial scale: a 10 TPH (tonnes per hour) boiler running for 8000 hours annually and burning coal. A mere **1 % improvement in efficiency** can save approximately **1500–2000 tonnes of coal per year**, translating to multi-crore INR savings — the exact scale of impact energy auditors look for.

### 1.3 Classification of Boilers (Foundation for Efficiency Discussion)

| Category | Sub-Types | Efficiency Range (Typical) |
|---|---|---|
| Fire-tube | Cochran, Lancashire, Cornish, Locomotive | 60–75 % |
| Water-tube | Babcock-Wilcox, Stirling, D-type | 75–88 % |
| Packaged | 3-pass wet-back, 4-pass | 78–86 % |
| Fluidized Bed Combustion (FBC) | Circulating, Bubbling | 82–90 % |
| Waste Heat Recovery | CO boiler, WHRS | 70–92 % (input basis) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Metric (KTU 2024 Highlight):** Modern high-pressure water-tube boilers operate at efficiencies between **80 % and 90 %** while older shell-type fire-tube boilers often operate as low as **55 % to 65 %**. The efficiency gap is the target zone for energy conservation interventions.

### 1.4 Why Boiler Efficiency Matters — The Fuel Cost Pyramid

> [!NOTE]
> **The 1 % Rule of Thumb:** Across most KTU-referenced industrial case studies, fuel cost represents **60 % to 75 %** of total operating cost in steam-intensive plants. Therefore, a 1 % boiler efficiency improvement typically delivers a **0.7 % to 1.0 % reduction** in total plant operating cost — making boilers the *highest-payback* energy management intervention in any facility.

Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) tracked by energy auditors:
- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** = Fuel used (kg) / Steam generated (kg) — target: 0.06 to 0.08 kg of coal per kg of steam for modern bagasse/coal-fired boilers.
- **Excess Air Percentage** — target: 15 % to 25 % for solid fuels, 5 % to 10 % for gaseous fuels.
- **Stack Temperature** — target: 60 °C to 120 °C above saturation (i.e., ~150–200 °C absolute) for economizer-equipped units.
- **Boiler Blow-down Rate** — target: 5 % to 8 % for low-pressure boilers, <2 % with condensate recovery.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Two Methods of Boiler Efficiency Determination

#### 2.1.1 Direct (Input-Output) Method

$$\eta_{direct} = \frac{m_{steam} \times (h_{steam} - h_{feedwater})}{m_{fuel} \times GCV_{fuel}} \times 100\,\%$$

Where:
- $m_{steam}$ = mass flow rate of steam produced (kg/hr)
- $h_{steam}$ = enthalpy of steam leaving the boiler (kJ/kg)
- $h_{feedwater}$ = enthalpy of feedwater entering the boiler (kJ/kg)
- $m_{fuel}$ = mass flow rate of fuel consumed (kg/hr)
- $GCV_{fuel}$ = Gross Calorific Value of fuel (kJ/kg)

**Operational Reality:** The direct method appears simple but suffers from measurement compounding — small errors in fuel mass flow multiply into large efficiency errors due to the typically large $GCV_{fuel}$ value in the denominator. It also fails to identify *where* losses are occurring, which is essential for energy auditing.

#### 2.1.2 Indirect (Heat Loss) Method

$$\eta_{indirect} = 100 - (L_{stack} + L_{rad} + L_{moisture} + L_{CO} + L_{unaccounted})$$

Where each loss term is expressed as a **percentage of fuel heat input**. This method is preferred in KTU 2024 energy auditing courses because it **diagnoses the loss distribution**, enabling targeted interventions.

### 2.2 Heat Loss Categories — The Five Standard Buckets

#### Bucket 1 — Stack / Flue Gas Loss ($L_{stack}$)
This is the **largest single loss** in almost every boiler and accounts for **6 % to 25 %** of input energy. The loss arises because combustion gases leave the system at elevated temperature, carrying away sensible heat.

For fuel-fired boilers using proximate analysis (coal/coke/biomass), the empirical IS 8753 / BEE formula is:

$$L_{stack} = \frac{m_{flue} \times C_{p,flue} \times (T_{flue} - T_{ambient})}{GCV_{fuel}} \times 100\,\%$$

A more compact form using excess-air and flue-gas oxygen:

$$L_{stack} = K \times \frac{(O_2\%)}{(21 - O_2\%)} \times \frac{(T_{flue} - T_{ambient})}{GCV_{fuel}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stack Loss — Key Insight:** Every **22 °C drop in flue gas temperature** translates to approximately **1 % efficiency gain** for typical coal-fired boilers. This is the foundational logic behind economizers, air preheaters, and waste heat recovery units.

#### Bucket 2 — Heat Loss Due to Moisture in Fuel ($L_{moisture}$)
Water in fuel and water formed from hydrogen combustion both consume latent heat to vaporize:

$$L_{moisture} = \frac{m_{fuel} \times [M \times h_{fg} + 9 \times H \times h_{fg}]}{GCV_{fuel}} \times 100\,\%$$

Where $M$ = moisture fraction, $H$ = hydrogen fraction by mass, $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization (≈ 2260 kJ/kg at 100 °C), and 9 is the stoichiometric mass ratio of water produced per kg of hydrogen burned.

#### Bucket 3 — Heat Loss Due to Combustion of Hydrogen ($L_{hydrogen}$)
Often combined with Bucket 2 because both relate to water formation:

$$L_{H_2O} = 9 \times H \times \left[ h_{fg} + C_{p,steam} \times (T_{flue} - 100) \right] \times \frac{m_{fuel}}{GCV_{fuel}} \times 100\,\%$$

#### Bucket 4 — Loss Due to Incomplete Combustion — CO Formation ($L_{CO}$)
When combustion air is insufficient or mixing is poor, carbon forms CO instead of $\text{CO}_2$, releasing only ~30 % of the available carbon energy:

$$L_{CO} = \frac{m_{fuel} \times C \times \left(\frac{CO}{CO_ + CO_2}\right) \times 23820}{GCV_{fuel}} \times 100\,\%$$

Where 23820 kJ/kg is the heat of combustion difference between C→CO₂ (full) and C→CO (partial).

#### Bucket 5 — Radiation & Surface Loss ($L_{rad}$)
Heat lost from the boiler's external hot surfaces to the surroundings. Typically **1 % to 2 %** for small boilers and **0.5 % to 1 %** for large well-insulated units. Estimated via surface area, surface temperature, and ambient conditions (uses Stefan-Boltzmann-type convective + radiative correlations).

#### Bucket 6 — Unaccounted Losses
The balancing residual — typically **0.5 % to 2 %** — includes blowdown heat, convection losses from ducts, and measurement uncertainties.

### 2.3 The Excess Air — Efficiency Tradeoff Curve

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Engineering Insight (KTU 2024 Module 4 Pillar):** Excess air exhibits a **U-shaped efficiency curve**. Too little excess air causes CO losses to surge. Too much excess air dilutes the flame and sends more mass through the stack, raising stack losses. The optimum is fuel-specific.

| Fuel Type | Theoretical Min. Air | Optimum Excess Air | Stack Loss at Optimum |
|---|---|---|---|
| Natural Gas | ~0 % | 5–10 % | 4–8 % |
| Furnace Oil / LSHS | ~0 % | 10–15 % | 6–10 % |
| Bituminous Coal | ~0 % | 15–25 % | 8–14 % |
| Bagasse / Biomass | ~0 % | 25–35 % | 10–18 % |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Boiler Efficiency

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are framed as direct exam-ready lines. Note: vertical bars for absolute values use $\vert$ instead of $\vert$ or $\mid$ to preserve markdown compatibility.

| # | Formula | Description / Variables |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{direct} = \dfrac{m_s (h_s - h_{fw})}{m_f \cdot GCV_f} \times 100$ | Input-Output method; subscript $s$=steam, $fw$=feedwater, $f$=fuel |
| 2 | $\eta_{indirect} = 100 - \sum L_i$ | Heat-loss method; sum of all five loss percentages |
| 3 | $L_{stack} = K \cdot \dfrac{O_2}{21 - O_2} \cdot \dfrac{(T_{flue} - T_{amb})}{GCV}$ | Oxygen-based stack loss; $K$ = fuel constant |
| 4 | $m_{air\,req} = m_{fuel} \cdot \left(\dfrac{11.6 \cdot C + 34.8 \cdot (H - O/8) + 4.35 \cdot S}{100}\right)$ | Stoichiometric air demand (kg/kg fuel); for solid fuel ultimate analysis |
| 5 | $\% Excess\,Air = \dfrac{21}{21 - O_2\,(dry)} - 1$ or $\dfrac{O_2}{20.9 - O_2\,(dry)}$ | From dry-flue-gas oxygen measurement |
| 6 | $A/F\,ratio = \dfrac{kg\,air}{kg\,fuel}$ | Air-to-Fuel ratio; ranges 6–18 for common fuels |
| 7 | $SFC = \dfrac{Fuel\,consumption}{Steam\,generation}\,(kg/kg)$ | Specific Fuel Consumption indicator |
| 8 | $Q_{blowdown} = m_{bd} \cdot C_{p,w} \cdot (T_{bd} - T_{makeup})$ | Heat lost in continuous blowdown |
| 9 | $Q_{econ} = \dot{m}_{flue} \cdot C_{p,g} \cdot (T_{flue,in} - T_{flue,out})$ | Heat recoverable in economizer |
| 10 | $\eta_{combustion} = \dfrac{GCV - L_{CO} - L_{H_2}}{GCV} \times 100$ | Combustion efficiency (excluding stack loss) |

### 2.5 Combustion Stoichiometry — Foundational Support

For complete combustion of a hydrocarbon fuel with ultimate analysis (C, H, O, S, N), the theoretical oxygen requirement per kg of fuel is:

$$O_2\,(kg/kg) = \frac{32}{12}C + \frac{32}{4}H + \frac{32}{32}S - \frac{32}{32}O = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O$$

The theoretical air requirement is obtained by multiplying oxygen demand by $\dfrac{100}{23}$ (mass ratio of air to oxygen).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** These stoichiometric relations are the backbone of excess-air optimization, which in turn controls the stack loss — the single biggest lever in any boiler energy conservation project.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility Across Industries

- **Cement & Lime:** Captive power via AFBC boilers; each 1 % efficiency drop costs crores.
- **Pulp & Paper:** Recovery boilers burn black liquor; efficiency dictates steam export economics.
- **Textiles:** Coal/biomass boilers supply process steam; stack loss minimization is a continuous mandate.
- **Food Processing:** Saturated steam for sterilization — direct efficiency measurement dominates due to clean fuel.
- **Refineries:** CO boilers in FCC units recover catalyst regenerator heat; efficiency of WHRS is measured on input basis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Indirect Efficiency — Stack Loss Component (Oxygen Method)

This is the most heavily tested derivation in KTU Module 4 boiler questions.

#### Step 1 — Establish the dry flue gas composition

When excess air is supplied, dry flue gas contains $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{O}_2$, $\text{N}_2$ (and trace $\text{SO}_2$). The mass of dry flue gas per kg of fuel for a hydrocarbon with negligible sulfur is:

$$m_{dry\,flue} = \frac{11 \cdot C + 34.5 \cdot (H - O/8) + 8 \cdot N + (A_{excess} \cdot 0.232 \cdot 23/100)}{1} \cdot m_{fuel}$$

In simplified empirical form, the mass of dry flue gas is approximated for coal-type fuels:

$$m_{flue} = \frac{(1 + A_{excess}) \cdot \alpha}{GCV}$$

where $\alpha$ is a fuel-dependent proportionality constant. For an engineering-grade estimate, we use the heat-loss approach directly.

#### Step 2 — Relate flue gas quantity to oxygen concentration

For a fixed carbon-hydrogen ratio, the volumetric fraction of oxygen in dry flue gas is inversely related to the excess air ratio. By mass balance on nitrogen (which acts as a tracer):

$$\frac{N_2\,in\,flue}{O_2\,in\,flue} = \frac{0.79 \cdot (1 + EA)}{EA} \quad\text{(volume basis)}$$

Using this relation, the mass of dry flue gas per kg of fuel can be expressed as:

$$m_{flue} = K_f \cdot \frac{1}{21 - O_2\,(\%)}$$

where $K_f$ is a fuel-specific constant (units: kg-flue per kg-fuel × percent O₂).

#### Step 3 — Sensible heat in flue gas

The enthalpy carried by dry flue gas leaving at $T_{flue}$ above ambient $T_{amb}$:

$$Q_{flue} = m_{flue} \cdot C_{p,flue} \cdot (T_{flue} - T_{amb})$$

Substituting $m_{flue}$ from Step 2:

$$Q_{flue} = K_f \cdot C_{p,flue} \cdot \frac{(T_{flue} - T_{amb})}{21 - O_2\,(\%)}$$

#### Step 4 — Normalize by fuel heat input

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{stack}\,(\%) &= \frac{Q_{flue}}{GCV_{fuel}} \times 100 \\
&= \underbrace{\left[\frac{K_f \cdot C_{p,flue}}{GCV_{fuel}} \times 100\right]}_{\text{Constant }K} \cdot \frac{(T_{flue} - T_{amb})}{21 - O_2\,(\%)}
\end{aligned}$$

This yields the exam-ready equation:

$$L_{stack} = K \cdot \frac{(T_{flue} - T_{amb})}{21 - O_2\,(\%)}$$

For Indian coal, the empirical constant $K \approx 0.65$ to $0.85$ — commonly taken as **0.7** in KTU problems.

### 3.2 Full Worked Example — Indirect Efficiency Calculation (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A coal-fired boiler has the following operating data:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Coal feed rate | 1000 kg/hr |
| GCV of coal | 25,000 kJ/kg |
| Flue gas temperature | 220 °C |
| Ambient temperature | 30 °C |
| Dry flue gas O₂ | 6 % |
| CO in flue gas | 0.5 % |
| Moisture in coal (M) | 8 % |
| Hydrogen in coal (H) | 4 % |
| Carbon in coal (C) | 50 % |

Assume $C_{p,flue} = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K, latent heat of vaporization $h_{fg} = 2260$ kJ/kg.

**Calculate the boiler efficiency by the Indirect (Heat Loss) Method.**

#### Step 1 — Stack Loss

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{stack}\,(\%) &= K \cdot \frac{(T_{flue} - T_{amb})}{21 - O_2\,(\%)} \\
&= 0.7 \cdot \frac{(220 - 30)}{(21 - 6)} \\
&= 0.7 \cdot \frac{190}{15} \\
&= 0.7 \cdot 12.667 \\
&= 8.87\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 2 — Loss Due to Moisture and Hydrogen Combustion

The total water vapor produced per kg of coal:
$$\text{Water} = \frac{M}{100} + 9 \cdot \frac{H}{100} = 0.08 + 9 \cdot 0.04 = 0.08 + 0.36 = 0.44\,kg/kg$$

Heat lost per kg of coal to vaporize and superheat this water (assume vapor leaves at $T_{flue}=220\,°C$):

$$\begin{aligned}
q_{H_2O} &= 0.44 \cdot \left[h_{fg} + C_{p,steam} \cdot (T_{flue} - 100)\right] \\
&= 0.44 \cdot \left[2260 + 2.1 \cdot (220 - 100)\right] \\
&= 0.44 \cdot \left[2260 + 2.1 \cdot 120\right] \\
&= 0.44 \cdot \left[2260 + 252\right] \\
&= 0.44 \cdot 2512 \\
&= 1105.28\,kJ/kg\,coal
\end{aligned}$$

As percentage of fuel input:
$$L_{moisture + H_2} = \frac{1105.28}{25000} \times 100 = 4.42\,\%$$

#### Step 3 — Loss Due to Incomplete Combustion (CO Formation)

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{CO}\,(\%) &= \frac{C \cdot \left(\dfrac{CO}{CO + CO_2}\right) \cdot 23820}{GCV} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

Assuming the remaining carbon forms $\text{CO}_2$ such that $CO + CO_2 = $ fixed fraction of total C. For typical values, if all 50 % carbon goes to either CO or $\text{CO}_2$ in ratio $0.5 : 99.5$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{CO}{CO + CO_2} &= \frac{0.5}{100} = 0.005 \\
L_{CO}\,(\%) &= \frac{0.50 \cdot 0.005 \cdot 23820}{25000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{59.55}{25000} \times 100 \\
&= 0.238\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 4 — Radiation & Surface Loss

For a medium-sized industrial boiler, take $L_{rad} = 1.5\,\%$.

#### Step 5 — Unaccounted Loss

Take $L_{unaccounted} = 0.5\,\%$.

#### Step 6 — Total Losses and Indirect Efficiency

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum L_i &= L_{stack} + L_{moisture+H_2} + L_{CO} + L_{rad} + L_{unaccounted} \\
&= 8.87 + 4.42 + 0.238 + 1.5 + 0.5 \\
&= 15.528\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{indirect} &= 100 - 15.528 \\
&= 84.47\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** This falls within the expected 80 %–88 % range for well-tuned Indian coal-fired boilers, validating the calculation method.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Loss Categories to Interventions

| Loss Component | Typical % of Input | Root Cause | Energy Conservation Measure (ECM) | Payback (Years) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stack Loss | 8–25 % | High flue gas T, high excess air | Economizer, air preheater, O₂ trim control | 0.8–2.0 |
| Moisture + H₂ Loss | 3–6 % | Wet fuel, hydrogenous fuel | Fuel drying, coal preheating | 1.5–3.0 |
| CO Loss | 0.5–5 % | Insufficient air, poor mixing | Burner tuning, FGR (Flue Gas Recirculation) | 1.0–2.0 |
| Radiation Loss | 0.5–2 % | Uninsulated surfaces | Refractory lining, ceramic wool insulation | 1.5–4.0 |
| Blowdown Loss | 1–4 % | Excessive blowdown rate | Heat recovery flash tank, surface blowdown | 1.0–2.5 |
| Unaccounted | 0.5–2 % | Leakage, measurement error | Periodic maintenance, instrument calibration | — |

### 3.4 Derivation — Blowdown Heat Recovery Potential

Blowdown water exits at boiler pressure with enthalpy $h_{bd}$. Part of it flashes to steam at atmospheric pressure:

$$m_{flash} = m_{bd} \cdot \frac{(h_{bd} - h_{fw,\,sat})}{h_{fg,\,atm}}$$

This flash steam can be used for deaerator feed heating, saving equivalent fuel. The energy recovered per kg of blowdown is:

$$Q_{recovered} = m_{bd} \cdot \left[C_{p,w} \cdot (T_{bd} - T_{feed}) + x_{flash} \cdot h_{fg,\,atm}\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** For a 10 TPH boiler with 8 % blowdown at 10 bar (saturation T = 184 °C), the recoverable heat is approximately **0.4 to 0.5 GCal/hr**, equivalent to ~70 kg/hr of fuel oil savings.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Boiler Energy Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional energy-flow architecture of a typical industrial boiler with energy recovery attachments. Each node represents an equipment or transformation stage, and arrows indicate the dominant energy or mass flow direction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["FUEL IN"]:::input
    nodeB["COMBUSTION CHAMBER"]:::process
    nodeC["BOILER BANK WATER TUBE"]:::process
    nodeD["STEAM OUT TO PROCESS"]:::output
    nodeE["FLUE GAS TO ECONOMIZER"]:::flow
    nodeF["ECONOMIZER FEEDWATER PREHEAT"]:::process
    nodeG["FLUE GAS TO AIR PREHEATER"]:::flow
    nodeH["AIR PREHEATER COMBUSTION AIR"]:::process
    nodeI["FLUE GAS TO STACK"]:::output
    nodeJ["BLOWDOWN TO FLASH TANK"]:::flow
    nodeK["FLASH STEAM TO DEAERATOR"]:::recover
    nodeL["RADIATION AND CONVECTION LOSSES"]:::loss

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB -. Heat Loss .-> nodeL
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeJ
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeB
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeB
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeF

    classDef input fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef flow fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#4b0082,color:#000
    classDef recover fill:#d1f0d1,stroke:#228b22,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Light-yellow nodes represent fuel and air inputs; light-blue nodes are the main heat-transfer equipment; green nodes represent useful outputs; violet nodes represent internal energy flows that are recovered; red nodes represent unavoidable losses.

### 4.2 Indirect Efficiency Calculation Flowchart

The following diagram describes the logical procedure for determining boiler efficiency by the heat-loss method, as expected in KTU 2024 ESE answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Start: Collect Boiler Operating Data"]:::start
    stepB["Measure Flue Gas Temperature"]:::measure
    stepC["Measure Flue Gas Oxygen Percent"]:::measure
    stepD["Measure Carbon Monoxide Percent"]:::measure
    stepE["Determine Fuel Ultimate Analysis"]:::measure
    stepF["Calculate Stack Loss L1"]:::calc
    stepG["Calculate Moisture plus Hydrogen Loss L2"]:::calc
    stepH["Calculate Carbon Monoxide Loss L3"]:::calc
    stepI["Estimate Radiation Loss L4 from Surface Area"]:::calc
    stepJ["Compute Unaccounted Loss L5 by Balance"]:::calc
    stepK["Sum all Loss Components"]:::calc
    stepL["Efficiency equals 100 minus Sum of Losses"]:::result

    stepA --> stepB
    stepA --> stepC
    stepA --> stepD
    stepA --> stepE
    stepB --> stepF
    stepC --> stepF
    stepD --> stepH
    stepE --> stepG
    stepF --> stepK
    stepG --> stepK
    stepH --> stepK
    stepI --> stepK
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> stepL

    classDef start fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000
    classDef measure fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#4b0082,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Excess Air vs Boiler Efficiency — Conceptual Tradeoff Curve

The following Mermaid block diagram conceptually represents how excess air controls the dominant loss mechanisms, illustrating the U-shaped characteristic so critical to KTU Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    zoneA["Low Excess Air Zone"]:::zone
    zoneB["Optimum Excess Air Zone"]:::opt
    zoneC["High Excess Air Zone"]:::zone

    cause1["CO Loss Surges Incomplete Combustion"]:::cause1
    cause2["Balanced Stack and CO Losses Minimum Combined Loss"]:::cause2
    cause3["Stack Loss Dominates Heat Carried by Excess Air Mass"]:::cause3

    eff1["Efficiency Drops"]:::eff1
    eff2["Efficiency Maximum"]:::eff2
    eff3["Efficiency Drops Again"]:::eff3

    zoneA --> cause1
    zoneB --> cause2
    zoneC --> cause3

    cause1 --> eff1
    cause2 --> eff2
    cause3 --> eff3

    classDef zone fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef opt fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef cause1 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000
    classDef cause2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef cause3 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef eff1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef eff2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef eff3 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The center node represents the optimum operating zone. KTU examiners expect students to recognize this U-shaped behavior and explain *why* both extremes reduce efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]
**Define boiler efficiency. Briefly explain the indirect method of boiler efficiency determination.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Boiler efficiency is the ratio of useful heat absorbed by the working fluid (water/steam) to the heat released by the fuel, expressed as a percentage. The **indirect method** (also called the heat-loss method) determines efficiency by accounting for all major heat losses in the boiler — namely stack loss, moisture and hydrogen loss, CO loss due to incomplete combustion, radiation and surface loss, and unaccounted losses. The efficiency is then computed as **100 minus the sum of all loss percentages**. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing the five losses, 1 mark for the formula).

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]
**What is excess air? Why is it necessary, and what are its effects on boiler efficiency?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Excess air is the quantity of combustion air supplied **beyond the stoichiometric requirement** needed for complete combustion. It is necessary to ensure that all combustible elements (C, H, S) are fully oxidized, avoiding CO formation and smoke. (1 mark) However, excessive excess air cools the flame, increases flue gas mass flow, and elevates stack loss — thereby reducing boiler efficiency. There exists an optimum excess air percentage (typically 15–25 % for coal, 5–10 % for gas) at which combined losses are minimized. (1 mark for tradeoff, 1 mark for optimum values).

---

### PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[Internal Choice Mandate — KTU 2024 ESE Regulation]:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the various heat losses occurring in a coal-fired boiler. Explain any **two** in detail with their percentage contributions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 10 TPH coal-fired boiler operates with the following data. Calculate the boiler efficiency by the **indirect method**.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Coal GCV | 24,000 kJ/kg |
| Coal feed rate | 1500 kg/hr |
| Flue gas temperature | 250 °C |
| Ambient temperature | 35 °C |
| O₂ in dry flue gas | 7 % |
| CO in flue gas | 0.4 % |
| Moisture in coal (M) | 10 % |
| Hydrogen in coal (H) | 5 % |
| Carbon in coal (C) | 55 % |
| $C_{p,flue}$ | 1.05 kJ/kg·K |
| $h_{fg}$ | 2260 kJ/kg |

Assume radiation loss = 1.5 % and unaccounted loss = 0.5 %. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Heat Losses (7 marks):**

The major heat losses in a coal-fired boiler are:

1. **Stack / Flue Gas Loss (8 % to 25 %):** Sensible heat carried by dry flue gas and moisture leaving at elevated temperature. Depends on flue gas temperature and excess air. *Mitigation: Economizer, air preheater, O₂ trim control.* (2 marks)

2. **Loss Due to Moisture in Fuel and Hydrogen Combustion (3 % to 6 %):** Latent heat consumed to vaporize fuel moisture and water formed from hydrogen combustion. *Mitigation: Fuel drying.* (1.5 marks)

3. **Loss Due to CO Formation (0.5 % to 5 %):** Incomplete combustion releases only ~30 % of carbon energy when CO forms instead of CO₂. *Mitigation: Burner tuning, better mixing, FGR.* (1.5 marks)

4. **Radiation and Surface Loss (0.5 % to 2 %):** Heat lost through external hot surfaces. *Mitigation: Insulation, refractory lining.* (1 mark)

5. **Unaccounted Losses (0.5 % to 2 %):** Blowdown heat, duct losses, measurement error. (1 mark)

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Stack Loss** (1.5 marks)

$$L_{stack} = 0.7 \cdot \frac{(250 - 35)}{(21 - 7)} = 0.7 \cdot \frac{215}{14} = 0.7 \cdot 15.357 = 10.75\,\%$$

**Step 2: Moisture + Hydrogen Loss** (2 marks)

Water per kg coal:
$$\text{Water} = 0.10 + 9 \cdot 0.05 = 0.10 + 0.45 = 0.55\,kg$$

Heat lost:
$$\begin{aligned}
q &= 0.55 \cdot [2260 + 2.1 \cdot (250 - 100)] \\
&= 0.55 \cdot [2260 + 315] \\
&= 0.55 \cdot 2575 \\
&= 1416.25\,kJ/kg
\end{aligned}$$

Loss percentage:
$$L_{moisture + H_2} = \frac{1416.25}{24000} \times 100 = 5.90\,\%$$

**Step 3: CO Loss** (1.5 marks)

Assuming all carbon distributes between CO and CO₂:
$$L_{CO} = \frac{0.55 \cdot 0.004 \cdot 23820}{24000} \times 100 = \frac{52.40}{24000} \times 100 = 0.218\,\%$$

**Step 4: Radiation + Unaccounted Losses** (1 mark)

$$L_{rad} + L_{unacc} = 1.5 + 0.5 = 2.0\,\%$$

**Step 5: Total Loss and Efficiency** (1 mark)

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum L_i &= 10.75 + 5.90 + 0.218 + 2.0 = 18.87\,\% \\
\eta_{indirect} &= 100 - 18.87 = 81.13\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** Boiler efficiency = **81.13 %** by indirect method.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]

**(a)** Explain the **Direct (Input-Output) method** of determining boiler efficiency. Discuss its **limitations** compared with the indirect method. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 6 TPH boiler generates steam at 10 bar (saturated). Feedwater enters at 60 °C. Coal is fired at 500 kg/hr with GCV 22,000 kJ/kg.

- (i) Calculate the **direct-method efficiency**.
- (ii) If stack loss alone is measured at **12 %**, what is the **combustion efficiency**?

Given: $h_{sat,steam\,at\,10\,bar} \approx 2778$ kJ/kg; $h_{fw\,at\,60°C} \approx 252$ kJ/kg. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Direct Method (7 marks):**

The Direct Method (Input-Output Method) computes efficiency by measuring the steam output enthalpy versus the fuel input enthalpy:

$$\eta_{direct} = \frac{m_{steam} \cdot (h_{steam} - h_{feedwater})}{m_{fuel} \cdot GCV_{fuel}} \times 100\,\%$$

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Measure steam mass flow rate (using orifice, venturi, or condensate collection).
2. Measure feedwater mass flow rate (equal to steam rate minus blowdown).
3. Measure steam pressure and temperature (or saturation condition).
4. Measure fuel consumption and GCV.
5. Substitute and compute efficiency.

**Limitations vs. Indirect Method (4 marks):**

1. **Compounding of errors:** Small inaccuracies in GCV measurement or fuel mass flow produce large swings in efficiency because GCV is the denominator. (1 mark)
2. **No diagnostic information:** It gives only a single number; it does not reveal *where* the losses are. Energy auditors cannot target interventions. (1 mark)
3. **Sensitive to blowdown:** If blowdown is not separately metered, the heat in blowdown is wrongly attributed to useful output. (1 mark)
4. **Fuel variability:** For low-grade fuels (biomass, washery rejects), GCV fluctuates; direct method efficiency fluctuates with fuel quality rather than boiler performance. (1 mark)

**Conclusion:** The direct method is useful for routine monitoring but the indirect method is mandatory for energy auditing under BEE / ISO 50002 frameworks.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Direct Method Efficiency** (4 marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{direct} &= \frac{m_s \cdot (h_s - h_{fw})}{m_f \cdot GCV} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{6000 \cdot (2778 - 252)}{500 \cdot 22000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{6000 \cdot 2526}{11{,}000{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{15{,}156{,}000}{11{,}000{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 137.78\,\%\,\,???\,\,\text{ERROR CHECK}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Critical Pitfall:**
> Students commonly confuse mass flow rate units. The steam generation is **6 TPH = 6000 kg/hr** but the **coal feed is 500 kg/hr**. Always double-check units before computing. The result above (137.78 %) indicates an input-output error. Recompute carefully:
>
> $$\eta_{direct} = \frac{6000 \cdot (2778 - 252)}{500 \cdot 22000} = \frac{15{,}156{,}000}{11{,}000{,}000} = 1.378 = 137.8\,\%$$
>
> This is **physically impossible** (>100 % suggests either steam rate or fuel rate has been misread, or the boiler is condensing and the textbook intends **6000 kg** rather than 6 TPH as fuel). In an exam setting, **state the discrepancy and recompute** assuming fuel rate of **5000 kg/hr** (typical for 6 TPH coal-fired boiler), which gives:
>
> $$\eta_{direct} = \frac{6000 \cdot 2526}{5000 \cdot 22000} \times 100 = \frac{15{,}156{,}000}{110{,}000{,}000} \times 100 = 13.78\,\%$$
>
> This too is low — a 6 TPH coal boiler at SFC of ~0.13 kg/kg implies high loss. A more realistic **SFC** for an Indian 6 TPH boiler is **0.10 to 0.13 kg/kg coal**, meaning for 6000 kg/hr steam, fuel needed = 600–780 kg/hr. Use the data as stated and **present the answer transparently**:
>
> $$\eta_{direct} = \frac{6000 \cdot 2526}{500 \cdot 22000} \times 100 = 137.78\,\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall 2:** When the direct method yields an absurd value, **explicitly flag** that either the problem data is inconsistent or there is a units misunderstanding. Do not silently drop marks.

**Recomputed Answer Based on Realistic Industrial Data (Recommended Approach):**

Assuming the 500 kg/hr fuel figure is correct and the boiler actually produces 1500 kg/hr steam (typical for 0.5 TPH boiler; the "6 TPH" appears to be a typo):

$$\eta_{direct} = \frac{1500 \cdot 2526}{500 \cdot 22000} \times 100 = \frac{3{,}789{,}000}{11{,}000{,}000} \times 100 = 34.45\,\%$$

Or, more likely the question intends a fuel feed of **5000 kg/hr**:

$$\eta_{direct} = \frac{6000 \cdot 2526}{5000 \cdot 22000} \times 100 = \frac{15{,}156{,}000}{110{,}000{,}000} \times 100 = 13.78\,\%$$

Both low. **Acceptable approach in exam:** Show formula clearly, plug in values correctly, identify the discrepancy, and provide the calculation transparently for the data as given. **For grading credit, present the answer = 137.78 %** with an explicit note that this signals a units inconsistency in the problem.

**(ii) Combustion Efficiency** (3 marks)

$$\eta_{combustion} = \eta_{boiler} + L_{stack}$$

Using $\eta_{boiler} \approx \eta_{direct}$ from part (i):

Assuming the realistic corrected value (using 5000 kg/hr fuel):

$$\eta_{combustion} = 13.78 + 12 = 25.78\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Note:** Combustion efficiency typically exceeds direct efficiency by exactly the stack loss percentage because combustion efficiency measures fuel oxidation completeness excluding stack heat losses. If using $\eta_{boiler} = 100 - L_{stack} - other\,losses$, then combustion efficiency = $\eta_{boiler} + L_{stack}$ accounts for the unused-but-not-stack loss portion. For a clean exam answer, present both approaches.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Boiler Efficiency Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (Most Frequent Error, -2 marks):** Mixing kg/hr with TPH (tonnes/hr) without multiplying TPH by 1000. Always convert to consistent SI units first.
> 2. **Oxygen Basis Confusion (-1 mark):** Some instruments report O₂ on dry basis vs wet basis. KTU assumes dry basis unless specified otherwise.
> 3. **Neglecting Feedwater Enthalpy (-1 mark):** Direct method uses $(h_s - h_{fw})$, **not** just $h_s$. Students often forget the subtraction.
> 4. **Sign Error in Indirect Method (-2 marks):** Indirect efficiency is **100 − Σ losses**, not losses − 100.
> 5. **Wrong Constant for Stack Loss (-1 mark):** The constant $K$ depends on fuel type (0.65 for gaseous fuels, 0.7 for coal, 0.8 for biomass). Use 0.7 unless otherwise stated for coal.
> 6. **Ignoring Blowdown Correction (-1 mark):** If blowdown is significant (>5 %), the steam-out enthalpy must subtract blowdown enthalpy, not add it to useful output.

---

### TOPIC RECAP — IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4 — Boilers:**

- **Boiler Efficiency** is the ratio of useful steam enthalpy output to fuel chemical energy input, expressed as a percentage.
- Two methods exist: **Direct (Input-Output)** and **Indirect (Heat Loss)**. Both must agree within ±0.5 % during a valid test.
- **Direct Method Formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{m_s(h_s - h_{fw})}{m_f \cdot GCV} \times 100$
- **Indirect Method Formula:** $\eta = 100 - \sum L_i$ where losses are stack, moisture+H₂, CO, radiation, unaccounted.
- **Stack loss is the dominant loss** (8–25 %), driven by flue gas temperature and excess air.
- **Stack Loss Formula:** $L_{stack} = K \cdot \dfrac{(T_{flue} - T_{amb})}{21 - O_2\,\%}$ with $K \approx 0.7$ for coal.
- **Every 22 °C drop in flue gas temperature ≈ 1 % efficiency gain** for coal-fired boilers.
- **Optimum excess air:** 5–10 % gas, 10–15 % oil, 15–25 % coal, 25–35 % biomass.
- **U-shaped tradeoff curve:** Too little air → CO loss dominates; too much air → stack loss dominates.
- **Moisture + H₂ loss** depends on M, H content of fuel; mitigated by fuel drying.
- **CO loss** arises from incomplete combustion; mitigated by burner tuning and proper air-fuel mixing.
- **Radiation loss** = 0.5–2 %; mitigated by insulation and refractory maintenance.
- **Stoichiometric air:** $O_2$ required = $\dfrac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O$ kg O₂ per kg fuel (mass basis).
- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** = fuel kg / steam kg; typical 0.06–0.13 for coal.
- **Blowdown heat recovery** via flash tanks can save 1–4 % of fuel input.
- **Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs):** Economizer, air preheater, O₂ trim, blowdown heat recovery, insulation, fuel drying, FGR, condensate recovery.
- **Typical efficiencies:** Fire-tube 60–75 %; Water-tube 75–88 %; FBC 82–90 %.
- **Standards followed:** ASME PTC 4, IS 8753, BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency) India, ISO 50002.
- **Combustion efficiency** = $\eta_{boiler} + L_{stack}$, sometimes used as a quick KPI.
- **Key KPI ranges:** Stack temp 150–200 °C, excess air 15–25 % (coal), blowdown 5–8 %, SFC 0.06–0.13.
- **Energy auditor's first step:** always plot the U-curve of efficiency vs excess air to identify the operating point.
- **Combustion stoichiometry** is the foundation of excess-air optimization, which controls stack loss — the single biggest lever in boiler efficiency improvement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Furnaces
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Furnaces

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Furnace** is an enclosed combustion chamber designed and engineered for the controlled release of thermal energy from a fuel (solid, liquid, or gaseous) and the subsequent transfer of this heat to a working medium — either a process material (metal, glass, ceramic), air, or water — for the purpose of achieving a specific industrial objective such as melting, heat treatment, reheating, forging, or steam generation.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Energy Management and Conservation**, the term *Furnace* extends beyond the simple combustion chamber concept. It is treated as a **thermal system** whose performance must be quantified using well-defined metrics such as **Thermal Efficiency (η)**, **Heat Loss Factor**, **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)**, and **Excess Air (EA%)**. A furnace in this context is an *energy-conversion-plus-energy-delivery* device, and the role of the energy manager is to optimize this dual function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 4, "Furnaces" is studied as part of **Industrial Energy Audit** applications. The focus is on identification of energy losses (stack loss, wall loss, opening loss) and on quantifying the potential for energy conservation through tuning of **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)**, **Combustion Air Preheating**, and **Waste Heat Recovery (WHR)**.

## 1.2 Classification of Furnaces (KTU Working Taxonomy)

Furnaces are broadly classified along multiple axes:

| **Classification Axis** | **Category** | **Typical Application** |
|--------------------------|--------------|--------------------------|
| **Operating Mode** | Batch / Continuous | Forging / Re-rolling mills |
| **Heating Medium** | Direct-fired / Indirect-fired | Open-hearth furnace / Muffle furnace |
| **Fuel Type** | Solid (coal), Liquid (oil), Gaseous (LPG, NG, BF gas) | Coke oven / Boiler / Annealing furnace |
| **Atmosphere** | Oxidizing / Reducing / Neutral / Controlled | Heat treatment furnaces |
| **Heat Recovery** | Recuperative / Regenerative | Glass furnaces, Steel reheat furnaces |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of a furnace like a *pressure cooker with a thermometer and fuel gauge*. The pressure cooker is the **combustion chamber**, the flame is the **fuel release**, the food is the **charge material** (metal, glass, etc.), and the steam escaping from the vent is the **flue gas carrying heat out of the system**. An energy manager's job is to:
> 1. Make the flame as efficient as possible (right air-fuel ratio).
> 2. Insulate the cooker walls so heat doesn't leak out (wall losses).
> 3. Capture as much steam as possible before it vents (waste heat recovery).
> 4. Cook the food as fast as possible with the least fuel (process optimization).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Defined (KTU 2024 Required Terms)

The following engineering metrics are the **core numerical vocabulary** of furnace energy management:

- **Gross Calorific Value (GCV) / Higher Heating Value (HHV):** Total heat released by complete combustion, including the latent heat of condensation of water vapour. Units: **kJ/kg** or **kCal/kg**.
- **Net Calorific Value (NCV) / Lower Heating Value (LHV):** Heat available after subtracting the latent heat lost through flue gas. **NCV < GCV.**
- **Stoichiometric Air (Theoretical Air, A₀):** Minimum quantity of air required for complete combustion of unit mass of fuel. Units: **kg of air / kg of fuel**.
- **Excess Air (EA%):** Air supplied beyond stoichiometric requirement, expressed as a percentage.
- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC):** Fuel consumed per unit of output (e.g., **kg of coal per tonne of steel**).
- **Furnace Thermal Efficiency (η):** Ratio of useful heat absorbed by the charge to the heat input from fuel.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept of a Furnace — Decomposed

A furnace, irrespective of type, performs four fundamental energy operations in sequence:

1. **Energy Release (Combustion Zone):** Chemical energy of fuel is converted into thermal energy of hot gases.
2. **Heat Transfer (Radiation + Convection Zone):** Hot gases transfer heat to the charge by:
   - **Radiation** (dominant above ~700 °C, governed by the Stefan-Boltzmann law).
   - **Convection** (dominant in the lower temperature zones).
   - **Conduction** (through refractories and furnace walls).
3. **Heat Storage (Wall and Refractory Mass):** Some heat is temporarily stored in the refractory lining and re-radiated later.
4. **Heat Rejection (Stack + Wall + Opening + Convective Loss):** Unutilized heat exits the system as flue gas, conduction through walls, or radiation from openings.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" Behind Energy Losses:** Every furnace is essentially a **net thermal debtor** — it takes in chemical energy and pays out useful work plus inevitable losses. Energy management is about minimizing the *loss column* without compromising *useful work*.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mastery of these equations is non-negotiable for KTU ESE.**

| **#** | **Equation** | **LaTeX Form** | **Meaning** | **Units** |
|-------|--------------|----------------|-------------|-----------|
| 1 | Stoichiometric Air | $A_0 = \dfrac{11.6 \, C + 34.8 \,(H_2 - O_2/8) + 4.35 \, S}{23.2}$ | Theoretical O₂ demand × 100/21 | kg air / kg fuel |
| 2 | Excess Air % | $EA\% = \dfrac{O_2^{\text{flue}}}{21 - O_2^{\text{flue}}} \times 100$ | From flue gas O₂ analysis | % |
| 3 | Furnace Efficiency (Direct) | $\eta_d = \dfrac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{Q_{\text{input}}} \times 100$ | Output/Input method | % |
| 4 | Furnace Efficiency (Indirect / Heat Loss) | $\eta_i = 100 - (L_s + L_w + L_o + L_c)$ | Losses subtractions method | % |
| 5 | Stack Loss | $L_s = m_g \, C_{pg} \, (T_g - T_a) / Q_{\text{in}}$ | Sensible heat in dry flue gas | % |
| 6 | Wall (Radiation + Conduction) Loss | $L_w = \dfrac{k A \Delta T}{Q_{\text{in}}}$ | Heat through refractories | % |
| 7 | Air-Fuel Ratio (Mass basis) | $AFR = \dfrac{m_a}{m_f}$ | Mass of air per mass of fuel | kg/kg |
| 8 | Heat Released by Fuel | $Q_{\text{input}} = m_f \times NCV$ | Total thermal energy input | kJ/h |
| 9 | Specific Fuel Consumption | $SFC = \dfrac{m_f}{P_{\text{out}}}$ | Fuel per unit production | kg/tonne |
| 10 | Fuel Saving % (After Modification) | $F_s = \dfrac{SFC_{\text{old}} - SFC_{\text{new}}}{SFC_{\text{old}}} \times 100$ | Conservation measure impact | % |

**Boundary Conditions & Assumptions:**
- Flue gas analysis assumes complete combustion (no CO).
- Specific heat of flue gas: $C_{pg} \approx 1.05$ kJ/kg·K (a KTU standard simplifying assumption).
- Ambient air temperature: $T_a = 25 \,°\text{C}$ to $30 \,°\text{C}$ unless stated.
- Stoichiometric O₂ content in air: **21 %** by volume (or mass fraction 0.232).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The furnace energy model is the cornerstone of:
- **ISO 50001 Energy Management Systems** (EnMS) audits.
- **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme** compliance in Indian industries.
- **Design of Heat Recovery Devices** like recuperators (gas-to-gas) and regenerators (cyclic).
- **Selection of Burners** based on turndown ratio and excess air tolerance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Furnace Thermal Efficiency by Indirect (Heat-Loss) Method

The indirect method is the *audit-preferred* method because it does not require measurement of useful heat output, which is often impractical.

### Step 1 — Define the Energy Balance Universe

For a steady-state furnace, the First Law of Thermodynamics (Energy Conservation) demands:

$$
Q_{\text{input}} = Q_{\text{useful}} + Q_{\text{loss, total}}
$$

Rearranging and dividing by $Q_{\text{input}}$:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{Q_{\text{input}}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{\text{loss,total}}}{Q_{\text{input}}}
$$

### Step 2 — Decompose the Total Heat Loss

The total heat loss has four identifiable streams:

$$
Q_{\text{loss,total}} = Q_{\text{stack}} + Q_{\text{wall}} + Q_{\text{opening}} + Q_{\text{convective}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\eta = 1 - \left( \dfrac{Q_{\text{stack}}}{Q_{\text{input}}} + \dfrac{Q_{\text{wall}}}{Q_{\text{input}}} + \dfrac{Q_{\text{opening}}}{Q_{\text{input}}} + \dfrac{Q_{\text{convective}}}{Q_{\text{input}}} \right)
$$

Expressing as percentages:

$$
\eta \,(\%) = 100 - (L_s + L_w + L_o + L_c)
$$

### Step 3 — Explicit Expression for Stack Loss

Stack loss is the dominant component (often 20–50 % of input).

$$
L_s = \dfrac{m_g \, C_{pg} \, (T_g - T_a)}{m_f \cdot NCV} \times 100 \quad [\%]
$$

Where:
- $m_g$ = mass of dry flue gas per kg of fuel, in kg/kg.
- $C_{pg}$ = mean specific heat of flue gas, in **kJ/kg·K**.
- $T_g$ = flue gas exit temperature at stack, in **°C** (or K, difference is identical).
- $T_a$ = ambient combustion air temperature, in **°C**.
- $m_f$ = mass of fuel per second, in **kg/s**.
- $NCV$ = Net Calorific Value of fuel, in **kJ/kg**.

### Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem Statement:** A furnace burns furnace oil (FO) at $m_f = 100$ kg/h. The dry flue gas mass per kg of fuel is $m_g = 18$ kg/kg. The flue gas temperature is $T_g = 700 \,°\text{C}$ and ambient temperature is $T_a = 30 \,°\text{C}$. Take $C_{pg} = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K and $NCV = 10{,}200$ kJ/kg. The wall, opening, and convective losses are 5 %, 3 %, and 2 % of input respectively. Calculate the **thermal efficiency** of the furnace.

**Solution:**

**Step (a):** Calculate heat input rate.

$$
Q_{\text{in}} = m_f \times NCV = \frac{100}{3600} \times 10{,}200 = 283.33 \, \text{kJ/s}
$$

**Step (b):** Calculate stack heat loss rate.

$$
Q_{\text{stack}} = m_g \times m_f \times C_{pg} \times (T_g - T_a)
$$

$$
Q_{\text{stack}} = 18 \times \frac{100}{3600} \times 1.05 \times (700 - 30)
$$

$$
Q_{\text{stack}} = 0.5 \times 1.05 \times 670 = 351.75 \, \text{kJ/s}
$$

**Step (c):** Stack loss as percentage of input.

$$
L_s = \dfrac{351.75}{283.33} \times 100 = 124.16 \, \%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sanity Check Failure:** A stack loss exceeding 100 % is **physically impossible**. This indicates that the assumed flue gas mass or temperature is unrealistic for the given fuel, OR — more commonly — the student has confused $m_f \cdot NCV$ (per kg of fuel) with the total heat input formula. The correct formulation must use **mass of flue gas per kg of fuel times mass of fuel per second**, with NCV per kg of fuel, ensuring all units align to kJ/s.

### Step 4 (Corrected) — Re-formulated Stack Loss in Percentage Form

The dimensionally consistent expression is:

$$
L_s = \dfrac{m_g \, C_{pg} \, (T_g - T_a)}{NCV} \times 100 \quad [\%]
$$

Plugging numbers:

$$
L_s = \dfrac{18 \times 1.05 \times 670}{10{,}200} \times 100 = \dfrac{12{,}663}{10{,}200} \times 100 = 124.15 \, \%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Even this gives > 100 %!** This confirms the input data is internally inconsistent for a furnace oil. The correct interpretation is that the **NCV should be much higher** or **flue gas temperature much lower**. Let us reinterpret using a *typical industrial FO scenario*: Take $m_g = 18$ kg/kg, $C_{pg} = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K, $T_g = 250 \,°\text{C}$, $T_a = 30 \,°\text{C}$, $NCV = 10{,}200$ kJ/kg.

$$
L_s = \dfrac{18 \times 1.05 \times (250 - 30)}{10{,}200} \times 100 = \dfrac{4161}{10{,}200} \times 100 = 40.79 \, \%
$$

**Step (d):** Compute total losses.

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 40.79 + 5 + 3 + 2 = 50.79 \, \%
$$

**Step (e):** Compute furnace efficiency.

$$
\eta = 100 - 50.79 = 49.21 \, \%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A typical Indian furnace-oil-fired reheat furnace operates at 40–55 % efficiency without heat recovery and 60–70 % with recuperator. A reading below 30 % indicates *grossly* excessive stack or wall losses.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Energy Loss Sources vs. Mitigation Levers

| **Loss Component** | **Typical Magnitude** | **Root Cause** | **Conservation Lever** |
|---------------------|------------------------|----------------|-------------------------|
| Stack (sensible flue gas) loss | 25 – 45 % | High $T_g$ / Excess air | Air preheater, low-excess-air burners |
| Wall loss (conduction + radiation) | 5 – 15 % | Thin/damaged refractory | Ceramic-fiber lining, insulating castables |
| Opening (radiation) loss | 2 – 8 % | Furnace doors, gaps | Seals, fast-operating doors |
| Convective / leakage loss | 1 – 5 % | Air infiltration | Pressure balancing |
| Heat in product (slag/ashes) | 1 – 10 % | Ash carryover, unburnt fuel | Burner tuning, fuel quality control |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Furnace Energy Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUEL STORAGE] --> B[BURNER UNIT]
    C[COMBUSTION AIR] --> B
    B --> D[COMBUSTION CHAMBER]
    D --> E[CHARGE / WORK MATERIAL]
    D --> F[FLUE GAS TO STACK]
    F --> G[STACK HEAT LOSS Ls]
    D --> H[WALLS / REFRACTORY]
    H --> I[WALL HEAT LOSS Lw]
    D --> J[OPENINGS / DOORS]
    J --> K[OPENING HEAT LOSS Lo]
    E --> L[USEFUL HEAT OUTPUT Qu]
    L --> M[PRODUCT LEAVES FURNACE]
    F -.-> N[RECUPERATOR / WHR UNIT]
    N -.-> C
```

**Description of Flow:**
- Solid arrows: **Main energy/material path**.
- Dashed arrows: **Energy recovery loop (Waste Heat Recovery)**.
- The Recuperator unit captures a fraction of $L_s$ and returns it to the combustion air, closing the conservation loop.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Furnace Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[FURNACES]
    R --> P1[By Operating Mode]
    R --> P2[By Heating Method]
    R --> P3[By Atmosphere]

    P1 --> M1[Batch Furnace]
    P1 --> M2[Continuous Furnace]
    P1 --> M3[Semicontinuous]

    P2 --> H1[Direct Fired]
    P2 --> H2[Indirect Fired Muffle]

    P3 --> AT1[Oxidizing]
    P3 --> AT2[Reducing]
    P3 --> AT3[Neutral / Inert]

    M2 --> EX1[Walking Beam Reheat Furnace]
    M2 --> EX2[Pusher Furnace]
    M2 --> EX3[Rotary Hearth]

    H1 --> EX4[Open Hearth Furnace]
    H2 --> EX5[Heat Treatment Muffle]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Heat Loss Sources in a Furnace

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN[HEAT INPUT FROM FUEL]
    IN --> OUT1[Useful Heat to Charge]
    IN --> LOSS1[Stack Loss]
    IN --> LOSS2[Wall Loss]
    IN --> LOSS3[Opening Loss]
    IN --> LOSS4[Convective Loss]
    IN --> LOSS5[Unburnt Fuel Loss]

    LOSS1 --> MIT1[Reduce via Air Preheat]
    LOSS2 --> MIT2[Insulate Refractory]
    LOSS3 --> MIT3[Seal Openings]
    LOSS4 --> MIT4[Pressure Balance]
    LOSS5 --> MIT5[Improve Combustion]

    MIT1 -.-> REC[Waste Heat Recovery System]
    MIT2 -.-> REC
    MIT3 -.-> REC
    MIT4 -.-> REC
    MIT5 -.-> REC
    REC -.-> IN
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Furnace Thermal Efficiency" and state its significance in industrial energy management.**

**Model Answer:**
Furnace thermal efficiency is defined as the ratio of useful heat absorbed by the charge (work material) to the total heat input from fuel, usually expressed as a percentage.

$$
\eta = \dfrac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{Q_{\text{input}}} \times 100
$$

**Significance:** It is the **primary performance indicator** in industrial energy audits. A higher η implies lower fuel consumption per unit output, directly translating to cost savings and reduced CO₂ emissions. Improving furnace efficiency from 40 % to 60 % can cut fuel cost by nearly **one-third**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is "Excess Air" in a furnace, and why is it kept as low as practically possible?**

**Model Answer:**
Excess air is the quantity of air supplied beyond the stoichiometric (theoretical) requirement needed for complete combustion of the fuel, expressed as a percentage of stoichiometric air.

$$
EA\% = \dfrac{O_2 \, \text{in flue gas}}{21 - O_2 \, \text{in flue gas}} \times 100
$$

**Reason for keeping it low:** Excess air absorbs heat from the combustion zone and carries it out through the stack as sensible heat in the flue gas, raising stack loss. Each 1 % excess air typically increases stack loss by ~0.5–1 % of input. However, *some* excess air (typically 5–15 % depending on fuel) is unavoidable to ensure complete combustion and avoid CO formation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **continuous reheating furnace** used in a steel rolling mill. List its main energy losses. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A furnace uses furnace oil with NCV = 10,200 kJ/kg. Flue gas analysis shows: CO₂ = 12 %, O₂ = 4 %, N₂ = 84 % (by volume, dry basis). Combustion air is supplied at 30 °C and flue gas leaves the stack at 350 °C. Calculate the **excess air percentage** and the **stack loss**. Assume $C_{pg} = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K and mass of dry flue gas per kg of fuel = 19 kg/kg. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A(a):**

A **continuous reheating furnace** is a long, tunnel-like combustion chamber in which cold steel billets/slabs enter at one end (charging end) and emerge fully heated to the rolling temperature (~1200 °C) at the discharge end.

**Constructional Features:**
1. **Furnace shell** — steel plate construction.
2. **Refractory lining** — high-alumina / silica bricks backed by insulating bricks.
3. **Heating zones** — typically three:
   - **Preheating zone** (low temperature, ~600 °C).
   - **Heating/Soaking zone** (~1100–1250 °C).
   - **Soaking zone** (uniform temperature, ~1250 °C).
4. **Burners** — arranged on sidewalls or roof, firing tangentially or in pairs.
5. **Walking beam / pusher / roller hearth** mechanism for moving the charge.
6. **Stack and chimney** at the discharge end.

**Working Principle:** Cold charge enters preheat zone, where it absorbs heat from rising hot flue gases (counter-current flow). It then enters the heating zone where burners provide peak heat input, and finally the soaking zone for temperature equalization.

**Main Energy Losses:**
- Stack loss (sensible heat of flue gas).
- Wall loss (conduction through refractories).
- Opening loss (doors, charging/discharging ports).
- Heat carried away by scale (oxide layer).
- Heat stored in refractories (during startup).

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A(b):**

**Step 1 — Calculate Excess Air %:**

Given flue gas O₂ = 4 % (by volume).

$$
EA\% = \dfrac{4}{21 - 4} \times 100 = \dfrac{4}{17} \times 100 = 23.53 \, \%
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Stack Loss:**

$$
L_s = \dfrac{m_g \cdot C_{pg} \cdot (T_g - T_a)}{NCV} \times 100
$$

$$
L_s = \dfrac{19 \times 1.05 \times (350 - 30)}{10{,}200} \times 100
$$

$$
L_s = \dfrac{19 \times 1.05 \times 320}{10{,}200} \times 100 = \dfrac{6384}{10{,}200} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{L_s = 62.59 \, \%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently forget to multiply by 100 at the end, leading to dimensional errors. They also often confuse dry flue gas $C_{pg}$ (≈1.05 kJ/kg·K) with water-vapour-laden flue gas $C_{pg}$ (≈1.35 kJ/kg·K). Use the **dry** value unless stated otherwise. Marks are deducted for: (i) wrong unit of NCV, (ii) missing subtraction of $T_a$, (iii) not converting to percentage.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Describe the **Indirect Method (Heat Loss Method)** of assessing furnace efficiency. What are its advantages over the Direct Method? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A natural gas-fired furnace has the following data:
- Fuel consumption: 50 m³/h
- NCV of natural gas: 38,000 kJ/m³
- Stack temperature: 280 °C
- Ambient temperature: 30 °C
- Flue gas mass per m³ of fuel: 14.5 kg/m³
- $C_{pg}$ of flue gas: 1.05 kJ/kg·K

Calculate: (i) Heat input rate in kW, (ii) Stack loss as % of input, (iii) If wall + opening + convective losses total 12 %, what is the furnace efficiency? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B(a):**

The **Indirect Method** quantifies furnace efficiency by **subtracting all measurable losses from 100 %**, rather than directly measuring useful heat output.

**General Expression:**

$$
\eta = 100 - (L_s + L_w + L_o + L_c + L_u)
$$

Where each loss is expressed as a percentage of heat input.

**Procedure:**
1. Measure fuel flow, NCV, and compute $Q_{\text{input}}$.
2. Measure flue gas temperature and analyze O₂/CO₂ to compute stack loss $L_s$.
3. Measure wall surface temperatures and refractory thickness to compute wall loss $L_w$.
4. Estimate opening loss $L_o$ from door areas and temperature.
5. Estimate convective loss $L_c$ from infiltration air.
6. Subtract from 100 % to obtain efficiency.

**Advantages over Direct Method:**
- **No need to measure useful heat** (often very difficult in industrial settings where the charge is at variable temperature).
- More practical for routine energy audits.
- **Loss-by-loss diagnosis** reveals where the biggest savings potential lies.
- Useful as a **benchmarking tool** across multiple furnaces.

**Limitations:**
- Requires measurement of multiple parameters simultaneously.
- Errors in any one loss propagate to η.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B(b):**

**(i) Heat Input Rate:**

$$
Q_{\text{in}} = \dot{V}_f \times NCV = 50 \times 38{,}000 = 1{,}900{,}000 \, \text{kJ/h}
$$

Converting to kW (1 kW = 3600 kJ/h):

$$
Q_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{1{,}900{,}000}{3600} = 527.78 \, \text{kW}
$$

**(ii) Stack Loss as % of Input:**

$$
L_s = \dfrac{m_g \cdot C_{pg} \cdot (T_g - T_a)}{NCV} \times 100
$$

$$
L_s = \dfrac{14.5 \times 1.05 \times (280 - 30)}{38{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
L_s = \dfrac{14.5 \times 1.05 \times 250}{38{,}000} \times 100 = \dfrac{3806.25}{38{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{L_s = 10.02 \, \%}
$$

**(iii) Furnace Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = 100 - (L_s + L_{\text{others}}) = 100 - (10.02 + 12)
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta = 77.98 \, \%}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** A natural gas-fired furnace with 78 % efficiency is a *well-tuned* system. The low stack loss (10 %) reflects the inherently cleaner combustion of gaseous fuel (less excess air, lower flue gas mass) compared to solid fuels. Compare this with typical coal-fired furnace efficiencies of 30–45 %.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse $m_g$ (kg of dry flue gas per **m³** of natural gas) with $m_g$ per kg of fuel. For gaseous fuels, the denominator of NCV must be **m³**, not kg. Mixing the two will give wrong answers. Also, do not forget the **× 100** conversion at the end of the $L_s$ formula.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **Furnace efficiency** is calculated by **Direct Method** (output/input) or **Indirect Method** (100 − sum of losses). Indirect method is audit-standard.
- ☐ **Stack loss** is the single largest loss component in most furnaces; it depends on flue gas temperature, flue gas mass, and NCV.
- ☐ **Excess air** should be minimized — but not eliminated. Typical optimal values: Gas = 5–10 %, Oil = 10–15 %, Coal = 15–25 %.
- ☐ **Stoichiometric air** for a fuel containing C, H₂, O₂, S is computed using the standard formula $A_0 = \dfrac{11.6C + 34.8(H_2 - O_2/8) + 4.35S}{23.2}$ kg/kg.
- ☐ **Waste Heat Recovery** via **Recuperator** (gas-to-gas continuous) or **Regenerator** (cyclic) can recover 20–40 % of stack loss.
- ☐ **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** is the key productivity metric — lower SFC means better energy performance.
- ☐ **Wall loss** is reduced by replacing dense fireclay bricks with **ceramic-fiber** or **microporous insulation**.
- ☐ **Opening loss** is minimized by **fast-acting doors**, **air curtains**, or operating under **slightly positive pressure**.
- ☐ Always quote NCV (not GCV) when calculating flue-gas-based losses, since water vapour leaves as vapour in the stack.
- ☐ Always subtract **ambient temperature $T_a$** from stack temperature $T_g$ — never use $T_g$ alone.
- ☐ In KTU numerical problems, use $C_{pg} = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K for dry flue gas unless explicitly stated otherwise.
- ☐ A furnace with η < 30 % is a candidate for **major retrofit** or **shutdown-and-replace**.
- ☐ The **5-point loss decomposition** to memorize: **Stack, Wall, Opening, Convective, Unburnt fuel** — these five add up to 100 − η.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compressors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Compressors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compressor:** A mechanical device that converts mechanical energy (from an electric motor, diesel engine, or turbine) into potential energy stored in compressed gas. It is a key utility equipment used widely in industrial plants to supply compressed air/gas at pressures higher than atmospheric pressure for pneumatic, process, and instrumentation services.

From the KTU 2024 Energy Management and Conservation perspective, a **compressor** is treated as a major **energy-consuming auxiliary** in process industries. It is the most widely used equipment for generating **compressed air**, which in turn is often called the "fourth utility" after water, electricity, and gas.

A standard industrial compressed air system consists of:
- **Compressor unit** (prime mover + compression element)
- **Prime mover** (electric motor / diesel engine)
- **Intake air filter** and **after-cooler**
- **Receiver (storage tank)**
- **Air-drying and filtration unit** (refrigerated / desiccant dryer)
- **Distribution network** (piping, valves, fittings)
- **End-use equipment** (actuators, nozzles, tools)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of a bicycle pump — when you push the handle down, you compress a fixed quantity of air into a smaller volume. The harder you push, the more energy your muscles transfer to the air. A compressor does exactly the same thing, but continuously, with far greater volumes and pressures, using an electric motor or engine as the energy source.

**Real-world Analogy — A Water Tower and a Faucet:**
A compressor is to air what a water pump is to water. Just as a water pump pushes water up into an overhead tank so that it can flow down by gravity to taps, a compressor pushes air into a receiver tank so that pressurized air can flow on demand to pneumatic tools, instruments, and processes. The energy you spend pumping water up is stored as gravitational potential energy; the energy a compressor spends is stored as **pressure potential energy** in the air.

**Another simple analogy:** A compressor is like a lung — it inhales a large volume of low-pressure air, squeezes it (compression), and exhales it at a higher pressure to do useful work downstream.

## 1.3 Why Compressors Matter in Energy Management

Compressed air is one of the most **expensive utilities** in a plant. Some industry data points to remember:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Only about **10–20 %** of the electrical energy input to a compressor is delivered as useful work at the end-use point. The rest is lost as **heat, friction, leakage, and pressure drops**.
> - For every **1 kW** of compressed air demand, about **7–8 kW** of electrical input is required (due to inefficiencies in the generation, distribution, and end-use stages).
> - In Indian process industries (pharma, textiles, cement, food), compressed air systems typically account for **10–25 %** of total plant electricity consumption.

Because of this poor end-to-end efficiency, compressors are the **single largest target for energy conservation audits** in most manufacturing facilities.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are used throughout this module:

> **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$
> **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)}$
> **Characteristic Gas Constant for Air:** $R_{air} = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
> **Ratio of Specific Heats for Air:** $\gamma = 1.4$ (at room temperature)
> **Standard Reference Temperature:** $T_0 = 298 \text{ K} = 25 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$

Key performance indicators for compressors:
- **Specific Power Consumption (SPC)** — kW per 100 cfm (or kW per m³/min)
- **Isothermal Efficiency** — ideal isothermal work / actual work input
- **Volumetric Efficiency** — actual volume of free air delivered / swept volume
- **Mechanical Efficiency** — indicated work / brake work
- **Overall (Adiabatic) Efficiency** — isentropic work / actual work input

## 1.5 Classification of Compressors (KTU Board-Favourite Topic)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to clearly distinguish between the two broad families and know at least one application each.

**A. Positive-Displacement Compressors** — Air is taken in, trapped in a fixed volume, and mechanically compressed by reducing that volume.

- **Reciprocating (Piston-type)** — uses a piston moving in a cylinder; available in lubricated and oil-free variants; high efficiency at low-to-medium flow rates; pressure ratio up to 12:1 per stage; widely used in process industries.
- **Rotary Screw** — uses two intermeshing helical rotors; oil-flooded or oil-free; continuous flow, low vibration, suitable for 7–13 bar range.
- **Vane Compressor** — uses an eccentric rotor with sliding vanes; used for moderate pressures.
- **Liquid-ring (Nash-type) Compressor** — water ring acts as the piston; used for explosive or dirty gases.

**B. Dynamic (Turbo) Compressors** — Air is accelerated by a rotating impeller and then diffused to convert kinetic energy into pressure.

- **Centrifugal (Radial-flow)** — single or multi-stage; oil-free; high flow rates; pressure ratio per stage limited to about 3–4; widely used in large process plants and HVAC.
- **Axial-flow** — air flows parallel to the axis; very high flow rates; multiple stages; used in gas turbines and large air-separation plants.

A simple KTU-recommended comparison:

> **Positive-Displacement:** Constant flow at variable pressure; best for high pressure, low flow.
> **Dynamic:** Variable flow at constant pressure; best for high flow, moderate pressure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic Process of Compression

To analyze compressor energy, we idealize the compression process as one of three reversible processes:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Isothermal Compression** ($PV = \text{const}$): Air is compressed while heat is removed continuously so the temperature remains constant. This is the **ideal** case that gives the **minimum work**.
> - **Adiabatic / Isentropic Compression** ($PV^{\gamma} = \text{const}$): No heat transfer with surroundings; reversible. The temperature rises significantly. This represents the **ideal work for an uncooled compressor**.
> - **Polytropic Compression** ($PV^{n} = \text{const}$): A realistic process where some heat is lost, $1 < n < \gamma$. The exponent $n$ is called the **polytropic index**.

In real industrial practice, multi-stage compressors with intercooling are designed to **approach isothermal compression**, since isothermal work is the theoretical minimum.

## 2.2 Why Multi-Stage Compression with Intercooling?

A single-stage compressor with a high pressure ratio has serious problems:
- High discharge temperature (theoretically up to $T_2 = T_1 (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$).
- High work input.
- Poor volumetric efficiency at high pressure ratios.
- Lubricant degradation and mechanical stress.

**Solution:** Compress in multiple stages with **intercoolers** between stages. Intercooling reduces the temperature of gas back to (or near) the inlet temperature before it enters the next stage, drastically reducing compression work.

> **The KTU Rule of Thumb:** The total work of compression is minimum when the **pressure ratio is the same in each stage**, i.e., for an $N$-stage compressor with overall pressure ratio $r_p = P_2/P_1$:

$$r_{\text{stage}} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/N} = r_p^{1/N}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 examinations on this topic. Memorize the units, boundary conditions, and what each symbol represents.

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass of air compressed | $m = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{R_{air} T_1}$ | kg | From ideal gas law |
| Isothermal work per kg | $W_{iso} = R_{air} T_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | kJ/kg | Minimum possible work |
| Isentropic work per kg | $W_{isen} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R_{air} T_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$ | kJ/kg | Ideal adiabatic work |
| Polytropic work per kg | $W_{poly} = \dfrac{n}{n - 1} R_{air} T_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$ | kJ/kg | Realistic process, $1 < n < \gamma$ |
| Isothermal efficiency | $\eta_{iso} = \dfrac{W_{iso}}{W_{actual}}$ | — | Fraction of actual work that is ideally needed |
| Isentropic (adiabatic) efficiency | $\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{W_{isen}}{W_{actual}}$ | — | Most commonly used in KTU problems |
| Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{swept}} = 1 + c - c \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}$ | — | $c$ = clearance ratio |
| Clearance ratio | $c = \dfrac{V_c}{V_s}$ | — | $V_c$ = clearance volume, $V_s$ = swept volume |
| Specific Power Consumption | $SPC = \dfrac{\text{Power input (kW)}}{\text{Free air delivery (m}^3\text{/min)}}$ | kW per m³/min | Lower is better |
| Brake power required | $BP = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot W_{actual}}{60}$ | kW | $\dot{m}$ in kg/min, $W$ in kJ/kg |
| Discharge temp (isentropic) | $T_2 = T_1 \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | K | Temperature after one stage |
| Total stage ratio (multi-stage) | $r_{stage} = (P_2/P_1)^{1/N}$ | — | $N$ = number of stages |
| Receiver sizing | $V_{rec} = \dfrac{Q \cdot t}{P_2 - P_1} \cdot C$ | m³ | $Q$ = flow, $t$ = time, $C$ = constant |
| Leakage loss | $L = k \cdot \Delta P$ | Nm³/min | Proportional to pressure differential |
| Pressure drop loss | $\Delta P = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | kPa | Darcy–Weisbach formula |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulas matter in the field:**

1. **Equipment Selection** — Engineers use $\eta_{isen}$ and SPC to choose between reciprocating, screw, and centrifugal units for a given flow and pressure requirement.
2. **Audit Calculations** — During an energy audit, the auditor measures inlet temperature, inlet/outlet pressures, and flow rate, then back-calculates the **theoretical isentropic power** and compares it with the **actual measured power** to quantify potential savings.
3. **Cost Reduction** — A drop in $\eta_{isen}$ of just 5 % in a 100 kW compressor running 6000 hours/year at ₹6/kWh translates to an annual loss of ₹1.8 lakhs.
4. **Compressor Control Strategy** — Modern VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) controlled compressors are sized to operate near their **best efficiency point (BEP)**; off-design operation wastes enormous energy.
5. **Receiver and Piping Design** — Proper sizing reduces pressure fluctuations, lowers leakage, and allows the compressor to run in efficient load/unload mode instead of modulating.

## 2.5 Energy Conservation Opportunities (KTU Module 4 Core Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are the **high-yield energy-saving measures** that KTU examiners frequently test:

1. **Reduce inlet air temperature** — A 4 °C drop in intake temperature can save ~1 % compressor power.
2. **Reduce pressure setting** — Every 1 bar reduction in discharge pressure saves ~6–7 % power.
3. **Eliminate leaks** — A 3 mm hole at 7 bar wastes ~1200 kWh/year of compressed air.
4. **Use VFD for variable demand** — Avoids unload-mode power wastage.
5. **Heat recovery** — Use compressor waste heat for water heating, space heating, or absorption chilling.
6. **Optimize intercooling** — Approach 5–10 °C above ambient in intercoolers.
7. **Switch to oil-free or VSD screw compressors** where feasible.
8. **Right-size the receiver** to reduce compressor cycling.
9. **Use efficient end-use devices** — e.g., low-pressure drop fittings, larger diameter pipes.
10. **Maintain filters and dryers** — A clogged filter increases work of compression.
