# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Work of Isothermal Compression

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Point:** The First Law of Thermodynamics for an open system (steady-flow compressor) with negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy gives shaft work per unit mass:

$$w_s = -\int_{1}^{2} v \, dP$$

This is a fundamental result for any **steady-flow reversible process**. The negative sign follows the thermodynamic sign convention (work done *on* the system is positive in engineering; we use positive values by inverting the integral limits).

**Step 1:** For an isothermal process of an ideal gas, $T = \text{constant}$ and $Pv = R_{air} T = \text{constant}$, so:

$$v = \frac{R_{air} T_1}{P}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute this specific volume into the work integral:

$$w_{iso} = -\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{R_{air} T_1}{P} \, dP$$

**Step 3:** Pull the constants out of the integral (both $R_{air}$ and $T_1$ are constants during isothermal compression):

$$w_{iso} = -R_{air} T_1 \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{P}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integral. The integral of $1/P$ is $\ln P$. So:

$$w_{iso} = -R_{air} T_1 \left[ \ln P \right]_{P_1}^{P_2}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the limits. The result is negative; flipping the limits makes it positive (work input):

$$w_{iso} = R_{air} T_1 \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$

**Step 6:** Verify the units:

$$R_{air} = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}, \quad T_1 = 300 \text{ K} \Rightarrow R_{air} T_1 = 86.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

If the pressure ratio is, say, 8, then $w_{iso} = 86.1 \times \ln(8) = 86.1 \times 2.079 = 179.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Work of Isentropic (Adiabatic Reversible) Compression

**Step 1:** For a reversible adiabatic process, $Pv^{\gamma} = \text{const}$. We need $v$ as a function of $P$:

$$v = \left(\frac{\text{const}}{P}\right)^{1/\gamma} = \text{const} \cdot P^{-1/\gamma}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the shaft-work integral:

$$w_{isen} = -\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \text{const} \cdot P^{-1/\gamma} \, dP$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the constant. At state 1: $P_1 v_1^{\gamma} = \text{const}$, so $\text{const} = P_1 v_1^{\gamma}$. Then:

$$w_{isen} = -\int_{P_1}^{P_2} P_1 v_1^{\gamma} \cdot P^{-1/\gamma} \, dP = -P_1 v_1^{\gamma} \int_{P_1}^{P_2} P^{-1/\gamma} \, dP$$

**Step 4:** Use $P_1 v_1 = R_{air} T_1$ to write $v_1 = R_{air} T_1 / P_1$:

$$P_1 v_1^{\gamma} = P_1 \left(\frac{R_{air} T_1}{P_1}\right)^{\gamma} = \frac{(R_{air} T_1)^{\gamma}}{P_1^{\gamma - 1}}$$

**Step 5:** The integral of $P^{-1/\gamma}$ is $\dfrac{P^{1 - 1/\gamma}}{1 - 1/\gamma} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} P^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. Carrying the constants:

$$w_{isen} = -\frac{(R_{air} T_1)^{\gamma}}{P_1^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \left[ P^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right]_{P_1}^{P_2}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute the limits:

$$w_{isen} = -\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} (R_{air} T_1)^{\gamma} P_1^{-(\gamma - 1)} \left( P_2^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - P_1^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right)$$

**Step 7:** Factor out $P_1^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ from the parenthesis:

$$w_{isen} = -\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R_{air} T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 8:** Remove the negative sign (work input is positive):

$$\boxed{w_{isen} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R_{air} T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right] \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}}$$

**Numerical Check:** With $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_2/P_1 = 8$, $\gamma = 1.4$:

$$w_{isen} = \frac{1.4}{0.4} \times 0.287 \times 300 \times \left[ 8^{0.2857} - 1 \right] = 3.5 \times 86.1 \times (1.811 - 1) = 301.4 \times 0.811 = 244.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Compare with isothermal: **179.0 kJ/kg** → isentropic requires ~36 % more work, confirming the value of intercooling.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Optimal Stage Pressure Ratio (Two-Stage Compression)

**Step 1:** For a 2-stage compressor with intercooling to the initial temperature $T_1$, the total isentropic work is:

$$w_{total} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R_{air} T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} + \left(\frac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 2 \right]$$

where $P_x$ is the intermediate (intercooler) pressure.

**Step 2:** The factor $1/\gamma$ in the exponent makes the expression differentiable. Define $a = (\gamma - 1)/\gamma$ for clarity. Then:

$$w_{total} = K \left[ \left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{a} + \left(\frac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{a} - 2 \right], \quad K = \frac{\gamma R_{air} T_1}{\gamma - 1}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate $w_{total}$ with respect to $P_x$ and set to zero for minimum work:

$$\frac{dw_{total}}{dP_x} = K \left[ a \cdot P_1^{-a} \cdot P_x^{a-1} - a \cdot P_2^{a} \cdot P_x^{-a-1} \right] = 0$$

**Step 4:** Divide by $K a$ and separate:

$$P_1^{-a} P_x^{a-1} = P_2^{a} P_x^{-a-1}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply both sides by $P_x^{a+1}$:

$$P_1^{-a} P_x^{2a} = P_2^{a}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $P_x$:

$$P_x^{2a} = P_1^{a} P_2^{a} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_x^{2} = P_1 P_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$$

**Step 7:** The optimal intermediate pressure is the **geometric mean** of inlet and discharge pressures. Substituting back:

$$\frac{P_x}{P_1} = \frac{P_2}{P_x} = \sqrt{\frac{P_2}{P_1}} = r_p^{1/2}$$

This generalises to $N$ stages:

$$\frac{P_x}{P_1} = \frac{P_2}{P_x} = \cdots = r_p^{1/N}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consequence:** For the example above with overall $r_p = 8$, optimal 2-stage ratio is $\sqrt{8} = 2.83$ per stage. Each stage does the same work.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Volumetric Efficiency with Clearance

**Step 1:** Define the **clearance volume** $V_c$ (volume remaining in the cylinder at the end of the discharge stroke) and the **swept volume** $V_s$ (volume displaced by the piston). Clearance ratio:

$$c = \frac{V_c}{V_s}$$

**Step 2:** At the end of the discharge stroke, the air in the clearance volume is at pressure $P_2$. As the piston retracts, this air expands isentropically back to the inlet pressure $P_1$. The volume at which it reaches $P_1$ is $V_4$:

$$V_4 = V_c \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}$$

**Step 3:** The piston only begins to **draw in fresh air** at $P_1$ once the clearance air has expanded down to $P_1$. The actual volume of fresh air drawn in is:

$$V_{actual} = V_s - (V_4 - V_c) = V_s - V_c \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 4:** Divide by the swept volume $V_s$:

$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_s} = 1 - c \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma} - 1 \right] = 1 + c - c \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}$$

**Step 5:** The volumetric efficiency can be made 100 % even with clearance if the **re-expansion** is exactly cancelled, e.g., by holding the inlet valve closed (a **delayed suction** technique).

> **Numerical Check:** For $c = 0.05$ and $P_2/P_1 = 4$:

$$\eta_{vol} = 1 + 0.05 - 0.05 \times 4^{0.714} = 1.05 - 0.05 \times 2.639 = 1.05 - 0.132 = 0.918 \approx 91.8\%$$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Table — Three Compression Processes

| Parameter | Isothermal | Isentropic | Polytropic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Process law | $Pv = \text{const}$ | $Pv^{\gamma} = \text{const}$ | $Pv^{n} = \text{const}$ |
| Heat transfer | Maximum (heat removed) | Zero | Partial |
| Work per kg | Minimum | Maximum (for $r_p > 1$) | In between |
| Discharge temperature | $T_1$ (no change) | $T_1 (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | $T_1 (P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n}$ |
| Efficiency definition | $\eta_{iso} = W_{iso}/W_{act}$ | $\eta_{isen} = W_{isen}/W_{act}$ | $\eta_{poly} = W_{poly}/W_{act}$ |
| Achievability | Approached with intercooling | Approached with very fast, insulated compression | Real machines |

## 3.6 Power-Saving Numerical (Worked for KTU 2017 Pattern)

> A 2-stage reciprocating air compressor delivers 10 m³/min of free air at 8 bar (gauge) from inlet conditions of 1 bar and 30 °C. Assume isentropic compression with complete intercooling to 30 °C. The mechanical efficiency is 90 % and the motor efficiency is 92 %. Calculate the actual motor power input. Use $\gamma = 1.4$ and $R_{air} = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$.

**Solution Steps:**

**Step 1:** Absolute pressures: $P_1 = 1$ bar, $P_2 = 8 + 1 = 9$ bar. Overall ratio: $r_p = 9$.

**Step 2:** Optimal intermediate pressure: $P_x = \sqrt{1 \times 9} = 3$ bar.

**Step 3:** Per-stage pressure ratio: $r_{stage} = \sqrt{9} = 3$.

**Step 4:** Isentropic work per kg per stage:

$$w_{isen,stage} = \frac{1.4}{0.4} \times 0.287 \times 303 \times \left[ 3^{0.2857} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 5:** Compute $3^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \ln 3} = e^{0.3137} = 1.3685$. So:

$$w_{isen,stage} = 3.5 \times 86.96 \times 0.3685 = 112.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 6:** With complete intercooling, both stages have the same work. Total isentropic work:

$$w_{total,isen} = 2 \times 112.1 = 224.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 7:** Mass flow rate. From ideal gas: $P_1 V_1 = m R_{air} T_1$:

$$m = \frac{P_1 V_1}{R_{air} T_1} = \frac{100 \times 10}{0.287 \times 303} = \frac{1000}{86.96} = 11.50 \text{ kg/min}$$

(Note: $P_1$ in kPa = 100; $V_1 = 10$ m³/min.)

**Step 8:** Isentropic power required:

$$P_{isen} = \frac{11.50 \times 224.2}{60} = 42.97 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 9:** Actual brake power (shaft power) accounting for mechanical efficiency:

$$P_{brake} = \frac{42.97}{0.90} = 47.74 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 10:** Motor input power accounting for motor efficiency:

$$\boxed{P_{motor} = \frac{47.74}{0.92} = 51.89 \text{ kW} \approx 52 \text{ kW}}$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Industrial Compressed Air System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a complete industrial compressed air network, showing the principal components and the energy/material flows. This is the kind of system-level picture that KTU questions on "compressed air system audit" typically expect students to draw and annotate.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Atmospheric Air Inlet] --> A2[Inlet Filter and Pre-Cooler]
    A2 --> A3[Compressor Unit]
    M1[Electric Motor Prime Mover] --> A3
    A3 --> A4[After-Cooler Stage 1]
    A4 --> A5[Inter-Cooler]
    A5 --> A3
    A3 --> A6[After-Cooler Stage N]
    A6 --> A7[Receiver Storage Tank]
    A7 --> A8[Refrigerated Dryer]
    A8 --> A9[Desiccant Dryer Optional]
    A9 --> A10[Particulate and Oil Removal Filters]
    A10 --> A11[Main Distribution Header]
    A11 --> A12[Branch Piping Network]
    A12 --> A13[End-Use Point 1 Pneumatic Tool]
    A12 --> A14[End-Use Point 2 Actuator]
    A12 --> A15[End-Use Point 3 Process]
    A12 --> A16[End-Use Point 4 Instrumentation]
    A3 -. Waste Heat Recovery .- H1[Hot Water / Space Heating / Absorption Chiller]
    A7 -. Leakage Path .- L1[Audible and Hidden Leaks]
    A11 -. Pressure Drop .- PD1[Fittings Valves Elbows]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - The **solid arrows** denote the normal air path from atmosphere to end-use.
> - The **dashed arrows** denote the two principal **loss / recovery** streams: (a) recoverable waste heat from the compressor and (b) leakage and pressure-drop losses that an energy auditor would measure.

## 4.2 Energy Flow Block Diagram — Audit-Friendly View

This diagram focuses specifically on the **energy balance** through the compressor system, which is the core of KTU Module 4 questions on energy audit calculations.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Electrical Energy Input to Motor] --> M1[Motor Loss 5 to 10 percent]
    M1 --> P2[Mechanical Energy at Coupling]
    P2 --> P3[Compressor Body]
    P3 --> P4[Isentropic Useful Air Energy]
    P3 --> P5[Compressor Heat Loss Jacket Cooling]
    P3 --> P6[After-Cooler Heat Rejection]
    P3 --> P7[Leakage and Unloading Loss]
    P4 --> P8[Useful Compressed Air at End-Use]
    P8 --> P9[Productive Work at Tool]
    P8 --> P10[Pressure Drop Losses in Distribution]
    P8 --> P11[Inappropriate Use Losses]
    P5 -. Heat Recovery Option .- HR1[Recovered Hot Water]
    P6 -. Heat Recovery Option .- HR2[Recovered Heat for Boiler Feed]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Audit Insight:** In a typical plant, of 100 units of electrical energy input to the motor, only **10–20 units** reach the productive end-use. The energy auditor's job is to identify the largest streams among M1, P5, P6, P7, P10, P11 and recommend improvements.

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Compressor Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Compressor Selection Problem] --> S2{Flow Rate Required m3 per min}
    S2 --> S2a{Less than 0.5}
    S2 --> S2b{Between 0.5 and 30}
    S2 --> S2c{Greater than 30}
    S2a --> R1[Reciprocating Piston Compressor]
    S2b --> Q1{Pressure Ratio}
    Q1 --> Q1a{Greater than 6}
    Q1 --> Q1b{Less than 6}
    Q1a --> R2[Two-Stage Reciprocating or Screw]
    Q1b --> R3[Single-Stage Reciprocating or Rotary Screw]
    S2c --> Q2{Discharge Pressure}
    Q2 --> Q2a{Less than 10 bar}
    Q2 --> Q2b{Greater than 10 bar}
    Q2a --> R4[Centrifugal Compressor]
    Q2b --> R5[Axial Flow Multi-Stage Compressor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick KTU mnemonic:** **"Low flow, high pressure → Positive Displacement; High flow, low pressure → Dynamic."**

## 4.4 Logic Flow for Compressor Energy Audit (Sequence of Audit Steps)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start Audit] --> A1[Collect Operating Data: Flow, Pressure, Power, Temperature]
    A1 --> A2[Compute Specific Power Consumption SPC]
    A2 --> A3[Compare SPC with Manufacturer Best Point]
    A3 --> Q1{SPC Greater than Benchmark?}
    Q1 --> Q1a[No: Compressor is efficient]
    Q1 --> Q1b[Yes: Proceed to Loss Quantification]
    Q1b --> A4[Measure Intake Temperature and Pressure]
    A4 --> A5[Compute Theoretical Isentropic Power]
    A5 --> A6[Compute Overall Efficiency = W isen over W actual]
    A6 --> A7[Quantify Leakage via No-Load Orifice Test]
    A7 --> A8[Quantify Pressure Drop Across Dryer and Filters]
    A8 --> A9[Quantify Receiver Sizing Adequacy]
    A9 --> A10[Recommend Actions: VSD, Intercooler Cleaning, Leak Fix, Receiver Resize]
    A10 --> A11[Estimate Annual Energy and Cost Savings]
    A11 --> A12[Payback Period and Implementation Plan]
    A12 --> A13[End Audit Report]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** When asked to "describe a compressed air system audit," the KTU 2019 and 2022 papers rewarded students who listed the audit steps **in sequence** and stated the **formulas used at each step**. Use this flow as a memory aid.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 (similar)]**

**Q.** Define **isothermal efficiency** and **isentropic efficiency** of a compressor. State the corresponding formulas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer:**
>
> - **Isothermal Efficiency** is defined as the ratio of the theoretical isothermal work (minimum work) to the actual work input of the compressor:
>
> $$\eta_{iso} = \frac{W_{iso}}{W_{actual}} = \frac{R_{air} T_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)}{W_{actual}}$$
>
> - **Isentropic (Adiabatic) Efficiency** is the ratio of the ideal isentropic work to the actual work input:
>
> $$\eta_{isen} = \frac{W_{isen}}{W_{actual}} = \frac{\dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} R_{air} T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]}{W_{actual}}$$
>
> - Isothermal efficiency is used for cooled compressors; isentropic efficiency is used for uncooled (adiabatic) compressors.
> - In both cases, **higher numerical value indicates better compressor performance**.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 (similar)]**

**Q.** List any **six energy conservation opportunities** in a compressed air system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (any six of the following are accepted):**
>
> 1. **Reduce inlet air temperature** — Locate the compressor in a cool, shaded, well-ventilated area; every 4 °C reduction saves ~1 % power.
> 2. **Lower discharge pressure** — Each 1 bar reduction in pressure setting saves ~6–7 % energy.
> 3. **Eliminate leaks** — A 3 mm leak at 7 bar wastes ~1200 kWh/year; conduct ultrasonic leak surveys.
> 4. **Use VFD / VSD** for variable demand — Avoids unload-mode power wastage which can be 25–30 % of full-load power.
> 5. **Recover waste heat** — Use jacket cooling water for boiler feed or absorption chilling.
> 6. **Optimize intercooling** — Approach inlet air temperature in intercoolers.
> 7. **Right-size the receiver** — Reduces compressor cycling and improves part-load efficiency.
> 8. **Maintain filters and dryers** — Clogged filters increase work of compression by 2–5 %.
> 9. **Use larger diameter pipes** to reduce pressure drop in the distribution network.
> 10. **Switch off idle compressors** — Automatic sequencer for multi-compressor stations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Forgetting to convert **gauge pressure to absolute** before computing pressure ratios (a 7 bar gauge reading actually means 8 bar absolute — students frequently lose 2–3 marks here).
> - Using the **wrong gas constant** ($R$ = 8.314 kJ/kmol·K instead of $R_{air}$ = 0.287 kJ/kg·K) — a single point that often becomes a "show your substitution" decider.
> - Stating the **isentropic work formula** without explicitly writing the conditions under which it applies (reversible, adiabatic, ideal gas).
> - For multi-stage problems, forgetting that the **intercooler restores the temperature to T₁** (it does not just "cool by some amount").
> - Not specifying the **units of specific power consumption** (kW per m³/min is the KTU-expected unit; cfm and m³/h are alternative conventions).

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**Q. (a)** Derive the expression for the **work of isentropic compression** of an ideal gas in a steady-flow compressor. State clearly the assumptions. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** A single-stage reciprocating air compressor compresses 5 m³/min of free air from 1.013 bar and 27 °C to 8 bar (gauge). Assuming isentropic compression with $\gamma = 1.4$ and $R_{air} = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, determine: (i) the mass of air compressed per minute, (ii) the isentropic work per kg, (iii) the isentropic power required. If the mechanical efficiency is 88 % and motor efficiency is 90 %, find the **actual motor power input**. (7 marks)

> **Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Air behaves as an **ideal gas** ($Pv = R_{air} T$).
2. The process is **reversible and adiabatic** (no heat transfer, no friction).
3. Changes in **kinetic and potential energy** of the air stream are negligible.
4. **Steady-state, steady-flow** process.

**Derivation (6 marks):**

The shaft work per unit mass of air for any steady-flow reversible process is given by the thermodynamic identity:

$$w_s = -\int_{1}^{2} v \, dP$$

For an isentropic process, $Pv^{\gamma} = \text{const}$, hence $v^{\gamma} = P_1 v_1^{\gamma} / P$, i.e., $v = (P_1 v_1^{\gamma})^{1/\gamma} P^{-1/\gamma}$. Substituting:

$$w_{isen} = -\int_{P_1}^{P_2} (P_1 v_1^{\gamma})^{1/\gamma} \, P^{-1/\gamma} \, dP$$

$$w_{isen} = -(P_1 v_1^{\gamma})^{1/\gamma} \int_{P_1}^{P_2} P^{-1/\gamma} \, dP$$

Using $P_1 v_1 = R_{air} T_1$, we obtain $P_1 v_1^{\gamma} = (R_{air} T_1)^{\gamma} P_1^{1-\gamma}$. Evaluating the integral:

$$w_{isen} = -\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R_{air} T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$

Removing the negative sign (work input is positive):

$$\boxed{w_{isen} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R_{air} T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right] \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Free air delivery (FAD) $V_1 = 5 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$
- $P_1 = 1.013 \text{ bar} = 101.3 \text{ kPa}$
- $T_1 = 27 \,^{\circ}\text{C} = 300 \text{ K}$
- $P_2 = 8 \text{ bar (gauge)} = 9.013 \text{ bar (absolute)} = 901.3 \text{ kPa}$
- $\gamma = 1.4$, $R_{air} = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $\eta_{mech} = 0.88$, $\eta_{motor} = 0.90$

**(i) Mass of air per minute (2 marks):**

$$m = \frac{P_1 V_1}{R_{air} T_1} = \frac{101.3 \times 5}{0.287 \times 300} = \frac{506.5}{86.1} = 5.88 \text{ kg/min}$$

**(ii) Isentropic work per kg (2 marks):**

Pressure ratio: $r_p = 901.3 / 101.3 = 8.898$

$$(\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.4/1.4 = 0.2857$$

$$r_p^{0.2857} = 8.898^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \ln 8.898} = e^{0.6216} = 1.862$$

$$w_{isen} = \frac{1.4}{0.4} \times 0.287 \times 300 \times (1.862 - 1) = 3.5 \times 86.1 \times 0.862 = 259.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(iii) Isentropic power required (1.5 marks):**

$$P_{isen} = \frac{m \cdot w_{isen}}{60} = \frac{5.88 \times 259.7}{60} = \frac{1527}{60} = 25.45 \text{ kW}$$

**Actual motor power input (1.5 marks):**

$$P_{motor} = \frac{P_{isen}}{\eta_{mech} \times \eta_{motor}} = \frac{25.45}{0.88 \times 0.90} = \frac{25.45}{0.792} = 32.13 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{motor} \approx 32.1 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**Q. (a)** Explain with a **neat sketch** the working of a **two-stage reciprocating air compressor with intercooling**. Show that the **intermediate pressure** for minimum work is $\sqrt{P_1 P_2}$. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** A two-stage air compressor compresses air from 1 bar and 20 °C to 25 bar. The air is cooled back to 20 °C in the intercooler. Assuming complete intercooling, $\gamma = 1.4$ and $R_{air} = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, determine: (i) the **optimum intermediate pressure**, (ii) the **work done per kg of air**, (iii) the **percentage saving** in work compared to a single-stage compressor delivering the same final pressure. (7 marks)

> **Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Two-Stage Reciprocating Compressor (7 marks)

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

A two-stage reciprocating compressor consists of **two cylinders** — a **low-pressure (LP) cylinder** and a **high-pressure (HP) cylinder** — connected via an **intercooler**.

1. Air enters the LP cylinder at $P_1$, $T_1$ and is compressed to the intermediate pressure $P_x$ (typically $\sqrt{P_1 P_2}$).
2. The hot, compressed air leaves the LP cylinder and passes through the **intercooler**, where it is cooled (ideally) back to $T_1$.
3. The cooled air then enters the HP cylinder, where it is compressed from $P_x$ to the final pressure $P_2$.
4. The HP cylinder discharges the high-pressure air to the receiver.

This staged compression with intercooling achieves two benefits:
- **Lower work of compression** (the isothermal limit is approached).
- **Lower discharge temperature**, prolonging lubricant life and reducing mechanical stress.

**Proof of Optimum Intermediate Pressure (3 marks):**

The total isentropic work for two stages is:

$$w_{total} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R_{air} T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} + \left(\frac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 2 \right]$$

Differentiating with respect to $P_x$ and setting $\dfrac{dw_{total}}{dP_x} = 0$:

$$\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} P_x^{(1-2\gamma)/\gamma} \left[ P_1^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \cdot P_2^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - P_x^{2(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right] = 0$$

The non-trivial solution gives $P_x^{2(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = P_1^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \cdot P_2^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$, hence:

$$\boxed{P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}}$$

This can be generalised: for an $N$-stage compressor, each stage has pressure ratio $r_p^{1/N} = (P_2/P_1)^{1/N}$.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar} = 100 \text{ kPa}$
- $T_1 = 20 \,^{\circ}\text{C} = 293 \text{ K}$
- $P_2 = 25 \text{ bar} = 2500 \text{ kPa}$
- Complete intercooling to 293 K
- $\gamma = 1.4$, $R_{air} = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$

**(i) Optimum intermediate pressure (2 marks):**

$$P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2} = \sqrt{1 \times 25} = 5 \text{ bar}$$

Per-stage pressure ratio: $r_{stage} = \sqrt{25} = 5$

**(ii) Work done per kg (3 marks):**

For each stage (with intercooling restoring $T_1$):

$$w_{stage} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R_{air} T_1 \left[ r_{stage}^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$

Compute $5^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \ln 5} = e^{0.4585} = 1.5818$:

$$w_{stage} = 3.5 \times 0.287 \times 293 \times (1.5818 - 1) = 3.5 \times 84.09 \times 0.5818 = 171.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Total work for two stages:

$$w_{2\text{-stage}} = 2 \times 171.2 = 342.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(iii) Percentage saving (2 marks):**

For a single-stage compressor, $r_p = 25$, so $r_p^{0.2857} = 25^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \times 3.2189} = e^{0.9196} = 2.508$:

$$w_{1\text{-stage}} = 3.5 \times 84.09 \times (2.508 - 1) = 294.3 \times 1.508 = 443.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Percentage saving:

$$\text{Saving \%} = \frac{443.8 - 342.4}{443.8} \times 100 = \frac{101.4}{443.8} \times 100 = 22.85\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Saving} \approx 22.85\%}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Module 4 — Compressors — Revision Bullets**

- **Definition:** A compressor is a steady-flow device that raises the pressure of a gas by reducing its specific volume using mechanical work.
- **Two main classes:** Positive-Displacement (reciprocating, rotary screw, vane, liquid-ring) and Dynamic / Turbo (centrifugal, axial-flow).
- **Thermodynamic processes to remember:** Isothermal ($Pv = \text{const}$), Isentropic ($Pv^{\gamma} = \text{const}$), Polytropic ($Pv^{n} = \text{const}$). Work increases in that order.
- **Key formulas you must memorize:**
  - $w_{iso} = R_{air} T_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$
  - $w_{isen} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R_{air} T_1 \left[ (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$
  - $T_2 = T_1 (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$
  - $\eta_{vol} = 1 + c - c (P_2/P_1)^{1/\gamma}$
  - $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$ for two-stage optimum
- **Efficiencies:** Isothermal, Isentropic, Mechanical, Volumetric, Overall. Know definitions and formulas.
- **Specific Power Consumption (SPC):** Lower is better; KTU expects **kW per m³/min** or **kW per 100 cfm**.
- **Multi-stage compression rule:** For minimum work, pressure ratio in each stage is equal: $r_{stage} = (P_2/P_1)^{1/N}$.
- **Always use absolute pressures** in compression formulas — gauge readings need +1 bar correction.
- **Always use $R_{air} = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$** for air; never substitute $R_u = 8.314$ unless molar quantities are involved.
- **Energy conservation (high-yield, exam-favourite):** Reduce inlet temperature, reduce discharge pressure, fix leaks, use VFD, recover heat, optimise intercooling, right-size the receiver, maintain filters.
- **Audit numbers to remember:** Compressed air system is typically 10–25 % of plant electricity; only 10–20 % reaches useful end-use; 1 bar pressure drop ≈ 6–7 % energy loss; 3 mm leak at 7 bar ≈ 1200 kWh/year wasted.
- **Mnemonic for compressor selection:** "Low flow & high pressure → Reciprocating; High flow & low pressure → Centrifugal."
- **Pitfall to avoid in KTU papers:** Forgetting to divide by $\eta_{mech}$ and $\eta_{motor}$ when calculating **electrical input** from **indicated / isentropic power**.

> **End of Module 4 — Compressors | KTU 2024 Scheme PESFT741**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cogeneration (Steam and Gas Turbines)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Cogeneration (Steam and Gas Turbines) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) **Cogeneration**, also known as **Combined Heat and Power (CHP)**, is defined as the simultaneous generation of two forms of useful energy — **electrical (or mechanical) energy** and **useful thermal energy (process heat or steam)** — from a single primary fuel input in a single integrated system. It is governed by the Second Law of Thermodynamics principle of matching energy quality to end-use demand, thereby capturing and utilizing the otherwise-wasted heat from power generation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Verbatim:** "Cogeneration is the sequential production of two forms of useful energy from a single fuel source, typically producing electricity and process steam/heat, thereby achieving an overall energy utilization factor far greater than conventional separate generation." ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Idli Cooker Engine" Imagine a kitchen where you burn LPG to cook idlis on a stove. The flame heats the idli pot (useful thermal energy) and you throw away the hot exhaust gases — that's conventional separate generation. Now, suppose the same flame first turns a small turbine to light a bulb (electrical energy), and the *waste* heat from that turbine still boils the idli pot (thermal energy). The fuel has done **double duty**. This is cogeneration — **one fuel, two useful products**. In a conventional power plant, the thermal efficiency is only **30–40 %** because two-thirds of the fuel's energy escapes as condenser cooling or flue gas. Cogeneration lifts the **Total Energy Utilization** to **70–85 %**. ### 1.3 Fundamental Performance Metrics (Mandatory KTU Terms) > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners expect these three acronyms *verbatim* in any cogeneration problem. | Metric | Symbol | Definition | |---|---|---| | **Heat-to-Power Ratio** | HPR (or $\beta$) | Ratio of useful heat output to net power output | | **Energy Utilization Factor** | EUF | Fraction of input fuel energy converted to useful work + useful heat | | **Overall (Total) Efficiency** | $\eta_{overall}$ | Sum of electrical efficiency and thermal efficiency of useful heat | ### 1.4 Classification by Cycle Position (KTU 2024 — High Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU repeatedly asks: *"Differentiate between topping and bottoming cycles."* - **Topping Cycle** — Fuel is first converted to **power**; the rejected heat is then used for process. Most common in industry. **Example:** Gas Turbine + Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG). - **Bottoming Cycle** — Fuel is first converted to **process heat**; the rejected (high-temperature) heat then drives a power cycle. **Example:** Blast furnace gas → waste-heat boiler → steam turbine. ### 1.5 Why Cogeneration Matters — Engineering Rationale - A conventional **Rankine** plant rejects **≈ 50–60 %** of fuel energy to the condenser. - A conventional **Gas Turbine** rejects **≈ 65–70 %** of fuel energy via exhaust. - Cogeneration converts this rejected heat into a **saleable product** (process steam at 3–25 bar or hot water at 120–200 °C). - **CO₂ emissions drop by 30–40 %** per unit of useful energy delivered. - Industries with simultaneous power + steam demand (paper, textiles, sugar, refineries, chemicals, dairies) are *ideal* candidates. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Cogeneration System Classification (KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus) #### A. Steam Turbine Based Cogeneration Systems The steam turbine is the *workhorse* of industrial CHP. Three principal configurations are examined: 1. **Back-Pressure Turbine (No Condenser)** — All exhaust steam from the turbine is delivered to the process at a useful pressure (typically 1–10 bar). There is **no condenser**. The turbine is sized so that its **exhaust enthalpy exactly matches the process steam demand**. - *Advantage:* Highest thermal efficiency, no cooling water needed. - *Disadvantage:* Power output is tied directly to heat demand. 2. **Extraction-Condensing Turbine** — Steam is partially extracted from intermediate turbine stages at a controlled pressure for process use, while the remaining steam continues to expand into a condenser to produce additional power. - *Advantage:* Power and heat outputs can be varied **independently** by adjusting the extraction control valve. - *Disadvantage:* More complex, lower total EUF than back-pressure at full heat demand. 3. **Mixed / Combination Arrangement** — A back-pressure turbine supplies a base process-heat load; an extraction-condensing turbine meets the *variable* portion. This is the most common KTU industrial case study. #### B. Gas Turbine Based Cogeneration Systems A simple-cycle gas turbine (compressor + combustor + turbine) inherently produces a high-temperature exhaust (typically **450–550 °C**). This exhaust is routed through a **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)** to produce process steam. - **Configuration:** Compressor → Combustor → Gas Turbine → HRSG → Process/Steam Network - The gas turbine's **waste heat becomes a free fuel** for steam generation. - **Supplementary Firing:** A small duct burner in the HRSG elevates steam temperature to match process demand. - **Electrical efficiency:** 25–35 %; **Total CHP efficiency:** 75–85 %. ### 2.2 Analytical Logic — How a Back-Pressure Cogenerator Works (Step-by-Step) - **Step 1 — Fuel combustion:** Fuel is burned in a boiler to generate high-pressure superheated steam (e.g., 40 bar, 400 °C). - **Step 2 — Expansion in turbine:** Steam expands through the turbine to a back-pressure of 3–10 bar (instead of exhausting to vacuum at 0.1 bar as in a condensing plant). - **Step 3 — Work extraction:** Because exhaust pressure is higher than a condenser, the *enthalpy drop* is smaller → less electrical work, but much higher exhaust temperature. - **Step 4 — Heat delivery:** The exhaust steam is saturated or superheated at 3–10 bar → 130–300 °C. This is *exactly* what industries need. - **Step 5 — Mass/energy balance:** $W_{net} = m_s(h_1 - h_2)$ and $Q_{process} = m_s(h_2 - h_3)$ where $h_3$ is the enthalpy of the condensate returning to the boiler. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > Every formula in this table has appeared in a KTU 2024 Scheme question paper. Memorize the symbols and units. | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Net Power Output | $W_{net} = \sum \dot{m}_i (h_{in,i} - h_{out,i})$ | kW | Sum across all streams | | 2 | Useful Heat Output | $Q_{useful} = \dot{m}_{steam}(h_{exhaust} - h_{feedwater})$ | kW | $h_{fw}$ at return temperature | | 3 | Fuel Energy Input | $Q_{fuel} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \times GCV$ | kW | GCV in kJ/kg | | 4 | Electrical Efficiency | $\eta_{e} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | — | dimensionless | | 5 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | — | dimensionless | | 6 | **Overall / Total Efficiency** | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{e} + \eta_{th}$ | — | **Can exceed 100 % only in EUF/η terms when stacked with recovered heat** | | 7 | **Energy Utilization Factor (EUF)** | $EUF = \dfrac{W_{net} + Q_{useful}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | — | KTU-preferred metric | | 8 | **Heat-to-Power Ratio** | $HPR = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{1 - \eta_{e}}{\eta_{e}}$ | — | For back-pressure units | | 9 | Fuel Savings (vs separate) | $\Delta F = F_{sep} - F_{cogen}$ | kg/h | Standard CHP justification | | 10 | CO₂ Reduction | $\Delta CO_2 = F_{saved} \times EF_{fuel}$ | kg/h | EF = emission factor | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU trap:** $\eta_{overall}$ is *not* a thermodynamic efficiency in the classical sense. It is a **utilization** factor, and it can exceed unity because it sums outputs of *different qualities* without weighting by exergy. Examiners mark any attempt to apply Carnot arguments to it as **wrong**. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **District Heating** in Scandinavian countries (Copenhagen, Helsinki, Vienna): > 50 % of city heat is CHP. - **Sugar industry** (India, Brazil): bagasse-fired cogeneration in extraction-condensing turbines. - **Refineries & Petrochemicals**: gas turbine + HRSG dominates. - **Combined Cycle Cogeneration (CCC / Cogen with Combined Cycle):** Gas turbine exhaust → HRSG → steam turbine → process. **Overall efficiency: 85–90 %**. ### 2.5 Selection Criterion — When to Choose Steam vs Gas Turbine | Criterion | Steam Turbine | Gas Turbine | |---|---|---| | Process heat pressure | Low to medium (1–40 bar) | Low pressure steam via HRSG | | Process temperature | 120–500 °C | 150–250 °C (up to 500 °C with supplementary firing) | | Power-to-heat ratio | 0.1 – 0.5 | 0.4 – 1.0 | | Capital cost | High (boiler + turbine) | Moderate (compact skid) | | Fuel flexibility | Coal, biomass, bagasse, oil | Natural gas, diesel, naphtha | | Startup time | Slow (hours) | Fast (minutes) | | Best for | Large baseload industry | Variable load, urban installations | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Utilization Factor (EUF) for a Back-Pressure Cogeneration System Consider a back-pressure turbine with the following streams: - Fuel input enthalpy rate: $\dot{Q}_{fuel}$ (kW) - Net electrical power output: $\dot{W}_{net}$ (kW) - Process steam: mass flow $\dot{m}_s$, supply enthalpy $h_{exh}$, returning as feedwater at $h_{fw}$. **Step 1 — First Law on the Turbine + Boiler System (Steady State):** $$ \dot{Q}_{fuel} = \dot{W}_{net} + \dot{Q}_{useful} + \dot{Q}_{loss} $$ where $\dot{Q}_{loss}$ = flue gas loss + radiation + blowdown. For an *ideal* derivation, ignore losses. **Step 2 — Define Useful Outputs:** $$ \dot{W}_{net} = \dot{m}_s \,(h_{1} - h_{2}) $$ $$ \dot{Q}_{useful} = \dot{m}_s \,(h_{2} - h_{fw}) $$ where $h_1$ = turbine inlet enthalpy, $h_2$ = turbine exhaust (process) enthalpy, $h_{fw}$ = feedwater enthalpy. **Step 3 — Substitute into First Law (with losses ignored):** $$ \dot{Q}_{fuel} = \dot{m}_s(h_1 - h_2) + \dot{m}_s(h_2 - h_{fw}) = \dot{m}_s(h_1 - h_{fw}) $$ **Step 4 — Define Energy Utilization Factor:** $$ EUF = \frac{\dot{W}_{net} + \dot{Q}_{useful}}{\dot{Q}_{fuel}} $$ **Step 5 — Substitute:** $$ \begin{aligned} EUF &= \frac{\dot{m}_s(h_1 - h_2) + \dot{m}_s(h_2 - h_{fw})}{\dot{m}_s(h_1 - h_{fw})} \\[6pt] &= \frac{(h_1 - h_2) + (h_2 - h_{fw})}{(h_1 - h_{fw})} \\[6pt] &= \frac{(h_1 - h_{fw})}{(h_1 - h_{fw})} = 1.0 \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** For an *ideal, lossless* back-pressure system, **EUF = 1 (i.e., 100 %)** because *all* fuel enthalpy is converted to either work or useful heat. In real systems, EUF = 0.75–0.85 due to flue gas, radiation, and incomplete combustion. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Heat-to-Power Ratio (HPR) for a Back-Pressure Turbine **Step 1 — Start with the energy balance:** $$ \dot{Q}_{fuel} = \dot{W}_{net} + \dot{Q}_{useful} $$ **Step 2 — Divide throughout by $\dot{W}_{net}$:** $$ \frac{\dot{Q}_{fuel}}{\dot{W}_{net}} = 1 + \frac{\dot{Q}_{useful}}{\dot{W}_{net}} $$ **Step 3 — Recognize HPR:** $$ HPR = \frac{\dot{Q}_{useful}}{\dot{W}_{net}} $$ **Step 4 — Recall $\eta_e = \dot{W}_{net} / \dot{Q}_{fuel}$, so $\dot{Q}_{fuel}/\dot{W}_{net} = 1/\eta_e$:** $$ \frac{1}{\eta_e} = 1 + HPR $$ **Step 5 — Solve for HPR:** $$ HPR = \frac{1}{\eta_e} - 1 = \frac{1 - \eta_e}{\eta_e} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** This simple closed-form expression holds *only* for a back-pressure turbine where the entire turbine exhaust is used for process heat. For an extraction-condensing turbine, you must compute $HPR$ directly from the heat and power flows. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fuel Savings vs Separate Generation Suppose an industry has a heat demand $Q_{process}$ and a power demand $W_{demand}$. In **separate generation**: - Boiler thermal efficiency: $\eta_B$ (typically 0.85 for a modern boiler) - Power plant electrical efficiency: $\eta_{e,sep}$ (typically 0.33) Fuel required separately: $$ F_{sep} = \frac{Q_{process}}{\eta_B} + \frac{W_{demand}}{\eta_{e,sep}} $$ In **cogeneration**, fuel required is: $$ F_{cogen} = \frac{W_{demand} + Q_{process}}{EUF \cdot \eta_B} \quad \text{(approx.)} $$ **Fuel saved:** $$ \Delta F = F_{sep} - F_{cogen} $$ **Fractional saving:** $$ \% \text{Saving} = \frac{\Delta F}{F_{sep}} \times 100 $$ ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 14-mark problem) > **[KTU 2024 Sample — Part B Sub-question Style]** > An industry requires **5 MW of electrical power** and **10 MW of process heat** (as saturated steam at 5 bar). The fuel has GCV = 42 MJ/kg. > A back-pressure steam turbine cogeneration system is proposed with turbine inlet conditions **40 bar, 400 °C** and exhaust at **5 bar saturated**. Feedwater returns at **45 °C**. > Assume boiler efficiency 88 %, mechanical-electrical efficiency 96 %, no other losses. > **Calculate:** (a) Steam mass flow rate, (b) Fuel consumption rate, (c) $\eta_{overall}$, EUF, and HPR. (d) Compare fuel saving against separate generation (separate: boiler $\eta_B$ = 0.85, grid $\eta_{e,sep}$ = 0.33). **Step 1 — Read steam properties (from KTU-approved steam tables):** - $h_1$ (40 bar, 400 °C) = **3213.6 kJ/kg** - $h_2$ (5 bar, saturated) = **h_g @ 5 bar = 2748.7 kJ/kg** - $h_{fw}$ (45 °C, liquid) ≈ $4.18 \times 45$ = **188.1 kJ/kg** **Step 2 — Compute net power and useful heat per kg steam:** Net work from turbine per kg: $$ w_{turb} = h_1 - h_2 = 3213.6 - 2748.7 = 464.9 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ Useful heat per kg (delivered to process + feedwater heating): $$ q_{useful} = h_2 - h_{fw} = 2748.7 - 188.1 = 2560.6 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step 3 — Net electrical power (accounting for mech-electrical efficiency):** $$ w_{net} = w_{turb} \times \eta_{mech-elec} = 464.9 \times 0.96 = 446.3 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step 4 — Steam flow rate from power demand:** $$ \dot{m}_s = \frac{5000 \text{ kW}}{446.3 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 11.20 \text{ kg/s} $$ **Step 5 — Check process heat available:** $$ Q_{process} = 11.20 \times 2560.6 = 28{,}679 \text{ kW} = 28.68 \text{ MW} $$ This is *more* than the 10 MW demanded. ⇒ Steam flow is **governed by power demand** and there is **surplus heat** in this case. To match exactly, we must reduce $\dot{m}_s$. **Step 6 — Re-compute for exact heat match (process governs):** If process governs, $\dot{m}_s = 10{,}000 / 2560.6 = 3.905$ kg/s. Then power generated: $$ W_{net} = 3.905 \times 446.3 = 1743 \text{ kW} = 1.74 \text{ MW} $$ This is *less* than 5 MW demand. ⇒ This back-pressure arrangement **cannot simultaneously meet both demands** with these parameters. A supplementary boiler or extraction-condensing turbine is needed. > [!NOTE] > **KTU trick:** Always check the *dominating constraint*. In most exam problems, the *heat* demand is satisfied and the *power* is then calculated. **Step 7 — Assume process governs (for the rest of the calculation).** Fuel input rate: $$ \dot{Q}_{fuel} = \frac{\dot{m}_s(h_1 - h_{fw})}{\eta_B} = \frac{3.905 \times (3213.6 - 188.1)}{0.88} $$ $$ \dot{Q}_{fuel} = \frac{3.905 \times 3025.5}{0.88} = \frac{11{,}815}{0.88} = 13{,}426 \text{ kW} = 13.43 \text{ MW} $$ **Step 8 — Compute efficiencies:** Electrical efficiency: $$ \eta_e = \frac{W_{net}}{\dot{Q}_{fuel}} = \frac{1.74}{13.43} = 0.1296 \approx 12.96 \% $$ Thermal efficiency: $$ \eta_{th} = \frac{Q_{useful}}{\dot{Q}_{fuel}} = \frac{10.00}{13.43} = 0.7446 \approx 74.46 \% $$ Overall (EUF): $$ EUF = \eta_e + \eta_{th} = 0.1296 + 0.7446 = 0.8742 \approx 87.4 \% $$ Heat-to-power ratio: $$ HPR = \frac{Q_{useful}}{W_{net}} = \frac{10.00}{1.74} = 5.75 $$ Verification via closed-form: $HPR = (1 - \eta_e)/\eta_e = (1 - 0.1296)/0.1296 = 6.72$ (mismatch because EUF ≠ 1 due to boiler losses). **Step 9 — Compare with separate generation:** Separate-generation fuel: $$ F_{sep} = \frac{10.00}{0.85} + \frac{1.74}{0.33} = 11.76 + 5.27 = 17.03 \text{ MW} $$ Cogeneration fuel: 13.43 MW (from Step 7). $$ \Delta F = 17.03 - 13.43 = 3.60 \text{ MW} $$ $$ \% \text{Saving} = \frac{3.60}{17.03} \times 100 = 21.14 \% $$ > [!NOTE] > KTU accepts an answer stating "approximately 20 % fuel saving" when the industrial case matches the assumed efficiencies. The conclusion is: **a back-pressure system is best when HPR of the process is high** (i.e., heat demand >> power demand). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Steam Turbine Cogeneration (Back-Pressure) ```mermaid flowchart LR fuelA[Fuel Source
Coal or Bagasse or Oil] --> boilerA[Boiler
Combustion + Heat Transfer] feedA[Feedwater Pump
h feedwater] --> econA[Economizer] boilerA --> econA econA --> drA[Drum or Once Through] drA --> supA[Superheater
40 bar 400 C] supA -->|h1| turbA[Back Pressure Turbine
Expansion to 5 bar] turbA -->|h2 Process Steam| procA[Process Plant
Steam at 5 bar] turbA -->|W net| genA[Generator
5 MW class] procA -->|hfw Condensate| condA[Condensate Return
45 C] condA --> feedA genA --> gridA[Industrial Grid] boilerA -->|Flue Gas Qloss| stackA[Stack] classDef power fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef thermal fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef stream fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class turbA,genA power class boilerA,procA,supA,econA thermal class fuelA,feedA,drA,condA,stackA,gridA stream ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Gas Turbine Cogeneration with HRSG ```mermaid flowchart TB airA[Atmospheric Air
25 C 1.013 bar] --> compA[Air Compressor
Pressure Ratio 12 to 16] compA -->|High P Air T1| combA[Combustion Chamber
Fuel Injection] fuelA[Natural Gas
GCV 50 MJ per kg] --> combA combA -->|T3 1200 C| gtbA[Gas Turbine
Expansion] gtbA -->|W shaft Power| genA[Electrical Generator
5 to 50 MW class] gtbA -->|Exhaust 540 C| hrsgA[HRSG
Heat Recovery Steam Generator] hrsgA -->|Steam 20 bar 250 C| procA[Process Plant
or District Heating] hrsgA -->|FW Return| feedA[Feedwater Pump] feedA --> hrsgA genA --> gridA[Grid] hrsgA -->|Flue Gas 120 C| stackA[Stack] classDef prime fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef heat fill:#DC143C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef elec fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class combA,gtbA prime class hrsgA,procA heat class compA,genA,gridA elec class airA,fuelA,feedA,stackA elec ``` ### 4.3 Topping vs Bottoming Cycle — Conceptual Block Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TOPPING[Cogeneration Topping Cycle] f1[Fuel] --> p1[Power Generation] p1 --> q1[Useful Heat Recovery] end subgraph BOTTOMING[Cogeneration Bottoming Cycle] f2[Fuel] --> q2[Process Heat High Temperature] q2 --> p2[Power Generation from Waste Heat] end classDef toppingCls fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef bottomCls fill:#8B008B,stroke:#000,color:#fff class f1,p1,q1 toppingCls class f2,q2,p2 bottomCls ``` ### 4.4 Schematic T-s Comparison: Conventional vs Cogeneration Steam Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CONV[Conventional Condensing Plant] a1[1 Boiler 40 bar 400 C] -->|Isoentropic Expansion| a2[2 0.1 bar] a2 -->|Condenser 32 C| a3[3 Saturated Liquid] a3 -->|Pump| a1 end subgraph COGEN[Back Pressure Cogeneration] b1[1 Boiler 40 bar 400 C] -->|Expansion to 5 bar| b2[2 Exhaust at 5 bar] b2 -->|Process Steam 152 C| b3[3 Condensate 45 C] b3 -->|Pump and Economizer| b1 end classDef conv fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef cogen fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,color:#000 class a1,a2,a3 conv class b1,b2,b3 cogen ``` > [!NOTE] > The T-s diagrams above are represented as block-flows because drawing an actual T-s curve in Mermaid is not natively supported. The *physical difference* — that the cogeneration plant's expansion terminates at point 2 (5 bar) instead of point 2 (0.1 bar) — is what matters. The smaller enthalpy drop in cogeneration is precisely why *all* the rejected heat is captured at a useful temperature. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]:** *Define the term "Cogeneration." Differentiate between topping and bottoming cycle cogeneration systems with one industrial example each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Cogeneration is the simultaneous production of two useful forms of energy — electrical (or mechanical) and thermal — from a single fuel source. **(1 mark)** - **Topping cycle:** Fuel is first converted to power, and the rejected heat is then used for process. Example: Gas turbine + HRSG. **(1 mark)** - **Bottoming cycle:** Fuel is first used for high-temperature process heat, and the rejected heat drives a power cycle. Example: Cement or steel kiln waste-heat boiler. **(1 mark)** #### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]:** *Define "Energy Utilization Factor" (EUF). For a back-pressure steam turbine cogeneration system, show that EUF is approximately equal to 1 under ideal conditions.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** EUF is the ratio of the sum of useful energy outputs (power + useful heat) to the fuel energy input. **(1 mark)** For a back-pressure turbine: $EUF = \dfrac{\dot{m}(h_1 - h_2) + \dot{m}(h_2 - h_{fw})}{\dot{m}(h_1 - h_{fw})} = 1$ **(2 marks — full derivation, see Section 3.1 above).** --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]:** **(a)** Explain with a neat schematic the working principle of a **steam turbine based back-pressure cogeneration system**. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A process industry requires **8 MW of process heat** as saturated steam at **8 bar**, and **3 MW of electrical power**. Fuel GCV = **44 MJ/kg**, boiler efficiency = **86 %**, turbine mechanical-electrical efficiency = **95 %**. Turbine inlet conditions: **30 bar, 350 °C**. Feedwater at **50 °C**. Calculate: (i) Steam mass flow rate (process governs) (ii) Electrical power actually generated (iii) $\eta_e$, $\eta_{th}$, EUF, and HPR (iv) Compare fuel consumption with separate generation (separate: $\eta_B$ = 0.82, $\eta_{e,sep}$ = 0.30). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** - Schematic with fuel → boiler → turbine → process → condensate → feedwater → boiler (loop). **2 marks** (description) + **2 marks** (diagram, refer to Section 4.1 mermaid block). - Working: fuel combustion → steam generation → expansion in turbine → work extraction at shaft → exhaust steam at 8 bar is fed directly to process plant. **1 mark.** - **Advantages (1 mark):** Highest total efficiency (~80–90 %); no condenser (no cooling water); simple; low emissions per unit of useful output. - **Limitations (1 mark):** Power output is *tied* to process heat demand; cannot operate if process shuts down; high back-pressure reduces specific work. **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Steam properties:** - $h_1$ (30 bar, 350 °C) = **3126.3 kJ/kg** - $h_2$ (8 bar, saturated) = **h_g @ 8 bar = 2769.1 kJ/kg** - $h_{fw}$ (50 °C) ≈ $4.18 \times 50$ = **209.0 kJ/kg** **Step 2 — Useful heat per kg steam:** $$ q_{useful} = h_2 - h_{fw} = 2769.1 - 209.0 = 2560.1 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step 3 — Steam mass flow rate (process governs):** $$ \dot{m}_s = \frac{8000 \text{ kW}}{2560.1 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 3.125 \text{ kg/s} $$ **Step 4 — Power generated:** Turbine work per kg = $h_1 - h_2 = 3126.3 - 2769.1 = 357.2$ kJ/kg Net work per kg = $357.2 \times 0.95 = 339.3$ kJ/kg $$ W_{net} = 3.125 \times 339.3 = 1060.3 \text{ kW} \approx 1.06 \text{ MW} $$ **Step 5 — Efficiencies:** Fuel input rate: $\dot{Q}_{fuel} = \dfrac{3.125 \times (3126.3 - 209.0)}{0.86} = \dfrac{3.125 \times 2917.3}{0.86} = \dfrac{9116.6}{0.86} = 10{,}600$ kW = 10.60 MW. $$ \eta_e = \frac{1.06}{10.60} = 0.10 = 10.0 \% $$ $$ \eta_{th} = \frac{8.00}{10.60} = 0.7547 = 75.47 \% $$ $$ EUF = 0.10 + 0.7547 = 0.8547 \approx 85.5 \% $$ $$ HPR = \frac{8.00}{1.06} = 7.55 $$ **Step 6 — Separate generation comparison:** $$ F_{sep} = \frac{8.00}{0.82} + \frac{1.06}{0.30} = 9.756 + 3.533 = 13.29 \text{ MW} $$ $$ \text{Fuel saved} = 13.29 - 10.60 = 2.69 \text{ MW} $$ $$ \% \text{Saving} = \frac{2.69}{13.29} \times 100 = 20.24 \% $$ **Conclusion:** A back-pressure CHP saves about **20 % fuel** vs separate generation. **(Distribution of 7 marks: 1 mark each for steps 1–5, 2 marks for step 6 + conclusion.)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Unit mismatch:** Using kJ/kg but stating $\dot{Q}_{fuel}$ in MW without dividing by 1000. > 2. **Forgetting to multiply by mech-elec efficiency** when computing $W_{net}$ — this drops 1 mark. > 3. **Using $h_2$ = saturated liquid** instead of saturated vapor for back-pressure exhaust. The exhaust from a back-pressure turbine is *saturated or superheated vapor* (not liquid). $h_2 = h_g$ at exhaust pressure. > 4. **Reporting EUF > 1** without acknowledging it is a "utilization factor," not a true efficiency. > 5. **Skipping feedwater enthalpy:** KTU *always* expects $h_{fw}$ to be subtracted; some students forget and over-estimate $Q_{useful}$ by 200–300 kJ/kg. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice:** **(a)** Explain the working of a **gas turbine cogeneration system with HRSG**. State its **electrical efficiency**, **thermal efficiency**, and **total efficiency** ranges. Why is supplementary firing used? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A gas turbine cogeneration system has the following data: - Compressor pressure ratio: **12** - Compressor isentropic efficiency: **88 %** - Turbine isentropic efficiency: **90 %** - Combustor inlet temperature (TIT) = **1200 K** - Combustor outlet temperature = 1200 K (no pressure drop assumed) - Ambient air: **300 K** - Combustion heating value added per kg of air: **500 kJ/kg of air** - Exhaust from turbine is fed to HRSG; air and fuel mass flow = **30 kg/s total** - HRSG generates **2.5 kg of steam per kg of exhaust gas** at 10 bar saturated. - Feedwater enters HRSG at **60 °C**. Compute: (i) Net power output (kW). (ii) Steam mass flow rate. (iii) Process heat in HRSG. (iv) $\eta_e$, $\eta_{th}$, and EUF. (Assume $c_{p,exhaust}$ = 1.05 kJ/kg·K, exhaust leaves HRSG at 150 °C.) **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** - Schematic: Air → Compressor → Combustor → Gas Turbine → HRSG → Process. **2 marks** (description + diagram, refer to Section 4.2 mermaid block). - HRSG captures turbine exhaust heat to produce steam. **1 mark.** - Efficiency ranges: $\eta_e$ = 25–35 %, $\eta_{th}$ = 45–55 %, EUF = 75–85 %. **2 marks.** - Supplementary firing: When process requires steam at higher temperature or pressure than the HRSG can naturally deliver, a duct burner in the HRSG raises exhaust temperature. **2 marks.** **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Isentropic compression temperature:** $$ T_{2s} = T_1 \times r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \times 12^{0.4/1.4} = 300 \times 12^{0.2857} $$ $$ T_{2s} = 300 \times 2.034 = 610.2 \text{ K} $$ **Step 2 — Actual compressor outlet temperature:** $$ T_2 = T_1 + \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_c} = 300 + \frac{610.2 - 300}{0.88} = 300 + 352.5 = 652.5 \text{ K} $$ **Step 3 — Isentropic expansion temperature:** $$ T_{4s} = T_3 \times (1/r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 1200 / 2.034 = 590.0 \text{ K} $$ **Step 4 — Actual turbine outlet temperature:** $$ T_4 = T_3 - \eta_t (T_3 - T_{4s}) = 1200 - 0.90 \times (1200 - 590.0) $$ $$ T_4 = 1200 - 0.90 \times 610.0 = 1200 - 549.0 = 651.0 \text{ K} \approx 378 °C $$ **Step 5 — Net work per kg of air:** Compressor work: $w_c = c_p (T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (652.5 - 300) = 354.3$ kJ/kg Turbine work: $w_t = c_p (T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1200 - 651.0) = 551.7$ kJ/kg Net work: $w_{net} = 551.7 - 354.3 = 197.4$ kJ/kg **Step 6 — Net power output:** $$ W_{net} = 197.4 \times 30 = 5922 \text{ kW} \approx 5.92 \text{ MW} $$ **Step 7 — Steam mass flow rate:** Exhaust mass flow $\approx$ 30 kg/s (fuel mass is small). Steam: $$ \dot{m}_{steam} = 2.5 \times 30 = 75 \text{ kg/s} $$ **Step 8 — Process heat in HRSG:** Exhaust enters HRSG at $T_4 = 651$ K, leaves at 150 °C = 423 K. $$ Q_{HRSG} = \dot{m}_{exh} \cdot c_{p,exh} \cdot (T_4 - T_{out}) = 30 \times 1.05 \times (651 - 423) $$ $$ Q_{HRSG} = 30 \times 1.05 \times 228 = 7182 \text{ kW} \approx 7.18 \text{ MW} $$ **Step 9 — Efficiencies:** Fuel input: $\dot{Q}_{fuel} = 30 \times 500 = 15{,}000$ kW = 15 MW. $$ \eta_e = \frac{5.92}{15.00} = 0.3947 = 39.5 \% $$ $$ \eta_{th} = \frac{7.18}{15.00} = 0.4787 = 47.9 \% $$ $$ EUF = 0.3947 + 0.4787 = 0.8734 \approx 87.3 \% $$ **Distribution of 7 marks:** Step 1: 1 mark. Step 2: 0.5. Step 3: 1. Step 4: 1. Step 5: 1. Steps 6–9: 1.5 marks. Step 10 (conclusion): 1 mark. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Gas Turbine Problems:** > 1. **Wrong $c_p$ value:** Use $c_p$ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K for compressor and turbine (cold-side air), and $c_p$ = 1.05–1.10 kJ/kg·K for hot exhaust gases in HRSG. Mixing them gives wrong answers. > 2. **Forgetting $\eta_t$ in the isentropic relation:** $T_4 = T_3 - \eta_t(T_3 - T_{4s})$, *not* $T_3 - (T_3 - T_{4s})$. > 3. **Including fuel mass in compressor inlet:** The compressor handles *air only*. Fuel mass is added *after* compression in modern GTs. If the problem gives "air + fuel mass flow," read carefully. > 4. **TIT vs TIT-corrected:** Some KTU questions say "Turbine Inlet Temperature" and some say "Combustor outlet." They are the same point. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these 12 points before the exam:** - ✅ **Cogeneration** = simultaneous generation of **power + useful heat** from a single fuel. - ✅ **Three primary metrics:** $\eta_e$, $\eta_{th}$, and **EUF** (= $\eta_e + \eta_{th}$). - ✅ **Heat-to-Power Ratio (HPR):** $HPR = \dfrac{1 - \eta_e}{\eta_e}$ for back-pressure units. - ✅ **Topping cycle:** Power first → heat next. **Bottoming cycle:** Heat first → power next. - ✅ **Steam turbine CHP** uses *back-pressure* (no condenser, max efficiency) or *extraction-condensing* (independent control). - ✅ **Gas turbine CHP** always uses **HRSG** to capture exhaust heat (450–550 °C). - ✅ **EUF for ideal back-pressure = 1.0**; real systems achieve 0.75–0.85. - ✅ **Fuel saving** typically **20–30 %** for steam-turbine cogen; up to **40 %** for gas-turbine cogen with HRSG. - ✅ **Steam table points to memorize:** $h$ at 30 bar 350 °C ≈ 3126 kJ/kg; at 40 bar 400 °C ≈ 3213 kJ/kg; $h_g$ at 5 bar ≈ 2748 kJ/kg; at 8 bar ≈ 2769 kJ/kg. - ✅ **Mechanical-electrical efficiency** is **~95–98 %**; **boiler efficiency** is **~85–90 %** in KTU problems. - ✅ **Combined Cycle Cogeneration** = Gas Turbine + HRSG + Steam Turbine: highest efficiency (**85–90 %**). - ✅ **CO₂ reduction** is in proportion to the *fuel saved*, typically **20–40 %** per unit of useful energy delivered.

topics.title: Heat Exchangers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Heat Exchangers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4: Heat Exchangers — Section 1

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Exchanger (HX):** A static device that facilitates the transfer of thermal energy (heat) between two or more fluids (or between a fluid and a solid surface) that are at different temperatures and are in thermal contact, but **never mix physically**. Heat transfer occurs through **conduction**, **convection**, and in some designs, **radiation**, governed by the Second Law of Thermodynamics — heat spontaneously flows from the hotter fluid to the cooler one.

In the context of **Energy Management and Conservation (PESFT741)**, a heat exchanger is treated as one of the most critical **energy-recovery equipment** classes. Industries deploy heat exchangers to recover waste heat from exhaust streams, flue gases, and process fluids, thereby reducing primary fuel consumption and improving the **Overall Plant Efficiency (OPE)**. Common industrial examples include **economizers**, **superheaters**, **condensers**, **evaporators**, **recuperators**, and **regenerators**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Terminology You MUST Know:**
> - **Hot Fluid** → the fluid that *gives up* heat (energy donor).
> - **Cold Fluid** → the fluid that *absorbs* heat (energy receiver).
> - **Tube-side fluid** → fluid flowing inside the tubes.
> - **Shell-side fluid** → fluid flowing across the tubes, inside the shell.
> - **Heat duty (Q)** → rate of heat transfer in **W** or **kW**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are sipping **hot tea** from a steel cup using a **ceramic mug as a holder**. The steel cup is hot (the hot fluid), the ceramic holder absorbs heat (the cold fluid), and the wall of the steel cup acts as the **separating surface** through which heat passes. The two never mix — yet the ceramic gets warmer while the tea cools down.

That is exactly what a heat exchanger does, only in a controlled, engineered, and far more efficient industrial setting.

Another everyday analogy is the **radiator of a car engine**:
- Hot coolant from the engine (hot fluid) flows through tubes.
- Outside air blown by a fan (cold fluid) sweeps across the tubes.
- Heat moves from coolant → air, cooling the engine *without any fluid mixing*.

| Real-World Example | Hot Fluid | Cold Fluid | Separator |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Car Radiator | Engine coolant | Atmospheric air | Metal tubes/fins |
| Room Heater (radiator) | Steam/hot water | Room air | Metal panel |
| Refrigerator evaporator | Refrigerant | Indoor air | Finned tubes |
| Power plant condenser | Exhaust steam | Cooling water | Tube bundle |

> [!TIP]
> **Energy Conservation Link:** Without heat exchangers, the waste heat from boilers, furnaces, and turbines would simply be vented to the atmosphere. A properly sized HX can recover **20–50%** of this waste heat, directly reducing fuel costs and emissions.

---

## 1.3 Why Heat Exchangers Matter in Energy Conservation

In any thermal power plant, petrochemical refinery, or HVAC system, **60–80% of the input energy exits as low-grade waste heat**. Heat exchangers are the *first line of defense* in an **Energy Conservation Measure (ECM)** audit. They reduce:
- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)**
- **CO₂ emissions**
- **Cooling water demand**
- **Process heating cost**

For KTU exam context, you must always remember that a heat exchanger is **not a heat generator** — it is a **heat transfer device** that obeys energy balance, not energy creation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Heat Exchangers (KTU High-Yield Taxonomy)

Heat exchangers are classified by the KTU 2024 syllabus along **four major axes**:

### A. Based on Flow Arrangement (MOST IMPORTANT for exams)

| Type | Description | Industrial Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Parallel Flow (Co-current)** | Both fluids enter at the same end and flow in the **same direction** | Rarely used industrially; low efficiency |
| **Counter Flow (Counter-current)** | Fluids enter at **opposite ends** and flow in **opposite directions** | **Most efficient**; universally preferred |
| **Cross Flow** | Fluids flow **perpendicular** to each other | Air-cooled condensers, radiators |
| **Shell and Tube** | One fluid in tubes, the other in the surrounding shell | **Most common industrial design** |

### B. Based on Construction

- **Shell-and-Tube HX**: Tubes bundled inside a cylindrical shell. The most versatile design.
- **Plate HX**: Multiple corrugated plates stacked together; very high heat transfer area per unit volume.
- **Finned-Tube HX**: Extended surface (fins) added to increase area on the gas/air side.
- **Spiral / Helical HX**: Compact, handles fouling and thermal stress well.
- **Double-Pipe HX**: Simplest design; one pipe inside another. Used for teaching.

### C. Based on Heat Transfer Mechanism

- **Direct Contact (Open)** — fluids physically mix (e.g., cooling towers).
- **Indirect Contact (Closed)** — fluids separated by a wall (most industrial HX).

### D. Based on Heat Transfer Process

- **Recuperators** — both fluids flow simultaneously through separate channels (steady-state).
- **Regenerators** — same matrix is alternately heated and cooled by the two fluids (cyclic).
- **Heat Pipe** — uses phase change (evaporation–condensation) of a working fluid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Compaction (heat transfer area per unit volume, m²/m³):**
> Plate > Spiraled > Shell-and-Tube > Double-pipe.
> Plate HX can reach **≈ 1000 m²/m³**, while a shell-and-tube gives only **≈ 100 m²/m³**.

---

## 2.2 Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)

For any heat exchanger, the rate of heat transfer is governed by:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

where:
- **$Q$** = heat duty (W or kW)
- **$U$** = Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (W/m²·K)
- **$A$** = effective heat transfer area (m²)
- **$\Delta T$** = appropriate mean temperature difference (K)

The reciprocal of U is the **total thermal resistance**, which is the **sum of four individual resistances** — a classic KTU derivation:

$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_o} + R_f$$

where:
- $h_i$ = inside (tube-side) convective coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $h_o$ = outside (shell-side) convective coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $x_w$ = wall thickness (m)
- $k_w$ = thermal conductivity of wall material (W/m·K)
- $R_f$ = combined fouling resistance (m²·K/W)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fouling** is the deposition of dirt, scale, sludge, or biological growth on heat-transfer surfaces. It **increases resistance** and **decreases U** over time. Energy auditors treat fouling as a top-priority ECM — periodic cleaning can recover **10–30%** of lost heat-transfer performance.

---

## 2.3 Energy Balance (Conservation of Energy)

For a heat exchanger with **no heat loss to surroundings**, energy in = energy out:

$$Q = \dot{m}_h \, c_{p,h} \, (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c} \, (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$

where $\dot{m}$ is mass flow rate (kg/s) and $c_p$ is specific heat (kJ/kg·K).

Heat Capacity Rate is defined as:

$$C = \dot{m} \, c_p \quad (\text{W/K or kW/K})$$

The fluid with the **smaller C** is called the **minimum fluid ($C_{min}$)**, the larger one **maximum ($C_{max}$)**.

---

## 2.4 Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

For **counter-flow** arrangement:

$$\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$

where:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$

For **parallel-flow**, $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$.

For **shell-and-tube** (not pure counter-flow), a **Correction Factor $F$** is applied:

$$\Delta T_m = F \cdot \text{LMTD}_{counter}$$

The factor **$F$** depends on two dimensionless groups:
- $P = \dfrac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$
- $R = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write $\ln \dfrac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}$ (inverted). The **numerator must be the larger $\Delta T$ minus the smaller $\Delta T$** to keep LMTD positive. Always check $T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} > T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$.

---

## 2.5 Effectiveness–NTU Method

When **outlet temperatures are unknown**, the **ε-NTU method** is used.

**Effectiveness** is defined as:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{C_{min}\,(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})}$$

**Number of Transfer Units:**

$$\text{NTU} = \frac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}}$$

The relation $\varepsilon = f(\text{NTU}, C^*)$ depends on the type of HX:

$$C^* = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}$$

| HX Type | Effectiveness Equation |
| :--- | :--- |
| Parallel Flow | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\!\left[-\text{NTU}(1 + C^*)\right]}{1 + C^*}$ |
| Counter Flow ($C^* < 1$) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\!\left[-\text{NTU}(1 - C^*)\right]}{1 - C^*\exp\!\left[-\text{NTU}(1 - C^*)\right]}$ |
| Counter Flow ($C^* = 1$) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\text{NTU}}{1 + \text{NTU}}$ |

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORIZE THIS TABLE — it covers ≈ 80% of KTU numerical questions on Heat Exchangers.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Q = U A \Delta T_m$ | Heat transfer rate (LMTD method) | W |
| 2 | $\Delta T_m = F \cdot \text{LMTD}$ | Mean temperature difference | K |
| 3 | $\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | Log mean temperature difference | K |
| 4 | $Q = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h}(T_{h,i}-T_{h,o})$ | Hot-side energy balance | W |
| 5 | $Q = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c}(T_{c,o}-T_{c,i})$ | Cold-side energy balance | W |
| 6 | $C = \dot{m} \, c_p$ | Heat capacity rate | W/K |
| 7 | $C^* = C_{min}/C_{max}$ | Capacity ratio | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{NTU} = UA/C_{min}$ | Number of transfer units | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\varepsilon = Q_{act}/Q_{max}$ | Heat exchanger effectiveness | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x_w}{k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_o} + R_f$ | Overall thermal resistance | m²·K/W |
| 11 | $Q = \varepsilon \, C_{min}\,(T_{h,i}-T_{c,i})$ | Heat duty via effectiveness | W |

**Note on units:** $\dot{m}$ in kg/s, $c_p$ in J/kg·K (or kJ/kg·K with Q in kW), temperatures in K or °C (differences only).

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility in Energy Audits

In an **Energy Audit (EA)** of any process plant, the auditor estimates the **energy value of waste heat streams** by sizing or checking the heat exchangers. Key performance indicators monitored are:

- **Heat duty recovered (kW)** — directly proportional to fuel saved.
- **Approach temperature** — smallest temperature difference between two fluids at any point. A smaller approach = more heat recovered, but **larger area = more capital cost**. Typical approach in industry: **5–10 °C**.
- **LMTD correction factor $F$** — should ideally be **≥ 0.75** for shell-and-tube design; below that, multi-shell passes are recommended.
- **Fouling factor** — monitored regularly; cleaning schedule set based on $R_f$ growth.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world energy-saving benchmark:** A well-maintained shell-and-tube economizer in a 100 MW boiler can recover **5–8 MW** of waste heat, saving roughly **₹ 4–6 crore/year** in fuel cost (at current coal prices).

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation of Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

### Setup
Consider a **counter-flow** heat exchanger. A differential element of area $dA$ transfers differential heat $dQ$. Across this element, the hot fluid cools by $dT_h$ and the cold fluid warms by $dT_c$.

### Step 1 — Differential heat transfer equation
By Newton's law of cooling across element $dA$:

$$dQ = U \, dA \, \Delta T$$

where $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$ at that location.

### Step 2 — Energy balance on each fluid

$$dQ = -\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} \, dT_h \quad \Rightarrow \quad dT_h = -\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} = -\frac{dQ}{C_h}$$

$$dQ = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} \, dT_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad dT_c = \frac{dQ}{C_c}$$

### Step 3 — Change in temperature difference

$$\begin{aligned}
d(\Delta T) &= dT_h - dT_c \\
&= -\frac{dQ}{C_h} - \frac{dQ}{C_c} \\
&= -dQ \left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right) \\
&= -dQ \left(\frac{C_h + C_c}{C_h C_c}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute dQ from Step 1

$$\begin{aligned}
d(\Delta T) &= -U \, dA \, \Delta T \left(\frac{C_h + C_c}{C_h C_c}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Separate variables and integrate

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} &= -U \left(\frac{C_h + C_c}{C_h C_c}\right) dA
\end{aligned}$$

Integrating from inlet (where $\Delta T = \Delta T_1$) to outlet (where $\Delta T = \Delta T_2$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} &= -\frac{U(C_h + C_c)}{C_h C_c} \int_0^A dA \\
\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) &= -\frac{U A (C_h + C_c)}{C_h C_c}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Substitute total heat transfer Q

From energy balance:
$$Q = C_h (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = C_c (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$$

Total temperature changes sum to $\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2$:

$$\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2 = (T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}) - (T_{h,o} - T_{c,i})$$

### Step 7 — Final expression for LMTD

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{U A} &= \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q \ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} \\
Q &= U A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} \\
\text{i.e.,} \quad Q &= U A \cdot \text{LMTD}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}\;}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Effectiveness–NTU Relation (Counter Flow)

For counter-flow at steady state, applying energy balance and combining with $Q = \varepsilon C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$:

For $C^* < 1$, after standard algebraic elimination (see Coulson & Richardson, Vol 1):

$$\boxed{\;\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\!\left[-\text{NTU}(1 - C^*)\right]}{1 - C^* \exp\!\left[-\text{NTU}(1 - C^*)\right]}\;}$$

For $C^* = 1$ (both fluids have equal heat capacity rates), $T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}$ remains constant along the length, and integrating yields:

$$\boxed{\;\varepsilon = \dfrac{\text{NTU}}{1 + \text{NTU}}\;}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Shell-and-Tube Oil Cooler (LMTD Method)

> **Problem:** Water ($c_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K) enters a counter-flow heat exchanger at 25 °C and leaves at 70 °C. Oil ($c_p = 2.0$ kJ/kg·K) enters at 150 °C and leaves at 80 °C. Mass flow rates: $\dot{m}_w = 1.0$ kg/s, $\dot{m}_o = 0.8$ kg/s. If $U = 500$ W/m²·K, find **(a)** heat duty, **(b)** LMTD, **(c)** required area.

### Step (a) — Heat duty from water side

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_w c_{p,w} (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}) \\
  &= 1.0 \times 4180 \times (70 - 25) \\
  &= 4180 \times 45 \\
  &= 188{,}100 \text{ W} \approx 188.1 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step (b) — Compute LMTD (counter-flow)

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_1 &= T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - 70 = 80 \text{ °C} \\
\Delta T_2 &= T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 80 - 25 = 55 \text{ °C} \\
\text{LMTD} &= \frac{80 - 55}{\ln(80/55)} = \frac{25}{\ln(1.4545)} = \frac{25}{0.3747} \\
&\approx 66.72 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step (c) — Required heat transfer area

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{Q}{U \cdot \text{LMTD}} = \frac{188{,}100}{500 \times 66.72} \\
  &= \frac{188{,}100}{33{,}360} \\
  &\approx 5.64 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

### Verification using oil side:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= 0.8 \times 2000 \times (150 - 80) = 1600 \times 70 = 112{,}000 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

This does **not match** the water-side value (188.1 kW). Therefore the inlet/outlet temperatures given are **inconsistent with energy balance** — a typical exam trap. The correct procedure: trust **one side's measurement** and recompute the unknown outlet temperature from the other side.

Correcting: using $Q = 112$ kW from oil side,

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{c,o} &= T_{c,i} + \frac{Q}{\dot{m}_w c_{p,w}} = 25 + \frac{112{,}000}{4180} \\
        &= 25 + 26.79 \approx 51.79 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always verify energy balance with **both sides**. If mismatch > 2%, re-compute missing outlet temperature from the side you trust most. Marks are often awarded specifically for this verification step.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Effectiveness–NTU Method

> **Problem:** In a counter-flow HX, $C_{min} = 1000$ W/K, $C_{max} = 2000$ W/K, $U = 400$ W/m²·K, $A = 5$ m². Hot fluid inlet: 200 °C, cold fluid inlet: 30 °C. Find the heat transfer rate.

### Step 1 — Compute NTU and C*

$$\begin{aligned}
C^* &= \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \frac{1000}{2000} = 0.5 \\
\text{NTU} &= \frac{U A}{C_{min}} = \frac{400 \times 5}{1000} = 2.0
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Compute effectiveness (counter-flow, $C^*<1$)

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-C^*)]}{1 - C^* \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-C^*)]} \\
           &= \frac{1 - \exp[-2 \times 0.5]}{1 - 0.5 \exp[-2 \times 0.5]} \\
           &= \frac{1 - e^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 \, e^{-1}} \\
           &= \frac{1 - 0.3679}{1 - 0.1839} \\
           &= \frac{0.6321}{0.8161} \approx 0.7746
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compute heat duty

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \varepsilon \, C_{min} (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) \\
  &= 0.7746 \times 1000 \times (200 - 30) \\
  &= 0.7746 \times 170{,}000 \\
  &= 131{,}680 \text{ W} \approx 131.7 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Outlet temperatures (optional, full marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{h,out} &= T_{h,in} - \frac{Q}{C_{min}} = 200 - \frac{131{,}680}{1000} \approx 68.32 \text{ °C} \\
T_{c,out} &= T_{c,in} + \frac{Q}{C_{max}} = 30 + \frac{131{,}680}{2000} \approx 95.84 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}$$

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define a heat exchanger. List any four types of heat exchangers used in industries.**

**Model Answer:**
A heat exchanger is a device that transfers heat from a hot fluid to a cold fluid without mixing them, the two being separated by a solid wall. Four common industrial types are:
1. **Shell-and-tube heat exchanger** — used in refineries and power plants.
2. **Plate heat exchanger** — used in dairy, food, and HVAC.
3. **Finned-tube heat exchanger** — used for gas–liquid heat transfer (air-cooled condensers).
4. **Double-pipe heat exchanger** — simplest design, used for small flow rates and pilot plants.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Define the term "effectiveness" of a heat exchanger. What is its range?**

**Model Answer:**
Effectiveness ($\varepsilon$) is the ratio of **actual heat transferred** to the **maximum possible heat transfer** in a heat exchanger:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})}$$

Range: $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$. A higher value indicates better heat-recovery performance. Typical industrial HX effectiveness lies between **0.5 and 0.8**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice Pattern)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Derive the expression for Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) for a counter-flow heat exchanger.**

**Solution Outline (full marks require the 7-step derivation shown in Section 3.1):**
1. Start with differential heat transfer $dQ = U \, dA \, \Delta T$.
2. Apply energy balance: $dT_h = -dQ/C_h$, $dT_c = dQ/C_c$.
3. Compute $d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c = -dQ (1/C_h + 1/C_c)$.
4. Substitute $dQ = U \Delta T \, dA$.
5. Integrate over area $0 \to A$, between $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$.
6. Solve for $\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$ in terms of $U A$, $C_h$, $C_c$.
7. Use total energy balance to replace $U A$ by $Q / \text{LMTD}$ and obtain the final result:

$$\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$

> Marks split: Step 1–3: 2 marks, Step 4–5: 3 marks, Step 6–7: 2 marks.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Hot oil ($c_p = 2.1$ kJ/kg·K) flowing at 2 kg/s cools from 180 °C to 90 °C in a counter-flow shell-and-tube HX, heating cold water ($c_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K) flowing at 1.5 kg/s from 30 °C. Calculate (i) water outlet temperature, (ii) LMTD, (iii) area if $U = 600$ W/m²·K, (iv) effectiveness.**

**Step (i) — Water outlet temperature**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_o c_{p,o} (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = 2 \times 2100 \times (180 - 90) \\
  &= 2 \times 2100 \times 90 = 378{,}000 \text{ W} = 378 \text{ kW} \\
T_{c,o} &= T_{c,i} + \frac{Q}{\dot{m}_w c_{p,w}} = 30 + \frac{378{,}000}{1.5 \times 4180} \\
        &= 30 + \frac{378{,}000}{6270} \\
        &= 30 + 60.29 \approx 90.29 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (ii) — LMTD (counter-flow)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_1 &= T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 180 - 90.29 = 89.71 \text{ °C} \\
\Delta T_2 &= T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 90 - 30 = 60 \text{ °C} \\
\text{LMTD} &= \frac{89.71 - 60}{\ln(89.71/60)} = \frac{29.71}{\ln(1.4952)} \\
            &= \frac{29.71}{0.4028} \approx 73.76 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (iii) — Required area**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \text{LMTD}} = \frac{378{,}000}{600 \times 73.76} = \frac{378{,}000}{44{,}256} \approx 8.54 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step (iv) — Effectiveness**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{min} &= \min(\dot{m}_o c_{p,o}, \dot{m}_w c_{p,w}) = \min(4200, 6270) = 4200 \text{ W/K} \\
\varepsilon &= \frac{Q}{C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})} = \frac{378{,}000}{4200 \times (180 - 30)} \\
            &= \frac{378{,}000}{4200 \times 150} = \frac{378{,}000}{630{,}000} \approx 0.6
\end{aligned}$$

> **Final Answers:** $T_{c,o} = 90.29$ °C, $\text{LMTD} = 73.76$ °C, $A = 8.54$ m², $\varepsilon = 0.6$.

---
