### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the Effectiveness–NTU method of heat-exchanger analysis. Why is it preferred over the LMTD method when outlet temperatures are unknown?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Effectiveness–NTU method** uses two dimensionless parameters to characterize a heat exchanger:
- $\varepsilon = Q_{actual}/Q_{max}$ — effectiveness.
- $\text{NTU} = UA/C_{min}$ — number of transfer units.

The functional relation $\varepsilon = f(\text{NTU}, C^*)$ depends on HX type. Standard results:
- Counter-flow: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-C^*)]}{1 - C^* \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-C^*)]}$ for $C^*<1$.
- Parallel-flow: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1+C^*)]}{1+C^*}$.
- For $C^* = 1$ counter-flow: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\text{NTU}}{1+\text{NTU}}$.

**Why preferred over LMTD:**
1. In the LMTD method we must know both inlet and outlet temperatures of both fluids to compute $\Delta T_1, \Delta T_2$. The $\varepsilon$–NTU method only requires **inlet temperatures, $U$, $A$**, and one outlet temperature.
2. When we need to **size a new HX** (find $A$ for given duty), NTU method directly yields area; LMTD method needs iterative trials.
3. When the **outlet temperature is unknown**, the LMTD method is unusable; the $\varepsilon$–NTU method solves the problem in one shot.
4. Energy auditors use NTU method to compute how **effective** an existing exchanger is and to predict how much fuel can be saved if it were replaced by a larger one.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**A shell-and-tube counter-flow HX has $C_h = 3000$ W/K and $C_c = 1500$ W/K. The hot fluid enters at 250 °C and the cold fluid at 50 °C. If $U = 500$ W/m²·K and $A = 6$ m², find (i) NTU, (ii) $C^*$, (iii) effectiveness, (iv) heat transfer rate.**

**Step (i) — NTU**

$$\text{NTU} = \frac{UA}{C_{min}} = \frac{500 \times 6}{1500} = 2.0$$

**Step (ii) — Capacity ratio**

$$C^* = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \frac{1500}{3000} = 0.5$$

**Step (iii) — Effectiveness (counter-flow, $C^*<1$)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{1 - \exp[-2 \times (1 - 0.5)]}{1 - 0.5 \exp[-2 \times (1 - 0.5)]} \\
            &= \frac{1 - e^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 e^{-1}} \\
            &= \frac{0.6321}{0.8161} \approx 0.7746
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (iv) — Heat duty**

$$Q = \varepsilon \, C_{min} (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}) = 0.7746 \times 1500 \times (250 - 50) = 0.7746 \times 1500 \times 200 \approx 232{,}380 \text{ W} \approx 232.4 \text{ kW}$$

> **Final Answers:** NTU = 2.0, $C^* = 0.5$, $\varepsilon = 0.7746$, $Q = 232.4$ kW.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Heat Exchanger questions:**
> 1. **Wrong LMTD ratio direction** — students write $\ln(\Delta T_2/\Delta T_1)$ instead of $\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$. LMTD becomes negative. **1 mark lost**.
> 2. **Failing to apply the Correction Factor $F$** for shell-and-tube. Pure counter-flow formula assumes both fluids in single pass. **2 marks lost**.
> 3. **Not verifying energy balance on both sides** — examiners explicitly award a 1-mark step called "energy-balance check".
> 4. **Mixing parallel and counter-flow formulas for $\varepsilon$** — the most common silly error. **3 marks lost**.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert units** — $c_p$ in kJ/kg·K and $\dot{m}$ in kg/s gives $Q$ in kW; with $c_p$ in J/kg·K, $Q$ is in W. Unit mismatch = **2 marks**.
> 6. **Fouling resistance ignored** — auditors expect $R_f$ to appear in the U-equation whenever "real-world performance" is asked. **1 mark lost**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Revision Checklist (pin this on your wall before the exam):**
> - Heat exchanger = indirect-contact heat transfer device; **no mixing** of fluids.
> - $Q = U A \Delta T_m$ — fundamental heat-transfer equation.
> - LMTD used when **outlet temperatures are known**; NTU used when **they are unknown**.
> - LMTD counter-flow > LMTD parallel-flow for the same inlet conditions → counter-flow is more efficient.
> - $\varepsilon$ range = **0 to 1**; practical range = **0.5–0.8**.
> - $\text{NTU} = UA/C_{min}$ — measures HX "size" in dimensionless form.
> - $C^* = C_{min}/C_{max}$ — ratio of capacity rates; bounded between 0 and 1.
> - Fouling increases $R_f$, decreases $U$, lowers $\varepsilon$ — **clean heat exchangers save energy**.
> - For shell-and-tube, always check correction factor $F$; require $F \ge 0.75$.
> - **Heat capacity rate $C = \dot{m} c_p$** is the most-used derived quantity in both methods.
> - Counter-flow effectiveness can approach 1 as NTU → ∞; parallel-flow caps out at $\varepsilon < 1/(1+C^*)$.
> - Plate HX > Spiral HX > Shell-and-Tube HX in **compactness (m²/m³)**.
> - Typical industrial **approach temperature** = 5–10 °C; smaller means more heat recovery but more area.
> - **Energy conservation link:** every kW of heat recovered = a kW of fuel input avoided = direct reduction in CO₂ emissions.
> - **Practice formulas 1–11** from Section 2.6 — they cover nearly every Part A and Part B question on this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lighting System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lighting System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Illumination / Lighting Engineering** is the branch of electrical engineering that deals with the design, application, and control of artificial light sources to provide adequate visibility, visual comfort, safety, and aesthetic appeal in residential, commercial, industrial, and outdoor environments — while minimizing energy consumption.

The human eye responds to electromagnetic radiation in the **visible spectrum** (wavelength $\lambda \approx 380\text{ nm}$ to $780\text{ nm}$). The science of measuring visible light in terms of the eye's response is called **Photometry**, which is distinct from radiometry (pure energy measurement).

### 1.2 Photometric Quantities — The Four Pillars

| Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Defining Equation | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Luminous Flux | $\Phi$ | **lumen (lm)** | Total light emitted per second | Quantity of visible light |
| Luminous Intensity | $I$ | **candela (cd)** | $I = \dfrac{\Phi}{\omega}$ | Flux per unit solid angle |
| Illuminance | $E$ | **lux (lx)** | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | Flux falling per unit area |
| Luminance | $L$ | **cd/m²** | $L = \dfrac{I}{A_{\text{proj}}}$ | Brightness as perceived |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Constants used throughout lighting design:**
> - $1 \text{ lux} = 1 \text{ lumen/m}^2$
> - $1 \text{ candela} = 1 \text{ lumen/steradian}$
> - Standard **maintained illuminance** for offices = **500 lux**,精细 work = 750–1000 lux, road lighting = 15–30 lux.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Water-Pipe Model"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine light as water flowing through a pipe:**
> - **Luminous Flux** ($\Phi$) → Total water **discharge** from the pipe (litres/sec).
> - **Luminous Intensity** ($I$) → Discharge per **direction** (litres/sec per steradian).
> - **Illuminance** ($E$) → How densely water **hits the floor** (litres/sec per m²).
> - **Distance** → The farther the pipe, the more spread out the water (= inverse square).
> - **Angle of fall** → A tilted jet covers more floor (= cosine law).
> - **Luminous Efficacy** → Litres delivered per *watt of pump energy* (lumens/watt).

Just as a poorly aimed shower wastes water, a poorly aimed luminaire wastes lumens. **Lighting design is essentially about *aiming* and *concentrating* light where it is needed.**

### 1.4 Why Lighting Matters in Energy Management

Lighting typically accounts for **20–30 %** of total electrical energy consumption in commercial buildings and can reach **40 %** in offices, schools, and hospitals. It is the **lowest-hanging fruit** in any energy audit because:

- Payback periods for retrofits are typically **6 months to 2 years**.
- Technology (LED) has matured dramatically.
- No behavioural change is required from the end user once installed.
- Maintenance and lamp-life benefits are substantial.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Fundamental Laws of Illuminance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every lighting calculation in KTU exams ultimately rests on **only two physical laws**: the **Inverse Square Law** and **Lambert's Cosine Law**. Master them.

#### A. Inverse Square Law

When a point source emits light uniformly in all directions, the illuminance on a surface **perpendicular** to the direction of incidence decreases with the **square of the distance**.

$$E = \frac{I}{d^2}$$

where $I$ = luminous intensity (cd) and $d$ = distance from source to surface (m).

> *Intuition:* Light spreads over an expanding spherical shell of area $4\pi d^2$. Same flux, larger area → lower density.

#### B. Lambert's Cosine Law

When the surface is **inclined** at angle $\theta$ to the direction of incidence, only the component $\cos\theta$ of the flux contributes to illuminance:

$$E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

When the surface is **horizontal** but the source is at height $h$ above it:

$$E = \frac{I \cos^3\theta}{h^2}$$

because $d = h/\cos\theta$, so $d^2 = h^2/\cos^2\theta$, giving:

$$E = \frac{I \cos^3\theta}{h^2}$$

### 2.2 Luminous Efficacy ($\eta$) — The Key Performance Metric

$$\eta = \frac{\Phi}{P} \quad \left[\frac{\text{lumens}}{\text{watt}}\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> Higher $\eta$ = more visible light per unit electrical input = **better**. This single number dictates technology choice in energy audits.

### 2.3 Types of Lamps — Comparative Analysis

| Lamp Family | Efficacy (lm/W) | Life (hrs) | CRI | CCT | Starting Time | Re-strike |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Incandescent (GLS)** | 10–17 | 1,000 | 100 | 2700 K | Instant | Instant |
| **Tungsten Halogen** | 15–25 | 2,000 | 100 | 3000 K | Instant | Instant |
| **Linear Fluorescent (T8/T5)** | 50–100 | 10,000–20,000 | 60–85 | 4000–6500 K | 1–3 s | Instant (electronic) |
| **CFL** | 50–75 | 8,000–15,000 | 80 | 2700–6500 K | 1–3 min | 2 min |
| **Mercury Vapour (MV)** | 35–55 | 12,000–24,000 | 45 | 4000 K | 4–8 min | 5–10 min |
| **High Pressure Sodium (HPSV)** | 80–120 | 20,000–28,000 | 25 | 2000 K | 4–6 min | 1–5 min |
| **Metal Halide (MH)** | 70–110 | 10,000–20,000 | 65–90 | 4000 K | 4–10 min | 10–15 min |
| **LED** | 80–200 | 30,000–100,000 | 70–95 | 2200–6500 K | Instant | Instant |

### 2.4 Colour Rendering Index (CRI) and CCT

- **CRI ($R_a$)**: Measure (0–100) of how faithfully the source renders colours compared to a reference. Sunlight = 100. Hospitals, textile, printing require CRI > 80.
- **Correlated Colour Temperature (CCT)**: Apparent "warmth" of the light in Kelvin. $2700\text{ K}$ = warm yellow; $4000\text{ K}$ = neutral white; $6500\text{ K}$ = cool daylight.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \Phi / A$ | Average illuminance on a surface | lux |
| 2 | $E = I / d^2$ | Point source, perpendicular surface | lux |
| 3 | $E = I \cos^3\theta / h^2$ | Horizontal surface, source at height $h$ | lux |
| 4 | $\eta = \Phi / P$ | Luminous efficacy | lm/W |
| 5 | $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi \times CU \times LLF}$ | Number of luminaires (Lumen Method) | — |
| 6 | $CU = \dfrac{\Phi_{\text{received}}}{\Phi_{\text{emitted}}}$ | Coefficient of Utilization (from tables) | — |
| 7 | $LLF = LLD \times LDD \times RSMF$ | Light Loss Factor (product of sub-factors) | — |
| 8 | $\Delta P = P_{\text{old}} - P_{\text{new}}$ | Demand reduction per fixture | W |
| 9 | $E_{\text{saved}} = \Delta P \times N \times h_{\text{op}} \times n_{\text{days}}$ | Annual energy saved | kWh/yr |
| 10 | $S_{\text{annual}} = E_{\text{saved}} \times C_{\text{elec}}$ | Annual monetary saving | ₹/yr |
| 11 | $\text{SPP} = \dfrac{I_{\text{inv}}}{S_{\text{annual}}}$ | Simple Payback Period | years |
| 12 | $\text{ROI \%} = \dfrac{S_{\text{annual}}}{I_{\text{inv}}} \times 100$ | Return on Investment | %/yr |

> *Where:* $LLD$ = Lamp Lumen Depreciation, $LDD$ = Luminaire Dirt Depreciation, $RSMF$ = Room Surface Maintenance Factor, $N$ = total number of fixtures, $h_{\text{op}}$ = daily operating hours, $C_{\text{elec}}$ = electricity tariff (₹/kWh).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Significance

- **Smart Cities & Street Lighting**: HPSV 250 W replaced by LED 90 W with adaptive dimming → 60 % reduction in municipal lighting bills.
- **Industrial Lighting**: Replacing 400 W MH with 150 W LED in factories reduces air-conditioning load as well, since LEDs emit far less infrared (heat).
- **Green Building Rating (LEED/IGBC)**: ASHRAE 90.1 / ECBC mandates Lighting Power Density (LPD) limits — typically $7\text{–}10\text{ W/m}^2$ for offices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

### 3.1 Derivation of the Lumen Method Formula

The Lumen Method is the *standard* indoor lighting design procedure prescribed by IS 3646 and adopted in KTU numerical problems.

**Given data (typical exam input):**
- Area $A$ to be illuminated
- Required maintained illuminance $E$
- Lamp flux $\Phi$ per lamp
- Coefficient of utilization $CU$ (from photometric data)
- Light loss factor $LLF$

**Step 1 — Total lumens required on the working plane**

The working plane (typically at 0.75 m above floor) needs illuminance $E$ over area $A$, so total useful flux demanded is:

$$\Phi_{\text{demanded}} = E \times A$$

**Step 2 — Account for utilization losses**

Only a fraction $CU$ of the lumens emitted by the lamps actually reaches the working plane. So, the lamps must emit:

$$\Phi_{\text{emitted,req}} = \frac{E \times A}{CU}$$

**Step 3 — Account for depreciation / losses**

Over the maintenance cycle, lamp lumen depreciation, dirt accumulation, and room surface ageing reduce output by factor $LLF$. So the *initial* emission must be higher:

$$\Phi_{\text{installed}} = \frac{E \times A}{CU \times LLF}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to number of luminaires**

If each luminaire holds $n$ lamps, each producing $\Phi$ lumens, total flux per luminaire = $n \cdot \Phi$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{N = \frac{E \times A}{n \cdot \Phi \cdot CU \cdot LLF}}$$

For a single-lamp luminaire ($n=1$):

$$N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \cdot CU \cdot LLF}$$

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Exam Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An office floor measures **$20\text{ m} \times 10\text{ m}$** with a ceiling height of **3.0 m**. The required maintained illuminance on the working plane (0.75 m above floor) is **500 lux**. LED panel luminaires are to be used, each fitted with one LED lamp of **4000 lumens** rated **36 W**. Take $CU = 0.60$ and $LLF = 0.80$. Calculate:
> (a) the number of luminaires required,
> (b) the total connected load,
> (c) the specific energy consumption per m² per 100 lux, and
> (d) compare with the existing system of $4 \times 36$ W fluorescent tubes per fixture with magnetic ballast consuming **$4 \times 40 = 160\text{ W}$** per fixture, if 35 such fixtures exist. Calculate annual energy saved (operating 10 hrs/day, 300 days/yr, ₹8/kWh) and simple payback if LED panel cost is ₹1,500 each.

#### Part (a): Number of Luminaires

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= 20 \times 10 = 200 \text{ m}^2 \\
E &= 500 \text{ lux} \\
\Phi &= 4000 \text{ lm} \\
CU &= 0.60 \\
LLF &= 0.80
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \cdot CU \cdot LLF} \\
  &= \frac{500 \times 200}{4000 \times 0.60 \times 0.80} \\
  &= \frac{100{,}000}{1920} \\
  &= 52.083
\end{aligned}$$

Rounding up: $N = 53 \text{ luminaires}$.

#### Part (b): Total Connected Load

$$P_{\text{total,LED}} = 53 \times 36 = 1908 \text{ W} = 1.908 \text{ kW}$$

#### Part (c): Specific Energy Consumption

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SEC} &= \frac{P_{\text{total}}}{A \times (E/100)} \\
           &= \frac{1908}{200 \times (500/100)} \\
           &= \frac{1908}{1000} \\
           &= 1.908 \text{ W/(m}^2 \cdot 100 \text{ lux)}
\end{aligned}$$

This is well below ECBC limits and indicates a high-efficiency design.

#### Part (d): Comparison and Energy Savings

**Existing system:**

$$P_{\text{existing}} = 35 \times 160 = 5600 \text{ W} = 5.6 \text{ kW}$$

**Demand reduction:**

$$\Delta P = 5600 - 1908 = 3692 \text{ W} = 3.692 \text{ kW}$$

**Annual energy saved:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{saved}} &= \Delta P \times h_{\text{op}} \times n_{\text{days}} \\
                  &= 3.692 \times 10 \times 300 \\
                  &= 11{,}076 \text{ kWh/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Annual monetary saving:**

$$S_{\text{annual}} = 11{,}076 \times 8 = \text{₹ }88{,}608 \text{ /yr}$$

**Investment for LED panels (assuming existing fluorescent fixtures are scrapped):**

$$I_{\text{inv}} = 53 \times 1500 = \text{₹ }79{,}500$$

**Simple Payback Period:**

$$\text{SPP} = \frac{I_{\text{inv}}}{S_{\text{annual}}} = \frac{79{,}500}{88{,}608} = 0.897 \text{ years}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SPP} \approx 10.77 \text{ months}}$$

**ROI:**

$$\text{ROI} = \frac{88{,}608}{79{,}500} \times 100 \approx 111.5\% \text{ per year}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** The LED retrofit here pays back in under one year. For KTU problems, always check whether the problem implies *replacement of complete fixture* or *lamp-only swap*. The ballast of a fluorescent fixture wastes ~20 % of input as heat — so lamp-only swap still leaves ballast losses.

### 3.3 Inverse Square Law — Quick Sanity-Check Problem

> A streetlight lamp has a luminous intensity of 2500 cd directly below it. The mounting height is 8 m. Find the illuminance at the point directly below the lamp on the road surface.

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{I \cos^3\theta}{h^2}
\end{aligned}$$

At the point directly below: $\theta = 0°$, so $\cos\theta = 1$.

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{2500 \times 1^3}{8^2} \\
  &= \frac{2500}{64} \\
  &= 39.06 \text{ lux}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{max}} \approx 39.1 \text{ lux}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of an Artificial Lighting System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Mains Supply 230V AC] --> A2[Protection MCB and ELCB]
    A2 --> A3[Energy Meter kWh]
    A3 --> A4[Switchgear Contactor or Relay]
    A4 --> A5[Control Module Manual Auto Dimmer Sensor]
    A5 --> A6[Driver or Ballast]
    A6 --> A7[Lamp or LED Module]
    A7 --> A8[Luminaire Reflector Diffuser Optics]
    A8 --> A9[Working Plane Illuminance lux]
    A9 --> A10[Occupancy or Daylight Feedback]
    A10 --> A5
```

### 4.2 Lamp Family Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Artificial Light Sources]
    L0 --> L1[Incandescent Family]
    L0 --> L2[Discharge Family]
    L0 --> L3[Electroluminescent Family]
    L1 --> L11[GLS Bulb]
    L1 --> L12[Tungsten Halogen]
    L2 --> L21[Low Pressure Discharge]
    L2 --> L22[High Pressure Discharge]
    L21 --> L211[Linear Fluorescent T5 T8]
    L21 --> L212[CFL]
    L22 --> L221[Mercury Vapour HPMV]
    L22 --> L222[Metal Halide MH]
    L22 --> L223[High Pressure Sodium HPSV SON]
    L22 --> L224[Low Pressure Sodium LPSV SOX]
    L3 --> L31[LED Panel Downlight Streetlight]
    L3 --> L32[OLED Emerging]
```

### 4.3 Energy Conservation Strategy Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Audit Existing Lighting System] --> S2[Measure Lux Levels and Loads]
    S2 --> S3{Over Illuminated or Inefficient Lamp}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Replace with LED or High Efficacy Lamp]
    S3 -- No --> S5[Optimise Controls]
    S4 --> S6[Delamp Excess Fixtures]
    S5 --> S7[Install Occupancy Sensors]
    S5 --> S8[Install Daylight Harvesting]
    S5 --> S9[Use Task Lighting Zoning]
    S6 --> S10[Re-measure and Verify Savings]
    S7 --> S10
    S8 --> S10
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11[Calculate Payback and ROI]
```

### 4.4 Lighting Control Architecture Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C0[Lighting Control Strategies]
    C0 --> C1[Manual Switching]
    C0 --> C2[Scheduled Timers]
    C0 --> C3[Occupancy Sensors PIR Ultrasonic]
    C0 --> C4[Daylight Harvesting Photocell]
    C0 --> C5[DALI or KNX Addressable Bus]
    C0 --> C6[BMS Integration IoT Cloud]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Insight:** The feedback arrow from working plane back to the control module represents a **closed-loop dimming system**. Daylight sensors measure incoming natural light and reduce artificial output proportionally, achieving 30–50 % additional savings on top of LED retrofit savings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define the following photometric quantities with units:
(i) Luminous flux
(ii) Luminous intensity
(iii) Illuminance

**Model Answer:**

> (i) **Luminous Flux ($\Phi$):** The total quantity of visible light energy emitted per second by a source, weighted by the eye's spectral sensitivity. **Unit: lumen (lm).**
>
> (ii) **Luminous Intensity ($I$):** The luminous flux emitted per unit solid angle in a given direction. $I = \Phi / \omega$. **Unit: candela (cd).**
>
> (iii) **Illuminance ($E$):** The luminous flux incident per unit area of a surface. $E = \Phi / A$. **Unit: lux (lx) = lm/m².**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between **Luminous Efficacy** and **Lamp Efficiency**. Why is luminous efficacy the preferred metric in energy audits?

**Model Answer:**

> - **Luminous Efficacy ($\eta$):** Ratio of luminous flux emitted to electrical power consumed. $\eta = \Phi / P$, in **lm/W**. It directly indicates *visible light per watt*.
> - **Lamp Efficiency:** An older term sometimes used interchangeably with efficacy, but technically the *radiant efficiency* is the ratio of radiant flux to power input.
> - In energy audits, **luminous efficacy** is preferred because it accounts for the eye's spectral response — only visible light matters for illumination. A lamp may have high radiant efficiency but low efficacy if its emission is in the infrared (as in old incandescent bulbs). Hence, efficacy is the true indicator of how efficiently a lamp produces *useful* light.

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the construction, working principle, and characteristics of a **High Pressure Sodium Vapour (HPSV) lamp**. Why is it preferred for street lighting despite its poor CRI? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industry hall of dimensions **$30\text{ m} \times 20\text{ m} \times 8\text{ m}$** (mounting height 7 m above working plane) is to be illuminated to a level of **300 lux**. Using **400 W metal halide lamps** (each producing **36,000 lumens**, requiring a luminaire with $CU = 0.65$, $LLF = 0.75$), determine:
(i) Number of luminaires
(ii) Connected load
(iii) Energy consumption if operated 12 hrs/day for 300 days/year
(iv) Specific energy consumption in $\text{W/m}^2/100\text{ lux}$
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

> **Construction:** An HPSV lamp is a high-pressure discharge lamp consisting of a **sintered alumina (ceramic) arc tube** containing **sodium, mercury, and a xenon starting gas**. Two tungsten electrodes are sealed at each end. The arc tube is enclosed in a hard glass outer bulb evacuated to provide thermal insulation. A **bimetallic switch** and a **starting probe/igniter** assist in initial breakdown.
>
> **Working Principle:**
> 1. When supply is switched on, the igniter applies a high-voltage pulse (~2–4 kV) that ionises the xenon gas, producing a faint bluish glow.
> 2. The arc heats up, vaporising mercury and sodium progressively.
> 3. As sodium vaporises (operating temperature ~2500 K), it produces the characteristic **yellow-orange light** by resonant radiation.
> 4. The arc stabilises; voltage across the lamp drops; ballast limits the current to a safe value.
> 5. Full light output is reached after **4–6 minutes**. Re-strike after switch-off requires 1–5 min cooling.
>
> **Characteristics:**
> - Efficacy: 80–120 lm/W (very high)
> - Life: 20,000–28,000 hours
> - CRI: 25 (very poor — colours appear yellowish-brown)
> - Warm-up time: 4–6 min; Re-strike: 1–5 min
> - Requires **choke (ballast) + igniter** for operation.
>
> **Why preferred for street lighting despite poor CRI?**
> - High **luminous efficacy** (≈100 lm/W) → low operating cost.
> - Long **lamp life** reduces replacement frequency on tall poles.
> - **Yellow-orange light** penetrates fog, dust, and rain better than white light (less scattering by aerosols) → better **visual range** in adverse weather.
> - Colour discrimination is not critical for road users; brightness perception and obstacle detection are.

**Model Solution for (b):**

> **Given:**
> $A = 30 \times 20 = 600 \text{ m}^2$, $E = 300 \text{ lux}$, $\Phi = 36{,}000 \text{ lm}$, $CU = 0.65$, $LLF = 0.75$.

**(i) Number of luminaires:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times CU \times LLF} \\
  &= \frac{300 \times 600}{36{,}000 \times 0.65 \times 0.75} \\
  &= \frac{180{,}000}{17{,}550} \\
  &= 10.256
\end{aligned}$$

$\therefore N = 11 \text{ luminaires}$ (rounded up).

**(ii) Connected Load:**

$$P_{\text{total}} = 11 \times 400 = 4400 \text{ W} = 4.4 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Annual Energy Consumption:**

$$E_{\text{annual}} = 4.4 \text{ kW} \times 12 \text{ hr/day} \times 300 \text{ days} = 15{,}840 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

**(iv) Specific Energy Consumption:**

$$\text{SEC} = \frac{P_{\text{total}}}{A \times (E/100)} = \frac{4400}{600 \times 3} = \frac{4400}{1800} = 2.44 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot100\text{ lux)}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the **Lumen Method** of lighting design. Define Coefficient of Utilization and Light Loss Factor with the factors affecting them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A classroom of size **$12\text{ m} \times 8\text{ m}$** has an existing installation of **12 fluorescent tube fixtures**, each fitted with **two 40 W tubes** and a magnetic ballast (loss = 8 W per tube). The classroom operates for **8 hrs/day, 250 days/year** at **₹7.50/kWh**.
> Propose replacement with **40 W LED tubes** delivering **4000 lumens each** (no ballast; driver loss included in rating), one per fixture. Take $CU = 0.55$, $LLF = 0.80$, required maintained illuminance = **400 lux**.
> Calculate:
> (i) Number of LED fixtures required
> (ii) Connected load before and after retrofit
> (iii) Annual energy saved and cost saved
> (iv) Simple payback if LED tube + driver costs ₹600 per fixture **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

> **Lumen Method:**
> This is the standard indoor lighting design procedure where the *total luminous flux required on the working plane* is calculated from the desired illuminance and area, then converted to a number of luminaires after accounting for losses.
>
> **Formula:**
>
> $$N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times CU \times LLF}$$
>
> **Step-by-step procedure:**
> 1. Determine the area $A$ from architectural plan.
> 2. Fix the maintained illuminance $E$ from IS 3646 / task requirements.
> 3. Choose lamp type and obtain its rated lumens $\Phi$.
> 4. Obtain $CU$ from the luminaire manufacturer's photometric data (function of Room Index and reflectances).
> 5. Compute $LLF$ as $LLD \times LDD \times RSMF$.
> 6. Calculate $N$ and round up to nearest integer; re-check spacing for uniformity.
>
> **Coefficient of Utilization ($CU$):** The ratio of luminous flux reaching the working plane to the total flux emitted by the lamps.
>
> $$CU = \frac{\Phi_{\text{at working plane}}}{\Phi_{\text{emitted}}}$$
>
> **Factors affecting $CU$:**
> - Room Index: $k = \dfrac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L + W)}$ (where $H_m$ = mounting height above working plane)
> - Reflectances of ceiling, walls, and floor
> - Luminaire distribution (direct, indirect, semi-direct)
> - Spacing-to-height ratio
>
> **Light Loss Factor ($LLF$):** A multiplier (always < 1) that accounts for all losses between the *initial* and *maintained* illumination over the cleaning/replacement cycle.
>
> $$LLF = LLD \times LDD \times RSMF$$
>
> where:
> - $LLD$ = Lamp Lumen Depreciation (e.g., 0.85 for fluorescent after 70 % life)
> - $LDD$ = Luminaire Dirt Depreciation (depends on environment cleanliness)
> - $RSMF$ = Room Surface Maintenance Factor (degradation of ceiling/wall reflectances)
>
> Typical $LLF$ for clean offices = 0.75–0.85; for industrial/dirty = 0.55–0.65.

**Model Solution for (b):**

> **Given:**
> $A = 12 \times 8 = 96 \text{ m}^2$, $E = 400$ lux, $\Phi = 4000$ lm, $CU = 0.55$, $LLF = 0.80$.

**(i) Number of LED fixtures (one tube per fixture, so $n=1$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times CU \times LLF} \\
  &= \frac{400 \times 96}{4000 \times 0.55 \times 0.80} \\
  &= \frac{38{,}400}{1760} \\
  &= 21.818
\end{aligned}$$

$\therefore N = 22 \text{ LED fixtures}$.

**(ii) Connected Load:**

**Before (fluorescent):** Each fixture = $2 \times 40\text{ W} + 2 \times 8\text{ W (ballast)} = 96 \text{ W}$
$$P_{\text{old}} = 12 \times 96 = 1152 \text{ W} = 1.152 \text{ kW}$$

**After (LED):** Driver loss included in 40 W rating per fixture.
$$P_{\text{new}} = 22 \times 40 = 880 \text{ W} = 0.880 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Annual Energy & Cost Saved:**

Demand reduction:
$$\Delta P = 1152 - 880 = 272 \text{ W} = 0.272 \text{ kW}$$

Annual energy saved:
$$E_{\text{saved}} = 0.272 \times 8 \times 250 = 544 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

Annual monetary saving:
$$S_{\text{annual}} = 544 \times 7.50 = \text{₹ }4080 \text{ /yr}$$

**(iv) Simple Payback Period:**

Investment = $22 \times 600 = \text{₹ }13{,}200$.

$$\text{SPP} = \frac{13{,}200}{4080} = 3.235 \text{ years} \approx 3 \text{ years and } 3 \text{ months}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing $LLF$ with $CU$:** Many students multiply them together incorrectly or apply $LLF$ to the *illuminance* formula rather than to the *number of luminaires* formula. $LLF$ must appear in the denominator.
> 2. **Forgetting ballast loss:** When the question says "two 40 W tubes with magnetic ballast (loss = 8 W/tube)", many students treat each fixture as 80 W. Correct value is 80 + 16 = **96 W per fixture**.
> 3. **Forgetting to round up:** The lumen method gives a fractional $N$; you must always round *up* (ceil), never down, otherwise illuminance falls below the specified value.
> 4. **Not specifying $n$:** When a luminaire holds more than one lamp (e.g., $4 \times 36$ W fluorescent), the formula becomes $N = EA/(n\Phi \cdot CU \cdot LLF)$. Forgetting $n$ loses full marks.
> 5. **Mixing units:** $A$ in m², $\Phi$ in lm, $E$ in lux, $P$ in kW for annual kWh. KTU strictly checks unit consistency.
> 6. **No annual figures:** Forgetting to multiply by operating days leads to wrong payback. Always show the intermediate annual energy saved step.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Illuminance** $E = \Phi / A$ (lux) — most heavily tested quantity.
> - **Luminous efficacy** $\eta = \Phi / P$ (lm/W) is the *single most important* parameter for lamp comparison in energy audits.
> - **Inverse Square Law** $E = I/d^2$ and **Cosine Law** $E = I\cos^3\theta / h^2$ are foundational.
> - **Lumen Method** formula: $\displaystyle N = \frac{E \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot CU \cdot LLF}$. Always round $N$ *up*.
> - **LLF = LLD × LDD × RSMF** (always < 1; typical 0.7–0.85 for clean offices).
> - **CU** is obtained from photometric tables; depends on **Room Index** $k = \dfrac{L \cdot W}{H_m(L+W)}$ and reflectances.
> - Lamp hierarchy by efficacy: **Incandescent < Halogen < CFL < Fluorescent < MH ≈ HPSV < LED**.
> - HPSV preferred for **street/foggy outdoor** (high efficacy, long life, low scattering); MH/LED for **colour-critical** indoor (CRI > 80).
> - Payback $\text{SPP} = I_{\text{inv}} / S_{\text{annual}}$ — always express in **years** with two decimals.
> - **ECBC / ASHRAE 90.1 LPD limits**: Office $\leq 8\text{–}10 \text{ W/m}^2$ — verify compliance in audit problems.
> - Energy-saving hierarchy in lighting: **(1) delamp → (2) replace with LED → (3) install controls → (4) use daylight harvesting**.
> - LED retrofit typically saves **50–75 %** electrical energy versus fluorescent with magnetic ballast.
> - Colour metrics: **CRI > 80** for indoor task work; **CCT 4000 K** for offices, **2700 K** for hospitality.
> - Always state units in the final answer; KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motors, Belts and Drives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Motors, Belts and Drives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electric Motors in Energy Management

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
An **electric motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms electrical input energy into mechanical output energy (rotational motion). In the context of Energy Management and Conservation, motors are treated as the **single largest electricity-consuming asset class** in industry, typically accounting for **60% to 70%** of total industrial electrical consumption globally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Motors are the **backbone of industrial energy audits**. A motor's efficiency curve, loading profile, and power factor directly determine the energy bill of a plant. Improper motor selection, under-loading, and old standard-efficiency motors are the most frequently flagged non-conformities in any KTU-aligned energy audit project.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Heart of the Factory"

Think of a motor as the **heart** of a factory. Just as the human heart continuously pumps blood to the rest of the body, an industrial motor continuously converts electrical "blood" (energy) into mechanical motion delivered to pumps, fans, compressors, and conveyors. If the heart becomes weak, inefficient, or pumps too hard when the body needs only a gentle flow, the entire system wastes energy. This is exactly what happens when an oversized motor runs at 30% load — it keeps "pumping" full magnetic flux but the mechanical load uses only a fraction of it, wasting the rest as heat.

### 1.1.1 Classification of Electric Motors

Electric motors are broadly classified into two families based on the input supply:

| Family | Sub-Type | Typical Application | Energy Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AC Motors** | Three-phase Induction (Squirrel Cage) | Pumps, fans, compressors | Most common — ~90% of industrial motors |
| **AC Motors** | Slip Ring Induction | High-inertia loads, crushers | Moderate efficiency |
| **AC Motors** | Synchronous Motor | Power factor correction + drive | Best efficiency at full load |
| **DC Motors** | Shunt, Series, Compound | Cranes, traction, elevators | Lower efficiency, more maintenance |
| **Special** | BLDC, PMSM, Reluctance | EVs, modern HVAC, IE5 class | Highest efficiency frontier |

### 1.1.2 Standard Efficiency Classes (IE Code — IEC 60034-30-1)

> [!NOTE]
> **International Efficiency (IE) Classes:**
> - **IE1** — Standard Efficiency (obsolete in many countries)
> - **IE2** — High Efficiency
> - **IE3** — Premium Efficiency (mandatory in EU since 2015; India BEE 3-star)
> - **IE4** — Super Premium Efficiency
> - **IE5** — Ultra Premium (synchreluctance, permanent magnet)

The transition from IE1 to IE4 typically reduces motor losses by **~20% to 40%**, directly translating to massive lifetime energy savings.

---

## 1.2 Belt Drives — Mechanical Power Transmission

**Formal Definition:**
A **belt drive** is a frictional power transmission system in which a flexible belt running over two or more pulleys transfers rotational motion and torque from a driving pulley to a driven pulley. It is one of the simplest and most ubiquitous elements in industrial mechanical power trains.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Bicycle Chain of Industry"

Imagine pedalling a bicycle. The chain (belt) grabs the small front sprocket (driver pulley) and pulls the larger rear sprocket (driven pulley). To climb a hill, you change the rear sprocket to a smaller one — this multiplies torque but reduces speed. Belt drives do exactly the same: by changing the **diameter ratio** of the pulleys, they alter the **speed-torque trade-off** of the driven machine. The catch is that belts **slip a little** (just as your fingers might slip on a wet chain) and this slippage directly becomes **lost mechanical energy** as heat.

### 1.2.1 Types of Belt Drives

| Type | Geometry | Typical Use | Slip | Power Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flat Belt** | Flat cross-section, runs on crowned pulleys | Long-center, low-power | Higher | Up to ~500 kW |
| **V-Belt** | Trapezoidal cross-section, wedges into groove | Most industrial drives | Low | 0.5 kW to 500 kW |
| **Timing (Synchronous) Belt** | Toothed belt, no slip | Precision, high-speed, low maintenance | Zero slip | Up to ~200 kW |
| **Poly-V / Multi-Rib** | Multiple thin V-ribs | Automotive, compact drives | Very low | Up to ~100 kW |

### 1.2.2 Key Geometric & Operating Parameters

Let the **driver pulley** have diameter $D_1$ (mm) and speed $N_1$ (rpm); the **driven pulley** have diameter $D_2$ and speed $N_2$. The belt linear velocity is $v$ (m/s) and the belt has a mass per unit length of $m$ (kg/m).

---

## 1.3 Mechanical Drives — Coupling Motor to Load

A **drive system** is the complete assembly that transmits the motor's mechanical output to the driven equipment, while allowing **speed/torque conversion** and **operational control**. From an energy management perspective, the drive is often more important than the motor itself, because it determines **how efficiently** the motor's capability is utilized.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why drives matter in energy conservation:**
> Affinity laws tell us that a fan or pump power varies with the **cube of speed**. A 20% reduction in speed → ~49% reduction in power. A Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) exploits exactly this to harvest enormous savings in centrifugal loads.

The two principal drive families are:
1. **Mechanical Drives** — gearboxes, belt drives, chain drives, hydraulic couplings.
2. **Electrical Drives** — Variable Frequency Drives (VFD / VSD), soft starters, direct-on-line.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Motor Theory — Losses, Efficiency, and Power Factor

A motor's input electrical power $P_{in}$ is converted to mechanical output power $P_{out}$ with an **efficiency** $\eta$. The difference is the total losses $P_{loss}$.

### 2.1.1 The Five Losses of an Induction Motor

Induction motor losses are conventionally broken into **five categories**. A clear understanding of these is non-negotiable for KTU exams:

1. **Stator Copper Loss ($P_{cu1}$)** — $I^2R$ loss in stator winding. Proportional to **load current squared**.
2. **Rotor Copper Loss ($P_{cu2}$)** — $I^2R$ loss in rotor bars/end rings. Also varies with load squared.
3. **Core (Iron) Loss ($P_{Fe}$)** — Hysteresis + eddy current losses in the laminations. Roughly **constant**, depends on voltage and frequency.
4. **Friction and Windage Loss ($P_{fw}$)** — Bearing friction + cooling fan drag. Roughly **constant**, depends on speed.
5. **Stray Load Loss ($P_{stray}$)** — Harmonic, leakage flux losses. Assumed proportional to **load squared**.

### 2.1.2 Power Flow Diagram (Conceptual Logic)

The flow of power inside an induction motor can be written as a cascade:

$$P_{in} \;\rightarrow\; P_{cu1} \;\rightarrow\; P_{Fe} \;\rightarrow\; P_{gap}\;(Air-gap\ Power)$$

$$P_{gap} \;\rightarrow\; P_{cu2} \;\rightarrow\; P_{mech} \;\rightarrow\; P_{fw} \;\rightarrow\; P_{out}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step logic of the power cascade:**
> 1. Input $P_{in} = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ is drawn from the supply.
> 2. Part of it is lost as stator copper heat — $P_{cu1} = 3 I_1^2 R_1$.
> 3. Core loss occurs in the iron — $P_{Fe}$ (hysteresis + eddy).
> 4. The remainder crosses the air gap as **air-gap power** $P_{gap} = P_{in} - P_{cu1} - P_{Fe}$.
> 5. A fraction $s$ of $P_{gap}$ is lost as rotor copper heat — $P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_{gap}$.
> 6. The mechanical power developed is $P_{mech} = (1-s)\,P_{gap}$.
> 7. Subtracting friction & windage gives the **shaft output power** $P_{out}$.

### 2.1.3 Slip — The Heart of Induction Motor Behavior

**Slip $s$** is the fractional difference between synchronous speed and actual rotor speed.

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} \;\;\text{(per unit, dimensionless)}$$

- $N_s = \dfrac{120\,f}{P}$ (synchronous speed in rpm, $f$ in Hz, $P$ = number of poles)
- Normal operating slip: **2% to 5%** for standard induction motors
- $P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_{gap}$ — this is the foundational energy-management relationship for induction motors.

### 2.1.4 Motor Efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{losses}}$$

Motor efficiency is **load-dependent**. It typically peaks between **75% and 100%** of rated load. Below 50% load, efficiency collapses because constant losses (core + friction) become dominant.

### 2.1.5 Power Factor

$$pf = \cos\phi = \frac{P_{in}}{S} = \frac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L}$$

- Power factor is **poor at light loads** (magnetizing current dominates).
- A 7.5 kW motor at no-load may draw ~3 A at pf 0.2, but at full load draws ~14 A at pf 0.88.

---

## 2.2 Belt Drive Theory — Tension, Velocity, and Power

### 2.2.1 Belt Velocity and Velocity Ratio

$$v = \frac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60} = \frac{\pi D_2 N_2}{60}\;\;\text{(m/s)}$$

**Velocity ratio (theoretical):**

$$VR = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{D_1}{D_2}$$

### 2.2.2 Slip Correction

In practice, belts slip by 1–4%. The **actual velocity ratio** is:

$$VR_{actual} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{D_1}{D_2}\,(1 - s_b)$$

where $s_b$ is the belt slip (typically 0.01 to 0.04).

### 2.2.3 Tensions in the Belt — Slack Side and Tight Side

Let $T_1$ = tension in the **tight (driving) side**, $T_2$ = tension in the **slack (driven) side**.

The **effective driving force** transmitted to the pulley rim is:

$$F = T_1 - T_2 \;\;\text{(N)}$$

**Euler's Belt Friction Equation (capstan equation):**

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction between belt and pulley, and $\theta$ is the **angle of lap** (contact arc) in radians.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the angle of lap matters so much:** A small pulley with a large driven pulley will have a small angle of lap on the small pulley. This means low contact, low friction, and high slip risk. The V-belt's trapezoidal cross-section increases the effective friction through a **wedging action** — the normal force $N$ on the belt sides produces a higher effective friction than a flat belt.

### 2.2.4 Power Transmitted by the Belt

$$P = (T_1 - T_2)\,v \;\;\text{(Watts)}$$

### 2.2.5 Centrifugal Tension — The Hidden Loss

When a belt runs, its own mass creates a **centrifugal tension** $T_c$ that tightens the belt and reduces the effective driving tension.

$$T_c = m\,v^2 \;\;\text{(N)}$$

where $m$ is the mass per unit length of the belt (kg/m) and $v$ is belt speed (m/s). The **effective tensions** on tight and slack sides become:

$$T_1^{eff} = T_1 - T_c, \quad T_2^{eff} = T_2 - T_c$$

Maximum power transmission occurs when $T_1 = 3\,T_c$ (this is a key KTU derivation — see Section 3).

### 2.2.6 Belt Length (Open Belt)

$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \frac{(D_1 - D_2)^2}{4C}\;\;\text{(mm)}$$

where $C$ is the center distance between the two pulleys.

### 2.2.7 Belt Efficiency

$$\eta_{belt} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1} = 1 - \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

---

## 2.3 Variable Frequency Drives (VFD) — The Crown Jewel of Motor Energy Savings

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle

A VFD controls motor speed by varying the **frequency** $f$ and **voltage** $V$ applied to a three-phase induction motor, while keeping the **V/f ratio** approximately constant to maintain constant air-gap flux.

### 2.3.2 Affinity Laws for Centrifugal Machines (Fans, Pumps, Compressors)

Let subscript $1$ denote the original operating point and subscript $2$ the new operating point.

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \quad (\text{Flow} \propto \text{Speed})$$

$$\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 \quad (\text{Pressure} \propto \text{Speed}^2)$$

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 \quad (\text{Power} \propto \text{Speed}^3)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cube Law is the single most energy-relevant equation in Module 4.** A throttling valve wastes the surplus pressure; a VFD eliminates the surplus. A 30% speed reduction → 65.7% power reduction. This is why VFDs deliver payback periods of 6–24 months on fan and pump applications.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> The following table is the **ready-reckoner** you should commit to memory for the ESE. Note: the pipe character has been replaced with `\mid` or `\vert` to preserve table syntax.

| # | Quantity / Topic | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = 120\,f / P$ | rpm; $f$ in Hz, $P$ = pole pairs × 2 |
| 2 | Slip | $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ | dimensionless, 0.02 to 0.05 typical |
| 3 | Rotor Copper Loss | $P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_{gap}$ | W; **KTU favorite** |
| 4 | Mechanical Power | $P_{mech} = (1-s)\,P_{gap}$ | W |
| 5 | Motor Efficiency | $\eta = P_{out} / P_{in}$ | decimal or % |
| 6 | Motor Input Power | $P_{in} = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | W; 3-phase |
| 7 | Belt Velocity | $v = \pi D_1 N_1 / 60$ | m/s |
| 8 | Velocity Ratio | $VR = D_1 / D_2$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Velocity Ratio w/ slip | $VR_{act} = (D_1/D_2)\,(1 - s_b)$ | includes belt slip $s_b$ |
| 10 | Euler Belt Friction | $T_1 / T_2 = e^{\mu \theta}$ | $\theta$ in **radians** |
| 11 | Power Transmitted | $P = (T_1 - T_2)\,v$ | W |
| 12 | Centrifugal Tension | $T_c = m\,v^2$ | N |
| 13 | Optimum $T_1$ | $T_1 = 3\,T_c$ | condition for **max power** |
| 14 | Max Belt Power | $P_{max} = T_c\,v$ | at $T_1 = 3T_c$ |
| 15 | Open Belt Length | $L = 2C + (\pi/2)(D_1 + D_2) + (D_1 - D_2)^2 / (4C)$ | mm |
| 16 | Affinity: Flow | $Q \propto N$ | linear |
| 17 | Affinity: Head | $H \propto N^2$ | square |
| 18 | Affinity: Power | $P \propto N^3$ | **cube** |
| 19 | Energy Saved (VFD) | $\Delta P = P_1 [1 - (N_2/N_1)^3]$ | W or kW |
| 20 | V/f Constant | $V / f \approx \text{const}$ | flux control basis |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Power Transmitted by a Belt (Centrifugal Tension Optimization)

This is the **classic KTU derivation**. We want to find the relationship between tensions that maximizes the power transmitted by a belt running at speed $v$.

### Step 1 — Write Power Transmitted in Terms of $T_1$, $T_2$, and $v$

The power delivered by the belt to the driven pulley is the product of the **net driving force** $(T_1 - T_2)$ and the **belt velocity** $v$:

$$P = (T_1 - T_2)\,v$$

### Step 2 — Relate $T_1$ and $T_2$ Through Euler's Equation

From Euler's belt friction equation:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta} = k \;\;(\text{let } k = e^{\mu \theta} \text{ be a constant})$$

Therefore:

$$T_2 = \frac{T_1}{k}$$

Substituting into the power equation:

$$P = \left(T_1 - \frac{T_1}{k}\right)\,v = T_1 \left(\frac{k-1}{k}\right) v$$

### Step 3 — Account for Centrifugal Tension $T_c$

The **effective tension** that contributes to power transmission is reduced by the centrifugal tension. So we use the net effective tension $T_1 - T_c$:

$$P = (T_1 - T_c) \left(\frac{k-1}{k}\right) v$$

### Step 4 — Differentiate $P$ With Respect to $v$ and Set to Zero

Note that $T_c$ itself depends on $v$ through $T_c = m v^2$. So:

$$P = \left(T_1 - m v^2\right) \left(\frac{k-1}{k}\right) v = \left(\frac{k-1}{k}\right) \left(T_1 v - m v^3\right)$$

Differentiate with respect to $v$ (treat $T_1$ as independent of $v$ for a fixed applied tension — this is a classic KTU simplifying assumption):

$$\frac{dP}{dv} = \left(\frac{k-1}{k}\right) \left(T_1 - 3 m v^2\right)$$

Set the derivative to zero for an extremum:

$$T_1 - 3 m v^2 = 0$$

$$T_1 = 3 m v^2$$

But $m v^2 = T_c$ (centrifugal tension), so:

$$\boxed{T_1 = 3\,T_c}$$

This is the **condition for maximum power transmission** — one of the most-tested KTU results.

### Step 5 — Maximum Power Magnitude

Substitute $T_1 = 3 T_c$ back into the power expression. Since $T_2 = T_1/k = 3T_c/k$, the effective driving force becomes:

$$T_1 - T_2 = 3T_c - \frac{3T_c}{k} = 3T_c \left(\frac{k-1}{k}\right)$$

$$P_{max} = 3T_c \left(\frac{k-1}{k}\right) v = T_c v \cdot \frac{3(k-1)}{k}$$

In the limit where $k = e^{\mu\theta}$ is large (high friction, large angle of lap), $\dfrac{3(k-1)}{k} \to 3$, so $P_{max} \to 3 T_c v$. For a clean textbook result (and the KTU standard answer):

$$\boxed{P_{max} = T_c \cdot v \;\;(\text{when } k \gg 1)}$$

### Step 6 — Second-Derivative Check (Maximum, not Minimum)

$$\frac{d^2 P}{dv^2} = \left(\frac{k-1}{k}\right) (-6 m v)$$

For $v > 0$, $m > 0$, $k > 1$, we have $\dfrac{d^2 P}{dv^2} < 0$, confirming a **maximum**. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Savings from VFD on a Centrifugal Pump

A centrifugal pump runs at $N_1$ rpm and draws $P_1$ kW. A VFD is installed to reduce speed to $N_2 = 0.8 N_1$ to match the new process demand. Compute the new power $P_2$ and the energy saved.

### Given
- Original speed $N_1$, original power $P_1$.
- New speed $N_2 = 0.8\,N_1$.
- Annual operating hours $H = 6000$ hr/year.
- Electricity cost $C_e = 8$ ₹/kWh.

### Step 1 — Apply the Affinity (Cube) Law for Power

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$

$$P_2 = P_1 \times (0.8)^3 = P_1 \times 0.512$$

### Step 2 — Compute Power Saved in kW

$$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = P_1 (1 - 0.512) = 0.488\,P_1$$

### Step 3 — Compute Annual Energy Saved

$$\Delta E = \Delta P \times H = 0.488\,P_1 \times 6000\;\text{kWh/year}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Annual Monetary Savings

$$\text{Savings} = \Delta E \times C_e = 0.488\,P_1 \times 6000 \times 8 \;\text{₹/year}$$

If $P_1 = 50$ kW:

$$\Delta E = 0.488 \times 50 \times 6000 = 146{,}400\;\text{kWh/year}$$

$$\text{Savings} = 146{,}400 \times 8 = \text{₹} 11{,}71{,}200 \;\text{per year}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A 20% speed cut → **48.8% power cut**. This single example justifies a ₹15-lakh VFD investment in under 16 months — exactly the kind of audit recommendation KTU expects you to defend numerically.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Condition for Maximum Efficiency of Belt Drive

Belt drive efficiency is defined as the ratio of power output (delivered to the driven pulley) to power input (delivered by the driving pulley), accounting for centrifugal tension.

### Step 1 — Define Efficiency

$$\eta_{belt} = \frac{(T_1 - T_2)\,v}{(T_1 + T_c)\,v} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 + T_c}$$

(Here, the input power must overcome both the useful tension difference **and** the centrifugal tension.)

### Step 2 — Substitute $T_2 = T_1 / k$ and $T_c = m v^2$

$$\eta = \frac{T_1 - T_1/k}{T_1 + m v^2} = \frac{T_1(1 - 1/k)}{T_1 + T_c}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Maximum-Power Condition $T_1 = 3 T_c$

$$\eta_{at\ P_{max}} = \frac{3T_c(1 - 1/k)}{3T_c + T_c} = \frac{3T_c(k-1)/k}{4T_c} = \frac{3(k-1)}{4k}$$

For typical $\mu\theta = 0.8$ to $1.2$, $k = e^{0.8} \approx 2.23$ to $e^{1.2} \approx 3.32$:

- At $k = 2.23$: $\eta \approx 3(1.23)/(4 \times 2.23) \approx 0.414 = 41.4\%$
- At $k = 3.32$: $\eta \approx 3(2.32)/(4 \times 3.32) \approx 0.524 = 52.4\%$

This shows why **high-friction V-belts with large angle of lap** are far more efficient than flat belts.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Energy-Saving Lever | Applicable To | Typical Savings | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Replace IE1 with IE3 motor | Constant-speed general | 3% to 5% | High |
| Right-size motor | Underloaded motors | 2% to 8% | High |
| Variable Frequency Drive | Fans, pumps, compressors | 30% to 60% | Very High |
| Power Factor Correction | Plants with low PF | 1% to 5% bill reduction | Medium |
| Star-Delta starter | Large AC motors at start | ~2% steady-state | Low |
| V-Belt → Cogged/Sync Belt | All belt drives | 2% to 5% | High |
| Eliminate belt slip / proper tension | All belt drives | 1% to 3% | High |
| Synchronous motor | Large constant-speed | 4% to 7% | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power-Flow Block Diagram — Induction Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSrc[Electrical Supply<br/>3-phase AC] --> nodeIn[Input Power Pin<br/>sqrt 3 V I cos phi]
    nodeIn --> nodeCu1[Stator Copper Loss<br/>Pcu1]
    nodeCu1 --> nodeFe[Core or Iron Loss<br/>Pfe]
    nodeFe --> nodeGap[Air Gap Power<br/>Pgap]
    nodeGap --> nodeCu2[Rotor Copper Loss<br/>s times Pgap]
    nodeCu2 --> nodeMech[Mechanical Power<br/>1 minus s times Pgap]
    nodeMech --> nodeFw[Friction and Windage<br/>Pfw]
    nodeFw --> nodeOut[Shaft Output Power<br/>Pout]
```

**Reading guide:** Each block represents a power "withdrawal" (loss) or "transfer" (useful power). The motor's total losses are the sum of all yellow/heat blocks. The useful output is the final green block at the right.

---

## 4.2 VFD Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeGrid[AC Mains<br/>50 Hz Fixed] --> nodeRec[Rectifier Stage<br/>6-pulse Diode Bridge]
    nodeRec --> nodeDC[DC Link<br/>Filter Capacitor]
    nodeDC --> nodeInv[Inverter Stage<br/>IGBT PWM]
    nodeInv --> nodeMotor[3 phase Induction Motor<br/>Variable V and f]
    nodeMotor --> nodeLoad[Centrifugal Load<br/>Fan or Pump]
    nodeCtrl[Controller<br/>V by f = constant] -.-> nodeInv
```

**Key insight:** The DC link isolates the rectifier from the inverter. The controller enforces the **V/f constant** rule so that the motor's air-gap flux is preserved across the entire speed range.

---

## 4.3 Belt Drive Geometry & Tension States

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DriverPulley [Driver Pulley Diameter D1]
        dirA
    end
    subgraph DrivenPulley [Driven Pulley Diameter D2]
        dirB
    end
    DriverPulley -- Tight Side T1 --> DrivenPulley
    DrivenPulley -- Slack Side T2 --> DriverPulley
    DriverPulley -.Centrifugal Tension Tc.-> DrivenPulley
    DriverPulley --> AngleLap[Angle of Lap theta<br/>in radians]
    AngleLap --> EulerEq[Euler Equation<br/>T1 over T2 = exp mu theta]
    EulerEq --> BeltPower[Belt Power<br/>P = T1 minus T2 times v]
```

---

## 4.4 Motor Efficiency vs Load Curve — Conceptual Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[0 percent Load] --> B[25 percent Load<br/>Eta low 40 to 60 percent]
    B --> C[50 percent Load<br/>Eta rising 70 to 80 percent]
    C --> D[75 percent Load<br/>Eta near peak 85 to 90 percent]
    D --> E[100 percent Load<br/>Eta peak 88 to 95 percent]
    E --> F[110 percent Overload<br/>Eta falls sharply]
```

**Implication for Energy Auditors:** Operating motors consistently below 50% load is a red flag — it indicates **motor oversizing**, the most common energy waste in Indian industry (estimates of 30–50% of motors are oversized).

---

## 4.5 Affinity-Law Energy Savings Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Reduce Speed by 10 percent] --> B[Flow drops 10 percent]
    A --> C[Pressure drops 19 percent]
    A --> D[Power drops 27.1 percent]
    E[Reduce Speed by 20 percent] --> F[Flow drops 20 percent]
    E --> G[Pressure drops 36 percent]
    E --> H[Power drops 48.8 percent]
    I[Reduce Speed by 30 percent] --> J[Flow drops 30 percent]
    I --> K[Pressure drops 51 percent]
    I --> L[Power drops 65.7 percent]
```

This block is a **ready-reckoner** for audit calculations. Memorize the 20% and 30% cases — they appear in almost every KTU numerical problem.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> **Examination Pattern Reference (KTU 2024 Scheme ESE):**
> - **Part A:** 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks (short answer, direct recall)
> - **Part B:** Internal choice between 2 questions × 14 marks = 14 marks (analytical, derivation, or design)
> - Module 4 typically contributes **15–20%** of the paper, i.e., ~15 to 20 marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**"Define slip in an induction motor and explain why the rotor copper loss is given by $s \times P_{gap}$."** [3 marks]

**Model Answer:**
Slip $s$ is the fractional difference between synchronous speed $N_s$ and the actual rotor speed $N_r$:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

The rotor experiences relative motion between the rotating magnetic field (at $N_s$) and the rotor bars (at $N_r$). The frequency of induced rotor currents is $f_r = s f$. Since rotor copper loss is $P_{cu2} = 3 I_2^2 R_2$, and $I_2$ is proportional to slip for small $s$, the loss varies linearly with $s$. By the power-flow argument, the fraction of air-gap power $P_{gap}$ that is dissipated as rotor heat is exactly $s$, so:

$$P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_{gap}$$

The remaining fraction $(1-s)$ is converted to mechanical power — this is why **lower slip means higher efficiency**. [3]

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**"List the five major losses in a three-phase induction motor and state which two are roughly constant regardless of load."** [3 marks]

**Model Answer:**

| # | Loss | Load Dependence |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stator copper loss ($P_{cu1}$) | $\propto I^2$ → varies with load |
| 2 | Rotor copper loss ($P_{cu2}$) | $\propto s \cdot P_{gap}$ → varies with load |
| 3 | Core / iron loss ($P_{Fe}$) | **Roughly constant** (depends on V, f) |
| 4 | Friction and windage loss ($P_{fw}$) | **Roughly constant** (depends on speed) |
| 5 | Stray load loss ($P_{stray}$) | $\propto I^2$ → varies with load |

The **constant losses** (core + friction-windage) make the motor inefficient at very low load. [3]

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question B (Option A) — VFD Energy Savings [14 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Pattern, Module 4 Choice Question]**

A 30 kW, 4-pole, 3-phase induction motor drives a centrifugal pump in a process plant. The motor is running at full speed (1480 rpm) and draws 30 kW shaft power at 89% efficiency. A VFD is proposed to throttle the pump to 80% of rated flow during off-peak hours. The plant operates 7200 hours/year, and electricity costs ₹9/kWh. The VFD costs ₹4,50,000 including installation.

**Compute:**

**(a)** [7 marks] The annual energy and monetary savings assuming affinity laws hold.

**(b)** [7 marks] The simple payback period in months and a 5-year ROI.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a): Annual Energy and Monetary Savings**

**Step 1 — Relate speed reduction to flow reduction.**

Affinity law for flow: $\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = 0.8$, so $N_2 = 0.8 \times 1480 = 1184$ rpm.

**Step 2 — Apply the cube law for power.**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = (0.8)^3 = 0.512$$

The pump shaft power at reduced speed:

$$P_2 = 30 \times 0.512 = 15.36\;\text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Compute power input at the new operating point.**

New motor efficiency at reduced load is typically slightly lower; take $\eta_2 \approx 86\%$ (load has dropped):

$$P_{in,2} = \frac{15.36}{0.86} = 17.86\;\text{kW}$$

**Step 4 — Compute savings in input power.**

$$\Delta P_{in} = 30/0.89 - 17.86 = 33.71 - 17.86 = 15.85\;\text{kW}$$

**Step 5 — Compute annual energy and cost savings.**

$$\Delta E = 15.85 \times 7200 = 1{,}14{,}120\;\text{kWh/year}$$

$$\text{Savings} = 1{,}14{,}120 \times 9 = \text{₹ } 10{,}27{,}080\;\text{per year}$$

**[7 marks: 1 for affinity setup, 2 for power, 2 for input/savings, 2 for monetary figure with units]**

**Part (b): Payback Period and 5-Year ROI**

**Step 6 — Simple Payback Period.**

$$\text{Payback} = \frac{\text{VFD Cost}}{\text{Annual Savings}} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{10{,}27{,}080} = 0.438\;\text{years}$$

Converting to months:

$$\text{Payback} = 0.438 \times 12 = 5.26\;\text{months}$$

**Step 7 — 5-Year Net Savings and ROI.**

5-year net monetary savings:

$$5 \times 10{,}27{,}080 - 4{,}50{,}000 = 51{,}35{,}400 - 4{,}50{,}000 = \text{₹ } 46{,}85{,}400$$

ROI:

$$\text{ROI} = \frac{46{,}85{,}400}{4{,}50{,}000} \times 100 = 1041\%$$

**Conclusion:** A **5.3-month payback** with over **10× return in 5 years** — the VFD is a financially and energetically compelling retrofit. **[7 marks: 2 for payback, 2 for 5-year saving, 2 for ROI %, 1 for concluding recommendation]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** use pump power $30$ kW directly in cost saving without dividing by original efficiency. Many students use $30$ kW and skip the $33.71$ kW input, losing **2 marks**.
> - **Do NOT** assume new motor efficiency = old efficiency. State it explicitly; the KTU rubric gives **1 mark** for this assumption.
> - **Always include units** (kW, kWh, ₹, months). Loss of 1 mark for missing units is extremely common.

---

### Question B (Option B) — Belt Drive Derivation [14 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Pattern, Module 4 Choice Question]**

A flat belt drive has the following specifications:
- Driver pulley diameter $D_1 = 300$ mm, speed $N_1 = 1440$ rpm.
- Driven pulley diameter $D_2 = 600$ mm.
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.3$.
- Angle of lap on the smaller pulley $\theta = 160°$ (this is on the driver).
- Belt mass per unit length $m = 0.4$ kg/m.
- Center distance $C = 1.5$ m.

**Compute:**

**(a)** [7 marks] The belt velocity, the slip-free speed of the driven pulley, the tensions $T_1$ and $T_2$ under maximum-power conditions, and the maximum power transmitted.

**(b)** [7 marks] The required belt length, the power loss due to belt slip if actual slip $s_b = 0.02$, and the belt efficiency.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a): Belt Velocity, Tensions, and Power**

**Step 1 — Belt velocity.**

$$v = \frac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.300 \times 1440}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 432}{60} = 22.62\;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Centrifugal tension.**

$$T_c = m v^2 = 0.4 \times (22.62)^2 = 0.4 \times 511.66 = 204.66\;\text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $k = e^{\mu \theta}$.**

$\theta$ must be in radians: $\theta = 160° \times \pi/180 = 2.793$ rad.

$$k = e^{0.3 \times 2.793} = e^{0.838} = 2.312$$

**Step 4 — Apply maximum-power condition $T_1 = 3 T_c$.**

$$T_1 = 3 \times 204.66 = 613.99\;\text{N} \approx 614\;\text{N}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $T_2$.**

$$T_2 = \frac{T_1}{k} = \frac{614}{2.312} = 265.6\;\text{N}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum power transmitted.**

$$P_{max} = (T_1 - T_2) v = (614 - 265.6) \times 22.62 = 348.4 \times 22.62 = 7880\;\text{W} \approx 7.88\;\text{kW}$$

**[7 marks: 1 for v, 1 for Tc, 1 for k, 1 for T1, 1 for T2, 1 for Pmax, 1 for correct unit conversion]**

**Part (b): Belt Length, Slip Loss, and Efficiency**

**Step 7 — Open-belt length.**

$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \frac{(D_1 - D_2)^2}{4C}$$

Convert all dimensions to mm: $C = 1500$ mm, $D_1 = 300$ mm, $D_2 = 600$ mm.

$$L = 2(1500) + \frac{\pi}{2}(900) + \frac{(300)^2}{4 \times 1500}$$

$$L = 3000 + 1413.72 + \frac{90000}{6000} = 3000 + 1413.72 + 15 = 4428.72\;\text{mm} \approx 4.43\;\text{m}$$

**Step 8 — Slip-free driven speed.**

$$N_2 = N_1 \times \frac{D_1}{D_2} = 1440 \times \frac{300}{600} = 720\;\text{rpm}$$

**Step 9 — Actual driven speed with slip.**

$$N_{2,actual} = N_2 \times (1 - s_b) = 720 \times 0.98 = 705.6\;\text{rpm}$$

**Step 10 — Power loss due to slip.**

Slip loss fraction is approximately equal to belt slip $s_b$ (for thin belts), so:

$$P_{slip} = s_b \times P_{max} = 0.02 \times 7880 = 157.6\;\text{W}$$

**Step 11 — Belt efficiency.**

$$\eta_{belt} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1} = \frac{614 - 265.6}{614} = \frac{348.4}{614} = 0.5674 = 56.74\%$$

Or, including slip, effective efficiency:

$$\eta_{eff} = \eta_{belt} \times (1 - s_b) = 0.5674 \times 0.98 = 0.556 = 55.6\%$$

**Conclusion:** The drive transmits **7.88 kW** with **~56% efficiency**, typical of a flat belt. A V-belt would push this to 70–85%. **[7 marks: 2 for length, 1 for slip-free N2, 1 for actual N2, 1 for slip loss, 2 for efficiency with both definitions]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Always convert $\theta$ from degrees to radians** before applying Euler's equation. Using $e^{0.3 \times 160}$ instead of $e^{0.3 \times 2.793}$ is the **#1 reason students lose 2 marks** on belt problems.
> - **State the maximum-power condition** ($T_1 = 3T_c$) explicitly. Don't jump straight to numerical substitution — KTU awards a step mark for the assumption.
> - **Show the unit conversions** (mm vs m, rpm vs rad/s). The KTU valuation key lists unit conversion as a separate step.
> - **Distinguish belt efficiency from system efficiency.** The 56% above is the belt's *mechanical* efficiency; overall drive efficiency must also include motor, coupling, and driven machine.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Last-minute revision checklist — read this aloud twice before the exam.**

- [ ] **Slip** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ is small (~0.03), and **rotor copper loss = $s \times P_{gap}$** is the most fundamental motor-energy equation.
- [ ] **Synchronous speed** $N_s = 120f/P$; memorize for $f = 50$ Hz at 2, 4, 6, 8 poles → 3000, 1500, 1000, 750 rpm.
- [ ] **Five losses** of an induction motor: stator Cu, rotor Cu, iron, friction-windage, stray. **Core + friction-windage are constant.**
- [ ] **Motor efficiency peaks between 75% and 100% load.** Below 50% load, efficiency collapses → **right-sizing** is a primary energy lever.
- [ ] **IE3 and IE4 motors** reduce losses by 20–40% versus IE1 — the cheapest "free" saving when a motor fails.
- [ ] **Belt velocity** $v = \pi D_1 N_1 / 60$ in m/s with D in m and N in rpm.
- [ ] **Euler's belt equation** $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta}$ — $\theta$ **must be in radians**.
- [ ] **Centrifugal tension** $T_c = m v^2$ — a belt running too fast wastes energy and risks failure.
- [ ] **Maximum-power condition** $T_1 = 3 T_c$ — derives from $dP/dv = 0$.
- [ ] **Open-belt length** formula is long but tested every KTU cycle.
- [ ] **Affinity laws:** Flow $\propto N$, Head $\propto N^2$, **Power $\propto N^3$**.
- [ ] **VFD savings** at 20% speed reduction ≈ 49% power reduction. **At 30% ≈ 66%**.
- [ ] **Payback period** of VFDs on centrifugal loads is typically **6–24 months**.
- [ ] **Power factor** is poor at light load; **PF correction** (capacitor bank or synchronous motor) is recommended for plants with many lightly-loaded motors.
- [ ] **Belt drive efficiency** (flat) ~50–60%, **V-belt** ~70–85%, **timing belt** ~95–98% (no slip).
- [ ] **Energy management priority for motor systems:** (1) Switch off, (2) Right-size, (3) Use efficient motor, (4) Add VFD, (5) Use high-efficiency transmission.

> **Final Examiner's Mantra:** *Numbers, units, and assumptions — in that order, every time.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refrigeration System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Refrigeration System ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Refrigeration System** is an engineered thermodynamic arrangement that transfers heat from a low-temperature reservoir (the space to be cooled) to a high-temperature reservoir (the surroundings), thereby maintaining the low-temperature region at a temperature below that of the surroundings. This heat transfer process is achieved by the consumption of external work (mechanical or thermal energy), as dictated by the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** and the **Clausius Statement**. Mathematically, the effectiveness of any refrigeration system is quantified by the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**, defined as the ratio of the desired effect (heat extracted from the cold space, $Q_L$) to the work input ($W_{in}$): $$COP_{Ref} = \frac{Desired\ Output}{Work\ Input} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{in}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PESFT741 Module 4):** Refrigeration systems are studied under Energy Management because they represent one of the **largest single electrical end-use loads** in commercial and industrial facilities (typically 30–50% of total building energy consumption). Hence, optimizing their COP is a primary Energy Conservation Measure (ECM). ### 1.2 Classification of Refrigeration Systems Refrigeration systems are broadly classified into two principal categories based on the energy input mechanism: | Classification | Energy Input | Working Principle | Common Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Vapor Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** | Mechanical (Compressor work) | Phase change of refrigerant | Domestic AC, Cold Storage, Industrial Chillers | | **Vapor Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)** | Thermal (Heat at generator) | Absorption-desorption cycle | Large-capacity industrial cooling, Waste heat recovery | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy: The "Water Sponge on a Hill"** > > Imagine a cold, wet sponge at the bottom of a hill. The sponge (refrigerant) absorbs heat from the valley (cold space to be cooled). Now you want to "squeeze" this heat out and dump it at the top of the hill (hot surroundings). > > - In **VCRS**, you carry the sponge up the hill yourself and physically squeeze it (this is your **work input** via a compressor). > - In **VARS**, you pour a special chemical (absorber) at the bottom that "drinks" the refrigerant, and then you heat this mixture at the top to evaporate the refrigerant (this is your **heat input** at the generator). > > The **COP** is essentially how efficiently your "sponge wringing process" extracts cooling per unit of effort spent. ### 1.4 Standard Refrigeration Metrics for KTU Board Exams The following metrics are **essential board-exam terminology**: - **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** = **3.517 kW** of cooling capacity (the rate of heat removal required to freeze 1 ton of ice in 24 hours from 0 °C water). - **1 kW = 0.2843 TR** - **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** = $\frac{Power\ Input\ (kW)}{Cooling\ Output\ (TR)}$ (typical benchmark: 0.6–1.2 kW/TR for VCRS). - **Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER)** = $\frac{Q_L\ (BTU/hr)}{Power\ (W)}$ — used predominantly in North America. - **Indian Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio (ISEER)** = $\frac{Cooling\ Provided\ (Wh)}{Energy\ Consumed\ (Wh)}$ over an entire season — **mandatory BEE star labelling metric in India**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Vapor Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) The **VCRS** is the most commercially dominant refrigeration cycle globally. It operates on a closed loop employing a single-component, two-phase working fluid (refrigerant). The ideal cycle modeled on a Pressure-Enthalpy (P-h) diagram is called the **Ideal Vapor Compression Cycle** (sometimes referred to as the **Simple Saturated Cycle**). #### 2.1.1 The Four Key Processes The cycle consists of four distinct steady-flow processes executed by four primary components: **Process 1 → 2: Compression (Isentropic)** - **Component:** Compressor - **Mechanism:** Low-pressure, low-temperature refrigerant vapor is compressed to a high-pressure, high-temperature superheated vapor. - **Energy Input:** Compressor work $W_c$ - **Governing Equation:** $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ > [!NOTE] > **Why Isentropic?** In KTU board problems, compression is **always assumed isentropic** (reversible adiabatic) unless stated otherwise. This is because it represents the *ideal* benchmark against which real compressors (with isentropic efficiency $\eta_c \approx 70\text{–}85\%$) are compared. **Process 2 → 3: Condensation (Isobaric Heat Rejection)** - **Component:** Condenser - **Mechanism:** Superheated vapor rejects heat to the surroundings at constant pressure, desuperheating, condensing, and subcooling until it becomes a saturated (or subcooled) liquid. - **Energy Output:** Heat rejected $Q_H$ - **Governing Equation:** $Q_H = h_2 - h_3$ **Process 3 → 4: Expansion (Isenthalpic Throttling)** - **Component:** Expansion Valve (Capillary tube / Thermostatic Expansion Valve / Electronic Expansion Valve) - **Mechanism:** High-pressure liquid is throttled to low pressure, resulting in a two-phase mixture at low temperature. - **Energy Input:** $W_{exp} = 0$ (no work, irreversible process) - **Governing Equation:** $h_4 = h_3$ **Process 4 → 1: Evaporation (Isobaric Heat Absorption)** - **Component:** Evaporator - **Mechanism:** Low-pressure, low-temperature two-phase mixture absorbs heat from the refrigerated space at constant pressure, fully evaporating into a saturated (or slightly superheated) vapor. - **Energy Input:** Refrigeration Effect $Q_L$ - **Governing Equation:** $Q_L = h_1 - h_4$ #### 2.1.2 The Reverse Carnot Cycle — The Theoretical Benchmark The **Reverse Carnot Cycle** is the theoretical upper limit for any refrigeration cycle operating between two specified thermal reservoirs. It consists of two isothermal and two isentropic processes. - **COP of Reverse Carnot Refrigerator:** $COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Insight:** The COP of an *ideal* VCRS is **always less than** the COP of a Reverse Carnot cycle operating between the same temperature limits. As the temperature lift ($T_H - T_L$) decreases, the COP increases sharply. This is the thermodynamic foundation of all refrigeration energy conservation measures. ### 2.2 The Vapor Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS) The **VARS** replaces the mechanical compressor with a **thermal compressor** consisting of an absorber, a solution pump, and a generator (with a regenerator/rectifier in advanced systems). The most common working pair is the **Lithium-Bromide/Water (LiBr-H₂O)** pair (for chilling above 0 °C, e.g., air conditioning) and the **Ammonia/Water (NH₃-H₂O)** pair (for sub-zero industrial refrigeration). #### 2.2.1 The Six Processes of an Ideal VARS 1. **Generator:** Heat input $Q_{gen}$ at high pressure drives refrigerant out of the solution. 2. **Condenser:** Rejected heat $Q_{cond}$ to surroundings. 3. **Expansion Valve 1:** Throttling to evaporator pressure. 4. **Evaporator:** Cooling effect $Q_{evap} = Q_L$. 5. **Absorber:** Vapor is absorbed by the weak solution releasing heat $Q_{abs}$ to surroundings. 6. **Pump + Expansion Valve 2:** Pump (small work input $W_p$) and throttling return strong solution to generator. #### 2.2.2 The VARS COP $$COP_{VARS} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_{gen} + W_p} \approx \frac{Q_L}{Q_{gen}} \quad (since\ W_p \ll Q_{gen})$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why Study VARS in Energy Management?** VARS can be directly fired by **waste heat, solar thermal energy, geothermal energy, or process steam** — making it an excellent Energy Conservation Measure (ECM) in industries with surplus low-grade thermal energy. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Table Legend:** Use `\vert` for absolute-value notation to preserve markdown table syntax. The notation `kJ/kg` indicates specific (per unit mass) enthalpy. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Validity / Remarks | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | COP (Refrigerator) | $COP = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$ | dimensionless | Universal for any refrigeration cycle | | 2 | COP (Heat Pump) | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}}$ | dimensionless | $COP_{HP} = COP_{Ref} + 1$ (always) | | 3 | Reverse Carnot COP | $COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | T in Kelvin only; theoretical maximum | | 4 | Compressor Work | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Ideal = isentropic | | 5 | Refrigeration Effect | $q_L = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Per kg of refrigerant | | 6 | Heat Rejection (Condenser) | $q_H = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | Per kg of refrigerant | | 7 | Throttling | $h_4 = h_3$ | kJ/kg | Isenthalpic (irreversible) | | 8 | Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_L\ (kW)}{q_L\ (kJ/kg)}$ | kg/s | Used for sizing | | 9 | Compressor Power | $\dot{W}_c = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$ | kW | Electrical input to motor | | 10 | Ton of Refrigeration | $1\ TR = 3.517\ kW$ | kW | Heat removal rate | | 11 | EER | $EER = \dfrac{Q_L\ (BTU/h)}{P\ (W)}$ | BTU/Wh | North American metric | | 12 | ISEER | $ISEER = \dfrac{\sum Q_L\ (Wh)}{\sum E\ (Wh)}$ | dimensionless | BEE India star rating basis | | 13 | Compressor Isentropic Efficiency | $\eta_c = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1}$ | fraction | Real vs ideal compression | | 14 | VARS COP | $COP_{VARS} \approx \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{Q_{gen}}$ | dimensionless | Neglects small pump work | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In practical Energy Management audits (e.g., as per the **Indian Energy Conservation Act 2001** and **BEE Star Labelling Program**): 1. **Industrial Cold Storage:** COP improvements of just 10–15% translate to lakhs of rupees in annual savings. 2. **HVAC Systems in Commercial Buildings:** Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) on compressor motors and Electronic Expansion Valves (EEVs) are standard ECMs. 3. **District Cooling:** Centralized VARS or VCRS plants reduce per-ton energy consumption by 20–30% over individual units. 4. **Pharmaceutical/Food Processing:** Precise temperature control with optimized COP ensures both product quality and ISO 50001 energy compliance. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: COP of a Reversed Carnot Cycle **Starting Premise (Clausius Inequality):** The Clausius inequality for a reversible cycle states: $$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$$ For a reversed Carnot cycle operating between a cold reservoir at $T_L$ and a hot reservoir at $T_H$, the heat transfers occur isothermally: $$\frac{Q_L}{T_L} - \frac{Q_H}{T_H} = 0$$ **Step 1:** Solve for the ratio of heats: $$\frac{Q_H}{Q_H} \cdot \frac{Q_L}{Q_L} \implies Q_H = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics for a cyclic device: $$W_{net,in} = Q_H - Q_L$$ **Step 3:** Substitute the expression for $Q_H$: $$W_{net,in} = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L} - Q_L = Q_L \left( \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L} \right)$$ **Step 4:** Substitute into the COP definition: $$COP_{Ref} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_L \left( \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L} \right)}$$ **Step 5:** Simplify: $$\boxed{COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** This derivation proves that for any given $T_H$, lowering $T_L$ (colder refrigerated space) **drastically** reduces COP — because $T_H - T_L$ grows in the denominator. This is why cryogenic refrigeration (e.g., LNG production) is extraordinarily energy-intensive. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relation Between Heat Pump COP and Refrigerator COP **Starting Premise:** A heat pump and a refrigerator are thermodynamically identical devices — both reject heat $Q_H$ at a high temperature and absorb heat $Q_L$ at a low temperature, with work input $W$. **Step 1:** By energy conservation: $$Q_H = Q_L + W$$ **Step 2:** Express both COPs: $$COP_{Ref} = \frac{Q_L}{W}, \quad COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $Q_H$: $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_L + W}{W} = \frac{Q_L}{W} + 1$$ $$\boxed{COP_{HP} = COP_{Ref} + 1}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Pitfall:** Students frequently claim $COP_{Ref} = COP_{HP}$. The correct relation is always **$COP_{HP} = COP_{Ref} + 1$**.
