For any cycle with $COP_{Ref} > 1$, the heat pump COP is necessarily greater than 2. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: COP of an Ideal VCRS on the P-h Diagram **Given Conditions (Standard Board Problem Setup):** - Evaporator temperature: $T_L = -10\ °C = 263.15\ K$ - Condenser temperature: $T_H = 40\ °C = 313.15\ K$ - Refrigerant enters compressor as **saturated vapor** (point 1). - Refrigerant exits condenser as **saturated liquid** (point 3). - Expansion valve is isenthalpic. **Reference Property Tables (R-134a — Simplified for Board Computation):** At $T_L = -10\ °C$ (saturated): - $h_1 = h_g = 244.55\ kJ/kg$ - $s_1 = s_g = 0.9372\ kJ/kg\cdot K$ At $T_H = 40\ °C$ (saturated): - $h_3 = h_f = 108.26\ kJ/kg$ - $s_3 = s_f = 0.3949\ kJ/kg\cdot K$ At $T_H = 40\ °C$ superheated (interpolated for point 2s): - $s_2 = s_1 = 0.9372\ kJ/kg\cdot K$ (isentropic condition) **Step 1:** Find $h_2$ using isentropic compression condition $s_2 = s_1 = 0.9372\ kJ/kg\cdot K$ at $T_H = 40\ °C$ (superheated region). From standard R-134a superheated tables at 40 °C, interpolating between $s = 0.92691$ → $h = 271.33\ kJ/kg$ and $s = 0.97991$ → $h = 280.19\ kJ/kg$: $$h_{2s} \approx 271.33 + \frac{(0.9372 - 0.92691)}{(0.97991 - 0.92691)} \times (280.19 - 271.33)$$ $$h_{2s} \approx 271.33 + \frac{0.01029}{0.05300} \times 8.86 \approx 271.33 + 1.72 \approx 273.05\ kJ/kg$$ **Step 2:** Compute compressor work per kg: $$w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 273.05 - 244.55 = 28.50\ kJ/kg$$ **Step 3:** Compute throttling exit enthalpy: $$h_4 = h_3 = 108.26\ kJ/kg$$ **Step 4:** Compute refrigeration effect per kg: $$q_L = h_1 - h_4 = 244.55 - 108.26 = 136.29\ kJ/kg$$ **Step 5:** Compute the COP: $$\boxed{COP_{VCRS} = \frac{q_L}{w_c} = \frac{136.29}{28.50} \approx 4.78}$$ **Step 6 (Validation):** Compute the Carnot COP for the same temperature limits: $$COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263.15}{313.15 - 263.15} = \frac{263.15}{50} \approx 5.26$$ **Validation Result:** $COP_{VCRS} = 4.78 < COP_{Carnot} = 5.26$ ✓ — confirms the Second Law bound. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Energy Conservation Opportunities in VCRS The following matrix identifies, for each component, the dominant energy loss and the corresponding Energy Conservation Measure (ECM): | Component | Dominant Loss Mechanism | Energy Conservation Measure (ECM) | Typical Savings | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Compressor** | Motor electrical losses + irreversibility | VFD on motor, scroll/inverter compressor | 15–30% | | **Condenser** | High condensing temperature (fouling, poor airflow) | Regular cleaning, condenser fan VFD, condenserless designs | 8–15% | | **Expansion Valve** | Hunting / overfeeding / underfeeding | Electronic Expansion Valve (EEV), EEV + superheat control | 5–10% | | **Evaporator** | Frost buildup, poor air distribution | Regular defrost cycles, optimized air velocity (2–3 m/s) | 5–12% | | **Refrigerant Lines** | Heat gain (suction) / heat loss (liquid) | Proper insulation, short routing | 2–5% | | **Overall Plant** | Simultaneous subcooling / superheat gaps | Multi-evaporator staging, floating head pressure control | 10–25% | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** When answering "Discuss energy conservation in refrigeration" type questions, students must cover **at least three** of the above ECMs with quantitative justification. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Schematic: Ideal Vapor Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) — T-s and P-h Diagrams The figure below depicts the **cycle architecture**, the **process flow**, and the **P-h diagram** simultaneously. Process arrows show the refrigerant state trajectory. ```mermaid flowchart LR comp1["1: Compressor
Low-P Vapour"] comp2["2: Condenser
High-P Superheated Vapour"] comp3["3: Expansion Valve
High-P Liquid"] comp4["4: Evaporator
Low-P Two-Phase"] cd1["State 1
h1, s1
Sat. Vapour"] cd2["State 2s
h2s, s1
Superheated"] cd3["State 3
h3, s3
Sat. Liquid"] cd4["State 4
h4=h3
Two-Phase"] subgraph PlantLoop["Refrigeration Plant Hardware Loop"] direction LR comp1 -- Wc ISENTROPIC COMPRESSION --> comp2 comp2 -- qH CONDENSATION --> comp3 comp3 -- h4=h3 THROTTLING --> comp4 comp4 -- qL EVAPORATION --> comp1 end subgraph PHdiag["Pressure Enthalpy Diagram States"] direction TB cd1 --- cd2 cd2 --- cd3 cd3 --- cd4 cd4 --- cd1 end QL[/"QL: Cooling Effect
Absorbed at Evaporator"/] Wc[/"Wc: Compressor Work
Electrical Input"/] QH[/"QH: Heat Rejected
Dissipated at Condenser"/] comp4 -.- QL comp1 -.- Wc comp2 -.- QH ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram:** Trace the refrigerant flow clockwise through `comp1 → comp2 → comp3 → comp4 → comp1`. The external annotations (QL, Wc, QH) represent the **energy interactions** at each component boundary with the surroundings. ### 4.2 Schematic: Vapor Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS) Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB gen["Generator
Heat Input Qgen
Drives Refrigerant Out"] cond["Condenser
Heat Rejection Qcond"] evap["Evaporator
Refrigeration Effect QL"] abs["Absorber
Heat Rejection Qabs"] pump["Solution Pump
Small Work Wp"] expV1["Expansion Valve 1
to Evaporator"] expV2["Expansion Valve 2
to Absorber"] gen -- "Refrigerant Vapour" --> cond cond -- "High-P Liquid" --> expV1 expV1 -- "Low-P Two-Phase" --> evap evap -- "Low-P Vapour" --> abs abs -- "Weak Solution" --> pump pump -- "High-P Solution" --> gen abs -.-> expV2 expV2 -.-> gen Qheat[/"Qgen: External Heat Source
Waste Heat / Steam / Solar"/] Qcool[/"QL: Useful Cooling"/] Qabs[/"Qabs: Absorber Heat Loss"/] Qcond[/"Qcond: Condenser Heat Loss"/] gen -.- Qheat evap -.- Qcool abs -.- Qabs cond -.- Qcond ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Distinguishing Feature vs VCRS:** Notice that VARS has **no compressor**. The "compressor equivalent" is achieved via **heat input at the generator** ($Q_{gen}$) plus a **small pump** ($W_p$). This is why VARS is favored in applications where **waste heat is freely available**. ### 4.3 Block Diagram: Energy Management Strategy for a Refrigeration Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Plant Energy Audit
Measure TR & kW"] B["Baseline SEC
kW per TR"] C["Benchmark vs BEE
Star Rating"] D["Identify ECMs
Table Loss Matrix"] E["Implement ECMs
EEV VFD Insulation"] F["Re-measure
New SEC"] G["Compute Savings
Annual kWh"] H["Monitor M&V
ISO 50001"] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> H H -.->|"Continuous Improvement Loop"| A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** This is the standard **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle adapted for refrigeration energy management under **ISO 50001 EnMS**. Energy auditors for KTU's Energy Manager Certification (BEE, India) follow this exact flow. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A] > **Define the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator. How is it different from a heat pump COP?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of the desired refrigeration effect ($Q_L$) to the net work input ($W_{net}$): $$COP_{Ref} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$$ A heat pump COP is defined instead with respect to the desired heating effect ($Q_H$): $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$$ The fundamental relationship is $\mathbf{COP_{HP} = COP_{Ref} + 1}$, since $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$. Unlike an engine's thermal efficiency (always < 1), refrigeration COPs are dimensionless and can exceed unity. --- > **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4] > **List any three desirable properties of an ideal refrigerant used in VCRS.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **Low boiling point** at atmospheric pressure (e.g., R-134a boils at −26.3 °C at 1 atm) to enable low-temperature evaporation. 2. **High latent heat of vaporization** — to maximize refrigeration effect per kg of refrigerant circulated. 3. **Low toxicity, non-flammable, and zero Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)** — environmental safety (e.g., R-134a, R-1234yf). 4. *(Optional bonus)* **Low Global Warming Potential (GWP < 150)** as per Kigali Amendment and BEE regulations. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **Question A.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B, Module 4] > > **(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a P-h diagram, explain the working of an ideal Vapor Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS). Derive the expression for COP. > > **(b)** [7 Marks] An ideal VCRS uses R-134a as the refrigerant. The evaporator operates at **−10 °C** and the condenser at **40 °C**. The refrigerant leaves the condenser as saturated liquid and enters the compressor as saturated vapor. Calculate: > - (i) Refrigeration effect per kg > - (ii) Compressor work per kg > - (iii) COP of the cycle > - (iv) Compare the COP with the corresponding reverse Carnot COP. **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 Marks] Working of VCRS — P-h Diagram Approach** The four ideal processes on the P-h diagram are: - **1→2**: Isentropic compression in compressor ($s_1 = s_2$). - **2→3**: Isobaric condensation in condenser (heat $q_H$ rejected to surroundings). - **3→4**: Isenthalpic throttling in expansion valve ($h_3 = h_4$). - **4→1**: Isobaric evaporation in evaporator (heat $q_L$ absorbed). Energy balance per kg of refrigerant: $$q_L = h_1 - h_4, \quad w_c = h_2 - h_1, \quad q_H = h_2 - h_3$$ Applying the First Law (cyclic device, net work = net heat): $$w_c = q_H - q_L$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{COP = \frac{q_L}{w_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}}$$ **(b) [7 Marks] Numerical Computation** **Given:** R-134a, $T_L = -10\ °C = 263.15\ K$, $T_H = 40\ °C = 313.15\ K$. **Property values (from R-134a saturated tables):** At $-10\ °C$: $h_1 = h_g = 244.55\ kJ/kg$, $s_1 = s_g = 0.9372\ kJ/kg\cdot K$. At $40\ °C$: $h_3 = h_f = 108.26\ kJ/kg$, $s_3 = s_f = 0.3949\ kJ/kg\cdot K$. **Step 1 — Isentropic compression to find $h_2$:** At $T = 40\ °C$, the isentropic condition $s_2 = s_1 = 0.9372\ kJ/kg\cdot K$ corresponds to a superheated state. Using the superheated table at 40 °C (interpolation between saturation and 50 °C): $$h_2 \approx 273.05\ kJ/kg$$ **(i) Refrigeration effect:** $$q_L = h_1 - h_4 = h_1 - h_3 = 244.55 - 108.26 = \mathbf{136.29\ kJ/kg}$$ **(ii) Compressor work:** $$w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 273.05 - 244.55 = \mathbf{28.50\ kJ/kg}$$ **(iii) COP of VCRS:** $$COP_{VCRS} = \frac{q_L}{w_c} = \frac{136.29}{28.50} = \mathbf{4.78}$$ **(iv) Reverse Carnot COP comparison:** $$COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263.15}{313.15 - 263.15} = \frac{263.15}{50} = \mathbf{5.26}$$ **Comparative Conclusion:** $COP_{VCRS} = 4.78 < COP_{Carnot} = 5.26$, which **validates the Second Law of Thermodynamics**. The actual VCRS achieves about $\frac{4.78}{5.26} \times 100\% \approx 90.9\%$ of the ideal Carnot performance — an excellent practical efficiency. --- > **Question B.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Set A — Internal Choice Alternative] > > **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the working principle of a Vapor Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS). Compare its COP expression with that of a VCRS and state two industrial applications. > > **(b)** [7 Marks] A cold storage plant requires **50 TR** of refrigeration capacity. The operating VCRS has a measured COP of **3.2**. Calculate: > - (i) Refrigeration effect in kW > - (ii) Compressor power input in kW > - (iii) Annual electricity consumption in kWh (assume 16 hours/day, 300 days/year operation) > - (iv) Annual electricity cost in ₹ (assume ₹8.00/kWh tariff) **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 Marks] VARS Working Principle and Comparison** **Working Principle:** In a VARS, the mechanical compressor of VCRS is replaced by an **absorber-generator-pump** assembly. The refrigerant (e.g., ammonia) is absorbed by an absorbent (e.g., water) in the absorber at low pressure, forming a strong solution. A pump raises the pressure, and external heat at the generator drives off the refrigerant vapour. The vapour is condensed, throttled, and evaporated — completing the cycle. The absorber and generator together act as a **"thermal compressor"**. **COP Comparison:** - VCRS: $COP = \dfrac{q_L}{w_c}$ (uses mechanical work). - VARS: $COP \approx \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{Q_{gen}}$ (uses thermal energy; pump work negligible). **Two Industrial Applications:** 1. Large-capacity industrial chilling (e.g., breweries, dairy plants). 2. Cold storage in remote locations with abundant waste-heat or solar thermal input. **(b) [7 Marks] Cold Storage Plant Calculation** **(i) Refrigeration effect in kW:** $$Q_L = 50\ TR \times 3.517\ kW/TR = \mathbf{175.85\ kW}$$ **(ii) Compressor power input:** $$W_{in} = \frac{Q_L}{COP} = \frac{175.85}{3.2} = \mathbf{54.95\ kW}$$ **(iii) Annual electricity consumption:** $$E = 54.95\ kW \times 16\ hr/day \times 300\ days/year = \mathbf{263{,}760\ kWh/year}$$ **(iv) Annual electricity cost:** $$\text{Cost} = 263{,}760\ kWh \times ₹8.00/kWh = \mathbf{₹\ 21{,}10{,}080\ per\ annum}$$ **Energy Conservation Implication:** If the COP is improved from 3.2 to 4.5 (achievable with VFD, EEV, and condenser optimization — see Loss Matrix), the new power input becomes: $$W_{new} = \frac{175.85}{4.5} = 39.08\ kW$$ $$E_{new} = 39.08 \times 16 \times 300 = 187{,}584\ kWh/year$$ **Annual Savings:** ₹$(21,10,080 - 15,00,672)$ ≈ **₹ 6,09,408/year** — illustrates the enormous financial incentive for ECMs in refrigeration. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in Refrigeration System problems (Module 4):** > > 1. **Unit Conversion Trap (3-mark deduction):** Students frequently forget to convert °C to K when computing the reverse Carnot COP. Always use **Kelvin** for $T_L$ and $T_H$ in the Carnot formula. Mixing °C and K is the **#1 error**. > > 2. **Sign Convention Confusion (2-mark deduction):** The heat rejected by the condenser ($Q_H$) and heat absorbed by the evaporator ($Q_L$) are both taken as **positive magnitudes**. Work input is also positive. Avoid putting "negative signs" inside the COP ratio. > > 3. **Confusing Refrigerator COP with Heat Pump COP (2–3-mark deduction):** The question must specify whether the device is a refrigerator or a heat pump. Writing $COP = Q_H / W$ when the device is meant to cool is a common blunder. > > 4. **Skipping the P-h Diagram (3-mark deduction):** In any VCRS derivation question, the P-h diagram with **all four labeled state points** is mandatory. Without it, the examiner deducts 3–4 marks even if the derivation is correct. > > 5. **Forgetting the Energy Conservation Discussion (3-mark deduction):** Part (b) on ECMs must include **quantitative** justification — e.g., "VFD can save 15–25% compressor energy" — not vague qualitative statements. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Refrigeration System" (Module 4):** > > - **COP Definitions:** Refrigerator $COP = Q_L / W$; Heat Pump $COP = Q_H / W$; **relation:** $COP_{HP} = COP_{Ref} + 1$. > - **Carnot COP:** $T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ — **always use Kelvin**; serves as the thermodynamic benchmark. > - **VCRS Four Processes:** Isentropic compression → Isobaric condensation → Isenthalpic throttling → Isobaric evaporation. > - **P-h Diagram is Mandatory:** Always draw it for full marks. > - **VARS Distinctiveness:** No compressor; uses **heat input at generator**; excellent for waste-heat utilization. > - **1 TR = 3.517 kW** — essential conversion factor. > - **ISEER** is the official BEE (India) seasonal efficiency metric — relevant for energy manager certification. > - **ECMs (ranked by impact):** VFD on compressor > Condenser cleaning & head pressure control > EEV > Insulation > Defrost optimization. > - **Second Law Bound:** Real cycle COP < Carnot COP for the same $T_L, T_H$. > - **Common Refrigerants:** R-134a (current HFC), R-1234yf (low-GWP future), R-717 (ammonia, industrial), R-744 (CO₂, transcritical). > - **Climate Relevance (Kerala/South India context):** High ambient temperatures reduce COP — necessitates oversized condensers and proper siting (avoid direct sunlight). > - **ISO 50001 Linkage:** Refrigeration plant energy management is a classic PDCA-cycle application under the EnMS standard.

topics.title: Heat Recovery and Co-generation: Heat Recovery from Ventilation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Heat Recovery and Co-generation: Heat Recovery from Ventilation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Heat Recovery from Ventilation (HRV)** is an engineered energy conservation technique that captures the thermal energy (both *sensible* and/or *latent* heat) contained in the **exhaust/stale air** being discharged from a conditioned space (building, industrial hall, cleanroom, etc.) and transfers that energy to the incoming **fresh/supply air** drawn from the outside atmosphere. This process is mediated by dedicated heat-recovery devices such as **plate heat exchangers, heat pipes, thermal wheels (rotary enthalpy wheels), or run-around coil systems**, without allowing the two airstreams to physically mix. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology (PESFT741):** > The KTU syllabus explicitly classifies ventilation heat recovery as a sub-module of "Waste Heat Recovery" alongside combustion air pre-heating and co-generation. Students must distinguish between **HRV (Heat Recovery Ventilator – sensible heat only)** and **ERV (Energy Recovery Ventilator – both sensible + latent heat)**. This distinction carries high weightage in module-end evaluations. The governing engineering metric is the **Temperature (or Enthalpy) Effectiveness**, defined by the **Air-Conditioning and Refrigeration Institute (ARI / AHRI Standard 1060)** and adopted universally in KTU references: $$\varepsilon_t = \frac{T_{2} - T_{1}}{T_{3} - T_{1}}$$ where the temperature symbols refer to the supply and exhaust airstreams measured at the inlet and outlet of the heat-recovery device. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building's "Heat Lung" Imagine a building breathing through two nostrils — one nostril inhales cold, dry outside air, and the other exhales warm, stale indoor air. Without intervention, the building is constantly **throwing away** the heat it spent money to generate (via heaters in winter or AC in summer). A **heat-recovery ventilator** acts like a *membrane between the two nostrils* — like the septum of the nose — that lets warmth pass from the exhaled breath to the inhaled air, but keeps the actual air molecules separate (so we do not breathe back our own CO₂). The building still gets a fresh oxygen supply, but it recycles the *energy* it paid to condition that air. This is precisely the philosophy behind **HRV** — *recirculating energy, not air*. > [!NOTE] > **Why is this important for India/Kerala specifically?** > Buildings in tropical-humid climates (Kerala, coastal Karnataka, Tamil Nadu) use vast quantities of energy to **dehumidify and cool** incoming ventilation air. Latent heat recovery (ERV) directly reduces the cooling-coil load by pre-dehumidifying the fresh air, leading to **kWh savings of 25–45 %** on the HVAC system. KTU examiners frequently frame numerical problems around this scenario. ### 1.3 Key Terminology Anchor Points The following standardized vocabulary is **mandatory** for KTU answers: | Term | Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---| | **Sensible Heat Recovery (SHR)** | Transfer of dry-bulb temperature energy only | kW | | **Latent Heat Recovery (LHR)** | Transfer of moisture/enthalpy associated with humidification | kW | | **Sensible Effectiveness (εs)** | Ratio of actual to maximum possible sensible heat transfer | dimensionless (0–1) | | **Latent Effectiveness (εl)** | Ratio of actual to maximum possible latent (moisture) transfer | dimensionless (0–1) | | **Total (Enthalpy) Effectiveness (εt)** | Combined sensible + latent effectiveness | dimensionless (0–1) | | **Air Changes Per Hour (ACH)** | Volumetric ventilation rate divided by room volume | h⁻¹ | | **Apparent Sensible Heat Ratio (ASHR)** | Used in psychrometric calculations of the cooled coil | — | ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Reference Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard psychometric/thermodynamic constants used in HRV design (must be memorized for KTU ESE):** > - **Specific heat of air (cₚ)** ≈ **1.005 kJ/kg·K** > - **Specific heat of water vapour (cₚ,v)** ≈ **1.88 kJ/kg·K** > - **Latent heat of vaporization (h_fg)** at 0 °C ≈ **2501 kJ/kg**; at typical indoor conditions ≈ **2450 kJ/kg** > - **Density of standard air (ρ)** ≈ **1.20 kg/m³** > - **Standard air-handling benchmarks**: ASHRAE 62.1 — minimum **8.5 L/s per person** (office); typical commercial ACH = **4–6**; hospital OTs = **20–25**. > - **High-efficiency HRV threshold**: εs ≥ **0.70** (AHRI 1060 certification minimum). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Ventilation Energy Goes to Waste In any air-conditioned building, three loads consume electricity: 1. **Transmission load** (heat through walls/roof) — can be reduced by insulation. 2. **Internal load** (occupants, lights, equipment) — can be reduced by efficient fixtures. 3. **Ventilation/Infiltration load** — *cannot* be eliminated (fresh air is a *legal/health requirement*), so the only way to reduce its energy cost is to **recover** part of the energy before exhausting it. **The "Why" behind HRV:** The exhaust air leaving a typical 22 °C, 50 % RH air-conditioned office carries roughly **40–55 kJ/kg of enthalpy**. Throwing it away means paying the chiller/heater to *re-cool/re-heat* an equivalent mass of incoming 32 °C, 70 % RH fresh air. Recovery of even 60 % of this enthalpy produces **massive chiller tonnage savings**. **The "How" — governing physical mechanism:** Heat transfer in an HRV is governed by the standard **conduction-convection equation**: $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$$ The novelty in HRV design is selecting the right *exchanger geometry* to maximize the **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** per unit fan-power consumed. ### 2.2 Classification of Ventilation Heat-Recovery Devices The KTU syllabus focuses on **five principal device types**. Each must be studied with its effectiveness range, pressure drop, cross-contamination risk, and ideal application. #### 2.2.1 Plate Heat Exchanger (Cross-flow / Counter-flow) - Two airstreams separated by **stationary metal plates** (aluminium or polymer). - Multiple parallel channels create a large surface area. - **Sensible only**, εs = **0.50–0.80**. - Zero cross-contamination. - Best for office HVAC, schools, hospitals. #### 2.2.2 Rotary Heat / Enthalpy Wheel - A slowly rotating cylinder (5–20 rpm) of honeycomb matrix. - Passes alternately through exhaust and supply airstreams. - **Sensible** version (sorption coating absent) → εs up to **0.85**. - **Enthalpy** version (silica gel or molecular sieve coated) → εs ≈ 0.70, εl ≈ 0.60. - Small cross-contamination (~1–5 % via purge sector). **Not recommended for hospital OTs or labs**. #### 2.2.3 Heat Pipe Heat Exchanger - Sealed tubes containing a **refrigerant** (typically R-134a, R-410A, or acetone). - One end (evaporator) in the warm airstream, the other (condenser) in the cold airstream. - Self-driven by capillary/two-phase thermosiphon action — **no moving parts**. - εs = **0.45–0.65**, ideal for handling **particulate-laden** exhausts. #### 2.2.4 Run-Around Coil System - Two finned coils (supply and exhaust side) connected by a **closed water-glycol loop** with a pump. - Suitable when the two airstreams are **physically far apart** (e.g., different floors of a hospital). - εs = **0.50–0.65**; εl very low (no moisture transfer). #### 2.2.5 Twin-Tower / Membrane ERV (Membrane Total Energy Exchanger) - Uses a **selectively permeable membrane** that passes water vapour but blocks air. - Both εs and εl achievable to **0.60–0.80**. - Excellent for humid tropical climates like Kerala. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Formula Table — Memorize Every Row for the ESE Numerical Part. Use \vert in place of absolute-value bars to keep the table intact.** | # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Legend | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\varepsilon_s = \dfrac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{3}-T_{1}}$ | $T_1$ = outside-air T entering; $T_2$ = supply-air T leaving HRV; $T_3$ = exhaust-air T entering HRV | °C or K | Sensible effectiveness | | 2 | $\varepsilon_l = \dfrac{h_{2}-h_{1}}{h_{3}-h_{1}}$ | $h$ = enthalpy of respective airstreams | kJ/kg | Latent / total effectiveness | | 3 | $Q_{rec} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{3}-T_{4})$ | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow of exhaust; $T_4$ = exhaust leaving HRV | kW | Recovered heat rate | | 4 | $Q_{max} = \dot{m}_{min} \cdot c_p \cdot \vert T_{hot,in} - T_{cold,in} \vert$ | minimum mass-flow between the two airstreams | kW | Maximum possible heat transfer | | 5 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{rec}}{Q_{max}}$ | — | — | General effectiveness | | 6 | $Q_{lat} = \dot{m} \cdot (W_{3}-W_{4}) \cdot h_{fg}$ | $W$ = humidity ratio; $h_{fg}$ ≈ 2450 kJ/kg | kW | Recovered latent heat rate | | 7 | $\dot{V}_{fan} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho}$ | $\rho \approx 1.20$ kg/m³ | m³/s | Volumetric fan flow | | 8 | $P_{fan} = \dfrac{\dot{V} \cdot \Delta P}{\eta_{fan}}$ | $\Delta P$ = HRV pressure drop; $\eta_{fan} \approx 0.55-0.70$ | W | Fan power for HRV | | 9 | $Annual Saving = Q_{rec} \cdot H_{op} \cdot R_e$ | $H_{op}$ = operating hours; $R_e$ = electricity rate ₹/kWh | ₹/year | Direct monetary savings | | 10 | $Simple Payback = \dfrac{C_{install}}{Annual Saving}$ | $C_{install}$ = installed cost of HRV | years | Investment appraisal | | 11 | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | temperature differences at the two ends | °C | LMTD for plate / rotary heat exchangers | | 12 | $NTU = \dfrac{U \cdot A}{(\dot{m} c_p)_{min}}$ | Number of Transfer Units | — | Heat-exchanger sizing | | 13 | $\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-NTU(1-C_r)}$ | counter-flow, equal specific heats; $C_r$ = capacity ratio | — | Effectiveness-NTU relation | | 14 | $COP_{sys,eq} = \dfrac{Q_{cool,avoided}}{W_{fan,added}}$ | — | — | Net thermodynamic benefit ratio | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Ventilation heat recovery is not a laboratory concept — it is one of the **most cost-effective energy retrofits** available globally. The **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency, India)** mandates in its *ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) 2017* and the newer **ECBC+ 2019** that large commercial buildings (>250 kVA connected load, or >1000 m² air-conditioned area) must include heat-recovery systems achieving **εs ≥ 0.5** on ventilation streams. The **ISO 16484**, **ASHRAE 90.1**, and **EN 16798** all corroborate these benchmarks. In Kerala, the *Indo-Swiss Building Energy Efficiency Project (BEEP)* has documented **30–42 % HVAC energy reduction** in retrofitted hospitals and IT parks using plate-type HRVs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industrial applications beyond buildings:** > - **Paint shops** (exhaust laden with solvent vapours — enthalpy wheels are the workhorse). > - **Cleanrooms** (semiconductor, pharma — membrane ERVs because contamination risk is non-negotiable). > - **Commercial kitchens** (heat-pipe HRVs reclaim both sensible and grease-bound latent heat). > - **Data centres** — run-around coil systems are popular because the exhaust and intake are physically distant. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sensible Temperature Effectiveness from First Principles **Statement:** Derive an expression for sensible heat-recovery effectiveness for an air-to-air heat exchanger given inlet/outlet temperatures of both airstreams. **Assumptions:** - Steady-state, no air leakage between streams. - No condensation/evaporation (sensible-only heat transfer). - Equal specific heat c_p on both sides. - No heat loss to surroundings. **Start:** The maximum possible heat that *could* be transferred is bounded by the smaller of the two streams cooling completely to the cold inlet: $$Q_{max} = (\dot{m} c_p)_{min} \cdot (T_{3} - T_{1})$$ **Step 1 — Actual heat recovered in the supply (cold) stream:** The cold stream enters at $T_1$ and leaves the exchanger at $T_2$, so: $$Q_{actual} = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} (T_2 - T_1)$$ **Step 2 — Define effectiveness:** By definition (energy balance basis): $$\varepsilon_s = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$\varepsilon_s = \frac{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c} (T_2 - T_1)}{(\dot{m} c_p)_{min} (T_3 - T_1)}$$ **Step 4 — Apply simplifying assumption** $\dot{m}_c c_{p,c} = (\dot{m} c_p)_{min}$ (balanced design, common case): $$\varepsilon_s = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_1}$$ This is the canonical **AHRI-1060** effectiveness expression. **Cross-verification using the hot stream:** $$\varepsilon_s = \frac{T_3 - T_4}{T_3 - T_1}$$ (used as an internal consistency check during commissioning). ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Heat Recovery for a Plate Heat Exchanger (LMTD Method) **Given:** A cross-flow plate HRV handles an exhaust airstream of $\dot{m}_h = 1.20$ kg/s entering at $T_3 = 26$ °C and leaving at $T_4 = 14$ °C. The supply airstream is $\dot{m}_c = 1.20$ kg/s entering at $T_1 = 32$ °C. Compute (a) the recovered heat rate, (b) sensible effectiveness, (c) supply-air outlet temperature. **Step 1 — Apply energy balance to the hot (exhaust) stream:** $$Q = \dot{m}_h c_p (T_3 - T_4)$$ $$Q = 1.20 \times 1.005 \times (26 - 14)$$ $$Q = 1.20 \times 1.005 \times 12$$ $$Q = 14.472 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2 — Energy balance on the cold (supply) stream:** $$Q = \dot{m}_c c_p (T_2 - T_1)$$ $$14.472 = 1.20 \times 1.005 \times (T_2 - 32)$$ $$T_2 - 32 = \frac{14.472}{1.206} = 12.0 \text{ °C}$$ $$T_2 = 44.0 \text{ °C}$$ **Step 3 — Sensible effectiveness:** $$\varepsilon_s = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_1} = \frac{44 - 32}{26 - 32} = \frac{12}{-6}$$ The negative sign indicates an *impossible* scenario — the supply cannot leave hotter than the exhaust inlet. The correct interpretation is that the inlet conditions are inconsistent (outside air should be cooler than exhaust in summer, hotter in winter). A revised problem with $T_1 = 32$ °C and $T_3 = 26$ °C is physically a *cooling* of exhaust to pre-cool the incoming fresh air — let us re-read the case as: outdoor 32 °C, indoor 26 °C exhaust. Hence supply should cool, not heat. We therefore swap the role: $T_1 = 32$ °C (outdoor, hot) entering the cold side and is being cooled; $T_3 = 26$ °C (indoor, cooler in summer due to AC) is now the hot stream being cooled further by the cold outdoor air... the correct topology in a tropical climate is: > **Outdoor air (hot, $T_1$) enters the HRV and is cooled by the cooler exhaust leaving the AC room.** Yes — let us re-state consistently: $T_1 = 32$ °C (outdoor entering supply side), $T_2$ = supply leaving HRV, $T_3 = 26$ °C (indoor exhaust entering HRV hot side), $T_4 = 14$ °C (exhaust leaving HRV). **Step 3 (corrected) — Sensible effectiveness for cooling case:** $$\varepsilon_s = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_3} = \frac{32 - 20}{32 - 26} = \frac{12}{6} = 2.0$$ Still impossible — effectiveness cannot exceed 1. This indicates that the given $\dot{m}_h = \dot{m}_c$ stream assumption breaks the energy balance when the heat transfer exceeds the smaller-capacity stream limit. The correct upper bound is $Q_{max} = \dot{m}_c c_p (T_1 - T_3) = 1.206 \times 6 = 7.236$ kW, half the value computed. Hence **a real HRV at 100 % effectiveness would recover only 7.236 kW**, meaning $T_2 = 32 - 7.236/1.206 = 26.0$ °C (matching $T_3$) and $T_4 = 26 - 7.236/1.206 = 20.0$ °C. With realistic $\varepsilon_s = 0.7$: $$Q_{actual} = 0.7 \times 7.236 = 5.065 \text{ kW}$$ $$T_2 = 32 - \frac{5.065}{1.206} = 27.80 \text{ °C}$$ $$T_4 = 26 - \frac{5.065}{1.206} = 21.80 \text{ °C}$$ The recovered heat (now reframed as *cooling-load avoided*) is 5.065 kW. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effectiveness-NTU Relation for Counter-Flow HRV **Start:** Differential energy balance over a control volume $dA$ of a counter-flow exchanger: $$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$ For both streams, $dQ = -\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} dT_h = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} dT_c$. **Step 1 — Integrate over the whole exchanger.** Using the standard $\varepsilon$-NTU derivation (Incropera, Fundamentals of Heat & Mass Transfer): $$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp\!\left[-NTU(1 - C_r)\right]}{1 - C_r \exp\!\left[-NTU(1 - C_r)\right]}$$ **Step 2 — For balanced streams** ($C_r = 1$): $$\varepsilon = \frac{NTU}{1 + NTU}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for required $A$** to achieve a target $\varepsilon = 0.75$ with $C_r = 0.85$ and $U = 35$ W/m²·K: $$0.75 = \frac{1 - e^{-NTU(0.15)}}{1 - 0.85 e^{-NTU(0.15)}}$$ Let $x = e^{-0.15\,NTU}$. Then $0.75 (1 - 0.85 x) = 1 - x$, hence: $$0.75 - 0.6375 x = 1 - x$$ $$x(1 - 0.6375) = 0.25$$ $$0.3625 x = 0.25 \Rightarrow x = 0.6897$$ $$NTU = -\frac{\ln(0.6897)}{0.15} = \frac{0.3716}{0.15} = 2.477$$ **Step 4 — Solve for heat-transfer area** with $\dot{m}_{min} c_p = 1.206$ kW/K = 1206 W/K: $$A = \frac{NTU \cdot (\dot{m} c_p)_{min}}{U} = \frac{2.477 \times 1206}{35} = 85.3 \text{ m}^2$$ This is the surface area a designer would specify for the plate pack. ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Economic Payback of an HRV Retrofit **Given:** HRV installed cost $C_{install}$ = ₹8,00,000; recovered heat $Q_{rec} = 30$ kW thermal; chiller COP = 3.0; electricity rate $R_e$ = ₹8/kWh; operating hours $H_{op}$ = 3000 h/year. **Step 1 — Equivalent electrical kW avoided** (assuming heat is offset against chiller load): $$P_{e,avoided} = \frac{Q_{rec}}{COP} = \frac{30}{3.0} = 10 \text{ kW}_e$$ **Step 2 — Annual monetary saving:** $$S = P_{e,avoided} \times H_{op} \times R_e = 10 \times 3000 \times 8 = \text{₹ } 2,40,000/\text{year}$$ **Step 3 — Simple payback:** $$\tau = \frac{C_{install}}{S} = \frac{8,00,000}{2,40,000} = 3.33 \text{ years}$$ **Step 4 — Net Present Value over 10 years** at discount rate $i = 10\%$: $$NPV = -C_{install} + S \times \frac{1-(1+i)^{-n}}{i} = -8,00,000 + 2,40,000 \times 6.144 = \text{₹ } 6,74,560$$ Strong positive NPV — economically viable retrofit. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Device Selection Cheat-Sheet | Selection Criterion | Plate HEX | Rotary Wheel | Heat Pipe | Run-Around | Membrane ERV | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Sensible effectiveness (εs) | 0.55–0.80 | 0.70–0.85 | 0.45–0.65 | 0.50–0.65 | 0.55–0.75 | | Latent effectiveness (εl) | 0 (none) | 0.50–0.70 (coated) | 0 (none) | 0 (none) | 0.50–0.75 | | Cross-contamination risk | **Zero** | Small (purge sector) | **Zero** | **Zero** | **Zero** | | Pressure drop (Pa) | 150–400 | 100–300 | 100–250 | 200–500 | 200–400 | | Frost risk in winter | Moderate | High | Moderate | Low | Low | | Best-fit climate | All | Temperate, dry | Industrial | Multi-zone | Humid tropical (Kerala) | | Capital cost (relative) | Low | Medium | Medium | High | High | ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define Sensible Heat Recovery and Latent Heat Recovery. How are they combined in an Energy Recovery Ventilator (ERV)?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Sensible Heat Recovery** is the transfer of *dry-bulb temperature energy* between two airstreams via a heat exchanger without any exchange of moisture. The governing metric is the **sensible effectiveness** $\varepsilon_s = (T_2 - T_1)/(T_3 - T_1)$, typically 0.50–0.85 for a plate or rotary HRV. **Latent Heat Recovery** is the transfer of *moisture (latent) energy* between airstreams, governed by the humidity ratio difference $\Delta W$ and the latent effectiveness $\varepsilon_l$. An **Energy Recovery Ventilator (ERV)** performs *both* sensible and latent exchange simultaneously — typically using a hygroscopic-coated rotary enthalpy wheel (silica-gel or molecular-sieve matrix) or a selectively-permeable membrane exchanger. ERVs are essential in **humid tropical climates** (Kerala, coastal India) because they pre-dehumidify the incoming fresh air, reducing the latent load on the cooling coil by up to 50 %. *(Examiner remark: 1 mark each for definition + ERV integration.)* --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **List any four types of ventilation heat-recovery devices. State one merit and one limitation of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Device | Merit | Limitation | |---|---|---| | Plate HEX | Zero cross-contamination | Sensible-only | | Rotary wheel | High εs (0.85) | Slight cross-leakage | | Heat pipe | No moving parts | Moderate effectiveness | | Run-around coil | Ducts can be remote | Pump adds parasitic power | | Membrane ERV | Both sensible + latent | High membrane cost | *(Examiner remark: 0.5 mark per merit/limitation pair; clean tabular answer is rewarded with full marks.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **KTU Examiner's Note (Valuation Warning):** > Both sub-parts (a) and (b) carry **7 marks each**. Sub-part (a) is usually a derivation/analytical question, and sub-part (b) is descriptive/comparative. Students frequently *lose 2–3 marks* by: > 1. **Forgetting to state the formula before substituting numerical values** — KTU examiners award a *separate method mark*. > 2. **Not mentioning the standard (AHRI/ASHRAE) reference** for effectiveness. > 3. **Skipping the energy-balance check** on both airstreams to verify the answer. > 4. **Confusing εs with εt** — a common silent error. --- #### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **(a)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(7 marks)** An air-conditioned office building has a ventilation flow rate of 5 m³/s. Outdoor air conditions are 35 °C DBT, 28 °C WBT; indoor exhaust conditions are 24 °C DBT, 18 °C WBT. A cross-flow plate heat-recovery ventilator with sensible effectiveness $\varepsilon_s = 0.65$ is installed. Determine: (i) The mass flow rate of supply and exhaust air. (ii) The supply-air temperature leaving the HRV. (iii) The sensible heat recovered in kW. (iv) The fan power added (assume fan efficiency 0.60, average HRV pressure drop 200 Pa). **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **(i) Mass flow rate** (using $\rho_{air} = 1.20$ kg/m³): $$\dot{m} = \rho \times \dot{V} = 1.20 \times 5 = 6.0 \text{ kg/s}$$ Both streams carry 6.0 kg/s (balanced design). **(ii) Supply-air temperature leaving HRV** using effectiveness: $$\varepsilon_s = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_3}$$ Here $T_1 = 35$ °C (outdoor entering HRV), $T_3 = 24$ °C (indoor exhaust entering HRV), and the hot stream is the *exhaust* that needs to release heat to the cool... actually, in a tropical cooling scenario, the outdoor air is *hotter* than the indoor exhaust, so the supply side heats the exhaust side. The supply air must be **cooled** by the cooler exhaust: $$\varepsilon_s = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_3} = 0.65$$ $$T_2 = T_1 - \varepsilon_s (T_1 - T_3) = 35 - 0.65 \times (35 - 24) = 35 - 0.65 \times 11 = 35 - 7.15$$ $$T_2 = 27.85 \text{ °C}$$ **(iii) Sensible heat recovered** (heat given up by supply air = heat gained by exhaust): $$Q_{rec} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_1 - T_2) = 6.0 \times 1.005 \times (35 - 27.85)$$ $$Q_{rec} = 6.03 \times 7.15 = 43.11 \text{ kW}$$ **(iv) Fan power** (two fans, each 200 Pa at 5 m³/s, efficiency 0.60): $$P_{fan} = \frac{\dot{V} \cdot \Delta P}{\eta_{fan}} = \frac{5 \times 200}{0.60} = 1666.67 \text{ W per fan}$$ Two fans → $P_{total} = 3.33$ kW. **Net thermodynamic benefit:** $$COP_{sys,eq} = \frac{43.11}{3.33} = 12.9$$ Excellent — the HRV returns ≈ 13 kWh of cooling benefit per kWh of fan electricity. *(Examiner remark: 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 2 marks for (iii), 1 mark for (iv).)* **(b)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(7 marks)** Compare **rotary enthalpy wheels, plate heat exchangers, and membrane ERVs** with respect to (i) effectiveness range, (ii) cross-contamination, (iii) suitability for tropical humid climates, (iv) maintenance and life. Recommend the best technology for a coastal hospital in Kerala. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Rotary Enthalpy Wheel | Plate HEX | Membrane ERV | |---|---|---|---| | (i) εs / εl | 0.70–0.85 / 0.50–0.70 | 0.55–0.80 / 0 | 0.55–0.75 / 0.50–0.75 | | (ii) Cross-contamination | Small (purge sector ~1–5 %) | Zero | Zero | | (iii) Tropical suitability | Moderate (frost risk low, but moisture adds load) | Good (sensible only) | **Excellent** (latent + sensible) | | (iv) Maintenance | High (motor, belt, purge) | Low (no moving parts) | Moderate (membrane fouling) | **Recommendation for coastal Kerala hospital:** A **membrane ERV** is preferred because: - It recovers **both sensible and latent** heat — vital for reducing the dehumidification load of coastal humid air. - **Zero cross-contamination** — non-negotiable for infection control in OTs and ICUs. - Although the membrane has moderate maintenance cost, the energy savings (30–40 % on HVAC) and regulatory compliance (ASHRAE 170, NABH standards) justify the premium. *(Examiner remark: 1.5 marks per parameter row + 1 mark for recommendation. Total 7.)* --- #### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(7 marks)** A pharmaceutical cleanroom has the following data: outside air 35 °C DBT, 60 % RH; supply air to room 22 °C DBT, 50 % RH; return air from room 24 °C DBT, 55 % RH; supply air mass flow $\dot{m} = 4.0$ kg/s. A membrane ERV with $\varepsilon_s = 0.70$ and $\varepsilon_l = 0.65$ is used. Calculate: (i) The temperature of supply air leaving the ERV (entering cooling coil). (ii) The humidity ratio of supply air leaving the ERV. (iii) The total recovered energy (sensible + latent) in kW. (iv) The annual energy cost saving if operating hours = 4000 h/year, chiller COP = 3.2, electricity rate ₹9/kWh. **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Given psychrometric data (use standard psychrometric chart relations):** - $W_{outdoor}$ at 35 °C, 60 % RH ≈ **0.0217 kg/kg** - $W_{return}$ at 24 °C, 55 % RH ≈ **0.0104 kg/kg** - $W_{supply,room}$ at 22 °C, 50 % RH ≈ **0.0082 kg/kg** **(i) Supply-air temperature after ERV:** $$T_2 = T_{outdoor} - \varepsilon_s (T_{outdoor} - T_{return}) = 35 - 0.70 \times (35 - 24) = 35 - 7.7 = 27.3 \text{ °C}$$ **(ii) Humidity ratio after ERV:** $$W_2 = W_{outdoor} - \varepsilon_l (W_{outdoor} - W_{return}) = 0.0217 - 0.65 \times (0.0217 - 0.0104)$$ $$W_2 = 0.0217 - 0.65 \times 0.0113 = 0.0217 - 0.00735 = 0.01435 \text{ kg/kg}$$ **(iii) Total recovered energy:** *Sensible portion:* $$Q_s = \dot{m} c_p (T_{outdoor} - T_2) = 4.0 \times 1.005 \times (35 - 27.3) = 4.02 \times 7.7 = 30.95 \text{ kW}$$ *Latent portion:* $$Q_l = \dot{m} (W_{outdoor} - W_2) h_{fg} = 4.0 \times (0.0217 - 0.01435) \times 2450$$ $$Q_l = 4.0 \times 0.00735 \times 2450 = 72.03 \text{ kW}$$ *Total recovered energy:* $$Q_{total} = Q_s + Q_l = 30.95 + 72.03 = 102.98 \text{ kW}$$ **(iv) Annual saving:** Equivalent electrical kW avoided (using chiller COP = 3.2): $$P_{e,avoided} = \frac{102.98}{3.2} = 32.18 \text{ kW}_e$$ Annual saving: $$S = 32.18 \times 4000 \times 9 = \text{₹ } 11,58,480/\text{year}$$ A very attractive economic proposition for a 4000 hr/yr cleanroom. *(Examiner remark: 2 marks each for (i)–(iii), 1 mark for (iv).)* **(b)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(7 marks)** Discuss the **role of run-around coil systems in multi-storey building heat recovery**. With a neat schematic, explain its principle, components, advantages, and limitations. Compare it with a plate HEX in terms of (a) cross-contamination, (b) effectiveness, (c) parasitic power. **Model Answer:** A **run-around coil system** is a *closed-loop* heat-recovery configuration in which the supply and exhaust airstreams are **physically separated** (no shared walls or rotating parts), connected only through a **pumped water-glycol loop**. Each airstream passes through a finned coil; the loop fluid is heated in the exhaust coil and releases that heat in the supply coil. **Schematic representation (text-based, KTU-acceptable):** ``` OUTSIDE AIR --> [SUPPLY COIL] --> [FILTER] --> [FAN] --> ROOM | v (Glycol loop, pumped) ^ ROOM EXHAUST --> [EXHAUST COIL] --> [FAN] --> DISCHARGE ``` **Components:** 1. Two finned heat-exchanger coils (one in each duct). 2. A **circulation pump** (with VFD for part-load control). 3. A **closed loop** of water + glycol (typically 30–40 % ethylene glycol to prevent freezing in cold climates). 4. **Expansion tank** and air-separation devices. 5. **Three-way valves** for capacity modulation. **Advantages:** - Ideal for **retrofit** scenarios where supply and exhaust ducts are *on different floors or wings*. - **Zero cross-contamination** (no shared surface, only the fluid loop contacts both streams). - Easily **modulated** by varying pump speed or glycol flow. **Limitations:** - Pump power is parasitic — typically 0.3–0.5 kW for every 50 kW thermal. - Effectiveness lower than rotary or plate (0.50–0.65). - Glycol maintenance, corrosion inhibitors required. **Comparison with plate HEX:** | Parameter | Plate HEX | Run-Around Coil | |---|---|---| | (a) Cross-contamination | Zero | Zero | | (b) Effectiveness | 0.55–0.80 | 0.50–0.65 | | (c) Parasitic power | Zero (only fans) | Pump adds 2–4 % of recovered heat | **Conclusion:** Plate HEX is preferred when streams are co-located; run-around coil wins when physical separation or **multi-zone recovery** is needed (e.g., hospitals, IT parks). *(Examiner remark: 2 marks schematic + principle, 2 marks advantages/limitations, 3 marks comparison table.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Marks Are Lost:** > 1. **Formula before substitution**: Always write the *generic* formula first, then plug values. Skipping this loses the 0.5–1 "method mark" that examiners are instructed to award separately. > 2. **Sign convention in effectiveness**: Many students confuse whether $T_1$ is outside-air or supply-air temperature. **Always define your notation clearly** before writing $\varepsilon_s$. > 3. **Forgetting the fan/pump parasitic power**: An HRV that recovers 40 kW but consumes 5 kW in fan power has a *net* benefit of 35 kW. Examiners love to test this by asking for the *net* COP. > 4. **Confusing sensible-only with total**: Plate HEX and heat pipe are *sensible-only*. Rotary wheel and membrane are *total*. Mentioning this in the first sentence of a descriptive answer scores easy marks. > 5. **Mixing up εs and εl**: $\varepsilon_s$ uses **temperature**, $\varepsilon_l$ uses **humidity ratio**. Drawing a quick psychrometric sketch before solving avoids this trap. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Definition (1 line):** HRV is the recovery of heat (and optionally moisture) from exhaust ventilation air before it is discharged. - [ ] **Two-stream non-contact principle** — fresh air and exhaust never mix; heat crosses via conduction/convection. - [ ] **Master formula:** $\varepsilon_s = (T_2 - T_1) / (T_3 - T_1)$ — memorize the *sign convention* (T₁ outside, T₂ supply leaving, T₃ exhaust entering). - [ ] **Five device families:** Plate, Rotary Wheel, Heat Pipe, Run-Around Coil, Membrane ERV — each with one signature strength (zero contamination, high εs, no moving parts, duct-flexibility, latent+sensible). - [ ] **HRV vs ERV:** HRV → sensible only. ERV → sensible **+ latent** (essential for Kerala's humid climate). - [ ] **Effectiveness benchmarks (AHRI 1060):** Plate 0.55–0.80; Rotary 0.70–0.85 (s), 0.50–0.70 (l); Membrane 0.55–0.75 (both); Heat Pipe 0.45–0.65; Run-around 0.50–0.65. - [ ] **Energy recovered:** $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ (sensible) and $Q_l = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta W \cdot h_{fg}$ (latent, $h_{fg} \approx 2450$ kJ/kg). - [ ] **Constant values to memorize:** $c_{p,air} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K; $\rho_{air} = 1.20$ kg/m³; $h_{fg} = 2450$ kJ/kg. - [ ] **NTU relation (counter-flow, equal c_p):** $\varepsilon = NTU / (1 + NTU)$. - [ ] **Economic metric:** Payback (years) = Capital Cost / Annual Saving. Always compute **net** saving after deducting fan/pump parasitic power. - [ ] **Applications:** Commercial AC, hospitals, cleanrooms, paint shops, kitchens, data centres. - [ ] **Standards governing practice:** AHRI 1060, ASHRAE 90.1, ASHRAE 62.1, EN 16798, BEE ECBC+ (India). - [ ] **Kerala-specific edge:** Latent recovery dominates — **ERV over HRV** is the textbook answer for hot-humid climates. - [ ] **Formula-derivation drill:** Be ready to derive $\varepsilon_s$ from the energy-balance definition in 90 seconds flat.

topics.title: Air Co-generation of Heat and Electricity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Air Co-generation of Heat and Electricity ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal KTU Definition **Air Co-generation of Heat and Electricity** refers to a Combined Heat and Power (CHP) configuration in which **air** (or the products of combustion — essentially air-dominated hot gases) acts as the **primary thermodynamic working fluid**. The system simultaneously generates useful electrical/mechanical power and useful thermal energy (steam, hot water, or process heat) in a single integrated plant, achieving far higher total fuel utilization than conventional separate generation. In engineering practice, an "air-based" cogeneration system is realized principally through the **open-cycle gas turbine (Brayton cycle)** topping arrangement, where atmospheric air is drawn in, compressed, heated by fuel combustion, expanded through a turbine to produce shaft work, and the **hot exhaust gases** (typically at **450–550 °C**) are routed through a **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)** or waste-heat boiler to produce process steam or hot water. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 4 (Air Cogeneration):** > The candidate must be able to identify air-standard cogeneration topologies, evaluate **Energy Utilization Factor (EUF)**, derive **Heat-to-Power Ratio (HPR)**, and analyse gas-turbine-based topping cycles with exhaust heat recovery. ### Types of Air-Based Cogeneration Configurations | Configuration | Working Fluid | Power Element | Heat Element | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | Open-cycle Gas Turbine + HRSG (Topping) | Air / Flue gas | Gas Turbine | HRSG | Industry, District Heating | | Closed-cycle Gas Turbine + Recuperator | Air (closed loop) | Gas Turbine | Recuperator/External Cooler | Nuclear, Solar | | Diesel Engine + Heat Recovery (Topping) | Air | Diesel Engine | Jacket + Exhaust boiler | Small industry, hospitals | | Air-bottoming Combined Cycle | Air | Steam Turbine | Gas Turbine exhaust | Large utility plants | ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Products-from-One-Fire" Principle Imagine lighting a single gas stove. In a normal kitchen, you use the flame *only* to cook (heat). In a clever kitchen, you would also place a small spinning wheel (a micro-turbine) above the flame that generates electricity *while* the flame still cooks your food. **Nothing about the fire changed** — you simply extracted two useful outputs (heat + shaft work) from the same fuel. The "air" in this analogy is the *oxygen* feeding the flame: the working fluid that carries the energy. In an air-cogeneration plant, atmospheric air is the energy carrier — it absorbs fuel energy in a combustor, drives a turbine, and then surrenders its remaining thermal energy to a heat-recovery unit. **One fuel → two products (electricity + process heat)**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Metrics to Memorise:** > - **EUF (Energy Utilization Factor)** = dimensionless ratio between (0, 1] > - **HPR (Heat-to-Power Ratio)** = dimensionless, design-matched to consumer > - **η_th (Thermal Efficiency)** = W_net / Q_fuel > - **η_total (Total / Overall Efficiency)** = (W_net + Q_useful) / Q_fuel > - **Pressure ratio (r_p)** typically 6–16 for industrial GT-CHP ### Why Air? - **Inexhaustible and free** of cost as the working fluid (open cycle). - **Non-toxic, non-flammable**, simplifying safety management. - **High specific heat (~1.005 kJ/kg·K)** allows compact heat exchangers. - **Operates at atmospheric pressure** at inlet — no closed-loop pressurisation cost. - Readily **preheated** by compression (regenerative cycle option). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Topping-Cycle Logic (Step-by-Step) Air-based cogeneration almost always operates in the **topping** mode because gaseous working fluids reach their **highest temperature in the prime mover (turbine)**, after which useful heat can be extracted from the exhaust. **Step 1 — Air Intake & Compression (State 1 → 2):** Atmospheric air at ambient (T₁ ≈ 298 K, P₁ ≈ 1 bar) is drawn into the **axial/centrifugal compressor** and pressurised isentropically (ideal) or polytropically (actual). The compressor consumes a large fraction of the turbine's gross output. $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\,(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ **Step 2 — Combustion / Heat Addition (State 2 → 3):** Fuel is burnt in the **combustion chamber** at (near-)constant pressure. The combustion products — still essentially air-dominated — are heated to the **Turbine Inlet Temperature (TIT)**, T₃, typically **1200–1500 K** in modern industrial machines. **Step 3 — Expansion & Power Production (State 3 → 4):** Hot gases expand through the **gas turbine**, producing shaft work. Expansion is isentropic (ideal) at constant pressure. $$T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{r_p}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ **Step 4 — Heat Recovery (State 4 → 5):** The exhaust, still at high temperature (≈ 773 K), passes through an **HRSG**, where its heat is transferred to a secondary water/steam loop. The exhaust is then released to the atmosphere (open cycle) at T₅ (≈ 423–450 K). > [!NOTE] > **Why "Topping"?** Because the **highest-grade energy (electricity) is produced first**, and the **lower-grade thermal energy is recovered from the waste stream**. This is the *opposite* of a "bottoming" cycle (used in certain steel/cement plants, where a gas turbine receives waste heat from a prior high-temperature industrial process). ### 2.2 Performance Metrics — The "Trinity" of Cogeneration Evaluation **A) Electrical (Thermal) Efficiency:** $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{fuel}} = \frac{W_T - W_C}{Q_{fuel}}$$ **B) Heat Recovery Efficiency (Boiler Thermal Efficiency):** $$\eta_{HR} = \frac{Q_{useful}}{m_{exh} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_4 - T_5)}$$ **C) Energy Utilization Factor (EUF) — THE Master Metric:** $$\boxed{\text{EUF} = \frac{W_{net} + Q_{useful}}{Q_{fuel}} = \eta_{th} + \eta_{HR}\cdot(1-\eta_{th})}$$ This is the **single most important equation** in KTU cogeneration problems. **D) Heat-to-Power Ratio (HPR):** $$\boxed{\text{HPR} = \frac{Q_{useful}}{W_{net}}}$$ HPR is the *design handshake* between the plant and the consumer — process industries with high heat demand (e.g., paper mills) need HPR ≈ 2–5; electricity-dominant users (e.g., data centres) need HPR ≈ 0.5–1.5. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Variables / Meaning | Typical Units | Validity / Note | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $W_C = m_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$ | Compressor work | kW | Open cycle, ideal gas | | 2 | $W_T = m_g \cdot c_{p,g} \cdot (T_3 - T_4)$ | Turbine work | kW | m_g ≈ m_a + m_fuel | | 3 | $W_{net} = W_T - W_C$ | Net shaft work | kW | Drives generator | | 4 | $Q_{fuel} = m_f \cdot \text{LHV}$ | Fuel energy input | kW | LHV = Lower Heating Value | | 5 | $Q_{useful} = m_g \cdot c_{p,g} \cdot (T_4 - T_5)$ | Recovered heat | kW | Up to HRSG exit | | 6 | $\eta_{th} = W_{net}/Q_{fuel}$ | Thermal efficiency | — | Typical 25–38% | | 7 | $\text{EUF} = (W_{net}+Q_{useful})/Q_{fuel}$ | Energy utilisation | — | Typical 70–85% | | 8 | $\text{HPR} = Q_{useful}/W_{net}$ | Heat to Power | — | Design parameter | | 9 | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | Compressor exit T | K | γ_air = 1.4 | | 10 | $T_4 = T_3 \cdot (1/r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | Turbine exit T | K | Ideal Brayton | | 11 | $r_p = P_2/P_1$ | Pressure ratio | — | 6–16 industrial | | 12 | $\eta_{Brayton} = 1 - (1/r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | Ideal Brayton η | — | Cold air-standard | | 13 | $\dot{m}_a = \rho_a \cdot \dot{V}_a$ | Air mass flow | kg/s | ρ ≈ 1.2 kg/m³ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic (KTU Examiners Love This):** *"WFHQ-E-H"* → **W**ork, **F**uel, **H**eat, **Q**, then **E**UF, then **H**PR. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **District Heating Networks (Copenhagen, Vienna):** Gas-turbine CHP with HRSG supplies 50–60% of city heat + electricity. - **Refineries & Petrochemical Plants:** GT-CHP provides process steam at 4–40 bar, eliminating separate boilers. - **Hospitals / Hotels (Diesel-CHP):** Compact, reliable, with low exhaust emissions after heat recovery. - **Captive Power in Sugar/Textile Mills:** Bagasse/biomass-fired topping cycle with exhaust heat recovery. - **Combined-Cycle Cogeneration:** A *second* steam-turbine bottoming layer is added to GT-CHP, pushing EUF above 85%. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Energy Utilization Factor (EUF) for an Air-Based Topping CHP **Starting Premise:** In a topping-cycle gas-turbine cogeneration system, fuel energy $Q_{fuel}$ is supplied at the combustor. Part of it becomes net shaft work $W_{net}$, and the remainder leaves the turbine in the form of exhaust enthalpy. A portion of the exhaust enthalpy is captured as $Q_{useful}$ in the HRSG, and the residual is rejected to the atmosphere as $Q_{rej}$. **Step 1 — Energy Balance on the Combustor:** $$Q_{fuel} = W_{net} + Q_{exhaust}$$ *Logic:* By the First Law, all the fuel's chemical energy must exit as net work (output) plus the hot exhaust stream (which still carries thermal energy to be recovered). **Step 2 — Decompose the Exhaust Stream:** $$Q_{exhaust} = Q_{useful} + Q_{rej}$$ *Logic:* The exhaust heat is partitioned into the *useful* portion captured by the HRSG and the *unrecovered* portion vented to the stack. **Step 3 — Substituting Step 2 into Step 1:** $$Q_{fuel} = W_{net} + Q_{useful} + Q_{rej}$$ **Step 4 — Define EUF as the ratio of *all* useful outputs to fuel input:** $$\text{EUF} = \frac{W_{net} + Q_{useful}}{Q_{fuel}}$$ **Step 5 — Rewrite in terms of thermal efficiency:** Let $\eta_{th} = W_{net}/Q_{fuel}$. Then $W_{net} = \eta_{th}\cdot Q_{fuel}$ and $Q_{rej} = Q_{fuel}(1-\eta_{th})$. If the heat-recovery efficiency of the HRSG is $\eta_{HR}$, then: $$Q_{useful} = \eta_{HR}\cdot Q_{rej} = \eta_{HR}\cdot Q_{fuel}\cdot(1-\eta_{th})$$ **Step 6 — Final Form:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{EUF} &= \frac{\eta_{th}\cdot Q_{fuel} + \eta_{HR}\cdot Q_{fuel}\cdot(1-\eta_{th})}{Q_{fuel}} \\ &= \eta_{th} + \eta_{HR}\cdot(1-\eta_{th}) \\ &= \eta_{th}(1-\eta_{HR}) + \eta_{HR} \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Insight for KTU Exam:** When $\eta_{HR} = 1$ (perfect heat recovery, i.e. exhaust cooled to ambient), then **EUF = 1 (100%)**. When $\eta_{HR} = 0$ (no heat recovery, i.e. a plain power plant), then **EUF = η_th**, as expected. This is the *proof of correctness* the examiner expects. --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Pattern) **Problem Statement:** A topping-cycle air-cogeneration plant has the following data: - Air mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_a = 25$ kg/s - Compressor inlet: $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_1 = 1$ bar - Pressure ratio: $r_p = 8$ - Turbine inlet temperature: $T_3 = 1300$ K - HRSG exit temperature: $T_5 = 450$ K - Fuel: Natural gas, LHV = 50 MJ/kg, mass flow = 0.45 kg/s - $c_{p,air} = c_{p,gas} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$ **Find:** (a) Net power output and thermal efficiency. (b) Useful heat output, HPR, and EUF. **Step 1 — Compressor Exit Temperature:** $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \cdot 8^{0.4/1.4} = 300 \cdot 8^{0.2857} = 300 \cdot 1.8114 \approx 543.4 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2 — Turbine Exit Temperature (ideal Brayton):** $$T_4 = T_3 \cdot (1/r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 1300 \cdot (1/8)^{0.2857} = 1300 / 1.8114 \approx 717.6 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3 — Compressor Work:** $$W_C = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1) = 25 \cdot 1.005 \cdot (543.4 - 300) = 25 \cdot 1.005 \cdot 243.4 \approx 6115 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4 — Fuel Mass Flow Contribution to Combustion Products:** $$\dot{m}_g = \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f = 25 + 0.45 = 25.45 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 5 — Turbine Work:** $$W_T = \dot{m}_g \cdot c_p \cdot (T_3 - T_4) = 25.45 \cdot 1.005 \cdot (1300 - 717.6) = 25.45 \cdot 1.005 \cdot 582.4 \approx 14\,900 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 6 — Net Power Output:** $$W_{net} = W_T - W_C = 14\,900 - 6115 = 8785 \text{ kW} \approx 8.79 \text{ MW}$$ **Step 7 — Fuel Energy Input:** $$Q_{fuel} = \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV} = 0.45 \cdot 50\,000 = 22\,500 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 8 — Thermal Efficiency:** $$\eta_{th} = W_{net}/Q_{fuel} = 8785/22\,500 = 0.3904 = 39.04\%$$ **Step 9 — Useful Heat Output (from exhaust cooling, T₄ → T₅):** $$Q_{useful} = \dot{m}_g \cdot c_p \cdot (T_4 - T_5) = 25.45 \cdot 1.005 \cdot (717.6 - 450) = 25.45 \cdot 1.005 \cdot 267.6 \approx 6845 \text{ kW} \approx 6.85 \text{ MW}$$ **Step 10 — Heat-to-Power Ratio:** $$\text{HPR} = Q_{useful}/W_{net} = 6845/8785 = 0.779$$ **Step 11 — Energy Utilization Factor:** $$\text{EUF} = (W_{net} + Q_{useful})/Q_{fuel} = (8785 + 6845)/22\,500 = 15\,630/22\,500 = 0.6947 = 69.5\%$$ **Step 12 — Verification Using the Compact Form:** Using $\eta_{HR}$: $Q_{rej}$ (per unit fuel) $= Q_{fuel}(1-\eta_{th}) = 22\,500 \cdot 0.6096 = 13\,716$ kW. $Q_{useful}$ (computed directly) = 6845 kW. Hence $\eta_{HR} = 6845/13\,716 = 0.499$. $$\text{EUF (check)} = \eta_{th} + \eta_{HR}(1-\eta_{th}) = 0.3904 + 0.499 \cdot 0.6096 = 0.3904 + 0.3042 = 0.6946 \;\checkmark$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** A well-designed GT-CHP with HRSG typically reaches **EUF 65–80%**. Values above 80% imply a combined-cycle (steam bottoming) addition. The 69.5% here is realistic for a single-shaft industrial unit. ### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix | Metric | Symbol | Computed Value | KTU Interpretation | |---|---|---|---| | Net Power | $W_{net}$ | 8.79 MW | Industrial-scale captive generation | | Useful Heat | $Q_{useful}$ | 6.85 MW | Steam at ~10 bar, process-grade | | Thermal Eff. | $\eta_{th}$ | 39.0% | Higher than simple Brayton (≈33%) due to TIT | | HPR | $Q/W$ | 0.78 | Slightly power-dominant plant | | EUF | $(W+Q)/Q_f$ | 69.5% | Strong combined utilization | | Heat-Rec. Eff. | $\eta_{HR}$ | 49.9% | Limited by HRSG pinch point (T₅=450 K) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Air-Based Topping-Cycle Cogeneration The following Mermaid block diagram represents the full material/energy flow of an open-cycle gas-turbine cogeneration plant with HRSG, including the air as working fluid and the secondary water-steam loop. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Atmospheric Air
T1 = 300 K, P1 = 1 bar] --> A2[AXIAL COMPRESSOR
W_C consumption
r_p = 8] A2 --> A3[Compressed Air
T2 = 543 K, P2 = 8 bar] A3 --> A4[COMBUSTION CHAMBER
Fuel Injection
Natural Gas LHV 50 MJ/kg] F1[Natural Gas Fuel
m_f = 0.45 kg/s] --> A4 A4 --> A5[Hot Combustion Gases
T3 = 1300 K, TIT] A5 --> A6[GAS TURBINE
W_T generation
Iso expansion] A6 --> A7[Turbine Exhaust
T4 = 718 K] A6 --> G1[Synchronous Generator

Heat Exchanger Network] W1[Feed Water
