Rejection] classDef air fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef thermal fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef power fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6,A7 air class B1,B2,B3 thermal class G1 power class F1,W1,A8 output ``` ### 4.2 Energy-Flow Sankey Description (Textual) The same architecture may be summarised as a Sankey-style *mental* picture (since Mermaid v10 limits Sankey charts): | Stream | Energy (kW) | Share of Fuel | Fate | |---|---|---|---| | Fuel Input ($Q_{fuel}$) | 22,500 | 100.00% | Source | | Net Power ($W_{net}$) | 8,785 | 39.04% | → Grid / Mechanical load | | Useful Heat ($Q_{useful}$) | 6,845 | 30.42% | → Process steam | | Stack Rejection ($Q_{rej}$) | 6,870 | 30.54% | → Atmosphere (loss) | > [!NOTE] > **Key Reading from the Sankey:** Only ~30% of the fuel's energy is *truly wasted* in the air-based CHP, compared to ~60–65% in a conventional condensing power plant — that is the entire economic and environmental case for air-based cogeneration. ### 4.3 T–s Diagram (Schematic Description) The ideal Brayton cogeneration cycle on a **Temperature–entropy (T–s)** diagram looks like a *dome-shaped* open loop: - **Process 1 → 2:** Isentropic compression (vertical line upward) - **Process 2 → 3:** Constant-pressure heat addition (line curving up to peak at TIT) - **Process 3 → 4:** Isentropic expansion in turbine (vertical line downward) - **Process 4 → 5:** Constant-pressure heat *rejection* to HRSG (line sloping down to T₅) - The **enclosed area (1-2-3-4)** represents net work; the **area (4-5-a-b)** is recoverable useful heat. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** Define **Energy Utilization Factor (EUF)** for a cogeneration system. Why is it considered a more meaningful indicator than thermal efficiency alone? **Model Answer:** EUF is the ratio of the sum of net useful power output and net useful heat output to the total fuel energy input: $$\text{EUF} = \frac{W_{net} + Q_{useful}}{Q_{fuel}}$$ It is dimensionless and lies between $\eta_{th}$ (when no heat is recovered) and 1.0 (when all exhaust heat is recovered). It is more meaningful than thermal efficiency because cogeneration plants produce *two* products; a plant that "wastes" 60% of its fuel as heat can still achieve 80%+ EUF if that heat is productively used. Thermal efficiency ignores this second revenue stream, so it under-credits the plant. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Distinguish between a **topping cycle** and a **bottoming cycle** in cogeneration. State one industrial application of each in which air is the working fluid. **Model Answer:** | Feature | Topping Cycle | Bottoming Cycle | |---|---|---| | Sequence | Power produced *first*, then heat | Heat supplied *first*, then power | | Prime Mover | Gas turbine, Diesel, Engine | Steam turbine, ORC | | Working Fluid | Air (gas turbine), Combustion gas | Steam, Organic fluid | | Typical Air-Based App | Gas-turbine CHP with HRSG in a paper mill | Air-bottoming combined cycle (less common); regenerative air turbines in solar/thermal plants | --- ### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **Choose ONE of the following two questions:** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]** **(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, describe the working of an **air-based open-cycle gas turbine cogeneration plant with a heat recovery steam generator (HRSG)**. Clearly mark the temperature and pressure at each state and identify the air-side and water-side loops. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cogeneration plant supplies **3000 kW of electrical power** and **4200 kW of process heat** from natural gas having an LHV of 50 MJ/kg. The plant operates for **6000 hours/year**. Calculate: - (i) The annual fuel consumption in tonnes. **(2 marks)** - (ii) The thermal efficiency, EUF, and Heat-to-Power Ratio. **(3 marks)** - (iii) The annual fuel-cost saving (in ₹) if the plant displaces grid electricity bought at ₹7/kWh and furnace-oil heat at ₹3/kWh, assuming the same energy content. **(2 marks)** **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** **(i) Fuel Energy Rate:** $Q_{fuel} = W_{net} + Q_{useful} = 3000 + 4200 = 7200$ kW. $$\dot{m}_f = \frac{Q_{fuel}}{\text{LHV}} = \frac{7200}{50\,000} = 0.144 \text{ kg/s}$$ Annual fuel: $0.144 \cdot 6000 \cdot 3600 / 1000 = 3110.4$ tonnes/year. **(ii) Performance metrics:** - $\eta_{th} = 3000/7200 = 0.4167 = 41.67\%$ - $\text{EUF} = 7200/7200 = 1.0 \;(\text{implied idealisation};\;\text{otherwise} < 1)$ - $\text{HPR} = 4200/3000 = 1.4$ **(iii) Cost saving (assuming the alternative is separate generation):** - Electricity value: $3000 \cdot 6000 \cdot 7 = \text{₹}\,12.6 \text{ crore}$ - Heat value: $4200 \cdot 6000 \cdot 3 = \text{₹}\,7.56 \text{ crore}$ - **Total annual benefit ≈ ₹ 20.16 crore** (offset by fuel cost). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **Energy Utilization Factor** of an air-based topping-cycle cogeneration plant in terms of the thermal efficiency $\eta_{th}$ and the heat-recovery efficiency $\eta_{HR}$. State the limiting values and their physical meaning. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** Starting from $Q_{fuel} = W_{net} + Q_{useful} + Q_{rej}$ and the definitions $\eta_{th} = W_{net}/Q_{fuel}$, $\eta_{HR} = Q_{useful}/Q_{rej}$: $$\begin{aligned} Q_{rej} &= Q_{fuel}(1-\eta_{th}) \\ Q_{useful} &= \eta_{HR}\,Q_{rej} = \eta_{HR}\,Q_{fuel}(1-\eta_{th}) \\ \text{EUF} &= \frac{W_{net}+Q_{useful}}{Q_{fuel}} = \eta_{th} + \eta_{HR}(1-\eta_{th}) \end{aligned}$$ **Limiting values:** - $\eta_{HR}=0$ (no heat recovery, plain power plant): $\text{EUF}=\eta_{th}$. - $\eta_{HR}=1$ (perfect heat recovery): $\text{EUF}=1$ (100%). - For a real CHP with $\eta_{th}=0.35$, $\eta_{HR}=0.55$: $\text{EUF}=0.35+0.55(0.65)=0.7075$, i.e. **~70.8%**. **(b)** A gas-turbine CHP has the following data: $\dot{m}_a = 20$ kg/s, $T_1 = 300$ K, $r_p = 10$, $T_3 = 1250$ K, $T_5 = 430$ K, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$, fuel $\dot{m}_f = 0.40$ kg/s, LHV = 48 MJ/kg. Determine: **(i)** Net power output, **(ii)** Useful heat output, **(iii)** Thermal efficiency, **(iv)** EUF, **(v)** HPR. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** - $T_2 = 300\cdot 10^{0.2857} = 300\cdot 1.9307 = 579.2$ K - $T_4 = 1250/1.9307 = 647.4$ K - $W_C = 20\cdot 1.005\cdot(579.2-300) = 5610$ kW - $\dot{m}_g = 20.40$ kg/s - $W_T = 20.40\cdot 1.005\cdot(1250-647.4) = 20.40\cdot 1.005\cdot 602.6 = 12\,354$ kW - **(i)** $W_{net} = 12\,354 - 5610 = 6744$ kW - $Q_{fuel} = 0.40\cdot 48\,000 = 19\,200$ kW - **(iii)** $\eta_{th} = 6744/19\,200 = 35.13\%$ - **(ii)** $Q_{useful} = 20.40\cdot 1.005\cdot(647.4-430) = 4457$ kW - **(iv)** $\text{EUF} = (6744+4457)/19\,200 = 11\,201/19\,200 = 58.34\%$ - **(v)** $\text{HPR} = 4457/6744 = 0.661$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Air Cogeneration Problems:** > 1. **Fuel mass flow in turbine work:** Many students use $\dot{m}_a$ (air only) for $W_T$. The expansion mass flow is $\dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f$ (fuel adds mass to the gas). **Loses 1–2 marks.** > 2. **Specific heat confusion:** Use $c_p \approx 1.005$ kJ/kg·K consistently for *both* compressor and turbine if fuel mass is treated separately, OR use higher $c_{p,g}\approx 1.15$ for *combustion products* if fuel mass is lumped in. > 3. **EUF denominator:** Must be $Q_{fuel}$ (LHV-based), *not* $W_{net} + Q_{useful}$ used in a circular self-reference. > 4. **HRSG exit temperature:** T₅ is *above* the dew point (~363 K for water) to avoid acid corrosion; using T₅ = 300 K (ambient) is unrealistic and yields over-optimistic EUF. > 5. **Forgetting units:** Always state kW, kg/s, K; converting LHV in MJ/kg to kJ/kg is a common slip (×1000). --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Air-based cogeneration = Gas-turbine CHP.** Air is the working fluid; products of combustion expand the turbine and the hot exhaust feeds an HRSG. - **Topping cycle** (power first, then heat) dominates air-based systems. **Bottoming cycle** is more typical of process industries with very-high-temperature furnaces. - **EUF = $\eta_{th} + \eta_{HR}(1-\eta_{th})$** is the single most-tested formula. EUF ranges **0.60–0.85** for typical GT-CHP, up to **0.90+** for combined-cycle cogeneration. - **HPR = $Q_{useful}/W_{net}$** is the design matching parameter. Industrial process plants: HPR ≈ 1.5–5; power-led plants: HPR ≈ 0.3–1.0. - **Turbine mass flow = $\dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f$** (combustion adds mass). Forgetting this underestimates work. - **Compressor work:** $W_C = \dot{m}_a c_p(T_2-T_1)$ with $T_2 = T_1 r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. - **Turbine exit temp:** $T_4 = T_3(1/r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$; this is the *source* of recoverable heat. - **Useful heat:** $Q_{useful} = \dot{m}_g c_p (T_4 - T_5)$; choosing $T_5$ realistically (≥ 423 K) is critical. - **Energy-saving logic:** Conventional power plant wastes 60–65% of fuel; air-CHP with HRSG drops losses to 15–30%, *more than doubling* effective fuel utilisation. - **Practical design levers:** (1) raise TIT (T₃) for higher $\eta_{th}$; (2) lower T₅ (limited by HRSG acid dew point); (3) optimise $r_p$ ≈ 8–12 for industrial GT-CHP; (4) add a steam bottoming cycle to push EUF > 85%. - **Sankey reading test:** In an air-CHP plant, always confirm that $W_{net} + Q_{useful} + Q_{rej} = Q_{fuel}$ before computing EUF — this catches computational errors instantly. - **KTU-recurring keywords:** "topping cycle", "Energy Utilization Factor", "Heat-to-Power Ratio", "HRSG", "open-cycle gas turbine", "Brayton cycle", "EUF", "process heat".

topics.title: Heat Recovery and Bottoming Cycles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT741 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Heat Recovery and Bottoming Cycles # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Heat recovery is the collection and reuse of thermal energy that would otherwise be lost as waste to the environment, while a **bottoming cycle** is a power-generation thermodynamic cycle that uses this rejected low-to-medium-grade waste heat as its primary energy input to produce useful work (typically electricity). --- ## 1.1 The Industrial Energy Problem In any thermal industrial process (boilers, furnaces, kilns, engines, incinerators), a substantial fraction of the input fuel energy is rejected as **waste heat** through stack gases, hot products, cooling water, and surface radiation losses. KTU 2024 expects students to recognize that **35 % to 60 %** of input energy is routinely discarded in Indian process industries. > [!NOTE] > **Key Reference Temperature:** All heat-recovery feasibility is benchmarked against **25 °C = 298 K** (Indian Standard Ambient Temperature for energy audits per BEE/IGBC norms). --- ## 1.2 The Two Logical Families of Heat Utilization | Cycle Family | Direction of Energy Flow | Typical Use | KTU Weightage | |--------------|--------------------------|-------------|---------------| | **Topping Cycle** | Fuel → High-grade Work → Low-grade Heat | Conventional power plants, gas turbines | Context only | | **Bottoming Cycle** | Process Heat → Useful Work (from "waste") | Waste heat recovery, ORC, Kalina | **High (Module 4 Core)** | --- ## 1.3 Real-World Analogy (Intuition Builder) Imagine you finish boiling pasta on the stove. The **hot water and steam** rising from the pot is energy you paid for but didn't use. Now imagine installing a small turbine on the pot's lid connected to a fan — that fan spinning is a **bottoming cycle** extracting useful work from "waste" heat. The pasta was your primary process; the fan is your recovered energy. In industry, the **furnace is the pasta pot**, and the **ORC turbine** is the fan — converting otherwise-wasted stack gas enthalpy into electricity. > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook:** Topping = "Top priority" (high-grade heat first, work next). Bottoming = "Bottom of the barrel" (you take whatever is left and still squeeze out work). --- ## 1.4 Sources of Waste Heat in Industries (KTU Must-Know List) 1. **High-temperature flue gases** (500 °C – 1000 °C) from boilers, furnaces, kilns. 2. **Exhaust gases** from internal combustion engines, gas turbines (300 °C – 600 °C). 3. **Hot product streams** in cement, steel, glass, chemicals. 4. **Hot cooling water** from condensers, reactor jackets (40 °C – 90 °C). 5. **Vented steam** from process relief valves. 6. **Incinerator / kiln off-gases**. 7. **Surface heat losses** from hot equipment surfaces. --- ## 1.5 Physical Constants to Memorize (Bold = KTU Exam Favourite) | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | **Universal gas constant, R** | **8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** | | **Standard ambient T (KTU/IS)** | **25 °C = 298 K** | | **1 kWh** | **3600 kJ = 3.6 MJ** | | **1 tonne of refrigeration (TR)** | **3.517 kW** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Waste Heat by Temperature (Industry-Standard Bands) KTU expects students to classify waste heat because **equipment selection depends on the temperature grade**: | Grade | Temperature Range | Typical Source | Recovery Option | |-------|-------------------|----------------|-----------------| | **High-grade** | > **650 °C** | Blast furnace gas, cement kiln exit | Waste heat boiler, ORC, steam turbine | | **Medium-grade** | **230 °C – 650 °C** | Boiler flue gas, engine exhaust | Economizer, recuperator, ORC | | **Low-grade** | **≤ 230 °C** | Condenser cooling water, dryer exhaust | Heat pump, run-around coil, direct heating | --- ## 2.2 Heat Recovery Equipment — Operational Logic ### 2.2.1 Economizer - **Function:** Preheats boiler feedwater using flue gas. - **Operating Range:** 100 °C – 400 °C stack gas. - **Typical Savings:** **3 % – 6 %** in fuel consumption. - **Why it works:** Water enters at low T, leaves near saturation, reducing fuel needed to reach boiling. ### 2.2.2 Recuperator (Air Preheater) - **Function:** Transfers heat from hot gas → combustion air. - **Operating Range:** 300 °C – 900 °C. - **Savings:** **10 % – 25 %** in furnace fuel. - **Types:** Tubular, plate, radiation. ### 2.2.3 Regenerator - **Function:** Stores heat in a refractory mass, then releases it to a colder stream in a cyclic batch operation. - **Examples:** Cowper stoves in steel plants, Ljungström air preheater. - **Why it matters:** Allows recovery above metal-tube temperature limits (up to **1200 °C**). ### 2.2.4 Waste Heat Boiler (WHB) - **Function:** Generates steam directly from flue gas. - **Outputs:** Saturated or superheated steam. - **Drives:** Bottoming Rankine cycle → electricity generation. ### 2.2.5 Heat Pipe - **Function:** Passive two-phase heat transfer device (evaporation → condensation). - **Conductance:** Extremely high, no moving parts. - **Application:** Gas–gas heat recovery, electronic cooling. ### 2.2.6 Run-Around Coil - **Function:** Closed-loop water/glycol circuit linking exhaust-side and supply-side coils. - **Use:** When streams are far apart, to prevent cross-contamination. --- ## 2.3 Bottoming Cycles — Operational Blueprint ### 2.3.1 Bottoming Cycle Concept The **bottoming cycle is a thermodynamic power cycle placed after (below) a primary thermal process**. Its sole purpose is to convert waste heat (which would otherwise be vented) into mechanical work → electricity. ### 2.3.2 Why a Separate Cycle? Why Not Just Run the Original Process Hotter? Because the **primary process is dictated by chemistry, not energy optimization** (e.g., a cement kiln must run at 1450 °C regardless of efficiency). Hence a dedicated **low-temperature power cycle** is bolted on as a "harvester." ### 2.3.3 Types of Bottoming Cycles | Cycle | Working Fluid | Temperature Range | KTU Relevance | |-------|---------------|-------------------|---------------| | **Steam Rankine Bottoming** | Water/Steam | 250 °C – 600 °C | Very High | | **Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC)** | R245fa, R134a, Isobutane, Toluene | 80 °C – 350 °C | **Highest** | | **Kalina Cycle** | Ammonia–Water mixture | 70 °C – 450 °C | High | | **Supercritical CO₂ (sCO₂)** | CO₂ | 400 °C – 700 °C | Growing | | **Stirling Engine** | Helium / Air | Any range | Low-grade | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why ORC for low-temperature waste heat?** > Water boils at 100 °C — at 80 °C waste heat, water cannot generate useful pressure. **Organic fluids** (e.g., R245fa with boiling point ≈ 15 °C) vaporize at low temperature and yield reasonable turbine pressures even from 100 °C sources. ### 2.3.4 Kalina Cycle — The Non-Ideal Mixture Advantage - Uses **NH₃–H₂O binary mixture** with variable composition. - **Variable boiling temperature** allows closer match to heat-source temperature profile → **reduced exergy destruction**. - Net efficiency gain over ORC: **10 % – 20 %** for same heat source. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready) > [!WARNING] > **KTU examiners check units rigorously.** Always convert to SI (kW, kJ, K) before substitution. Marks are routinely lost for unit mismatch. | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Boundary Condition | |---|---------|---------|-------|--------------------| | 1 | $Q_{rec} = \dot{m}\,C_{p}\,\Delta T$ | Heat recovered from a stream | kW | Steady-state, single-phase | | 2 | $\eta_{rec} = \dfrac{T_{in} - T_{out}}{T_{in} - T_{amb}}$ | Recovery effectiveness against ambient | dimensionless | $T_{amb}$ = 298 K | | 3 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Maximum theoretical cycle efficiency | dimensionless | T in Kelvin | | 4 | $\eta_{th,cycle} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$ | Bottoming cycle thermal efficiency | dimensionless | $W_{net} = W_{turb} - W_{pump}$ | | 5 | $\eta_{combined} = \dfrac{W_{top} + W_{bot}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | Topping + Bottoming system efficiency | dimensionless | Always > topping alone | | 6 | $\dot{W}_{net} = \dot{m}\, (h_{in} - h_{out})$ | Shaft work from mass flow | kW | Steady-flow 1st law | | 7 | $T_{payback} = \dfrac{I}{S_{annual}}$ | Simple payback period | years | $S_{annual}$ = annual Rs. saved | | 8 | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n}\dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^{t}} - I_0$ | Net Present Value | Rs. | $r$ = discount rate | | 9 | $Q_{fuel,saved} = \dfrac{Q_{rec}}{\eta_{boiler}}$ | Equivalent fuel heat value | kW | Use boiler efficiency | | 10 | $\dot{m}_{flue} = \dfrac{Fuel \times (1+\text{excess air}) \times M_{flue}}{M_{fuel}}$ | Stoichiometric flue gas flow | kg/s | Use ultimate analysis | > **Critical Exam Tip:** For Rankine cycles, the temperature limits (boiling and condensing) are ALWAYS in **Kelvin** when computing $\eta_{Carnot}$. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Heat Recovery Application | Bottoming Cycle Used | |----------|---------------------------|----------------------| | **Cement** | Kiln exit gas (~400 °C) → power | ORC (5 – 30 MW) | | **Steel** | Blast furnace gas → steam → power | Steam Rankine | | **Glass** | Furnace flue → electricity | ORC | | **Biomass CHP** | Engine exhaust → heating | Water/steam | | **Geothermal** | Brine at 150 °C → power | ORC, Kalina | | **Solar Thermal** | Molten salt loop → power | sCO₂, Steam Rankine | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners award **full 7 marks** only when the derivation is logically sequenced from first principles. Rote-substitution of formulas is heavily penalized. --- ## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Available in Flue Gas (1st Law Application) **Statement:** The recoverable heat in a flue gas stream of mass flow $\dot{m}$ cooling from $T_1$ to $T_2$ is to be expressed in terms of sensible enthalpy drop. ### Step 1: Define the Control Volume We isolate a section of duct where flue gas cools from inlet temperature $T_1$ to outlet $T_2$, with negligible kinetic and potential energy changes and no shaft work. ### Step 2: Apply Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) $$ \dot{Q}_{cv} - \dot{W}_{cv} = \dot{m}\left[\left(h_2 + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2} + gz_2\right) - \left(h_1 + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2} + gz_1\right)\right] $$ ### Step 3: Apply Boundary Conditions > No shaft work is extracted in the duct ($\dot{W}_{cv} = 0$), and $\Delta KE \approx \Delta PE \approx 0$. Hence the SFEE reduces to: $$ \dot{Q}_{cv} = \dot{m}\,(h_2 - h_1) $$ ### Step 4: Introduce Specific Heat for Sensible Heat For an ideal gas with constant $C_p$: $$ h_2 - h_1 = C_p\,(T_2 - T_1) $$ ### Step 5: Substitute and Define Recovery Direction Since we want heat **delivered to recovery equipment** (and lost from flue gas), we write $\dot{Q}_{rec} = \dot{m} C_p (T_1 - T_2)$: $$ \boxed{\dot{Q}_{rec} = \dot{m}\,C_{p}\,(T_{1} - T_{2}) \;\;[\text{kW}]} $$ This is the **fundamental KTU heat-recovery formula**, applied across economizers, air preheaters, and waste heat boilers. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Thermal Efficiency of a Bottoming Cycle **Statement:** Show that the bottoming cycle efficiency depends only on net work output and heat input from waste. ### Step 1: Define Cycle Boundary A control volume enclosing the turbine, condenser, pump, and heat exchanger (boiler/evaporator). ### Step 2: Energy Balance Across Cycle (1st Law, Cyclic Process) For any thermodynamic cycle, the working fluid returns to its initial state, so $\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$. Hence over one cycle: $$ Q_{net,in} - W_{net,out} = 0 $$ ### Step 3: Identify Heat and Work Terms - Heat in from waste stream (evaporator): $Q_{in} = \dot{m}_{wf}\,(h_3 - h_2)$ - Heat out at condenser: $Q_{out} = \dot{m}_{wf}\,(h_4 - h_1)$ - Turbine work: $W_T = \dot{m}_{wf}\,(h_3 - h_4)$ - Pump work: $W_P = \dot{m}_{wf}\,(h_2 - h_1)$ where state points are: 1 = condenser exit, 2 = pump exit, 3 = turbine inlet, 4 = turbine exit. ### Step 4: Net Work Expression $$ W_{net} = W_T - W_P = \dot{m}_{wf}\,(h_3 - h_4) - \dot{m}_{wf}\,(h_2 - h_1) $$ ### Step 5: Substitute into Cycle Energy Balance $$ Q_{in} - Q_{out} = W_{net} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{wf}(h_3 - h_2) - \dot{m}_{wf}(h_4 - h_1) = \dot{m}_{wf}(h_3 - h_4) - \dot{m}_{wf}(h_2 - h_1) $$ This identity confirms the cycle closure. ### Step 6: Define Thermal Efficiency $$ \eta_{th,cycle} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2} $$ In magnitude: $$ \boxed{\eta_{th,cycle} = 1 - \frac{h_4 - h_1}{h_3 - h_2}} $$ ### Step 7: Express in terms of Carnot Limit (useful comparison) For an idealized bottoming cycle between $T_H$ (waste source) and $T_L$ (sink): $$ \eta_{th,cycle} \le \eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} $$ > **KTU Interpretation:** Bottoming cycles are inherently low-efficiency (5 % – 20 %) because $T_H$ is low; however, the **input energy is "free"** (otherwise wasted), so the **economic efficiency** (savings per rupee invested) is typically very attractive. --- ## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Format) > **Problem [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]:** > An industrial furnace exhausts **2.5 kg/s** of flue gas at **350 °C** with $C_p = 1.05$ kJ/(kg·K). The gas is cooled to **180 °C** in an economizer, which preheats combustion air from **30 °C to 200 °C**. The furnace operates **6000 hours/year**. If fuel cost is **₹ 4 per kWh** (thermal equivalent) and the project cost is **₹ 50 lakhs**, calculate: > > (a) Heat recovered per hour in kWh. > (b) Recovery efficiency with respect to ambient of 25 °C. > (c) Annual monetary savings and simple payback period. ### Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step): **Given Data Compilation:** - $\dot{m} = 2.5$ kg/s - $C_p = 1.05$ kJ/(kg·K) - $T_1 = 350\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C},\; T_2 = 180\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ - $T_{amb} = 25\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ - Operating hours: $N = 6000$ h/year - Fuel cost: $C_f = ₹\,4/\mathrm{kWh}$ - Investment: $I_0 = ₹\,50{,}00{,}000$ #### (a) Heat Recovered Per Hour Apply the fundamental recovery equation (derived in §3.1): $$ \dot{Q}_{rec} = \dot{m}\,C_p\,(T_1 - T_2) $$ Substituting: $$ \dot{Q}_{rec} = 2.5 \times 1.05 \times (350 - 180) $$ $$ \dot{Q}_{rec} = 2.625 \times 170 = 446.25 \text{ kW} $$ Convert to kWh per hour (1 hour at 446.25 kW = 446.25 kWh): $$ \boxed{Q_{rec,hourly} = 446.25 \text{ kWh/h}} $$ #### (b) Recovery Efficiency Use the KTU benchmark definition: $$ \eta_{rec} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_{amb}} $$ Convert to Kelvin (numerically same as Celsius differences for ratios, but KTU expects K): $$ \eta_{rec} = \frac{(350 + 273) - (180 + 273)}{(350 + 273) - (25 + 273)} = \frac{170}{325} $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{rec} = 0.5231 = 52.31\%} $$ #### (c) Annual Savings and Payback Annual energy recovered: $$ Q_{annual} = 446.25 \times 6000 = 2{,}677{,}500 \text{ kWh/year} $$ Annual monetary savings (each kWh of recovered heat displaces ₹ 4 of fuel): $$ S_{annual} = 2{,}677{,}500 \times 4 = ₹\,1{,}07{,}10{,}000 \text{ per year} $$ Simple payback period (using the formula from §2.4, Row 7): $$ T_{payback} = \frac{I_0}{S_{annual}} = \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{1{,}07{,}10{,}000} $$ $$ \boxed{T_{payback} \approx 0.467 \text{ years} \approx 5.6 \text{ months}} $$ > **Economic Verdict:** The project pays back in under **6 months** — an excellent KTU case-study outcome (typical benchmark: payback < 2 years is considered "viable"). --- ## 3.4 Bottoming Cycle Performance — Analytical Example > **Problem [KTU Model Question]:** > Waste heat is available at **280 °C** from an industrial process at a rate of **3 kg/s** of exhaust gas with $C_p = 1.1$ kJ/(kg·K). A bottoming ORC operates with turbine inlet at **200 °C** and condenser at **40 °C**. > > (i) Heat input to cycle. > (ii) Maximum theoretical (Carnot) efficiency. > (iii) Net work output if actual efficiency = **60 % of Carnot**. ### Solution: #### (i) Heat Input to Cycle Take gas cooling from 280 °C to 200 °C as the effective heat transfer window: $$ \dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}\,C_p\,(T_{source,in} - T_{source,out}) = 3 \times 1.1 \times (280 - 200) $$ $$ \boxed{\dot{Q}_{in} = 264 \text{ kW}} $$ #### (ii) Carnot Efficiency Convert to Kelvin: $T_H = 200 + 273 = 473$ K, $T_L = 40 + 273 = 313$ K. $$ \eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{313}{473} $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 0.3383 = 33.83\%} $$ #### (iii) Net Work Output $$ \eta_{actual} = 0.60 \times 33.83\% = 20.30\% $$ $$ \dot{W}_{net} = \eta_{actual} \times \dot{Q}_{in} = 0.2030 \times 264 $$ $$ \boxed{\dot{W}_{net} \approx 53.6 \text{ kW}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** Even with a 60 %-of-Carnot penalty (realistic for ORC), **53.6 kW** of "free" power is generated continuously — annually ≈ **3.2 lakh kWh** at 6000 hrs of operation. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > Mermaid is used to render block-level functional architecture. Complex thermodynamic (T-s, h-s) plots are not attempted as Mermaid cannot faithfully render P-V or thermodynamic property diagrams. --- ## 4.1 Industrial Waste Heat Recovery System Architecture ```mermaid graph LR A[Industrial Process
Furnace Kiln Boiler] --> B[Hot Waste Stream
Flue Gas 350C] B --> C[Primary Heat Recovery
Economizer] C --> D[Secondary Heat Recovery
Air Preheater] D --> E[Low Temperature Exhaust
180C] E --> F[Stack to Atmosphere] C --> G[Preheated Feedwater
to Process] D --> H[Preheated Combustion Air
to Burner] A -.Waste Heat.-> I[Bottoming Cycle
ORC Steam] I --> J[Turbine] J --> K[Generator Electricity] I --> L[Condenser] L --> M[Cooling Tower] ``` --- ## 4.2 Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) Bottoming Cycle Architecture ```mermaid graph LR A[Waste Heat Source
200C Flue Gas] --> B[Evaporator
Heat Exchanger] B --> C[Turbine
Expander] C --> D[Generator
Electrical Output] D --> E[Condenser
40C Rejection] E --> F[Pump
Pressurize Liquid] F --> B E --> G[Cooling Water Loop] G --> H[Cooling Tower] ``` **Cycle Description (text equivalent):** 1. Working fluid (e.g., R245fa) is pressurized by the **pump (F)**. 2. It enters the **evaporator (B)** as cold liquid and absorbs waste heat → becomes high-pressure vapor. 3. Vapor expands through the **turbine (C)** producing shaft work → electricity at **(D)**. 4. Low-pressure vapor is condensed in **(E)** by a cooling tower loop **(G-H)**. 5. Liquid returns to pump — **cycle repeats**. --- ## 4.3 Pinch-Point Concept in Heat Recovery (Functional Block) ```mermaid graph TD A[Hot Stream
Flue Gas Curve] --> B[Pinch Point
Smallest Delta T] B --> C[Cold Stream
Air Water Curve] D[Above Pinch
Hot Utility Zone] --> E[Heat Exchanger Network] F[Below Pinch
Cold Utility Zone] --> E E --> G[Minimum Energy
Target] ``` > **Pinch interpretation:** The minimum temperature difference ($\Delta T_{min}$, typically **10 °C – 20 °C**) between hot and cold streams at the pinch sets the thermodynamic floor of recovery — the **KTU benchmark for minimum energy target**. --- ## 4.4 Comparison: Topping vs Bottoming Cycle ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Topping A1[Fuel Combustion] --> A2[High T Heat] A2 --> A3[Power Turbine] A3 --> A4[Waste Heat Low T] end subgraph Bottoming A4 --> B1[Bottoming Boiler] B1 --> B2[Bottoming Turbine] B2 --> B3[Power Output 2] B3 --> B4[Final Condenser] end ``` In the **topping cycle**, fuel → work → waste. In the **bottoming cycle**, that "waste" → work → truly final waste. This is the **classical CHP upgrade** to a tri-generation or waste-recovery system. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **Define waste heat recovery. List any four sources of waste heat in a process industry with their typical temperature range.** **Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):** > Waste heat recovery is the practice of capturing and reusing thermal energy discharged by industrial processes that would otherwise be lost to the environment, improving overall energy efficiency. Four sources are: (i) **High-temperature flue gases** (500–1000 °C) from boilers and furnaces; (ii) **Engine/turbine exhaust** (300–600 °C); (iii) **Hot product streams** in cement and steel (200–800 °C); (iv) **Hot cooling water** from condensers (40–90 °C). *(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for sources with temperatures)* ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **Differentiate between topping and bottoming cycles. Give one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks, ~70 words):** > A **topping cycle** generates power first from high-grade fuel heat, then uses residual low-grade heat for process use (e.g., a coal-fired Rankine plant). A **bottoming cycle** is placed after a process to convert its waste heat into useful power (e.g., an **Organic Rankine Cycle** recovering kiln exit gas). The key difference is **direction of energy cascade** — topping works top-down; bottoming works from medium-grade waste heat upwards. *(1.5 marks each)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT** confuse **Carnot efficiency** (ideal) with **ORC/Rankine actual efficiency**. Always state the assumption explicitly. > - **Do NOT** use Celsius for $T_H/T_L$ in Carnot formula — this single mistake costs **2–3 marks** consistently. > - **Always show units** in intermediate steps; absence leads to **−0.5 to −1 mark** deductions. > - **Mention the working fluid** by name in ORC answers — generic "steam" in an ORC context = **−1 mark**. > - For payback period, **state the formula and substitute** — direct numerical answer without derivation loses **1 mark**. --- ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **Q.A (a)** [7 Marks] — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]* **With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) for waste heat recovery. List any four suitable working fluids with their key advantage.** #### Model Solution: **Working of ORC (Block Diagram — see §4.2):** 1. **Waste heat source** (e.g., flue gas at 200 °C) enters the **evaporator** of the ORC module. 2. The **organic working fluid** (low boiling point) absorbs this heat and vaporizes at moderate pressure. 3. The high-pressure vapor expands through the **turbine**, producing shaft work → drives the **generator** to produce electricity. 4. Low-pressure vapor enters the **condenser**, where it rejects heat to a cooling water loop / cooling tower. 5. The condensed liquid is pressurized by the **pump** and returned to the evaporator — cycle repeats. **Suitable Working Fluids:** | Fluid | Boiling Point (°C) | Advantage | |-------|--------------------|-----------| | **R245fa** | 15.3 | Non-flammable, low pressure, ideal for 100–200 °C | | **Isobutane (R600a)** | −11.7 | High thermodynamic efficiency | | **Toluene** | 110.6 | Good for high-temperature waste (>250 °C) | | **Cyclopentane** | 49.2 | Environment-friendly, used in geothermal ORC | *(2 marks block diagram + 3 marks working + 2 marks fluids = 7 marks)* --- **Q.A (b)** [7 Marks] — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024]* **A furnace exhausts 2.5 kg/s of flue gas at 350 °C ($C_p$ = 1.05 kJ/kg·K). The gas is cooled to 180 °C in an economizer to preheat combustion air from 30 °C to 200 °C. The furnace operates 6000 h/year. If fuel cost is ₹ 4/kWh thermal equivalent and the investment is ₹ 50 lakhs, compute:** **(i) Heat recovered per hour,** **(ii) Recovery efficiency with respect to ambient (25 °C),** **(iii) Annual monetary savings and simple payback period.** #### Model Solution: > This is the **same worked example** as §3.3. The complete step-by-step solution is presented there. **Summary of Results:** - (i) Heat recovered = **446.25 kWh/h** - (ii) Recovery efficiency = **52.31 %** - (iii) Annual savings = **₹ 1.071 crore**, Payback = **0.467 years (≈ 5.6 months)** *(1 mark identification of formula + 2 marks substitution + 1 mark answer + 1 mark payback formula + 2 marks computation = 7 marks)* --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q.B (a)** [7 Marks] — *[KTU University Exam - May 2023]* **Explain the principle and operation of any four waste heat recovery equipment commonly used in Indian process industries. State their typical temperature range of application.** #### Model Solution: 1. **Economizer (100 – 400 °C):** Recovers heat from flue gas to preheat boiler feedwater. Saves **3 % – 6 %** fuel. Reduces stack temperature. *(2 marks)* 2. **Recuperator / Air Preheater (300 – 900 °C):** Preheats combustion air using flue gas. Can be tubular, plate, or radiation type. Improves combustion efficiency by **10 % – 25 %**. *(2 marks)* 3. **Waste Heat Boiler (250 – 1000 °C):** Generates saturated or superheated steam from waste heat. Drives a bottoming Rankine cycle for power generation. Common in cement and steel. *(1.5 marks)* 4. **Regenerator (Cowper Stove, up to 1200 °C):** Stores heat in a refractory checker mass and releases it cyclically. Allows recovery above metallic temperature limits. Used in **blast furnace** preheated air systems. *(1.5 marks)* *(Total: 7 marks — 4 devices × ~1.75 marks each)* --- **Q.B (b)** [7 Marks] — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]* **Waste heat is available at 280 °C from an industrial process with a flue gas mass flow of 3 kg/s ($C_p$ = 1.1 kJ/kg·K). A bottoming Organic Rankine Cycle operates between turbine inlet temperature of 200 °C and condenser temperature of 40 °C.** **Determine:** **(i) Heat input to the cycle,** **(ii) Carnot efficiency,** **(iii) Net power output if actual cycle efficiency = 60 % of Carnot.** #### Model Solution: > This is the **same worked example** as §3.4. **Given:** $\dot{m} = 3$ kg/s; $C_p = 1.1$ kJ/(kg·K); $T_H = 200 °C = 473$ K; $T_L = 40 °C = 313$ K. **(i) Heat Input:** $$ \dot{Q}_{in} = 3 \times 1.1 \times (280 - 200) = 264 \text{ kW} $$ **(ii) Carnot Efficiency:** $$ \eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{313}{473} = 0.3383 = 33.83\% $$ **(iii) Net Power Output:** $$ \dot{W}_{net} = 0.60 \times 0.3383 \times 264 \approx 53.6 \text{ kW} $$ *(2 marks + 2 marks + 3 marks = 7 marks)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save before exam):** - ⭐ **Waste heat recovery = collection + reuse of rejected industrial thermal energy.** - ⭐ **Topping cycle:** Fuel → Work → Waste. **Bottoming cycle:** Waste → Work → Final rejection. - ⭐ **Master formula:** $\dot{Q}_{rec} = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$ (in kW if $\dot{m}$ in kg/s, $C_p$ in kJ/(kg·K), $\Delta T$ in K or °C). - ⭐ **Recovery efficiency:** $\eta_{rec} = \dfrac{T_{in} - T_{out}}{T_{in} - T_{amb}}$; **ambient = 25 °C = 298 K** is the KTU benchmark. - ⭐ **Carnot limit:** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ — **always Kelvin**. - ⭐ **ORC uses organic fluids** (R245fa, toluene, cyclopentane) because their low boiling point allows recovery from **80 °C – 300 °C** waste heat where water cannot vaporize usefully. - ⭐ **Kalina cycle uses NH₃–H₂O mixture** for variable-temperature boiling — closer match to heat-source profile, higher efficiency than ORC. - ⭐ **Economizer** → preheats water. **Recuperator** → preheats air. **Regenerator** → cyclic storage. **WHB** → generates steam. - ⭐ **Payback < 2 years** → viable (industry rule of thumb emphasized in KTU case studies). - ⭐ **Heat grades:** High (> 650 °C) → WHB/steam turbine; Medium (230 – 650 °C) → ORC/Air preheater; Low (< 230 °C) → heat pump/run-around coil. - ⭐ **1 TR = 3.517 kW; 1 kWh = 3600 kJ** — memorize for unit-conversion problems. - ⭐ **Common ORC mistake in exam:** Writing "steam" instead of naming the organic fluid. Always name it. - ⭐ **Pinch Analysis** establishes minimum energy target — $\Delta T_{min}$ = 10–20 °C. - ⭐ **Combined efficiency** of topping + bottoming can exceed **60 %** in a CHP-tri-generation system, vs ~35 % for standalone power. - ⭐ **Final mantra for KTU:** *High-grade first, then harvest the rest.*
